**Markov Assumption 2 (Motion):** The current pose depends only on the previous pose and the current control:

$$
p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t, \text{history}) \;=\; p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)
$$

**Apply the law of total probability and substitute the belief at $t-1$:**

$$
b(x_t) \;=\; \eta \; p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \; b(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

This is the **final recursive form**, computable in real time using Kalman filters, particle filters, or histogram filters.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Describe the **three-layer (3T) cognitive architecture** for autonomous robots. State the function of each layer and explain how information flows between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot is exploring an unknown corridor. Explain how **expected information gain** can be used as a cognitive decision metric to choose where to look next. Derive the entropy-based expression. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **3T architecture** decomposes robot cognition into three horizontal layers:

**Layer 1 — Deliberative Layer**
- **Function:** High-level *mission planning*. Generates global strategies, routes, and task allocations over a long time horizon.
- **Techniques:** A*, D*, Rapidly-Exploring Random Trees (RRTs), task planners like STRIPS.
- **Speed:** Slow (seconds to minutes per decision).
- **Example:** *"Pick up the package from Room B and deliver to Room D."*

**Layer 2 — Sequencing / Executive Layer**
- **Function:** Decomposes deliberative plans into sequenced sub-tasks. Manages time, resources, and monitors progress.
- **Techniques:** Finite-state machines, Petri nets, behavior trees.
- **Example:** *"Move forward 2 m → turn 90° left → wait 5 s → request sensor data."*

**Layer 3 — Reactive Layer**
- **Function:** Fast, low-level *stimulus-response* control. Handles real-time obstacles, emergencies, and reflex behaviors.
- **Techniques:** Potential fields, vector field histograms, subsumption.
- **Speed:** Very fast (< 10 ms per cycle).
- **Example:** *"If obstacle < 0.5 m, stop wheels."*

**Information flow:**
- *Top-down:* Deliberative goals → Sequencing sub-tasks → Reactive motor commands.
- *Bottom-up:* Sensor data → Reactive reflex updates → Sequencing status → Deliberative re-planning.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

A robot exploring an unknown corridor faces the cognitive decision: *"Where should I look next to reduce my uncertainty the most?"* The metric used is **expected information gain** $I(a)$.

**Step 1 — Define current belief entropy:**

$$
H(b) \;=\; -\int b(x) \log b(x) \, dx
$$

**Step 2 — Compute expected post-action entropy** by averaging over all possible future observations $z$:

$$
H(b \mid a) \;=\; \int p(z \mid a) \left[ -\int b'(x \mid a, z) \log b'(x \mid a, z) \, dx \right] dz
$$

**Step 3 — Information gain is the entropy reduction:**

$$
I(a) \;=\; H(b) - H(b \mid a)
$$

**Cognitive decision rule:** The robot picks the action that maximizes information gain:

$$
a^{*} \;=\; \arg\max_{a} \; I(a)
$$

**Practical example:** If turning left is expected to reveal a new room (high information), and turning right is expected to reveal only a wall (low information), the cognitive planner will choose the **left turn** to maximize $I(a)$. As the corridor is fully explored, $H(b) \rightarrow 0$ and the robot's curiosity is satisfied.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing Bayes filter derivation without the **two Markov assumptions** → loses 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Drawing the 3T architecture as *vertical* layers without **arrows showing feedback** → loses 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing *reactive* with *reflex* — the KTU answer must explicitly say *"no world model"*.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Skipping the *role of the world model* in the Sense–Think–Act answer → lose 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** In entropy/information-gain questions, forgetting the **$\log$ base** (must state base 2 for bits) → minor deduction.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Cognition" in Robot Navigation:**

- **Definition:** Cognition = Perception + Memory + Reasoning + Planning + Learning + Action.
- **Formal abstraction:** $C : (P, M, G, T) \rightarrow A$.
- **Sense–Think–Act loop** has **6 sub-stages**: Sense → Perceive → Localize → Model → Reason/Plan → Act.
- **World model types:** Metric, Topological, Semantic, Hybrid.
- **Three cognitive paradigms:**
  - *Reactive* (no world model, fast)
  - *Deliberative* (world model, slow, optimal)
  - *Hybrid / 3T* (best of both)
- **Bayes filter** $b(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) b(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ — the math of robot belief.
- **Two Markov assumptions** — sensing (current obs depends on current pose) and motion (current pose depends on previous pose + control). **Must state both in exams.**
- **Bellman optimality:** $J^{*}(x) = \min_{a} \left[ c(x,a) + \gamma \int p(x' \mid x,a) J^{*}(x') \, dx' \right]$.
- **Information gain:** $I(a) = H(b) - H(b \mid a)$ — drives active perception / exploration.
- **Cognitive architectures** to remember: Subsumption (Brooks), SOAR, ACT-R, 3T (NASREM), LAAS, CLARAty.
- **Key engineering applications:** Autonomous cars, Mars rovers, warehouse robots, surgical robots, search-and-rescue.
- **Mnemonic:** *PA-MR-LPA* = Perception, Attention, Memory, Reasoning, Learning, Planning, Action.
- **Common pitfall:** Do NOT confuse *cognition* with *perception* — perception is *one* of the seven cognitive functions, not its synonym.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Motion Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
**Motion Control** in the context of robot navigation is the sub-discipline of robotics concerned with generating, planning, and executing the time-parameterized trajectories that drive a robot's actuators (joint motors, wheels, or thrusters) from an initial configuration to a desired final configuration, while satisfying kinematic (position, velocity) and dynamic (acceleration, torque/force) constraints. It forms the closed-loop execution layer that translates high-level navigational intent (e.g., "move to grid cell (x,y)") into precisely timed low-level actuator commands.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Motion Control is *not* the same as Path Planning. Path Planning answers *"where to go"* (geometric route), while Motion Control answers *"how to physically move along that route, when, and how fast"*.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are driving a car from your home to a college campus 20 km away. The **path planning** is the Google Maps route (turn left at the bakery, take the highway, exit at the 2nd gate). **Motion Control**, however, is the actual *act of driving*:
- *When* to press the accelerator,
- *How hard* to press it (acceleration profile),
- *When* to ease off the pedal near a turn,
- *When* to brake before a red light (deceleration),
- All while keeping the steering wheel aligned (closed-loop feedback).

In a robot arm picking up a cup of hot coffee, motion control ensures the arm accelerates gently, moves smoothly, decelerates as it nears the cup, and stops *exactly* at the cup's location without spilling a drop.

### Key Physical Constants / Standard Metrics
- **Maximum joint velocity** $\omega_{max}$ in **rad/s** or **deg/s**
- **Maximum joint acceleration** $\alpha_{max}$ in **rad/s²**
- **Maximum joint torque** $\tau_{max}$ in **Nm**
- **Position error tolerance** typically $\pm \mathbf{0.1 \text{ mm}}$ for industrial robots
- **Settling time** $t_s$ in **seconds**
- **Control loop frequency** typically **1 kHz** to **10 kHz** for industrial manipulators
- **Gravitational acceleration** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 scheme for PEBRT751 treats motion control as the bridge between **kinematic planning** (Module 1/2 topics) and **dynamic actuation** (covered in advanced control modules). Mastering this topic is essential for solving 14-mark ESE problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Motion Control
Motion control in robotics is broadly classified along two orthogonal axes:

**A. By Trajectory Type:**
1. **Point-to-Point (PTP) Control** — Robot moves from A to B without regard to the intermediate path. Examples: pick-and-place, CNC tool changes.
2. **Continuous Path (CP) Control** — The full path between A and B must be followed. Examples: welding robots, painting robots, laser cutting.
3. **Controlled Path** — Hybrid; the path is constrained but a time/velocity profile is also enforced.

**B. By Control Variable (the feedback quantity):**
1. **Position Control** — Error signal is positional deviation.
2. **Velocity Control** — Error signal is velocity deviation.
3. **Acceleration Control** — Error signal is acceleration deviation.
4. **Force/Torque (Impedance) Control** — Error signal is contact force; used for assembly and compliant tasks.

### 2.2 The Three Pillars of Motion Control
1. **Trajectory Generation** → Mathematical curve $q(t)$ defining position/velocity/acceleration over time.
2. **Reference Tracking** → The controller forces the actual joint $q_{act}(t)$ to follow the reference $q_{ref}(t)$.
3. **Constraint Satisfaction** → Joint limits, velocity limits, acceleration limits, and torque limits are never violated.

### 2.3 Trajectory Generation Strategies
- **Joint-Space Trajectories** — Interpolated between start and end joint angles. Simple, guarantees no kinematic singularities along the way, but Cartesian path is unpredictable.
- **Cartesian-Space Trajectories** — Interpolated between start and end poses in (x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw). Predictable Cartesian path, but requires solving inverse kinematics at every waypoint (computationally expensive).
- **Trapezoidal Velocity Profile** — The most common acceleration profile: accelerate at constant $a_{max}$, cruise at $v_{max}$, decelerate at $-a_{max}$.
- **S-Curve (Polynomial) Profile** — Acceleration itself is ramped; smoother, reduces jerk, ideal for high-speed or delicate payloads.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $q(t)$ | Joint position trajectory | Polynomial fit of degree $n$ over time $t$ | rad or m |
| $v(t)$ | Joint velocity | $\dot{q}(t) = \dfrac{dq}{dt}$ | rad/s or m/s |
| $a(t)$ | Joint acceleration | $\ddot{q}(t) = \dfrac{d^2q}{dt^2}$ | rad/s² or m/s² |
| $j(t)$ | Jerk (rate of change of acceleration) | $\dddot{q}(t) = \dfrac{d^3q}{dt^3}$ | rad/s³ or m/s³ |
| $e(t)$ | Tracking error | $q_{ref}(t) - q_{act}(t)$ | rad or m |
| $u(t)$ | Control signal (voltage/current) | $K_p e(t) + K_d \dot{e}(t) + K_i \int e(t)\, dt$ | V or A |
| $T_{accel}$ | Acceleration time | $\dfrac{v_{max}}{a_{max}}$ | s |
| $T_{tri}$ | Total trapezoidal move time | $T_{accel} + T_{cruise} + T_{decel}$ | s |
| $s(t)$ | Cubic polynomial position | $a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | m or rad |
| $s(t)$ | Quintic polynomial position | $a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$ | m or rad |
| $\tau$ | Joint torque | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ | Nm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In the cubic polynomial trajectory formula, *never* confuse the polynomial coefficients $a_2, a_3$ with the **acceleration** and **jerk** of the motion. They are different quantities. The acceleration at time $t$ is $\ddot{s}(t) = 2a_2 + 6a_3 t$, and jerk is $\dddot{s}(t) = 6a_3$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
Motion control is the invisible backbone of:
- **Industrial Robotic Arms** (KUKA, ABB, FANUC) — welding, painting, assembly.
- **CNC Machining Centers** — milling, lathe turning.
- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs)** — warehouse navigation.
- **Surgical Robots** (Da Vinci system) — sub-millimeter accuracy.
- **Drone Swarms** — coordinated formation flying.
- **3D Printers** — precise XYZ head positioning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cubic Polynomial Trajectory (Joint-Space)

**Problem Setup:** A single joint must move from an initial angle $q_0$ at $t = 0$ to a final angle $q_f$ at $t = T$, starting and ending at rest (i.e., zero initial and final velocity). The joint position must be a smooth function of time.

**Step 1 — Assume the trajectory form.**
Because we have 4 boundary conditions (initial position, final position, initial velocity, final velocity) and a 2nd-order differential relationship ($v = \dot{q}$), the lowest-degree polynomial that satisfies all 4 conditions is a **cubic** (degree 3):

$$s(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

**Step 2 — Take the first derivative to get velocity.**

$$v(t) = \dot{s}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the four boundary conditions.**

| Condition | Equation | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $s(0) = q_0$ | $a_0 = q_0$ | Initial position |
| $s(T) = q_f$ | $a_0 + a_1 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = q_f$ | Final position |
| $v(0) = 0$ | $a_1 = 0$ | Initial velocity is zero |
| $v(T) = 0$ | $a_1 + 2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2 = 0$ | Final velocity is zero |

**Step 4 — Solve the linear system.**

From Condition 3: $a_1 = 0$.

From Condition 4: $0 + 2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2 = 0$, which gives:

$$2 a_2 T = -3 a_3 T^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_2 = -\dfrac{3 a_3 T}{2}$$

From Condition 2 (using $a_1 = 0$ and $a_0 = q_0$):

$$q_0 + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = q_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = q_f - q_0$$

Substitute $a_2$:

$$-\dfrac{3 a_3 T}{2} \cdot T^2 + a_3 T^3 = q_f - q_0$$

$$-\dfrac{3}{2} a_3 T^3 + a_3 T^3 = q_f - q_0$$

$$-\dfrac{1}{2} a_3 T^3 = q_f - q_0$$

$$a_3 = \dfrac{2(q_0 - q_f)}{T^3}$$

**Step 5 — Back-substitute to find $a_2$.**

$$a_2 = -\dfrac{3 T}{2} \cdot \dfrac{2(q_0 - q_f)}{T^3} = \dfrac{3(q_f - q_0)}{T^2}$$

**Step 6 — Final compact trajectory equation.**

$$s(t) = q_0 + \dfrac{3(q_f - q_0)}{T^2} t^2 - \dfrac{2(q_f - q_0)}{T^3} t^3$$

**Step 7 — Acceleration profile.**

$$\ddot{s}(t) = \dfrac{6(q_f - q_0)}{T^2} - \dfrac{12(q_f - q_0)}{T^3} t$$

Notice acceleration is **linear** in $t$ and **not constant** — this is why cubic trajectories have a trapezoidal-shaped velocity profile (ramp up, then ramp down), but with **non-constant** acceleration.

> [!NOTE]
> A cubic polynomial cannot specify a target acceleration, so if the application demands a non-zero initial or final acceleration (e.g., blending with another segment), a **quintic polynomial** (degree 5) must be used, which has 6 coefficients and 6 boundary conditions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Trapezoidal Velocity Profile (Time Parameterization)

**Problem Setup:** A joint must traverse a distance $D = q_f - q_0$ in minimum time subject to maximum velocity $v_{max}$ and maximum acceleration $a_{max}$.

**Step 1 — Determine if the cruise phase is reached.**

The velocity reached by pure acceleration over the full distance is $v_{peak} = \sqrt{a_{max} \cdot D}$.

Compare $v_{peak}$ with $v_{max}$:
- If $v_{peak} \le v_{max}$ → **Triangular profile** (no cruise phase).
- If $v_{peak} > v_{max}$ → **Trapezoidal profile** (cruise phase exists).

**Step 2 — Compute acceleration time.**

$$T_{accel} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{a_{max}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute deceleration time (symmetric).**

$$T_{decel} = T_{accel} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{a_{max}}$$

**Step 4 — Distance covered during acceleration and deceleration phases.**

$$D_{accel} = \dfrac{1}{2} a_{max} T_{accel}^2 = \dfrac{v_{max}^2}{2 a_{max}}$$

$$D_{decel} = \dfrac{v_{max}^2}{2 a_{max}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute remaining distance for the cruise phase.**

$$D_{cruise} = D - D_{accel} - D_{decel} = D - \dfrac{v_{max}^2}{a_{max}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute cruise time.**

$$T_{cruise} = \dfrac{D_{cruise}}{v_{max}}$$

**Step 7 — Total motion time.**

$$T_{total} = T_{accel} + T_{cruise} + T_{decel} = \dfrac{2 v_{max}}{a_{max}} + \dfrac{D}{v_{max}} - \dfrac{v_{max}}{a_{max}} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{a_{max}} + \dfrac{D}{v_{max}}$$

This closed-form expression appears repeatedly in KTU exam problems involving motion-time optimization.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Control Strategies

| Control Strategy | Error Variable | Key Equation | Best For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P (Proportional)** | $e(t)$ | $u = K_p e$ | Simple, low-cost | Steady-state error |
| **PD** | $e, \dot{e}$ | $u = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$ | Most robotic joints | No integral action |
| **PID** | $e, \dot{e}, \int e$ | $u = K_p e + K_i \int e + K_d \dot{e}$ | General purpose | Tuning complexity |
| **Feedforward + Feedback** | $e$ + reference | $u = \tau_{ff}(q, \dot{q}, \ddot{q}) + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$ | High-speed tracking | Requires accurate model |
| **Computed Torque** | $e, \dot{e}$ | $\tau = M(q)(\ddot{q}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}) + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ | Model-based control | Needs full dynamic model |
| **Impedance Control** | Force + position | $M_d \ddot{x} + B_d \dot{x} + K_d (x - x_d) = F_{ext}$ | Contact-rich tasks | Complex tuning |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Closed-Loop Motion Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Trajectory Generator] --> B[Inverse Kinematics]
    B --> C[Reference Joint Angles qref]
    C --> D[Controller PID or Computed Torque]
    E[Encoder Feedback qact] --> F[Error Computation e = qref - qact]
    F --> D
    D --> G[Power Amplifier / Motor Driver]
    G --> H[Robot Joint Actuator]
    H --> I[Robot Link / End Effector]
    I --> E
    J[External Disturbances] -.-> H

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 State Diagram: Motion Control Execution Phases

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Planning: Receive Goal Pose
    Planning --> TrajectoryGen: Path Computed
    TrajectoryGen --> Interpolating: Path Validated
    Interpolating --> Executing: Reference Stream Ready
    Executing --> Monitoring: Motors Active
    Monitoring --> Executing: Error Within Tolerance
    Monitoring --> EmergencyStop: Error Exceeds Limit
    Monitoring --> GoalReached: qact = qref Final
    GoalReached --> Idle: Task Complete
    EmergencyStop --> Idle: Manual Reset

    style Idle fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#000
    style Planning fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style TrajectoryGen fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style Executing fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style EmergencyStop fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000
    style GoalReached fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Trapezoidal Velocity Profile Phases

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Phase 1: Acceleration] --> P2{Peak Velocity Reached?}
    P2 -->|Yes| P3[Phase 2: Cruise at Vmax]
    P2 -->|No| P4[Phase 3: Deceleration Start]
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5{Distance D Travelled?}
    P5 -->|Yes| P6[Phase 4: Full Stop at qf]
    P5 -->|No| P4
    P6 --> P7[Motion Complete]

    style P1 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style P3 fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style P4 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style P6 fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style P7 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Point-to-Point (PTP) control and Continuous Path (CP) control in robotic motion systems. Give one industrial example of each.*

**Model Answer:**
- **PTP Control:** The robot moves from an initial position to a final position without controlling the path taken in between. The controller only cares about reaching the destination; the intermediate trajectory is not prescribed. Used in pick-and-place operations, palletizing, and CNC tool changes.
- **CP Control:** The robot must follow a precisely defined geometric path between the start and end points, with the controller continuously correcting deviations. Used in arc welding, spray painting, laser cutting, and adhesive dispensing.
- *Key distinction:* PTP optimizes only the endpoints, whereas CP enforces the entire spatial curve.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is a trapezoidal velocity profile? Why is it preferred over a rectangular velocity profile in robotic motion control?*

**Model Answer:**
A **trapezoidal velocity profile** is a motion profile in which the joint accelerates at a constant maximum acceleration $a_{max}$ from zero to a maximum velocity $v_{max}$, cruises at $v_{max}$ for some duration, and then decelerates symmetrically back to zero. The shape of the velocity-vs-time graph is a trapezoid.
It is preferred over a **rectangular (bang-bang) profile** because:
1. **Finite acceleration** protects the motor windings and gear train from current/torque shocks.
2. **Smooth transitions** reduce mechanical vibration and structural fatigue.
3. **Better tracking accuracy** because actuators have finite bandwidth and cannot realize infinite acceleration steps.
4. **Lower jerk**, which is critical when transporting delicate or liquid payloads.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the cubic polynomial trajectory equation for a single joint moving from initial angle $q_0 = 20°$ to final angle $q_f = 80°$ in $T = 4$ seconds, assuming zero initial and final velocity. Compute the joint's position, velocity, and acceleration at $t = 2$ seconds. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the architecture of a PID-based motion controller for a robot joint. State the role of each of the three terms and discuss what happens to system response if the derivative gain $K_d$ is set to zero. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a)

**Step 1 — Use the cubic polynomial formula derived earlier:**

$$s(t) = q_0 + \dfrac{3(q_f - q_0)}{T^2} t^2 - \dfrac{2(q_f - q_0)}{T^3} t^3$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$q_0 = 20°, \quad q_f = 80°, \quad T = 4 \text{ s}, \quad \Delta q = q_f - q_0 = 60°$$

$$s(t) = 20 + \dfrac{3 \times 60}{16} t^2 - \dfrac{2 \times 60}{64} t^3$$

$$s(t) = 20 + 11.25\, t^2 - 1.875\, t^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute position at $t = 2$ s:**

$$s(2) = 20 + 11.25 \times 4 - 1.875 \times 8 = 20 + 45 - 15 = 50°$$

**Step 4 — Compute velocity at $t = 2$ s:**

$$v(t) = \dot{s}(t) = 22.5\, t - 5.625\, t^2$$

$$v(2) = 22.5 \times 2 - 5.625 \times 4 = 45 - 22.5 = 22.5°/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute acceleration at $t = 2$ s:**

$$a(t) = \ddot{s}(t) = 22.5 - 11.25\, t$$

$$a(2) = 22.5 - 22.5 = 0°/\text{s}^2$$

**Result Table:**

| Time $t$ (s) | Position $s(t)$ (°) | Velocity $v(t)$ (°/s) | Acceleration $a(t)$ (°/s²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 20.00 | 0.00 | 22.50 |
| 1 | 29.38 | 16.88 | 11.25 |
| **2** | **50.00** | **22.50** | **0.00** |
| 3 | 70.63 | 16.88 | -11.25 |
| 4 | 80.00 | 0.00 | -22.50 |

Note: At $t = T/2$, the velocity peaks and acceleration is zero, confirming the cubic's mid-point cruise behavior.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b)

The **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller** computes a control signal as a weighted sum of three error-based terms:

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \cdot \dot{e}(t)$$

where $e(t) = q_{ref}(t) - q_{act}(t)$ is the tracking error.

**Role of Each Term:**

1. **Proportional Term ($K_p e$):** Provides an instantaneous corrective action proportional to the current error. Larger $K_p$ → faster response, but excessive $K_p$ causes overshoot and oscillation.

2. **Integral Term ($K_i \int e\, dt$):** Accumulates past errors and eliminates steady-state error. Without it, a constant disturbance would leave a permanent positional offset.

3. **Derivative Term ($K_d \dot{e}$):** Acts on the *rate of change* of error, providing a damping/braking action as the error approaches zero. It anticipates future error and reduces overshoot.

**Effect of Setting $K_d = 0$:**
- The system becomes a **PI controller**.
- The damping effect vanishes → the system becomes underdamped.
- Symptoms: large overshoot, sustained oscillations, ringing, and possible instability if $K_p$ and $K_i$ are aggressive.
- Settling time increases and mechanical stress on the joint rises.
- In extreme cases, the joint may overshoot the target, reverse, overshoot again, and never settle — a textbook unstable second-order response.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** In Q-A(a), many students forget to convert the polynomial coefficients $a_2, a_3$ into actual *velocity* and *acceleration* by differentiation. Marks are awarded for $\dot{s}(t)$ and $\ddot{s}(t)$ separately. A common error is to report $a_2 = 11.25$ as the "acceleration" — it is not; the actual acceleration at any time is $\ddot{s}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t$.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** A single-axis robot joint must move through a distance $D = 1.5$ m. The maximum allowable velocity is $v_{max} = 1.0$ m/s and the maximum allowable acceleration is $a_{max} = 2.0$ m/s². Determine whether the motion uses a trapezoidal or triangular velocity profile, and compute the total motion time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the concept of feedforward control in robotic motion control. How does it complement feedback (PID) control, and what happens if the inverse dynamic model used for feedforward is inaccurate? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a)

**Step 1 — Check if the cruise phase is reached.**

The peak velocity achievable over distance $D$ by pure triangular motion is:

$$v_{peak} = \sqrt{a_{max} \cdot D} = \sqrt{2.0 \times 1.5} = \sqrt{3.0} \approx 1.732 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with $v_{max}$.**

$$v_{peak} = 1.732 \text{ m/s} \; > \; v_{max} = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$$

Since $v_{peak} > v_{max}$, the joint **will reach the maximum velocity** before decelerating. Therefore, the profile is **TRAPEZOIDAL**.

**Step 3 — Compute acceleration and deceleration times.**

$$T_{accel} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{a_{max}} = \dfrac{1.0}{2.0} = 0.5 \text{ s}$$

By symmetry, $T_{decel} = 0.5$ s.

**Step 4 — Compute the distance covered during the acceleration and deceleration phases.**

$$D_{accel} = \dfrac{1}{2} a_{max} T_{accel}^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 2.0 \times 0.25 = 0.25 \text{ m}$$

$$D_{decel} = 0.25 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the cruise distance and time.**

$$D_{cruise} = D - D_{accel} - D_{decel} = 1.5 - 0.25 - 0.25 = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

$$T_{cruise} = \dfrac{D_{cruise}}{v_{max}} = \dfrac{1.0}{1.0} = 1.0 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 — Compute total motion time.**

$$T_{total} = T_{accel} + T_{cruise} + T_{decel} = 0.5 + 1.0 + 0.5 = 2.0 \text{ s}$$

**Verification using the closed-form expression:**

$$T_{total} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{a_{max}} + \dfrac{D}{v_{max}} = \dfrac{1.0}{2.0} + \dfrac{1.5}{1.0} = 0.5 + 1.5 = 2.0 \text{ s} \quad \checkmark$$

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b)

**Feedforward Control Concept:**
Feedforward control is an **open-loop** strategy that uses a *mathematical model* of the robot's dynamics to pre-compute the actuator command $\tau_{ff}$ that *should* ideally produce the desired trajectory $q_{ref}(t), \dot{q}_{ref}(t), \ddot{q}_{ref}(t)$.

For a robotic manipulator, the inverse dynamics model is:

$$\tau_{ff} = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$

where:
- $M(q)$ is the inertia matrix,
- $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ represents Coriolis and centrifugal terms,
- $G(q)$ is the gravitational torque vector.

**Complementarity with Feedback (PID):**
- **Feedforward** provides the *bulk* of the torque to follow the trajectory, drastically reducing the burden on the feedback loop.
- **Feedback (PID)** then corrects the *small residual errors* caused by model imperfections, unmodelled dynamics (friction, payload variation, disturbances).
- This combination is called **Feedback-Feedforward Control**:

$$\tau_{total} = \tau_{ff} + \tau_{fb} = \tau_{ff} + \left[ K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e} \right]$$

- The advantages are: lower tracking error, faster response, and the feedback gains can be reduced (improving robustness).

**Effect of Inaccurate Inverse Dynamic Model:**
- The feedforward term will be incorrect → the system will rely more heavily on the feedback term.
- For *small* model errors, PID can compensate, but with degraded tracking and possible oscillations.
- For *large* errors, the feedback term may saturate the actuator, leading to instability or limit cycling.
- In the worst case, the controller may fail to track the trajectory at all.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** In Q-B(a), a very common error is to *always* assume a trapezoidal profile. Students must compute $v_{peak} = \sqrt{a_{max} D}$ and compare with $v_{max}$ first. If $v_{peak} < v_{max}$, the profile is **triangular**, and the formula for total time becomes $T_{total} = 2\sqrt{D/a_{max}}$. Failure to perform this check costs 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Motion Control ≠ Path Planning.** Path planning is geometric (where); motion control is dynamic (how and when).
- **PTP vs CP:** PTP = endpoint-only, CP = full-path enforcement.
- **Cubic polynomial** has 4 coefficients; satisfies position + velocity boundary conditions. Use when initial/final velocity = 0.
- **Quintic polynomial** has 6 coefficients; allows control of initial/final acceleration as well. Use for blending multi-segment trajectories.
- **Trapezoidal profile** is the workhorse of industrial robotics; check $v_{peak} = \sqrt{a_{max} D}$ vs $v_{max}$ to decide triangular vs trapezoidal.
- **S-curve profile** adds a jerk-limiting phase; preferred for high-speed, delicate-payload, or passenger-carrying robots.
- **PID components:** $K_p$ (speed), $K_i$ (steady-state error elimination), $K_d$ (damping).
- **Feedforward + Feedback** is the gold-standard architecture: feedforward handles nominal dynamics, feedback handles disturbances.
- **Computed Torque Control** linearizes the nonlinear robot dynamics by using the full inverse model.
- **Control loop frequency** for industrial manipulators: 1–10 kHz. Slower loops lead to instability.
- **Tracking error** $e(t) = q_{ref}(t) - q_{act}(t)$ — must converge to zero as $t \to \infty$ for a stable controller.
- **Units to remember:** rad, rad/s, rad/s², rad/s³, Nm, Hz.
- **Jerk** is the time-derivative of acceleration; minimum-jerk trajectories are smoothest and biomimetically natural.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perception: Sensors for Perception - Schematic Representation of the Sensing Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Perception: Sensors for Perception - Schematic Representation of the Sensing Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Perception: Sensors for Perception

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robot Navigation**, **Perception** is the cognitive and computational process by which an autonomous agent acquires, interprets, and models meaningful information about its physical surroundings and its own internal state, using data captured from the environment. It is the functional bridge between the *physical world* and the *symbolic/planning world* of the robot.

A **Sensor** is a physical transducer that converts a measurable physical phenomenon (light intensity, acoustic pressure, electromagnetic radiation, mechanical contact) into an electrical signal that can be sampled, digitized, and processed by the robot's computational hardware.

A **Sensing Unit** (or **Sensor Suite / Perception Stack**) is the integrated, modular assembly of hardware transducers, signal-conditioning electronics, embedded firmware, and high-level processing algorithms that collectively form a robot's perceptual front-end.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology (Verbatim):**
> *Perception: Sensors for Perception — Classification of sensors (active/passive, contact/non-contact, proprioceptive/exteroceptive); schematic representation of the sensing unit; sensor characteristics: range, resolution, accuracy, precision, bandwidth, latency, update rate; sensor models.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **blindfolded human being trying to walk through a busy marketplace**. The blindfold represents the *absence of sensors*. To navigate, the human would rely on:

| Sense | Robot Equivalent | Real-world Stimulus |
|---|---|---|
| 👂 Hearing | Microphone / Sonar / Ultrasonic | Acoustic waves |
| 👁️ Sight | Camera / LiDAR | Light / EM radiation |
| ✋ Touch | Tactile / Bumper switch | Mechanical contact |
| 🦵 Proprioception | IMU / Encoders / Gyroscope | Internal body state |
| 👃 Smell | Gas / Chemical sensors | Chemical concentration |

A **sensing unit** in a robot is exactly this — the *electronic eyes, ears, and skin* of the machine. The **schematic representation** is the *wiring diagram* of these senses: showing which transducer connects to which signal conditioner, which goes to the ADC, and which ultimately reaches the central processing unit (CPU/NPU/GPU).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Perception the First Step in Navigation?**
> The classical robot navigation pipeline is the **Sense → Plan → Act** loop. Without accurate perception, planning becomes guesswork, and actuation becomes unsafe. A famous industry quote: *"You cannot navigate what you cannot perceive."*

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in Bold)

The following standard physical constants are foundational to almost every sensor equation in this module:

- Speed of light in vacuum: **c = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Speed of sound in air at 20 °C: **v_sound ≈ 343 m/s**
- Standard gravitational acceleration: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Standard magnetic field of Earth: **B_Earth ≈ 25 to 65 µT** (varies by latitude)
- Standard atmospheric pressure at sea level: **P₀ = 101.325 kPa**

**Standard sensor performance metrics (recurring in KTU questions):**

- **Range (R)** — maximum and minimum measurable distance, expressed in metres (m) or metres/second (m/s).
- **Resolution (δ)** — smallest detectable increment of the measured quantity.
- **Accuracy (α)** — closeness of measurement to the *true* value.
- **Precision (π)** — repeatability of measurement under identical conditions.
- **Bandwidth (BW)** — frequency range over which the sensor responds reliably, in **Hz**.
- **Latency (τ_L)** — time delay between physical stimulus and digitized output, in **ms**.
- **Update Rate (f_u)** — number of measurements produced per second, in **Hz**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Sensors

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires a **multi-axis classification** of sensors. A single sensor (e.g., a camera) can belong to multiple categories simultaneously.

### 2.1.1 Axis 1 — Energy Source
- **Active sensors**: emit their own energy (e.g., LiDAR, Radar, Sonar, IR time-of-flight).
- **Passive sensors**: rely on ambient energy (e.g., monocular camera, microphone, thermocouple, accelerometer as gravito-inertial sensor).

### 2.1.2 Axis 2 — Physical Contact
- **Contact sensors**: require physical touch (e.g., bumpers, tactile skin, limit switches, force/torque sensors at joints).
- **Non-contact sensors**: sense at a distance (e.g., cameras, ultrasonic, LiDAR, capacitive proximity).

### 2.1.3 Axis 3 — Reference Frame
- **Proprioceptive sensors**: measure the robot's *internal* state (wheel odometry, IMU, joint encoders, gyro, current sensors in motors).
- **Exteroceptive sensors**: measure the *external* environment (cameras, LiDAR, GPS, ultrasonic, radar, sonar).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU high-yield distinction:** A *wheel encoder* is **proprioceptive** (it measures wheel rotation, not the world). A *GPS* is **exteroceptive** (it measures the world position). A *depth camera* is **exteroceptive**. An *IMU* is **proprioceptive** (it measures the body's inertial state, not the world).

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Major Sensor Modalities

### 2.2.1 Vision Sensors (Cameras)

A camera is essentially a **2-D photometry sampler**. Its mathematical model is the **pinhole camera model**, derived in detail in Section 3.

- **Monochrome / RGB**: 1- or 3-channel intensity map.
- **Stereo**: two cameras with known baseline, mimics biological binocular vision.
- **RGB-D**: RGB + per-pixel depth (e.g., Microsoft Kinect, Intel RealSense, Orbbec Astra).
- **Event camera (DVS)**: asynchronous, triggered by pixel-level brightness change (µs latency).

### 2.2.2 Ranging Sensors

These produce direct or indirect measurements of geometric distance.

- **Time-of-Flight (ToF) LiDAR**: emits a short laser pulse, measures round-trip time. Range = (c·τ) ⁄ 2.
- **Phase-shift LiDAR**: emits continuous modulated wave, measures phase difference between emitted and received.
- **Ultrasonic / Sonar**: emits a 40 kHz acoustic burst, measures echo time. Range = (v_sound·τ) ⁄ 2.
- **Structured light**: projects a known IR pattern, observes distortion to triangulate depth.

### 2.2.3 Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)

A 6-DoF IMU integrates three **MEMS accelerometers** (measuring specific force **a**) and three **MEMS gyroscopes** (measuring angular velocity **ω**). Often supplemented by a 3-axis **magnetometer** (measuring Earth's magnetic field) to form a 9-DoF MARG sensor array.

### 2.2.4 Satellite Navigation (GNSS / GPS)

Provides absolute position in a global geodetic frame (WGS-84). Accuracy is **±2 m** for civilian L1 C/A code, **sub-cm** for RTK-GPS.

### 2.2.5 Wheel Odometry

Proprioceptive sensors (encoders) on each drive wheel integrate wheel rotation to estimate pose. Subject to systematic drift on wheel slip.

## 2.3 The Schematic Representation of a Sensing Unit

A **schematic** of a sensing unit is a **block-level functional architecture** showing the signal/data flow from the physical world to the robot's central processor. The standard layered representation is:

1. **Physical Layer (Transducer array)** — the actual sensor hardware.
2. **Signal Conditioning Layer** — amplifiers, filters, multiplexers.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) Layer** — samples analog signals into discrete digital values.
4. **Driver / Firmware Layer** — embedded software (e.g., ROS drivers, I²C/SPI/CAN protocols).
5. **Perception / Processing Layer** — feature extraction, SLAM, object detection.
6. **Application Layer** — high-level navigation, planning, control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The schematic abstraction allows engineers to *decouple* hardware and software concerns. A ROS 2 driver for a Velodyne LiDAR can be swapped with one for a Hesai LiDAR without changing the perception algorithm, because the schematic interface (point cloud topic) remains the same.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LiDAR range | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$ | c = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s, τ = round-trip time (s) |
| 2 | Sonar range | $R = \dfrac{v_{sound} \cdot \tau}{2}$ | v_sound ≈ 343 m/s in air at 20 °C |
| 3 | Pinhole projection (image plane) | $u = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}$ | f = focal length (m), X, Z in metres |
| 4 | Pinhole projection (full) | $u = f_x \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z} + c_x$ | f_x in pixels, c_x = principal point (pixels) |
| 5 | Depth from disparity | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | B = stereo baseline (m), d = disparity (pixels) |
| 6 | IMU specific force | $\mathbf{a}_{measured} = \mathbf{a}_{true} - \mathbf{g}$ | g = 9.81 m/s² along local up |
| 7 | Gyro integration (orientation) | $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \displaystyle\int_0^t \omega(\tau)\, d\tau$ | θ in rad, ω in rad/s |
| 8 | Accelerometer double integration | $p(t) = p_0 + v_0 t + \displaystyle\int_0^t\!\!\int_0^\tau a(u)\, du\, d\tau$ | p in m, a in m/s² |
| 9 | Sensor accuracy | $\alpha = \vert m - t \vert$ | m = measured, t = true value |
| 10 | Sensor precision | $\pi = \sigma_{repeated}$ | σ = standard deviation of repeated measurements |
| 11 | Range resolution | $\delta R = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot BW}$ | BW = signal bandwidth (Hz) |
| 12 | Angular resolution (LiDAR) | $\delta\theta = \dfrac{2\pi}{N_{beams}}$ | N_beams = number of beams per revolution |
| 13 | Field of View (FOV) | $\text{FOV} = 2\arctan\left(\dfrac{d}{2f}\right)$ | d = sensor dimension, f = focal length |
| 14 | Odometric update (differential) | $\Delta s = \dfrac{\Delta\phi_r + \Delta\phi_l}{2} \cdot r$ | r = wheel radius, Δϕ = rotation increment |
| 15 | SNR | $\text{SNR} = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | dB |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time-of-Flight Range Equation (LiDAR / Radar / Sonar)

A pulse of energy is emitted at time **t = 0**, travels to a target at distance **R**, reflects, and returns to the receiver. The total round-trip distance is **2R**, and the round-trip time is **τ**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speed} &= \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Time}} \\[4pt]
c &= \frac{2R}{\tau} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad R &= \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2} \quad \text{(LiDAR / Radar in vacuum/air)} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad R &= \frac{v_{sound} \cdot \tau}{2} \quad \text{(Sonar in air)} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad R &= \frac{v_{sound} \cdot \tau}{2} \quad \text{(Sonar in water, where } v_{sound} \approx 1480 \text{ m/s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion-logic commentary:** *Line 1 writes the kinematic definition. Line 2 substitutes distance = 2R because the wave must travel to the target AND back. Line 3 isolates R algebraically. Lines 4–5 simply replace c with v_sound when the medium is air or water.*

### 3.1.1 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem.** A scanning LiDAR emits a 905 nm laser pulse toward a wall. The receiver detects the echo after **τ = 66.7 ns**. Compute the distance to the wall.

**Solution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{(2.998 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}) \cdot (66.7 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s})}{2} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{2.998 \times 10^{8} \cdot 6.67 \times 10^{-8}}{2} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{19.997 \approx 20.00}{2} \\[4pt]
  &= 10.00 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion-logic commentary:** *We convert 66.7 ns to scientific notation, multiply c × τ to get the round-trip distance, then halve to obtain the one-way range.*

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pinhole Camera Projection Model

Consider a 3-D world point **P = (X, Y, Z)** projected through a pinhole at the origin onto a 2-D image plane at distance **f** (focal length) along the **Z-axis**. The image-plane point is **p = (x, y)**.

By **similar triangles** (since the light ray from **P** passes through the pinhole in a straight line):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \theta_x &= \frac{x}{f} = \frac{X}{Z} \quad \text{(angle subtended in X-direction)} \\[4pt]
\tan \theta_y &= \frac{y}{f} = \frac{Y}{Z} \quad \text{(angle subtended in Y-direction)} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad x &= f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} \quad ; \quad y = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion-logic commentary:** *The two right triangles share the vertex at the pinhole; one has legs (f, x), the other has legs (Z, X). They are similar, so the ratios of corresponding legs are equal.*

In **pixel coordinates** (with principal point **c_x, c_y** in pixels and focal lengths expressed in pixels **f_x, f_y**):

$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= f_x \cdot \frac{X}{Z} + c_x \\[4pt]
v &= f_y \cdot \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y
\end{aligned}
$$

In **homogeneous matrix form**, this is the canonical 3×4 camera matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &\sim
\begin{bmatrix}
f_x & 0 & c_x & 0 \\
0 & f_y & c_y & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

where the symbol **∼** denotes projective equality (i.e., the two vectors are equal up to a non-zero scalar).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Depth from Stereo Disparity

Two cameras, separated by a baseline **B**, view the same 3-D point **P** at depth **Z**. Because of the baseline, the point projects to pixel column **u_L** in the left image and **u_R** in the right image. The **disparity** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d = u_L - u_R
\end{aligned}
$$

By similar triangles (left camera baseline, then ray geometry):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{B}{Z} &= \frac{B - (u_R \cdot Z/f) + (u_L \cdot Z/f)}{Z} \quad \text{(after algebra)} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{B}{Z} &= \frac{d}{f} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad Z &= \frac{B \cdot f}{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion-logic commentary:** *The key geometric insight: disparity is inversely proportional to depth. A point at infinity has zero disparity; a point on the camera has infinite disparity.*

### 3.3.1 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem.** A stereo camera has focal length **f = 800 px** and baseline **B = 0.12 m**. A feature is observed with disparity **d = 16 px**. Compute depth.

**Solution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{B \cdot f}{d} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{0.12 \text{ m} \cdot 800 \text{ px}}{16 \text{ px}} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{96}{16} \\[4pt]
  &= 6.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion-logic commentary:** *Pixel units cancel; we are left with metres.*

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Range Resolution from Signal Bandwidth

For a pulsed ranging system, two targets can be distinguished only if their echoes arrive at non-overlapping times. The minimum resolvable time difference is the **pulse width** **T_p**, related to bandwidth by **BW ≈ 1 / T_p**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta R &= \frac{c \cdot T_p}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot BW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion-logic commentary:** *T_p is the inverse of BW for a baseband pulse. Halving the time gives the one-way distance, hence the factor of 2.*

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — IMU Integration Chain (Orientation → Position)

A gyroscope outputs **angular velocity** **ω(t)** in rad/s. Orientation is the integral:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta(t) &= \theta_0 + \int_0^t \omega(\tau)\, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

An accelerometer outputs **specific force** **f(t) = a(t) − g**. To obtain acceleration in the inertial frame, we must rotate by the orientation **R(t)** derived above:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{a}_{inertial}(t) &= \mathbf{R}(t) \cdot \mathbf{f}(t) + \mathbf{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

Velocity and position are obtained by successive integration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{v}(t) &= \mathbf{v}_0 + \int_0^t \mathbf{a}_{inertial}(\tau)\, d\tau \\[4pt]
\mathbf{p}(t) &= \mathbf{p}_0 + \int_0^t \mathbf{v}(\tau)\, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion-logic commentary:** *Each integration accumulates a small error, hence IMU-only navigation drifts quadratically with time (acc → vel drift grows linearly, pos drift grows quadratically). This is why IMU is fused with GPS / vision / wheel odometry in an Extended Kalman Filter.*

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Trade-offs

| Sensor | Range | Resolution | Accuracy | Update Rate | Latency | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Camera (mono) | 0.5 – ∞ m | ~0.1 Mpx | Context-dep. | 30 – 60 Hz | 33 – 100 ms | Rich semantics, low cost | No direct depth, poor in dark |
| Stereo Camera | 0.5 – 20 m | Sub-cm @ 1 m | ~1 % @ 5 m | 30 Hz | 33 ms | True depth, passive | Fails on textureless surfaces |
| LiDAR (ToF) | 0.1 – 200 m | 0.1 – 1 cm | ±2 cm | 10 – 20 Hz | 50 ms | 360° geometric map | Expensive, sparse at distance |
| Ultrasonic | 0.02 – 10 m | ~1 cm | ±1 cm | 20 – 40 Hz | 25 ms | Cheap, robust in dust | Narrow cone, specular reflection |
| Radar | 0.1 – 250 m | ~10 cm | ±0.1 m | 20 Hz | 50 ms | Works in fog, rain, dust | Low resolution, clutter |
| IMU (6-DoF) | N/A | 0.001 m/s² | Drift ~1°/min | 100 – 1000 Hz | 1 – 5 ms | High rate, no external ref. | Drift over time |
| Wheel Odometry | N/A | 0.1 mm | Drift ~1 %/m | 100 Hz | 1 ms | Very high rate, local accuracy | Slip, drift |
| GPS (L1) | Global | N/A | ±2 m | 1 – 10 Hz | 100 ms | Absolute global pose | Useless indoors, urban canyon |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

The Mermaid diagrams below use:
- **Alphanumeric node IDs only** (e.g., `node1`, `transducerA`).
- **No reserved keywords** (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) as node names.
- **Plain uppercase text inside quoted labels** — no bold, italics, or HTML.

## 4.2 Block-Level Schematic — Generic Sensing Unit (Sense → Process → Output)

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    tr1 --> wave1[ENERGY WAVE LIGHT SOUND EM]

    tgt --> wave2[REFLECTED OR SCATTERED WAVE]
    wave2 --> tr2[TRANSDUCER 2 PASSIVE RECEIVER]

    adc --> fw[FIRMWARE AND DRIVER LAYER ROS NODE]
    fw --> proc[PERCEPTION PROCESSING FEATURE EXTRACTION SLAM DETECTION]
    proc --> out[DIGITAL OUTPUT POINT CLOUD IMAGE POSE ESTIMATE]
    out --> nav[CENTRAL NAVIGATION PLANNER]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    end

    lid --> eth
    son --> amp
    imu --> adc
    enc --> can
    gps --> can
    amp --> mux
    mux --> adc
    adc --> feat
    eth --> slam
    can --> fus
    feat --> slam
    slam --> fus
    fus --> obj
    obj --> plan
    plan --> ctl
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Schematic — Active vs Passive Sensor Signal Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p[POWER SUPPLY] --> e[EMITTER TRANSDUCER]
    e --> emw[EMITTED WAVE]
    emw --> w[ENVIRONMENT]
    w --> rew[RETURNING ECHO]
    rew --> r[RECEIVER TRANSDUCER]
    r --> a[AMPLIFIER]
    a --> f[FILTER]
    f --> d[ADC]
    d --> s[SIGNAL PROCESSOR]

    amb[AMBIENT ENERGY SUN LAMP NOISE] --> r2[RECEIVER TRANSDUCER PASSIVE]
    r2 --> a2[AMPLIFIER]
    a2 --> f2[FILTER]
    f2 --> d2[ADC]
    d2 --> s2[SIGNAL PROCESSOR]
    s2 --> o2[DIGITAL OUTPUT]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Schematic — Camera Sensing Unit

```mermaid

    lens --> iris[IRIS AND SHUTTER]

    tmg --> isp[IMAGE SIGNAL PROCESSOR ISP]
    isp --> demosaic[DEMOSAICING]
```
