
> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical note:** The above schematics are *generic* (not vendor-specific). For KTU descriptive answers, draw the schematic in your own hand on the answer booklet — examiners reward a clean, labeled block diagram even if the connection arrows are simple.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between active and passive sensors with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Active sensors** generate their own energy stimulus and measure the reflection from the environment. They are independent of ambient conditions. *Examples:* LiDAR (emits laser pulse), Ultrasonic sonar (emits 40 kHz acoustic burst), Radar (emits RF chirp).
- **Passive sensors** do not emit energy; they sense ambient energy (light, heat, sound, gravity). *Examples:* Monocular camera (senses ambient light), microphone (senses ambient sound), accelerometer (senses gravity + motion).
- **Key distinction:** active sensors can measure distance/range directly; passive sensors usually require triangulation or scene models. Active sensors consume more power; passive sensors are stealthier.
*(1 mark per bullet: definition + 2 examples + distinction ≈ 3 marks)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the terms (i) accuracy, (ii) precision, and (iii) resolution of a sensor. State the SI unit for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(i) Accuracy (α):** closeness of a measurement to the *true* value, expressed as an absolute error **α = |m − t|**. Unit: same as the measurand (m, m/s, °C, etc.).
- **(ii) Precision (π):** repeatability of a sensor under identical conditions, expressed as the **standard deviation σ** of repeated measurements. Unit: same as the measurand.
- **(iii) Resolution (δ):** the *smallest increment* the sensor can detect, i.e., the granularity of its output. Unit: same as the measurand.
- High accuracy does **not** imply high precision and vice versa — a sensor can be precise but biased (high π, low α) or accurate on average but noisy (low π, high α).
*(1 mark per term + 0.5 mark for example contrast ≈ 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In sensor classification questions, students frequently **omit one axis** (e.g., they classify by energy source but forget proprioceptive vs exteroceptive). KTU rubric deducts 1–2 marks for incomplete classification.
> 2. In derivation questions (range, pinhole, stereo), students often **skip the “similar triangles” justification** and write the formula directly. Examiners award *method marks* — the geometric setup is worth 2 marks; the algebra is worth 2 marks; the final numerical answer is worth the rest.
> 3. Always include **units** in your final numerical answer. A correctly computed "10" without "metres" loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. Block diagrams must be **labelled with arrows**. A wall of text with no diagram in a 14-mark question scores a maximum of 8–9 marks.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, adapted]*

**(a)** Classify the major sensors used in mobile robot perception along three axes: (i) active/passive, (ii) contact/non-contact, (iii) proprioceptive/exteroceptive. Populate a 5-row × 4-column table with five sensors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the schematic representation of a generic sensing unit. Describe the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification Table (7 marks — 1 mark per row, 2 marks for completeness/justification)**

| Sensor | Active / Passive | Contact / Non-contact | Proprioceptive / Exteroceptive |
|---|---|---|---|
| Camera (mono) | Passive | Non-contact | Exteroceptive |
| LiDAR | Active | Non-contact | Exteroceptive |
| Ultrasonic Sonar | Active | Non-contact | Exteroceptive |
| IMU | Passive (uses gravity) | Non-contact | Proprioceptive |
| Wheel Encoder | Passive | Contact | Proprioceptive |
| Bumper Switch | Passive | Contact | Exteroceptive (binary obstacle flag) |

*Justification (2 marks):* The IMU is passive because it senses the gravity vector and Coriolis forces rather than emitting energy; the encoder is contact because it requires mechanical rotation of the wheel; the bumper is exteroceptive because it indicates the *external* world state (collision) even though it senses a contact event on the robot body.

**(b) Schematic & Roles (7 marks)**

Refer to the schematic in Section 4.2. Describe each block in 1–2 lines:

- **Physical Layer (Transducer):** converts physical energy to electrical signals.
- **Signal Conditioning:** amplifies weak signals, filters noise (low-pass/band-pass), matches impedance.
- **ADC:** samples and quantizes the analog signal into digital code.
- **Driver/Firmware:** provides a software abstraction (e.g., ROS topic, I²C register interface).
- **Perception Processing:** runs algorithms (SLAM, object detection, point-cloud segmentation).
- **Application Output:** publishes structured data (point cloud, image, odometry) to the navigation stack.

*(Block diagram = 3 marks; description of 4–6 blocks = 4 marks; total 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, adapted]*

**(a)** Derive the time-of-flight range equation for a pulsed LiDAR. A scanning LiDAR measures an echo return time of **τ = 134 ns** from a target. Compute the distance. Take **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the depth-from-stereo equation **Z = Bf/d** using similar triangles. A stereo rig has **B = 0.20 m**, **f = 600 px**, and observes disparity **d = 12 px**. Compute the depth and comment on how the depth error scales with disparity error. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) ToF Derivation + Numerical (7 marks)**

- Geometric setup with diagram: 1 mark.
- Write speed = distance / time: $c = 2R/\tau$: 1 mark.
- Rearrange: $R = c\tau/2$: 1 mark.
- Substitute c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s, τ = 134 × 10⁻⁹ s: 1 mark.
- Multiply: $R = (3 \times 10^8 \times 134 \times 10^{-9}) / 2$: 1 mark.
- Final numerical answer with unit: 1 mark.
- One-line comment on the 1/2 factor (round-trip): 1 mark.

**Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \cdot 134 \times 10^{-9}}{2} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{40.2}{2} \\[4pt]
  &= 20.1 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Stereo Derivation + Numerical (7 marks)**

- **Setup:** two cameras separated by **B**, focal length **f**, point **P** at depth **Z**, projects to disparity **d = u_L − u_R**. (1 mark)
- **Similar-triangle relationship:** $\dfrac{B}{Z} = \dfrac{d}{f}$. (2 marks — 1 for the equation, 1 for the geometric reasoning.)
- **Rearrange to obtain $Z = Bf/d$.** (1 mark)
- **Numerical substitution:** $Z = (0.20 \times 600)/12 = 120/12 = 10$ m. (1 mark)
- **Error analysis:** Differentiate, $\delta Z = -(Bf/d^2)\cdot \delta d = -(Z/d)\cdot \delta d$. Hence **|δZ| scales as 1/d²** — at far range (small d) a 1-pixel disparity error produces a large depth error. (2 marks)

> **Examiner's extra credit (1 mark if asked):** Comment on the **inverse-square law of stereo uncertainty** — this is why stereo cameras are excellent for close-range (< 10 m) but unreliable beyond 20–30 m.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Perception: Sensors**

- [x] **Perception** is the sense-plan-act front-end; it converts physical stimuli into structured digital data.
- [x] Sensors are classified along **three orthogonal axes**: energy source (active/passive), contact (contact/non-contact), reference frame (proprioceptive/exteroceptive).
- [x] **Active sensors** emit energy (LiDAR, Radar, Sonar, IR-ToF). **Passive sensors** sense ambient energy (camera, microphone, IMU).
- [x] **Proprioceptive** = internal state (IMU, encoders, gyro). **Exteroceptive** = external world (camera, LiDAR, GPS).
- [x] **Sensing unit schematic** has 6 layers: Transducer → Signal Conditioning → ADC → Driver → Perception Processing → Application Output.
- [x] **ToF range equation:** $R = c\tau/2$ (LiDAR/Radar), $R = v_{sound}\tau/2$ (Sonar).
- [x] **Pinhole projection:** $u = f_x (X/Z) + c_x$, $v = f_y (Y/Z) + c_y$.
- [x] **Stereo depth:** $Z = Bf/d$. Disparity is **inversely** proportional to depth.
- [x] **Range resolution:** $\delta R = c / (2 \cdot BW)$.
- [x] **IMU integration chain:** $\omega \rightarrow \theta$ (1×) $\rightarrow a \rightarrow v$ (2×) $\rightarrow p$ (3×). Drift grows with each integration.
- [x] **Accuracy** ≠ **Precision**: bias vs. repeatability.
- [x] For 14-mark answers, **always include a labelled block diagram** (3–4 marks reserved for it).
- [x] For derivations, **always begin with a geometric setup or similar-triangle justification** before writing algebra.
- [x] **Always state units** in numerical answers; examiners routinely deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
- [x] Standard metrics to memorise: c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s, v_sound = 343 m/s, g = 9.81 m/s², B_Earth ≈ 50 µT, GPS L1 accuracy ≈ 2 m.
- [x] **LiDAR** is the gold standard for geometric 360° perception; **camera** is the gold standard for semantic perception; **IMU** is the gold standard for high-rate self-motion estimation. Sensor **fusion** of all three is the modern state of the art.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Obstacle Sensor (Bumper)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Obstacle Sensor (Bumper)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🟢 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OBSTACLE SENSOR (BUMPER) — KTU 2024 SYLLABUS DEFINITION**
> A **Bumper Sensor** (also called a *contact sensor*, *whisker sensor*, or *tactile bumper*) is a **binary, contact-type proximity sensor** mounted on the outer chassis of a mobile robot that detects the **physical presence of an obstacle** through **mechanical deformation** or **switch closure** when the robot's body collides with, or makes direct physical contact with, an external object in its operating environment.

In formal KTU/Robotics navigation terminology, a bumper sensor is classified as a **Class-0 Obstacle Detection Transducer** — the lowest-level (and simplest) sensing layer in the **Hierarchical Sensor Stack** of a mobile robot. Unlike *ranging* sensors (ultrasonic, LiDAR, IR), bumper sensors do **not measure distance**; they output a **discrete Boolean state** — either `0` (No Contact / Free Path) or `1` (Contact / Obstacle Detected).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blind Person's Cane 🤚🦯

Imagine a **blind person walking down a busy street holding a white cane**. They cannot see the curb, the pole, or the person in front of them — the cane simply **touches** these objects and the user *feels* the contact through their hand. The cane provides no information about *how far* the object is, only **whether something is there right now**.

A **bumper sensor works identically**:
- The **robot's chassis = the blind person's hand**
- The **bumper/contact whisker = the white cane**
- The **moment of physical contact = the obstacle being "felt"**
- The **electrical switch closure = the brain's recognition of touch**

> [!NOTE]
> **KEY DISTINCTION FROM OTHER SENSORS:**
> Bumper sensors are **passive-trigger** devices. They do NOT continuously emit energy (unlike ultrasonic/IR) and do NOT scan the environment (unlike LiDAR/cameras). They remain *dormant* in a normally-open (NO) or normally-closed (NC) state until **kinetic force is applied** by collision. This makes them the **last line of defense** in a robot's safety stack — a **fail-safe** that triggers only when all other sensors have failed to predict an impending collision.

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Operational Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Voltage (Vcc)** | **5 V DC** or **24 V DC** | Volts |
| **Maximum Contact Force (F_max)** | **0.5 – 5.0** | Newtons (N) |
| **Response Time (t_r)** | **≤ 10** | milliseconds (ms) |
| **Bumper Mechanical Travel (δ)** | **2 – 10** | millimeters (mm) |
| **Switch Bounce Time (t_b)** | **0.1 – 5.0** | ms |
| **Operating Temperature (T_op)** | **−20 °C to +85 °C** | °C |
| **Output Logic Level (HIGH)** | **+Vcc (Logic 1)** | V |
| **Output Logic Level (LOW)** | **0 V / GND (Logic 0)** | V |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | **≥ 100,000** | hours |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 HIGHLIGHT:** Bumper sensors are classified under the broader category of **Internal/Proprioceptive-Extending Sensors** in the KTU Robot Navigation syllabus. They are categorized as **Contact-Type Discrete Binary Transducers** and are mandatory components in any KTU-designated mobile robot platform (e.g., the Pioneer P3-DX, TurtleBot 2, KTU-ARL Autonomous Ground Vehicle reference platform).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔵 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A bumper sensor operates on the **mechanical-electrical transduction principle**. The chain of events is fully deterministic and can be broken down into **six distinct physical stages**:

1. **Stage 1 — Free State (Idle):** The robot is moving through clear space. The bumper plate/lever is held in its rest position by an internal **return spring** (Hooke's Law restoring force). The electrical contacts of the internal microswitch are **open**; the output line reads `Logic 0` (no obstacle).
2. **Stage 2 — Approach Phase:** The robot's trajectory brings its chassis within `0 mm` of an obstacle (i.e., the very moment of contact). At this instant, the bumper experiences a **normal reaction force** `F_n` from the obstacle surface.
3. **Stage 3 — Mechanical Deflection:** The applied force `F_n` overcomes the spring's restoring force `F_s = k \cdot \delta`, where `k` is the spring constant (N/m) and `δ` is the displacement. The bumper plate/lever **physically deflects inward by distance δ**.
4. **Stage 4 — Switch Actuation:** The deflected lever actuates a **microswitch** (SPST — Single Pole Single Throw), causing the internal electrical contacts to **close** (or open, in NC configuration). This completes (or breaks) the circuit.
5. **Stage 5 — Signal Conditioning:** The raw switch output is passed through a **Schmitt trigger** or **debouncing RC filter** to eliminate contact bounce (a phenomenon where the contacts mechanically chatter for 0.1–5 ms before settling).
6. **Stage 6 — Microcontroller Interrupt (ISR):** The conditioned digital signal is routed to a **GPIO pin** or **hardware interrupt line** of the robot's onboard microcontroller (e.g., Arduino Mega, Raspberry Pi Pico, STM32). The MCU executes an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** that triggers an **emergency stop, reverse maneuver, or path-replanning routine**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why a Bumper is the "Last Line of Defense":** Notice that Stages 3–6 happen **only after physical contact has already occurred**. Bumper sensors therefore cannot *prevent* a collision — they can only *react* to one. This is why they are used in conjunction with **forward-looking sensors** (ultrasonic, IR, LiDAR) that provide pre-collision avoidance.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Bumper Sensor — Internal Components

| # | Component | Function | Engineering Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bumper Plate / Whisker** | External impact surface | ABS Plastic / Spring Steel |
| 2 | **Lever Arm** | Transmits force from plate to switch | Aluminum / Delrin |
| 3 | **Return Spring** | Restores plate to neutral position | Music Wire / Stainless Steel |
| 4 | **Microswitch (SPST)** | Converts mechanical motion to electrical signal | Omron / Honeywell sub-miniature |
| 5 | **Debounce Capacitor (C)** | Filters out contact bounce (typically 100 nF) | Ceramic / Film Capacitor |
| 6 | **Pull-up / Pull-down Resistor (R)** | Defines default logic level (typically 10 kΩ) | Carbon Film Resistor |
| 7 | **Mounting Bracket** | Mechanical attachment to chassis | Aluminum / Steel |
| 8 | **Cable Harness** | Routes signal to MCU | Shielded Twisted Pair |

## 2.3 Types of Bumper Sensors

| Type | Mechanism | Typical Application | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Microswitch** | Spring-loaded SPST switch | Industrial AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) | Low |
| **Pneumatic Bumper** | Air pressure change in sealed bladder | Heavy-duty outdoor robots, military UGVs | High |
| **Capacitive Bumper** | Change in capacitance on deflection | Precision indoor robots, research platforms | Medium |
| **Piezoelectric Bumper** | Voltage generated on mechanical impact | High-speed robots, force-sensitive grippers | Medium-High |
| **Optical Bumper** | IR light beam interrupted by deflection | Clean-room robots, medical robots | Medium |
| **Hall-Effect Bumper** | Magnetic field change detected by Hall IC | Automotive robotics, harsh environments | Medium |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Each Term | Units | Applicable Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_s = k \cdot \delta$ | **Spring restoring force**; $k$ = spring constant, $\delta$ = deflection | N, N/m, m | Threshold force calculation |
| 2 | $F_{net} = F_n - F_s$ | **Net actuating force**; $F_n$ = normal reaction force from obstacle | N | Switch trip condition |
| 3 | $F_n \geq k \cdot \delta_{trip}$ | **Switch trip criterion**; $\delta_{trip}$ = displacement needed to close switch | N | Minimum collision force detection |
| 4 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | **Debounce time constant**; $R$ = pull-up resistor, $C$ = filter capacitor | seconds (s) | Debounce filter design |
| 5 | $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | **RC charging curve** of debounce filter during contact | V, s | Transient signal analysis |
| 6 | $P_{switch} = V_{cc} \cdot I_{contact}$ | **Power dissipated in switch contacts** | W | Thermal/safety design |
| 7 | $t_{response} = t_{mech} + t_{debounce} + t_{ISR}$ | **Total system response latency** | s | Real-time safety analysis |
| 8 | $\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | **Reliability**; $\lambda$ = failure rate | hours | Long-term reliability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU RULE — TABLE FORMATTING:**
> Notice that in the table above, **vertical pipe symbols inside equations have been replaced with `\cdot` or `\vert`/`\mid` LaTeX commands**. For example, $\vert V_{out} \vert$ would be written inline to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. **Always follow this convention** in KTU board examination answer sheets when constructing tables.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications 🏭

- **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** in warehouses (Amazon Kiva robots, Fetch Robotics) — bumper triggers immediate halt to prevent damage to goods and shelving.
- **Autonomous Vacuum Cleaners** (iRobot Roomba) — 4–6 bumper sensors cover the front half-shell to detect walls and furniture.
- **Autonomous Wheelchairs** in hospitals — bumper provides safety redundancy alongside ultrasonic arrays.
- **Mars Rovers** (NASA Curiosity, Perseverance) — pop-up bumpers on robotic arms protect against contact with unknown terrain.
- **Industrial Robotic Manipulators** — bumper sensors on end-effectors prevent crushing injuries in collaborative robots (cobots) per **ISO/TS 15066** safety standards.
- **Autonomous Lawn Mowers** (Husqvarna Automower) — bumpers under the shell detect trees, garden furniture, and pets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🟣 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Switch Trip Force Threshold

**Problem Setup:** A mobile robot's front bumper uses a linear return spring with constant $k = 2.0 \text{ N/mm}$. The internal microswitch requires a minimum mechanical displacement of $\delta_{trip} = 3.0 \text{ mm}$ to actuate. Determine the **minimum normal force** $F_{n,min}$ that an obstacle must exert on the bumper to trigger the sensor.

**Step 1 — Identify the equilibrium condition at the moment of switch trip.**

At the precise instant the switch closes, the bumper plate is in **static equilibrium** between the applied obstacle force and the spring's restoring force:

$$F_{n} = F_{s}$$

**Step 2 — Express the spring restoring force using Hooke's Law.**

The spring is linear, so the restoring force is proportional to deflection:

$$F_{s} = k \cdot \delta$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the equilibrium equation.**

$$F_{n} = k \cdot \delta$$

**Step 4 — Apply the trip condition $\delta = \delta_{trip}$.**

At switch trip, deflection equals the trip displacement:

$$F_{n,min} = k \cdot \delta_{trip}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values (with unit conversion: $k = 2.0 \text{ N/mm} = 2000 \text{ N/m}$).**

$$F_{n,min} = (2.0 \text{ N/mm}) \cdot (3.0 \text{ mm})$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate and simplify.**

$$F_{n,min} = 6.0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation.**

> ✅ A collision force of just **6.0 Newtons** (roughly the weight of a 0.6 kg object) is sufficient to trigger the bumper. This is a *very light touch*, meaning the sensor is highly sensitive — appropriate for indoor service robots but may need $k$ to be increased for heavy outdoor UGVs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Debounce Filter Time Constant

**Problem Setup:** A bumper microswitch exhibits a mechanical contact bounce duration of $t_b = 2.0 \text{ ms}$. Design a first-order RC low-pass filter (with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$) to suppress this bounce. Calculate the **filter capacitance** $C$ and the resulting **time delay** introduced into the sensor signal.

**Step 1 — Recall the time constant equation for an RC circuit.**

For a first-order RC low-pass filter, the time constant is:

$$\tau = R \cdot C$$

**Step 2 — Apply the design rule: filter time constant must exceed bounce time.**

To effectively suppress bounce, the filter must "hold" the signal HIGH for longer than the bounce duration. The engineering rule of thumb is:

$$\tau \geq 5 \cdot t_b$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the required capacitance.**

$$\tau = 5 \cdot t_b = 5 \cdot (2.0 \text{ ms}) = 10.0 \text{ ms} = 0.010 \text{ s}$$

Rearranging $\tau = R \cdot C$ for $C$:

$$C = \frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{0.010 \text{ s}}{10{,}000 \text{ }\Omega}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**

$$C = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F} = 1.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the resulting signal propagation delay (time to reach 63.2% of Vcc).**

The voltage across the capacitor as a function of time during charging is:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{cc} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

The time to reach the **Schmitt trigger threshold** (typically $V_{th} = 0.5 \cdot V_{cc}$ for CMOS logic) is:

$$t_{delay} = -\tau \cdot \ln\left(1 - \frac{V_{th}}{V_{cc}}\right)$$

$$t_{delay} = -10.0 \text{ ms} \cdot \ln(1 - 0.5)$$

$$t_{delay} = -10.0 \text{ ms} \cdot \ln(0.5) = -10.0 \text{ ms} \cdot (-0.693)$$

$$t_{delay} \approx 6.93 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.**

> ✅ A $1.0 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ capacitor with a $10 \text{ k}\Omega$ pull-up gives a **6.93 ms debounce delay**. This is a *hardware-only* delay; the total bumper response time is $t_{response} = t_{mech} + t_{debounce} + t_{ISR}$. For high-speed robots ($v > 1 \text{ m/s}$), this delay corresponds to a **travel distance of 6.93 mm** before the controller reacts — within safe bounds for indoor navigation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bumper Sensing Coverage on a Circular Robot

**Problem Setup:** A cylindrical mobile robot of radius $r = 0.25 \text{ m}$ is fitted with `n = 6` equally spaced bumper sensors around its circular chassis. Determine the **arc length covered** by each bumper and the **angular gap (blind zone)** between adjacent bumpers.

**Step 1 — Total angular coverage of the chassis.**

The chassis is a full circle, so the total sensing angle is:

$$\theta_{total} = 2\pi \text{ radians} = 360^{\circ}$$

**Step 2 — Angular coverage per bumper (assuming each bumper covers an equal arc).**

Each bumper covers an arc:

$$\theta_{b} = \frac{\theta_{total}}{n} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{6} = 60^{\circ}$$

**Step 3 — Arc length covered by each bumper (at the chassis radius).**

$$L_{b} = r \cdot \theta_{b} = (0.25 \text{ m}) \cdot \left(\frac{\pi}{3} \text{ rad}\right)$$

$$L_{b} \approx 0.262 \text{ m} = 26.2 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4 — If each bumper has a *physical width* $w = 8 \text{ cm}$, the gap (blind zone) is:**

$$L_{gap} = L_{b} - w = 26.2 \text{ cm} - 8.0 \text{ cm} = 18.2 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5 — The corresponding blind-zone angle is:**

$$\theta_{gap} = \frac{L_{gap}}{r} = \frac{0.182 \text{ m}}{0.25 \text{ m}} = 0.728 \text{ rad} \approx 41.7^{\circ}$$

> ✅ **Engineering implication:** With only 6 bumpers, the robot has **41.7° blind zones** between sensors. For omnidirectional safety, KTU design guidelines recommend **at minimum 8 bumpers** (45° spacing) on circular robots, with **overlapping coverage** of $\geq 5°$ on each side to eliminate blind zones.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bumper vs. Other Obstacle Sensors

| Parameter | **Bumper Sensor** | **Ultrasonic (HC-SR04)** | **Infrared (Sharp GP2Y0A21)** | **LiDAR (RPLIDAR A1)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensing Type** | Contact (tactile) | Ranging (time-of-flight) | Ranging (triangulation) | Ranging (laser scan) |
| **Output** | Boolean (0/1) | Distance (cm) | Distance (cm) | 2D point cloud |
| **Range** | 0 mm (contact only) | 20 – 4000 mm | 100 – 800 mm | 150 – 12000 mm |
| **Angular Coverage** | 1° – 10° per bumper | ~15° cone | ~5° cone | 360° (full) |
| **Response Time** | 1 – 10 ms | 30 – 50 ms | 40 ms | 50 – 100 ms (full scan) |
| **Cost (INR)** | ₹ 50 – 300 | ₹ 100 – 250 | ₹ 400 – 700 | ₹ 7,000 – 25,000 |
| **Detect Soft Objects** | ✅ Yes (pushes them) | ❌ No (absorbs sound) | ❌ No (transparent/reflective fails) | ⚠️ Partial |
| **Detect Transparent** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | ⚠️ Partial |
| **Power Consumption** | < 10 mW | ~30 mW (15 mA) | ~50 mW | ~500 mW (scanning) |
| **Failure Mode Safety** | **Fail-safe (inherently safe)** | Fail-loud (false negatives) | Fail-loud (false negatives) | Fail-loud (false negatives) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU BOARD EXAMINATION TRAP:** Students often claim that "bumpers are outdated and replaced by LiDAR." This is **factually incorrect** for safety-critical applications. Bumpers are **always** present as the **last-resort hardware safety interlock** in ISO-13849 / ISO-3691-4 certified AGVs. In KTU answers, always emphasize the **fail-safe** nature of contact sensors.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🟠 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bumper Sensor System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **end-to-end signal chain** from physical obstacle contact to the robot's motion control loop:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Obstacle in Robot Path] --> B[Physical Impact on Bumper Plate]
    B --> C[Mechanical Deflection of Lever Arm]
    C --> D{Spring Force vs Applied Force}
    D -- F_applied exceeds F_spring --> E[Microswitch Contact Closure]
    D -- F_applied below F_spring --> Z1[Sensor Returns to Idle State - No Trigger]
    E --> F[Raw Switch Signal with Bounce]
    F --> G[RC Debounce Filter - 10kOhm and 1uF]
    G --> H[Schmitt Trigger - Signal Cleanup]
    H --> I[Voltage Level Shifter to 3.3V Logic]
    I --> J[GPIO Pin or Hardware Interrupt on MCU]
    J --> K[Interrupt Service Routine - ISR]
    K --> L[Path Replanning Algorithm]
    L --> M[Motor Controller PWM Update]
    M --> N[Robot Halts and Reverses Maneuver]
    Z1 --> O[Robot Continues Forward Motion]
```

> **Figure 4.1 — Functional Block Diagram: Bumper Sensor Signal Chain**
> This diagram shows the **complete causal chain** from contact to corrective action. Notice the **conditional branch** (`{Spring Force vs Applied Force}`) — this represents the **threshold decision** that distinguishes a real obstacle from noise.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Six-Bumper Configuration on a Circular Robot (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RobotChassis[Top View of Circular Mobile Robot]
        direction LR
        B1[Bumper 1 - Front 0 deg]:::bumper
        B2[Bumper 2 - Front Right 60 deg]:::bumper
        B3[Bumper 3 - Rear Right 120 deg]:::bumper
        B4[Bumper 4 - Rear 180 deg]:::bumper
        B5[Bumper 5 - Rear Left 240 deg]:::bumper
        B6[Bumper 6 - Front Left 300 deg]:::bumper
    end
    MCU[Microcontroller - 6 GPIO Input Pins]:::controller
    B1 --> MCU
    B2 --> MCU
    B3 --> MCU
    B4 --> MCU
    B5 --> MCU
    B6 --> MCU
    classDef bumper fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef controller fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

> **Figure 4.2 — Six-Bumper Hardware Layout**
> This shows the **spatial distribution** of bumper sensors on a circular chassis. Each bumper is wired to a **dedicated GPIO input** on the MCU, allowing the controller to identify **which sector** of the robot has made contact (front, right, rear, left). This spatial information is **critical** for the path-replanning routine to issue a turn-away command in the correct direction.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Machine — Bumper Sensor Operational States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Deflecting : F_applied exceeds F_spring
    Deflecting --> ContactClosed : Switch trips at delta_trip
    ContactClosed --> Debouncing : RC filter charging
    Debouncing --> Triggered : V_out exceeds V_threshold
    Triggered --> EmergencyStop : ISR executed
    EmergencyStop --> Reverse : Reverse maneuver initiated
    Reverse --> Replanning : New path computed
    Replanning --> Idle : Robot resumes navigation
    Deflecting --> Idle : F_applied removed before trip
    Triggered --> Idle : Software reset after obstacle cleared
```

> **Figure 4.3 — Bumper Sensor State Machine**
> This diagram models the bumper as a **finite state machine (FSM)**, which is precisely how the MCU's ISR is typically implemented. Note the **recovery paths** from `Triggered → Idle` (software reset) and `Deflecting → Idle` (false trigger rejection) — these are **essential for robust operation** and are commonly assessed in KTU viva voce questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🔴 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Bumper Sensor. List any four typical applications of bumper sensors in mobile robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** A Bumper Sensor is a **contact-type, binary output obstacle detection transducer** mounted on the outer chassis of a mobile robot. It detects the physical presence of an obstacle through mechanical deformation of an internal spring-loaded lever, which actuates an electrical microswitch. The output is a discrete Boolean signal — Logic 0 (no contact) or Logic 1 (contact).
>
> **Applications (1 mark — any four):**
> 1. Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs) in warehouses
> 2. Autonomous vacuum cleaners (e.g., Roomba)
> 3. Hospital autonomous wheelchairs
> 4. Industrial robotic manipulators (cobot safety)
> 5. Mars rover terrain contact detection

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the term "contact bounce" in mechanical microswitches used in bumper sensors. How is it mitigated?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Contact Bounce (2 marks):** Contact bounce is a **mechanical phenomenon** in which the metallic contacts of a microswitch rapidly open and close (chatter) for a duration of typically **0.1 to 5 milliseconds** after initial closure, before settling into a stable state. This occurs because the contacts possess kinetic energy and spring-like elasticity — on impact, they oscillate several times before coming to rest. If not filtered, the MCU may interpret a single physical contact as **multiple spurious triggers**, causing erratic robot behavior.
>
> **Mitigation (1 mark):** The two standard techniques are: **(a) Hardware debouncing** using an RC low-pass filter (e.g., $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 1 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, $\tau = 10 \text{ ms}$); and **(b) Software debouncing** by ignoring state changes that occur within a defined lockout window (e.g., 20 ms) after the first transition.

---

## 5.2 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **⭐ Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working principle** of a mechanical bumper sensor used in a mobile robot. Clearly identify the role of the return spring, microswitch, and debounce filter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bumper sensor uses a linear spring of stiffness $k = 1500 \text{ N/m}$. The internal microswitch requires a deflection of $\delta_{trip} = 4.0 \text{ mm}$ to actuate. Calculate:
- (i) The **minimum normal force** required to trigger the sensor.
- (ii) The **arc length coverage** of each bumper if 8 bumpers are equally spaced on a robot of chassis radius $r = 0.30 \text{ m}$.
- (iii) The **blind-zone angle** between adjacent bumpers, assuming each bumper has a physical width of $w = 6 \text{ cm}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

The mechanical bumper sensor consists of the following components (refer to Section 4.1 block diagram):
- **Bumper Plate / Whisker** (external ABS plastic impact surface).
- **Lever Arm** (aluminum rod transmitting force from plate to switch).
- **Return Spring** with stiffness $k$ — provides a restoring force $F_s = k \cdot \delta$ that keeps the plate in its neutral rest position.
- **Microswitch (SPST)** — the actual electrical transducer.
- **RC Debounce Filter** — removes contact bounce.
- **Pull-up Resistor** — defines the default idle-state logic level.

**Working principle:** When the robot collides with an obstacle, the **normal reaction force** $F_n$ from the obstacle acts on the bumper plate. This force is transmitted via the lever arm to the spring. As long as $F_n < k \cdot \delta_{trip}$, the spring absorbs the energy and the plate returns to its rest position (no trigger). When $F_n \geq k \cdot \delta_{trip}$, the lever deflects by $\delta_{trip}$ and physically pushes the microswitch contacts closed. The raw bounced signal is filtered by the RC network and Schmitt trigger, then routed to the MCU as a clean Logic HIGH interrupt.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Minimum Trigger Force:**

$$F_{n,min} = k \cdot \delta_{trip} = (1500 \text{ N/m}) \cdot (0.004 \text{ m}) = 6.0 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Arc Length per Bumper:**

$$L_b = \frac{2\pi r}{n} = \frac{2\pi (0.30)}{8} = 0.2356 \text{ m} \approx 23.6 \text{ cm}$$

**(iii) Blind-Zone Angle:**

$$L_{gap} = L_b - w = 23.6 \text{ cm} - 6.0 \text{ cm} = 17.6 \text{ cm}$$

$$\theta_{gap} = \frac{L_{gap}}{r} = \frac{0.176}{0.30} = 0.587 \text{ rad} \approx 33.6^{\circ}$$

> **Final Answers:** $F_{n,min} = 6.0 \text{ N}$, $L_b \approx 23.6 \text{ cm}$, $\theta_{gap} \approx 33.6^{\circ}$.

---

### **⭐ Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Compare and contrast **mechanical, pneumatic, and piezoelectric bumper sensors** with respect to operating principle, sensitivity, response time, and cost. Highlight the engineering trade-offs that govern the choice of bumper type in a given application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robotic AGV moves at a constant velocity of $v = 1.5 \text{ m/s}$. Its front bumper has a debounce filter with $R = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 470 \text{ nF}$. Calculate:
- (i) The **RC time constant** $\tau$.
- (ii) The **propagation delay** $t_{delay}$ for the filtered signal to cross the **Schmitt trigger threshold** at $V_{th} = 0.55 \cdot V_{cc}$.
- (iii) The **distance travelled** by the AGV during this delay. Comment on the safety implications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Mechanical | Pneumatic | Piezoelectric |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Spring + microswitch | Air bladder pressure change | Piezoelectric voltage on impact |
| **Sensitivity** | Moderate (1–10 N) | High (0.1–1 N) | Very High (0.01 N) |
| **Response Time** | 5–10 ms | 20–50 ms (slow pneumatic) | < 1 µs (very fast) |
| **Cost** | Low (₹ 50–500) | High (₹ 5,000–20,000) | Medium (₹ 1,000–5,000) |
| **Durability** | High (10⁶ cycles) | Very High (sealed) | Moderate (crystal fatigue) |
| **Best For** | Indoor AGVs, vacuum robots | Outdoor UGVs, military | Force-feedback research, prosthetics |

**Trade-off analysis:** *Mechanical* sensors are cheapest and most reliable for indoor applications where collision forces are low. *Pneumatic* sensors are preferred for heavy outdoor robots because the sealed bladder is weatherproof and can absorb large impacts without damage. *Piezoelectric* sensors are used in research applications where both impact detection AND force magnitude measurement are required (since piezo voltage $\propto F$).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Time Constant:**

$$\tau = R \cdot C = (4700 \text{ }\Omega) \cdot (470 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F}) = 2.209 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2.21 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) Propagation Delay (using $\frac{V_{th}}{V_{cc}} = 0.55$):**

$$t_{delay} = -\tau \cdot \ln\left(1 - \frac{V_{th}}{V_{cc}}\right) = -2.21 \text{ ms} \cdot \ln(1 - 0.55)$$

$$t_{delay} = -2.21 \text{ ms} \cdot \ln(0.45) = -2.21 \text{ ms} \cdot (-0.799)$$

$$t_{delay} \approx 1.77 \text{ ms}$$

**(iii) Distance Travelled During Delay:**

$$d = v \cdot t_{delay} = (1.5 \text{ m/s}) \cdot (0.00177 \text{ s}) = 2.65 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 2.65 \text{ mm}$$

**Safety Implication:** During the **1.77 ms debounce delay**, the AGV travels an additional **2.65 mm** beyond the contact point. For an indoor AGV at 1.5 m/s, this is **negligible** and well within safe tolerances. However, if the AGV were to operate at $v = 5.0 \text{ m/s}$, the same delay would result in $d = 8.85 \text{ mm}$ of additional travel — approaching the upper safety limit. **High-speed AGVs require either lower RC time constants (smaller C) or hardware Schmitt triggers with lower thresholds.**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU BOARD EXAMS:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert $k$ from **N/mm to N/m** when computing $F_{n,min}$. A wrong unit conversion yields $F_{n,min} = 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N}$ instead of $6.0 \text{ N}$. **Always show the unit conversion step explicitly.**
>
> 2. **Confusing Bumper with Proximity Sensor (–1 mark):** Writing "bumper sensor measures distance to obstacle." It does **NOT** — it outputs a binary contact/no-contact signal. State this distinction clearly.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Debounce Filter (–2 marks):** In a construction question, many students describe the microswitch but **omit the RC debounce network**. This is considered an **incomplete answer** in KTU valuation.
>
> 4. **Using `$\vert x \vert$` Inside Markdown Tables (–0 marks but renders broken):** When writing a formula sheet, never use the pipe character `|` for absolute value inside a table cell — use $\vert$ or $\mid$ LaTeX commands instead. The examiner's system may auto-fail your table.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Final Engineering Interpretation (–1 mark):** Always end numerical answers with a **one-line interpretation** of the result ("This means the sensor triggers on a very light touch..."). Examiners reward the **engineering judgment** shown.
>
> 6. **Not Drawing a Block Diagram (–2 marks on 7-mark question):** KTU 2024 scheme requires a **neat labeled block diagram** for any "with neat diagram" question. A pure textual answer is **incomplete**.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Bumper = Contact-Type Binary Sensor**, NOT a ranging sensor. Output is Boolean `0/1`.
- ⭐ **Six internal stages:** Free State → Approach → Deflection → Switch Actuation → Signal Conditioning → MCU Interrupt.
- ⭐ **Threshold equation:** $F_{n,min} = k \cdot \delta_{trip}$ (must include **unit conversion** in exam).
- ⭐ **Debounce equation:** $\tau = R \cdot C$, with design rule $\tau \geq 5 \cdot t_{bounce}$.
- ⭐ **Propagation delay formula:** $t_{delay} = -\tau \cdot \ln(1 - V_{th}/V_{cc})$.
- ⭐ **Coverage formula:** $L_b = 2\pi r / n$ per bumper on a circular chassis.
- ⭐ **Types:** Mechanical, Pneumatic, Capacitive, Piezoelectric, Optical, Hall-Effect.
- ⭐ **Fail-safe nature:** Bumpers are the **last line of defense** in ISO-certified AGVs.
- ⭐ **MTBF:** $\geq 100{,}000$ hours; **operating voltage:** 5 V or 24 V DC.
- ⭐ **Always include the RC debounce filter + Schmitt trigger** in any construction diagram.
- ⭐ **Examiners want:** Block diagram + labeled components + working principle + numerical example + engineering interpretation.
- ⭐ **Avoid:** Confusing bumpers with ultrasonic/IR/LiDAR, omitting units, using `|` inside tables.
- ⭐ **KTU 2024 buzzword:** "Class-0 Obstacle Detection Transducer" — use this exact phrase.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Odometry Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Odometry Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Odometry** is a fundamental technique in mobile robotics used to estimate the change in a robot's position and orientation (pose) over time by integrating the measurements of motion sensors, most commonly **wheel encoders** attached to the robot's drive wheels. The term is derived from the Greek words *hodos* (meaning journey/travel) and *metron* (meaning measure) — literally, the **measurement of travel**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> Odometry is the process of estimating a robot's instantaneous position by integrating its wheel rotation measurements (or other motion sensors) over time. It is the most widely used form of **dead reckoning** in mobile robotics, providing real-time, high-frequency, and inexpensive localization.

Formally, given a stream of encoder pulses from the left and right wheels, the robot's pose update in the 2D world frame $(x, y, \theta)$ at each time step $\Delta t$ is computed as:

$$x_{t+1} = x_t + \Delta s \cos(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2})$$

$$y_{t+1} = y_t + \Delta s \sin(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2})$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta \theta$$

where $\Delta s$ is the incremental translation of the robot and $\Delta \theta$ is the incremental rotation during the interval $\Delta t$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Walking Blindfolded"

Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to walk from your bed to your room's door. You cannot see the door, so you estimate your position by:
- **Counting your footsteps** (this is analogous to counting encoder pulses from wheels).
- **Tracking the direction** you turned (this is the heading $\theta$).
- **Compensating for stride length** (analogous to wheel diameter and gear ratios).

If your step size is **3 cm** and you take **20 steps forward**, you estimate you are **60 cm** ahead. If you then turn **90° right** and take **10 more steps**, you estimate you are **60 cm** right of your origin. This is **odometry** — and like walking blindfolded, errors accumulate the longer you move, because you are integrating small errors over time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:** Think of a car's **odometer** (the distance counter on the dashboard). It only measures distance traveled, not actual position on a map. An odometer cannot tell you *where* the car is on a road, but combined with steering and speed, it forms a *dead reckoning* system. This is exactly the principle used in mobile robots.

## 1.3 Classification of Odometry

Odometry is broadly classified based on the sensor modality used:

| Type | Sensor | Strength | Limitation |
|------|--------|----------|------------|
| **Wheel Odometry** | Wheel encoders (optical/magnetic) | Cheap, high sample rate | Slippage, terrain errors |
| **Visual Odometry (VO)** | Cameras (mono/stereo/RGB-D) | Works in many terrains | Computationally expensive, lighting dependent |
| **Inertial Odometry (IO)** | IMU (gyroscope + accelerometer) | Very high rate, no slip | Drift in position, bias |
| **Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO)** | Camera + IMU | Robust, low drift | Sensor fusion complexity |
| **LiDAR Odometry (LO)** | 2D/3D LiDAR | Accurate, lighting invariant | Expensive |

For the KTU 2024 Scheme, the focus of **Module 1** is predominantly on **wheel odometry** for differential drive and Ackermann-steered robots.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** Odometry is a **relative** (incremental) localization method — it requires an **initial pose estimate** to operate. It is **not absolute** like GPS.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Encoder Resolution** ($P$): typically **100 to 10,000 pulses per revolution (PPR)**.
- **Wheel Radius** ($r$): usually **3 cm to 30 cm** for mobile robots.
- **Track Width / Wheelbase** ($b$ or $L$): distance between left and right wheels.
- **Sampling Rate**: typical encoders operate at **10 kHz to 100 kHz**.
- **Position Update Rate**: **10 Hz to 100 Hz** in most robotic stacks (e.g., ROS).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The operation of a wheel odometry system can be broken down into the following sequential stages:

1. **Pulse Generation:** As a wheel rotates, an optical or magnetic encoder generates a discrete number of electrical pulses per revolution. Each pulse corresponds to a known angle of rotation, called the **angular resolution**.

$$\text{Angular Resolution} = \frac{2\pi}{P} \text{ radians/pulse}$$

where $P$ is the number of pulses per revolution.

2. **Pulse Counting:** A counter (hardware or software) accumulates the number of pulses from the left wheel encoder ($\Delta N_L$) and the right wheel encoder ($\Delta N_R$) over a fixed time interval $\Delta t$.

3. **Angular Displacement Calculation:** The rotation angle of each wheel is computed as:

$$\Delta \phi_L = \frac{2\pi \cdot \Delta N_L}{P} \quad , \quad \Delta \phi_R = \frac{2\pi \cdot \Delta N_R}{P}$$

4. **Linear Displacement of Each Wheel:** The arc length traversed by each wheel along the ground is:

$$\Delta s_L = r \cdot \Delta \phi_L \quad , \quad \Delta s_R = r \cdot \Delta \phi_R$$

5. **Robot Frame Motion:** For a **differential drive** robot with track width $b$ (distance between wheels), the robot's incremental translation and rotation are:

$$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2} \quad \text{(arc length traveled by midpoint)}$$

$$\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b} \quad \text{(rotation of the robot body)}$$

6. **Pose Update in World Frame:** The robot's pose in the global $(x, y, \theta)$ frame is updated using the mid-point integration scheme:

$$x_{t+1} = x_t + \Delta s \cos\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)$$

$$y_{t+1} = y_t + \Delta s \sin\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta \theta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:** Steps 1–4 convert raw electrical pulses into geometric wheel motion. Steps 5–6 fuse the two wheels' motions using **rigid body kinematics** to recover the robot's body motion, then transform this body motion into the world frame using the current heading $\theta$. This is essentially a **numerical integration of the robot's velocity over time**.

## 2.2 Types of Wheel Encoders

- **Incremental Encoder:** Outputs pulses proportional to rotation. Requires a reference (home) position. Most common in robotics.
- **Absolute Encoder:** Outputs a unique digital code for each angular position. No reference needed, more expensive.
- **Optical Encoder:** Uses a slotted disc and a photodetector. High resolution.
- **Magnetic Encoder:** Uses Hall sensors and a magnetized disc. Robust to dust and vibration.

## 2.3 Sources of Error in Odometry

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Topic:** Understanding error sources is critical. The two broad categories are:

**Systematic (Deterministic) Errors** — can be corrected by calibration:
- Unequal wheel diameters ($\Delta D$).
- Misalignment of wheels (the actual wheelbase $b_{actual} \neq b_{nominal}$).
- Finite encoder resolution (quantization).
- Finite sampling rate (sampled position vs. continuous position).

**Non-Systematic (Non-Deterministic) Errors** — caused by environment:
- Wheel slippage on slippery surfaces.
- Travel over uneven terrain.
- Bumps and obstacles causing wheel lift.
- External forces (e.g., a person pushing the robot).

The error in odometry grows **unboundedly** with distance — for indoor mobile robots, position error can be as high as **10% of distance traveled** without correction.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi \Delta N}{P}$ | Angular rotation of a wheel | radians |
| 2 | $\Delta s = r \cdot \Delta \phi$ | Linear distance traveled by a wheel | meters |
| 3 | $v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$ | Linear velocity of robot center | m/s |
| 4 | $\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b}$ | Angular velocity of robot | rad/s |
| 5 | $\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}$ | Incremental translation per step | meters |
| 6 | $\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}$ | Incremental heading change | radians |
| 7 | $x_{t+1} = x_t + \Delta s \cos(\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2)$ | Position update (x) | meters |
| 8 | $y_{t+1} = y_t + \Delta s \sin(\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2)$ | Position update (y) | meters |
| 9 | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta \theta$ | Heading update | radians |
| 10 | $R = \frac{b \cdot (\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L)}{2(\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L)}$ | Instantaneous turn radius | meters |
| 11 | $\dot{x} = v \cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v \sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$ | Kinematic model (continuous) | — |
| 12 | $v_R = r \omega_R, \quad v_L = r \omega_L$ | Wheel linear velocities | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** When $\Delta s_R = \Delta s_L$, then $\Delta \theta = 0$ (pure straight-line motion) and $R = \infty$ (no rotation). When $\Delta s_R = -\Delta s_L$, the robot rotates in place (zero turning radius).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Odometry is the **backbone** of nearly every mobile robotic system because:
- It is **real-time** (high frequency updates).
- It is **cheap** (encoders cost ₹200–₹2000 each).
- It has **low computational cost** (no image processing).
- It forms the **prediction step** of the Bayes filter in SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) and AMCL (Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization).
- It is used in **ROS (Robot Operating System)** as the standard `odom` → `base_link` transform in `nav_msgs/Odometry` messages.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pose Update Equations for Differential Drive Robot

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a **differential drive mobile robot** with:
- Left wheel radius $r$, right wheel radius $r$ (assumed equal).
- Track width $b$ (distance between wheel contact points).
- Encoder resolution $P$ pulses per revolution.
- Number of pulses counted on left wheel in time $\Delta t$: $\Delta N_L$.
- Number of pulses counted on right wheel in time $\Delta t$: $\Delta N_R$.

The robot is moving on a 2D plane, with global frame $\{W\}$ and robot body frame $\{R\}$ at position $(x, y, \theta)$ with respect to the world.

**Goal:** Derive the position update equations that map encoder counts to incremental world-frame motion.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Convert pulses to wheel angular rotation.**

Each pulse corresponds to a rotation of $\frac{2\pi}{P}$ radians. Therefore, the angular rotation of the left and right wheels during the sampling interval $\Delta t$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \phi_L &= \frac{2\pi \cdot \Delta N_L}{P} \\
\Delta \phi_R &= \frac{2\pi \cdot \Delta N_R}{P}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a direct application of the definition of angular resolution.

**Step 2: Compute linear arc length traveled by each wheel.**

Since the wheel rolls without slipping, the contact point of each wheel traces an arc of length equal to the radius times the angular rotation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta s_L &= r \cdot \Delta \phi_L = r \cdot \frac{2\pi \cdot \Delta N_L}{P} = \frac{2\pi r}{P} \cdot \Delta N_L \\
\Delta s_R &= r \cdot \Delta \phi_R = \frac{2\pi r}{P} \cdot \Delta N_R
\end{aligned}
$$

Define the constant $c_m = \frac{2\pi r}{P}$ (meters per pulse), called the **encoder-to-distance conversion factor**.

**Step 3: Compute the robot's incremental translation and rotation in the body frame.**

The midpoint of the wheel axis travels a distance equal to the average of the two wheel arc lengths (this is exact for a circular arc):

$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}
$$

For the rotation, we use the **rigid body constraint** that the two wheels and the robot body form a rigid triangle. The instantaneous center of rotation (ICR) lies on the line through the two wheels. Using the relationship between arc length and angle for a circle of radius $R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta s_R &= (R + b/2) \cdot \Delta \theta \\
\Delta s_L &= (R - b/2) \cdot \Delta \theta
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting these two equations:

$$
\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L = b \cdot \Delta \theta
$$

Therefore:

$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}
$$

**Step 4: Express incremental displacement in the world frame.**

The incremental displacement vector in the robot body frame is $(\Delta x_R, \Delta y_R) = (\Delta s, 0)$ — i.e., the robot moves forward by $\Delta s$ along its own x-axis. The 2D rotation matrix that transforms body frame coordinates to world frame coordinates is:

$$
R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

Applying the mid-point integration method (using the average heading $\theta_t + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2}$ to reduce discretization error):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + \Delta s \cdot \cos\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right) \\
y_{t+1} &= y_t + \Delta s \cdot \sin\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right) \\
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \Delta \theta
\end{aligned}
$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 5$ cm, track width $b = 30$ cm, and encoders with $P = 1000$ pulses per revolution. At time $t = 0$, the robot is at $(0, 0, 0)$. In the first interval $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, the left encoder counts 200 pulses and the right encoder counts 250 pulses. Compute the new pose $(x, y, \theta)$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the conversion factor.**

$$
c_m = \frac{2\pi r}{P} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.05}{1000} = \frac{0.1\pi}{1000} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/pulse}
$$

**Step 2: Compute wheel displacements.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta s_L &= c_m \cdot \Delta N_L = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 200 = 0.06283 \text{ m} \\
\Delta s_R &= c_m \cdot \Delta N_R = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 250 = 0.07854 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute incremental translation and rotation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta s &= \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2} = \frac{0.07854 + 0.06283}{2} = 0.07069 \text{ m} \\
\Delta \theta &= \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b} = \frac{0.07854 - 0.06283}{0.30} = 0.05236 \text{ rad} \approx 3^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute new pose using mid-point integration.**

With initial heading $\theta_0 = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= 0 + 0.07069 \cdot \cos\left(0 + \frac{0.05236}{2}\right) = 0.07069 \cdot \cos(0.02618) \\
x_1 &= 0.07069 \times 0.99966 = 0.07066 \text{ m} \\
y_1 &= 0 + 0.07069 \cdot \sin(0.02618) = 0.07069 \times 0.02618 = 0.00185 \text{ m} \\
\theta_1 &= 0 + 0.05236 = 0.05236 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Pose:** $(x, y, \theta) = (0.0707 \text{ m}, 0.00185 \text{ m}, 0.0524 \text{ rad})$.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison: Odometry vs. Other Localization Methods

| Property | Wheel Odometry | GPS | Visual Odometry | IMU |
|----------|----------------|-----|-----------------|-----|
| Cost | Very Low | Medium | Medium | Low |
| Accuracy (Short) | High | Medium | High | High (rotation) |
| Accuracy (Long) | Poor (drift) | High | Medium | Poor (position drift) |
| Works Indoors | Yes | No | Yes | Yes |
| Provides Heading | Yes | No | Yes (indirect) | Yes (gyro) |
| Update Rate | 10–100 Hz | 1–10 Hz | 10–30 Hz | 100–1000 Hz |
| Error Growth | Linear / Quadratic | Bounded | Sub-linear | Cubic |

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    READ --> COMPUTE[Compute Delta s_L and Delta s_R]
    COMPUTE --> DECIDE{Direction of Motion}
    DECIDE -->|Straight| STRAIGHT[Delta theta = 0]
    DECIDE -->|Curved| CURVED[Delta theta nonzero]
    DECIDE -->|In Place| INPLACE[v_R = -v_L]
    STRAIGHT --> UPDATE[Update Pose]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q1. Define Odometry. List any two sources of error in wheel odometry.

**Model Answer:**
**Odometry** is a method of estimating a robot's change in position over time by integrating the measurements of motion sensors, typically wheel encoders. It is a form of **dead reckoning** that uses the wheel rotations to compute the robot's incremental translation ($\Delta s$) and rotation ($\Delta \theta$), which are then accumulated to obtain the pose $(x, y, \theta)$.

**Two Sources of Error:**
1. **Systematic Error:** Unequal effective wheel diameters (caused by manufacturing tolerance, unequal load, or tire pressure differences).
2. **Non-Systematic Error:** Wheel slippage on slippery or uneven terrain, causing the wheel to rotate without producing ground displacement.

*(Any two relevant errors with brief explanation — 3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q2. Distinguish between systematic and non-systematic errors in odometry.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Systematic Errors | Non-Systematic Errors |
|--------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Nature** | Deterministic, repeatable | Random, unpredictable |
| **Cause** | Robot hardware (wheel mismatch, misalignment) | Environment (slip, bumps, obstacles) |
| **Correction** | Calibration | Cannot be calibrated; requires external sensing |
| **Example** | Wheel diameter difference $\Delta D$ | Wheel slip on wet floor |
| **Growth** | Linear with distance | Variable, often unbounded |

Systematic errors accumulate linearly, while non-systematic errors can produce sudden, large jumps in the estimated pose.

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a wheel odometry system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the position update equations for a differential drive mobile robot using wheel encoder measurements. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Working of Wheel Odometry System (7 Marks)**

The wheel odometry system consists of the following sequential stages (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Encoder Mounting:** Optical or magnetic encoders are mounted on the motor shaft or wheel shaft. As the wheel rotates, a slotted disc passes through a photodetector (optical) or a magnet rotates past a Hall sensor (magnetic), generating electrical pulses.

2. **Pulse Counting:** A microcontroller (e.g., Arduino, STM32) counts the pulses from the left and right encoders during a fixed sampling interval $\Delta t$ (typically 10–100 ms).

3. **Angular Displacement Calculation:** Using the encoder resolution $P$ (pulses per revolution), the angular rotation of each wheel is computed:
   $$\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi \cdot \Delta N}{P}$$

4. **Linear Displacement of Wheels:** The arc length rolled by each wheel is $\Delta s = r \cdot \Delta \phi$, where $r$ is the wheel radius.

5. **Body-Frame Kinematics:** Using the track width $b$, the robot's body-frame translation and rotation are:
   $$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2} \quad , \quad \Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}$$

6. **World-Frame Pose Update:** Using the rotation matrix and current heading $\theta_t$, the world-frame pose is updated (mid-point integration):
   $$x_{t+1} = x_t + \Delta s \cos(\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2)$$
   $$y_{t+1} = y_t + \Delta s \sin(\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2)$$
   $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta \theta$$

7. **Output:** The updated pose $(x, y, \theta)$ is published (e.g., as a ROS `nav_msgs/Odometry` message) for use by higher-level navigation, mapping, or control modules.

*(3 marks for block diagram, 4 marks for explanation — total 7 marks)*

**(b) Derivation of Position Update Equations (7 Marks)**

Refer to **Section 3.1.2** above for the complete step-by-step derivation. Key steps to include in the exam answer:

- Step 1: Convert pulses to angular rotation (1 mark).
- Step 2: Compute linear wheel displacement (1 mark).
- Step 3: Use rigid body kinematics with the ICR concept to derive $\Delta s$ and $\Delta \theta$ in terms of $b$, $\Delta s_L$, $\Delta s_R$ (3 marks).
- Step 4: Apply the 2D rotation matrix to project body-frame motion to world frame using mid-point integration (2 marks).

Final result:
$$x_{t+1} = x_t + \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2} \cos\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{2b}\right)$$
$$y_{t+1} = y_t + \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2} \sin\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{2b}\right)$$
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the forward kinematics of a differential drive robot and derive the instantaneous center of rotation (ICR) and the radius of curvature. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 8$ cm, track width $b = 40$ cm, and encoders with $P = 500$ pulses/rev. Starting from pose $(0, 0, 0)$, the robot records the following encoder counts in 4 successive intervals of $\Delta t = 0.2$ s:

| Interval | $\Delta N_L$ | $\Delta N_R$ |
|----------|--------------|--------------|
| 1 | 100 | 200 |
| 2 | 200 | 200 |
| 3 | 200 | 100 |
| 4 | 100 | 100 |

Compute the robot's pose at the end of each interval. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Forward Kinematics and ICR Derivation (7 Marks)**

The **forward kinematics** of a differential drive robot relates the wheel angular velocities ($\omega_L, \omega_R$) to the robot's body-frame linear and angular velocities $(v, \omega)$.

The linear velocity of each wheel is $v_L = r \omega_L$ and $v_R = r \omega_R$. The body-frame velocity of the midpoint of the wheel axis is:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$

The angular velocity of the body is:

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b}$$

**Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR):**
The ICR is the point about which the robot instantaneously rotates. It lies on the line connecting the two wheels, on the side of the faster wheel. If the ICR is at distance $R$ from the robot center (perpendicular to the wheel axis), then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_R &= \omega (R + b/2) \\
v_L &= \omega (R - b/2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting and using $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/b$, we get:

$$R = \frac{b(v_R + v_L)}{2(v_R - v_L)}$$

**Special Cases:**
- $v_R = v_L$ → $R = \infty$ (straight-line motion, no rotation).
- $v_R = -v_L$ → $R = 0$ (rotation in place about the wheel midpoint).
- $v_L = 0$ → $R = b/2$ (rotation about the stationary left wheel).

*(7 marks: 2 marks for forward kinematics, 3 marks for ICR derivation, 2 marks for special cases)*

**(b) Numerical Pose Computation (7 Marks)**

**Setup:**
- $r = 0.08$ m, $b = 0.40$ m, $P = 500$ pulses/rev.
- Conversion factor: $c_m = \frac{2\pi r}{P} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.08}{500} = 1.0053 \times 10^{-3}$ m/pulse.

**Interval 1: $\Delta N_L = 100$, $\Delta N_R = 200$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta s_L &= 1.0053 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 = 0.10053 \text{ m} \\
\Delta s_R &= 1.0053 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 = 0.20106 \text{ m} \\
\Delta s &= (0.20106 + 0.10053)/2 = 0.15080 \text{ m} \\
\Delta \theta &= (0.20106 - 0.10053)/0.40 = 0.25133 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

Starting from $(0, 0, 0)$:
$$x_1 = 0.15080 \cos(0.12566) = 0.1496 \text{ m}$$
$$y_1 = 0.15080 \sin(0.12566) = 0.01890 \text{ m}$$
$$\theta_1 = 0.25133 \text{ rad}$$

**Interval 2: $\Delta N_L = 200$, $\Delta N_R = 200$ (straight line)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta s_L &= 0.20106 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta s_R = 0.20106 \text{ m} \\
\Delta s &= 0.20106 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta \theta = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Pose 2: $\theta_2 = \theta_1 = 0.25133$ rad.
$$x_2 = 0.1496 + 0.20106 \cos(0.25133) = 0.1496 + 0.1948 = 0.3444 \text{ m}$$
$$y_2 = 0.01890 + 0.20106 \sin(0.25133) = 0.01890 + 0.04995 = 0.0689 \text{ m}$$

**Interval 3: $\Delta N_L = 200$, $\Delta N_R = 100$ (right wheel slower → turning right)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta s_L &= 0.20106 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta s_R = 0.10053 \text{ m} \\
\Delta s &= 0.15080 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta \theta = (0.10053 - 0.20106)/0.40 = -0.25133 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

Pose 3: $\theta_3 = 0.25133 - 0.25133 = 0$ rad.
$$x_3 = 0.3444 + 0.15080 \cos(0 + (-0.25133)/2) = 0.3444 + 0.15080 \cos(-0.12566)$$
$$x_3 = 0.3444 + 0.15080 \times 0.99211 = 0.3444 + 0.1496 = 0.4940 \text{ m}$$
$$y_3 = 0.0689 + 0.15080 \sin(-0.12566) = 0.0689 - 0.01890 = 0.0500 \text{ m}$$

**Interval 4: $\Delta N_L = 100$, $\Delta N_R = 100$ (straight)**

$$\Delta s = 0.10053 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta \theta = 0$$

Pose 4: $\theta_4 = 0$ rad.
$$x_4 = 0.4940 + 0.10053 = 0.5945 \text{ m}$$
$$y_4 = 0.0500 \text{ m}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Interval | $x$ (m) | $y$ (m) | $\theta$ (rad) |
|----------|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | 0.1496 | 0.0189 | 0.2513 |
| 2 | 0.3444 | 0.0689 | 0.2513 |
| 3 | 0.4940 | 0.0500 | 0.0000 |
| 4 | 0.5945 | 0.0500 | 0.0000 |

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks per interval computation, 1 mark for summary table)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** In Interval 3, students often forget to assign the **negative sign** to $\Delta \theta$ when the right wheel is slower. This is the most common mistake — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mid-point Integration:** Using $\theta_t$ instead of $(\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2)$ in the cosine/sine arguments leads to a small but **observable** error, especially in long turns. The mid-point method is the KTU-recommended scheme.
> 3. **Unit Consistency:** Always convert wheel radius $r$ and track width $b$ from **cm to meters** before plugging into equations. Mixing units is the #1 reason for numerical errors.
> 4. **Initial Pose:** If a problem gives a non-zero initial pose, students must use it as $x_0, y_0, \theta_0$ in the update equations. Forgetting this loses a full mark.
> 5. **Radians vs. Degrees:** $\theta$ must be in **radians** for trigonometric functions unless otherwise specified.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Odometry** = estimation of position change by integrating motion sensor data (most commonly wheel encoders).
- ✅ It is a **dead reckoning** technique — relative, not absolute. Requires an **initial pose**.
- ✅ The two main types of encoders are **incremental** (pulses) and **absolute** (unique codes per position).
- ✅ The encoder-to-distance conversion factor is $c_m = \frac{2\pi r}{P}$ meters per pulse.
- ✅ Differential drive kinematics: $\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}$ and $\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}$.
- ✅ Mid-point integration: use $(\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2)$ in the cosine and sine arguments for world-frame updates.
- ✅ Update order: $x_{t+1} \rightarrow y_{t+1} \rightarrow \theta_{t+1}$.
- ✅ **Systematic errors** = deterministic, corrected by calibration (e.g., wheel diameter mismatch, encoder resolution).
- ✅ **Non-systematic errors** = random, environment-induced (e.g., wheel slip, bumps, external pushes).
- ✅ Odometry drift grows **unbounded** with distance — typically 5–10% error over long runs.
- ✅ Solutions to drift: fuse odometry with **IMU (EKF)**, **GPS (outdoor)**, **LiDAR/Visual SLAM (indoor)**, or use **scan matching**.
- ✅ The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** lies on the wheel axis line, at distance $R = \frac{b(v_R + v_L)}{2(v_R - v_L)}$ from the robot center.
- ✅ Special cases: $v_R = v_L$ → straight line ($R = \infty$); $v_R = -v_L$ → in-place rotation ($R = 0$).
- ✅ In **ROS**, odometry is published as `nav_msgs/Odometry` with a `Twist` (velocity) and a `Pose` (position + orientation).
- ✅ The standard TF tree is: `odom` → `base_link` (published by odometry), then `base_link` → other sensors.
- ✅ For KTU exams, **always state units**, **show derivation steps**, and **draw the block diagram** in long answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heading Sensors - Gyroscopes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Heading Sensors - Gyroscopes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gyroscope (Heading Sensor):** A gyroscope is a heading sensor that measures **angular velocity** (rate of rotation) about one or more axes, based on the principle of **conservation of angular momentum** or the **Coriolis effect**. In robot navigation, it is used to determine and maintain orientation (heading/attitude) by providing rotational information that is independent of external references such as magnetic fields or visual landmarks.

In the context of **robot navigation**, gyroscopes form the angular-rate-measuring backbone of an **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**. They provide **yaw, pitch, and roll rate** data which, when integrated over time, yield the absolute orientation of the mobile platform relative to an initial reference frame.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEBRT751 Module 1):** Under the heading "Heading Sensors," the syllabus explicitly lists **Gyroscopes** alongside compasses, inclinometers, and IMUs. Students must clearly distinguish between **angular rate sensors (gyros)** and **absolute heading sensors (compasses)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy – "The Spinning Top":** Imagine a child's spinning top. Once spun, it stubbornly resists being tilted sideways — it wants to keep pointing in the same direction. If you push it, instead of falling over, it slowly **wobbles (precesses)** around the vertical axis. A gyroscope does exactly the same: a rapidly spinning rotor wants to keep its spin axis pointing in a fixed direction in space, and any attempt to rotate the platform is **opposed by angular momentum**, allowing us to *measure* that rotation.

A simpler analogy is a **bicycle wheel** held by both ends of its axle. If you try to twist the axle left or right while the wheel is spinning, your hands feel a strong resistance — that resistance is a manifestation of **gyroscopic rigidity**, and the rate at which the wheel tilts corresponds to the **angular velocity** being measured.

## 1.3 Why Gyroscopes are Critical in Robot Navigation

- **Independence from external references** — works in GPS-denied, magnetically distorted, or visually degraded environments.
- **High bandwidth / fast response** — typically **>100 Hz** update rate, ideal for dynamic robotic platforms.
- **Provides orientation between absolute fixes** — bridges gaps between GPS, beacons, or visual odometry samples.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (remember these for exams):**
> - Earth rotation rate: **Ωₑ ≈ 15.041 °/hour ≈ 7.2921 × 10⁻⁵ rad/s**
> - Standard gyro output units: **°/s (degrees per second)** or **rad/s**
> - Drift rate of tactical-grade IMU: **~1 °/hour**
> - Drift rate of MEMS-grade gyroscope: **~10–1000 °/hour**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle – Angular Momentum & Precession

The fundamental physics rests on **Newton's law for rotating bodies**:
1. A spinning mass possesses **angular momentum** $\vec{H} = I\vec{\omega}$, where $I$ is the moment of inertia and $\vec{\omega}$ is the spin rate.
2. When an external torque $\vec{\tau}$ is applied, $\vec{H}$ does **not** align with the new rotation axis instantly — it **precesses** about that axis.
3. The precession rate is governed by: $\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt} = \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{H}$, where $\vec{\Omega}$ is the precession (platform) angular velocity.
4. By measuring the **torque required to keep the spin axis fixed** (or by detecting the **Coriolis force** on a vibrating mass), the gyroscope infers the platform's rotation rate.

## 2.2 Two Underlying Physical Effects

| Effect | Physical Basis | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| **Sagnac Effect** | Counter-propagating light beams travel different path lengths in a rotating frame | Ring Laser Gyro (RLG), Fiber-Optic Gyro (FOG) |
| **Coriolis Effect** | A moving mass in a rotating frame experiences a perpendicular inertial force | MEMS Gyroscopes, Hemispherical Resonator Gyro |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks *"On what principle does a Ring Laser Gyro work?"* — the one-word answer is **Sagnac**. For a MEMS gyro, the answer is **Coriolis**.

## 2.3 Classification of Gyroscopes (KTU High-Yield)

| Category | Examples | Bias Drift | Cost | Use Case in Robotics |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical (spinning-mass)** | Classical rotor gyro | Low | High | Historical / aerospace |
| **Optical** | Ring Laser Gyro (RLG) | < 0.001 °/h | Very high | Aircraft, ships |
| **Optical (fiber)** | Fiber Optic Gyro (FOG) | 0.01–1 °/h | Medium-high | Autonomous vehicles, UAVs |
| **MEMS** | Vibrating structure gyro | 1–1000 °/h | Very low | Consumer drones, mobile robots, AGVs |
| **HRG** | Hemispherical Resonator Gyro | < 0.01 °/h | High | Spacecraft |

## 2.4 Key Performance Parameters (Examiner's Favourites)

- **Bias (Offset):** Constant error output when the true rate is zero. Affects long-term accuracy.
- **Bias Stability / Drift:** How much the bias changes over time. Expressed in **°/hour**.
- **Scale Factor Error:** Non-linearity between true and reported angular rate.
- **Angle Random Walk (ARW):** Noise-induced uncertainty; units **°/√h**.
- **Bandwidth:** Maximum rate at which the sensor responds; typically **>50 Hz** for MEMS.
- **g-Sensitivity:** Output change per unit g of linear acceleration (ideally zero).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table for ESE/IA — at least one formula is asked almost every cycle.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Angular momentum of spin | $H = I\omega_s$ | kg·m²/s | $I$ = moment of inertia, $\omega_s$ = spin rate |
| 2 | Gyroscopic precession rate | $\Omega_p = \dfrac{\tau}{H \sin\theta}$ | rad/s | $\theta$ = angle between $\vec{H}$ and torque axis |
| 3 | Sagnac phase shift (FOG) | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{8\pi A}{\lambda c}\,\Omega$ | rad | $A$ = loop area, $\lambda$ = wavelength, $c$ = speed of light |
| 4 | Sagnac fringe shift (RLG) | $\Delta N = \dfrac{4A}{\lambda L}\,\Omega$ | fringes | $L$ = path length of laser cavity |
| 5 | Coriolis acceleration | $\vec{a}_c = 2\,\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}$ | m/s² | $\vec{v}$ = velocity of proof mass |
| 6 | Coriolis force on proof mass | $\vec{F}_c = -2m\,\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}$ | N | $m$ = mass of proof mass |
| 7 | Heading integration (1-axis yaw) | $\psi(t) = \psi(t_0) + \displaystyle\int_{t_0}^{t} \omega_z(\tau)\,d\tau$ | rad | Causes drift if $\omega_z$ has bias |
| 8 | Drift-induced heading error | $\delta\psi(t) = b_z \, t$ | rad | $b_z$ = gyro bias in rad/s |
| 9 | Angular rate from accelerometer pair (in Coriolis gyro) | $\omega = \dfrac{a_c}{2v}$ | rad/s | From $a_c = 2\Omega v$ |
| 10 | Earth rate component on horizontal axis | $\omega_E = \Omega_e \cos\phi$ | rad/s | $\phi$ = latitude |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **UAV/drone flight control:** MEMS gyros provide sub-degree attitude estimation at 1 kHz refresh.
- **Autonomous Ground Vehicles (AGV):** FOGs deliver < 0.1 °/h drift during GPS outages.
- **Indoor mobile robots:** MEMS gyros integrated with wheel odometry suppress wheel-slip errors.
- **Marine & submarine navigation:** RLGs supply heading without depending on magnetic compasses.
- **Spacecraft:** HRGs and RLGs operate in radiation-prone, vacuum environments where mechanical gyros fail.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Gyroscopic Precession Rate (Mechanical Gyro)

**Problem:** A disc of moment of inertia $I = 0.05\,\text{kg·m}^2$ spins at $\omega_s = 1500\,\text{rad/s}$ about its own axis. The spin axis is horizontal. A torque $\tau = 0.4\,\text{N·m}$ is applied about a vertical axis. Determine the precession (heading) rate.

**Step 1 — Express the angular momentum of the spinning rotor.**

$$
H = I\,\omega_s
$$

This is the magnitude of the angular momentum vector directed along the spin axis. We compute the numerical value:

$$
H = (0.05)(1500) = 75\,\text{kg·m}^2/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the gyroscopic equation relating applied torque to the rate of change of angular momentum.**

The applied torque causes the angular momentum vector to *rotate* (precess) rather than to tilt. Mathematically:

$$
\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt} = \vec{\Omega}_p \times \vec{H}
$$

**Step 3 — Take magnitudes, assuming the spin axis is perpendicular to the torque axis ($\theta = 90^\circ$, so $\sin\theta = 1$).**

$$
\tau = \Omega_p \, H \sin\theta
$$

Solving for the precession rate:

$$
\Omega_p = \frac{\tau}{H \sin\theta}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\Omega_p = \frac{0.4}{75 \times 1} = 5.333 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to degrees per second for engineering readability.**

$$
\Omega_p = 5.333 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 0.3056\,^\circ/\text{s}
$$

**Result:** The platform (or spin axis) precesses at approximately **0.306 °/s** about the vertical axis. This is the *measured* rotation rate in a mechanical gyroscope.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sagnac Phase Shift in a Fiber-Optic Gyro (FOG)

**Problem:** A fiber-optic gyroscope uses a coil of radius $R = 0.05\,\text{m}$ wound with $N = 500$ turns of single-mode fiber. Light of vacuum wavelength $\lambda = 1550\,\text{nm}$ is used. The platform rotates at $\Omega = 10\,^\circ/\text{h}$. Find the Sagnac phase shift.

**Step 1 — State the general Sagnac phase-shift formula for a fiber coil of total area $A_{\text{tot}}$.**

$$
\Delta\phi = \frac{8\pi A_{\text{tot}}}{\lambda c}\,\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Express the total enclosed area in terms of coil turns and loop area.**

$$
A_{\text{tot}} = N \cdot \pi R^2
$$

Numerical evaluation:

$$
