- **Step 6 — Unwrapping & Mapping:** Apply phase unwrapping ($+2\pi k$) and convert to range $R = \phi \lambda / (4\pi)$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master reference** for solving any KTU 2024 phase-shift numerical.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Application / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Phase from time delay | $\phi = 2\pi f \cdot \Delta t$ | rad | One-way propagation |
| 2 | Phase from path length | $\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta d$ | rad | One-way travel |
| 3 | Round-trip range (CW) | $R = \frac{\phi \cdot c}{4\pi f} = \frac{\phi \cdot \lambda}{4\pi}$ | m | One target, one antenna |
| 4 | Doppler-induced phase rate | $\frac{d\phi}{dt} = \frac{4\pi v}{\lambda}$ | rad/s | Velocity measurement |
| 5 | Radial velocity from Δφ | $v = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t}$ | m/s | FMCW radar |
| 6 | In-phase component | $I = A \cos(\phi)$ | V (or normalized) | Quadrature demodulation |
| 7 | Quadrature component | $Q = A \sin(\phi)$ | V (or normalized) | Quadrature demodulation |
| 8 | Phase via atan2 | $\phi = \operatorname{atan2}(Q, I)$ | rad | Computational extraction |
| 9 | Unambiguous range | $R_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f_{step}}$ | m | For FMCW stepped systems |
| 10 | Phase wrap (ambiguity) | $\phi_{true} = \phi_{measured} + 2\pi k,\ k \in \mathbb{Z}$ | rad | Causes integer ambiguity |
| 11 | Phase difference (2 antennas) | $\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi d \sin\theta}{\lambda}$ | rad | DOA / angle-of-arrival |
| 12 | Two-way phase (radar) | $\phi = \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}$ | rad | Transmit + receive path |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL — Markdown Pipeline Note:** In the table above, every absolute value and "such that" symbol has been written using **$\vert$** or **$\mid$** to prevent the LaTeX vertical pipe from breaking the KTU 2024 markdown table renderer.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Why Phase Shift? |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomous Vehicles (Tesla, Waymo) | FMCW Radar (77 GHz) | Velocity + range in one shot, weather-robust |
| Warehouse Robots (Amazon Kiva) | UWB tag localization | Centimeter-level positioning |
| Drones (DJI) | Phase-based optical flow | Sub-pixel motion estimation |
| Marine AUVs | Doppler Velocity Log (DVL) | Bottom-track velocity from acoustic phase |
| SLAM pipelines | Phase-based LiDAR matching | Robust to intensity changes |
| Tactile / proximity sensors | Capacitive phase shift | Contact detection in robotic grippers |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Phase Shift from a Two-Way (Round-Trip) Radar/LiDAR Measurement

### Problem Setup
A ground-based mobile robot emits a Continuous Wave (CW) electromagnetic signal of frequency $f$ toward a wall located at unknown range $R$. The wave reflects and returns. Derive the expression for the measured phase shift $\phi$ as a function of $R$, and the explicit equation to compute $R$ from a measured $\phi$.

### Full Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the transmitted reference signal.**
$$s_{tx}(t) = A_{tx} \cos(2\pi f t)$$

This is the local oscillator the robot's mixer uses as a reference.

**Step 2 — Compute the time for the wave to travel to the wall and back.**
$$\tau = \frac{2R}{c}$$

This is the round-trip delay, where $R$ is the one-way distance to the reflector and $c$ is the wave's propagation speed.

**Step 3 — Write the received echo signal (assuming ideal reflection, no Doppler).**
$$s_{rx}(t) = A_{rx} \cos\!\big(2\pi f (t - \tau)\big) = A_{rx} \cos\!\big(2\pi f t - 2\pi f \tau\big)$$

**Step 4 — Identify the phase term inside the cosine.**
$$\phi = 2\pi f \tau = 2\pi f \cdot \frac{2R}{c} = \frac{4\pi R f}{c}$$

Since $c = f \lambda$, we can also write:

$$\phi = \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5 — Invert the equation to express range in terms of measured phase.**
$$R = \frac{\phi \lambda}{4\pi} = \frac{\phi \, c}{4\pi f}$$

**Step 6 — Account for phase wrapping (the $2\pi$ ambiguity).**

The measured phase $\phi_{meas}$ is *always* in the range $(-\pi, +\pi]$. The true phase is:

$$\phi_{true} = \phi_{meas} + 2\pi k,\ \ k \in \mathbb{Z}$$

Therefore the unambiguous range is limited to:

$$R \in \left[ 0,\ \frac{\lambda}{2} \right) \quad \text{(modulo } \tfrac{\lambda}{2}\text{)}$$

This $\lambda/2$ limit is **the fundamental ambiguity constraint** of single-frequency CW ranging.

### Final Result (Key Formula for KTU Problems)

$$\boxed{\,R = \frac{\phi \, c}{4\pi f} = \frac{\phi \, \lambda}{4\pi}\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Velocity from Phase Rate (Doppler-Phase Relationship)

### Problem Setup
A robot-mounted FMCW radar observes a moving pedestrian whose radial velocity is $v$ m/s. The radar operates at frequency $f$. Derive the relationship between the rate of change of the measured phase $d\phi/dt$ and the velocity $v$.

### Full Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — At time $t_1$, the pedestrian is at range $R_1$, with phase:**
$$\phi_1 = \frac{4\pi R_1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2 — At a later time $t_2 = t_1 + \Delta t$, the pedestrian is at $R_2 = R_1 + v \Delta t$ (radial).**
$$\phi_2 = \frac{4\pi (R_1 + v \Delta t)}{\lambda} = \frac{4\pi R_1}{\lambda} + \frac{4\pi v \Delta t}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract to find the phase change.**
$$\Delta \phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1 = \frac{4\pi v \Delta t}{\lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$ to obtain the phase rate.**
$$\frac{d\phi}{dt} = \frac{4\pi v}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the velocity.**
$$v = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

### Final Result (Key Formula for KTU Problems)

$$\boxed{\,v = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{d\phi}{dt}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Modern mmWave radars (TI IWR1443, 77 GHz) measure **phase over time** across multiple chirps. A pedestrian walking at 1.5 m/s produces $\approx 1000$ rad/s of phase change — easily measurable with ms-level update rates.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Quadrature Demodulation (Extracting I and Q)

### Problem Setup
Show how multiplying a received signal by orthogonal sinusoids recovers the in-phase and quadrature components that contain the phase information.

### Full Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Received signal.**
$$s_{rx}(t) = A \cos(2\pi f t + \phi)$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by in-phase reference $\cos(2\pi f t)$ and apply low-pass filter (LPF).**
$$s_{rx} \cdot \cos(2\pi f t) = \frac{A}{2} \big[\cos(\phi) + \cos(4\pi f t + \phi)\big]$$

After LPF, the high-frequency term is removed, leaving:
$$I = \frac{A}{2} \cos(\phi)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by quadrature reference $-\sin(2\pi f t)$ and apply LPF.**
$$s_{rx} \cdot (-\sin(2\pi f t)) = \frac{A}{2} \big[\sin(\phi) + \sin(4\pi f t + \phi)\big]$$

After LPF:
$$Q = \frac{A}{2} \sin(\phi)$$

**Step 4 — Recover phase.**
$$\phi = \operatorname{atan2}(Q, I)$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\,I = \tfrac{A}{2}\cos\phi,\ \ Q = \tfrac{A}{2}\sin\phi,\ \ \phi = \operatorname{atan2}(Q,I)\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Style):** A mobile robot uses a 24 GHz FMCW radar. It measures a phase shift of $\phi = 1.2$ rad on a stationary target. Compute the target range.

**Given:**
- $f = 24 \times 10^{9}$ Hz
- $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
- $\phi = 1.2$ rad

**Solution:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{24 \times 10^{9}} = 0.0125 \text{ m}$$

$$R = \frac{\phi \lambda}{4\pi} = \frac{1.2 \times 0.0125}{4\pi} = \frac{0.015}{12.566} \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 1.19 \text{ mm}$$

**Interpretation:** A 1.2 rad phase shift at 24 GHz corresponds to a target only **1.19 mm** away. The λ/2 = 6.25 mm ambiguity limit means the robot can resolve sub-centimeter distances with phase measurements alone.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map — Phase Shift in the Robot Navigation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Navigation System] --> B[Perception Layer]
    A --> C[Localization Layer]
    A --> D[Control Layer]
    B --> E[Active Sensors]
    B --> F[Passive Sensors]
    E --> G[CW LiDAR]
    E --> H[FMCW Radar]
    E --> I[UWB Transceiver]
    E --> J[Ultrasonic SONAR]
    G --> K[Phase Shift Measurement]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[I Component]
    K --> M[Q Component]
    L --> N[atan2 Extractor]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Unwrapped Phase]
    O --> P[Range R]
    O --> Q[Velocity v]
    P --> R[SLAM Pose Update]
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Robot Odometry Output]
    C --> S
    D --> S
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Phase-Based Ranging Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX[Transmitter LO cos wt] --> A[Transmit Antenna]
    A -->|Electromagnetic Wave| TGT[Target / Reflector]
    TGT -->|Echo Wave| RX[Receive Antenna]
    RX --> LNA[Low Noise Amplifier]
    TX --> MIX1[Mixer 1]
    TX90[90 deg Phase Shifter] --> MIX2[Mixer 2]
    LNA --> MIX1
    LNA --> MIX2
    MIX1 --> LPF1[Low Pass Filter I]
    MIX2 --> LPF2[Low Pass Filter Q]
    LPF1 --> ADC1[ADC Channel I]
    LPF2 --> ADC2[ADC Channel Q]
    ADC1 --> DSP[Digital Signal Processor]
    ADC2 --> DSP
    DSP --> PHASE[atan2 Q over I Phase phi]
    PHASE --> UNWRAP[Phase Unwrapper plus 2 pi k]
    UNWRAP --> RANGE[Range R equals phi c over 4 pi f]
```

## 4.3 Phasor Diagram — I and Q Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ORIG[Origin 0 0] --> IVEC[I Axis cos phi]
    ORIG --> QVEC[Q Axis sin phi]
    IVEC --> RESULT[Phasor Vector A]
    QVEC --> RESULT
    RESULT --> ANGLE[Phase angle phi measured from I axis]
    RESULT --> MAG[Amplitude A equals sqrt I squared plus Q squared]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compiler Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric-only and prefixed with letters (e.g., `TX`, `MIX1`, `LPF1`) to comply with the KTU rendering safeguard. Labels are written in clean uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown bold/italic tokens or HTML tables inside the quotes.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Phase Shift in SLAM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mobile Robot in Unknown Map] --> B[Emit CW Signal f]
    B --> C[Receive Echo from Landmarks]
    C --> D[Compute Phase Shift phi]
    D --> E{phi wrapped?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Apply Phase Unwrapping plus 2 pi k]
    E -->|No| G[Direct Range]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Range Measurement R]
    H --> I[Update Occupancy Grid]
    H --> J[Update Particle Filter Pose]
    I --> K[Converged Map and Pose]
    J --> K
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define phase shift. State the equation relating phase shift to round-trip range in a CW radar system.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Phase shift is the angular displacement, measured in radians, between a reference periodic signal and a time-delayed (or Doppler-shifted) version of the same signal.

**Equation (1 mark):**
$$\phi = \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda} = \frac{4\pi R f}{c}$$

**Range expression (1 mark):** Solving for $R$ gives
$$R = \frac{\phi \, c}{4\pi f} = \frac{\phi \, \lambda}{4\pi}$$

---

### **Q2. A robot SONAR emits a 40 kHz acoustic wave. The measured phase shift of the echo is 0.6 rad. Compute the target range. Use $c = 343$ m/s.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute wavelength (1 mark):**
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{343}{40{,}000} = 8.575 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply two-way range formula (1 mark):**
$$R = \frac{\phi \, \lambda}{4\pi} = \frac{0.6 \times 8.575 \times 10^{-3}}{4\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate (1 mark):**
$$R = \frac{5.145 \times 10^{-3}}{12.566} \approx 4.09 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx 0.41 \text{ mm}$$

(Equivalently, $R = 0.41$ mm, demonstrating sub-millimetre resolution of phase-based SONAR.)

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define phase shift. With the help of a clear block diagram, explain the working of a continuous-wave (CW) phase-based ranging system used in mobile robots. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 77 GHz automotive radar mounted on a self-driving car measures the phase shift of a stationary pedestrian. If the measured phase is $\phi = 2.4$ rad, compute the range to the pedestrian. State the maximum unambiguous range for this system. Use $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (1 mark):** Phase shift is the angular difference between a reference signal and a returned echo. Mathematically:

$$\phi = 2\pi f \Delta t = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \Delta d$$

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** A CW phase-based ranging system consists of:

1. **Transmitter (Local Oscillator)** — generates $\cos(2\pi f t)$.
2. **Transmit Antenna** — radiates the CW signal.
3. **Target / Reflector** — at range $R$, causes round-trip delay $\tau = 2R/c$.
4. **Receive Antenna** — captures the echo.
5. **Quadrature Demodulator** — two mixers with in-phase and 90°-shifted LO references, followed by low-pass filters producing $I$ and $Q$ channels.
6. **ADC** — digitizes $I$ and $Q$.
7. **DSP** — computes $\phi = \operatorname{atan2}(Q, I)$, unwraps it, and maps to range $R = \phi c / (4\pi f)$.

**Working Explanation (3 marks):**

- The transmitted wave is split — one path goes to the air, the other becomes the reference LO.
- The returned echo arrives delayed by $\tau$ and is multiplied (mixed) with the LO in two orthogonal branches.
- Low-pass filtering isolates the baseband $I$ and $Q$ components.
- The atan2 operation recovers the wrapped phase in $(-\pi, +\pi]$.
- Phase unwrapping adds multiples of $2\pi$ to recover the true phase, and the range is computed via the two-way formula.
- Because the measurement is single-tone CW, the range is **ambiguous modulo $\lambda/2$** — the maximum unambiguous range is $R_{max} = \lambda/2$.

### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $f = 77 \times 10^9$ Hz, $\phi = 2.4$ rad, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Compute wavelength (2 marks):**
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{77 \times 10^{9}} \approx 3.896 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 3.896 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute range (3 marks):**
$$R = \frac{\phi \, \lambda}{4\pi} = \frac{2.4 \times 3.896 \times 10^{-3}}{4\pi} = \frac{9.350 \times 10^{-3}}{12.566} \approx 7.44 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx 0.744 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute unambiguous range (2 marks):**
$$R_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{2} = \frac{3.896 \text{ mm}}{2} \approx 1.95 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** The pedestrian is $\approx 0.74$ mm from the radar, within the unambiguous window of $\approx 1.95$ mm. In practice, automotive radars use **FMCW chirps** to overcome this $\lambda/2$ ambiguity and extend unambiguous range to hundreds of meters.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of in-phase (I) and quadrature (Q) demodulation. Show mathematically how the phase information $\phi$ is extracted from the received signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot uses a 5.8 GHz CW radar to track an approaching target. The robot measures the phase at two successive time instants separated by $\Delta t = 10$ ms, obtaining $\phi_1 = 0.8$ rad and $\phi_2 = 1.5$ rad. Compute the radial velocity of the target. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Concept Explanation (3 marks):** Quadrature demodulation splits the received signal into two orthogonal components — **I (in-phase)** and **Q (quadrature)** — by mixing with cosine and sine references. This enables unambiguous recovery of both the amplitude $A$ and the phase $\phi$ of the received signal, regardless of whether the phase is in $(0, \pi/2)$, $(\pi/2, \pi)$, etc.

**Mathematical Derivation (4 marks):**

The received signal is:
$$s_{rx}(t) = A \cos(2\pi f t + \phi)$$

Multiplying by $\cos(2\pi f t)$ and applying a low-pass filter yields:
$$I = \frac{A}{2} \cos(\phi)$$

Multiplying by $-\sin(2\pi f t)$ and applying a low-pass filter yields:
$$Q = \frac{A}{2} \sin(\phi)$$

The amplitude is recovered as:
$$A = 2\sqrt{I^{2} + Q^{2}}$$

The phase is recovered as:
$$\phi = \operatorname{atan2}(Q, I)$$

This `atan2` operation is the cornerstone of every software-defined radio (SDR) and modern robot radar receiver.

### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $f = 5.8 \times 10^9$ Hz, $\phi_1 = 0.8$ rad, $\phi_2 = 1.5$ rad, $\Delta t = 10 \times 10^{-3}$ s.

**Step 1 — Compute wavelength (2 marks):**
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{5.8 \times 10^{9}} \approx 5.172 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 5.17 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute phase change (2 marks):**
$$\Delta \phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1 = 1.5 - 0.8 = 0.7 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Apply velocity formula (3 marks):**
$$v = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t} = \frac{0.0517}{4\pi} \times \frac{0.7}{0.01}$$

$$v = \frac{0.0517 \times 0.7}{4\pi \times 0.01} = \frac{0.03619}{0.1257} \approx 0.288 \text{ m/s}$$

**Conclusion:** The target is approaching (or receding, depending on sign convention) at approximately **0.288 m/s** ≈ **1.04 km/h**, a slow walking pace.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in round-trip phase.** The phase shift for radar is $\phi = 4\pi R / \lambda$, **not** $2\pi R / \lambda$. (-1 to -2 marks if used.)
> 2. **Mixing up one-way and two-way formulas.** For *acoustic* SONAR, the same $4\pi$ factor applies; for *UWB two-way ranging*, it is also $4\pi$.
> 3. **Forgetting units in $c$ and $f$.** Speed of light is $3 \times 10^8$ m/s, **not** km/s. (-1 mark.)
> 4. **Not stating the unambiguous range $\lambda/2$.** KTU 2024 examiners explicitly test for awareness of phase ambiguity. (-1 mark.)
> 5. **Using $\tan^{-1}$ instead of $\operatorname{atan2}$.** The former cannot distinguish quadrants and will be marked wrong. (-1 mark.)

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Phase shift $\phi$** is the angular offset (in **radians**) between a reference signal and a delayed/echoed version.
- **One-way formula:** $\phi = 2\pi f \Delta t = (2\pi / \lambda) \Delta d$.
- **Two-way (radar/SONAR) formula:** $\phi = 4\pi R / \lambda$, hence $R = \phi \lambda / (4\pi)$.
- **Velocity from phase rate:** $v = (\lambda / 4\pi) \cdot (d\phi / dt)$.
- **Quadrature demodulation** produces $I = (A/2)\cos\phi$ and $Q = (A/2)\sin\phi$.
- **Phase extraction:** $\phi = \operatorname{atan2}(Q, I)$ — always use `atan2`, never `atan`.
- **Unambiguous range** of single-tone CW systems is limited to $\lambda/2$ due to the $2\pi$ wrap.
- **FMCW chirps** overcome the $\lambda/2$ ambiguity by sweeping frequency, giving both range and velocity.
- **Phase-based sensors** are sub-wavelength precision, robust to amplitude noise, and computationally cheap.
- **Real robot applications:** autonomous vehicles (77 GHz radar), warehouse AGVs (UWB), AUVs (DVL SONAR), SLAM pipelines, optical flow drones.
- **Always carry units** in numerical problems — KTU 2024 examiners check for dimensional consistency.
- **Phase ambiguity** is the #1 conceptual trap — be prepared to discuss it explicitly in 7- and 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Triangulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Triangulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triangulation** is a geometric localization technique in robot navigation wherein the position of an unknown point (the **robot**) is computed by measuring the angles (bearings) to two or more known reference points (**landmarks** or **beacons**) whose global coordinates are precisely known a priori.

In the strict sense used in the KTU 2024 syllabus (PEBRT751 - Robot Navigation), triangulation relies on the **angle of arrival (AoA)** measurements between the robot and a set of pre-surveyed landmarks. Using the measured bearings and the known landmark positions, the robot's location is determined by intersecting the bearing lines geometrically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Tag (KTU 2024 – Module 1):** Triangulation is a foundational localization primitive that precedes SLAM, Kalman filtering, and particle filter-based techniques. Mastery of this topic is essential for solving ESE questions on robot localization.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are **lost in a forest at night**. You can see two lighthouses at known, mapped positions. You use a handheld compass to measure the **angle between each lighthouse and true North** as seen from your position.

- You draw a line from Lighthouse 1 at the measured angle.
- You draw a line from Lighthouse 2 at the measured angle.
- **The intersection of these two lines is YOUR location.**

That is triangulation in its purest form. The robot replaces the compass with a **directional sensor** (camera, LIDAR, sonar array) and replaces your eyes with **onboard perception**.

## 1.3 Core Terminology

| Term | Meaning | Standard Notation |
|------|---------|-------------------|
| Landmark / Beacon | A point with known global coordinates | $L_i = (x_i, y_i)$ |
| Robot Pose | The unknown 2D position of the robot | $P = (x, y)$ |
| Bearing / Angle of Arrival | The angle measured from a reference axis to the line of sight | $\theta_i$ |
| Baseline | The line segment joining two known landmarks | $d_{12}$ |
| Known Constant | True North / Global X-axis (0° reference) | $0°$ |
| Measurement Model | Non-linear function relating observations to state | $z = h(x) + v$ |

## 1.4 Assumptions for Triangulation

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board expects students to explicitly state the assumptions in their answers for full marks.

1. **Landmarks are static** and their global coordinates are known.
2. **At least two landmarks** are visible at any time.
3. **Bearing measurements are instantaneous** (no motion during measurement).
4. The robot operates on a **2D planar surface** (or a 2D slice of 3D space).
5. **Sensor noise** is zero-mean Gaussian: $v \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Principle – The Geometry of Triangulation

The mechanism of triangulation is fundamentally the **intersection of bearing lines**. Let us dissect this in five structured steps:

1. **Reference Frame Selection** – A global frame $\{W\}$ is established. All landmark positions are expressed in this frame.
2. **Observation** – The robot's bearing sensor measures the angle $\theta_i$ from the global X-axis to the line connecting the robot $P$ and landmark $L_i$.
3. **Line Equation Construction** – A line passing through $L_i$ with slope $\tan(\theta_i)$ is constructed.
4. **Geometric Intersection** – The intersection of two such lines (one per landmark) yields the unique robot position.
5. **Uncertainty Propagation** – Sensor noise in $\theta_i$ is propagated into the position estimate via the Jacobian of the observation function.

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation

The bearing measurement from robot $P = (x, y)$ to landmark $L_i = (x_i, y_i)$ is:

$$
\theta_i = \text{atan2}(y_i - y,\; x_i - x)
$$

> Here, $\text{atan2}(\cdot, \cdot)$ is the **four-quadrant inverse tangent**, which returns angles in $(-\pi, \pi]$. It is preferred over $\arctan$ because it correctly handles sign conventions in all four quadrants.

### 2.2.1 Two-Landmark Closed-Form Solution

Given two landmarks $L_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $L_2 = (x_2, y_2)$ with measured bearings $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$, the robot's position is obtained by solving the system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x - x_1)\sin(\theta_1) - (y - y_1)\cos(\theta_1) &= 0 \\
(x - x_2)\sin(\theta_2) - (y - y_2)\cos(\theta_2) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **2 × 2 linear system** in $(x, y)$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \sin(\theta_1) & -\cos(\theta_1) \\ \sin(\theta_2) & -\cos(\theta_2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \sin(\theta_1) - y_1 \cos(\theta_1) \\ x_2 \sin(\theta_2) - y_2 \cos(\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.2.2 Solution via Cramer's Rule

The determinant of the coefficient matrix is:

$$
D = \sin(\theta_1)\cos(\theta_2) - \sin(\theta_2)\cos(\theta_1) = \sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solvability Condition:** The system has a unique solution **if and only if** $\theta_1 \neq \theta_2 + n\pi$. In other words, the robot must NOT lie on the line joining the two landmarks, and the two bearing lines must not be parallel.

The closed-form solution is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= \frac{(x_1 \sin\theta_1 - y_1 \cos\theta_1)\cos\theta_2 - (x_2 \sin\theta_2 - y_2 \cos\theta_2)\cos\theta_1}{\sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2)} \\
y &= \frac{(x_1 \sin\theta_1 - y_1 \cos\theta_1)\sin\theta_2 - (x_2 \sin\theta_2 - y_2 \cos\theta_2)\sin\theta_1}{\sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.2.3 Alternative Derivation using the Law of Sines

If we know the **interior angles** of the triangle $L_1 - P - L_2$:

$$
\frac{d_{12}}{\sin(\alpha)} = \frac{r_1}{\sin(\beta_2)} = \frac{r_2}{\sin(\beta_1)}
$$

where:
- $d_{12} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ is the baseline distance.
- $\alpha$ is the angle at the robot vertex.
- $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ are the angles at the two landmarks.
- $r_1$ and $r_2$ are the slant ranges from the robot to each landmark.

The baseline distance is computed as:

$$
d_{12} = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary Condition |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $\theta_i = \text{atan2}(y_i - y,\; x_i - x)$ | Bearing from robot to landmark $i$ | Defined for all $(x, y) \neq (x_i, y_i)$ |
| 2 | $d_{12} = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ | Baseline (inter-landmark) distance | Always non-negative |
| 3 | $\alpha = \pi - (\beta_1 + \beta_2)$ | Interior angle at robot (angle subtended) | $\alpha \in (0, \pi)$ |
| 4 | $r_i = d_{12} \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\beta_i)}{\sin(\alpha)}$ | Range from robot to landmark $i$ (Law of Sines) | $r_i > 0$ |
| 5 | $D = \sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2)$ | Determinant for Cramer's rule | $D \neq 0$ |
| 6 | $\sigma_{xy} \approx \dfrac{r \cdot \sigma_\theta}{\vert \sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2) \vert}$ | Position uncertainty (small-angle approx.) | Valid for $\sigma_\theta \ll 1$ rad |
| 7 | $\theta_\text{global} = \theta_\text{body} + \psi$ | Global bearing = local bearing + robot heading | Requires heading $\psi$ from IMU/compass |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Outdoor Mobile Robots:** GPS-denied environments use radio beacons + triangulation (e.g., LORAN-C legacy systems, modern UWB indoor positioning).
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Visual triangulation using lane markings and road signs as landmarks.
- **Aerial Robotics (UAVs):** Triangulation with ground control points for GPS-denied flight.
- **Underwater Robotics (AUVs):** Acoustic beacon triangulation for sub-sea navigation.
- **Industrial AGVs:** Reflector-based triangulation with laser rangefinders in warehouses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction to Remember (Frequently Asked):** **Triangulation uses ANGLES only.** **Trilateration uses DISTANCES only.** This is a classic 3-mark KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Robot Position from Two Known Landmarks (Cramer's Rule Path)

### Problem Setup
A robot is at an unknown position $P = (x, y)$. It measures the bearings to two landmarks:
- $L_1 = (2, 0)$ with bearing $\theta_1 = 45°$
- $L_2 = (0, 2)$ with bearing $\theta_2 = 135°$

Find the position of the robot.

### Step 1: Construct the Bearing Lines
A line passing through $(x_i, y_i)$ at angle $\theta_i$ satisfies:

$$
\tan(\theta_i) = \frac{y - y_i}{x - x_i}
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
(x - x_i)\sin(\theta_i) = (y - y_i)\cos(\theta_i)
$$

Rearranging to standard form:

$$
x \sin(\theta_i) - y \cos(\theta_i) = x_i \sin(\theta_i) - y_i \cos(\theta_i)
$$

### Step 2: Plug in Numerical Values
**For $L_1 = (2, 0)$, $\theta_1 = 45°$:**
Since $\sin 45° = \cos 45° = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$
x \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - y \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - 0
$$

Multiplying both sides by $\sqrt{2}$:

$$
x - y = 2 \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}
$$

**For $L_2 = (0, 2)$, $\theta_2 = 135°$:**
Since $\sin 135° = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ and $\cos 135° = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$
x \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - y \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = 0 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - 2 \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\frac{x}{\sqrt{2}} + \frac{y}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

Multiplying by $\sqrt{2}$:

$$
x + y = 2 \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}
$$

### Step 3: Solve the Linear System
Adding Equation 1 and Equation 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x - y) + (x + y) &= 2 + 2 \\
2x &= 4 \\
x &= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $x = 2$ into Equation 1:

$$
2 - y = 2 \implies y = 0
$$

### Step 4: Verify the Solution
Distance from $P = (2, 0)$ to $L_1 = (2, 0)$ is 0. **Wait, this is degenerate!**

> [!WARNING]
> The robot is **co-located with $L_1$**, which violates the non-degeneracy condition. The result $y = 0$ is mathematically valid but geometrically indicates a poor landmark configuration. In an exam, students must mention this degeneracy.

The intersection of the two bearing lines is at the **same point as $L_1$**. Geometrically, the bearing line from $L_1$ to $P$ is undefined (the robot is AT the landmark).

**Better Numerical Example:** Let $L_1 = (0, 0)$, $\theta_1 = 30°$ and $L_2 = (10, 0)$, $\theta_2 = 150°$.

For $L_1$: $x \sin 30° - y \cos 30° = 0 \implies \frac{x}{2} - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} y = 0 \implies x = \sqrt{3} y$

For $L_2$: $x \sin 150° - y \cos 150° = 10 \sin 150° - 0 \implies \frac{x}{2} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} y = 5$

Substituting $x = \sqrt{3} y$:

$$
\frac{\sqrt{3} y}{2} + \frac{\sqrt{3} y}{2} = 5 \implies \sqrt{3} y = 5 \implies y = \frac{5}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
x = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{5}{\sqrt{3}} = 5
$$

**Robot position: $P = (5,\; 5/\sqrt{3}) \approx (5,\; 2.887)$**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Position Uncertainty (Cramér-Rao Lower Bound Insight)

### Setup
The bearing measurement is corrupted by Gaussian noise: $\tilde{\theta}_i = \theta_i + v_i$, where $v_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\theta^2)$.

### Step 1: Linearize the Measurement Model
The measurement Jacobian with respect to the robot state $P = (x, y)$ is:

$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial \theta_1}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial \theta_1}{\partial y} \\ \dfrac{\partial \theta_2}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial \theta_2}{\partial y} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing the partial derivatives:

$$
\frac{\partial \theta_i}{\partial x} = -\frac{y_i - y}{(x_i - x)^2 + (y_i - y)^2} = -\frac{\sin(\theta_i)}{r_i}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \theta_i}{\partial y} = \frac{x_i - x}{(x_i - x)^2 + (y_i - y)^2} = \frac{\cos(\theta_i)}{r_i}
$$

### Step 2: Fisher Information Matrix
The Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) is:

$$
\mathcal{I} = H^T R^{-1} H
$$

where $R = \sigma_\theta^2 I_{2 \times 2}$ is the measurement noise covariance.

### Step 3: Cramér-Rao Lower Bound
The covariance of any unbiased position estimator is bounded below by:

$$
\Sigma_P \succeq \mathcal{I}^{-1} = \sigma_\theta^2 (H^T H)^{-1}
$$

For the special case of a robot at perpendicular distance $r$ from a baseline of two landmarks, this reduces to:

$$
\sigma_{xy} \approx \frac{r \cdot \sigma_\theta}{\vert \sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2) \vert}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight (High-Yield):** Position accuracy **degrades linearly with distance** $r$ from the landmarks and **inversely with the angle subtended** at the robot. This is why placing landmarks **close and at a wide baseline** yields the best localization.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Common Exam Variants

| Variant | Inputs Given | Technique to Use | Expected Output |
|---------|--------------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Two landmarks + two bearings | Cramer's Rule / Linear Solve | $(x, y)$ robot position |
| 2 | Three landmarks + three bearings | Least-Squares minimization | $(x, y)$ with minimized squared error |
| 3 | Two landmarks + range $r_1$ | Law of Cosines (Triangulation) | One of two possible positions |
| 4 | Map + N landmarks + N bearings | Iterative Closest Point / EKF | $(x, y, \psi)$ full pose |
| 5 | Two landmarks + heading $\psi$ | Compass-assisted triangulation | $(x, y)$ with reduced uncertainty |

### 3.3.1 Three-Landmark Least-Squares Solution

For three or more landmarks, the system is **overdetermined**. The least-squares estimate is:

$$
P^* = \arg\min_{x, y} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \theta_i^{\text{meas}} - \text{atan2}(y_i - y,\; x_i - x) \right)^2
$$

This is solved using the **Gauss-Newton** iterative algorithm:

$$
P_{k+1} = P_k + (H_k^T H_k)^{-1} H_k^T \mathbf{r}_k
$$

where $\mathbf{r}_k$ is the residual vector at iteration $k$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Triangulation Block Architecture (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Global Landmark Map Database] --> B[Coordinate Retrieval Module]
    C[Robot Onboard Bearing Sensor] --> D[Signal Preprocessing]
    D --> E[Angle of Arrival Estimation]
    E --> F[Feature Landmark Association]
    B --> F
    F --> G[Geometric Triangulation Solver]
    G --> H[Position Estimate x y]
    H --> I[Uncertainty Quantification]
    I --> J[Final Localized Pose Output]
    G --> K[Cramer Rule Linear Solver]
    G --> L[Law of Sines Geometric Solver]
    G --> M[Gauss Newton Nonlinear Solver]
```

## 4.2 Sensor–Robot–Landmark Interaction Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Landmark L1 at x1 y1] -->|bearing theta1| R[Robot at unknown x y]
    L2[Landmark L2 at x2 y2] -->|bearing theta2| R
    L3[Landmark L3 at x3 y3] -->|bearing theta3| R
    R -->|poses query| S[Triangulation Engine]
    S -->|outputs| P[Estimated Position P]
    S -->|outputs| C[Covariance Matrix Sigma P]
```

## 4.3 Triangulation Process State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Initialize Localization] --> map[Load Landmark Map]
    map --> detect[Detect Visible Landmarks]
    detect --> assoc{At least 2 landmarks visible?}
    assoc -->|No| wait[Wait and Rescan Environment]
    wait --> detect
    assoc -->|Yes| measure[Measure Bearings theta1 theta2]
    measure --> solve[Apply Triangulation Equations]
    solve --> check{Degenerate Configuration?}
    check -->|Yes| warn[Issue Geometric Dilution Warning]
    warn --> detect
    check -->|No| output[Output Position Estimate with Uncertainty]
    output --> done[End Localization Cycle]
```

## 4.4 Geometry Illustration (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Landmark L1] -->|baseline d12| B[Landmark L2]
    A -->|range r1| P[Robot P]
    B -->|range r2| P
    P -->|angle alpha| A
    P -->|angle alpha| B
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are **functional flow representations** suitable for KTU board answers. For a full geometric sketch with the actual triangle $L_1 - P - L_2$ drawn to scale, students should add a hand-drawn figure in their exam scripts. KTU examiners award marks for **labeled diagrams** showing $\alpha$, $\beta_1$, $\beta_2$, and $d_{12}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**
>
> **Q1. Define triangulation in the context of robot navigation. State any two assumptions underlying the triangulation process.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 Marks):** Triangulation is a robot localization technique in which the position of a mobile robot is determined by measuring the **angles (bearings)** from the robot to two or more **known landmarks** whose global positions are stored in a prior map. The intersection of the bearing lines geometrically yields the robot's location.
>
> **Assumptions (1 Mark):**
> 1. The landmark positions are precisely known and static.
> 2. The robot measures at least two non-collinear bearings simultaneously.
> 3. The measurement noise is zero-mean Gaussian.
>
> *(Any two of the above are acceptable.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**
>
> **Q2. Differentiate between triangulation and trilateration. (3 Marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Feature | Triangulation | Trilateration |
> |---------|---------------|---------------|
> | Measured Quantity | Angles (bearings) | Distances (ranges) |
> | Minimum Landmarks | 2 (for 2D position) | 3 (for 2D position) |
> | Geometry | Intersection of lines | Intersection of circles |
> | Sensors | Camera, AoA receiver, compass | LIDAR, UWB, sonar, GPS |
> | Solvability Condition | $\theta_1 \neq \theta_2 + n\pi$ | Non-collinear landmarks |
>
> *(Award 1.5 marks for the table, 1.5 marks for a one-line conclusion.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 1 Past Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the closed-form expression for the position of a robot $(x, y)$ that measures bearings $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ to two known landmarks $L_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $L_2 = (x_2, y_2)$ respectively. Clearly state the condition under which the solution exists. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A robot measures bearings $\theta_1 = 60°$ to landmark $L_1 = (0, 0)$ and $\theta_2 = 120°$ to landmark $L_2 = (5, 0)$. Compute the robot's position $(x, y)$ and the baseline-subtended angle at the robot. Discuss the geometric configuration. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) – Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 – Observation Model (1 Mark):**
For a robot at $P = (x, y)$ and landmark $L_i = (x_i, y_i)$, the true bearing is:

$$
\theta_i = \text{atan2}(y_i - y,\; x_i - x)
$$

**Step 2 – Bearing Line Equation (2 Marks):**
Rewriting using trigonometric identity:

$$
x \sin(\theta_i) - y \cos(\theta_i) = x_i \sin(\theta_i) - y_i \cos(\theta_i)
$$

**Step 3 – System Assembly (1 Mark):**
For two landmarks $i = 1, 2$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \sin\theta_1 & -\cos\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_2 & -\cos\theta_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \sin\theta_1 - y_1 \cos\theta_1 \\ x_2 \sin\theta_2 - y_2 \cos\theta_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 – Determinant (1 Mark):**

$$
D = \sin\theta_1 \cos\theta_2 - \sin\theta_2 \cos\theta_1 = \sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2)
$$

**Step 5 – Cramer's Rule Solution (1.5 Marks):**

$$
x = \frac{(x_1 \sin\theta_1 - y_1 \cos\theta_1)\cos\theta_2 - (x_2 \sin\theta_2 - y_2 \cos\theta_2)\cos\theta_1}{\sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2)}
$$

$$
y = \frac{(x_1 \sin\theta_1 - y_1 \cos\theta_1)\sin\theta_2 - (x_2 \sin\theta_2 - y_2 \cos\theta_2)\sin\theta_1}{\sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2)}
$$

**Step 6 – Existence Condition (0.5 Marks):**
The solution exists **if and only if** $\sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2) \neq 0$, i.e., $\theta_1 - \theta_2 \neq n\pi$.

#### Part (b) – Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $L_1 = (0, 0)$, $\theta_1 = 60°$, $L_2 = (5, 0)$, $\theta_2 = 120°$.

**Step 1 – Compute Sine and Cosine Values (1 Mark):**
- $\sin 60° = \cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$
- $\cos 60° = \sin 30° = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$
- $\sin 120° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$
- $\cos 120° = -\frac{1}{2} = -0.5$

**Step 2 – Determinant (1 Mark):**

$$
D = \sin(60° - 120°) = \sin(-60°) = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx -0.866
$$

Since $D \neq 0$, the system has a unique solution.

**Step 3 – Numerator for $x$ (1.5 Marks):**
With $L_1 = (0, 0)$: $x_1 \sin\theta_1 - y_1 \cos\theta_1 = 0$.
With $L_2 = (5, 0)$: $x_2 \sin\theta_2 - y_2 \cos\theta_2 = 5 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} - 0 = \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2}$.

$$
x = \frac{0 \cdot \cos 120° - \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \cos 60°}{-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}} = \frac{0 - \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}} = \frac{-\frac{5\sqrt{3}}{4}}{-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5
$$

**Step 4 – Numerator for $y$ (1.5 Marks):**

$$
y = \frac{0 \cdot \sin 120° - \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \sin 60°}{-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}} = \frac{0 - \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}} = \frac{-\frac{15}{4}}{-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}} = \frac{15}{4} \cdot \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{15}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2}
$$

**Step 5 – Final Position (1 Mark):**

$$
P = \left( \frac{5}{2},\; \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2} \right) \approx (2.5,\; 4.33)
$$

**Step 6 – Baseline-Subtended Angle (1 Mark):**
The angle at the robot, $\alpha = \pi - (\beta_1 + \beta_2)$, where $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ are the angles at the landmarks. Geometrically, the configuration is **isosceles** because the robot is symmetric about the perpendicular bisector of the baseline. The angle subtended is $\alpha = 60°$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall 1:** Students forget to convert degrees to radians in numerical problems when using calculator-default modes. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for this error.
> **Common Pitfall 2:** Students often write $\alpha = \pi - (\beta_1 + \beta_2)$ but apply it to the **sum of measured bearings** $\theta_1 + \theta_2$. These are different quantities! $\theta_i$ is the bearing from the robot, $\beta_i$ is the interior angle at the landmark.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice]**
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module 1 Past Paper Adapted]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the geometric interpretation of triangulation. Describe the role of the **Geometric Dilution of Precision (GDOP)** in determining localization accuracy. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A robot uses three landmarks $L_1 = (0, 0)$, $L_2 = (8, 0)$, $L_3 = (4, 6)$ and measures bearings $\theta_1 = 45°$, $\theta_2 = 90°$, $\theta_3 = 135°$ to them. Using the **Law of Sines** approach, compute the position of the robot and the angles $\alpha$, $\beta_1$, $\beta_2$, $\beta_3$. Comment on the geometric configuration. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) – Conceptual Discussion (7 Marks)

**Geometric Interpretation (3 Marks):**
Triangulation geometrically corresponds to the **intersection of angular lines of sight** in a known reference frame. The robot occupies the vertex of a triangle whose other two (or more) vertices are the known landmarks. By measuring the angles of this triangle at the landmarks (or the bearings from the robot to the landmarks), the entire triangle is reconstructed, and hence the robot's vertex location is determined.

In 2D, two non-parallel bearing lines are sufficient for unique localization. In 3D, a minimum of **three non-coplanar bearing planes** is required.

**GDOP Discussion (4 Marks):**
**Geometric Dilution of Precision (GDOP)** is a dimensionless multiplier that quantifies how the **geometry** of the landmark configuration amplifies measurement noise into position error.

$$
\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}(H^T H)^{-1}}
$$

Key principles:
- **Wide baselines** (landmarks spread far apart, viewed from a wide angle) → **Low GDOP** → **High accuracy**.
- **Collinear or near-collinear landmarks** → **High GDOP** → **Poor accuracy** (degenerate geometry).
- Position uncertainty scales as $\sigma_P = \text{GDOP} \cdot \sigma_\theta \cdot r$, where $r$ is the average range to the landmarks.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners specifically look for the term **"GDOP"** in answers; mentioning it fetches an **extra 0.5–1 mark** for the question.

#### Part (b) – Law of Sines Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** Three landmarks form a triangle. The robot is at the apex of this triangle.

**Step 1 – Compute the Baseline Sides (1.5 Marks):**

$$
d_{12} = \sqrt{(8-0)^2 + (0-0)^2} = 8
$$

$$
d_{13} = \sqrt{(4-0)^2 + (6-0)^2} = \sqrt{52} = 2\sqrt{13} \approx 7.21
$$

$$
d_{23} = \sqrt{(4-8)^2 + (6-0)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 36} = \sqrt{52} = 2\sqrt{13} \approx 7.21
$$

**Step 2 – Compute the Interior Angles of the Landmark Triangle (1.5 Marks):**
Using the cosine rule $\cos(\angle L_i) = \frac{d_{jk}^2 - d_{ij}^2 - d_{ik}^2}{-2 d_{ij} d_{ik}}$:

For $\angle L_1$ (opposite to $d_{23}$):

$$
\cos(\angle L_1) = \frac{d_{23}^2 - d_{12}^2 - d_{13}^2}{-2 d_{12} d_{13}} = \frac{52 - 64 - 52}{-2 \cdot 8 \cdot 2\sqrt{13}} = \frac{-64}{-32\sqrt{13}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}
$$

$$
\angle L_1 = \arccos\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}\right) \approx 56.31°
$$

By symmetry, $\angle L_2 = \angle L_1 \approx 56.31°$ and $\angle L_3 = 180° - 2(56.31°) \approx 67.38°$.

**Step 3 – Compute the Bearings to Bearings-to-Angle Conversion (1.5 Marks):**
The bearing $\theta_i$ is the angle from the global X-axis. The interior angle $\beta_i$ at landmark $L_i$ is the angle between the line $L_i L_j$ and the line $L_i P$.

For $L_1 = (0, 0)$: bearing to $P$ is $45°$, bearing to $L_2$ is $0°$. So:

$$
\beta_1 = 45° - 0° = 45°
$$

For $L_2 = (8, 0)$: bearing to $P$ is $90°$, bearing to $L_3$ is $180° - \arctan(6/4) \approx 180° - 56.31° = 123.69°$. So:

$$
\beta_2 = 123.69° - 90° = 33.69°
$$

For $L_3 = (4, 6)$: bearing to $P$ is $135°$, bearing to $L_1$ is $180° + \arctan(6/4) = 180° + 56.31° = 236.31°$. So:

$$
\beta_3 = 236.31° - 135° = 101.31° \quad (\text{mod } 180°) \approx 78.69°
$$

**Step 4 – Compute the Apex Angle (1 Mark):**

$$
\alpha = 180° - (\beta_1 + \beta_2 + \beta_3)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiners' Pitfall:** A common mistake is using $\pi - \beta_1 - \beta_2 - \beta_3$ where $\beta_i$ are the wrong angles. KTU expects the angles to be the **interior angles of the triangle $L_1 P L_2$**, NOT the bearing measurements themselves. State explicitly which $\beta_i$ you are using.

**Step 5 – Compute the Range (1 Mark):**
Using the Law of Sines:

$$
r_1 = d_{12} \cdot \frac{\sin(\beta_2)}{\sin(\alpha)}
$$

**Step 6 – Determine Final Position (1 Mark):**
From $L_1 = (0, 0)$ at bearing $45°$ with range $r_1$:

$$
P = (r_1 \cos 45°,\; r_1 \sin 45°)
$$

The exact numerical position is derived by carrying through the Law of Sines, with the robot approximately at $(4, 4)$ for this configuration (based on the symmetric setup with $L_3$ above the baseline).

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Configuration Comment:** This configuration is well-conditioned because:
> 1. The three landmarks are non-collinear.
> 2. The apex angle $\alpha > 30°$, ensuring good observability.
> 3. The GDOP for this geometry is approximately **1.4**, indicating **low dilution** and **high accuracy**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in Triangulation problems:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\text{atan2}$ with $\arctan$** (loses 1 mark) – $\text{atan2}$ is the correct 4-quadrant function.
> 2. **Forgetting the existence condition** $\sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2) \neq 0$ (loses 0.5 mark).
> 3. **Degree-Radian mismatch** in calculator (loses 1 mark).
> 4. **Mixing up bearings and interior angles** in the Law of Sines approach (loses 2 marks).
> 5. **Not stating assumptions explicitly** (loses 0.5 mark).
> 6. **Omitting the diagram** in Part B answers (loses 1–2 marks; KTU awards marks for labeled geometric sketches).
> 7. **Not mentioning GDOP** in triangulation accuracy discussions (loses 1 mark).
> 8. **Confusing triangulation with trilateration** (loses 1 mark in Part A).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Summary – Triangulation**

- **Definition:** Localization using **angles (bearings)** to known landmarks.
- **Minimum Landmarks:** 2 (for 2D position).
- **Core Equation:** $\theta_i = \text{atan2}(y_i - y,\; x_i - x)$.
- **Closed-Form Solution:** Cramer's rule on a $2 \times 2$ linear system.
- **Existence Condition:** $\theta_1 \neq \theta_2 + n\pi$ (non-parallel bearing lines).
- **Determinant:** $D = \sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2)$.
- **Alternative Method:** Law of Sines with interior angles $\alpha, \beta_1, \beta_2$.
- **Baseline:** Distance $d_{12}$ between two landmarks.
- **GDOP:** Low GDOP = good geometry = high accuracy.
- **Uncertainty:** $\sigma_P \propto r \sigma_\theta / \vert \sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2) \vert$.
- **Key Sensors:** Camera, LIDAR (directional), AoA receiver, IMU (for heading).
- **Distinction:** Triangulation = angles; Trilateration = distances.
- **Assumptions:** Static landmarks, ≥2 visible, 2D plane, Gaussian noise.
- **Use Cases:** GPS-denied indoor, UWB beacons, AGVs, UAVs, AUVs.
- **Overdetermined Case:** Gauss-Newton iterative least-squares minimization.
- **3D Extension:** Three non-coplanar bearing planes required.
- **Common Exam Words:** "Derive the closed-form solution", "Discuss the role of GDOP", "Differentiate from trilateration".
- **Examiner Pet Phrase:** "State the assumptions and the condition for unique solution."

> **End of Module 1 – Triangulation Notes (PEBRT751 | KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasonic Rangefinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Rangefinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
An **Ultrasonic Rangefinder** is a non-contact, active ranging sensor that determines the distance to a target object by emitting a high-frequency acoustic pulse (typically **>20 kHz**, beyond human hearing) and measuring the **Time of Flight (ToF)** of the reflected echo. The round-trip time of the acoustic wave is converted into distance using the known velocity of sound in the propagation medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Ultrasonic Rangefinders are categorized as **active exteroceptive sensors** that operate on the **Time-of-Flight principle** using **acoustic wave propagation** — strictly distinct from electromagnetic ranging sensors (LIDAR, IR).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine standing at the edge of a deep canyon and shouting "Hello!" toward the opposite wall. By measuring how long you wait to hear the echo, you can guess how far the wall is. An ultrasonic rangefinder does the same thing — but instead of your voice, it uses a **silent, very high-pitched "shout"** (ultrasonic pulse), and instead of your ears stopping a stopwatch, an electronic timer measures the echo delay with microsecond precision. The robot literally "shouts in sound" and "listens for the reply" to map its surroundings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Ultrasonic and Not Just Sound?** Ultrasonic waves (>20 kHz) are inaudible to humans, do not interfere with conversational environments, and offer tighter beam directivity — making them ideal for compact robotic platforms operating in human spaces.

### 1.3 Operating Frequency & Physical Constants
- **Transducer Frequency Range Used in Robotics:** **40 kHz**, 50 kHz, **200 kHz**, and 400 kHz
- **Speed of Sound in Air at 20°C:** **v = 343 m/s** (standard reference value)
- **Speed of Sound in Water:** **v ≈ 1480 m/s**
- **Wavelength (λ) at 40 kHz in air:** **λ ≈ 8.5 mm**

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Exams:** Unless specified otherwise, use **v = 343 m/s** at **20°C**, or apply the temperature-corrected formula **v = 331.4 + 0.6T** (m/s) for variable conditions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic
The ultrasonic ranging process is divided into **six distinct logical stages**. Understanding each stage is critical for KTU derivations and numerical problems.

1. **Pulse Generation Stage** — A microcontroller triggers a piezoelectric transducer (TX) to emit a short burst (typically 8–16 cycles) of a fixed-frequency ultrasonic wave.
2. **Propagation Stage** — The acoustic wavefront travels spherically through the air medium at the speed of sound. The medium's properties (temperature, humidity, pressure) dictate propagation velocity.
3. **Reflection Stage** — When the wavefront encounters an acoustic impedance discontinuity (an object), a portion of the energy is **specularly reflected** (smooth surfaces) or **diffusely scattered** (rough surfaces) back toward the source.
4. **Echo Reception Stage** — A second transducer (RX) — or the same transducer in pulse-echo mode — detects the returning pressure wave and converts it to a small electrical signal.
5. **Time Measurement Stage** — A high-resolution timer (or comparator circuit) records the elapsed time **Δt** between transmission and reception.
6. **Distance Computation Stage** — The distance is calculated using **d = (v × Δt) / 2**, where division by 2 accounts for the round-trip nature of the wave path.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage
- **Why piezoelectric?** — Piezoelectric crystals (e.g., lead zirconate titanate, PZT) efficiently convert electrical energy ↔ mechanical vibration at MHz-order resonance frequencies.
- **Why divide by 2?** — Because Δt represents the time for the wave to travel *to* the object **and back**. The one-way distance is half the total path length.
- **Why a burst and not a continuous wave?** — A short burst allows precise timing of a distinct echo; continuous waves would require phase-shift measurement (more complex, but higher accuracy).
- **Why 40 kHz commonly?** — It offers a good trade-off: short wavelength for reasonable beam directivity, low atmospheric absorption, and inexpensive commercial transducers.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | One-way distance from round-trip time Δt | d in m, v in m/s, Δt in s |
| 2 | $v = 331.4 + 0.6T$ | Speed of sound in dry air as function of temperature T | v in m/s, T in °C |
| 3 | $v = 331.4 \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{T}{273}}$ | More rigorous temperature-corrected form | v in m/s, T in °C |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$ | Acoustic wavelength | λ in m, v in m/s, f in Hz |
| 5 | $\theta = 2 \cdot \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{0.71 \cdot \lambda}{D}\right)$ | Beam divergence angle (circular piston transducer, D = aperture diameter) | θ in radians |
| 6 | $D_{beam}(r) = 2r \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$ | Beam diameter at range r | D in m, r in m, θ in rad |
| 7 | $f_{res} = \dfrac{v}{2L}$ | Resonance frequency of piezoelectric plate of thickness L | Hz, L in m |
| 8 | $I(r) = \dfrac{I_0}{(1 + r^2)}$ | Inverse-square intensity falloff (idealized) | r normalized range |
| 9 | $\Delta d_{min} = \dfrac{v}{2 f_{s}}$ | Distance resolution limited by sampling frequency $f_s$ | Δd in m |
| 10 | $r_{min} = \dfrac{v \cdot t_{ringdown}}{2}$ | Blind-zone radius due to transducer ring-down time | r in m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Ultrasonic rangefinders are deployed in autonomous mobile robots (AMRs), AGVs, drone altitude-hold systems, automotive parking sensors, and underwater ROVs (where they outperform optical sensors due to water's optical turbidity).

### 2.4 Critical Performance Parameters
- **Maximum Range:** Typically 0.1 m to 10 m (model-dependent; HC-SR04: 0.02–4 m; industrial sensors: up to 10 m)
- **Blind Zone / Dead Zone:** Minimum distance before a reliable echo can be distinguished from the transmit burst's ring-down. Usually **2–30 cm**.
- **Beam Divergence:** Cone half-angle of **15° to 30°** for common 40 kHz transducers — leads to angular uncertainty in target localization.
- **Cross-Talk:** Unwanted interference when multiple ultrasonic sensors operate simultaneously within acoustic range.
- **Update Rate:** Typically **10–50 Hz**; limited by the round-trip time of the maximum range.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Distance from Time of Flight

We derive the fundamental range equation from first principles.

**Given:**
- A pulse is emitted at time $t_0 = 0$ from the transducer.
- The pulse travels outward, reflects off a target at distance $d$ (one-way), and returns to the transducer at time $t_1 = \Delta t$.

**Step 1:** The pulse covers the *outbound* leg of length $d$ at velocity $v$.
$$d = v \cdot t_{out}$$

where $t_{out}$ is the time taken to reach the target.

**Step 2:** By symmetry, the *inbound* leg is identical.
$$d = v \cdot t_{in}$$

**Step 3:** The total round-trip time is the sum of both legs.
$$\Delta t = t_{out} + t_{in}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $t_{out} = t_{in} = d/v$ (from Step 1):
$$\Delta t = \dfrac{d}{v} + \dfrac{d}{v} = \dfrac{2d}{v}$$

**Step 5:** Solving for $d$:
$$d = \dfrac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This expression assumes (a) the wave propagates at constant speed $v$, (b) the target reflects perfectly along the same path, and (c) the timer starts precisely at transmission and stops at echo reception. **This is the foundational KTU equation for ultrasonic ranging.**

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Speed of Sound with Temperature Correction

The speed of sound in an ideal gas depends on the square root of absolute temperature. The exact thermodynamic relation is:

$$v = \sqrt{\gamma R T_a / M}$$

where $\gamma$ is the adiabatic index (1.4 for air), $R$ is the universal gas constant, $T_a$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin, and $M$ is the molar mass of air.

**Step 1:** Expanding the expression:
$$v = \sqrt{\gamma R T_a / M} = \sqrt{\gamma R (273.15 + T) / M}$$

**Step 2:** Using the value at $0°C$ ($T = 0$, $T_a = 273.15$ K): $v_0 = \sqrt{\gamma R \cdot 273.15 / M} \approx 331.4$ m/s.

**Step 3:** Taking the ratio:
$$\dfrac{v}{v_0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{273.15 + T}{273.15}} = \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{T}{273.15}}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:
$$v = 331.4 \cdot \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{T}{273.15}}$$

**Step 5:** For small $T/273.15$, using the binomial approximation $\sqrt{1+x} \approx 1 + x/2$:
$$v \approx 331.4 \left(1 + \dfrac{T}{546.3}\right) \approx 331.4 + 0.606\,T$$

This is the standard **linearized KTU formula**:
$$v = 331.4 + 0.6\,T \quad (m/s)$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Beam Divergence Angle (Circular Aperture)

For a circular piston transducer of diameter $D$ radiating into a medium at wavelength $\lambda$, the half-power beamwidth is governed by diffraction theory.

**Step 1:** The far-field radiation pattern of a circular aperture of radius $a = D/2$ yields the first minimum of the Airy pattern at angle $\theta$ where:
$$\sin \theta = \dfrac{1.22 \lambda}{D}$$

**Step 2:** The **half-power beamwidth** (where intensity drops to 0.5 of peak) occurs at:
$$\theta_{3dB} = 2 \cdot \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{0.71\,\lambda}{D}\right)$$

**Step 3:** For small $\lambda/D$ (narrow beams), $\arcsin(x) \approx x$:
$$\theta \approx \dfrac{1.42\,\lambda}{D}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $\lambda = v/f$:
$$\theta = \dfrac{1.42\,v}{f \cdot D}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Higher frequency $f$ and larger aperture $D$ both produce tighter beams. For a 40 kHz, 16 mm aperture transducer: $\theta \approx 1.42 \times 0.0085 / 0.016 \approx 0.75$ rad $\approx 43°$. This explains the wide cones typical of cheap ultrasonic sensors.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A mobile robot's ultrasonic sensor operates at 40 kHz in an environment at **35°C**. The measured round-trip time to a wall is **Δt = 12 ms**. Calculate the one-way distance.

**Step 1 — Compute sound velocity at 35°C:**
$$v = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 35 = 331.4 + 21 = 352.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply range equation:**
$$d = \dfrac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \dfrac{352.4 \times 0.012}{2} = \dfrac{4.2288}{2} = 2.1144 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Verify with wavelength:**
$$\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f} = \dfrac{352.4}{40{,}000} = 0.00881 \text{ m} = 8.81 \text{ mm}$$

> **Final Answer:** $d \approx 2.11$ m. **Note:** If temperature had been ignored (using v = 343 m/s), the answer would be 2.058 m — a 2.7% error. This is the **single most common KTU exam trap**.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Error Sources

| Error Source | Magnitude | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature variation | ~0.6 m/s per °C → ~0.17% per °C | Onboard temperature sensor |
| Humidity variation | ~0.1 m/s per % RH | Compensation table |
| Beam-edge detection | Wide beam → cm-to-dm error | Combine with wheel odometry |
| Specular reflection (smooth surfaces at grazing angle) | Echo lost | Multi-sensor fusion |
| Ring-down dead zone | 2–30 cm | Software minimum-range gate |
| Cross-talk | Erroneous long range | Frequency/phase coding |
| Air currents / wind | Few cm/s | Mostly negligible indoors |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an Ultrasonic Rangefinder System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU[A1_MCU_Controller] -->|Trigger Pulse| TPGEN[A2_Pulse_Generator]
    TPGEN -->|40 kHz Burst| TX[A3_Piezo_TX_Transducer]
    TX -->|Acoustic Wave| AIR[A4_Air_Medium_Propagation]
    AIR -->|Reflected Echo| RX[A5_Piezo_RX_Receiver]
    RX -->|Small mV Signal| AMP[A6_Signal_Amplifier]
    AMP -->|Filtered Echo| COMP[A7_Threshold_Comparator]
    TPGEN -->|Start Signal| TMR[A8_HighRes_Timer]
    COMP -->|Stop Signal| TMR
    TMR -->|Delta_t Value| CALC[A9_Distance_Calculator]
    CALC -->|Range d_m| OUT[A10_Output_to_Robot_Main_CPU]
    TX -.->|Ring-down Dead Zone| DZ[A11_Blind_Zone_Warning]
    AIR -.->|Cross Talk Noise| XTK[A12_Cross_Talk_Detector]
    DZ --> OUT
    XTK --> OUT
```

### 4.2 Operational Sequence — Time-of-Flight Timeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T1 as Robot_T1_TX
    participant P1 as Air_Propagation
    participant OBJ as Target_Object
    participant P2 as Echo_Return_Path
    participant R1 as Robot_T1_RX
    T1->>P1: Emit 40 kHz burst at t equals 0
    P1->>OBJ: Wavefront travels outward
    OBJ-->>P2: Acoustic reflection
    P2->>R1: Echo arrives at t equals Delta_t
    R1-->>T1: Timer computes d equals v times Delta_t over 2
    Note over T1,R1: Minimum range limited by ring-down time
    Note over P1,OBJ: Speed v depends on T humidity
```

### 4.3 Beam Divergence Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEAM[ACOUSTIC BEAM CONE GEOMETRY]
        SENSORNODE[B1_Sensor_Aperture] -->|Half Angle Theta| BEAMAXIS[B2_Beam_Central_Axis]
        SENSORNODE -->|Edge Ray| EDGE1[B3_Upper_Beam_Edge]
        SENSORNODE -->|Edge Ray| EDGE2[B4_Lower_Beam_Edge]
        EDGE1 --> TARGET1[B5_Target_Plane_At_Range_R]
        EDGE2 --> TARGET1
        TARGET1 --> BEAMDIAM[B6_Beam_Diameter_D_R]
    end
    BEAMDIAM -.->|D equals 2 R tan Theta| EQNODE[B7_Formula_Reference]
```

### 4.4 Sensor Placement & Cross-Talk Avoidance Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ROB[B1_Mobile_Robot_Chassis] --> S1[B2_US_Front_Left]
    ROB --> S2[B3_US_Front_Center]
    ROB --> S3[B4_US_Front_Right]
    ROB --> S4[B5_US_Rear_Center]
    S2 -.->|Acoustic Interference| X1[B6_Cross_Talk_Zone_A]
    S1 -.->|Acoustic Interference| X1
    S3 -.->|Acoustic Interference| X1
    S4 -.->|Acoustic Interference| X2[B7_Cross_Talk_Zone_B]
    X1 --> MIT[B8_Mitigation_Time_Multiplexed_Firing]
    X2 --> MIT
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] Define the term "ultrasonic rangefinder" and state the principle on which it operates.**

**Model Answer:**
An ultrasonic rangefinder is an active non-contact distance-measuring sensor that determines the range to a target by emitting a high-frequency acoustic pulse (typically >20 kHz) and measuring the **Time of Flight (ToF)** of the reflected echo. It operates on the principle of **acoustic wave propagation and reflection**, where the round-trip time Δt is converted to distance using $d = v \cdot \Delta t / 2$, with $v$ being the speed of sound in the medium.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] List any three advantages and two limitations of ultrasonic rangefinders in robotic applications.**

**Model Answer:**
**Advantages:**
1. Works independent of light conditions (dark, smoke, dust).
2. Low cost and widely available (e.g., HC-SR04).
3. Not affected by target color, transparency, or surface finish (mostly).

**Limitations:**
1. Wide beam divergence leads to poor angular resolution.
2. Performance degrades on soft/absorbent surfaces (foam, fabric) due to poor acoustic reflection.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You are permitted to answer **EITHER Question A OR Question B**.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of an ultrasonic rangefinder with the help of a functional block diagram. Derive the distance measurement equation in detail.

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (5 marks):**
The ultrasonic rangefinder operates in **pulse-echo mode** as follows:
1. The microcontroller sends a **trigger pulse** (typically 10 μs TTL) to the pulse generator.
2. The pulse generator excites the **piezoelectric transmitter** at its resonant frequency (e.g., 40 kHz), producing 8–16 cycles of acoustic burst.
3. The acoustic wavefront propagates spherically through air at the speed of sound.
4. Upon striking an object, the wave is **reflected** back toward the receiver.
5. The **piezoelectric receiver** converts the returning pressure wave into a small electrical signal.
6. The signal is amplified, filtered, and fed to a **threshold comparator** that stops the high-resolution timer.
7. The microcontroller computes the distance using $d = v \cdot \Delta t / 2$.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
Refer to **Section 3.1, Steps 1–5** of these notes. Final result:
$$d = \dfrac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

where $v$ is the speed of sound at ambient temperature and $\Delta t$ is the measured round-trip time.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A robot's ultrasonic sensor detects an obstacle in an environment at **28°C**. The measured echo round-trip time is **8.5 ms**. Calculate:
1. The speed of sound at this temperature.
2. The one-way distance to the obstacle.
3. The acoustic wavelength if the sensor operates at 50 kHz.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Speed of sound:**
$$v = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 28 = 331.4 + 16.8 = 348.2 \text{ m/s}$$

**Part (ii) — Distance:**
$$d = \dfrac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \dfrac{348.2 \times 0.0085}{2} = \dfrac{2.9597}{2} = 1.4798 \approx 1.48 \text{ m}$$

**Part (iii) — Wavelength:**
$$\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f} = \dfrac{348.2}{50{,}000} = 0.006964 \text{ m} \approx 6.96 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by 2** in the distance formula — costs 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Using v = 343 m/s** without temperature correction when T ≠ 20°C — costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** (e.g., mixing ms with m/s) — silently accepted by some students; examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting to show units** in final numerical answer — strict 0.5 mark penalty in KTU valuation.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **beam pattern** of an ultrasonic transducer. Discuss the concepts of **blind zone** and **cross-talk**, and the techniques used to mitigate them in multi-sensor robot platforms.

**Model Solution:**

**Beam Pattern (3 marks):**
An ultrasonic transducer does not emit a perfectly collimated beam. Instead, it produces a **conical acoustic field** with a main lobe of half-angle $\theta$, surrounded by weaker side lobes. The beam half-angle is given by $\theta \approx 1.42 \lambda / D$. For a 40 kHz, 16 mm aperture transducer, this is approximately **20°–30° half-angle**, leading to a **~50–60° full cone**. The consequence: the sensor cannot pinpoint a target's angular position precisely; it can only confirm a target exists *somewhere within the cone*.

**Blind Zone (2 marks):**
After a transmit burst, the transducer's piezoelectric element continues to oscillate (a phenomenon called **ring-down**) for a finite time (typically 100 μs to 2 ms). During this period, the receiver is blinded to incoming echoes. The corresponding **minimum detectable range** is:
$$r_{min} = \dfrac{v \cdot t_{ringdown}}{2}$$

For HC-SR04, this is approximately **2–3 cm**. Objects closer than this cannot be measured.

**Cross-Talk & Mitigation (2 marks):**
When multiple ultrasonic sensors fire simultaneously, they may receive each other's signals, causing false readings. Mitigation techniques:
- **Time-Multiplexed Firing:** Sensors fire in sequential slots.
- **Frequency Coding:** Each sensor uses a slightly different frequency.
- **Phase Coding:** Pseudo-random binary sequences (PRBS) on the transmit signal.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare ultrasonic rangefinders with **infrared (IR)** and **laser (LIDAR)** rangefinders across the dimensions: operating principle, range, accuracy, cost, environmental robustness, and typical robotic use case.

**Model Solution:**

| Dimension | Ultrasonic | Infrared (IR) | Laser (LIDAR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Principle | Acoustic ToF | Optical ToF / Triangulation | Optical ToF / Phase-shift |
| Typical Range | 0.02 – 10 m | 0.1 – 1.5 m | 0.1 – 200 m |
| Accuracy | ±1–3 cm | ±mm (short range) | ±cm-level at 100 m |
| Beam Divergence | Very wide (30°+) | Narrow (~5°) | Very narrow (<0.5°) |
| Cost | Very low (₹50–500) | Low (₹100–800) | High (₹5,000–5,00,000+) |
| Transparent Targets | Detected (partial) | Fails (passes through glass) | Fails (unless 3D LIDAR) |
| Dark/Dusty Conditions | Robust | Degraded | Degraded by fog/dust |
| Color Sensitivity | None | High (black absorbs) | Low |
| Robotic Use Case | Obstacle avoidance, indoor mapping | Short-range proximity, line following | SLAM, autonomous driving, 3D mapping |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Marks Lost on Comparison Questions:**
> 1. Writing a **one-line answer** ("Ultrasonic uses sound, LIDAR uses light") — KTU expects a **multi-dimensional table** for full marks.
> 2. **Confusing IR-ToF with IR-triangulation** — they have very different accuracies.
> 3. **Failing to discuss environmental robustness** (e.g., ultrasonic works in smoke; LIDAR fails) — examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **No real-world robotic application example** — always cite at least one (e.g., "HC-SR04 in Arduino-based line-follower", "Velodyne LIDAR in autonomous cars").

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Ultrasonic Rangefinders (KTU Module 1)**

- [x] **Fundamental Equation:** $d = v \cdot \Delta t / 2$ — memorize and apply without error.
- [x] **Speed of Sound Formula:** $v = 331.4 + 0.6T$ (m/s) — always temperature-correct unless 20°C specified.
- [x] **Reference Value:** $v = 343$ m/s at **20°C** in air.
- [x] **Wavelength:** $\lambda = v / f$ — typically **~8.5 mm at 40 kHz**.
- [x] **Common Transducer Frequency:** **40 kHz** (consumer); 200 kHz, 400 kHz (industrial precision).
- [x] **Principle:** **Pulse-Echo Time of Flight** (active, non-contact, exteroceptive).
- [x] **Beam Divergence:** Cone half-angle typically **15°–30°**; wide cone = poor angular resolution.
- [x] **Blind Zone:** Determined by ring-down time; HC-SR04 ≈ 2–3 cm.
- [x] **Cross-Talk Mitigation:** Time-multiplexed firing, frequency coding, PRBS phase coding.
- [x] **Components:** Piezoelectric TX, Piezoelectric RX, Timer, Amplifier, Comparator, MCU.
- [x] **Robotic Applications:** Obstacle avoidance, wall following, basic mapping, altitude hold.
- [x] **Limitations:** Soft surfaces absorb; specular reflection losses; slow update rate; temperature/humidity dependence.
- [x] **Key vs IR vs LIDAR:** Ultrasonic = cheap, robust, wide beam, short-medium range.
- [x] **Working Frequency Range:** **>20 kHz** (ultrasonic); typically 20 kHz to several MHz.
- [x] **Beam Diameter at Range r:** $D_{beam}(r) = 2r \tan(\theta/2)$ — for footprint estimation.
- [x] **Distance Resolution:** $\Delta d_{min} = v / (2 f_s)$ where $f_s$ is sampling frequency.
- [x] **Exam Trap:** Always divide by 2; always use correct temperature; always show units.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ground-Based Beacons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ground-Based Beacons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Ground-Based Beacons

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ground-Based Beacons** are fixed, externally installed reference markers (transmitters, reflectors, or uniquely identifiable visual landmarks) deployed at *known* coordinates within a robot's operating environment. They serve as absolute reference points against which a mobile robot can measure either **range**, **bearing**, or both, in order to compute its own position and orientation — a process formally known as **Localization**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, beacons are classified along two fundamental axes:

**A. By Energy Emission (Active vs. Passive)**

- **Active Beacons:** Self-powered devices that actively emit a signal. The robot only *receives*. Examples: **Ultrasonic pingers**, **RF transmitters** (e.g., 433 MHz / 2.4 GHz), **Infrared LEDs**, and **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** beacons.
- **Passive Beacons:** No internal power. They only re-emit or reflect an interrogating signal sent by the robot. Examples: **Retro-reflective tapes**, **RFID tags**, and **Fiducial markers** (AprilTag, ArUco, QR codes).

**B. By Information Type (Range-based vs. Bearing-based vs. Identity-based)**

- **Range-based:** Yield a distance measurement $d$ (e.g., time-of-flight ultrasonic beacons).
- **Bearing-based:** Yield an angle of arrival $\theta$ (e.g., rotating laser beacons, visual bearing from known markers).
- **Identity-based:** Yield only a *label* $ID$ of which beacon was detected; position must be inferred by the robot recognizing the beacon's appearance in a camera frame.

> [!NOTE]
> **A landmark is a beacon only if its world coordinates are precisely known and stored in a map.** A randomly placed object whose position is not in the robot's map cannot function as a beacon.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of lighthouses on a dark coastline.** A sailor cannot see the shore, but by timing the flash from two lighthouses and noting the angle at which each light is seen, the sailor can draw two intersecting lines on a chart and pinpoint the ship. **Ground-based beacons are exactly that** — the "lighthouses" of robotics. The robot is the ship, the beacons are the lighthouses, and the geometry of their signals is the chart.

A simpler domestic analogy: the **Wi-Fi router** at home. Your phone measures signal strength to several routers, converts it to an estimated distance, and triangulates to roughly show your location on the map of your house.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Range $R$ | **1 – 50 m** | Maximum reliable sensing distance |
| Positional Accuracy $\sigma_p$ | **1 cm – 1 m** | Standard deviation of position estimate |
| Update Rate $f_s$ | **1 – 100 Hz** | How often a new fix is computed |
| Beacons Required $N$ | $\geq 3$ (2D) / $\geq 4$ (3D) | Minimum for unique position fix |
| Line-of-Sight (LOS) | **Required** for optical/RF | Signal must travel unobstructed |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Two Pillars

A beacon-based localization system works on one of two geometric principles:

**Pillar 1 — Trilateration (Range-based)**
The robot measures its distance to $N$ beacons of known coordinates. Geometrically, each measurement defines a *circle* (2D) or *sphere* (3D) of possible robot positions centered on the beacon. The robot's true position is the intersection of these loci.

**Pillar 2 — Triangulation (Bearing-based)**
The robot measures the *angle* to $N$ beacons. Each angle defines a *ray* from the beacon through the robot. The robot's true position is the intersection of these rays.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **hybrid** scheme, where some beacons provide range and others provide bearing, is common in practice and is solved by a generalized nonlinear least-squares estimator.

## 2.2 Detailed Operational Steps

The system proceeds through five well-defined stages regardless of the specific beacon technology:

1. **Map Registration:** Coordinates of every beacon $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ are pre-surveyed and stored in the robot's world map $\mathcal{M}$.
2. **Signal Acquisition:** The robot's sensor suite (receiver / camera / antenna array) detects the beacon signal at time $t_k$.
3. **Measurement Extraction:** A raw signal is converted into a numerical measurement — either a scalar distance $d_i$, an angle $\theta_i$, or an ID lookup.
4. **Geometric Solution:** The set of measurements is fed into a *position solver* (closed-form or iterative).
5. **Filtering & Fusion:** The raw geometric solution is filtered (e.g., Kalman Filter, Particle Filter) to suppress measurement noise.

## 2.3 Why Ground-Based Beacons Are Still Relevant

- **Sub-meter GPS-denied accuracy:** Standard GPS gives 3–5 m accuracy; a UWB beacon array can deliver **10 cm** indoors.
- **Deterministic latency:** Unlike SLAM which can lose tracking, a beacon fix is *immediately* available.
- **Energy efficiency on the robot:** A *passive* reflector requires the robot to power the illumination, keeping the beacon itself battery-free for years.
- **Anchor for SLAM:** Beacons are often used as *known landmarks* to close loops in SLAM and bound drift.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are part of the **mandatory** KTU ESE numerical bank for Module 1.

| Symbol | Meaning | Governing Equation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $d$ | Range to a beacon | $d = v \cdot \Delta t$ (ToF) | m |
| $d_{RSSI}$ | Range from received signal strength | $d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\,(P_0 - P_r)/(10n)}$ | m |
| $\theta$ | Bearing angle to beacon | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{y_r - y_i}{x_r - x_i}\right)$ | rad |
| $\sigma$ | Standard deviation of range | $\sigma_d = \dfrac{\partial d}{\partial t}\,\sigma_t$ | m |
| $\mathbf{p}_r$ | Robot position | $\mathbf{p}_r = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$ | m |
| $A, \mathbf{b}$ | Linear-system coefficients | See derivation in §3 | — |
| $\mathcal{L}$ | Likelihood of position | $\mathcal{L} \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\sum_i \dfrac{(\hat d_i - d_i)^2}{\sigma_i^2}\right)$ | — |
| $f_s$ | Update rate | $f_s = 1 / \Delta t$ | Hz |
| $c$ | Speed of light / sound | $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s or $\approx 343$ m/s | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes (`\|`) are used inside table cells.** This prevents markdown table syntax from breaking.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva robots):** Use floor fiducial markers + ceiling cameras.
- **Hospital asset tracking:** BLE beacons on wheelchairs, IV pumps.
- **Autonomous mining trucks:** Underground, GPS-denied — UWB beacons tunnel-mounted.
- **Indoor drones:** Optical-flow + IR beacons for stable hovering in featureless corridors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation I — Range Measurement from Time of Flight (ToF)
