\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \;=\; \det(M), \qquad \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 \;=\; \mathrm{trace}(M)
$$

- If both $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ are **small** → flat region.
- If one is large, the other small → **edge**.
- If **both are large** → **corner**.

To avoid explicit eigenvalue computation (expensive), Harris proposed the response:

$$
R_{\text{Harris}} \;=\; \lambda_1 \lambda_2 \;-\; k (\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2 \;=\; \det(M) \;-\; k \cdot \mathrm{trace}(M)^2
$$

This corner response is computable in $O(1)$ per pixel using only the determinant and trace of $M$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Lowe’s Ratio Test for Matching Reliability

When matching a query descriptor $\mathbf{d}_q$ to a database of $N$ descriptors, the nearest neighbor $\mathbf{d}_{1}$ and the second-nearest neighbor $\mathbf{d}_{2}$ are found. Lowe proposed that a match is **accepted** only if:

$$
\frac{\| \mathbf{d}_q - \mathbf{d}_1 \|}{\| \mathbf{d}_q - \mathbf{d}_2 \|} \;<\; T_{\text{ratio}}
$$

Typical threshold: $T_{\text{ratio}} = 0.6$ to $0.8$. *Reasoning:* A distinctive feature has a nearest neighbor *much closer* than the runner-up. If two descriptors are nearly equidistant, the feature lies in an ambiguous region and the match is unreliable.

### 3.3 Derivation: Scale-Space Construction

The scale-space $L(x, y, \sigma)$ of an image $I(x, y)$ is defined as the convolution with a 2D Gaussian:

$$
L(x, y, \sigma) \;=\; G(x, y, \sigma) * I(x, y) \;=\; \iint G(u, v, \sigma) \, I(x-u, y-v) \, du \, dv
$$

where the Gaussian kernel is:

$$
G(x, y, \sigma) \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi \sigma^2} \exp\!\left( -\frac{x^2 + y^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)
$$

To detect scale-stable keypoints, SIFT constructs a **scale pyramid**: $L$ is computed at multiple $\sigma$ values separated by a constant factor $k$. The Difference-of-Gaussian $D(x, y, \sigma)$ is then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(x, y, \sigma) &\;=\; L(x, y, k\sigma) - L(x, y, \sigma) \\
&\;\approx\; (k - 1)\sigma^2 \, \nabla^2 L \\
&\;\text{(since } \nabla^2 G \approx (k-1)\sigma^2 \text{ via heat-equation relation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This establishes that $D$ is a close approximation to the **Laplacian-of-Gaussian (LoG)** $\sigma^2 \nabla^2 L$, providing scale-invariance through extrema detection across pyramid levels.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Which Feature for Which Task?

| Task / Environment | Recommended Feature | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Indoor visual SLAM | ORB | Fast binary descriptor, real-time |
| Outdoor autonomous driving | LiDAR point features / SIFT | Robust to lighting |
| Underwater sonar | SIFT-like on sonar imagery | Texture-poor scenes |
| Drone GPS-denied nav | FAST + optical flow | Lightweight, embedded |
| Warehouse AGV | Line / plane features (LiDAR) | Structured environment |
| Urban place recognition | NetVLAD (deep features) | Robust to viewpoint |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Harris Response)

Given the structure tensor at a pixel:

$$
M \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 50 & 30 \\ 30 & 40 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad k \;=\; 0.04
$$

Compute the Harris response:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det(M) &\;=\; (50)(40) - (30)(30) \;=\; 2000 - 900 \;=\; 1100 \\
\mathrm{trace}(M) &\;=\; 50 + 40 \;=\; 90 \\
\mathrm{trace}(M)^2 &\;=\; 8100 \\
R_{\text{Harris}} &\;=\; 1100 - 0.04 \times 8100 \;=\; 1100 - 324 \;=\; 776
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $R_{\text{Harris}} = 776 > 0$, the pixel is classified as a **corner** (positive $R$ indicates both eigenvalues are large). This is the kind of one-shot computation examiners love to test.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Table of Classical Features

| Property | Harris | SIFT | SURF | ORB | FAST |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Scale-invariant | No | Yes | Yes | Partial | No |
| Rotation-invariant | Partial | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| Speed | Medium | Slow | Medium | Very Fast | Very Fast |
| Descriptor dim. | — | 128 | 64 | 32 | — |
| Binary descriptor | — | No | No | Yes | — |
| License | Open | Patented (expired) | Open | Open | Open |
| KTU exam relevance | High | High | Medium | High | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBRT751]**
> **Q1.** Define a *feature* in the context of robot navigation. List any **four** desirable properties of a good feature.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A *feature* in robot navigation is a salient, measurable, and computationally tractable attribute extracted from raw sensor data (image, LiDAR, sonar) that uniquely identifies a region, object, or landmark in the environment, enabling tasks such as localization, mapping, and SLAM. *(1.5 marks)*

**Four desirable properties:**
1. **Distinctiveness** — unique signature enabling reliable matching.
2. **Invariance** — robust to scale, rotation, illumination, and viewpoint changes.
3. **Repeatability** — reliably detected under varying conditions and time.
4. **Compactness / Locality** — low-dimensional descriptor robust to occlusion. *(1.5 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBRT751]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between a **keypoint** and a **descriptor** in feature extraction.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **keypoint** is the *spatial location* $(\mathbf{p} = (x, y))$ in the sensor data where a salient structure (corner, blob, edge junction) has been detected; it answers the question *“where is the feature?”* *(1.5 marks)*

A **descriptor** is the *numerical fingerprint* $\mathbf{d} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ computed from the local neighborhood of the keypoint; it encodes the local appearance and answers the question *“what does the feature look like?”* *(1.5 marks)*

The keypoint provides geometric location, while the descriptor provides appearance information for matching.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBRT751 Module 1]**
>
> **QUESTION A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete feature extraction pipeline used in robot navigation. List the major stages and state the purpose of each. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Derive the Harris corner detector response from the structure tensor and explain how the eigenvalues classify image regions into flat, edge, and corner types. *(7 marks)*

#### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

**Part (a) — Feature Extraction Pipeline (7 Marks)**

The complete feature extraction pipeline consists of the following sequential stages, each serving a distinct purpose:

1. **Sensor Data Acquisition:** Raw data $\mathcal{S}$ is captured from cameras, LiDAR, IMU, or sonar sensors. *Purpose:* Provide the input observation from which features will be extracted.
2. **Pre-processing:** Noise removal, distortion correction, intensity normalization, and contrast equalization. *Purpose:* Stabilize the signal so that downstream detection is repeatable.
3. **Saliency / Response Map Computation:** A response function $R(\mathbf{x})$ is evaluated (e.g., Harris $R$, DoG $D$). *Purpose:* Quantify how distinctive each location is.
4. **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS):** Local maxima of $R$ are selected. *Purpose:* Suppress redundant detections and isolate strong keypoints.
5. **Sub-Pixel Refinement:** Taylor expansion fitting or quadratic interpolation locates $\mathbf{p}_i$ to sub-pixel accuracy. *Purpose:* Improve localization precision.
6. **Orientation Assignment:** A canonical orientation $\theta_i$ is computed (e.g., gradient histogram, intensity centroid). *Purpose:* Establish rotation invariance.
7. **Descriptor Generation:** A local patch is sampled and encoded into the descriptor vector $\mathbf{d}_i$. *Purpose:* Provide the appearance fingerprint.
8. **Matching:** Descriptor distances are computed and the ratio test applied. *Purpose:* Establish correspondences for navigation tasks.

*(Full marks awarded for naming all 8 stages, stating purposes, and providing a labeled block diagram — see SECTION 4.1.)*

**Part (b) — Harris Corner Derivation (7 Marks)**

Starting from the shifted intensity function:

$$
E(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \sum_{W} \big[ I(x+\Delta x, y+\Delta y) - I(x, y) \big]^2
$$

Apply a first-order Taylor expansion:

$$
I(x+\Delta x, y+\Delta y) \approx I(x,y) + I_x \Delta x + I_y \Delta y
$$

Substitute and simplify:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(\Delta x, \Delta y) &\approx \sum_{W} \big[ I_x \Delta x + I_y \Delta y \big]^2 \\
&= \sum_{W} \big[ (\Delta x)^2 I_x^2 + 2 \Delta x \Delta y I_x I_y + (\Delta y)^2 I_y^2 \big] \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x & \Delta y \end{bmatrix} \left( \sum_{W} \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix} \right) \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x & \Delta y \end{bmatrix} M \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $M$ is the structure tensor. The eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ of $M$ classify regions:

| Region Type | Eigenvalue Behavior | $R_{\text{Harris}}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Flat | $\lambda_1 \approx \lambda_2 \approx 0$ | $R \approx 0$ |
| Edge | One $\lambda$ large, other small | $R < 0$ |
| Corner | Both $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ large | $R \gg 0$ |

The Harris response avoids explicit eigendecomposition by using:

$$
R_{\text{Harris}} = \det(M) - k \cdot \mathrm{trace}(M)^2 = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2
$$

with $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$. *(Final marks for: complete derivation 4 marks, classification table 2 marks, final response formula 1 mark.)*

---

> **QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE):**
> **(a)** Explain the SIFT feature extraction algorithm in detail. Discuss its invariance properties and the role of the Difference-of-Gaussian (DoG) pyramid. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Define a feature descriptor formally. With a suitable diagram, describe the ORB feature extraction pipeline. Explain how rotational invariance is achieved. *(7 marks)*

#### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

**Part (a) — SIFT Algorithm (7 Marks)**

**SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform)** by Lowe (2004) extracts features that are invariant to scale, rotation, and partially to illumination and viewpoint.

**Stages:**

1. **Scale-Space Construction:** The image is convolved with Gaussians $G(\sigma)$ at multiple scales to build a scale pyramid.
2. **DoG Pyramid:** Adjacent Gaussian-blurred images are subtracted to form the Difference-of-Gaussian:
$$
D(x, y, \sigma) = L(x, y, k\sigma) - L(x, y, \sigma)
$$
The DoG approximates the Laplacian-of-Gaussian $\sigma^2 \nabla^2 L$ via the heat equation, enabling efficient scale-invariant keypoint localization. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Keypoint Localization:** Extrema of $D$ across 3D $(x, y, \sigma)$ are detected. Low-contrast candidates and edge responses are rejected. *(1 mark)*
4. **Orientation Assignment:** A 36-bin gradient orientation histogram is computed in a neighborhood. The dominant peak and any peak above 80% of it establish the canonical orientation(s). *(1 mark)*
5. **Descriptor Construction:** A 16×16 neighborhood around the keypoint is divided into 4×4 sub-blocks. Each sub-block produces an 8-bin gradient orientation histogram, yielding a **128-dimensional** descriptor. The descriptor is normalized to unit length to provide illumination invariance. *(1.5 marks)*
6. **Matching:** Euclidean distance between descriptors is used, with **Lowe’s ratio test** applied to filter ambiguous matches. *(1 mark)*

**Invariance properties:** Translation (via local detection), rotation (via canonical orientation), scale (via DoG pyramid), partial illumination (via gradient-based, normalized descriptor), and partial viewpoint (via coarse spatial binning). *(1 mark)*

**Part (b) — ORB Feature Pipeline (7 Marks)**

**Formal definition of a feature descriptor:** A feature descriptor is a function $D(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{p}) : \mathcal{S} \times \mathbb{R}^{2} \to \mathbb{R}^{n}$ that maps a keypoint location $\mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$ in a sensor observation $\mathcal{S}$ to an $n$-dimensional vector encoding the local appearance around $\mathbf{p}$. *(1 mark)*

**ORB = oFAST (oriented FAST) + rBRIEF (rotated BRIEF):**

1. **Keypoint Detection:** Apply FAST on the image. The intensity centroid method computes the moments:
$$
m_{pq} = \sum_{x,y \in B} x^p y^q \, I(x, y)
$$
The centroid is $C = (m_{10}/m_{00}, m_{01}/m_{00})$. The orientation is:
$$
\theta = \mathrm{atan2}(m_{01}, m_{10})
$$
This provides rotational invariance. *(2 marks)*
2. **BRIEF Descriptor (Base):** A set of $n$ binary tests $\tau$ on pixel pairs $(x_a, x_b)$ within the patch:
$$
\tau(\mathbf{p}; x_a, x_b) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } I(x_a) < I(x_b) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$
This produces an $n$-bit binary string. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Steered BRIEF (Rotational Invariance):** The $n$ pixel pairs are rotated by the patch's orientation $\theta$, forming a matrix $R(\theta)$ applied to the pair coordinates. The steered descriptor is:
$$
\mathbf{d}_{\text{ORB}} = R(\theta) \, \mathbf{d}_{\text{BRIEF}}
$$
*(1.5 marks)*
4. **Matching:** Hamming distance is used since descriptors are binary. *(1 mark)*

**Key advantages of ORB:** real-time speed (orders of magnitude faster than SIFT), compact 32-byte descriptor, and full rotation invariance — making it ideal for embedded robot navigation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing keypoint with descriptor:** Examiners explicitly deduct 1–2 marks if you interchange the definitions.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of orientation:** Many students describe SIFT/ORB but omit *why* orientation is assigned (to make descriptors rotation-invariant). This costs ~2 marks.
> 3. **No numerical example:** A question worth 7 marks always expects at least one worked numerical computation (e.g., Harris $R$ value, distance metric, descriptor size). Pure theory without numbers is capped at ~5 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up invariant hierarchy:** Translation ⊂ Rotation ⊂ Scale ⊂ Affine ⊂ Projective. Confusing the order is a frequent error.
> 5. **Wrong ratio threshold value:** Remember **T ≈ 0.6 to 0.8** for Lowe’s ratio test. Writing 0.5 or 0.9 is penalized.
> 6. **Using vertical pipes** `\vert x \vert` in tables breaks KTU's answer-book scanning; prefer the **horizontal bar** or `|x|` outside tables.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Re-read Before Exam**

- **Feature:** salient, measurable, distinct attribute of sensor data; answers *"where"* and *"what"* uniquely.
- **Two-stage decomposition:** **Keypoint detection** (localization) + **Descriptor computation** (appearance).
- **Formal mapping:** $f: \mathcal{S} \to \mathbb{R}^{n}$.
- **5 Desiderata:** distinctiveness, invariance, repeatability, compactness, locality.
- **Harris Response:** $R = \det(M) - k \cdot \mathrm{trace}(M)^2$ with $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$; positive → corner, negative → edge, ~0 → flat.
- **SIFT:** DoG pyramid; 128-D descriptor; scale + rotation invariant; Euclidean matching + Lowe's ratio test.
- **ORB:** oFAST + steered BRIEF; 32-byte binary descriptor; rotation via intensity centroid moment; Hamming distance matching.
- **FAST:** Bresenham circle of 16 pixels; $\geq 12$ contiguous pixels brighter/darker → corner; very fast but no scale invariance.
- **Invariance hierarchy:** Translation → Rotation → Scale → Affine → Projective → Illumination.
- **Lowe's ratio test threshold:** $T \approx 0.6$ – $0.8$.
- **Distance metrics:** $L_2$ for float (SIFT/SURF), Hamming for binary (ORB/BRIEF/BRISK).
- **Topological roles in navigation:** Localization, SLAM, Loop Closure, Data Association, Place Recognition.
- **Exam numerical hook:** Always practice computing $R_{\text{Harris}}$ from a given $2 \times 2$ structure tensor; this is a guaranteed 2-mark question in Part A.
- **Diagram expectation:** Always draw a labeled block diagram of the feature extraction pipeline — examiners award 1–2 marks just for a clean diagram.
- **Sensor mapping:** Camera → point/texture features; LiDAR → geometric features; Sonar → blob features; IMU → statistical features.
- **Real-world examples to cite in answers:** Autonomous cars (Tesla, Waymo), drone SLAM (DJI Mars drone), Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance), warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Target Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Target Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robotic Navigation**, the **Target Environment** refers to the structured or unstructured physical, perceptual, and operational space within which a mobile robot is required to perform its navigation tasks. It encapsulates every external factor that influences the robot's perception, decision-making, and motion — including the geometry of the workspace, the type of obstacles, the nature of landmarks, the dynamics of moving agents, and the degree of prior knowledge available about the surroundings.

According to the **Russell & Norvig PEAS framework** (Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors) — which is the standard reference for classifying robotic agents in KTU 2024 syllabus — the Environment is the second of the four descriptors that fully specify a robotic agent's task definition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 1):**
> The Target Environment is the foundation upon which every subsequent navigation strategy (path planning, localization, SLAM, obstacle avoidance) is built. Misclassifying the environment leads to choosing an incompatible algorithm.

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Environment Description (PEAS)

The target environment is fully characterized using the **PEAS descriptor**:

| Pillar | Full Form | What it Defines for Navigation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **P** | Performance Measure | Goal attainment metric (e.g., reach target in minimum time, minimum energy, collision-free). |
| **E** | Environment | The physical world the robot inhabits (indoor, outdoor, structured, unstructured). |
| **A** | Actuators | The drive system (DC motors, stepper motors, omni-wheels, manipulators). |
| **E (cont.)** | Sensors | Perception hardware (LIDAR, sonar, IMU, GPS, cameras). |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Delivery Robot in a Hospital"

Imagine a small wheeled robot delivering medicines inside a multi-floor hospital:

- **Structured (known) parts** of the hospital are the corridors, lift lobbies, and numbered rooms — the robot has a **map** and knows where walls are.
- **Unstructured (unknown) parts** are the patients walking in corridors, temporarily parked trolleys, or a fallen signboard — the robot has never seen these before and must react in **real time**.
- The **sensors** (LIDAR + cameras) are the robot's "eyes," the **actuators** (motors + wheels) are its "legs," and the **performance measure** is "deliver medicine to Room 304 within 5 minutes without bumping into anything."

This mental model — *known + unknown, static + dynamic* — is exactly what the **Target Environment** encapsulates in robotic terms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> **Target Environment** = The complete specification of the operating space of a robot, including its geometric structure, dynamic elements, observability, determinism, and the level of prior knowledge available to the navigation system.

## 1.4 Why "Target Environment" Matters in KTU Examinations

- It is the **first concept** in Module 1 — every later module (Localization, Path Planning, SLAM) presumes you understand the environment properties.
- Examiner frequently tests the **classification of environments** using the **2×2×2×2 taxonomy** (8 combinations).
- Direct questions appear in **Part A (3 marks)** almost every semester.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Canonical Properties of a Target Environment

The Target Environment is classified along **four orthogonal axes**, producing $2^4 = 8$ canonical environment types. These are the **highest-weightage properties** for KTU 2024 board questions.

### Axis 1 — Observability (Accessible vs. Inaccessible / Partially Observable)

- **Fully Observable:** The robot's sensors capture the **complete state** of the environment at every time instant.
  - *Example:* A chess-playing robot arm sees the entire board.
  - *Navigation example:* A robot inside a small glass chamber with overhead cameras tracking every object.

- **Partially Observable:** Due to sensor limitations, noise, or occlusion, the robot can perceive only a **subset** of the world state.
  - *Example:* A vacuum-cleaning robot with a forward-facing LIDAR cannot see behind walls.
  - *This is the most common real-world case for mobile robots.*

- **Unobservable:** The agent has **no sensors** at all (rare, mostly theoretical).

### Axis 2 — Determinism (Deterministic vs. Stochastic / Non-Deterministic)

- **Deterministic:** The next state of the environment is **completely determined** by the current state and the robot's action. There is no randomness.
  - *Example:* A robot arm assembling a known mechanical part on a fixed jig.

- **Stochastic / Non-Deterministic:** The same action from the same state can lead to **different next states** with some probability distribution.
  - *Example:* A self-driving car on a wet road — applying the same brake force can produce slightly different stopping distances.
  - The uncertainty is captured mathematically using **probability** $P(s' \mid s, a)$.

### Axis 3 — Episodic vs. Sequential

- **Episodic:** Each perception-action cycle is **independent**. The robot's decision in episode $t$ does not depend on previous episodes.
  - *Example:* A quality-inspection camera classifying each manufactured chip independently.

- **Sequential:** The current decision **affects all future decisions**.
  - *Example:* A maze-solving robot — a wrong turn at step 10 affects the entire subsequent path.
  - *Navigation tasks are almost always sequential.*

### Axis 4 — Static vs. Dynamic

- **Static:** The environment **does not change** while the robot is deliberating (except by the robot's own actions).
  - *Example:* A pre-mapped warehouse with fixed shelving.

- **Dynamic:** Other agents or phenomena **change the environment** while the robot is deliberating.
  - *Example:* A busy pedestrian crosswalk.
  - **Semidynamic:** The environment itself is static, but its **performance measure changes with time** (e.g., a delivery deadline is approaching).

### Additional Properties (Frequently tested)

- **Discrete vs. Continuous:**
  - *Discrete:* Finite / countable states (e.g., a robot moving on a grid of cells).
  - *Continuous:* Infinite real-valued states (e.g., $x, y, \theta \in \mathbb{R}$ for a mobile robot's pose).
- **Known vs. Unknown:**
  - *Known:* A prior map is available.
  - *Unknown:* The robot must build the map while navigating (**SLAM scenario**).
- **Single-agent vs. Multi-agent:**
  - *Single:* Only the robot is making decisions.
  - *Multi:* Other intelligent agents are present (e.g., a robot sharing space with humans).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Property — The Logic Chain

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Logic Test:** If a student explains **why** a navigation environment is *partially observable* and *stochastic* (e.g., "sensor noise + moving obstacles"), the answer receives **full marks (3/3)** instead of partial credit (2/3).

The logic chain for a typical KTU answer is:

1. **State what property the environment has** (e.g., partially observable).
2. **Why** — cite a real cause (sensor range, occlusion, noise).
3. **Consequence** — explain what navigation strategy is forced (e.g., probabilistic localization, Kalman filter).
4. **Example** — ground it in a real robot (e.g., TurtleBot in a corridor).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Symbol / Equation | Description / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Robot Pose (2D) | $p = (x, y, \theta)$ | Position $(x, y)$ in metres + heading $\theta$ in radians. |
| 2 | Environment State | $s \in S$ | $S$ is the state space (continuous $\mathbb{R}^n$ or discrete). |
| 3 | Transition Function | $s' = f(s, a)$ | Deterministic case: next state = function of current state + action. |
| 4 | Stochastic Transition | $P(s' \mid s, a)$ | Probability of reaching $s'$ from $s$ via action $a$. |
| 5 | Sensor Observation | $z = h(s)$ or $P(z \mid s)$ | Deterministic / probabilistic observation model. |
| 6 | Belief State (Partially Observable) | $b(s) = P(s \mid z_{1:t}, a_{1:t-1})$ | Posterior probability distribution over states. |
| 7 | Grid Map Cell | $m_{i,j} \in \{0, 1\}$ | $0$ = free, $1$ = occupied (occupancy grid). |
| 8 | Continuous Workspace | $W \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ or $\mathbb{R}^3$ | The physical region the robot operates in. |
| 9 | Free Space | $W_{\text{free}} = W \setminus W_{\text{obs}}$ | Workspace minus obstacle region $W_{\text{obs}}$. |
| 10 | Sensor Range | $r_{\text{sensor}}$ | Maximum reliable perception distance (metres). |
| 11 | Time Step | $\Delta t$ | Discrete interval between control updates (seconds). |
| 12 | Odometry Uncertainty | $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_\theta$ | Standard deviations of pose estimate noise. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Reminder for KTU:**
> - Angles: **radians** in formulas, but can be expressed in degrees when answering.
> - Distances: **metres (m)**.
> - Probabilities: **dimensionless**, range $\vert P \vert \le 1$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the target environment is **not academic** — it directly drives engineering decisions:

- **Algorithm selection:** Static + Fully Observable → A* search suffices. Dynamic + Partially Observable → D* Lite or Monte Carlo Localization.
- **Sensor choice:** Outdoor + Unknown → GPS + LIDAR. Indoor + Known → wheel odometry + ultrasonic.
- **Safety certification:** Industrial robots in **structured, deterministic, static** environments need lighter safety standards than **dynamic, multi-agent** service robots.
- **Compute hardware:** A continuous-state, stochastic environment demands GPU-accelerated particle filters; a discrete deterministic one can run on an 8-bit microcontroller.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Belief Update in a Partially Observable Environment

This is the **central mathematical derivation** for a partially observable target environment and frequently appears in **Part B (14-mark) questions**.

### 3.1.1 Setup

A mobile robot operates in a **partially observable, stochastic, sequential** environment — the most realistic case. We want to compute the **belief** (posterior probability) over the robot's state $s_t$ at time $t$, given the entire history of observations $z_{1:t}$ and actions $a_{1:t-1}$.

By the definition of conditional probability:

$$
b(s_t) = P(s_t \mid z_{1:t}, a_{1:t-1})
$$

This is the **belief state** — it summarizes all information the robot has about where it is.

### 3.1.2 Applying Bayes' Theorem

Using Bayes' theorem, the posterior is proportional to the **likelihood** $\times$ **prior**:

$$
P(s_t \mid z_{1:t}, a_{1:t-1}) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid s_t, a_{1:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}) \cdot P(s_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, a_{1:t-1})
$$

where $\eta = \dfrac{1}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, a_{1:t-1})}$ is the normalizer.

### 3.1.3 Markov Assumption

We invoke the **Markov assumption** — the current state $s_t$ is sufficient to predict the next observation; older history is irrelevant:

$$
P(z_t \mid s_t, a_{1:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}) = P(z_t \mid s_t)
$$

Substituting back:

$$
b(s_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid s_t) \cdot P(s_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, a_{1:t-1})
$$

### 3.1.4 Prediction Step (Action Update)

We now marginalize over all possible previous states $s_{t-1}$ to predict $s_t$ from the prior belief and the action:

$$
P(s_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, a_{1:t-1}) = \int P(s_t \mid s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}) \cdot b(s_{t-1}) \, ds_{t-1}
$$

This integral is the **prediction step**. The term $P(s_t \mid s_{t-1}, a_{t-1})$ is the **motion model** (transition probability).

### 3.1.5 Final Recursive Belief Update Equation

Combining the two steps into a single recursive form — the **Bayes filter**, foundational to localization in navigation:

$$
b(s_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid s_t) \cdot \int P(s_t \mid s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}) \cdot b(s_{t-1}) \, ds_{t-1}
$$

For **discrete** state spaces, the integral becomes a **summation**:

$$
b(s_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid s_t) \cdot \sum_{s_{t-1}} P(s_t \mid s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}) \cdot b(s_{t-1})
$$

### 3.1.6 Interpretation

- The **motion model** $P(s_t \mid s_{t-1}, a_{t-1})$ is **environmental noise** (wheel slip, drift).
- The **sensor model** $P(z_t \mid s_t)$ is **measurement noise** (LIDAR error, camera blur).
- The recursion is the mathematical reason why **Kalman Filters**, **Particle Filters**, and **Histogram Filters** are the standard tools for robot localization in any non-trivial target environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:**
> In the 14-mark Part B question, writing the **belief recursion** above and labelling each term (motion model, sensor model, normalizer) typically fetches **9–10 marks** out of 14. The remaining marks go to the **example mapping** (e.g., which filter for which environment).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Environment Property × Algorithm Mapping

This matrix is a **favourite KTU question** ("Discuss the choice of algorithm based on environment properties" — 7 marks).

| Environment Class | Static / Dynamic | Observable | Deterministic | Recommended Algorithm |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Structured Indoor** | Static | Fully | Deterministic | A* / Dijkstra on known grid. |
| **Warehouse (Dynamic)** | Dynamic (people) | Partially | Stochastic | D* Lite + Particle Filter. |
| **Outdoor Unknown** | Dynamic (vehicles) | Partially | Stochastic | SLAM (EKF-SLAM or Graph-SLAM). |
| **Maze Solving** | Static | Partially | Deterministic | Wall-following / BFS / DFS. |
| **Self-Driving Car** | Highly Dynamic | Partially | Stochastic | POMDP + Deep RL. |
| **Industrial Manipulator** | Static | Fully | Deterministic | Forward / Inverse Kinematics. |
| **Planetary Rover** | Static (mostly) | Partially | Stochastic | Visual Odometry + SLAM. |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Occupancy Grid Mapping

A 2D grid cell $m_{i,j}$ is updated using the **log-odds** form of Bayes:

$$
l_{t}(m_{i,j}) = l_{t-1}(m_{i,j}) + l_{\text{sensor}}(m_{i,j}) - l_{0}
$$

where $l = \log \dfrac{P(m=1)}{P(m=0)}$ is the **log-odds** representation.

If a sensor reports the cell as occupied with $P(\text{occupied} \mid z) = 0.8$, then $l_{\text{sensor}} = \log \dfrac{0.8}{0.2} \approx 1.386$.

The posterior occupancy probability becomes:

$$
P(m_{i,j}=1) = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{l_{t}(m_{i,j})}}
$$

This is the **mathematical heart** of how a robot incrementally builds a map of the target environment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Target Environments (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Target Environment] --> B[Observability]
    A --> C[Determinism]
    A --> D[Temporal Structure]
    A --> E[Dynamics]

    B --> B1[Fully Observable]
    B --> B2[Partially Observable]
    B --> B3[Unobservable]

    C --> C1[Deterministic]
    C --> C2[Stochastic]

    D --> D1[Episodic]
    D --> D2[Sequential]

    E --> E1[Static]
    E --> E2[Dynamic]
    E --> E3[Semidynamic]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — How the Robot Interacts with its Target Environment

```mermaid
graph LR
    TE[Target Environment] -->|Sensor Data z| SM[Sensor Module]
    SM -->|Noisy Observation| PR[Perception and State Estimator]
    PR -->|Belief b of s| PL[Planner Module]
    PL -->|Action a| AC[Actuator Module]
    AC -->|Motion| TE
    PR -->|Map Update m| WM[World Model Database]
    WM -->|Prior Knowledge| PL
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow for Algorithm Selection in Navigation

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Is the environment known?} -->|Yes| Q2{Is it static?}
    Q1 -->|No| SLAM[Use SLAM]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Is it fully observable?}
    Q2 -->|No| DStar[Use D Star Lite]
    Q3 -->|Yes| AStar[Use A Star]
    Q3 -->|No| KFilter[Use Kalman or Particle Filter with A Star]
    SLAM --> EKFSLAM[EKF SLAM or Graph SLAM]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Belief Update Loop (Recursion Visual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    bt1[Belief at t minus 1] -->|Motion Model P of s t given s t minus 1, a t minus 1| PRED[Prediction Step]
    PRED -->|Predicted Belief| CORR[Correction Step]
    OBS[New Observation z t] -->|Sensor Model P of z t given s t| CORR
    CORR -->|Normalized Posterior| bt[Belief at t]
    bt -->|Feeds Next Cycle| bt1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for the Mermaid Diagrams:**
> - The first diagram is the **canonical taxonomy** — memorize it.
> - The second diagram is the **closed-loop perception-action architecture** — the "loop" is the heart of any autonomous navigation system.
> - The third diagram is a **decision tree** you can reproduce in a 7-mark answer.
> - The fourth diagram visually represents the **Bayes filter recursion** derived in Section 3.1.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Classify the environment of a Mars exploration rover with respect to observability, determinism, episodic/sequential nature, and static/dynamic behaviour. Justify each classification with a one-line reason.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Mars exploration rover operates in an environment that is:

1. **Partially observable (1 mark):** Onboard sensors (stereo cameras, IMU) have limited range; the rover cannot see behind rocks or over crater rims in a single instant.

2. **Stochastic / Non-deterministic (1 mark):** Wheel slip on loose regolith, sand accumulation, and sensor noise make the same command produce slightly different outcomes.

3. **Sequential (0.5 marks):** Each navigation decision affects subsequent ones — a wrong path in dune navigation cannot be undone easily.

4. **Static (0.5 marks):** The Martian surface does not change during the rover's deliberation (no moving agents), though the rover is itself semi-dynamic due to power/thermal constraints.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "static" for Mars and lose 0.5 marks because Mars does experience dust devils and wind — strictly speaking, it is *mostly static*, but for KTU, "static" is accepted with a one-line justification.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between episodic and sequential environments. Give one navigation example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Episodic | Sequential |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition (1 mark)** | Each perception-action cycle is independent of previous ones. | Current action influences all future decisions. |
| **Dependence on history** | None. | Full history matters. |
| **Navigation Example (1 mark each)** | A quality-control robot inspecting each PCB on a conveyor — the inspection of PCB #5 does not depend on the inspection of PCB #1. | A maze-solving robot — a turn made at junction 1 directly affects the reachable junctions later. |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]` (14 Marks)

**a)** With the help of the **PEAS framework**, define the target environment for a **floor-cleaning robot** used in a hotel lobby. Clearly state the **Performance Measure, Environment, Actuators, and Sensors**. (7 marks)

**b)** The same robot is later deployed in a **shopping mall** during peak hours. Discuss how the **four canonical properties** (observability, determinism, episodic/sequential, static/dynamic) of its target environment change, and explain the **algorithmic implications** of each change. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**PEAS Descriptor for a Floor-Cleaning Robot in a Hotel Lobby:**

| Pillar | Specification for Hotel Lobby (1.5 marks) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Performance Measure (P)** | Percentage of floor area covered, cleaning quality (dust removal), time taken, battery consumed, number of collisions, customer disturbance index. |
| **Environment (E)** | Indoor, structured (furniture in fixed positions), partially observable (LIDAR has limited range), mostly static (only guests walking), continuous 2D workspace $W \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$, known hotel floor plan. |
| **Actuators (A)** | Two differential-drive wheels (DC geared motors), one or two side brushes, vacuum suction motor, bumper. |
| **Sensors (S)** | 2D LIDAR for mapping, wheel encoders for odometry, cliff sensors (IR), bump sensor, IMU for orientation, optional camera for obstacle classification. |

**Key Point for Full Marks (1 mark):** The robot uses an **occupancy grid** internally because the environment is **known and structured**, so a static $m_{i,j}$ map suffices.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Change of properties when deployed in a shopping mall at peak hours:**

| Property | Hotel Lobby | Shopping Mall Peak Hours | Algorithmic Implication (1 mark per row + 0.5 for explanation) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Observability** | Mostly fully observable (no crowds, fixed furniture). | **Partially observable** — crowds occlude LIDAR returns. | Need **probabilistic sensor model** $P(z \mid s)$; particle filter. |
| **Determinism** | Mostly deterministic. | **Stochastic** — pedestrian paths are unpredictable. | Use **D* Lite** or **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** for replanning. |
| **Sequential / Episodic** | Sequential (path planning). | **Sequential + time-critical** — must reason over time. | Use **time-parameterized trajectories**; possibly POMDP. |
| **Static / Dynamic** | Static (furniture fixed). | **Highly dynamic** — moving people and carts. | Add **social-force models** or **deep RL** policies. |

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):** A robot designed for a structured, static lobby is algorithmically inadequate for a dynamic mall. The same physical hardware must be paired with a more sophisticated **navigation stack** (e.g., ROS Navigation2 with dynamic reconfiguration).

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**a)** Derive the **Bayes filter recursion** for belief update in a partially observable target environment. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 marks)

**b)** Compare the **Kalman Filter, Extended Kalman Filter, and Particle Filter** as belief-update implementations for the following target environments: (i) Indoor linear 2D corridor, (ii) Non-linear 2D warehouse with beacons, (iii) Highly non-linear outdoor terrain with LIDAR. Justify each choice. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define the belief state (1 mark):**

$$
b(s_t) = P(s_t \mid z_{1:t}, a_{1:t-1})
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' theorem (1 mark):**

$$
b(s_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid s_t, a_{1:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}) \cdot P(s_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, a_{1:t-1})
$$

**Step 3 — Markov assumption (1 mark):**

$$
P(z_t \mid s_t, a_{1:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}) = P(z_t \mid s_t)
$$

**Step 4 — Prediction step via marginalization (2 marks):**

$$
P(s_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, a_{1:t-1}) = \int P(s_t \mid s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}) \cdot b(s_{t-1}) \, ds_{t-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Final recursion (1 mark):**

$$
b(s_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid s_t) \cdot \int P(s_t \mid s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}) \cdot b(s_{t-1}) \, ds_{t-1}
$$

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Markov property (state is sufficient summary of history).
- Known motion model $P(s_t \mid s_{t-1}, a_{t-1})$.
- Known sensor model $P(z_t \mid s_t)$.
- Discrete-time control and observation.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

| Environment | Belief Filter Choice | Justification (≈2 marks each) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Indoor linear 2D corridor** | **Kalman Filter (KF)** | Linear motion (constant velocity), linear LIDAR range model, Gaussian noise. KF is **optimal**, low compute, closed-form. |
| **(ii) Non-linear 2D warehouse with beacons** | **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** | Range-bearing to beacons is non-linear ($h(s) = \sqrt{(x-x_b)^2 + (y-y_b)^2}$). EKF linearizes via Jacobian $H = \partial h / \partial s$. Still Gaussian assumption. |
| **(iii) Highly non-linear outdoor terrain with LIDAR** | **Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization)** | Multi-modal belief (e.g., robot may be in two similar valleys). Non-Gaussian posterior. Particle filter represents arbitrary distributions with weighted samples. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The choice of belief-update algorithm is **driven entirely by the target environment's properties** — linearity, Gaussianity, and multimodality.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing "known" with "observable":** A known environment (a prior map exists) is **not** the same as a fully observable one (sensors see everything now). Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** When writing formulas like $b(s_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid s_t) \cdot (\cdots)$, students often write the **normalizer** $\eta$ but forget to mention it ensures the belief sums/integrates to $1$.
> 3. **Static ≠ Deterministic:** Static means the world does not change; deterministic means the action produces a unique next state. A static environment can still be stochastic (noisy sensors).
> 4. **In Bayes filter derivation,** skipping the Markov assumption costs 1 mark.
> 5. **For diagrams,** unlabelled arrows in a closed-loop architecture cost 1 mark.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Target Environment**

- [ ] **PEAS** framework is the canonical descriptor — always use it as the first step in any 14-mark answer.
- [ ] **Four orthogonal axes** of environment classification: Observability, Determinism, Episodic/Sequential, Static/Dynamic → $2^4 = 8$ canonical types.
- [ ] **Most navigation environments are** *partially observable, stochastic, sequential, dynamic* — the hardest case.
- [ ] **Bayes filter recursion** is the mathematical backbone: $b(s_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid s_t) \cdot \int P(s_t \mid s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}) \cdot b(s_{t-1}) \, ds_{t-1}$.
- [ ] **Markov assumption** must always be stated when deriving the Bayes filter.
- [ ] **Continuous workspace** $W \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ (2D) or $\mathbb{R}^3$ (3D) for mobile robots.
- [ ] **Occupancy grid** uses log-odds: $l_t(m) = l_{t-1}(m) + l_{\text{sensor}}(m) - l_0$.
- [ ] **Known ≠ Observable, Static ≠ Deterministic, Sequential ≠ Dynamic** — these are common confusion pairs.
- [ ] **Filter choice** is environment-driven: linear + Gaussian → KF; mildly non-linear → EKF; multi-modal / highly non-linear → Particle Filter.
- [ ] **PEAS table** is the **highest-yield** 7-mark content in any 14-mark question — write it cleanly with 4 columns.
- [ ] **Robot pose** is the 3-tuple $p = (x, y, \theta)$ — remember to include the **heading angle** $\theta$ in radians.
- [ ] **Probability notation** for KTU: use $P(s' \mid s, a)$ — never forget the conditional bar.
- [ ] **Indoor + structured + static** → A*; **Outdoor + unknown + dynamic** → SLAM with particle filter.
- [ ] **Probabilities sum to 1**; **log-odds are dimensionless**; **distances in metres**; **angles in radians** (or degrees if clearly stated).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environment Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Environment Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environment Representation** in mobile robotics is the formal, mathematical encoding of the robot's operating workspace (the "world model") into a data structure that the navigation, localization, and planning algorithms can query, update, and reason about in real time.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PEBRT751 – Robot Navigation**, environment representation forms the foundational layer of the perception–decision–action pipeline. The robot translates raw streams from exteroceptive sensors (LIDAR, sonar, RGB-D cameras) and proprioceptive sensors (wheel odometry, IMU) into a persistent, spatially indexed model.

The representation must satisfy four engineering constraints simultaneously:
- **Completeness** — every navigable region and obstacle is encoded.
- **Consistency** — repeated observations yield stable estimates.
- **Compactness** — the map fits in on-board memory (often **< 1 GB** for indoor robots).
- **Computational Tractability** — updates must occur at the sensor frame rate (**10–40 Hz** for 2D LIDAR).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Tourist Map

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a tourist arriving in a new city with two map options on their phone.
> - **Option A (Metric Map):** A scaled, geographically accurate street map. Every road, building, and bench has a precise GPS coordinate. Useful for calculating exact distances.
> - **Option B (Topological Map):** A simplified subway diagram. Stations are nodes, connections are edges. The tourist does not need exact coordinates — only the sequence of transfers.
>
> A robot similarly has a "scaled map" (occupancy grid) and a "subway diagram" (topological graph) at its disposal. The choice depends on the task: precise path tracking vs. global route planning.

## 1.3 The World Model Pipeline

$$
\text{Raw Sensor Data} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Feature Extraction} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Map Update} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{World Model}
$$

The **World Model** is the live, in-memory data structure consumed by the localization filter (e.g., EKF, Particle Filter) and the path planner (e.g., A*, D* Lite, RRT).

## 1.4 Why Environment Representation Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of PEBRT751 explicitly establishes that **no navigation algorithm can function without a prior or concurrently built environment representation.** Path planning, obstacle avoidance, and SLAM are all conditional on this representation.

The selected representation directly dictates:
- Sensor fusion strategies.
- The complexity of localization (a sparse landmark map makes EKF tractable; a dense grid makes Particle Filters necessary).
- Memory footprint and CPU load on the embedded board.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Environment Representations

Mobile robotics literature (and the KTU 2024 syllabus) classifies environment representations along three orthogonal axes:

### A. By Spatial Dimensionality
- **2D Representations** — Floor-plan style, $x$–$y$ plane. Standard for indoor service robots.
- **2.5D Representations** — 2D grid with a height/elevation value per cell (e.g., for ramps).
- **3D Representations** — Voxel grids, OctoMaps, point clouds. Used in UAVs and autonomous vehicles.

### B. By Geometric vs. Topological
- **Geometric (Metric) Representations:** Preserve metric distances. Examples: occupancy grids, feature maps, point clouds.
- **Topological Representations:** Preserve connectivity. The world is a graph $G(V, E)$ where $V$ are distinctive places and $E$ are traversable paths.
- **Hybrid (Metric-Topological) Representations:** Combine local metric accuracy with global topological structure.

### C. By Spatial Discretization
- **Continuous:** Raw point clouds, landmark coordinate lists.
- **Discrete:** Uniform grids, quad-trees, Voronoi diagrams.

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Representation Type

### 2.2.1 Occupancy Grid Map (Metric, Discrete, 2D)
- The world is discretized into a regular lattice of $m \times n$ cells.
- Each cell stores a probability $p(m_i)$ of being **occupied**.
- Pioneered by **Moravec and Elfes (1985)** — the canonical KTU reference.
- Resolution $\Delta$ (cell side length) is a critical design parameter.

### 2.2.2 Feature (Landmark) Map
- The world is a sparse set of distinguishable landmarks, each with Cartesian coordinates and a unique ID.
- Used heavily in **EKF-SLAM** and **FastSLAM**.
- Landmark covariance matrices are stored to model measurement uncertainty.

### 2.2.3 Topological Map
- Nodes = distinctive places (rooms, intersections). Edges = navigable connections.
- Edge weights encode traversability cost or distance.
- Excellent for **global path planning** in large warehouses.

### 2.2.4 Voronoi Diagram
- The set of points equidistant from two or more obstacles.
- Maximizes clearance from obstacles → ideal for safe path planning.
- Computed via Fortune's algorithm in $O(n \log n)$ time, where $n$ is the number of obstacle sites.

### 2.2.5 Quad-Tree and OctoMap
- Hierarchical, memory-efficient spatial decompositions.
- A **Quad-Tree** recursively subdivides a 2D square into four equal quadrants.
- An **OctoMap** extends this to 3D (eight sub-volumes per node).
- Memory savings can reach **80–90%** versus dense grids for sparse environments.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Occupancy Probability | $p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ | $m_i$ = cell $i$, $z$ = measurement, $x$ = pose | Core of probabilistic grids |
| 2 | Bayes Update for Grid | $p(m_i \mid z_t) = \dfrac{p(z_t \mid m_i)\, p(m_i)}{p(z_t)}$ | Conditional probability | Sensor fusion per cell |
| 3 | Log-Odds Form | $\ell_i = \ln \dfrac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Numerical stability |
| 4 | Log-Odds Update | $\ell_{i,t} = \ell_{i,t-1} + \ell_{\text{sensor model}}$ | Additive update | Fast grid updates |
| 5 | Grid Resolution vs. Memory | $M = \dfrac{A_{\text{env}}}{\Delta^2}$ | $M$ = cells, $A$ = area in m², $\Delta$ in m | Memory budgeting |
| 6 | Voronoi Distance | $d_{\text{vor}}(p) = \min_j \;\vert p - o_j \vert$ | $o_j$ = obstacle site | Safe path clearance |
| 7 | Quad-Tree Depth Limit | $d_{\max} = \lceil \log_4 (N_{\text{leaf}}) \rceil$ | $N_{\text{leaf}}$ = terminal cells | Tree balance |
| 8 | Topological Edge Weight | $w_e = \alpha L_e + \beta C_e + \gamma T_e$ | $L$=length, $C$=cost, $T$=traffic | Graph search |
| 9 | Landmark Covariance Update | $\Sigma_{L,t} = \Sigma_{L,t-1} - K (\cdot) K^{\top}$ | Kalman correction | EKF-SLAM |
| 10 | Octree Volume Savings | $S_{\text{oct}} = 1 - \dfrac{N_{\text{alloc}}}{N_{\text{max}}}$ | Fractional saving | 3D memory planning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The log-odds formulation (Row 4) is the de-facto standard in ROS (`nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`) because additive updates avoid the floating-point underflow that plagues direct probability multiplication.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Use hybrid 3D point clouds + HD topological lane graphs.
- **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva):** Rely primarily on 2D occupancy grids with $\Delta = 0.05$ m.
- **UAV Swarms (DJI, Skydio):** Deploy OctoMaps for 3D obstacle avoidance.
- **Domestic Vacuum Robots (iRobot Roomba):** Use coarse topological maps for room segmentation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Occupancy Probability via Bayes' Theorem

**Goal:** Derive the recursive update rule for a single cell $m_i$ in an occupancy grid given a new sensor measurement $z_t$ and the robot pose $x_t$.

### Step 1 — Posterior Setup
We seek the posterior probability that cell $m_i$ is occupied, given the entire history of measurements $z_{1:t}$ and robot poses $x_{1:t}$:

$$
p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})
$$

By the **Markov assumption** (cell state depends only on the most recent measurement and prior), this becomes:

$$
p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) \propto p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Rule

$$
p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) = \dfrac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)\; p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
$$

The denominator is a normalizing constant independent of $m_i$, so it is absorbed into a normalization step after summation.

### Step 3 — Convert to Log-Odds
To avoid numerical underflow and to make the update **additive** (a KTU-favorite property), define the log-odds ratio $\ell$:

$$
\ell_i \;\triangleq\; \ln \dfrac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}
$$

where the denominator $1 - p(m_i)$ is the probability that the cell is **free**.

### Step 4 — Log-Odds Update Rule
Taking the logarithm of the Bayes expression and using the additive property of logarithms:

$$
\ell_{i,t} = \ell_{i,t-1} + \ln \dfrac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - \ell_{i,0}
$$

where $\ell_{i,0}$ is the prior log-odds (typically $0$, corresponding to $p = 0.5$, i.e., "unknown").

### Step 5 — Inverse Transform to Recover Probability
To recover the probability from the log-odds, apply the inverse logistic transform:

$$
p(m_i) = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp(\ell_i)}
$$

This compact derivation is the **backbone of every ROS-based mapping node**, including `gmapping` and `cartographer`.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Grid Memory vs. Resolution Trade-off

**Goal:** Show how the cell side length $\Delta$ governs the total memory footprint $M$ of an occupancy grid covering a rectangular area $A = L_x \times L_y$.

### Step 1 — Number of Cells Along Each Axis
The number of cells along the $x$-axis is $N_x = L_x / \Delta$, and along the $y$-axis is $N_y = L_y / \Delta$. Hence the total cell count is:

$$
M = N_x \cdot N_y = \dfrac{L_x L_y}{\Delta^2} = \dfrac{A}{\Delta^2}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Memory in Bytes
If each cell stores one byte (8-bit probability in standard ROS representation), total memory is:

$$
\text{Mem} = M \cdot 1\,\text{byte} = \dfrac{A}{\Delta^2} \;\text{bytes}
$$

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example
Suppose $A = 20\,\text{m} \times 30\,\text{m} = 600\,\text{m}^2$ and $\Delta = 0.05\,\text{m}$:

$$
M = \dfrac{600}{(0.05)^2} = \dfrac{600}{0.0025} = 240{,}000 \;\text{cells}
$$

$$
\text{Mem} = 240{,}000 \;\text{bytes} \approx 234.4 \;\text{kB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Halving the resolution (from $0.05$ m to $0.025$ m) **quadruples** memory consumption. KTU examiners frequently test this quadratic scaling behavior.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Voronoi Clearance Maximization

**Goal:** Demonstrate why the Voronoi diagram maximizes the minimum distance to obstacles — a property that makes it the preferred representation for safe navigation in cluttered environments.

### Step 1 — Definition
The Voronoi edge consists of all points $p$ equidistant from at least two obstacle sites $o_j$ and $o_k$:

$$
V = \{ p \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid \vert p - o_j \vert = \vert p - o_k \vert,\; j \neq k \}
$$

### Step 2 — Clearance Function
The clearance at point $p$ is defined as the minimum distance to any obstacle site:

$$
C(p) = \min_{j=1,\dots,n} \vert p - o_j \vert
$$

### Step 3 — Maximization
To prove that points on the Voronoi edge locally maximize $C(p)$, we take the gradient and set it to zero. The locus of points where the two nearest obstacles swap dominance corresponds exactly to the bisector — the Voronoi edge. Any infinitesimal motion away from this edge brings $p$ closer to one of the two nearest obstacles, reducing $C(p)$. Hence:

$$
C(p) \geq C(p + \delta p) \quad \forall\, \delta p \;\text{small}
$$

This is the **mathematical foundation of safety-first path planners** in industrial robotics.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Occupancy Grid | Feature Map | Topological Map | Voronoi | OctoMap |
|----------|----------------|-------------|------------------|---------|---------|
| **Memory Use** | High | Low | Very Low | Low | Medium (sparse) |
| **Localization** | Particle Filter | EKF / Graph-SLAM | Simple matching | Hybrid | 3D ICP / NDT |
| **Path Planning** | A*, D* | A* (sparse) | Dijkstra / BFS | A* on graph | RRT* in 3D |
| **Resolution Adaptivity** | Fixed | N/A | N/A | Adaptive (sites) | Adaptive (octree) |
| **Best For** | Indoor 2D SLAM | Outdoor / landmark-rich | Warehouse routing | Cluttered narrow | UAV / 3D |
| **Update Complexity** | $O(N)$ cells touched | $O(1)$ per landmark | $O(1)$ node insertion | $O(n \log n)$ build | $O(\log n)$ per voxel |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Environment Representations

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ENVIRONMENT REPRESENTATION] --> B[By Geometry]
    A --> C[By Dimensionality]
    A --> D[By Discretization]

    B --> B1[Metric or Geometric]
    B --> B2[Topological]
    B --> B3[Hybrid Metric Topological]

    B1 --> B11[Occupancy Grid]
    B1 --> B12[Feature Map]
    B1 --> B13[Point Cloud]
    B2 --> B21[Graph G V E]
    B3 --> B31[Local Metric plus Global Graph]

    C --> C1[2D]
    C --> C2[2 Point 5D]
    C --> C3[3D]

    C1 --> C11[Floor Plan Grid]
    C2 --> C21[Grid plus Elevation]
    C3 --> C31[Voxel Grid]
    C3 --> C32[OctoMap]

    D --> D1[Continuous]
    D --> D2[Discrete]

    D1 --> D11[Raw Point Cloud]
    D2 --> D21[Uniform Grid]
    D2 --> D22[Quad Tree]
    D2 --> D23[Voronoi Diagram]
```

## 4.2 Occupancy Grid Map Update Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Sensor Scan LIDAR or Sonar] --> P1[Pose Estimate from Odometry]
    P1 --> T1[Coordinate Transform to World Frame]
    S1 --> T1
    T1 --> R1[Ray Casting Through Grid Cells]
    R1 --> U1[Log Odds Update for Each Cell]
    U1 --> M1[Updated Occupancy Grid Map]
    M1 --> Q1[Query by Path Planner and Localizer]
    M1 --> L1[Persistent Storage in Map Server]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Navigation Stack

```mermaid
graph LR
    SENS[Sensor Layer LIDAR IMU Camera] --> PERC[Perception Module]
    ODOM[Wheel Odometry] --> LOC[Localization Module]
    PERC --> REP[Environment Representation]
    LOC --> REP
    REP --> PLAN[Global Path Planner A Star]
    REP --> LOCAL[Local Planner DWA]
    PLAN --> CTRL[Motion Controller]
    LOCAL --> CTRL
    CTRL --> ACT[Actuators Wheels Steer]
    ACT --> ENV[Physical Environment]
    ENV --> SENS
```

## 4.4 Quad-Tree Subdivision Schematic (Textual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Root Node Full Environment] --> N1[Northwest Quadrant]
    ROOT --> N2[Northeast Quadrant]
    ROOT --> N3[Southwest Quadrant]
    ROOT --> N4[Southeast Quadrant]

    N2 --> N2A[NE Sub NW]
    N2 --> N2B[NE Sub NE]
    N2 --> N2C[NE Sub SW]
    N2 --> N2D[NE Sub SE]

    N2B --> LEAF1[Leaf Uniformly Free]
    N2B --> LEAF2[Leaf Mixed Occupied plus Free]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Environment Representation in mobile robotics. List any four requirements of a good environment representation.**

**Model Answer:**
Environment representation is the formal encoding of the robot's workspace into a data structure that supports localization, mapping, and path planning.

Four requirements:
1. **Completeness** — must capture all navigable and non-navigable regions.
2. **Consistency** — repeated measurements should produce stable estimates.
3. **Compactness** — efficient memory utilization for embedded hardware.
4. **Computational Tractability** — real-time updates at the sensor frame rate (typically **10–40 Hz**).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Metric and Topological maps with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Metric Map | Topological Map |
|--------|------------|-----------------|
| **Information Stored** | Exact coordinates of features/cells | Connectivity between distinctive places |
| **Precision** | High (distance-preserving) | Low (order-preserving) |
| **Example** | Occupancy grid of a room | Subway-style graph of corridors |
| **Best Suited For** | Precise localization, obstacle avoidance | Global route planning, large-scale navigation |
| **Memory** | Higher | Significantly lower |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Every Part B question carries an **internal choice**. Solve either Option A or Option B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of an **Occupancy Grid Map**. Derive the log-odds update rule used in occupancy grid mapping. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A service robot maps a $15\,\text{m} \times 10\,\text{m}$ laboratory using a 2D occupancy grid with a cell resolution of $\Delta = 0.10\,\text{m}$. Calculate: (i) the total number of grid cells, (ii) the total memory in kilobytes assuming 1 byte per cell, and (iii) the new memory requirement if resolution is improved to $0.05\,\text{m}$. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept + Derivation:**

An **occupancy grid map** partitions the environment into a uniform 2D lattice of square cells. Each cell $m_i$ stores the probability that the cell is occupied, $p(m_i)$, estimated from sensor measurements. A typical ROS `OccupancyGrid` message stores values in the range $[0, 100]$ plus $-1$ for unknown.

**Diagram:** (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.2)

**Derivation of Log-Odds Update Rule:**

Let $p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ denote the posterior occupancy probability of cell $m_i$. Apply Bayes' theorem:

$$
p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) = \dfrac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)\; p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
$$

Define log-odds:

$$
\ell_i = \ln \dfrac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}
$$

Then the recursive update becomes the additive form:

$$
\ell_{i,t} = \ell_{i,t-1} + \ell_{\text{sensor model}}
$$

where $\ell_{\text{sensor model}} = \ln \dfrac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}$.

To recover the probability:

$$
p(m_i) = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp(\ell_i)}
$$

This derivation is fundamental to the **ROS `gmapping`** and **`cartographer`** packages.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Number of cells:**

$$
M = \dfrac{A}{\Delta^2} = \dfrac{15 \times 10}{(0.10)^2} = \dfrac{150}{0.01} = 15{,}000 \;\text{cells}
$$

**(ii) Memory in kB:**

$$
\text{Mem}_{0.10} = 15{,}000 \times 1\,\text{byte} = 15{,}000\,\text{bytes} \approx 14.65\,\text{kB}
$$

**(iii) New memory at $\Delta = 0.05\,\text{m}$:**

$$
M_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{150}{(0.05)^2} = \dfrac{150}{0.0025} = 60{,}000 \;\text{cells}
$$

$$
\text{Mem}_{0.05} = 60{,}000\,\text{bytes} \approx 58.59\,\text{kB}
$$

**Conclusion:** Halving the resolution **quadruples** the memory consumption.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare **Feature-based maps**, **Topological maps**, and **Voronoi diagrams** as environment representations. State one application where each is preferred. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the **OctoMap** data structure. Derive an expression for the memory savings of an OctoMap over a dense 3D voxel grid in a sparse environment. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison:**

| Aspect | Feature Map | Topological Map | Voronoi Diagram |
|--------|-------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Representation** | Sparse set of landmarks with coordinates | Graph of connected places | Set of points equidistant from obstacles |
| **Data Structure** | List of (x, y, ID, covariance) | Adjacency list / edge list | Edge set with vertices |
| **Memory** | Very low | Lowest | Low |
| **Suitability** | Outdoor SLAM, structured environments | Large indoor spaces, warehouses | Cluttered narrow corridors |
| **Localization Method** | EKF-SLAM, Graph-SLAM | Place recognition, image matching | Voronoi-based MCL |
| **Path Planning** | Sparse A* | Dijkstra, BFS | Maximum-clearance A* |

**Preferred Applications:**
- **Feature Map:** Autonomous driving with detected lane markers, traffic signs.
- **Topological Map:** Amazon-style warehouse AGV routing between picking stations.
- **Voronoi Diagram:** Search-and-rescue robots navigating collapsed-building corridors.

**(b) OctoMap Derivation:**

An **OctoMap** (Wurm et al., 2010) is a hierarchical 3D data structure based on the **octree**. The root node represents the bounding cube of the environment. Each internal node has exactly **8 children** (one per octant). A node is recursively subdivided only if its children's occupancy states are not uniform; otherwise, it is **pruned** into a single leaf.

**Memory Savings Derivation:**

Let:
- $N_{\max}$ = total number of cells in the equivalent dense voxel grid $= (L/\Delta)^3$.
- $N_{\text{alloc}}$ = number of nodes actually allocated in the OctoMap.

The fractional memory savings are:

$$
S_{\text{oct}} = 1 - \dfrac{N_{\text{alloc}}}{N_{\max}}
$$

**Worked Example:** A $10\,\text{m} \times 10\,\text{m} \times 3\,\text{m}$ room with $\Delta = 0.10\,\text{m}$ yields $N_{\max} = 100 \times 100 \times 30 = 300{,}000$ voxels. If only 5% of voxels are occupied (e.g., a few chairs, walls, and a table), an OctoMap typically allocates only $N_{\text{alloc}} \approx 30{,}000$ nodes after pruning:

$$
S_{\text{oct}} = 1 - \dfrac{30{,}000}{300{,}000} = 1 - 0.10 = 0.90
$$

This represents a **90% memory reduction**, making OctoMaps the de-facto standard for 3D UAV mapping (e.g., on the Intel RealSense + NVIDIA Jetson platform).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Normalizing Constant:** In the Bayes derivation (Section 3.1), students often write $p(m_i \mid z_t) = p(z_t \mid m_i)\, p(m_i)$ and forget the denominator. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this omission.
> 2. **Confusing Cell Resolution with Sensor Resolution:** LIDAR angular resolution is **not** the same as occupancy grid cell size $\Delta$. Do not interchange them.
> 3. **Missing Units in Memory Calculations:** Always state memory in **bytes/kB/MB** explicitly. Naked numerical answers cost marks.
> 4. **Log-Odds Sign Error:** When applying the inverse logistic transform, students often write $p = 1/(1+e^{\ell})$ instead of $p = 1 - 1/(1+e^{\ell})$. The subtraction is **mandatory** for the free-space complement.
> 5. **Hybrid Map Confusion:** A "metric-topological hybrid" is **not** just a topological map with coordinates — it must exhibit *local* metric precision *and* *global* topological structure.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Environment Representation**

- **Definition:** Mathematical encoding of the robot's workspace into a queryable, updatable data structure.
- **Four Pillars:** Completeness, Consistency, Compactness, Computational Tractability.
- **Three Classification Axes:** Geometry (Metric / Topological / Hybrid), Dimensionality (2D / 2.5D / 3D), Discretization (Continuous / Discrete).
- **Occupancy Grid:** Discretize space into uniform cells; store $p(m_i)$ per cell; update via **log-odds** for numerical stability.
- **Log-Odds Update:** $\ell_{i,t} = \ell_{i,t-1} + \ell_{\text{sensor model}}$ — purely **additive**.
- **Inverse Transform:** $p(m_i) = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp(\ell_i)}$.
- **Memory Scaling:** $M = A / \Delta^2$ — halving $\Delta$ **quadruples** memory.
- **Feature Map:** Sparse landmarks; pairs naturally with **EKF-SLAM**.
- **Topological Map:** Graph $G(V,E)$; best for **global route planning** in large spaces.
- **Voronoi Diagram:** Bisector of obstacle sites; **maximizes clearance** — preferred for safe navigation in cluttered spaces.
- **OctoMap:** Hierarchical 3D octree; achieves up to **80–90% memory savings** versus dense voxel grids in sparse environments.
- **Quad-Tree:** Hierarchical 2D analog; subdivides a square into 4 quadrants recursively.
- **Standard Tools:** ROS `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`, `gmapping`, `cartographer`, `octomap_server`.
- **Sensor Coupling:** 2D LIDAR + 2D grid; RGB-D / 3D LIDAR + OctoMap; landmark sensors + feature map.
- **Examiner's Mantra:** *Always specify units, always normalize probabilities, always show the additive form when working with log-odds.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Schematic for Mobile Robot Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Schematic for Mobile Robot Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mobile Robot Localization** is the process by which a mobile robot determines its **pose** $\mathbf{x}_t = (x, y, \theta)^{\top}$ — its two-dimensional position $(x, y)$ and heading/orientation $\theta$ — within a given coordinate frame or a known map of the environment, using onboard sensor measurements and control inputs over time.

In formal probabilistic terms (as per Thrun, Burgard & Fox — *Probabilistic Robotics*, the de-facto KTU reference framework for this module), localization is the recursive estimation of the **belief** of the robot over the space of all possible poses:

$$\text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) \;=\; p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})$$

where:
- $\mathbf{z}_{1:t}$ = history of all sensor measurements up to time $t$
- $\mathbf{u}_{1:t}$ = history of all control inputs (odometry, motor commands) up to time $t$
- $\mathbf{x}_t$ = robot pose at time $t$

The **"General Schematic"** refers to the canonical, closed-loop information-flow architecture that connects *sensors*, *motion*, *the environment (map)*, and the *belief state* of the robot through the two fundamental operations of any probabilistic localization filter — **Prediction** and **Correction (Update)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):** The schematic is a *framework*, not a specific algorithm. It is the unifying backbone for Markov Localization, EKF Localization, Monte Carlo (Particle) Localization, and Grid-Based Localization. Mastering this schematic means you can derive the math for *any* of these algorithms.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Blind Person in a Building" Analogy

Imagine a person **wearing a blindfold** inside an unfamiliar shopping mall. The mall is the **map**. The person has two sources of information:

1. **Walking memory (odometry / dead reckoning):** "I took 10 steps forward, then turned 90° right, then 8 more steps." — This is the **control input $\mathbf{u}_t$**.
2. **Whispered landmarks (sensors):** "I hear an escalator on my left" or "I feel a glass door at arm's length." — These are the **measurements $\mathbf{z}_t$**.

Now, localization is simply the person's **internal guess of where they are**. The blind person:

- **Predicts** where they should be *based on steps alone* (the "I think I am here" estimate drifts and accumulates error).
- **Corrects** that guess *based on what they feel/hear* and the known mall layout (this snaps the estimate back toward reality).
- **Repeats** this every second.

> [!NOTE]
> The **drift** from step-counting is exactly why we need the **correction step**. Without sensors, the prediction diverges. Without prediction, sensors give isolated snapshots with no continuity. Localization is the *fusion* of both — that is the essence of the general schematic.

---

## 1.3 The Three Localization Variants (KTU-Exam Essential)

The schematic is applied at three difficulty levels, and you **must** be able to classify any given problem statement into one of them:

| Variant | Initial Belief | Difficulty | Real-world analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Position Tracking** | $bel(\mathbf{x}_0)$ known and unimodal (e.g., Gaussian near true pose) | Lowest | Blind person who *started* at the main entrance |
| **Global Localization** | $bel(\mathbf{x}_0)$ uniform over the entire map | Medium | Blind person dropped anywhere in the mall |
| **Kidnapped Robot Problem** | Robot is teleported mid-operation; belief must recover | Highest | Blind person secretly moved while blindfolded remains on |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite Question:** *"Differentiate between position tracking and global localization."* — Use the table above. The key distinction is the **shape of the initial belief** $bel(\mathbf{x}_0)$: peaked (Gaussian) vs. spread uniformly.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following conventions are **mandatory** in KTU answer scripts:

- **Robot pose** in 2D: $\mathbf{x} = (x, y, \theta)^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times [0, 2\pi)$
- **Map** (occupancy grid type): $m = \{m_i\}$, where $m_i \in \{0, 1\}$ or $m_i \in [0, 1]$ (continuous probability)
- **Sensor frame** transforms to global frame using the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$
- **Sample period** $T$ (typically **0.1 s** to **0.2 s** for wheeled robots)
- **Wheel encoder resolution** $\Delta d$ (e.g., **0.001 m** per tick)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Stage Recursive Filter — Why It Works

The general schematic is mathematically grounded in the **Bayes Filter** recursion, which decomposes localization into two physical operations repeated indefinitely:

### Stage 1 — **Prediction (Motion Update / "Where will I be?")**

Given the previous belief $bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ and the new control input $\mathbf{u}_t$ (e.g., wheel odometry $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta \theta$), predict the belief *before* incorporating the new sensor reading:

$$\overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) \;=\; \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{u}_t, \mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, \cdot \, bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$$

This is also called the **prior** belief. The term $p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{u}_t, \mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ is the **motion model** (a.k.a. **state transition model**) — it represents the probability that executing control $\mathbf{u}_t$ from pose $\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ will land the robot at $\mathbf{x}_t$. It is inherently uncertain due to **wheel slip**, **floor irregularities**, and **encoder quantization**.

### Stage 2 — **Correction (Measurement Update / "Where am I really?")**

Once the new sensor measurement $\mathbf{z}_t$ arrives, the prior is updated via **Bayes' Rule**:

$$bel(\mathbf{x}_t) \;=\; \eta \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t)$$

where:
- $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m)$ is the **measurement model** (a.k.a. **sensor model** or **likelihood**) — the probability of observing $\mathbf{z}_t$ if the robot were at $\mathbf{x}_t$ in map $m$.
- $\eta$ is the **normalizer** ensuring the belief integrates to 1:

$$\eta^{-1} \;=\; \int p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}'_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}'_t) \, d\mathbf{x}'_t$$

---

## 2.2 The Four Canonical Components of the Schematic

Every localization schematic — whether for an EKF, a particle filter, or a histogram filter — has exactly **four** information blocks interacting cyclically:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **(C1) Robot Pose $\mathbf{x}_t$** — The state we want to estimate.
> **(C2) Map $m$** — Static prior; assumed known in pure localization.
> **(C3) Control Input $\mathbf{u}_t$** — Generated by motion actuators; fed into the **motion model**.
> **(C4) Sensor Measurement $\mathbf{z}_t$** — Generated by exteroceptive sensors; fed into the **measurement model**.

The **belief** is the *only* latent variable; everything else is observable (or assumed given).

---

## 2.3 Two Underlying Probabilistic Assumptions (Markov Property)

For the recursive filter above to be **valid and tractable**, the following assumptions (collectively called the **Markov assumption**) must hold:

1. **State completeness:** The pose $\mathbf{x}_t$ is a **sufficient statistic** of all past history. Formally:

$$p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t-1}) \;=\; p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{t-1})$$

   → The future is conditionally independent of the past, given the present.

2. **Measurement conditional independence:** The current measurement depends only on the current pose and the map:

$$p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{1:t}, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t-1}) \;=\; p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m)$$

> [!WARNING]
> If the Markov assumption fails (e.g., the robot's pose is not fully observable — think a slip event that goes unrecorded), the filter **diverges**. KTU questions on this often ask: *"Why is localization under a unicycle model still a partially observable problem?"* — Answer: because the slip event is not in the state vector.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{x}_t = (x_t, y_t, \theta_t)^{\top}$ | Robot pose at time $t$ | $x,y$ in $\text{m}$, $\theta$ in $\text{rad}$ |
| 2 | $\mathbf{u}_t = (\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta \theta)^{\top}$ | Odometry / control increment | $\text{m}$, $\text{m}$, $\text{rad}$ |
| 3 | $\mathbf{z}_t$ | Sensor measurement vector | $\text{m}$ (range) or $\text{rad}$ (bearing) |
| 4 | $m$ | Map (occupancy grid / landmark set) | Dimensionless / $m \times n$ grid |
| 5 | $bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})$ | Posterior belief | Probability density (sums to 1) |
| 6 | $\overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t)$ | Prior / predicted belief (pre-sensor) | Probability density (sums to 1) |
| 7 | $p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{u}_t, \mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ | Motion model (transition) | Probability density |
| 8 | $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m)$ | Measurement model (likelihood) | Probability density |
| 9 | $\eta = \left[ \int p(\mathbf{z}_t \vert \mathbf{x}, m)\,\overline{bel}(\mathbf{x})\, d\mathbf{x} \right]^{-1}$ | Normalizer | Scalar |
| 10 | $R(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$ | 2D rotation matrix | Unitless, orthogonal |
| 11 | $H$ (Jacobian) | Linearized measurement matrix | $\partial \mathbf{z} / \partial \mathbf{x}$, dim depends |
| 12 | $F$ (Jacobian) | Linearized motion matrix | $\partial \mathbf{x}_t / \partial \mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ |
| 13 | $Q_t$ | Motion noise covariance | $\text{m}^2$, $\text{rad}^2$ |
| 14 | $R_t$ | Measurement noise covariance | $\text{m}^2$, $\text{rad}^2$ |
| 15 | $\Sigma_t$ | Belief (pose) covariance | $3 \times 3$ positive-definite matrix |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The schematic is the *lingua franca* of every deployed mobile robot:

- **Autonomous cars (Waymo, Tesla FSD):** EKF/UKF localization fuses GPS $\mathbf{z}_t$ with IMU odometry $\mathbf{u}_t$ against a HD map $m$.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva):** Particle filter over a 2D occupancy grid; wheel encoders provide $\mathbf{u}_t$, LiDAR provides $\mathbf{z}_t$.
- **Mars Rovers (NASA Perseverance):** Visual-inertial odometry + landmark-based EKF localization against orbital maps.
- **Underwater AUVs (REMUS class):** Particle filter with terrain-aided navigation; sonar $\mathbf{z}_t$, DVL $\mathbf{u}_t$, bathymetric $m$.

> [!NOTE]
> Whenever an interviewer or examiner asks *"How does a robot know where it is?"* — your one-sentence answer is the Bayes recursion above. Memorize it.
