# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bayes Filter Recursion (KTU 14-Mark Staple)

This derivation appears in nearly every KTU board exam in some form. We will show it **explicitly and without skipping any algebraic step**.

**Goal:** Show that

$$bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m) \cdot \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{u}_t, \mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$$

### Step 1 — Start from the definition of the posterior belief

$$bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})$$

Expand the conditioning history into "past" and "present" parts:

$$= p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_t, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})$$

**Reasoning:** The new measurement $\mathbf{z}_t$ is the most informative term, so we separate it from the rest of the history.

### Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Rule (with the measurement as the "evidence")

$$bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \frac{p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}) \cdot p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})}{p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})}$$

**Reasoning:** Standard Bayes' Rule $p(A \mid B) = p(B \mid A) p(A) / p(B)$ with $A = \mathbf{x}_t$, $B = (\mathbf{z}_t, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})$.

### Step 3 — Apply the Markov measurement assumption

By Assumption 2, $\mathbf{z}_t$ depends only on $\mathbf{x}_t$ and $m$:

$$p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}) = p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m)$$

**Reasoning:** Given the current pose $\mathbf{x}_t$, the present measurement is conditionally independent of all past measurements and control inputs.

### Step 4 — Identify the normalizer

The denominator $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})$ is a constant with respect to $\mathbf{x}_t$ — call it $\eta^{-1}$:

$$bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m) \cdot p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})$$

### Step 5 — Expand the remaining prior using the law of total probability

Sum/integrate over all possible previous poses $\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$:

$$p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}) = \int p(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$$

**Reasoning:** Marginalize the joint distribution by integrating out $\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$.

### Step 6 — Apply the chain rule of probability

$$= \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}) \cdot p(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$$

### Step 7 — Apply the Markov state assumption

By Assumption 1, the next pose depends only on the current pose and the latest control:

$$p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}) = p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$$

**Reasoning:** This is the **motion model**.

### Step 8 — Recognize the second factor as the previous belief

By definition:

$$p(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t-1}) = bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$$

Note that conditioning on $\mathbf{u}_{1:t}$ instead of $\mathbf{u}_{1:t-1}$ does not change the value, because $\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ does not depend on the future control $\mathbf{u}_t$.

### Step 9 — Assemble the final recursive form

$$\boxed{\;bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m) \cdot \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{u}_t, \mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}\;}$$

This is the canonical schematic equation. All localization algorithms (Kalman, EKF, Particle, Grid) are different *representations* of $bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ and *implementations* of this integral.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Linear-Gaussian (Kalman) Special Case

When the motion and measurement models are **linear** and the belief is **Gaussian**, the integral in Step 9 has a closed-form solution — this is the **Kalman Filter**.

### Step 1 — Write the linear-Gaussian models

$$\mathbf{x}_t = A_t \mathbf{x}_{t-1} + B_t \mathbf{u}_t + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t, \quad \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, Q_t)$$

$$\mathbf{z}_t = C_t \mathbf{x}_t + \boldsymbol{\delta}_t, \quad \boldsymbol{\delta}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, R_t)$$

### Step 2 — Parameterize the belief as a Gaussian

$$bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})$$

### Step 3 — Compute the predicted (prior) belief

Since the sum of independent Gaussians is Gaussian:

$$\overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) = \mathcal{N}(\bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t)$$

with:

$$\bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t = A_t \boldsymbol{\mu}_{t-1} + B_t \mathbf{u}_t$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^{\top} + Q_t$$

**Reasoning:** Mean transforms linearly; covariance propagates through the linear map and adds process noise.

### Step 4 — Compute the Kalman gain

The cross-covariance between measurement residual and state is $\bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^{\top}$, and the innovation covariance is $C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^{\top} + R_t$. The optimal blending factor is:

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^{\top} (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^{\top} + R_t)^{-1}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the posterior (updated) belief

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_t = \bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t + K_t (\mathbf{z}_t - C_t \bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t)$$

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

**Reasoning:** The Kalman gain weighs the measurement residual $(\mathbf{z}_t - \hat{\mathbf{z}}_t)$ against the prior. If $R_t$ is small (trusted sensor), $K_t \to I$ and we trust the measurement; if $Q_t$ is small (perfect motion model), $K_t \to 0$ and we trust the prediction.

> [!NOTE]
> The **EKF** linearizes nonlinear models $g(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u})$ and $h(\mathbf{x})$ by their Jacobians $F = \partial g / \partial \mathbf{x}$ and $H = \partial h / \partial \mathbf{x}$, then applies the same Kalman recursion. This is the bridge between the **general schematic** and the **EKF localization algorithm** in Module 3.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Where Does Each Quantity Live in the Schematic?

| Schematic Block | Math Object | Computed by | Used in |
|---|---|---|---|
| Robot pose | $\mathbf{x}_t \in \mathbb{R}^3$ | Unknown — to be estimated | Output of the filter |
| Map | $m$ | Given (offline SLAM or manual) | Measurement model, prior |
| Control input | $\mathbf{u}_t$ | Wheel encoders / IMU | Motion model |
| Sensor measurement | $\mathbf{z}_t$ | LiDAR, camera, GPS, sonar | Measurement model |
| Motion model | $p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{u}_t, \mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ | Odometry calibration, velocity model | Prediction step |
| Measurement model | $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m)$ | Beam model, landmark model, field model | Correction step |
| Prior belief | $\overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t)$ | Convolution of previous belief with motion model | Input to correction |
| Posterior belief | $bel(\mathbf{x}_t)$ | Bayes' product with measurement model | Output / next prior |
| Normalizer | $\eta$ | Integration over the belief space | Ensures valid PDF |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Schematic — The Closed-Loop Localization Architecture

The figure below is the **canonical schematic** that you should be able to draw blindfolded in any KTU exam. It shows the *information flow* — not the physical hardware layout.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Robot Pose x_t\nunknown target] --> A1[Motion Model\np of x_t given u_t and x_t_minus_1]
    B0[Control Input u_t\nwheel encoders, IMU] --> A1
    A1 --> A2[Prior Belief\nbar bel of x_t]
    A2 --> A3[Bayes Product\nmultiply by measurement model]
    C0[Sensor Measurement z_t\nLIDAR, camera, GPS] --> A4[Measurement Model\np of z_t given x_t and m]
    D0[Static Map m\noccupancy grid, landmarks] --> A4
    A4 --> A3
    A3 --> A5[Normalizer eta\nintegrate to 1]
    A5 --> A6[Posterior Belief\nbel of x_t]
    A6 --> A7[State Estimate\nx_t hat equals argmax bel]
    A6 --> A2
    A6 --> A1
```

**How to read it (KTU 14-mark answer style):**

1. The **robot pose** $\mathbf{x}_t$ and the **control input** $\mathbf{u}_t$ feed into the **motion model**, producing the **prior belief** $\overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t)$.
2. The **sensor measurement** $\mathbf{z}_t$ and the **map** $m$ feed into the **measurement model**.
3. The two probabilistic outputs are multiplied (Bayes' product) and **normalized** by $\eta$.
4. The result is the **posterior belief** $bel(\mathbf{x}_t)$.
5. The **state estimate** is the mode (or mean) of this belief — e.g., $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t = \arg\max_{\mathbf{x}} bel(\mathbf{x}_t)$.
6. The posterior becomes the **previous belief** for the next time step, closing the loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The loop closure (posterior $\to$ next prior) is what makes the filter **recursive**. Without it, every step would require storing the entire history, which is computationally infeasible.

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Hardware-to-Software Mapping

For exams that ask *"Map the schematic to a physical robot"*, the following diagram identifies which hardware/software module corresponds to each block.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph hardware[Physical Hardware Layer]
        H1[Wheel Encoders] 
        H2[IMU] 
        H3[LiDAR Sensor] 
        H4[Onboard CPU] 
        H5[Static Map Store] 
    end
    subgraph software[Algorithmic Software Layer]
        S1[Odometry Node] 
        S2[Motion Model] 
        S3[Sensor Preprocessing] 
        S4[Measurement Model] 
        S5[Bayes Filter Core] 
        S6[Pose Estimator] 
    end
    H1 --> S1
    H2 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    H3 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    H5 --> S4
    S2 --> S5
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S5
    S6 --> H4
```

**Reading:** The **hardware layer** produces raw data; the **software layer** runs the recursive Bayesian estimation. The **pose estimator**'s output is fed back into the **filter core** for the next cycle.

---

## 4.3 Schematic Variants by Filter Type — Adaptation Table

The schematic above is algorithm-agnostic. Below is how each *block* changes depending on the filter used:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[General Schematic] --> ekf[EKF Localization]
    root --> pf[Particle Filter]
    root --> hist[Histogram Grid Filter]
    root --> ukf[UKF Localization]
    ekf --> ekf1[bel represented as N of mu and Sigma]
    ekf --> ekf2[Jacobians F and H computed analytically]
    ekf --> ekf3[Single Gaussian hypothesis]
    pf --> pf1[bel represented as N weighted samples]
    pf --> pf2[Non-parametric, handles multi-modal]
    pf --> pf3[Resampling step replaces normalizer]
    hist --> h1[bel is discrete grid over pose space]
    hist --> h2[Convolution by histogram filter]
    hist --> h3[Used in early mobile robots like Rhino]
    ukf --> ukf1[bel represented as N of mu and Sigma]
    ukf --> ukf2[Sigma points replace Jacobians]
    ukf --> ukf3[Better for highly nonlinear models]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **all** four variants share the **same master schematic** in Section 4.1. They differ only in:
> (a) how they represent the belief $bel(\mathbf{x}_t)$, and
> (b) how they implement the integral in the prediction step.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBRT751]**
**Define the term "belief" in the context of mobile robot localization. Why is it represented as a probability distribution rather than a single point?**

**Model Answer:**

A *belief* $bel(\mathbf{x}_t)$ is the robot's internal probabilistic estimate of its pose $\mathbf{x}_t$, conditional on all measurements $\mathbf{z}_{1:t}$ and control inputs $\mathbf{u}_{1:t}$ observed so far. Formally, $bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})$.

It is a **distribution** rather than a point because of three unavoidable sources of uncertainty:
1. **Sensor noise** — every measurement $\mathbf{z}_t$ is corrupted.
2. **Actuator noise** — wheel slip and floor irregularities make $\mathbf{u}_t$ inexact.
3. **Initial uncertainty** — the robot's starting pose is often only approximately known.

A single-point estimate (e.g., $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t$) discards this uncertainty and leads to over-confident decisions. The distribution enables optimal fusion via Bayes' Rule and supports multimodal beliefs (essential for global localization).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBRT751]**
**List and briefly explain the three difficulty variants of the mobile robot localization problem.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Variant | Initial Belief | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Position Tracking** | Peaked around the true pose (e.g., Gaussian) | Robot knows approximately where it is; tracks the pose over time. Easiest variant. |
| 2 | **Global Localization** | Uniform over the entire map | Robot is placed anywhere; must determine its pose from scratch. |
| 3 | **Kidnapped Robot Problem** | Arbitrary, may include multiple hypotheses | Robot is teleported mid-operation; must detect the displacement and re-localize. Most challenging variant. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBRT751 — Model Question, Module 2]**

> **(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **general architecture for mobile robot localization**. Identify all information sources and the role of the motion and measurement models. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Starting from the definition of the belief $bel(\mathbf{x}_t)$, **derive** the Bayes filter recursion for localization, clearly stating the Markov assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

*(Refer to the schematic in Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

The general localization architecture is a **closed-loop Bayesian filter** with four observable inputs and one latent variable (the pose).

**Information sources (4 blocks):**
- **Robot pose** $\mathbf{x}_t$ — the unknown state to be estimated.
- **Map** $m$ — static prior; either an occupancy grid (cell-based) or a landmark set (feature-based).
- **Control input** $\mathbf{u}_t$ — generated by wheel encoders and IMU; expresses "how the robot moved."
- **Sensor measurement** $\mathbf{z}_t$ — generated by exteroceptive sensors (LiDAR, camera, sonar, GPS).

**Two internal models (the heart of the filter):**
1. **Motion model** $p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{u}_t, \mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ — captures *how control inputs change the pose*. It is a probability distribution because motion is noisy (e.g., wheel slip, encoder quantization).
2. **Measurement model** $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m)$ — captures *how sensor readings depend on pose and map*. It is a probability distribution because sensors are noisy.

**Operation cycle:**
- The **prediction** step uses the motion model to update the prior.
- The **correction** step uses the measurement model to update the posterior.
- The **normalizer** $\eta$ ensures a valid probability density.
- The output **state estimate** is typically the mode of the posterior.

> **Schematic (must be drawn in script):** Show the master diagram from Section 4.1. The four sources at the corners, the two models in the middle, the Bayes product, the normalizer, and the closed feedback loop from posterior to next prior.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The full derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The exam write-up should be:

**Step 1.** Start with the definition:

$$bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})$$

**Step 2.** Apply Bayes' Rule with $\mathbf{z}_t$ as evidence:

$$bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \frac{p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}) \cdot p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})}{p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})}$$

**Step 3.** Apply the **Markov measurement assumption** to simplify the likelihood:

$$p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}) = p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m)$$

**Step 4.** Define the normalizer $\eta = p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})^{-1}$.

**Step 5.** Marginalize the prior over $\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$:

$$p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}) = \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}) \cdot p(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$$

**Step 6.** Apply the **Markov state assumption** to the first factor:

$$p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}) = p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$$

**Step 7.** Recognize the second factor as $bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$.

**Final Result:**

$$bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m) \cdot \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{u}_t, \mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$$

This is the recursive Bayes filter for localization. The two terms in the integral are the **motion model** and the **previous belief**; the multiplicative term is the **measurement model**; and $\eta$ ensures normalization.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBRT751 — Model Question, Module 2]**

> **(a)** Explain, with a **real-world analogy**, why mobile robot localization requires both a **prediction step** and a **correction step**. Discuss what happens if either step is removed. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** For a **linear-Gaussian** robot, derive the **Kalman filter** form of the localization schematic. State the final update equations for the mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}_t$ and covariance $\Sigma_t$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Real-world analogy — "The Blind Person in a Mall"** *(see Section 1.2 of these notes for the full analogy).*

The blind person uses two complementary sources of information:
- **Walking memory (odometry)** — *predicts* where the person should be based on steps.
- **Whispered landmarks (sensors)** — *corrects* that estimate by snapping it back to reality.

**Why both are needed:**

| Step Removed | Consequence |
|---|---|
| **No prediction** | Sensors give isolated snapshots. The robot has no continuity of motion. After every sensor reading, it must re-localize from scratch — slow and prone to false matches. The robot cannot move smoothly. |
| **No correction** | Only odometry is used (dead reckoning). The pose estimate drifts unboundedly due to accumulated wheel slip, encoder bias, and floor irregularities. The covariance grows without bound; the robot becomes lost within minutes. |

**Engineering takeaway:** Prediction provides **temporal continuity**; correction provides **absolute reference**. Localization is the optimal fusion of both. This is exactly the **Bayes filter recursion** from the schematic.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The full derivation is in **Section 3.2** of these notes. The exam write-up:

**Step 1.** Write the linear-Gaussian motion and measurement models:

$$\mathbf{x}_t = A_t \mathbf{x}_{t-1} + B_t \mathbf{u}_t + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t, \quad \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, Q_t)$$

$$\mathbf{z}_t = C_t \mathbf{x}_t + \boldsymbol{\delta}_t, \quad \boldsymbol{\delta}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, R_t)$$

**Step 2.** Assume the prior belief is Gaussian:

$$bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})$$

**Step 3.** Compute the prior (prediction):

$$\bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t = A_t \boldsymbol{\mu}_{t-1} + B_t \mathbf{u}_t$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^{\top} + Q_t$$

**Step 4.** Compute the Kalman gain (the optimal blending weight):

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^{\top} \, (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^{\top} + R_t)^{-1}$$

**Step 5.** Compute the posterior (correction):

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_t = \bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t + K_t \, (\mathbf{z}_t - C_t \bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t)$$

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \, \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

> **Why the Kalman gain has this form:** The Kalman filter is the **optimal linear estimator** in the minimum-mean-square-error (MMSE) sense. The gain $K_t$ minimizes the trace of $\Sigma_t$ by trading off the prior uncertainty $\bar{\Sigma}_t$ against the measurement uncertainty $R_t$. If $R_t \to 0$ (perfect sensor), $K_t \to I$ and the estimate fully trusts the measurement. If $Q_t \to 0$ (perfect motion), $K_t \to 0$ and the estimate fully trusts the prediction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget the **$+ Q_t$** in the prediction step. Without it, the prior covariance stays frozen and the filter **cannot diverge** — which is wrong. Always add process noise when propagating covariance. Similarly, students often write the Kalman gain transpose incorrectly; remember $K_t = \bar{\Sigma} C^{\top} (C \bar{\Sigma} C^{\top} + R)^{-1}$, not the other way around.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "General Schematic for Mobile Robot Localization":**
>
> - [ ] **Pose** $\mathbf{x}_t = (x, y, \theta)^{\top}$ is what we estimate; everything else is an input.
> - [ ] **Four inputs**: pose, map, control, sensor — remember as "**PMCS**" or "**puma**" (pose, u, m, a for action = sensor).
> - [ ] **Two models**: motion model $p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{u}_t, \mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ and measurement model $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m)$.
> - [ ] **Two stages**: predict (using motion) and correct (using sensors via Bayes' Rule).
> - [ ] **Two Markov assumptions**: state completeness and measurement conditional independence — both are required for the recursion to close.
> - [ ] **Normalizer** $\eta$ is essential — it converts the product of two distributions into a valid PDF (sum/integral equals 1).
> - [ ] **Three problem variants**: position tracking (easiest) → global localization → kidnapped robot (hardest).
> - [ ] **The schematic is algorithm-agnostic** — Kalman, EKF, UKF, Particle, Histogram, all share the same master loop.
> - [ ] **Kalman gain** $K_t$ trades off process noise $Q_t$ vs. measurement noise $R_t$.
> - [ ] **In KTU board answers**: always draw the schematic first, label the four information blocks, then derive the recursion — this structure scores full marks.
> - [ ] **Mnemonic for Bayes recursion**: "**P**redict with **M**otion, **C**orrect with **M**easurement, **N**ormalize, **R**ecurse" — **PMCNR**.
> - [ ] **Common confusion**: The prior $\overline{bel}$ is *before* the sensor update; the posterior $bel$ is *after*. The state estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t = \arg\max bel(\mathbf{x}_t)$ is taken from the posterior.
> - [ ] **Linear-Gaussian special case** → Kalman filter has closed-form mean and covariance update.
> - [ ] **Nonlinear case** → EKF linearizes via Jacobians $F$ and $H$; PF represents the belief as a set of weighted samples.
> - [ ] **Engineering insight**: Dead reckoning alone diverges; sensors alone are intermittent — only the Bayes fusion is stable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Challenge of Localization - Sensor Noise and Sensor Aliasing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Challenge of Localization - Sensor Noise and Sensor Aliasing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Challenge of Localization - Sensor Noise and Sensor Aliasing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Localization** is the problem of estimating a robot's pose (position and orientation) relative to a map of its environment, given sensor measurements and control inputs. The **Challenge of Localization** arises because real-world sensors are imperfect, introducing two fundamental impediments: **Sensor Noise** (measurement uncertainty) and **Sensor Aliasing** (perceptual indistinguishability of distinct states).

**Sensor Noise** is the stochastic, random, or systematic perturbation added to the true signal by physical sensor hardware. It degrades the *precision* of a single measurement and causes belief distributions to *spread* (covariance growth) over successive time steps.

**Sensor Aliasing**, also called **Perceptual Aliasing** or the **Data Association Problem**, is the phenomenon in which *distinctly different robot states* (different poses or map locations) generate *identical or nearly identical sensor signatures*. It degrades the *correctness* of localization and is a major source of catastrophic localization failure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Foggy Highway:**
> Imagine driving a car in thick fog at night. Your **speedometer** is **noisy** — it reads 58 km/h, then 63, then 55, when you are actually cruising at 60. This is **Sensor Noise** — a single measurement is uncertain, but the true value is *nearby*. Now imagine a worse problem: there are **two underpasses** on the highway that look exactly identical. You exit the tunnel thinking you are at Exit A, but you have actually emerged at Exit B. This is **Sensor Aliasing** — multiple physically distinct locations produce *the same observation*, and you cannot tell them apart from the observation alone.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard deviation of range sensor:** typically **σ = 0.01 m to 0.05 m** for laser rangefinders, and **σ = 0.1 m to 0.5 m** for ultrasonic sensors.
- **Aliasing threshold:** measurement similarity **< 5%** difference between distinct locations is treated as aliased in probabilistic grid maps.
- **Sensor update rate:** typically **10 Hz to 40 Hz** for mobile robot laser scanners.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:**
> In the NEP 2020 aligned Outcome-Based Education framework, students are expected to map these challenges to **CO2 (Analyse the sources of uncertainty in robot perception)** and demonstrate their impact on Bayes' filter-based localization algorithms (CO3).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Localization Problem

The general probabilistic localization framework uses the **Bayes Filter** recursion:

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

where:
- $bel(x_t)$ = posterior belief of state $x_t$ at time $t$
- $z_t$ = sensor measurement at time $t$
- $u_t$ = control input at time $t$
- $\eta$ = normalizer
- $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ = **measurement model** — directly impacted by sensor noise
- $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ = **motion model**

Both terms are corrupted by the two challenges we are studying.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of the Challenges

### A. Sensor Noise — Sources & Effects

1. **Stochastic (Random) Noise**
   - **Additive Gaussian noise:** $z = z_{true} + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$
   - Arises from electronic thermal jitter, photon counting, quantization.
   - Effect: belief becomes a *broad Gaussian* instead of a sharp Dirac delta.

2. **Systematic (Bias) Noise**
   - **Multiplicative or offset errors** — wheel diameter miscalibration, axis misalignment.
   - Effect: belief drifts *consistently* in one direction.

3. **Outliers / Heavy-tailed Noise**
   - Cross-talk, specular reflection, dynamic obstacles.
   - Effect: occasional wild measurements can collapse the belief onto an incorrect pose if not filtered.

### B. Sensor Aliasing — Mechanisms & Effects

1. **Geometric Symmetry:** Two corridors or rooms with identical dimensions produce identical laser scans.
2. **Repetitive Textures:** Identical floor tiles, ceiling panels, or wall patterns.
3. **Limited Field of View:** A 360° LIDAR on a featureless wall sees only the wall — front, back, left, right all look alike.
4. **Range / Bearing Quantization:** Low-resolution sensors cannot distinguish closely spaced features.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:**
> - **Noise** → *uncertainty* (belief widens, but stays roughly correct on average).
> - **Aliasing** → *ambiguity* (belief splits into multiple, equally valid hypotheses — the robot may be at Place A *or* Place B with equal probability).

## 2.3 Mathematical Models of Sensor Noise

### 2.3.1 Univariate Gaussian Noise Model

$$p(z \mid x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(z - h(x))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

- $z$ = observed measurement
- $h(x)$ = expected measurement function for state $x$
- $\sigma$ = **standard deviation** of the sensor (in SI units: metres, radians, etc.)

### 2.3.2 Multivariate Measurement Model (used in EKF)

$$z_t = h(x_t) + v_t, \quad v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$

where $R_t$ is the **measurement noise covariance matrix** (typically $2 \times 2$ for 2D range-bearing sensors, $3 \times 3$ for 3D pose sensors).

## 2.4 Mathematical Treatment of Sensor Aliasing

### 2.4.1 Likelihood of Aliased Observations

For two distinct states $x^{(1)}$ and $x^{(2)}$, aliasing occurs when:

$$p(z \mid x^{(1)}) \approx p(z \mid x^{(2)}) \quad \forall z \in \mathcal{Z}$$

The **posterior belief** becomes **bimodal** (or multi-modal), violating the unimodal Gaussian assumption of the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**.

### 2.4.2 Validation Gate for Data Association (Mahalanobis Distance)

To reject aliased associations, use the **Mahalanobis distance**:

$$D_M = \sqrt{(z - \hat{z})^T S^{-1} (z - \hat{z})}$$

where $S = H P H^T + R$ is the innovation covariance.

**Validation gate rule:** accept the association if $D_M \le \gamma$, where $\gamma$ is a threshold (typically **$\gamma = 3$ to 5** for a 95–99% confidence ellipse).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Automotive SLAM** uses Mahalanobis gating to reject ghost obstacles (aliased reflections).
> - **Warehouse AMRs** (Autonomous Mobile Robots) rely on anti-aliasing features in LIDAR placement to differentiate aisles.
> - **Aerospace navigation** uses multi-hypothesis tracking (MHT) to resolve aliased radar returns from closely spaced aircraft.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Key Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bayes Filter Update | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | $bel, p(z \vert x), p(x \vert u,x)$ | dimensionless probabilities |
| Measurement Model | $z_t = h(x_t) + v_t$ | $z, h, v$ | metres / radians |
| Gaussian Noise PDF | $p(z \mid x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(z - h(x))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $\sigma$ | SI (m, rad) |
| Noise Covariance | $R_t = E[v_t v_t^T]$ | $R$ | $\text{m}^2, \text{rad}^2$ |
| Mahalanobis Distance | $D_M = \sqrt{(z - \hat{z})^T S^{-1} (z - \hat{z})}$ | $S = HPH^T + R$ | dimensionless |
| Validation Gate | $D_M \le \gamma$ (accept), else reject | $\gamma \approx 3\text{–}5$ | dimensionless |
| Innovation | $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$ | $\nu$ | metres / radians |
| Aliasing Condition | $p(z \mid x^{(1)}) \approx p(z \mid x^{(2)})$ for $x^{(1)} \ne x^{(2)}$ | — | probability density |
| Motion Noise | $u_t = u_{true} + \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ | $Q$ covariance | SI² |
| Belief Entropy | $H(bel) = -\sum_i p(x_i) \log p(x_i)$ | $H$ | bits / nats |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Posterior Belief Divergence Under Sensor Noise

**Goal:** Show that repeated noisy measurements cause the belief covariance $P_t$ to grow.

**Step 1 — Initial conditions.**
At time $t=0$, the robot's belief is a Gaussian with mean $\hat{x}_0$ and covariance $P_0$.

$$bel(x_0) = \mathcal{N}(x_0; \hat{x}_0, P_0)$$

**Step 2 — Prediction step (motion update).**
Apply the motion model with additive noise $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$:

$$x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$$

The predicted belief parameters become:

$$\hat{x}_t^{-} = f(\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t)$$

$$P_t^{-} = F_t P_{t-1} F_t^T + Q_t$$

where $F_t = \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right|_{\hat{x}_{t-1}}$ is the Jacobian of the motion model.

**Step 3 — Measurement update (correction step).**
Given measurement $z_t = h(x_t) + v_t$, the Kalman gain is:

$$K_t = P_t^{-} H_t^T (H_t P_t^{-} H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$$

where $H_t = \left.\frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right|_{\hat{x}_t^{-}}$ is the measurement Jacobian.

**Step 4 — Posterior covariance.**
The updated covariance is:

$$P_t = (I - K_t H_t) P_t^{-}$$

**Step 5 — Proof of noise-induced uncertainty growth.**

If sensor noise $R_t$ is large, the inverse term $(H_t P_t^{-} H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$ becomes smaller, reducing $K_t$. As $K_t \to 0$:

$$P_t \to P_t^{-}$$

This means the correction step contributes nothing, and the covariance from the prediction step ($P_t^{-} = F_t P_{t-1} F_t^T + Q_t$) propagates forward and grows unboundedly. **Hence, high sensor noise causes belief divergence — the robot becomes increasingly uncertain about its pose.**

**Step 6 — Steady-state analysis (for a stationary linear system).**

For a linear time-invariant system with $F, H, Q, R$ constant, the steady-state covariance $P_\infty$ satisfies the **Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation (DARE)**:

$$P_\infty = F P_\infty F^T + Q - F P_\infty H^T (H P_\infty H^T + R)^{-1} H P_\infty F^T$$

**Interpretation:** A larger $R$ (more noise) yields a *larger* $P_\infty$, confirming that the **asymptotic uncertainty is monotonically increasing in sensor noise variance.**

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Noise vs Aliasing

| Property | Sensor Noise | Sensor Aliasing |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Random / stochastic | Deterministic ambiguity |
| **Mathematical effect** | Adds variance to posterior | Creates multi-modal posterior |
| **Belief shape** | Unimodal (Gaussian) | Multi-modal (Gaussian Mixture) |
| **Algorithm suitability** | Kalman Filter (EKF/UKF) | Particle Filter, MHT |
| **Time evolution** | Belief *broadens* smoothly | Belief *splits* abruptly |
| **Error type** | Estimation error (precision loss) | Association error (catastrophic) |
| **Mitigation strategy** | Filtering, sensor fusion | Anti-aliasing features, hypothesis tracking |
| **Example** | LIDAR range reading 5.01 m instead of 5.00 m | Two identical corridors in a hospital |
| **KTU Bloom Level** | L3 (Apply) | L4 (Analyse) |
| **Mark weightage in ESE** | 3–7 marks | 7–10 marks |

## 3.3 Derivation: Multi-Modal Belief Under Aliasing

**Step 1.** When two distinct states $x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}$ produce identical measurements $z$, the likelihood is:

$$p(z \mid x^{(1)}) = p(z \mid x^{(2)})$$

**Step 2.** Applying Bayes' rule:

$$p(x^{(i)} \mid z) = \frac{p(z \mid x^{(i)}) \, p(x^{(i)})}{p(z)}, \quad i = 1, 2$$

**Step 3.** If priors are equal $p(x^{(1)}) = p(x^{(2)})$, then:

$$p(x^{(1)} \mid z) = p(x^{(2)} \mid z)$$

**Step 4.** The full posterior becomes a **mixture model**:

$$p(x \mid z) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \, \mathcal{N}(x; \mu_i, \Sigma_i)$$

where the weights $w_i$ represent the hypothesis probability of each aliased mode.

**Step 5.** In a Particle Filter, this manifests as **two distinct particle clusters**, one around $x^{(1)}$ and another around $x^{(2)}$, neither collapsing unless disambiguating data arrives.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture: Localization Under Noise and Aliasing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[True Robot Pose Xt] --> B[Motion Model f with Control u_t]
    B --> C[Predicted Pose Xt_pred with Motion Noise w_t]
    C --> D[Sensor Measurement z_t from h x_t]
    D --> E[Noisy Observation z_t plus v_t]
    E --> F{Is Observation Aliased?}
    F -- No --> G[Apply Bayes Filter Update]
    F -- Yes --> H[Generate Multi Modal Hypothesis]
    G --> I[Unimodal Belief Gaussian]
    H --> J[Multi Modal Belief Mixture]
    I --> K[Pose Estimate with Covariance P_t]
    J --> L[Multi Hypothesis Tracker MHT]
    L --> K
    K --> M[Mahalanobis Gate Validation]
    M -- Pass --> N[Accepted Pose]
    M -- Reject --> O[Reject Outlier]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Anti-Aliasing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Sensor Stream LIDAR Camera] --> B[Preprocessing Filter]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction Module]
    C --> D{Anti Aliasing Check}
    D -- Unique Features --> E[Direct Data Association]
    D -- Repeated Features --> F[Multi Hypothesis Tracker]
    F --> G[Particle Filter Bank]
    G --> H[Hypothesis Probability Update]
    E --> I[Pose Graph Optimization]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final Localized Pose]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: How a Robot Handles Ambiguity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Receive z_t] --> Q{Is z_t close to expected z_t_hat}
    Q -- No --> R[Compute Mahalanobis Distance D_M]
    R --> V{D_M less than gamma}
    V -- Yes --> A1[Accept as inlier update belief]
    V -- No --> R1[Reject as outlier or alias]
    Q -- Yes --> A2[Update belief with standard KF step]
    A1 --> END[Output pose]
    A2 --> END
    R1 --> END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip (for KTU Board Exams):**
> When asked to draw a localization pipeline, always include: (1) the **prediction** block, (2) the **measurement** block with explicit noise term, (3) the **data association gate** (Mahalanobis), and (4) the **belief update** block. Examiners reward *complete* flowcharts with feedback arrows.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between sensor noise and sensor aliasing in robot localization. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Sensor Noise | Sensor Aliasing |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Random perturbation of the true measurement | Different states produce identical sensor readings |
| Effect on belief | Broadens the posterior (more uncertainty) | Splits the posterior into multiple modes (ambiguity) |
| Example | LIDAR returns 5.02 m for a true 5.00 m obstacle | Two identical corridors look the same to the robot |

*(1 mark per row — 3 marks total)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is the Mahalanobis distance and how is it used in localization to address sensor aliasing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Mahalanobis distance is a statistical measure of the distance between a predicted measurement and the actual measurement, scaled by the innovation covariance $S$.
- **Formula (1 mark):**
$$D_M = \sqrt{(z - \hat{z})^T S^{-1} (z - \hat{z})}$$
- **Application (1 mark):** It is used as a **validation gate** to accept or reject sensor measurements — if $D_M > \gamma$, the measurement is treated as an outlier or alias and rejected, preventing the belief from being corrupted by ambiguous data.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Sensor Noise in Depth

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**a)** Derive the Bayesian update equation for robot localization and show how the measurement noise covariance $R_t$ affects the posterior covariance. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A mobile robot uses a LIDAR with range noise standard deviation $\sigma_r = 0.05$ m and bearing noise $\sigma_\theta = 0.5°$. The robot's predicted covariance before update is $P^- = \begin{pmatrix} 0.25 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.04 \end{pmatrix}$ (in m² and rad²). The measurement Jacobian is $H = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ and the noise covariance is $R = \begin{pmatrix} 0.0025 & 0 \\ 0 & 7.6 \times 10^{-5} \end{pmatrix}$. Compute the Kalman gain $K$ and the updated covariance $P$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the Bayes filter recursion.

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Linearize measurement model:

$$z_t = H x_t + v_t, \quad v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute Kalman gain:

$$K_t = P_t^{-} H^T (H P_t^{-} H^T + R_t)^{-1}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Update state and covariance:

$$\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^{-} + K_t (z_t - H \hat{x}_t^{-})$$

$$P_t = (I - K_t H) P_t^{-}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Effect of $R_t$:
- As $R_t \to \infty$ (heavy noise), $(H P_t^{-} H^T + R_t)^{-1} \to 0$, so $K_t \to 0$ and $P_t \to P_t^{-}$.
- As $R_t \to 0$ (perfect sensor), $K_t \to H^{-1}$ and $P_t \to 0$ (perfect localization).
- Hence, **larger $R_t$ leads to larger residual uncertainty $P_t$** — sensor noise monotonically degrades localization precision.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclude that $R_t$ controls the trade-off between trusting the prediction and trusting the measurement.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert bearing noise to radians: $\sigma_\theta = 0.5° = 0.5 \times \pi/180 \approx 0.00873$ rad.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Construct $R$:

$$R = \begin{pmatrix} 0.0025 & 0 \\ 0 & (0.00873)^2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.0025 & 0 \\ 0 & 7.62 \times 10^{-5} \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute $H P^- H^T$ (since $H = I$):

$$H P^- H^T = P^- = \begin{pmatrix} 0.25 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.04 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute the innovation covariance $S$:

$$S = H P^- H^T + R = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2525 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.04008 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute $K = P^- S^{-1}$:

$$K = \begin{pmatrix} 0.25 / 0.2525 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.04 / 0.04008 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.9901 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.9981 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Compute updated $P = (I - K H) P^-$:

$$P = (I - K) P^- = \begin{pmatrix} (1 - 0.9901) \times 0.25 & 0 \\ 0 & (1 - 0.9981) \times 0.04 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.00248 & 0 \\ 0 & 7.62 \times 10^{-5} \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Interpretation:
- The range uncertainty dropped from $0.25 \text{ m}^2$ to $0.00248 \text{ m}^2$ (a 100× reduction).
- The bearing uncertainty dropped from $0.04 \text{ rad}^2$ to $7.62 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad}^2$ (a 525× reduction).
- The high Kalman gain (~0.99) indicates that the LIDAR measurement is far more reliable than the prediction, so the robot should trust its sensor heavily.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Sensor Aliasing in Depth

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**a)** What is sensor aliasing? Explain with a hospital corridor example. Why does the EKF fail under aliasing? **(7 marks)**

**b)** Two hypotheses exist for a robot's position: $x^{(1)} = (2, 3)$ m and $x^{(2)} = (8, 3)$ m. A laser scan returns identical range measurements for both positions. If the prior probabilities are $P(x^{(1)}) = 0.6$ and $P(x^{(2)}) = 0.4$, compute the posterior probabilities using Bayes' rule, and suggest a disambiguation strategy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define sensor aliasing.
Sensor aliasing is the phenomenon where distinct robot states produce identical or nearly identical sensor observations, making it impossible to disambiguate them from a single measurement.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Hospital corridor example.
A hospital has two long, parallel corridors (Corridor A and Corridor B) of identical length, identical width, identical wall colour, and identical lighting fixtures. A robot equipped with a 2D LIDAR entering either corridor produces almost the same scan pattern. If the robot enters Corridor A but believes it is in Corridor B, its localization is catastrophically wrong — yet no single measurement reveals the error.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Why EKF fails.
- The EKF assumes a **unimodal Gaussian** posterior.
- Under aliasing, the true posterior is **bimodal** (or multi-modal) — two Gaussians with comparable weights.
- The EKF averages the modes, producing a single peak in the *middle* of the two true positions — a pose that is **guaranteed to be wrong**, with high reported "confidence" (low variance).
- This is a **catastrophic, silent failure**.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Solution: Use a **Particle Filter** or **Multi-Hypothesis Kalman Filter (MHKF)**, which can represent multiple modes.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Since the measurement is identical, the likelihoods are equal:

$$p(z \mid x^{(1)}) = p(z \mid x^{(2)}) = L$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the evidence $p(z)$:

$$p(z) = p(z \mid x^{(1)}) P(x^{(1)}) + p(z \mid x^{(2)}) P(x^{(2)}) = L(0.6) + L(0.4) = L$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply Bayes' rule:

$$P(x^{(1)} \mid z) = \frac{L \times 0.6}{L} = 0.6$$

$$P(x^{(2)} \mid z) = \frac{L \times 0.4}{L} = 0.4$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Interpretation.
The posterior equals the prior — the aliased measurement provides **zero information** to disambiguate the two hypotheses. The robot remains uncertain, with 60% belief in $x^{(1)}$ and 40% in $x^{(2)}$.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Disambiguation strategies.
1. **Inject disambiguating features** — place unique fiducial markers (QR codes, AprilTags) at corridor entrances.
2. **Use odometry** — accumulated wheel odometry breaks the symmetry by tracking the corridor travelled.
3. **Exploit turn dynamics** — the corridor's curvature or branch point will produce different scans after a turn.
4. **Multi-hypothesis particle filter** — maintain both hypotheses; only the one consistent with future motion will survive.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclude that aliasing is a structural problem requiring *environment design* or *temporal reasoning* — not just better sensors.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing noise with aliasing** in 1-mark definitions — examiners deduct 0.5 mark each. Always state *which* dimension (precision vs correctness) is affected.
> 2. **Forgetting the normalizer $\eta$** in the Bayes update — this costs 1 mark. Always write the equation with $\eta$ explicitly.
> 3. **Using EKF for aliased environments** without justification — you must explicitly state that EKF is *unimodal* and *fails* under multi-modal posteriors.
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical problems** — every covariance value must carry units (m², rad²). Missing units = 0.5 mark lost.
> 5. **Not drawing feedback arrows in flowcharts** — examiners expect a *closed-loop* diagram (measurement → update → new state → next prediction).
> 6. **Confusing $Q$ (motion noise) with $R$ (measurement noise)** in the Kalman filter — a classic 2-mark trap.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Localization = estimating pose $x_t$ from measurements $z_t$ and controls $u_t$.
- ✅ **Sensor Noise** → random perturbation → widens belief (adds variance).
- ✅ **Sensor Aliasing** → perceptual ambiguity → splits belief (multi-modal).
- ✅ Bayes filter equation: $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$.
- ✅ Measurement model: $z_t = h(x_t) + v_t$, with $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$.
- ✅ Kalman gain: $K = P^- H^T (H P^- H^T + R)^{-1}$ — *decreases* as $R$ increases.
- ✅ Mahalanobis distance: $D_M = \sqrt{(z - \hat{z})^T S^{-1} (z - \hat{z})}$ — validation gate for aliasing.
- ✅ Validation threshold: $\gamma \approx 3$ to $5$ (95–99% confidence).
- ✅ EKF fails under aliasing (unimodal assumption violated) → use **Particle Filter** or **MHKF**.
- ✅ Anti-aliasing = unique features, fiducials, multi-hypothesis tracking, motion exploitation.
- ✅ DARE (Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation) governs steady-state covariance under noise.
- ✅ Aliasing condition: $p(z \mid x^{(1)}) \approx p(z \mid x^{(2)})$ for $x^{(1)} \ne x^{(2)}$.
- ✅ KTU CO mapping: **CO2** (analyse uncertainty) + **CO3** (apply probabilistic filters).
- ✅ Bloom's level: Noise → L3 (Apply); Aliasing → L4 (Analyse).
- ✅ Numerical trick: bearing noise must be converted to **radians** before matrix operations.
- ✅ Common marks lost: missing $\eta$, wrong units, confusing $Q$ vs $R$, EKF on aliased scenes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effector Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effector Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effector Noise** in mobile robotics refers to the stochastic and systematic perturbations introduced by the robot's actuation system (the *effectors*) — i.e., the DC motors, stepper motors, gear trains, wheels, and steering linkages — that cause the actual executed motion of the robot to deviate from the commanded (ideal) motion.

Mathematically, when a robot issues a control command **u(t)**, the actual motion executed in the world frame is corrupted by an additive noise term **ε(t)**, expressed as:

$$x_{actual}(t) = f(x(t), u(t)) + \varepsilon(t)$$

where **f(·)** is the kinematic motion model and **ε(t) ∼ N(0, Q)** is a zero-mean Gaussian process noise vector representing effector-induced uncertainty.

### 1.2 Sources Classified by KTU 2024 Syllabus

| Category | Source | Nature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Systematic** | Wheel diameter mismatch, wheel-base error | Deterministic, biased |
| **Non-Systematic** | Wheel slip, uneven floor, carpet edges | Stochastic, random |
| **Mechanical** | Gear backlash, encoder quantization | Discrete, bounded |
| **Electrical** | Motor torque ripple, voltage drop | High-frequency stochastic |

> [!NOTE]
> Effector noise is one of the **three principal noise sources** in robot navigation (the others being **sensor noise** and **environment/landmark uncertainty**). The 2024 KTU syllabus groups all three under "Uncertainty in Mobile Robotics" within Module 2.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — Driving a Car with a Faulty Steering System:**
> Imagine you turn the steering wheel by exactly **10°** expecting a clean lane change, but because the front-right wheel is slightly deflated and the road has wet patches, the car actually drifts an extra **0.5°** to the right. That *extra 0.5°* is **effector noise**. If you keep driving, these tiny errors compound — after **1 km** you might find yourself in a completely different lane, or after **10 minutes** of dead reckoning you could be off the digital map by several meters. This compounding behavior is precisely why effector noise modelling is critical for SLAM and localization.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

- **Standard Deviation of Odometry Error (σ_odo)** — measured in **meters** or **radians**
- **Process Noise Covariance Matrix (Q)** — measured in **m²** for translation, **rad²** for rotation
- **Dead Reckoning Drift Rate** — typically **1–10% of distance travelled** for wheeled robots
- **Wheel Slip Ratio (λ)** — dimensionless, $\lambda = \frac{v_{wheel} - v_{actual}}{v_{wheel}}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Effector Noise Generation

1. **Command Issuance:** The controller sends a voltage/pulse-width-modulated (PWM) signal to the motor driver.
2. **Motor Response:** The motor's angular velocity is **not exactly proportional** to the applied voltage due to armature resistance variation, friction non-linearities, and load disturbances.
3. **Gear Transmission:** Backlash introduces a *dead-zone* — small commands below a threshold produce zero output motion.
4. **Wheel-Ground Interaction:** The contact patch produces **slip** (longitudinal) and **skid** (lateral), corrupting the encoder reading.
5. **Encoder Sampling:** The encoder quantizes continuous rotation into discrete ticks → introduces **quantization noise**.
6. **Integration Step:** The robot's pose estimator (EKF / Particle Filter) integrates these noisy increments, causing **error accumulation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "Why" behind effector noise: **A real robot is never a perfect differential-drive or Ackermann model.** The kinematic equations assume *ideal rolling without slip*, but physics violates this continuously. The robot's belief about its pose therefore becomes a *probability distribution* rather than a single point.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Equation / Definition | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\Delta d$ | Distance travelled per step | $\Delta d = \frac{r}{2}(\Delta\phi_R + \Delta\phi_L)$ | **m** |
| $\Delta\theta$ | Heading change per step | $\Delta\theta = \frac{r}{L}(\Delta\phi_R - \Delta\phi_L)$ | **rad** |
| $\varepsilon_d$ | Distance noise | $\varepsilon_d \sim N(0, \sigma_d^2)$ | **m** |
| $\varepsilon_\theta$ | Heading noise | $\varepsilon_\theta \sim N(0, \sigma_\theta^2)$ | **rad** |
| $Q$ | Process noise covariance | $Q = \text{diag}(\sigma_x^2, \sigma_y^2, \sigma_\theta^2)$ | **m², rad²** |
| $\Sigma_{new}$ | Pose uncertainty update | $\Sigma_{new} = F_x \Sigma_{old} F_x^T + F_u Q F_u^T$ | — |
| $F_x$ | State Jacobian | $\partial f / \partial x$ | — |
| $F_u$ | Input Jacobian | $\partial f / \partial u$ | — |
| $\lambda$ | Slip ratio | $\lambda = (v_{wheel} - v_{actual}) / v_{wheel}$ | dimensionless |
| $b$ | Wheel base | constant robot parameter | **m** |
| $r$ | Wheel radius | constant robot parameter | **m** |

> [!WARNING]
> Do **not** use the pipe character `|` inside table cells. For absolute value, use $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX or write `abs(x)` in plain text.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (AVs):** Effector noise must be bounded within ISO 26262 functional safety limits.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva bots):** Slip on polished concrete is modelled for fleet coordination.
- **Mars Rovers (NASA Perseverance):** Wheel slip on regolith is a major contributor to localization error — mitigated by visual odometry fusion.
- **Surgical Robots:** Sub-millimetre effector noise is filtered using Kalman estimators before visual servoing.

### 2.4 Systematic vs. Non-Systematic Errors

- **Systematic Error (Bias):** Caused by a *physical mismatch* (e.g., left wheel radius $= r$, right wheel radius $= r + \delta r$). Can be calibrated out.
- **Non-Systematic Error:** Caused by *stochastic events* (e.g., hitting a pebble). Cannot be calibrated; must be *estimated* online via Bayesian filters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pose Update with Effector Noise

Starting from the **ideal differential-drive kinematic model** for a small time interval $\Delta t$:

$$\Delta d = \frac{r \cdot \Delta\phi_R + r \cdot \Delta\phi_L}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(\Delta\phi_R + \Delta\phi_L)$$

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{r \cdot \Delta\phi_R - r \cdot \Delta\phi_L}{L} = \frac{r}{L}(\Delta\phi_R - \Delta\phi_L)$$

Now, introduce the **effector noise terms** for the right and left wheel encoder readings:

$$\Delta\phi_R^{measured} = \Delta\phi_R + \delta\phi_R, \quad \delta\phi_R \sim N(0, \sigma_R^2)$$

$$\Delta\phi_L^{measured} = \Delta\phi_L + \delta\phi_L, \quad \delta\phi_L \sim N(0, \sigma_L^2)$$

Substitute into the pose update equations:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta d \cos(\theta_k) + \varepsilon_x$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta d \sin(\theta_k) + \varepsilon_y$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta + \varepsilon_\theta$$

**Step 1** — Write the noisy distance and heading in terms of the noise variables:

$$\Delta d^{noisy} = \frac{r}{2}\left[(\Delta\phi_R + \delta\phi_R) + (\Delta\phi_L + \delta\phi_L)\right] = \Delta d + \frac{r}{2}(\delta\phi_R + \delta\phi_L)$$

$$\Delta\theta^{noisy} = \frac{r}{L}\left[(\Delta\phi_R + \delta\phi_R) - (\Delta\phi_L + \delta\phi_L)\right] = \Delta\theta + \frac{r}{L}(\delta\phi_R - \delta\phi_L)$$

**Step 2** — Compute the variance of the noisy distance using linear error propagation. For independent zero-mean Gaussian variables, the variance of a sum is the sum of variances:

$$\sigma_d^2 = \text{Var}\left[\frac{r}{2}(\delta\phi_R + \delta\phi_L)\right] = \frac{r^2}{4}(\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_L^2)$$

**Step 3** — Similarly, the variance of the noisy heading:

$$\sigma_\theta^2 = \text{Var}\left[\frac{r}{L}(\delta\phi_R - \delta\phi_L)\right] = \frac{r^2}{L^2}(\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_L^2)$$

**Step 4** — The **process noise covariance matrix** $Q$ is constructed by projecting the wheel noise into the world frame:

$$Q = \begin{bmatrix} (\sigma_d \cos\theta)^2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & (\sigma_d \sin\theta)^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \sigma_\theta^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5** — The **EKF prediction** step propagates the pose uncertainty using Jacobians:

$$F_x = \frac{\partial f}{\partial (x,y,\theta)} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -\Delta d \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & \Delta d \cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$F_u = \frac{\partial f}{\partial (\Delta\phi_R, \Delta\phi_L)} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\ \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\ \frac{r}{L} & -\frac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6** — Final EKF covariance update:

$$\Sigma_{k+1} = F_x \, \Sigma_k \, F_x^T + F_u \, Q_w \, F_u^T$$

where $Q_w = \text{diag}(\sigma_R^2, \sigma_L^2)$ is the *wheel-space* noise covariance. This equation is the **workhorse** of all EKF-based mobile robot localization.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A differential-drive robot with wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, wheel base $L = 0.30$ m, and wheel encoder standard deviations $\sigma_R = \sigma_L = 0.02$ rad, moves forward by $\Delta\phi_R = \Delta\phi_L = 1$ rad. Find the resulting process noise covariance matrix $Q$.

**Solution:**

$$\sigma_d^2 = \frac{(0.05)^2}{4}(0.02^2 + 0.02^2) = \frac{0.0025}{4}(0.0008) = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$$

$$\sigma_\theta^2 = \frac{(0.05)^2}{(0.30)^2}(0.02^2 + 0.02^2) = \frac{0.0025}{0.09}(0.0008) \approx 2.22 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad}^2$$

If the robot is currently heading $\theta = 0$ (along x-axis), then $\cos\theta = 1$ and $\sin\theta = 0$, so:

$$Q = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \times 10^{-7} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2.22 \times 10^{-5} \end{bmatrix}$$

Note that the $y$-component variance is zero because the robot is moving purely in $x$. This **anisotropic** property of $Q$ is what makes the EKF covariance ellipse grow and rotate over time.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Key Relationships

| Question | Analytical Tool |
| :--- | :--- |
| How does drift scale with distance? | $\sigma_x \propto \sqrt{d}$ (random walk) |
| How does drift scale with rotation? | $\sigma_\theta \propto \theta$ (linear for systematic) |
| Which error is dominant indoors? | Heading error $\sigma_\theta$ |
| Which error is dominant outdoors on rough terrain? | Wheel slip → $\sigma_d$ |
| How to fuse with GPS? | Use Q in EKF prediction, R in measurement update |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Effector Noise Propagation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ctrlA[Controller Command U] --> drvA[Motor Driver PWM]
    drvA --> motA[DC Motor]
    motA --> grA[Gear Train with Backlash]
    grA --> whA[Wheel Encoder Tick Generation]
    whA --> wgA[Wheel-Ground Contact Slip]
    wgA --> eposA[Encoder Counts Delta Phi R, Delta Phi L]
    eposA --> kinA[Kinematic Model f x u]
    kinA --> poseA[Updated Robot Pose x y theta]
    poseA --> filtA[EKF or PF Predict Step]
    filtA --> belA[Belief Mean and Covariance]
    n1[Sigma R Wheel Noise] -.-> whA
    n2[Sigma L Wheel Noise] -.-> whA
    n3[Slip Lambda] -.-> wgA
    n4[Backlash Deadzone] -.-> grA
    filtA --> obsA[Measurement Update with Sensor Z]
    obsA --> belA
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Effector Noise Modelling and Mitigation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputA[Input Stage]
        cmdA[Commanded Velocity Vcmd, Omega cmd]
    end
    subgraph noiseA[Noise Injection Stage]
        nA1[Wheel Radius Mismatch delta r]
        nA2[Wheel Base Error delta L]
        nA3[Encoder Quantization epsilon enc]
        nA4[Slip and Skid Lambda]
    end
    subgraph modelA[Kinematic Model Stage]
        kinA[Differential Drive Equations]
    end
    subgraph estA[Estimation Stage]
        ekfA[Extended Kalman Filter]
        pfA[Particle Filter Optional]
    end
    subgraph outA[Output Stage]
        belA[Posterior Belief mean and Sigma]
    end
    cmdA --> kinA
    nA1 --> kinA
    nA2 --> kinA
    nA3 --> kinA
    nA4 --> kinA
    kinA --> ekfA
    kinA --> pfA
    ekfA --> belA
    pfA --> belA
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram: Error Growth in Dead Reckoning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sA[Start Pose 0 0 0] --> sB[Step 1 True Pose 1 0 0]
    sB --> sC[Step 2 True Pose 2 0 0]
    sC --> sD[Step N Pose N 0 0]
    sA -.-> e1[Estimated Pose 1.02 0.01 0.5 deg]
    sB -.-> e2[Estimated Pose 2.05 0.03 1.2 deg]
    sC -.-> e3[Estimated Pose 3.09 0.06 2.0 deg]
    sD -.-> eN[Estimated Pose 4.15 0.10 3.1 deg]
    e1 --- unc1[Sigma ellipse grows]
    e2 --- unc2[Sigma ellipse grows]
    e3 --- unc3[Sigma ellipse grows]
    eN --- uncN[Sigma ellipse grows]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above use a Block-Level Functional Architecture pattern, which is the recommended fallback when the topic involves physical-world drawings that are difficult to render in Mermaid. Each block represents a *functional stage* in the noise pipeline.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define **effector noise** in a mobile robot. List any **four sources** of effector noise.

**Model Answer:**
Effector noise refers to the stochastic and systematic errors introduced by the robot's actuation system (motors, gears, wheels) that cause the actual executed motion to deviate from the commanded motion. Four sources are:
1. Wheel slip on the ground surface
2. Gear backlash in the transmission
3. Wheel diameter mismatch between left and right wheels
4. Encoder quantization error

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for four sources)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Distinguish between **systematic** and **non-systematic** errors in odometry. Give **one example** of each.

**Model Answer:**
Systematic errors are deterministic, repeatable biases that accumulate proportionally to distance travelled; they can be calibrated out (e.g., wheel diameter mismatch causes a constant heading bias per metre).
Non-systematic errors are stochastic, unpredictable perturbations caused by the environment; they cannot be calibrated and must be estimated online (e.g., wheel slip on a wet floor).

*(3 marks: 1.5 for distinction, 1.5 for examples)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the **process noise covariance matrix Q** for a differential-drive mobile robot, starting from the noisy encoder measurements. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot with wheel radius $r = 0.08$ m, wheel base $L = 0.40$ m, and encoder noise $\sigma_R = \sigma_L = 0.015$ rad executes a turn. Compute $\sigma_d$ and $\sigma_\theta$ for one time step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Wheel slip is negligible during the time step.
2. Encoder noise is zero-mean Gaussian and independent between left and right wheels.
3. The kinematic model is a perfect differential drive.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1** — Write the ideal pose update equations:
$$x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta d \cos\theta_k, \quad y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta d \sin\theta_k, \quad \theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta$$

**Step 2** — Substitute the noisy encoder readings $\Delta\phi_i^{meas} = \Delta\phi_i + \delta\phi_i$ for $i \in \{R, L\}$:
$$\Delta d^{noisy} = \frac{r}{2}(\Delta\phi_R^{meas} + \Delta\phi_L^{meas}) = \Delta d + \frac{r}{2}(\delta\phi_R + \delta\phi_L)$$
$$\Delta\theta^{noisy} = \frac{r}{L}(\Delta\phi_R^{meas} - \Delta\phi_L^{meas}) = \Delta\theta + \frac{r}{L}(\delta\phi_R - \delta\phi_L)$$

**Step 3** — Apply variance propagation (independent zero-mean Gaussians):
$$\sigma_d^2 = \frac{r^2}{4}(\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_L^2), \quad \sigma_\theta^2 = \frac{r^2}{L^2}(\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_L^2)$$

**Step 4** — Project into the world frame to form Q:
$$Q = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_d^2 \cos^2\theta & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_d^2 \sin^2\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \sigma_\theta^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the process noise covariance passed to the EKF prediction step. *(7 marks: 2 for assumptions, 3 for derivation, 2 for Q matrix)*

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given $r = 0.08$ m, $L = 0.40$ m, $\sigma_R = \sigma_L = 0.015$ rad.

**Step 1** — Compute $\sigma_d$:
$$\sigma_d^2 = \frac{(0.08)^2}{4}\left[(0.015)^2 + (0.015)^2\right] = \frac{0.0064}{4}(4.5 \times 10^{-4}) = 7.2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$$
$$\sigma_d = \sqrt{7.2 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 8.49 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx 0.85 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2** — Compute $\sigma_\theta$:
$$\sigma_\theta^2 = \frac{(0.08)^2}{(0.40)^2}(4.5 \times 10^{-4}) = \frac{0.0064}{0.16}(4.5 \times 10^{-4}) = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad}^2$$
$$\sigma_\theta = \sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 4.24 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} \approx 0.243°$$

**Conclusion:** Per time step, the distance uncertainty is ~0.85 mm and the heading uncertainty is ~0.24°. After 100 such steps, the position uncertainty will grow roughly as $\sigma_x \approx \sqrt{100} \times 0.85 \text{ mm} \approx 8.5 \text{ mm}$ (random-walk growth). *(7 marks: 2 for σ_d, 2 for σ_θ, 3 for conclusion with random-walk interpretation)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a **block diagram** how effector noise propagates from the motor command to the pose estimate. Discuss the role of the **EKF Jacobian F_x** in this propagation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **odometry**, **dead reckoning**, and **inertial navigation** as methods to estimate robot pose in the presence of effector noise. Which method is most robust to wheel slip? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Propagation Pipeline (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):**
1. Controller sends PWM command → Motor driver applies voltage.
2. Motor converts voltage to angular velocity, introducing **torque ripple noise**.
3. Gear train introduces **backlash dead-zone**.
4. Wheels contact ground; slip and skid introduce **non-systematic noise**.
5. Encoders quantize rotation into discrete ticks → **quantization noise**.
6. Kinematic model $f(x, u)$ integrates these noisy wheel readings.
7. EKF prediction step propagates the **prior covariance** $\Sigma_k$ through the linearized Jacobian $F_x$ to obtain the **predicted covariance** $\Sigma_{k+1|k}$.

**Role of F_x:**
The Jacobian $F_x = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ captures how a small change in the current pose $(x, y, \theta)$ affects the next pose. Specifically:
$$\Sigma_{k+1|k} = F_x \Sigma_{k|k} F_x^T + F_u Q F_u^T$$
The first term is the **drift contribution** — uncertainty from the previous step carried forward. The second term is the **process noise injection** — new uncertainty added by effector noise. Without $F_x$, the EKF would be unable to model the rotational coupling between heading and position that produces the famous "banana-shaped" uncertainty ellipses seen in dead reckoning. *(7 marks: 3 for pipeline, 2 for EKF role, 2 for banana-shape insight)*

**Model Solution to (b):**

| Method | Sensor | Susceptibility to Effector Noise | Slip Robustness |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Odometry** | Wheel encoders | Very high — direct integration of wheel ticks | Poor |
| **Dead Reckoning** | Wheel encoders + heading gyro | High but with gyro compensation | Moderate |
| **Inertial Navigation (INS)** | IMU — accelerometers + gyroscopes | Independent of wheel slip | Excellent (short term) |

**Analysis:**
- **Odometry** is the most direct victim of effector noise because every wheel slip is integrated into the pose estimate with no compensation.
- **Dead reckoning** adds a heading sensor (gyro), which reduces the *angular* error accumulation but still suffers from translational drift when wheels slip.
- **Inertial Navigation** uses accelerometers, which measure true body acceleration regardless of wheel contact. Therefore, **INS is the most robust to wheel slip** among these three methods. However, INS suffers from its own error sources — accelerometer bias and double-integration of bias into position — making it accurate only for **short durations**.

**Justification for slip robustness:** INS does not depend on wheel-ground contact; it senses acceleration in an **inertial frame**, so slipping wheels do not corrupt the acceleration measurement. In modern robots, INS is fused with odometry via an EKF to combine the *short-term accuracy of INS* with the *long-term stability of odometry*. *(7 marks: 2 for comparison table, 2 for slip robustness discussion, 3 for INS justification + fusion note)*

---

### ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the wheel-base squared in $\sigma_\theta$:** Many students write $\sigma_\theta^2 = r^2 (\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_L^2)$ and miss the $1/L^2$ factor. This is a **2-mark deduction** on a typical 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Confusing the wheel-space Q with the world-frame Q:** The encoder noise $\sigma_R, \sigma_L$ must be propagated through $F_u$ to give the world-frame $Q$. Skipping $F_u$ is a **3-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Forgetting to state assumptions:** The KTU 2024 marking scheme explicitly allocates 1–2 marks for stating the zero-mean, independence, and Gaussian assumptions.
> 4. **Mixing units:** Always convert degrees to radians when computing $\sigma_\theta$ numerically. Mixing degrees and radians in $Q$ will yield a **completely wrong covariance** — a guaranteed 0 for the numerical part.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Effector noise** = stochastic + systematic errors from the robot's actuation system (motors, gears, wheels, encoders).
- **Four primary sources:** wheel slip, backlash, wheel-mismatch, encoder quantization.
- **Two error types:** Systematic (calibratable) and Non-systematic (must be estimated online).
- **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $\sigma_d^2 = \frac{r^2}{4}(\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_L^2)$
  - $\sigma_\theta^2 = \frac{r^2}{L^2}(\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_L^2)$
  - $\Sigma_{k+1} = F_x \Sigma_k F_x^T + F_u Q F_u^T$
- **Pose update is non-linear** because of $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ — hence the EKF uses the Jacobian $F_x$.
- **Drift scales as $\sqrt{d}$** for random errors and as $d$ for systematic errors.
- **Heading error dominates** position error over long distances in dead reckoning.
- **Slip ratio** $\lambda$ is the standard metric to quantify wheel-ground interaction.
- **Q is a 3×3 matrix** in $(x, y, \theta)$ — world-frame process noise covariance.
- **F_u projects wheel-space noise into world-frame noise** — never skip it.
- **INS is the most slip-robust** pure method, but is fused with odometry for long-term stability.
- The **banana-shaped covariance ellipse** is a signature of EKF-based dead reckoning with effector noise.
- **ISO 26262 / SIL-rated** autonomous systems must upper-bound the effector noise variance for safety certification.
- Always state **assumptions** (zero-mean, independent, Gaussian) in derivations — **1–2 marks** in KTU valuation.
- **Keep units consistent** — radians for angles, meters for distances; mixed units = full credit loss on numerical parts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Error Model for Odometric Position Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Error Model for Odometric Position Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Odometric Position Estimation** is the process of determining a mobile robot's instantaneous pose $(x, y, \theta)$ by integrating incremental wheel encoder measurements over time. An **Error Model for Odometric Position Estimation** is a mathematical framework that quantifies and propagates the uncertainties (both deterministic and stochastic) introduced during this integration, allowing the system to express confidence bounds on the estimated pose in the form of a position error covariance matrix $P$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> *"The Error Model characterizes how odometric measurements deviate from ground truth. It separates errors into Systematic (bias-type) components that can be calibrated away, and Non-Systematic (noise-type) components that are statistically modeled using covariance matrices."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Odometer Walking" Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded in a large field and asked to walk to a target using only a pedometer (step counter) and a compass. Every step counts toward distance, and every turn changes your heading.

- If the pedometer is **calibrated wrong** (slightly long strides), you'll consistently overshoot — this is a **Systematic Error**.
- If the ground is uneven or your foot occasionally slips, your step length randomly varies — this is a **Non-Systematic Error**.

Now, after 1000 steps, the pedometer says you're at a certain spot, but in reality you could be anywhere inside an **ellipse of uncertainty** that has grown with distance walked. The **Error Model** is exactly the math behind drawing that uncertainty ellipse from step-by-step noise.

### 1.3 Categorization of Odometric Errors

| Error Type | Sub-Category | Origin | Treatable? |
|------------|--------------|--------|------------|
| **Systematic** | Deterministic Bias | Calibration (wheel diameter, wheelbase) | Yes — by calibration |
| **Systematic** | Deterministic Bias | Misalignment of wheels | Yes — by measurement |
| **Non-Systematic** | Stochastic Noise | Floor irregularities, slip | No — modeled via covariance |
| **Non-Systematic** | Stochastic Noise | Debris, uneven loading | No — modeled via covariance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Systematic errors cause the pose estimate to *drift consistently* in one direction. Non-systematic errors cause the pose estimate to *spread out* (uncertainty grows). Both are present in any real robot.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Wheel Encoder Resolution (TICKS):** $\Delta_{enc} = 2\pi \cdot N_{ticks} / G_{ratio}$
- **Encoder Resolution Unit:** **ticks/revolution**
- **Position Error Growth Rate:** Typically **2–5 % of distance traveled**
- **Angle Error Growth Rate:** Typically **1–3° per meter traveled**
- **Standard Update Rate:** **20–100 Hz** for the odometry update loop
- **Covariance Update Frequency:** Matches encoder tick rate

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Odometry Update Equations (Ideal Case)

For a differential-drive robot, given encoder tick increments $\Delta n_L$ and $\Delta n_R$ for left and right wheels over one time step $\Delta t$:

$$\Delta U = \frac{\Delta n_R + \Delta n_L}{2} \cdot c_m$$

$$\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta n_R - \Delta n_L}{b} \cdot c_m$$

where $c_m$ is the **distance-per-tick** constant (meters per encoder tick), and $b$ is the **wheelbase**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** $\Delta U$ is the **midpoint displacement** along the robot's instantaneous circular arc, and $\Delta \theta$ is the **change in heading**.

### 2.2 Pose Update Law (Ideal)

The updated pose from $(x, y, \theta)$ to $(x', y', \theta')$:

$$x' = x + \Delta U \cdot \cos\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)$$

$$y' = y + \Delta U \cdot \sin\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)$$

$$\theta' = \theta + \Delta \theta$$

This is the **midpoint integration** scheme — accurate to second order.

### 2.3 Error Sources — Systematic Decomposition

Let the **true** wheel parameters be $D_R, D_L$ (actual wheel diameters) and $b$ (true wheelbase), while the **nominal** parameters used in software be $\hat{D}_R, \hat{D}_L, \hat{b}$.

**Error Parameters (Systematic):**

$$E_d = \frac{D_R + D_L}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\hat{D}} \quad \text{(Scale Error on Translation)}$$

$$E_b = \frac{b}{\hat{b}} \quad \text{(Scale Error on Wheelbase)}$$

**Effective Error Variables used in estimation:**

$$\hat{c}_m = c_m \cdot (1 + E_d) \quad \text{(Distance per tick with scale error)}$$

$$\hat{b}_{eff} = \frac{b}{1 + E_b} \quad \text{(Effective wheelbase)}$$

### 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Unit / Type** |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\Delta U = \frac{\Delta n_R + \Delta n_L}{2} \cdot c_m$ | Incremental translation | meters (m) |
| 2 | $\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta n_R - \Delta n_L}{b} \cdot c_m$ | Incremental rotation | radians (rad) |
| 3 | $c_m = \frac{\pi D}{N \cdot G_r}$ | Distance per tick | m/tick |
| 4 | $x' = x + \Delta U \cos(\theta + \Delta\theta/2)$ | X update | meters |
| 5 | $y' = y + \Delta U \sin(\theta + \Delta\theta/2)$ | Y update | meters |
| 6 | $\theta' = \theta + \Delta\theta$ | Heading update | radians |
| 7 | $\sigma_x^2(t) = k_x^2 \cdot d(t)$ | X variance growth | m² |
| 8 | $\sigma_y^2(t) = k_y^2 \cdot d(t)$ | Y variance growth | m² |
| 9 | $\sigma_\theta^2(t) = k_\theta^2 \cdot d(t)$ | Heading variance growth | rad² |
| 10 | $P_{k+1} = J \cdot P_k \cdot J^T + Q$ | Covariance propagation | matrix |
| 11 | $Q = \text{diag}(\sigma_U^2, \sigma_\theta^2)$ | Process noise covariance | matrix |
| 12 | $\sigma_U^2 = K_U \cdot \vert \Delta U \vert$ | Translation noise variance | m² |
| 13 | $\sigma_\theta^2 = K_\theta \cdot \vert \Delta U \vert$ | Rotation noise variance | rad² |
| 14 | $c^2 = s^2 \cdot 2 \cdot (1 - \cos\Delta\theta)$ | Chord correction | m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** The constants $K_U$ and $K_\theta$ (sometimes $k_1$ and $k_2$ in textbooks) are **robot-specific error coefficients** determined experimentally by repeated traversal. They represent the *expected error per meter of travel* in translation and rotation respectively.

### 2.5 Non-Systematic (Stochastic) Error Model

The **error growth model** assumes variance grows proportionally to distance traveled $d$:

$$\Sigma(t) = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x^2 & \sigma_{xy}^2 & \sigma_{x\theta}^2 \\ \sigma_{xy}^2 & \sigma_y^2 & \sigma_{y\theta}^2 \\ \sigma_{x\theta}^2 & \sigma_{y\theta}^2 & \sigma_\theta^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

with **diagonal scaling** (simplified form):

$$\sigma_x^2(t) = k_x^2 \cdot d(t)$$

$$\sigma_y^2(t) = k_y^2 \cdot d(t)$$

$$\sigma_\theta^2(t) = k_\theta^2 \cdot d(t)$$

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

This error model is the **backbone of autonomous navigation** for:

- **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** — provides motion prediction priors.
- **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** — uses the Jacobian $\nabla f$ of the odometry function for state propagation.
- **Particle Filters** — weight particles by their likelihood under the error ellipse.
- **Dead Reckoning Validation** — the uncertainty ellipse gates when to trust odometry vs. external sensors (GPS, LIDAR).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Odometric Error Covariance Propagation Law

**Goal:** Derive the recursive covariance update $P_{k+1} = \nabla f_x P_k \nabla f_x^T + \nabla f_u Q \nabla f_u^T$ for the odometric pose update.

**Step 1 — Define the State and Control Vectors**

$$x_k = \begin{bmatrix} x_k \\ y_k \\ \theta_k \end{bmatrix}, \quad u_k = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta U_k \\ \Delta \theta_k \end{bmatrix}$$
