The nonlinear update function is $f(x_k, u_k)$:

$$x_{k+1} = f(x_k, u_k) = \begin{bmatrix} x_k + \Delta U_k \cos(\theta_k + \Delta\theta_k/2) \\ y_k + \Delta U_k \sin(\theta_k + \Delta\theta_k/2) \\ \theta_k + \Delta\theta_k \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Jacobian with Respect to the State ($\nabla f_x$)**

$$\nabla f_x = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -\Delta U \sin(\theta + \Delta\theta/2) \\ 0 & 1 & \Delta U \cos(\theta + \Delta\theta/2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This step uses the **chain rule** on the trigonometric terms, treating $\theta + \Delta\theta/2$ as the effective heading during the arc traversal.

**Step 3 — Compute the Jacobian with Respect to Control ($\nabla f_u$)**

$$\nabla f_u = \frac{\partial f}{\partial u} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta + \Delta\theta/2) & -\frac{1}{2}\Delta U \sin(\theta + \Delta\theta/2) \\ \sin(\theta + \Delta\theta/2) & \frac{1}{2}\Delta U \cos(\theta + \Delta\theta/2) \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Each column corresponds to a partial derivative with respect to $\Delta U$ and $\Delta \theta$ respectively.

**Step 4 — Define the Process Noise Covariance $Q_k$**

The control increments $\Delta U$ and $\Delta \theta$ are themselves noisy. Their variances scale with motion magnitude:

$$Q_k = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{\Delta U}^2 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_{\Delta \theta}^2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} K_U \vert \Delta U \vert & 0 \\ 0 & K_\theta \vert \Delta U \vert \end{bmatrix}$$

The **off-diagonal** is zero because encoder left/right noise is often assumed independent.

**Step 5 — Compose the Propagation Law**

The **Taylor expansion** of $f$ around $(x_k, u_k)$ yields:

$$P_{k+1} = \nabla f_x \, P_k \, \nabla f_x^T + \nabla f_u \, Q_k \, \nabla f_u^T$$

This is the fundamental error model used in **EKF-SLAM** and most probabilistic mobile robot architectures.

### 3.2 Derivation: Systematic Error Bias Coupling

**Goal:** Show how a wheel diameter error $\epsilon_D$ accumulates over distance.

**Step 1 — Single-step displacement under bias:**

A wheel of true diameter $D$ but nominal diameter $\hat{D}$ produces an *overestimated* distance per revolution by factor:

$$E_d = \frac{\hat{D} - D}{D} \quad \text{(signed relative error)}$$

**Step 2 — Displacement per tick:**

$$\Delta U_{true} = c_{m,true} \cdot \Delta n = \frac{\pi D}{N G_r} \Delta n$$

$$\Delta U_{measured} = c_{m,nom} \cdot \Delta n = \frac{\pi \hat{D}}{N G_r} \Delta n = \Delta U_{true}(1 + E_d)$$

**Step 3 — Accumulated translation bias after $N_{steps}$ steps:**

$$B_U = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{steps}} \Delta U_{true,i} \cdot E_d = E_d \cdot d_{total}$$

where $d_{total}$ is the total path length. **The translation bias grows linearly with distance.**

**Step 4 — Equivalent angular bias due to wheelbase error:**

A wheelbase error $E_b$ rotates the robot by an extra angle per arc:

$$B_\theta = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{steps}} \frac{\Delta U_i}{\hat{b}} \cdot E_b = \frac{E_b}{\hat{b}} \cdot d_{total}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem:** A differential drive robot with $D_L = D_R = 0.20$ m, $b = 0.50$ m travels along a straight line for 5 m. The encoders report $\Delta n_L = 2500$ and $\Delta n_R = 2510$ ticks. Given $N = 1000$ ticks/rev and $G_r = 1$, find the **position error covariance** after 1 m of travel assuming $K_U = 0.05$ m²/m and $K_\theta = 0.02$ rad²/m.

**Step 1 — Compute $c_m$:**

$$c_m = \frac{\pi D}{N G_r} = \frac{\pi \times 0.20}{1000 \times 1} = 6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/tick}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\Delta U$ and $\Delta \theta$:**

$$\Delta U = \frac{2500 + 2510}{2} \times 6.2832 \times 10^{-4} = 1.5710 \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta \theta = \frac{2510 - 2500}{0.50} \times 6.2832 \times 10^{-4} = 0.01257 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Pose update (initial pose $(0, 0, 0)$):**

$$x' = 0 + 1.5710 \cdot \cos(0.006285) = 1.57097 \text{ m}$$

$$y' = 0 + 1.5710 \cdot \sin(0.006285) = 0.00986 \text{ m}$$

$$\theta' = 0 + 0.01257 \text{ rad} = 0.72°$$

**Step 4 — Process noise variance after 1 m:**

$$\sigma_{\Delta U}^2 = K_U \cdot \vert \Delta U \vert = 0.05 \times 1.5710 = 0.07855 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\sigma_{\Delta \theta}^2 = K_\theta \cdot \vert \Delta U \vert = 0.02 \times 1.5710 = 0.03142 \text{ rad}^2$$

**Step 5 — Final pose with uncertainty:**

$$\hat{x} = (1.57097, \ 0.00986, \ 0.01257)$$

$$\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 0.07855 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.07855 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.03142 \end{bmatrix} \text{ (simplified diagonal model)}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Which Formula Handles Which Error

| **Error Type** | **Mathematical Tool** | **KTU-Recognized Formula** | **Mark Importance** |
|----------------|----------------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| Wheel diameter mismatch | Scale factor $E_d$ | $\Delta U_{true}(1+E_d)$ | **High (7+ marks)** |
| Wheelbase mismatch | Angular scale $E_b$ | $\Delta \theta_{true} / (1+E_b)$ | **High (7+ marks)** |
| Wheel slip | Stochastic noise $Q$ | $\sigma_U^2 = K_U \vert \Delta U \vert$ | **High (14 marks)** |
| Encoder quantization | Discrete tick noise | $Q_{enc} = (c_m/2)^2$ | Medium (3 marks) |
| Floor unevenness | Heading drift | $\sigma_\theta^2 = K_\theta \vert \Delta U \vert$ | **High (7+ marks)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of the Odometric Error Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wheel Encoders Left and Right] --> B[Tick Delta Computation]
    B --> C[Convert to Delta U and Delta Theta]
    C --> D[Nominal Parameter Application]
    D --> E{Pose Update Function}
    E --> F[Updated Pose x y theta]
    G[Error Parameter Estimation] --> H[Systematic Error E_d E_b]
    H --> I[Error Covariance Matrix Q]
    I --> J[Kalman Filter Propagation]
    J --> K[Posterior Covariance P k plus 1]
    F --> L[Final Position Estimate with Uncertainty]
    K --> L
```

### 4.2 Error Propagation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[State xk yk thetak] --> nodeB[Compute Jacobian Fx]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Propagate Prior Covariance Pk]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Add Process Noise Qk]
    nodeD --> nodeE[New Covariance Pk plus 1]
    nodeF[Control u k Delta U Delta theta] --> nodeG[Compute Jacobian Fu]
    nodeG --> nodeD
    nodeE --> nodeH[Output Uncertainty Ellipse]
```

### 4.3 Architecture of the Error Model Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Raw Encoder Pulses] --> node2[Counters and Ticks]
    node2 --> node3[Parameter Block D, b, N, Gr]
    node3 --> node4[Compute cm and wheelbase]
    node4 --> node5[Compute Delta U and Delta theta]
    node5 --> node6[Apply Systematic Correction E_d, E_b]
    node6 --> node7[Pose Update x prime, y prime, theta prime]
    node7 --> node8[Covariance Update Pk plus 1]
    node8 --> node9[Publish to Navigation Stack]
    node5 --> node10[Non Systematic Noise Qk]
    node10 --> node8
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow: When to Trust Odometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Odometry Update] --> nodeB{Is Slip Detected?}
    nodeB -- YES --> nodeC[Inflate Q matrix]
    nodeB -- NO --> nodeD{Is Floor Known Smooth?}
    nodeD -- YES --> nodeE[Use Default K_U K_theta]
    nodeD -- NO --> nodeF[Inflate K_theta]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Propagate P with high uncertainty]
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Send to EKF for sensor fusion]
```

### 4.5 Uncertainty Ellipse Evolution (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT0[Start t=0 sigma=0] --> nodeT1[After 1 m sigma small]
    nodeT1 --> nodeT2[After 5 m sigma moderate]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3[After 20 m sigma large]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT4[After 100 m sigma very large]
    nodeT0 --> nodeC[Injected Noise Qk]
    nodeC --> nodeT1
    nodeT1 --> nodeC
    nodeT2 --> nodeC
    nodeT3 --> nodeC
    nodeT4 --> nodeC
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the odometric error model. Differentiate between systematic and non-systematic errors with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**

The odometric error model is a probabilistic representation of the uncertainty in pose $(x, y, \theta)$ obtained by integrating wheel encoder data. It quantifies deviations from ground truth.

- **Systematic errors** are *deterministic biases* caused by imperfection in the robot's mechanical and calibration parameters. Examples: wheel diameter mismatch, wheelbase error, and wheel misalignment. These can be measured and corrected via calibration.
- **Non-systematic errors** are *stochastic disturbances* caused by the environment. Examples: wheel slip, uneven flooring, debris, and asymmetric load. These cannot be eliminated and must be statistically modeled using variance.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for differentiation with examples)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the equations for incremental translation $\Delta U$ and rotation $\Delta \theta$ in differential drive odometry and explain the meaning of each term.*

**Model Answer:**

$$\Delta U = \frac{\Delta n_R + \Delta n_L}{2} \cdot c_m$$

$$\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta n_R - \Delta n_L}{b} \cdot c_m$$

where:
- $\Delta n_L, \Delta n_R$ are the **incremental encoder counts** for left and right wheels in one time step.
- $c_m$ is the **distance-per-tick** factor (meters per tick), given by $\pi D / (N G_r)$.
- $b$ is the **wheelbase** (distance between wheel contact points).

$\Delta U$ represents the **arc length traveled** by the midpoint of the axle. $\Delta \theta$ is the **change in heading angle**.

(3 marks: 1 each for equations, 1 for explanation)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Derivation + Application**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the pose update equations of a differential drive mobile robot using the midpoint integration method. Show the resulting position $(x', y', \theta')$ in terms of the current pose and incremental motion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential drive robot with wheel diameter $D = 0.25$ m, wheelbase $b = 0.50$ m, encoder resolution $N = 2000$ ticks/rev, and gear ratio $G_r = 1$ records $\Delta n_L = 4000$ and $\Delta n_R = 4100$ ticks in one update. Initial pose: $(0, 0, 0)$. Determine:

- (i) The distance per tick $c_m$. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The incremental translation $\Delta U$ and rotation $\Delta \theta$. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The new pose $(x', y', \theta')$. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** *(Refer to Section 3.1 Step 1 derivation)*

The differential drive kinematics with midpoint integration:

$$x' = x + \Delta U \cos\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$$

$$y' = y + \Delta U \sin\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$$

$$\theta' = \theta + \Delta \theta$$

This scheme is second-order accurate because it uses the **midpoint heading** $\theta + \Delta\theta/2$ for the trigonometric projection rather than $\theta$.

**Part (b):**

(i) $c_m = \frac{\pi D}{N G_r} = \frac{\pi \times 0.25}{2000 \times 1} = 3.927 \times 10^{-4}$ m/tick

(ii) $\Delta U = \frac{4100 + 4000}{2} \times 3.927 \times 10^{-4} = 1.5900$ m

$$\Delta \theta = \frac{4100 - 4000}{0.50} \times 3.927 \times 10^{-4} = 0.07854 \text{ rad}$$

(iii) Midpoint heading: $\theta + \Delta\theta/2 = 0.03927$ rad

$$x' = 0 + 1.5900 \times \cos(0.03927) = 0 + 1.5900 \times 0.99923 = 1.5888 \text{ m}$$

$$y' = 0 + 1.5900 \times \sin(0.03927) = 0 + 1.5900 \times 0.03925 = 0.0624 \text{ m}$$

$$\theta' = 0 + 0.07854 = 0.07854 \text{ rad} = 4.50°$$

**Final Pose:** $(1.5888, \ 0.0624, \ 0.07854 \text{ rad})$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the systematic error model for differential drive odometry. Derive expressions for the translation error $E_d$ and the wheelbase error $E_b$ and show how they affect the computed displacement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a differential drive robot, the following data is observed during a straight-line calibration run of 10 m: $D_L = 0.1005$ m, $D_R = 0.0995$ m, $b_{true} = 0.500$ m, $b_{nominal} = 0.498$ m. Encoder counts recorded: $\Delta n_L = 15800$, $\Delta n_R = 15600$. Compute:

- (i) $E_d$ and $E_b$. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) The true and nominal displacements. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The accumulated position bias after the 10 m run. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** *(Refer to Section 3.2 derivation)*

Systematic errors arise from parameter mismatches. If $\hat{D}$ is the nominal diameter and $D$ is the true diameter, the **translation scale error** is:

$$E_d = \frac{\hat{D} - D}{D}$$

The **wheelbase error** is:

$$E_b = \frac{\hat{b} - b}{b}$$

These biases are deterministic and accumulate linearly with distance, causing the pose estimate to drift by $B_U = E_d \cdot d_{total}$ and the heading to drift by $B_\theta = (E_b / \hat{b}) \cdot d_{total}$.

**Part (b):**

(i) Mean true diameter: $D = (0.1005 + 0.0995)/2 = 0.1000$ m

Nominal diameter assumed: $\hat{D} = 0.1000$ m (assume matched)

$$E_d = \frac{0.1000 - 0.1000}{0.1000} = 0$$

$$E_b = \frac{0.498 - 0.500}{0.500} = -0.004 \quad (-0.4\%)$$

(ii) With $N = 1000$ ticks/rev, $c_m = \pi \times 0.100 / 1000 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}$ m/tick

True displacement (using $b_{true} = 0.500$):
$$\Delta U_{true} = \frac{15800 + 15600}{2} \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} = 4.9349 \text{ m}$$
$$\Delta \theta_{true} = \frac{15600 - 15800}{0.500} \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} = -0.001257 \text{ rad}$$

Nominal displacement (using $b_{nom} = 0.498$):
$$\Delta \theta_{nom} = \frac{15600 - 15800}{0.498} \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} = -0.001262 \text{ rad}$$

(iii) For a 10 m run with $E_d = 0$ and $E_b = -0.004$:

Translation bias: $B_U = 0 \times 10 = 0$ m

Angular bias: $B_\theta = (-0.004 / 0.498) \times 10 = -0.0803$ rad $= -4.60°$

**Result:** The robot would appear to have turned 4.60° extra clockwise compared to ground truth.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mistake in $c_m$:** Students often forget to include $G_r$ in the denominator, leading to a factor-of-2 or factor-of-10 error. Always write $c_m = \pi D / (N G_r)$.
> 2. **Midpoint Heading vs. Start Heading:** Using $\theta$ instead of $\theta + \Delta\theta/2$ in the cosine/sine terms loses 1–2 marks for failing to apply midpoint integration.
> 3. **Sign of $E_b$:** Confusing the sign of the wheelbase bias (positive vs. negative) leads to wrong sign on angular drift.
> 4. **Covariance Dimension:** Some students write $P$ without specifying units. The KTU key demands $\sigma^2$ in m² for translation and rad² for rotation.
> 5. **Forgetting to Update $P$ in EKF:** In a multi-step problem, students often compute the first $P_{k+1}$ correctly but fail to use it as $P_k$ for the next step.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2**

- [x] **Odometry** = integration of wheel encoder counts to estimate pose $(x, y, \theta)$.
- [x] **Distance per tick:** $c_m = \pi D / (N G_r)$ — *always* include gear ratio.
- [x] **Translation per step:** $\Delta U = (\Delta n_R + \Delta n_L) c_m / 2$.
- [x] **Rotation per step:** $\Delta \theta = (\Delta n_R - \Delta n_L) c_m / b$.
- [x] **Midpoint integration pose update** uses heading $\theta + \Delta\theta/2$ (not $\theta$).
- [x] **Systematic errors** are *deterministic*: scale factor $E_d$ and wheelbase factor $E_b$.
- [x] **Non-systematic errors** are *stochastic*: modeled with $K_U$ and $K_\theta$ coefficients.
- [x] **Variance growth:** $\sigma^2$ scales **linearly** with distance traveled.
- [x] **Covariance propagation:** $P_{k+1} = \nabla f_x P_k \nabla f_x^T + \nabla f_u Q_k \nabla f_u^T$.
- [x] **Process noise:** $Q = \text{diag}(K_U \vert \Delta U \vert, \ K_\theta \vert \Delta U \vert)$.
- [x] **Systematic bias accumulates** linearly with distance — calibration reduces but does not eliminate drift.
- [x] **Slip, bumps, debris** → non-systematic noise source → inflate $Q$ matrix in EKF.
- [x] **Uncertainty ellipse** grows with distance — used by EKF/Particle filter for sensor fusion.
- [x] **KTU often asks for derivation** of pose update with midpoint method (7 marks).
- [x] **KTU often asks for** identification of error types in a scenario-based problem (3 marks).
- [x] **Always convert radian to degree** in final numerical answers: multiply by $180/\pi$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization-Based Navigation versus Programmed Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Localization-Based Navigation versus Programmed Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Localization-Based Navigation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Localization-Based Navigation (Map-Based Navigation):** A reactive, sensor-driven navigation paradigm in which a mobile robot continuously estimates its own pose (position and orientation) within a known or concurrently built map of the environment, and dynamically re-plans its trajectory to reach a target while avoiding obstacles.

The robot does **not** blindly follow a fixed sequence of motor commands. Instead, it repeatedly performs the perception → localization → planning → action cycle, making it resilient to wheel slip, drift, environmental change, and dynamic obstacles. The governing cognitive loop is the classical **Sense–Plan–Act** architecture.

The dominant mathematical framework is **Bayesian Probabilistic Localization**, typically expressed by the recursive update:

$$
\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

where:
- $x_t$ = robot pose at time $t$
- $z_t$ = sensor measurement
- $u_t$ = control command (odometry)
- $m$ = environment map
- $\eta$ = normalization constant

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This is the conceptual backbone of **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs)** — the modern successor to Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs).

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Programmed Solutions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Programmed Solutions (Pre-scripted / Open-Loop Navigation):** A deterministic, feed-forward navigation scheme in which the entire robot trajectory is encoded as a fixed sequence of waypoints, joint angles, or wheel velocities **before execution begins**, with little or no feedback from the environment during motion.

The robot essentially "plays back" a stored script. Examples include:
- Teach-pendant trajectories in industrial arms
- Line-following AGVs with magnetic tape guides
- Hard-coded roomba edge-following routines
- Pre-computed CNC G-code paths

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

| Navigation Style | Human Analogy | Robot Analogy |
|-----------------|---------------|---------------|
| **Localization-Based** | Driving a car using Google Maps + GPS | An autonomous vacuum (Roomba) mapping the room |
| **Programmed Solutions** | A driver following memorized turn-by-turn instructions given once at the start | A factory conveyor robot repeating the same welding path 1,000 times per shift |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:** *Localization = "Look, think, move."* *Programmed = "Just move."*

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **Pose:** $(x, y, \theta)$ — the 2-D position and heading of the robot.
- **Odometry:** Estimation of pose change from wheel encoders.
- **SLAM:** **S**imultaneous **L**ocalization **a**nd **M**apping — building the map *and* localizing at the same time.
- **Waypoint:** A stored $(x, y)$ coordinate that the robot must pass through in order.
- **Dead Reckoning:** Open-loop pose estimation by integration of velocity commands.
- **Belief State:** Probability distribution over possible robot poses.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of Localization-Based Navigation

The pipeline is structured as a closed-loop Sense–Plan–Act cycle:

1. **Sensing (Perception):** Onboard sensors (LiDAR, camera, IMU, wheel encoders, GPS) capture raw data about the surroundings and self-motion.
2. **Feature Extraction:** Landmarks, edges, or point clouds are identified from raw sensor data.
3. **Localization:** The robot's pose $x_t$ is estimated by matching observed features to the stored map $m$ using probabilistic filters (Kalman, Particle, EKF).
4. **Path Planning:** A global path (A*, Dijkstra, RRT*) is computed from current pose to goal; a local planner (DWA, MPC) handles immediate obstacles.
5. **Motion Control:** Velocity commands $u_t = (v, \omega)$ are issued to the drive system.
6. **Loop Closure / Feedback:** The new pose becomes the prior for the next iteration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** Feedback from sensors compensates for model uncertainty, wheel slip, and environmental change, producing a self-correcting system.

## 2.2 Operational Pipeline of Programmed Solutions

1. **Pre-computation (Off-line):** The trajectory (sequence of joint angles, wheel velocities, or $(x,y)$ waypoints) is either taught by a human operator or computed off-line.
2. **Storage:** The trajectory is stored in non-volatile memory (EEPROM, flash, SD card).
3. **Execution (On-line):** The controller reads the script and commands the actuators at the prescribed timestamps.
4. **Optional Open-Loop Verification:** Limit switches or hard interlocks may abort motion, but no spatial feedback is used to correct trajectory deviations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Limitation:** Because there is no closed-loop spatial feedback, any drift, slip, or environmental disturbance is **invisible** to the controller and accumulates unboundedly.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

| Parameter | Localization-Based Navigation | Programmed Solutions |
|-----------|------------------------------|----------------------|
| **Feedback Type** | Closed-loop (Sense–Plan–Act) | Open-loop (pre-scripted) |
| **Sensor Dependency** | High (LiDAR, camera, IMU) | Low / None |
| **Computational Cost** | High (onboard CPU/GPU required) | Negligible (microcontroller sufficient) |
| **Adaptability to Environment** | High — dynamic obstacles handled | Zero — environment must be frozen |
| **Robustness to Drift** | High — re-localization corrects error | Poor — error grows with distance |
| **Implementation Cost** | High (sensors + computing) | Low (just motors + driver) |
| **Setup Time** | Low after mapping | High for complex paths |
| **Repeatability** | Medium (sensor noise) | Very high (deterministic) |
| **Typical Application** | AMRs, autonomous cars, drones | AGVs with magnetic tape, CNC machines, assembly-line arms |
| **Failure Mode** | Graceful degradation | Catastrophic collision |
| **Skill Required** | Robotics engineer + AI/ML | Technician for teach-pendant |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Warehouse Logistics:** Amazon Kiva robots use localization-based SLAM; older factories still run on magnetic-tape AGVs (programmed).
- **Medical Robotics:** Da Vinci surgical arms use programmed solutions (the surgeon commands the path, but kinematic accuracy is pre-calibrated).
- **Planetary Rovers:** NASA's Perseverance uses visual localization (Visual Odometry + SLAM) — pure programming would fail on unknown terrain.
- **Agriculture:** Autonomous tractors use RTK-GPS localization; older harvesters use row-following (programmed).

## 2.5 Key Quantitative Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Accuracy floor of wheel odometry alone:** $\sim 5\text{–}10\%$ of distance traveled.
- **Typical LiDAR localization accuracy:** $\pm 2 \text{ cm}$ at $10 \text{ Hz}$.
- **Industrial robot repeatability (programmed):** $\pm 0.02 \text{ mm}$ (e.g., FANUC, KUKA).
- **Sensor refresh for real-time SLAM:** $\geq 10 \text{ Hz}$ for ground robots, $\geq 100 \text{ Hz}$ for drones.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Programmed Solutions Accumulate Error

A programmed solution typically uses **dead reckoning** for any self-localization. Consider a differential-drive robot commanded to travel straight for time $T$ at wheel velocity $v$.

**Step 1 — Wheel encoder reports displacement $d$:**

$$
d = v \cdot T
$$

**Step 2 — Assuming perfect rolling, the wheel circumference constraint gives angular rotation $\phi$:**

$$
\phi = \frac{d}{r} = \frac{v \cdot T}{r}
$$

where $r$ is the wheel radius.

**Step 3 — Ideal pose update (no slip):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + d \cos(\theta_t) \\
y_{t+1} &= y_t + d \sin(\theta_t) \\
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce slip. Let $\epsilon$ be the slip fraction (e.g., $\epsilon = 0.05$ means 5% slip). Actual displacement becomes:**

$$
d_{\text{actual}} = v \cdot T \cdot (1 - \epsilon)
$$

**Step 5 — Pose error after one segment $\Delta x_{\text{err}}$:**

$$
\Delta x_{\text{err}} = d - d_{\text{actual}} = v T \epsilon
$$

**Step 6 — Summed over $N$ segments of equal length, total error grows linearly:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} v T \epsilon_i \\
&\approx N \cdot v T \cdot \bar{\epsilon} \quad \text{(assuming average slip } \bar{\epsilon}\text{)} \\
&= L_{\text{total}} \cdot \bar{\epsilon}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $L_{\text{total}} = N v T$ is the total commanded distance.

**Conversion logic:** This algebraic chain proves that the **positional error of a programmed solution is proportional to total distance traveled**, with the proportionality constant equal to the slip ratio. Over a 100 m traverse with 5% slip, the robot ends up **5 m off-target** — usually enough to collide with obstacles or miss docking stations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Insight:** This linear-error growth is the *defining quantitative weakness* of programmed solutions. KTU examiners love asking students to *derive* this and contrast it with the bounded error of localization-based systems.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison: Bounded vs Unbounded Error

| Solution Type | Error Behavior | Governing Equation |
|---------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Programmed (dead reckoning only) | **Unbounded linear growth** | $E(t) = \bar{\epsilon} \cdot v \cdot t$ |
| Programmed (with IMU integration) | **Unbounded but slower** (drift $\sim 0.1\%$/min) | $E(t) \approx k_{\text{IMU}} \cdot t$ |
| Localization-based (Kalman filter) | **Bounded** (corrected by map match) | $\sigma_{x}^2 \le \sigma_{\text{prior}}^2$ |
| Localization-based (SLAM with loop closure) | **Globally consistent** | $E \to 0$ upon loop closure |

## 3.3 Cost-Complexity Trade-off Analysis

The computational cost of localization-based navigation is dominated by the planner. For a grid map of resolution $\delta$ covering area $A$, an A* search has complexity:

$$
\mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{A}{\delta^2} \log \frac{A}{\delta^2}\right)
$$

For a programmed solution, the cost of execution is:

$$
\mathcal{O}(N)
$$

where $N$ is the number of waypoints. This is a **factor of $10^4$–$10^6$** cheaper in real time, justifying programmed solutions in highly structured, unchanging environments.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Localization-Based Navigation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sensor Suite: LiDAR, Camera, IMU, Encoders] --> B[Perception Module: Feature Extraction]
    B --> C[Localization Module: Kalman / Particle Filter]
    M[Stored Map: Occupancy Grid or Feature Map] --> C
    C --> D[Belief State: Pose Estimate xt]
    D --> E[Global Planner: A* or RRT*]
    G[Goal Pose] --> E
    E --> F[Local Planner: DWA or MPC]
    F --> H[Motion Controller: v, omega]
    H --> I[Robot Actuators]
    I --> J[Environment]
    J -. feedback .-> A
```

**Caption:** The closed-loop Sense–Plan–Act cycle. The dashed feedback arrow is what fundamentally distinguishes this from programmed navigation.

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Programmed Solutions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Off-line Trajectory Generator: Teach Pendant or G-code] --> B[Trajectory Storage: EEPROM / Flash]
    G[Goal Specification] --> A
    B --> C[Trajectory Interpolator]
    C --> D[Motor Driver: PWM / Step Pulses]
    D --> E[Robot Actuators]
    E --> F[Environment]
    F -. optional interlocks .-> D
```

**Caption:** Open-loop pipeline. The dashed line represents *only* hardware safety interlocks — **no spatial feedback for trajectory correction**.

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Which Strategy to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need a navigation solution] --> B{Is the environment static and fully known?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is the path repetitive and fixed?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Programmed Solution: Low cost, high repeatability]
    C -- No --> E[Use Localization-Based: Adapt to variant paths]
    B -- No --> F{Is on-board sensing available?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Localization / SLAM: Required for unknown terrain]
    F -- No --> H[Redesign: Add sensors or constrain environment]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture (Side-by-Side)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Localization-Based
        L1[Sense] --> L2[Plan] --> L3[Act] --> L1
    end
    subgraph Programmed
        P1[Pre-compute] --> P2[Store] --> P3[Replay] --> P2
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Differentiate between localization-based navigation and programmed solutions in mobile robots. List two real-world examples of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Localization-based navigation** uses real-time sensor feedback (LiDAR, camera, GPS) to estimate the robot's pose within a map and dynamically re-plans a path. *Examples:* Autonomous car (Waymo), Amazon Kiva warehouse robot.
- **Programmed solutions** execute a pre-stored sequence of motion commands without spatial feedback, relying on dead reckoning. *Examples:* Magnetic-tape AGV, CNC milling machine, industrial welding arm with teach-pendant. *(1 mark for definition each, 1 mark for examples.)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Why is a programmed navigation solution unsuitable for an autonomous planetary rover? Justify with reference to error accumulation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A planetary rover operates on unstructured, unknown terrain where wheel slip is unpredictable (sand, rocks). Because programmed solutions use open-loop dead reckoning, positional error grows linearly with distance traveled ($E = \bar{\epsilon} \cdot L_{\text{total}}$). Over a 1 km traverse, even 2% slip accumulates to **20 m of error** — enough to crash the rover or miss science targets. Hence, visual-odometry-based localization is mandatory for planetary exploration.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the Sense–Plan–Act architecture used in localization-based mobile robot navigation, with a neat block diagram. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the expression for accumulated position error in a programmed (dead-reckoning) solution and show that the error grows linearly with distance traveled. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
The Sense–Plan–Act cycle consists of three repeating phases:
1. **Sense:** Onboard sensors (LiDAR, camera, IMU, encoders) capture environment data $z_t$ and self-motion $u_t$.
2. **Plan:** A localization filter (e.g., EKF) updates the belief state $\text{bel}(x_t)$. A global planner (A*) computes a path from current pose to goal; a local planner (DWA) handles dynamic obstacles.
3. **Act:** The motion controller issues velocity commands $(v, \omega)$ to the drive system.

The cycle repeats at $10$–$100 \text{ Hz}$. The closed-loop nature means that any drift between planned and actual motion is detected at the next sensing step and corrected. *(4 marks for explanation, 3 marks for block diagram.)*

**Model Solution (b):**
*(See derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*
Starting from $d = vT$ and $d_{\text{actual}} = vT(1-\epsilon)$:

$$
\Delta x_{\text{err}} = vT\epsilon
$$

Summing over $N$ segments of total length $L = NvT$:

$$
E_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} vT\epsilon_i = L \cdot \bar{\epsilon}
$$

This proves **linear growth of error with distance**. A worked numerical example should follow: for $L = 100 \text{ m}$ and $\bar{\epsilon} = 0.05$, $E = 5 \text{ m}$ — unacceptable for obstacle-rich environments. *(5 marks for derivation, 2 marks for numerical example.)*

---

> **Q3B. [Internal Choice, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Compare localization-based navigation and programmed solutions across ten engineering parameters in a tabular format. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A differential-drive robot with wheel radius $r = 0.05 \text{ m}$ is commanded to travel straight for $T = 60 \text{ s}$ at wheel speed $v = 0.2 \text{ m/s}$. If the slip ratio is $\epsilon = 0.03$, compute (i) the commanded distance, (ii) the actual distance, and (iii) the accumulated position error. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
*(See the KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet in Section 2.3.)* Tabulate the ten parameters: feedback type, sensor dependency, computational cost, adaptability, robustness, cost, setup time, repeatability, application, failure mode. *(1 mark per correctly tabulated row, with 3 additional marks for insightful commentary.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Commanded distance:**
$$
d = vT = 0.2 \times 60 = 12 \text{ m}
$$

**(ii) Actual distance traveled (with slip):**
$$
d_{\text{actual}} = vT(1-\epsilon) = 12 \times (1 - 0.03) = 12 \times 0.97 = 11.64 \text{ m}
$$

**(iii) Accumulated position error:**
$$
\Delta x_{\text{err}} = d - d_{\text{actual}} = 12 - 11.64 = 0.36 \text{ m}
$$

Equivalently, using the direct formula:
$$
\Delta x_{\text{err}} = vT\epsilon = 12 \times 0.03 = 0.36 \text{ m} \;\checkmark
$$

This 36 cm error is the dead-reckoning shortfall after a single straight segment. Over many segments, errors accumulate. *(2 marks each for parts i, ii, iii; 1 mark for units/sanity check.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "odometry" with "localization."** Odometry is *open-loop* incremental dead reckoning; *localization* closes the loop with absolute map measurements. Many students lose 2–3 marks by using the terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Always specify the error behavior** (linear, bounded, or globally consistent) when comparing the two approaches — examiners award 2 marks for this specific phrase.
> 3. **In derivation questions, you MUST show the integration step** $\sum \to L \cdot \bar{\epsilon}$. Skipping the summation loses 3 marks.
> 4. **Do not omit units** in numerical answers. A "0.36" without "metres" is treated as incomplete.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Localization-Based Navigation** = closed-loop, sensor-driven, adaptive, computationally expensive, robust to drift.
- ✅ **Programmed Solutions** = open-loop, pre-scripted, cheap, deterministic, but error grows linearly with distance.
- ✅ **Dead Reckoning** is the *mathematical* core of programmed solutions and *not* a form of localization.
- ✅ **Bayesian belief update** is the formal mathematical core of probabilistic localization.
- ✅ **Error growth in programmed solutions:** $E = \bar{\epsilon} \cdot L_{\text{total}}$ (linear, unbounded).
- ✅ **Error growth in localization-based:** **bounded** by sensor/map noise floor.
- ✅ **Industrial repeatability** of programmed robotic arms: $\pm 0.02 \text{ mm}$ — unmatched by any SLAM system.
- ✅ **Typical applications matrix:** *AMR & autonomous cars → localization*; *AGV on magnetic tape, CNC, assembly arms → programmed.*
- ✅ **Loop closure** in SLAM is what makes long-term autonomy possible.
- ✅ **Decision rule:** Structured & static → programmed; unstructured or dynamic → localization-based.
- ✅ **Key formulas to memorize:** dead-reckoning pose update, A* complexity, slip-error equation.
- ✅ **Bolded exam words:** Sense–Plan–Act, dead reckoning, SLAM, belief state, waypoint, pose, odometry.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Belief Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Belief Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In probabilistic robotics, **Belief Representation** refers to the formal mathematical framework a robot uses to internally encode its subjective knowledge (and corresponding uncertainty) about its own state — such as pose $(x, y, \theta)$, velocity, map features, or the location of objects — given all the sensor measurements and control actions it has processed up to the current time step.

Formally, the robot's **belief** at time $t$ is the posterior probability distribution over the state space $\mathcal{X}$, conditioned on the history of actions and observations:

$$
bel(x_t) \;=\; p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

where:
- $x_t$ is the robot state at time $t$
- $z_{1:t} = \{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_t\}$ is the sequence of sensor measurements
- $u_{1:t} = \{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_t\}$ is the sequence of control actions

The closely related **prediction** (or prior belief before incorporating the latest measurement) is:

$$
\overline{bel}(x_t) \;=\; p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEBRT751 – Module 2):**
> Belief representation is the foundational layer for every localization algorithm. Without a clearly defined belief structure, the Bayes filter, Kalman filter, particle filter, and grid localization have nothing to operate on. KTU frequently tests the comparison between belief representation schemes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded in a long, dark corridor. You have no idea where you are, but you have:
- A rough memory of the layout (a **map**)
- A pedometer (a **noisy odometer**)
- Sporadic glimpses of light from windows (a **noisy sensor**)

Your brain does **not** store a single point "I am at step 42." Instead, it maintains a **fuzzy mental picture**: *"I am probably near step 40–45, more likely around 42, but I might be off by 5."* That fuzzy mental picture is your **belief**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:**
> Belief = "the robot's internal, uncertain, probabilistic idea of where it is." Belief Representation = "the mathematical format in which that idea is stored inside the computer."

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Symbols

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $bel(x_t)$ | Posterior belief (after measurement) | dimensionless (probability density) |
| $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ | Predicted belief (before measurement) | dimensionless |
| $x_t$ | Robot state at time $t$ | m, rad |
| $z_t$ | Measurement at time $t$ | sensor-dependent |
| $u_t$ | Control input at time $t$ | m, rad |
| $\eta$ | Normalizer in Bayes rule | dimensionless |
| $\mathcal{X}$ | State space | discrete set or $\mathbb{R}^n$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Belief Representation Schemes

Belief representations fall into two broad families based on whether the state components are assumed to be statistically independent.

**A. Decomposable (Factored) Representations**

The state is split into independent sub-components, and the joint distribution factorises:

$$
p(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i)
$$

This is computationally cheap but is **valid only when the sub-states are truly independent** (e.g., a 1D position on a flat rail). Typical example: **1D Kalman filter** with scalar Gaussian.

**B. Holistic (Non-Decomposable) Representations**

The state is treated as a single entity; no independence assumption is made. Examples: **multi-dimensional Gaussian**, **grid maps**, **particle sets**, **topological graphs**.

### 2.2 The Four Canonical Belief Representations

#### (i) Parametric / Gaussian Representation

Assumes the belief is a **multivariate normal distribution**:

$$
bel(x_t) \;=\; \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)
$$

- $\mu_t$ — mean (best estimate of the state)
- $\Sigma_t$ — covariance matrix (encodes uncertainty and cross-correlation between state components)

This is the basis of the **Kalman filter family (EKF, UKF)**. It works beautifully for unimodal, mildly non-linear problems but **fails catastrophically** for multi-modal beliefs (e.g., a robot that is equally likely to be in either of two rooms — the so-called "kidnapped robot" or "global localization" problem).

#### (ii) Grid-Based (Histogram) Representation

The continuous state space $\mathcal{X}$ is discretised into a finite set of cells $\mathcal{X} = \{x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \dots, x^{(K)}\}$, and a probability value is stored for each cell:

$$
bel(x_t^{(i)}) \;\approx\; p(x_t = x^{(i)} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}), \quad i = 1, \dots, K
$$

- The set of all $K$ probabilities forms a discrete probability vector.
- Resolution (and thus memory & CPU cost) grows **exponentially** with the dimensionality of $\mathcal{X}$ — a major drawback in high-dimensional spaces.
- Excellent for **global localization** because it can represent multiple hypotheses simultaneously.

#### (iii) Sampling-Based / Particle Representation

The belief is approximated by a finite set of weighted samples (particles):

$$
\mathcal{S}_t \;=\; \{ \langle x_t^{[m]}, w_t^{[m]} \rangle \}_{m=1}^{M}
$$

where $x_t^{[m]}$ is the state hypothesis of particle $m$, and $w_t^{[m]}$ is its non-negative weight with $\sum_m w_t^{[m]} = 1$.

- As $M \to \infty$, the empirical distribution converges to the true belief.
- Can represent **arbitrary, multi-modal, non-Gaussian** distributions.
- Suffers from the **particle deprivation** problem in long runs.
- Foundation of the **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** algorithm.

#### (iv) Topological Representation

The environment is abstracted into a graph of distinctive places (nodes) and the connections between them (edges):

$$
\mathcal{G} \;=\; (V, E), \quad V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots\}, \quad E \subseteq V \times V
$$

- Each node $v_i$ represents a visually or geometrically distinctive location.
- A discrete belief is maintained over the nodes: $bel(v_i) = p(\text{robot at node } v_i)$.
- Highly memory-efficient and well-suited to large-scale environments, but the **distinctiveness assumption** (each place must look unique) limits applicability.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Posterior belief (with current measurement) | Bayes filter update |
| 2 | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ | Predicted belief (before current measurement) | Bayes filter prediction |
| 3 | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | Bayes measurement update | Core of every filter |
| 4 | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | Markov prediction (continuous) | Prediction step |
| 5 | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \sum_{x_{t-1}} p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1})$ | Markov prediction (discrete/grid) | Grid localization |
| 6 | $bel(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ | Parametric Gaussian belief | Kalman / EKF |
| 7 | $\mathcal{S}_t = \{ x_t^{[m]}, w_t^{[m]} \}_{m=1}^{M}$ | Particle-set belief | Monte Carlo Localization |
| 8 | $\sum_i bel(x_t^{(i)}) = 1$ | Beliefs are normalised probability distributions | Validity check in exams |
| 9 | $\eta = \left[ \int p(z_t \mid x') \, \overline{bel}(x') \, dx' \right]^{-1}$ | Bayes normaliser | Derivation question |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Table Note:** In KTU exam answer sheets, equation 9 is written as $\eta = \left[ \sum_{x'} p(z_t \mid x') \, \overline{bel}(x') \right]^{-1}$ for the discrete case. Note the use of the proper mid-vertical bar `\mid` rather than a bare pipe character.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Belief Representation Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Self-driving cars (highways) | Extended Kalman Filter (Gaussian) | Unimodal tracking on structured roads |
| AGVs in warehouses | Particle filter | Multi-modal global localization |
| Indoor cleaning robots | Grid-based | Multi-room, multi-hypothesis |
| Visual SLAM drones | Topological | Large outdoor spaces, loop closure |
| Underwater AUVs | Hybrid Gaussian + Grid | Sensor-degraded environments |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes Rule to the Belief Update Equation

This is the **single most important derivation** in probabilistic robotics. KTU frequently asks students to derive the Bayes filter measurement update.

**Goal:** Express $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ in terms of the predicted belief $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ and the measurement model $p(z_t \mid x_t)$.

**Step 1 — Expand the posterior using the definition of conditional probability.**

$$
bel(x_t) \;=\; p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

*Explanation:* We condition on $x_t$ in the numerator, multiplying by the prior and dividing by the evidence (denominator). The action $u_t$ is included because it influenced the state $x_t$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Markov property (sensor measurement depends only on the current state).**

$$
p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; p(z_t \mid x_t)
$$

*Explanation:* The Markov assumption says $z_t$ is conditionally independent of all past measurements and controls given the present state $x_t$. The robot's current measurement is fully explained by where it is *now*.

**Step 3 — Recognise the prior term as the predicted belief $\overline{bel}(x_t)$.**

$$
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; \overline{bel}(x_t)
$$

*Explanation:* By definition, $\overline{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ — the state at time $t$ conditioned on everything *except* the most recent measurement $z_t$.

**Step 4 — Substitute Steps 2 and 3 into Step 1.**

$$
bel(x_t) \;=\; \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the normaliser $\eta$ because the denominator does not depend on $x_t$.**

$$
bel(x_t) \;=\; \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)
$$

where

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{1}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})} \;=\; \left[ \int p(z_t \mid x') \, \overline{bel}(x') \, dx' \right]^{-1}
$$

for continuous state spaces, and

$$
\eta \;=\; \left[ \sum_{x'} p(z_t \mid x') \, \overline{bel}(x') \right]^{-1}
$$

for discrete state spaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The integral (or sum) in the denominator is taken over *all* possible states $x'$ because the denominator represents the total probability of having observed $z_t$ regardless of where the robot actually is. The normaliser $\eta$ guarantees that the resulting belief integrates to **1** (i.e., $\int bel(x_t) \, dx_t = 1$).

### 3.2 Derivation: The Markov Prediction Step

**Goal:** Express $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ using the previous belief and the motion model.

**Step 1 — Total probability over the previous state.**

$$
\overline{bel}(x_t) \;=\; p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; \int p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, x_{t-1}) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

*Explanation:* Marginalise out $x_{t-1}$ using the law of total probability.

**Step 2 — Apply Markov to the state transition.**

$$
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, x_{t-1}) \;=\; p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})
$$

*Explanation:* The future state depends only on the previous state and the most recent control, not on the entire history.

**Step 3 — Recognise the second factor as $bel(x_{t-1})$.**

$$
p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \;=\; bel(x_{t-1})
$$

*Explanation:* $u_t$ does not retroactively affect the past state $x_{t-1}$, so it can be dropped from the conditioning set.

**Step 4 — Combine.**

$$
\overline{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

For discrete state spaces:

$$
\overline{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \sum_{x_{t-1}} p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Together, the prediction step (Section 3.2) and the update step (Section 3.1) form the **recursive Bayes filter**. Every belief representation — Gaussian, grid, particle, topological — is essentially a different way of *implementing* these two equations.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Gaussian (KF) | Grid | Particle | Topological |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Distribution shape | Unimodal | Discrete multi-modal | Arbitrary | Discrete |
| Memory | $O(n^2)$ | $O(K)$ | $O(M)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ |
| Computation per step | $O(n^3)$ | $O(K)$ | $O(M)$ | $O(\deg(v))$ |
| Handles multi-modality | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Resolution | Infinite (parametric) | Finite grid | Particle density | Place distinctiveness |
| Global localization | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Local tracking | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Kidnapped robot recovery | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | Moderate |
| Non-linear models | EKF approx | Exact | Exact | Exact |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Belief Representation Hierarchy

The following Mermaid block renders the functional architecture of belief representation schemes and their algorithmic instantiations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[State Space X] --> B{Belief Representation Scheme}
    B --> C[Parametric Gaussian]
    B --> D[Grid Based Histogram]
    B --> E[Sampling Based Particles]
    B --> F[Topological Graph]
    C --> C1[Kalman Filter]
    C --> C2[Extended Kalman Filter EKF]
    C --> C3[Unscented Kalman Filter UKF]
    D --> D1[Grid Localization]
    D --> D2[Markov Localization Discrete]
    E --> E1[Monte Carlo Localization MCL]
    E --> E2[Particle Filter]
    F --> F1[Topological Markov Localization]
    F --> F2[Distinctive Place Matching]
    C1 --> G[Localization Output Pose]
    C2 --> G
    C3 --> G
    D1 --> G
    D2 --> G
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
```

### 4.2 Bayes Filter Belief Update Cycle

The block-level functional architecture of how beliefs evolve through every time step is rendered below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Previous Belief bel x t minus 1] --> P1[Prediction Step Integral or Sum]
    P1 --> S2[Predicted Belief bar bel x t]
    S2 --> M1[Measurement Model p z t given x t]
    M1 --> U1[Bayes Update bel eta times p z t given x t times bar bel x t]
    U1 --> S3[Posterior Belief bel x t]
    Z1[Sensor Measurement z t] --> M1
    U1 --> N1[Normaliser eta]
    N1 --> U1
    S3 --> A1[Action u t plus 1]
    A1 --> P1
```

### 4.3 Particle Belief Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Initial Particle Set S t minus 1] --> P1[Apply Motion Model to Each Particle]
    P1 --> P2[Predicted Particles x bar t superscript m]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Importance Weights w t superscript m equals p z t given x bar t superscript m]
    P3 --> P4[Weighted Particle Set]
    P4 --> P5{Resampling Trigger Neff less than threshold}
    P5 -->|Yes| P6[Resample with Replacement]
    P5 -->|No| P7[Keep Current Particles]
    P6 --> P8[New Particle Set S t]
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation for Exams:** When asked to draw a Bayes filter block diagram in the KTU exam, the minimum acceptable answer includes: (1) a box for the previous belief, (2) a motion model block, (3) a predicted belief output, (4) a measurement model block receiving $z_t$, (5) a Bayes update block producing the new belief, and (6) a feedback arrow returning to step (1). Missing any of these elements typically costs 2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define belief representation in robot navigation. List any two belief representation techniques used in mobile robots.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Belief representation is the internal probabilistic data structure used by a robot to encode its uncertain knowledge about its state (e.g., position, orientation, map) conditioned on the history of sensor measurements and control actions. Mathematically, the belief at time $t$ is the posterior $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.

**Two techniques (1 mark):**
1. Parametric / Gaussian representation (used in Kalman filter family)
2. Sampling-based / Particle representation (used in Monte Carlo Localization)

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** A definition alone earns 2 marks. Naming the techniques earns the third mark. Always pair the technique with the algorithm that uses it.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between $bel(x_t)$ and $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | $bel(x_t)$ | $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Posterior after incorporating $z_t$ | Prediction before incorporating $z_t$ |
| Conditional on | $z_{1:t}$ and $u_{1:t}$ | $z_{1:t-1}$ and $u_{1:t}$ |
| Bayes filter stage | Update step | Prediction step |
| Example | Robot updates pose after seeing a landmark | Robot predicts pose after issuing a wheel-rotation command |

(1 mark per correct row; example earns the third mark.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3 (A).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Bayes filter algorithm** for belief representation. Derive the measurement update equation. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compare and contrast **Gaussian**, **grid-based**, and **particle-based** belief representations under the headings: distribution shape, memory complexity, ability to handle multi-modal beliefs, and suitability for global localization. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Bayes filter algorithm and derivation (7 marks):**

The Bayes filter is the **recursive backbone** of every belief representation in probabilistic robotics. It has two stages executed at every time step:

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

```
   bel(x_{t-1}) ---->[ Motion Model ]----> bel_bar(x_t)
                                               |
                                               v
                                [ Measurement Model p(z_t|x_t) ]
                                               |
                                               v
                              bel(x_t) = eta * p(z_t|x_t) * bel_bar(x_t)
                                               |
                                       <----- z_t
```

**Derivation of the measurement update (4 marks):**

Step 1 — Start with the definition of the posterior.

$$
bel(x_t) \;=\; p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

Step 2 — Apply Bayes' rule by conditioning on $x_t$:

$$
bel(x_t) \;=\; \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

Step 3 — Use the Markov property for the measurement:

$$
p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; p(z_t \mid x_t)
$$

Step 4 — Recognise the prior term as the predicted belief:

$$
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; \overline{bel}(x_t)
$$

Step 5 — Combine and introduce the normaliser $\eta$:

$$
bel(x_t) \;=\; \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)
$$

The denominator does not depend on $x_t$ and is absorbed into the normaliser:

$$
\eta \;=\; \left[ \sum_{x'} p(z_t \mid x') \, \overline{bel}(x') \right]^{-1} \quad \text{(discrete)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Forgetting the Markov assumption in Step 3 is the **#1 reason students lose 2 marks** on this derivation. The examiner expects the explicit statement: *"The current measurement depends only on the current state."* Skipping this statement is treated as an incomplete derivation.

---

**(b) Comparison of representation schemes (7 marks):**

| Criterion | Gaussian | Grid-Based | Particle-Based |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Distribution shape** | Unimodal, symmetric | Discrete, multi-modal | Arbitrary, multi-modal |
| **Memory complexity** | $O(n^2)$ — covariance matrix | $O(K)$ — one value per cell | $O(M)$ — one particle |
| **Multi-modal belief support** | ❌ Cannot represent | ✅ Yes, naturally | ✅ Yes, naturally |
| **Global localization** | ❌ Not suitable | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Excellent |
| **Computational cost** | $O(n^3)$ per step | $O(K)$ per step | $O(M)$ per step |
| **Best use case** | Local tracking with good prior | Small-medium maps, indoor | Any non-linear, non-Gaussian problem |

(1.5 marks per criterion × 4 criteria = 6 marks + 1 mark for overall conclusion/justification.)

---

**Q3 (B) — Internal Choice Alternative.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**(a)** Explain the **particle-based belief representation** in detail. Describe the four key steps of Monte Carlo Localization (prediction, weighting, resampling, output). **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Derive the **Markov prediction equation** used in the Bayes filter and explain its physical meaning with one example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Particle-based belief representation (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A particle-based belief approximates the posterior $bel(x_t)$ by a finite set of $M$ weighted samples:

$$
\mathcal{S}_t \;=\; \{ \langle x_t^{[m]}, w_t^{[m]} \rangle \}_{m=1}^{M}, \quad \sum_{m=1}^{M} w_t^{[m]} = 1
$$

Each particle $x_t^{[m]}$ is a hypothesis about the robot's true state, and $w_t^{[m]}$ is its importance weight.

**Four key MCL steps (1.5 marks each):**

1. **Prediction (Sampling from motion model):** For every particle $x_{t-1}^{[m]}$, draw a new sample

   $$x_t^{[m]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[m]})$$

   This propagates each hypothesis forward by sampling from the stochastic motion model.

2. **Weighting (Measurement update):** Compute the importance weight of each particle using the measurement model:

   $$w_t^{[m]} \;=\; p(z_t \mid x_t^{[m]})$$

   Particles that better explain the observed measurement receive higher weights. The weights are then normalised so that $\sum_m w_t^{[m]} = 1$.

3. **Resampling:** Draw $M$ new particles from the current set with replacement, where each particle is selected with probability proportional to its weight. This concentrates particles in high-probability regions and discards low-weight particles. A common trigger is when the **effective sample size**

   $$N_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sum_{m=1}^{M} (w_t^{[m]})^2}$$

   drops below a threshold (typically $M/2$).

4. **Output (Pose estimate):** The robot's best pose estimate is typically the **weighted mean**:

   $$\hat{x}_t \;=\; \sum_{m=1}^{M} w_t^{[m]} \, x_t^{[m]}$$

   (or the mode of the particle distribution in multi-modal cases).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why particle filters work:** As $M \to \infty$, the empirical distribution of the particle set converges to the true belief $bel(x_t)$ by the strong law of large numbers. This is what makes MCL a **consistent** estimator.

---

**(b) Markov prediction derivation (7 marks):**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Step 1 — Start from the definition of the predicted belief.

$$
\overline{bel}(x_t) \;=\; p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
$$

Step 2 — Marginalise over the previous state using total probability.

$$
\overline{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \int p(x_t, x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

$$
= \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

Step 3 — Apply Markov to the state transition.

$$
p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})
$$

Step 4 — Recognise the second factor as the previous belief.

$$
p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \;=\; bel(x_{t-1})
$$

(The conditioning on $u_t$ is dropped because $u_t$ cannot retroactively affect the past.)

Step 5 — Combine.

$$
\overline{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

For the discrete case:

$$
\overline{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \sum_{x_{t-1}} p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1})
$$

**Physical meaning (2 marks):** The prediction equation says: *"To predict where the robot might be after the next control, sum (or integrate) over all places it could have been a moment ago, weighted by how likely each place was (the previous belief), and then propagate each of those through the motion model."*

**Example:** A differential-drive robot issues the command $u_t =$ "move forward 0.5 m." The motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ returns a Gaussian centered at $x_{t-1} + 0.5$ m (with some noise). The new predicted belief is the convolution of the old belief with this Gaussian — intuitively, the old belief "smears" forward.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Particle Filters):**
> Three common errors that cost marks:
> 1. Forgetting to **normalise** the weights after Step 2 (lose 1 mark).
> 2. Confusing the **measurement model** $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ with the **motion model** $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ (lose 2 marks).
> 3. Writing "particles are independent" — they are **NOT independent**; the set as a whole represents the belief.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Belief Representation**

- **Core definition:** $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — must be memorised word-for-word.
- **Predicted belief:** $\overline{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ — one measurement less.
- **Two-step cycle:** Prediction (motion) → Update (measurement). This is the **Bayes filter**.
- **Measurement update equation:** $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$ — derived from Bayes + Markov.
- **Prediction equation:** $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$.
- **Normaliser $\eta$:** Ensures the belief is a valid probability distribution summing to 1.
- **Four representation schemes:**
  1. **Gaussian (KF/EKF/UKF)** — unimodal, $O(n^2)$ memory, fails for global localization.
  2. **Grid-based** — discrete, multi-modal, $O(K)$ memory, exponential in dimensions.
  3. **Particle-based (MCL)** — arbitrary, multi-modal, $O(M)$ memory, $N_{\text{eff}}$ resampling.
  4. **Topological** — graph of distinctive places, very compact, requires distinctiveness.
- **Markov assumption:** "The future is conditionally independent of the past given the present." Used in both derivations.
- **Internal choice rule:** If asked to "compare" representations in a 14-mark question, always use a **table with at least 4 criteria**; bare prose answers are marked down.
- **Mermaid block in exam:** A clean block diagram of the Bayes filter cycle is worth 3 marks on its own; practice drawing it from memory.
- **No code required:** KTU 2024 scheme PEBRT751 does not require code in the answer; emphasise equations, block diagrams, and tabular comparisons.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Map Representation - Continuous Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Map Representation - Continuous Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuous Map Representation** is a method of representing the robot's operating environment using mathematically continuous geometric primitives (lines, polygons, points, curves) defined in a real-valued Cartesian coordinate space, as opposed to discretizing the environment into finite cells (e.g., grids).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (PEBRT751), a **continuous representation** stores spatial information using:

- **Real-valued coordinates** $(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$ (or $(x, y, z) \in \mathbb{R}^3$ in 3D).
- **Parametric / analytic equations** of geometric primitives.
- **Feature descriptors** (e.g., corners, edges, walls) extracted from sensor data.
- **Symbolic landmarks** placed in an unbounded, continuous metric frame.

The two most dominant paradigms in this category are:

| Paradigm | Storage Unit | Coordinate Type |
|----------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Geometric Map** | Lines, arcs, polygons | Continuous ($\mathbb{R}^n$) |
| **Topological Map** | Nodes (places) + Edges (paths) | Symbolic, but embedded in continuous metric space |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "A CAD Blueprint vs. a Pixelated JPEG"**
>
> Imagine a building plan drawn by an architect: walls are infinite-thin lines, doors are points, and rooms are closed polygons drawn with a precision of 0.1 mm. This is **continuous representation** — the map is described by *exact* geometric equations.
>
> Now imagine the same plan drawn on graph paper where every square of 1 cm represents a fixed region. The wall no longer has a precise equation; it just "occupies" certain squares. This is a **discrete / grid representation**.
>
> **Continuous representation favors precision and compactness**, while discrete representation favors fast lookup and probabilistic updates (e.g., occupancy grids).

### 1.3 Why Continuous Representation is Required in Robot Navigation

- **Memory efficiency** — A long straight wall is stored as 2 endpoints, not thousands of grid cells.
- **Geometric accuracy** — Sub-millimeter fidelity is possible, which is essential for industrial manipulators and surgical robots.
- **Analytical path planning** — Algorithms like *Visibility Graphs* and *Voronoi Diagrams* need precise line equations.
- **Sensor fusion** — Lidar and stereo-camera features (corners, line segments) naturally produce geometric primitives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The metric is **typically Euclidean** ($\mathbb{R}^2$ or $\mathbb{R}^3$), and the standard reference frame is the **World Frame** (also called *Global Frame*), denoted $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Continuous Map Representations

The four pillars discussed under PEBRT751 Module 2 are:

1. **Point / Landmark Maps**
2. **Polyline (Line-Feature) Maps**
3. **Polygon Maps**
4. **Topological (Graph) Maps**

Each is built on top of continuous coordinate geometry.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Coordinate Frame Definition**
A continuous map exists in a global frame $W = \{O_w; X_w, Y_w, Z_w\}$. Every object (wall, landmark, robot pose) is referenced with respect to this frame.

**Step 2 — Primitive Selection**
Choose the geometric primitive that best matches the sensor data:
- Lidar scan of a corridor → *line segments*.
- Camera view of a doorway → *points / corners*.
- Floor plan of a warehouse → *polygons*.

**Step 3 — Equation / Parameter Encoding**
Each primitive is stored as its mathematical descriptor (e.g., for a line: endpoints or Hough parameters).

**Step 4 — Association (Data Association)**
When a new sensor observation arrives, the algorithm matches it to an existing primitive (e.g., using ICP, RANSAC, or Mahalanobis distance).

**Step 5 — Update**
The descriptor is refined using estimators (e.g., Kalman Filter, Least Squares).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for Continuous Map Representation**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | 2D Point | $P = (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$ | Fundamental landmark / corner |
| 2 | 3D Point | $P = (x, y, z) \in \mathbb{R}^3$ | Used in 3D SLAM / LiDAR |
| 3 | Line — Implicit Form | $ax + by + c = 0$ | $a^2 + b^2 = 1$ for normal form |
| 4 | Line — Parametric Form | $(x, y) = (x_1, y_1) + t \cdot (x_2 - x_1, y_2 - y_1)$ | $t \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 5 | Line — Polar / Hough | $r = x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta$ | $r$ = distance, $\theta$ = angle |
| 6 | Point-to-Line Distance | $d = \dfrac{\vert a x_0 + b y_0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$ | Perpendicular distance |
| 7 | Circle | $(x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2$ | $(h, k)$ = centre, $r$ = radius |
| 8 | Euclidean Distance | $d(P_1, P_2) = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ | Between two points |
| 9 | Polygon — Signed Area | $A = \dfrac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert$ | Shoelace formula |
| 10 | Line Intersection (2 lines) | $P = L_1 \cap L_2$ | Solved via 2×2 linear system |
| 11 | Line Segment Length | $L = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}$ | Endpoint distance |
| 12 | Robot Pose in World | $\xi = (x, y, \theta)^{\top}$ | 2D pose, $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ |
| 13 | SE(2) Transform | $T = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | Rigid-body motion |
| 14 | Mahalanobis Distance | $d_M = \sqrt{(P - \mu)^{\top} \Sigma^{-1} (P - \mu)}$ | Data association gate |

> **Units reminder:** Distances in **metres (m)**, angles in **radians (rad)** unless explicitly stated, areas in **square metres (m²)**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Store road boundaries and lane markings as continuous polylines in HD maps.
- **Warehouse Robots (Amazon Kiva):** Use topological graphs to navigate between shelving aisles.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Operate on sub-millimetre geometric maps of patient anatomy.
- **Indoor Cleaning Robots (Roomba):** Combine topological nodes (rooms) with continuous wall boundaries.
- **UAV Swarms:** Use landmark maps for GPS-denied navigation in indoor environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Point-to-Line Perpendicular Distance

**Given:** A line in implicit form $L: ax + by + c = 0$ with normalization $a^2 + b^2 = 1$, and a query point $P_0 = (x_0, y_0)$.

**Goal:** Compute the perpendicular (shortest) Euclidean distance from $P_0$ to $L$.

**Step 1:** Consider any two points $P_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2 = (x_2, y_2)$ lying on $L$. Since they satisfy the line equation:

$$a x_1 + b y_1 + c = 0$$
$$a x_2 + b y_2 + c = 0$$

**Step 2:** Subtract the two equations to get the direction vector of $L$:

$$a (x_1 - x_2) + b (y_1 - y_2) = 0$$

This confirms that the normal vector to $L$ is $\mathbf{n} = (a, b)$.

**Step 3:** The vector from $P_1$ (on $L$) to $P_0$ is:

$$\mathbf{v} = (x_0 - x_1, \; y_0 - y_1)$$

**Step 4:** Project $\mathbf{v}$ onto the normal $\mathbf{n}$. The scalar projection equals the perpendicular distance:

$$d = \frac{\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\vert \mathbf{n} \vert} = \frac{a (x_0 - x_1) + b (y_0 - y_1)}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$$

**Step 5:** Expand and substitute $a x_1 + b y_1 = -c$:

$$d = \frac{a x_0 + b y_0 - (a x_1 + b y_1)}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}} = \frac{a x_0 + b y_0 + c}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$$

**Step 6:** Taking the absolute value to obtain a non-negative distance:

$$\boxed{\,d = \frac{\vert a x_0 + b y_0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-tested formula** in PEBRT751 continuous-representation problems. KTU examiners frequently give 2 line segments and ask for the intersection or perpendicular distance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Intersection Point of Two Lines

**Given:** Two lines in implicit form:

$$L_1: a_1 x + b_1 y + c_1 = 0$$
$$L_2: a_2 x + b_2 y + c_2 = 0$$

**Goal:** Find the unique intersection point $P = (x, y)$.

**Step 1:** Treat the two equations as a $2 \times 2$ linear system in unknowns $x$ and $y$:

$$\begin{aligned}
a_1 x + b_1 y &= -c_1 \\
a_2 x + b_2 y &= -c_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the determinant (also called the *line cross product*):

$$\Delta = a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1$$

**Step 3:** If $\Delta = 0$, the lines are **parallel** (no intersection or infinitely many). Otherwise, solve via Cramer's Rule:

$$x = \frac{b_1 c_2 - b_2 c_1}{\Delta}, \qquad y = \frac{a_2 c_1 - a_1 c_2}{\Delta}$$

**Step 4 (Vector form for clarity):** The matrix inversion yields:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \begin{pmatrix} b_2 & -b_1 \\ -a_2 & a_1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} -c_1 \\ -c_2 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Line-of-Sight Distance in Lidar Scan

**Given:** A robot at $R = (x_r, y_r)$ emits a laser beam at bearing $\phi$ (measured from +X axis). The first wall along this beam lies on the line $L: ax + by + c = 0$.

**Goal:** Compute the hit distance $d_{hit}$.

**Step 1:** The laser ray can be parameterized as:

$$\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= x_r + t \cos\phi \\
y(t) &= y_r + t \sin\phi, \qquad t \geq 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the wall equation:

$$a (x_r + t \cos\phi) + b (y_r + t \sin\phi) + c = 0$$

**Step 3:** Collect terms in $t$:

$$t (a \cos\phi + b \sin\phi) = - (a x_r + b y_r + c)$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $t$:

$$t = \frac{-(a x_r + b y_r + c)}{a \cos\phi + b \sin\phi}$$

**Step 5:** The signed distance is the value of $t$:

$$\boxed{\,d_{hit} = \frac{a x_r + b y_r + c}{a \cos\phi + b \sin\phi}\,}$$

(With the sign chosen such that the distance is positive for an obstacle in front of the robot.)

> This derivation is the **backbone of EKF-SLAM and feature-based localization** in PEBRT751.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Continuous vs. Discrete

| Property | Continuous Representation | Discrete (Grid) Representation |
|----------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Memory | **Low** (few parameters) | **High** (cell count scales with area) |
| Precision | Sub-cm possible | Limited by cell size |
| Update Speed | Slow (linear algebra) | Fast (one cell increment) |
| Probabilistic Fusion | Harder | Trivial (log-odds per cell) |
| Best for | Structured indoor / outdoor | Unknown, unstructured |
| Path Planning | Visibility / Voronoi | A*, Dijkstra on graph |
| Examples | Polylines, Voronoi, Topological | Occupancy grid, bitmap |
| Storage per Wall | 2 endpoints | $L / \Delta$ cells |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy of Continuous Map Representations

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Continuous Map Representation] --> B[Geometric Maps]
    A --> C[Topological Maps]
    A --> D[Hybrid Maps]

    B --> B1[Point Landmark Map]
    B --> B2[Polyline Map]
    B --> B3[Polygon Map]
    B --> B4[Voronoi Diagram]

    C --> C1[Nodes = Places]
    C --> C2[Edges = Paths]
    C --> C3[Adjacency List]

    D --> D1[Geometric + Topological]
    D --> D2[Metric-Topological Hybrid]

    B1 --> B1a[Single landmark: corner, pillar]
    B2 --> B2a[Wall: 2 endpoints + thickness]
    B3 --> B3a[Room: closed polygon vertices]
    B4 --> B4a[Max clearance path for navigation]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Continuous-Map Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Sensor Input Lidar or Camera] --> B[Feature Extraction RANSAC, Hough, SIFT]
    B --> C[Data Association Mahalanobis Gate]
    C --> D[Primitive Storage Polyline, Polygon, Point DB]
    D --> E[State Estimator EKF or Graph SLAM]
    E --> F[Updated Continuous Map]
    F --> G[Path Planner Visibility, Voronoi, A star]
    G --> H[Motion Controller Wheels, Steer]

    A --> A1[Raw Scan]
    C --> C1[Match threshold]
    D --> D1[KD Tree indexing]
    E --> E1[Pose + Map covariance]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Polyline Map of a Room

```
+----------------------+
|                      |
|   P1 -----------P2   |
|   |              |   |
|   |   (Robot)    |   |
|   |      .       |   |
|   |              |   |
|   P4 -----------P3   |
|                      |
+----------------------+

P1 = (0,0)   P2 = (10,0)   P3 = (10,8)   P4 = (0,8)
Line 1: (0,0) to (10,0)   y = 0, 0 ≤ x ≤ 10
Line 2: (10,0) to (10,8)  x = 10, 0 ≤ y ≤ 8
Line 3: (10,8) to (0,8)   y = 8, 0 ≤ x ≤ 10
Line 4: (0,8) to (0,0)    x = 0, 0 ≤ y ≤ 8

Total storage: 8 floats + 4 line descriptors.
Equivalent 0.1 m grid: 100 x 80 = 8000 cells.
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Storage ratio:** The continuous polyline map needs ~8 numbers; the discrete grid needs **8000 cells** for the same room — a 1000× compression.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
Define *continuous map representation* in robot navigation. How does it differ from a discrete (occupancy grid) representation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Continuous map representation describes the environment using **mathematical primitives** (points, lines, polygons) stored as real-valued coordinates in $\mathbb{R}^2$ or $\mathbb{R}^3$. It is **unbounded and resolution-free**.
>
> A discrete (occupancy grid) representation partitions the environment into **finite cells** of fixed size, each holding a probability of occupancy. It is **bounded, resolution-limited, and memory-intensive**.
>
> Continuous maps offer higher **precision and compactness**, while discrete grids offer **faster probabilistic updates and easier path planning via A\* on graphs**.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for continuous features, 1 mark for discrete features and contrast.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
List any **three geometric primitives** used in continuous map representation and write the **implicit equation** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Primitive | Implicit Equation |
|---|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Straight line | $ax + by + c = 0$ |
| 2 | Circle | $(x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2$ |
| 3 | Ellipse | $\dfrac{(x-h)^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{(y-k)^2}{b^2} = 1$ |

*(1 mark per correct primitive + equation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions are **full 14-mark problems with internal choice**. The standard split is **Part (a) = 7 marks** (conceptual/derivation) and **Part (b) = 7 marks** (numerical/application). Model solutions are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **four types of continuous map representations** used in mobile robot navigation. Compare their storage complexity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A mobile robot is at position $P_r = (2, 1)$ in the world frame. A wall is described by the line $3x + 4y - 10 = 0$. Calculate the **perpendicular distance** from the robot to the wall and determine whether the robot is in front of the wall (assume the wall's normal points toward the origin).

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) [7 Marks]**

The four types of continuous map representations are:

1. **Point (Landmark) Map** — Stores a set of distinguishable features as 2D coordinates: $M = \{p_i = (x_i, y_i)\}$.
   - *Storage:* $O(N)$ for $N$ landmarks.
2. **Polyline (Line Feature) Map** — Stores walls as line segments: $L_i = [(x_{i,1}, y_{i,1}), (x_{i,2}, y_{i,2})]$.
   - *Storage:* $O(N)$ for $N$ walls.
3. **Polygon Map** — Stores closed regions as ordered vertex lists: $R = [v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k]$.
   - *Storage:* $O(k)$ per room.
4. **Topological (Graph) Map** — Stores nodes (places) and edges (connectivity).
