   - *Storage:* $O(V + E)$ for $V$ vertices, $E$ edges.

A polyline representation of an indoor corridor is the **most common** in PEBRT751 applications.

*(Breakdown: 1 mark per type, 1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for storage comparison.)*

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $P_r = (2, 1)$, Line: $3x + 4y - 10 = 0$, with $a = 3, \; b = 4, \; c = -10$.

**Step 1 — Compute the denominator (normal magnitude):**

$$\sqrt{a^2 + b^2} = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator (signed distance numerator):**

$$a x_0 + b y_0 + c = (3)(2) + (4)(1) + (-10) = 6 + 4 - 10 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute the perpendicular distance:**

$$d = \frac{\vert 0 \vert}{5} = 0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

The robot is **exactly on the wall**. In practice this is unsafe; the controller must drive the robot back to a clearance of at least the robot's radius (typically 0.3 m).

*(Breakdown: 1 mark substituting, 1 mark denominator, 1 mark numerator, 1 mark answer, 2 marks interpretation, 1 mark unit.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the equation for the **perpendicular distance from a point $(x_0, y_0)$ to a line $ax + by + c = 0$** using vector projection. State all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A corridor is described by two parallel line segments:
- $L_1$: $4x - 3y + 5 = 0$
- $L_2$: $4x - 3y - 15 = 0$

A robot is at the point $P_r = (1, 2)$. Find:
- (i) The perpendicular distance from the robot to $L_1$.
- (ii) The perpendicular distance from the robot to $L_2$.
- (iii) The **width of the corridor**.
- (iv) Verify whether the robot lies **inside** the corridor.

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) [7 Marks]** (See Derivation 1 in Section 3.1 for the full step-by-step derivation. Examiner expectations:)

- **Assumption 1:** Line is in implicit (non-vertical) form. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Assumption 2:** Normal vector $\mathbf{n} = (a, b)$ may or may not be unit length. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Steps 1–6** as derived in Section 3.1. *(5 marks)*
- **Final boxed formula** clearly stated. *(1 mark)*

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**(i) Distance to $L_1$: $4x - 3y + 5 = 0$**

$$d_1 = \frac{\vert 4(1) - 3(2) + 5 \vert}{\sqrt{4^2 + (-3)^2}} = \frac{\vert 4 - 6 + 5 \vert}{\sqrt{16 + 9}} = \frac{\vert 3 \vert}{5} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Distance to $L_2$: $4x - 3y - 15 = 0$**

$$d_2 = \frac{\vert 4(1) - 3(2) - 15 \vert}{5} = \frac{\vert 4 - 6 - 15 \vert}{5} = \frac{\vert -17 \vert}{5} = \frac{17}{5} = 3.4 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Corridor width (since lines are parallel, use signed-subtraction trick):**

For parallel lines $a x + b y + c_1 = 0$ and $a x + b y + c_2 = 0$:

$$W = \frac{\vert c_2 - c_1 \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}} = \frac{\vert -15 - 5 \vert}{5} = \frac{20}{5} = 4 \text{ m}$$

**Cross-check:** $W = d_1 + d_2$ only if the robot is *between* the lines. Here $0.6 + 3.4 = 4.0$ m ✓. **Robot IS inside the corridor.**

**(iv) Inside-corridor verification:**

The robot lies between $L_1$ and $L_2$ if and only if the two signed expressions $a x_0 + b y_0 + c_1$ and $a x_0 + b y_0 + c_2$ have **opposite signs**.

- For $L_1$: $4(1) - 3(2) + 5 = +3 > 0$
- For $L_2$: $4(1) - 3(2) - 15 = -17 < 0$

The signs are opposite ⇒ **the robot lies inside the corridor.** ✓

*(Breakdown: (i) 1.5 marks, (ii) 1.5 marks, (iii) 2 marks, (iv) 2 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting absolute value** in the distance formula — results in a *negative* distance which is geometrically meaningless. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Not normalising** — if the line is given as $2x + 2y + 1 = 0$, students sometimes write $\sqrt{8}$ in the denominator and lose a mark for not simplifying.
> 3. **Mixing up line-equation form** — using $y = mx + c$ instead of $ax + by + c = 0$ causes confusion in Cramer's rule intersections.
> 4. **Ignoring unit labels** in the final answer — examiners deduct **0.5 mark** if "m" is missing.
> 5. **Assuming lines always intersect** — if $a_1 b_2 = a_2 b_1$, students must explicitly state "lines are parallel / coincident" for full credit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Continuous Map Representation**

- [x] **Definition:** Map built from geometric primitives in $\mathbb{R}^2$ / $\mathbb{R}^3$ (NOT grid cells).
- [x] **Four Types:** Point landmark, Polyline, Polygon, Topological graph.
- [x] **Master Formula:** $d = \dfrac{\vert a x_0 + b y_0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$ — memorize and know its derivation.
- [x] **Line intersection:** Use Cramer's Rule with $\Delta = a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1$.
- [x] **Shoelace formula** for polygon area: $A = \dfrac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert$.
- [x] **Parametric form** preferred for ray-tracing and collision checking.
- [x] **Hough / Polar form** $r = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ is the bridge between Lidar scans and line extraction.
- [x] **Robot pose** in 2D: $\xi = (x, y, \theta)^{\top}$, often represented as $SE(2)$ matrix.
- [x] **Storage** advantage: continuous maps use $\sim$1000× less memory than fine grids.
- [x] **Probabilistic update** of continuous maps is done via **EKF-SLAM** or **Graph-SLAM**, not via log-odds.
- [x] **Path planning algorithms** that consume continuous maps: **Visibility Graph**, **Voronoi Diagram**, **RRT**.
- [x] **Real-world use cases:** HD maps for self-driving cars, warehouse AMRs, surgical robots, UAV swarms.
- [x] **Examiner's mantra:** Always carry the **absolute value**, always **label the unit (m)**, always state **assumptions** (e.g., "line not vertical", "robot is in 2D plane").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decomposition Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decomposition Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of mobile robot path planning, **Decomposition Strategies** (also called *Cell Decomposition* methods) constitute a class of continuous-configuration-space planning algorithms that partition the robot's free workspace $\mathcal{W}_{free}$ into a finite collection of simple, non-overlapping regions called **cells**, such that the path-planning problem in the continuous domain can be reduced to an equivalent discrete graph-search problem (typically a **Region Adjacency Graph**, RAG).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Decomposition strategies are roadmap-like representations that divide the configuration space (or workspace) into convex or simply-connected polygonal regions, construct a connectivity graph where each cell is a node and adjacent cells share a connecting edge, and then apply classical graph-search algorithms (such as Dijkstra or A*) to compute a collision-free trajectory.

The general mathematical formulation is given by:

$$\mathcal{W}_{free} \;=\; \bigcup_{i=1}^{N} C_i \quad \text{where} \quad C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset \;\; \forall \, i \neq j$$

Here, $C_i$ denotes the $i^{th}$ cell, and the union spans the entire free space without any overlap. A connectivity graph $G = (V, E)$ is then constructed such that each cell $C_i$ is mapped to a node $v_i \in V$, and an edge $e_{ij} \in E$ exists between $v_i$ and $v_j$ if and only if cells $C_i$ and $C_j$ are *adjacent* (i.e., share a common boundary or corridor).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are navigating a large, crowded shopping mall with a floor plan that contains irregularly shaped stores, curving hallways, and oddly placed kiosks. Rather than calculating a complex continuous curve through the chaos, you first **draw a grid or polygon overlay** on the floor plan. Each grid square or polygon becomes a "cell" — and you only need to remember *which cells* you must walk through, not the exact geometric path. The mall is now reduced to a simple connect-the-dots puzzle.

This is precisely the power of decomposition: a geometrically complex 2D plane is converted into a manageable discrete graph that a computer can search using elementary algorithms.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Metrics

| Term | Meaning | Standard Notation |
|------|---------|-------------------|
| Workspace | The physical 2D area where the robot operates | $\mathcal{W}$ |
| Free Space | The subset of workspace free of obstacles | $\mathcal{W}_{free}$ |
| Cell | A non-overlapping subdivision of free space | $C_i$ |
| Region Adjacency Graph (RAG) | Graph encoding cell-to-cell adjacency | $G = (V, E)$ |
| Convexity Property | A cell where any two points inside are line-of-sight reachable | Strictly convex |
| Cell Resolution | Fineness of the decomposition | $\delta$ (meters/cell) |
| Critical Point | A vertex of the workspace obstacle polygon | $p_{cr}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Metric — Cell Resolution ($\delta$):** The smaller the value of $\delta$, the higher the path fidelity but the greater the computational load. KTU problems typically expect students to discuss this trade-off, formally called **resolution completeness vs. time complexity**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Decomposition Methods

Decomposition strategies in mobile robot navigation are taxonomically grouped into three primary classes. Understanding this hierarchy is **critical** because KTU frequently asks classification-based 3-mark questions.

### 2.1.1 Exact Cell Decomposition (ECD)

- The polygonal boundaries of obstacles are used as *exact* cell walls.
- Cells are formed by extending obstacle edges until they hit other obstacles or workspace walls.
- The resulting cells are **trapezoids** (or triangles) and are mutually exclusive.
- **Examples:** Trapezoidal Decomposition, Boustrophedon Decomposition, Morse-Based Decomposition.
- **Property:** Resolution-**complete** (with respect to cell walls), not resolution-**optimal**.

### 2.1.2 Approximate Cell Decomposition (ACD)

- The workspace is overlaid by a **uniform or non-uniform grid** of fixed resolution.
- Cells are pre-defined rectangles (or squares) and the decomposition is independent of obstacle geometry.
- **Example:** Uniform Grid Decomposition, Quad-Tree Decomposition.
- **Property:** Resolution-**complete** (in the limit $\delta \to 0$), computationally simpler, widely used in real-time systems.

### 2.1.3 Adaptive / Hierarchical Cell Decomposition

- Combines both — uses a coarse grid initially, and recursively refines cells that contain obstacles.
- **Example:** Quadtree-based methods, Octree (3D extension).
- **Property:** Saves memory by not over-resolving open spaces; widely used in 3D motion planning and SLAM.

## 2.2 Operational Workflow (Why and How)

1. **Workspace Modeling:** Represent the robot's environment using polygonal obstacle models $O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_n$ each with a set of vertices $V(O_i)$.
2. **Critical Point Extraction:** Compute the set of all obstacle vertices — these are the *critical points* $P = \bigcup_i V(O_i)$ that drive exact decomposition.
3. **Cell Generation:** Sweep a vertical (or horizontal) line across the workspace. Each time the line intersects an obstacle vertex, a new cell boundary is created. The cell is bounded by two consecutive critical x-coordinates.
4. **Connectivity Graph Construction:** For every pair of adjacent cells (cells sharing an edge segment), add an undirected edge weighted by Euclidean or traversal cost.
5. **Graph Search:** Apply Dijkstra, A*, BFS, or DFS over the RAG to find a path between start cell $C_{start}$ (containing $q_{start}$) and goal cell $C_{goal}$ (containing $q_{goal}$).
6. **Path Smoothing (Optional):** Within each cell, since cells are convex, a straight-line segment is always a valid path. Concatenate straight segments to obtain the final continuous trajectory.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Convexity:** A cell is required to be convex so that a straight line between any two points inside the cell is guaranteed to remain inside. This eliminates expensive local collision checks within cells and reduces path planning to a simple line-of-sight query.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Boundary Condition / Note |
|---|-------------------|--------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Free space partition | $\mathcal{W}_{free} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{N} C_i$ | $C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$ |
| 2 | Cell adjacency condition | $C_i \sim C_j \iff \overline{C_i} \cap \overline{C_j} \neq \emptyset$ | Closure must share a non-empty boundary |
| 3 | Critical point set | $P = \bigcup_{k=1}^{m} V(O_k)$ | Vertices of all $m$ polygonal obstacles |
| 4 | Sweep line invariant (Trapezoidal) | $x(p_{k}) < x_{sweep} < x(p_{k+1})$ | $p_k$ and $p_{k+1}$ are consecutive sorted critical points |
| 5 | Cell resolution (Grid) | $\delta = \frac{L_{max}}{2^n}$ | $L_{max}$ = workspace extent, $n$ = subdivision level |
| 6 | A* heuristic (used on RAG) | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | $g(n)$ = cost-so-far, $h(n)$ = admissible Euclidean estimate |
| 7 | Convex hull check | $\forall\, x \in [x_1, x_2], y \in [y_1(x), y_2(x)]$ | Cell is convex iff $y_1(x), y_2(x)$ are linear |
| 8 | Path length bound | $\ell_{path} \leq \sum_{i=1}^{K-1} d(c_i, c_{i+1}) + \epsilon$ | $K$ = number of cells traversed, $\epsilon$ = smoothing error |
| 9 | Memory complexity (Grid) | $M = \mathcal{O}(N^2 / \delta^2)$ | $N$ = workspace side length |
| 10 | Quadtree memory | $M = \mathcal{O}(N^2 / \delta^2_{min})$ | Only obstacle-containing cells are refined |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Notation Trap:** Students often write $|x|$ (absolute value) in markdown tables using the pipe symbol. KTU's automated evaluation tools may parse this as a column separator. Always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ inside any table row.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva, Fetch):** Grid decomposition enables fast, deterministic navigation in structured shelves.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Adaptive decomposition in occupancy grids is the foundation of ROS Navigation Stack's local and global planners.
- **Aerial Drones:** Octree-based decomposition (3D) is used for collision-free flight planning through cluttered urban environments.
- **Underwater ROVs:** Topological-morse decomposition handles unstructured seafloor terrain.
- **Planetary Rovers (NASA):** Boustrophedon-style decomposition is used to plan back-and-forth survey patterns on Mars.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Cells in Trapezoidal Decomposition

Let the workspace be a rectangular region containing $n$ polygonal obstacles, each with $v_i$ vertices. The total number of critical points is:

$$N_{cr} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i$$

A trapezoidal decomposition extends vertical lines upward and downward from each critical point. The number of resulting trapezoidal cells $N_{trap}$ is bounded by:

$$N_{trap} \;=\; \mathcal{O}\!\left(N_{cr} + 1\right)$$

### 3.1.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Sort all critical x-coordinates in ascending order:

$$x_1 < x_2 < \ldots < x_{N_{cr}}$$

**Step 2:** Initialize cell count $N_{trap} = 1$ (representing the unbounded cell to the left of the leftmost critical point).

**Step 3:** For each critical vertical line $x = x_k$, the line partitions the existing cells it traverses into two. The number of *new* cells added is:

$$\Delta N_{trap}(k) = (\text{Number of cells crossed by } x = x_k)$$

**Step 4:** Summing over all critical lines:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{trap} &= 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N_{cr}} \Delta N_{trap}(k) \\
&\leq 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N_{cr}} (v_{max}(k) + 1) \\
&\leq 1 + (N_{cr} + 1)(v_{max} + 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $v_{max}$ is the maximum number of obstacle edges intersected by any single vertical sweep line.

**Step 5:** Using the upper bound $v_{max} \leq N_{cr}$, the result simplifies to:

$$N_{trap} \;\leq\; \mathcal{O}\!\left(N_{cr}^{\,2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Each vertical sweep line at a critical x-coordinate can intersect every obstacle edge exactly once, hence the quadratic worst case. In practice, most environments yield $N_{trap} = \mathcal{O}(N_{cr})$, but KTU expects the worst-case bound.

## 3.2 Derivation: A* Search Cost Over the RAG

The total time complexity of finding a path in a Region Adjacency Graph using A* is:

$$T_{A^*} = \mathcal{O}\!\left(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert + \vert E \vert\right)$$

### 3.2.1 Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1:** The RAG is constructed such that $\vert V \vert = N_{trap}$ (one node per cell).

**Step 2:** Each cell has at most $b$ neighbors (bounded by its perimeter divided by minimum cell size). Thus:

$$\vert E \vert = \mathcal{O}(b \cdot N_{trap}) = \mathcal{O}(N_{trap})$$

**Step 3:** The priority queue operations in A* cost $\mathcal{O}(\log \vert V \vert)$ per node insertion.

**Step 4:** The total cost becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{A^*} &= \sum_{v \in V} \mathcal{O}(\log \vert V \vert) + \mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert) \\
&= \vert V \vert \cdot \mathcal{O}(\log \vert V \vert) + \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert) \\
&= \mathcal{O}\!\left(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $\vert V \vert = N_{trap} = \mathcal{O}(N_{cr}^2)$:

$$T_{A^*} = \mathcal{O}\!\left(N_{cr}^{\,2} \log N_{cr}\right)$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Boustrophedon vs. Trapezoidal

| Property | Trapezoidal Decomposition | Boustrophedon Decomposition |
|----------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| Cell shape | Trapezoid / Triangle | Trapezoid with cell-spanning chord |
| Critical points used | All obstacle vertices | Only "in-events" (lower edges of obstacles) |
| Number of cells | Higher | Lower (often $N_{cr} / 2$) |
| Coverage suitability | Limited | Excellent (lawn-mower patterns) |
| Computation cost | $\mathcal{O}(N_{cr} \log N_{cr})$ | $\mathcal{O}(N_{cr} \log N_{cr})$ |
| Reduction in edges | None | Halves cell count on average |

## 3.4 Quadtree Refinement Equation

For a cell of side length $s$ in a quadtree decomposition, the decision to subdivide is governed by the boolean predicate:

$$\text{Split}(C) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{True} & \text{if } \mathrm{Area}(C \cap \mathcal{W}_{obs}) > \alpha \cdot \mathrm{Area}(C) \\
\text{False} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}$$

where $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ is the obstacle-coverage threshold (typically $\alpha = 0.5$).

The maximum number of quadtree nodes at subdivision level $k$ is:

$$N_{quad}(k) = 4^k$$

The total memory is bounded by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{quad} &= \sum_{k=0}^{k_{max}} 4^k \cdot (\text{cells split at level } k) \\
&\leq \sum_{k=0}^{k_{max}} 4^k = \frac{4^{k_{max}+1} - 1}{3} = \mathcal{O}\!\left(4^{k_{max}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Decomposition-Based Planner

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workspace Input polygon Obstacles P] --> B[Critical Point Extractor sort vertices by x]
    B --> C[Decomposition Engine choose method]
    C --> D1[Trapezoidal Method vertical sweep]
    C --> D2[Boustrophedon Method chord sweep]
    C --> D3[Quadtree Method recursive grid]
    C --> D4[Approximate Grid uniform overlay]
    D1 --> E[Cell Library C1 to CN]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Region Adjacency Graph Builder]
    F --> G[Graph G with V and E]
    G --> H[Graph Search Module A star or Dijkstra]
    H --> I[Optimal Path Sequence of Cells]
    I --> J[Path Smoother line within each cell]
    J --> K[Final Continuous Trajectory Output]
```

## 4.2 Trapezoidal Decomposition State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitSort
    InitSort --> SweepReady: Critical points sorted by x
    SweepReady --> ProcessVertex: At next critical x value
    ProcessVertex --> ExtendLine: Draw vertical line up and down
    ExtendLine --> UpdateCells: New trapezoids formed
    UpdateCells --> SweepReady: More critical points
    UpdateCells --> BuildGraph: All critical points processed
    BuildGraph --> SearchPath: RAG ready
    SearchPath --> [*]: Path returned
```

## 4.3 Quadtree Recursive Refinement Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Root Cell entire workspace] --> Check{Obstacle area exceeds threshold}
    Check -->|No| Leaf1[Mark as Free Cell]
    Check -->|Yes| Sub[Subdivide into 4 children]
    Sub --> NE[Northeast Child]
    Sub --> NW[Northwest Child]
    Sub --> SE[Southeast Child]
    Sub --> SW[Southwest Child]
    NE --> CheckNE{Obstacle in NE}
    NW --> CheckNW{Obstacle in NW}
    SE --> CheckSE{Obstacle in SE}
    SW --> CheckSW{Obstacle in SW}
    CheckNE -->|Yes| SubNE[Subdivide again]
    CheckNE -->|No| LeafNE[Mark as Free]
    CheckNW -->|Yes| SubNW[Subdivide again]
    CheckNW -->|No| LeafNW[Mark as Free]
    CheckSE -->|Yes| SubSE[Subdivide again]
    CheckSE -->|No| LeafSE[Mark as Free]
    CheckSW -->|Yes| SubSW[Subdivide again]
    CheckSW -->|No| LeafSW[Mark as Free]
```

## 4.4 Adjacency Graph Illustration (Abstract)

```mermaid
graph LR
    cellA[Cell A start] --- cellB[Cell B]
    cellB --- cellC[Cell C corridor]
    cellC --- cellD[Cell D]
    cellC --- cellE[Cell E branch]
    cellD --- cellF[Cell F goal]
    cellE --- cellF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Mermaid does not natively render physical geometric trapezoids. The block diagrams above faithfully capture the **logical architecture** and **control flow** of the decomposition algorithms, which is the standard expected by KTU evaluators when physical drawings are infeasible.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q:** Define cell decomposition. List any two advantages and one disadvantage.

**Model Answer:**
Cell decomposition is a path-planning technique in which the robot's free workspace $\mathcal{W}_{free}$ is partitioned into a finite set of non-overlapping regions called cells, and a graph is constructed representing adjacency between cells.

**Advantages:**
1. **Resolution completeness** — finds a solution if one exists (for exact methods).
2. **Graph search reuse** — once the graph is built, multiple queries (start-goal pairs) can be solved cheaply using A* or Dijkstra.

**Disadvantage:**
1. **Computational cost** — exact decomposition has $\mathcal{O}(N_{cr}^2)$ worst-case complexity, and fine grid decomposition leads to large memory footprints.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q:** Differentiate between exact and approximate cell decomposition.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Exact Cell Decomposition | Approximate Cell Decomposition |
|--------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Cell boundaries | Follow obstacle vertices exactly | Independent, regular grid lines |
| Resolution | Inherently complete | Complete only as $\delta \to 0$ |
| Cell shape | Trapezoid / Triangle | Fixed squares / rectangles |
| Examples | Trapezoidal, Boustrophedon | Uniform grid, Quad-tree |
| Computation | Higher (polygon intersection) | Lower (bitmask checks) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Students must answer exactly ONE of the two choices. Each part below carries 7 marks, totaling 14 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q:** With a neat block diagram, explain the **trapezoidal cell decomposition algorithm** for mobile robot path planning. Derive the upper bound on the number of cells generated.

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Input:** Workspace $\mathcal{W}$ and set of polygonal obstacles $O_1, \ldots, O_n$ with vertices.
2. **Critical Point Identification:** Compute the set $P = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_m\}$ of all obstacle vertices, sorted by x-coordinate.
3. **Sweep Line Initialization:** Initialize a vertical sweep line at $x = -\infty$.
4. **Sweep Iteration:** For each critical point $p_k = (x_k, y_k)$:
   - Move the sweep line to $x = x_k$.
   - Extend a vertical line segment from $p_k$ upward and downward until it hits another obstacle or workspace boundary.
   - Create a new cell boundary at $x = x_k$.
5. **Cell Completion:** Between two consecutive critical x-coordinates, the area bounded by the workspace walls and the vertical lines forms a **trapezoid** (or triangle if the upper and lower boundaries meet).
6. **Graph Construction:** Build a Region Adjacency Graph $G$ with one node per cell and edges between adjacent cells.
7. **Path Search:** Apply A* search from $C_{start}$ to $C_{goal}$.

**Derivation of Upper Bound:**

The total number of critical points is $N_{cr} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i$. Each sweep line can intersect at most $v_{max}$ obstacle edges. The number of new cells added at each critical point is bounded by $v_{max} + 1$. Thus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{trap} &\leq 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N_{cr}} (v_{max} + 1) \\
&\leq 1 + (N_{cr} + 1)(v_{max} + 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

With $v_{max} \leq N_{cr}$:

$$N_{trap} \leq \mathcal{O}\!\left(N_{cr}^{\,2}\right)$$

**Conclusion:** Trapezoidal decomposition yields a path-plan graph whose size is at most quadratic in the number of critical points.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q:** Explain **Boustrophedon decomposition**. Why is it preferred for coverage path planning?

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Boustrophedon decomposition (Greek *boustrophedon* = "as the ox turns while plowing") is a variant of exact cell decomposition in which a cell boundary is created only at *lower* obstacle vertices (called **in-events**), and at *upper* vertices (called **out-events**) the existing cells are merged.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. Sweep a vertical line left-to-right.
2. At a **lower vertex** (in-event): insert a vertical line, splitting the current cell.
3. At an **upper vertex** (out-event): remove the middle vertical line, merging adjacent cells.
4. The result is a decomposition with fewer, larger cells — typically half the count of trapezoidal decomposition.

**Why Preferred for Coverage Planning:**

- **Back-and-forth (lawnmower) pattern:** Within each cell, a simple zigzag trajectory can cover the area completely without missing strips.
- **Reduced cell count** → fewer turns → less energy consumption and shorter mission time.
- **Lower graph size** → faster re-planning if the environment changes.

**Conclusion:** Boustrophedon decomposition is the de-facto standard for area-coverage missions such as floor cleaning, lawn mowing, agricultural spraying, and seabed mapping.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q:** Describe **approximate cell decomposition using a uniform grid**. Discuss its time and space complexity. How does the resolution $\delta$ affect the trade-off between path quality and computation time?

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Description:**

1. Overlay a 2D grid of cell size $\delta \times \delta$ on the workspace.
2. Mark each cell as **FREE**, **OBSTACLE**, or **MIXED** (boundary cell).
3. For MIXED cells, refine the check (e.g., ray-casting to determine if the cell is fully free or fully blocked).
4. Construct a 4-connected (or 8-connected) grid graph.
5. Apply A* or BFS from the start cell to the goal cell.

**Complexity Analysis:**

- **Number of cells:** $N = \lceil L_x / \delta \rceil \cdot \lceil L_y / \delta \rceil = \mathcal{O}(L^2 / \delta^2)$, where $L$ is the workspace side.
- **Memory:** $M = \mathcal{O}(L^2 / \delta^2)$ bits for the grid.
- **Time (A* search):** $T = \mathcal{O}(N \log N) = \mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{L^2}{\delta^2} \log \frac{L^2}{\delta^2}\right)$.

**Resolution Trade-off:**

- **Small $\delta$:** Smoother path closer to true optimum, but memory and time grow quadratically.
- **Large $\delta$:** Faster planning, but path may detour around narrow passages (a cell containing a thin obstacle wall becomes fully blocked).

**Conclusion:** Uniform grid decomposition is the simplest, most parallelizable planner, but it is **not resolution-complete** in finite time — a narrow corridor narrower than $\delta$ may be classified as blocked.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q:** Explain the **quadtree-based adaptive cell decomposition** algorithm. State one advantage and one disadvantage compared to uniform grid.

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Description:**

1. Start with one root cell covering the entire workspace.
2. For each cell $C$, evaluate: $\mathrm{Area}(C \cap \mathcal{W}_{obs}) > \alpha \cdot \mathrm{Area}(C)$?
3. If yes, subdivide $C$ into 4 equal quadrants (NE, NW, SE, SW) and recurse.
4. Stop when: (a) no obstacle overlap, or (b) maximum depth $k_{max}$ reached, or (c) cell area is below a minimum threshold.
5. Build adjacency graph among the leaf nodes.
6. Apply A* search.

**Maximum Number of Leaves:**

$$N_{leaves} \leq 4^{k_{max}}$$

where $k_{max}$ is the maximum subdivision depth.

**Advantage over Uniform Grid:**

- **Memory efficiency:** Open areas use one large cell; only obstacle-rich areas are finely subdivided. For sparse environments, $N_{leaves} \ll 4^{k_{max}}$.

**Disadvantage:**

- **Tree management overhead:** Recursive subdivision and neighbor-finding across the tree are more complex than a flat grid array.

**Conclusion:** Quadtrees are ideal for 2.5D / 3D extensions (octrees) and for environments with non-uniform obstacle density such as outdoor terrain.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing configuration space and workspace:** Decomposition operates on **workspace** (or its free subset). Configuration-space decomposition (for manipulators) follows a different procedure. Marks are deducted if these are conflated.
> 2. **Forgetting convexity justification:** Always state *why* cells must be convex — straight-line property of convex sets.
> 3. **Skipping the graph construction step:** Many students jump from cells directly to A* without explicitly constructing the RAG. The graph $G = (V, E)$ is **mandatory** in the answer.
> 4. **Mixing boustrophedon with trapezoidal:** Boustrophedon uses only *in-events* (lower vertices), not all critical points. Writing "same as trapezoidal" loses 2-3 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the resolution trade-off discussion:** In approximate decomposition, the $\delta$ vs. optimality trade-off is a 2-mark KTU favorite.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** Always assume polygonal, static, known obstacles unless stated otherwise.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Decomposition** = partition free space $\mathcal{W}_{free}$ into cells + build adjacency graph + graph search.
- **Three classes:** Exact, Approximate, Adaptive/Hierarchical.
- **Exact methods** use obstacle vertices as critical points; **approximate** methods use fixed grid.
- **Convexity** of cells is the key property enabling straight-line path concatenation.
- **RAG** $G = (V, E)$ has one node per cell; edges between adjacent cells.
- **A\* / Dijkstra** is the standard search algorithm over the RAG.
- **Time complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(N_{cr}^2 \log N_{cr})$ for exact, $\mathcal{O}(L^2/\delta^2 \cdot \log L^2/\delta^2)$ for grids.
- **Trapezoidal:** all critical points → trapezoid cells.
- **Boustrophedon:** only in-events → larger cells, ideal for coverage.
- **Quadtree:** recursive 4-way subdivision, memory-efficient for sparse obstacles.
- **Resolution completeness** holds for exact methods; approximate methods achieve it only in the limit.
- **Real-world uses:** warehouse robots, autonomous vehicles (occupancy grids), drones (octree), planetary rovers (boustrophedon coverage).
- **Trade-off:** smaller $\delta$ → smoother path, more memory & time; larger $\delta$ → faster, suboptimal.
- **Memory pipe-trap:** write $\vert x \vert$ not $|x|$ inside markdown tables.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Current Challenges in Map Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Current Challenges in Map Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Map Representation in Robotics** is the formal encoding of spatial information about an environment into a computational data structure that a robot can use for **localization, path planning, and decision-making**. It is the bridge between raw sensor measurements (LIDAR, camera, IMU, sonar) and high-level autonomous behaviour.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology, a map representation is judged on four orthogonal axes:
- **Geometric Fidelity** (how accurately the map preserves metric shape)
- **Semantic Richness** (how much meaning/label is attached to regions)
- **Computational Footprint** (memory in **MB** and CPU in **FLOPS**)
- **Temporal Stability** (how well it adapts to a changing world)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The focus is on the *current* (state-of-the-art) challenges — meaning problems that are still **open research questions** as of 2024, not solved textbook problems. Students should be ready to discuss *why* a challenge is hard, not merely *what* it is.

## 1.2 Types of Map Representations (Quick Taxonomy)

| Family | Core Data Structure | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Metric (Occupancy Grid)** | 2D/3D array of cells | Easy probabilistic update | Quadratic memory growth |
| **Topological** | Graph of nodes & edges | Compact, human-readable | Loses metric detail |
| **Hybrid (Metric–Topological)** | Local grids + global graph | Balanced | Complex fusion logic |
| **Point-Cloud / NDT** | Unordered 3D points | High geometric fidelity | No semantics, large size |
| **Semantic / Feature-based** | Objects with attributes | Task-aware | Needs heavy ML inference |
| **Neural Implicit (NeRF / SDF)** | MLP weights | Continuous, photo-realistic | Slow query, hard to update |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it this way:** A robot's map is like a *Google Map* for a blindfolded delivery driver. 
> - An **occupancy grid** is a giant chessboard where every square is marked *free* or *blocked* — accurate but expensive.
> - A **topological map** is a subway map — it tells you *which station connects to which*, but not the exact street distance.
> - A **semantic map** is a tourist map with labels like *"kitchen"*, *"stairs"*, *"fire exit"* — useful for high-level commands, useless for centimetre-level parking.
> 
> The *current challenges* in map representation are essentially the trade-offs the cartographer must face: **cost vs. accuracy vs. richness vs. freshness**.

## 1.4 The Four Pillars of "Current" Challenge

1. **Scale** — from a **20 m²** room to a **city block** of **1 km²**.
2. **Dynamics** — moving people, rearranged furniture, opened/closed doors.
3. **Semantics** — going beyond geometry to *"what is this object and how do I interact with it?"*
4. **Lifelong operation** — maps that learn continuously for **months** without drift or bloat.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Map Representation

Every representation $\mathcal{M}$ can be factored as:

$$
\mathcal{M} \;=\; \langle \, G,\; S,\; U,\; T \, \rangle
$$

where
- $G$ = geometric/spatial encoding (cells, points, surfaces)
- $S$ = semantic labels or features
- $U$ = uncertainty / confidence estimate
- $T$ = timestamp / temporal validity window

The **"current challenges"** arise because researchers are trying to push **all four** components simultaneously to a level that no single representation handles well.

## 2.2 The Six Major Categories of Open Challenges

### Challenge 1 — The Scale-Memory Bottleneck
- An occupancy grid of size $N_x \times N_y \times N_z$ cells at resolution $r$ covers:
$$
\text{Area} = N_x \cdot N_y \cdot r^2 \quad [\text{m}^2]
$$
- Each cell needs $\geq 1\,\text{byte}$ (binary) to $\geq 8\,\text{bytes}$ (floating log-odds).
- A **1 km²** city at **0.1 m** resolution needs $\sim 10^8$ cells, i.e. ~**100 MB** even in binary form.

### Challenge 2 — The Dynamic-World Problem
- Classic SLAM assumes **static** $P(\text{world})$.
- Real environments have moving agents, changing lights, opening doors.
- The Bayes filter must distinguish **persistent** vs **transient** observations.

### Challenge 3 — Resolution vs. Real-Time Trade-off
- Update time $T_{\text{update}} \propto N_x N_y N_z$ for grid maps.
- Higher $r$ (finer resolution) → better collision avoidance but slower planning.

### Challenge 4 — Sensor Heterogeneity & Calibration Drift
- LIDAR, stereo camera, IMU, GNSS all have different noise profiles $\sigma_L, \sigma_C, \sigma_I$.
- Long-term IMU bias drift corrupts map alignment after **hours** of operation.

### Challenge 5 — Semantic & Open-Set Recognition
- Training a CNN on 20 classes fails when the robot meets an *unseen* object.
- Open-world SLAM must say *“I don’t know”* rather than hallucinate.

### Challenge 6 — Multi-Robot & Lifelong Fusion
- When $k$ robots each maintain a local map $\mathcal{M}_i$, the merged map $\mathcal{M}_{\text{global}}$ suffers from:
$$
\text{Conflict} = \sum_{i,j} \mathbb{1}\bigl[\,P(m_i = \text{occupied}) \neq P(m_j = \text{occupied})\,\bigr]
$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m(x,y) \in [0,1]$ | Occupancy probability of cell $(x,y)$ | Occupancy grid update |
| 2 | $l(x,y) = \log \frac{P(m \mid z)}{1 - P(m \mid z)}$ | Log-odds form (Moravec & Elfes) | Avoids numerical underflow |
| 3 | $l_t = l_{t-1} + l_{\text{sensor}} - l_{0}$ | Recursive log-odds update | Real-time grid SLAM |
| 4 | $\text{Mem} = N_x N_y N_z \cdot B$ bytes | Memory cost, $B$ = bytes/cell | Scale planning |
| 5 | $\sigma_{\text{pose}} \approx \sigma_{\text{odom}} \cdot \sqrt{d}$ | Pose drift grows with $\sqrt{\text{distance}}$ | Justifies loop closure |
| 6 | $\text{ICP cost} = \sum_{i} \Vert p_i - q_i \Vert^{2}$ | Iterative Closest Point alignment | Map registration |
| 7 | $H(\mathcal{M}) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Map information entropy | Lifelong mapping metric |
| 8 | $\text{FLOPS}_{\text{ray}} = O(N_x N_y)$ per ray | Cost of one sensor ray-trace | Real-time guarantee |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the *lingua franca* of any robotics R&D interview. Industries using them include **autonomous driving (Waymo, Tesla), warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva), agricultural robots (John Deere), and planetary rovers (NASA JPL).**

## 2.4 The "Venn Diagram" of Trade-offs

Every modern map representation lives in the overlap of three forces:

1. **Memory Budget** $\downarrow$
2. **Localisation Accuracy** $\uparrow$
3. **Semantic Expressiveness** $\uparrow$

The *current challenge* is that no representation simultaneously optimises all three. A **neural SDF** is accurate and semantic but memory-heavy; a **binary grid** is memory-light but semantically empty.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Memory Footprint of an Occupancy Grid

We want to find the bytes required to store a 3D voxel grid of a corridor of length $L$, width $W$, height $H$, with voxel edge $r$.

**Step 1 — Number of cells along each axis**

$$
N_x = \left\lceil \frac{L}{r} \right\rceil, \quad N_y = \left\lceil \frac{W}{r} \right\rceil, \quad N_z = \left\lceil \frac{H}{r} \right\rceil
$$

The ceiling accounts for the fact that a partial voxel at the boundary must still be stored.

**Step 2 — Total voxel count**

$$
N_{\text{total}} = N_x \cdot N_y \cdot N_z = \left\lceil \frac{L}{r} \right\rceil \left\lceil \frac{W}{r} \right\rceil \left\lceil \frac{H}{r} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 3 — Memory in bytes**

$$
M_{\text{bytes}} = N_{\text{total}} \cdot B
$$

where $B$ is the bytes per voxel.

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)**

Take a corridor $L=50$ m, $W=4$ m, $H=3$ m, $r=0.05$ m, $B=1$ byte.

$$
N_x = \lceil 50 / 0.05 \rceil = 1000, \quad N_y = \lceil 4 / 0.05 \rceil = 80, \quad N_z = \lceil 3 / 0.05 \rceil = 60
$$

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 1000 \times 80 \times 60 = 4\,800\,000 \text{ voxels}
$$

$$
M_{\text{bytes}} = 4\,800\,000 \times 1 \approx 4.8 \text{ MB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Halving the resolution $r$ (i.e. doubling the precision to $0.025$ m) multiplies memory by **8×** because memory scales as $1/r^3$. This is the *exact* reason 3D grids are impractical for city-scale mapping without compression (octomap, voxel hashing).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Recursive Log-Odds Occupancy Update (Elfes, 1989)

We start from the **odds form** of Bayes' rule.

**Step 1 — Define odds**

$$
O(x) \;=\; \frac{P(m = \text{occupied} \mid x)}{P(m = \text{free} \mid x)} \;=\; \frac{P(x)}{1 - P(x)}
$$

**Step 2 — Bayes update for a new measurement $z$**

$$
O(x \mid z) \;=\; \frac{P(z \mid \text{occ}) \cdot P(\text{occ})}{P(z \mid \text{free}) \cdot P(\text{free})} \cdot O(x)
$$

Define the **inverse sensor model** term:

$$
O_{\text{sensor}}(z) \;=\; \frac{P(z \mid \text{occ})}{P(z \mid \text{free})}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the logarithm on both sides**

$$
\log O(x \mid z) \;=\; \log O_{\text{sensor}}(z) \;+\; \log O(x)
$$

**Step 4 — Define the log-odds variable $l \equiv \log O$**

$$
l(x \mid z) \;=\; l_{\text{sensor}}(z) \;+\; l(x)
$$

**Step 5 — Recursive form for time steps $1 \to t$**

$$
l_t(x) \;=\; l_{t-1}(x) \;+\; l_{\text{sensor}}(z_t) \;-\; l_0
$$

The $l_0 = \log(O_0)$ is the prior (usually $0$ for an uninformative $0.5$ prior).

**Step 6 — Recover probability for path planning**

$$
P_t(x) \;=\; 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{\,l_t(x)}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why log-odds?** The update is a **single integer addition per cell** — perfect for embedded CPUs running at **10 Hz** on AMRs. The probability form requires two multiplications and a sigmoid, which is wasteful.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pose Drift vs. Distance Travelled

Wheel odometry integrates incremental motion, and each step adds Gaussian noise $\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_{\Delta}^2)$.

**Step 1 — Position after $k$ steps of length $\delta$**

$$
x_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \delta + \varepsilon_i, \quad \varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{\Delta}^2)
$$

**Step 2 — Variance by linearity of expectation**

$$
\operatorname{Var}(x_k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_{\Delta}^2 = k\,\sigma_{\Delta}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $k = d / \delta$, where $d$ is total distance**

$$
\sigma_{\text{drift}}^{2}(d) = \frac{d}{\delta}\,\sigma_{\Delta}^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \sigma_{\text{drift}}(d) = \sigma_{\Delta}\sqrt{\frac{d}{\delta}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical sanity check**

For $\delta = 0.1$ m, $\sigma_{\Delta} = 0.01$ m, $d = 100$ m:

$$
\sigma_{\text{drift}} = 0.01 \cdot \sqrt{100/0.1} = 0.01 \cdot \sqrt{1000} \approx 0.316 \text{ m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Implication:** Without loop closure, a robot that has travelled **100 m** in a corridor will have an open-loop pose uncertainty of **~30 cm** — far larger than the **5 cm** clearance of a typical door. This is *why* current research focuses on **lifelong SLAM with topological anchors**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Representation | Update $O(\cdot)$ | Query $O(\cdot)$ | Memory Scaling | Robust to Dynamics | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Binary Grid | $O(N)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(r^{-2})$ | Low | Indoor static |
| Probabilistic Grid | $O(N)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(r^{-2})$ | Medium | Indoor SLAM |
| Octomap | $O(\log N)$ | $O(\log N)$ | $O(r^{-3} \text{ compressed})$ | Medium | 3D outdoor |
| Point Cloud (raw) | $O(k)$ | $O(\log k)$ | $O(k)$ | High | LIDAR SLAM |
| Topological Graph | $O(E)$ | $O(\log V)$ | $O(V+E)$ | High | Large buildings |
| Neural SDF | $O(1)$ query, $O(\text{MLP})$ | $O(1)$ | Constant (weights) | Low | Static replicas |
| Hybrid Metric-Topo | $O(N_{\text{local}})$ | $O(\log V)$ | $O(N_{\text{local}} + V)$ | High | Long-term autonomy |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Part A:** 2 marks for definition + 1 mark for diagram or example. *(Total 3 marks)*
> - **Part B (ESE Module Choice):** Sub-parts of **7 + 7 = 14 marks**. Examiner expects *one* full-page diagram and *at least* one equation.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define map representation in robot navigation. List any two current challenges faced in representing large-scale dynamic environments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *map representation* is the formal computational encoding of an environment's spatial and semantic information, enabling a robot to perform **localization, path planning, and decision-making**.

Two current challenges:
1. **Scale-Memory Bottleneck** — Representing a 1 km² outdoor area at 0.1 m resolution requires $\sim 10^8$ cells.
2. **Dynamic Environment Handling** — Distinguishing between persistent structure and transient moving objects (people, vehicles) within a single unified map.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark per challenge — total 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**Differentiate between metric and topological map representations. State one advantage and one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Metric Map | Topological Map |
|---|---|---|
| Data | Grid of cells / points | Graph of nodes and edges |
| Information | Exact geometry | Connectivity only |
| **Advantage** | Centimetre-level accuracy | Memory efficient for large areas |
| **Limitation** | Quadratic memory growth | Loses fine geometric detail |

*(1 mark for table, 0.5 mark per advantage/limitation pair.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern:** The KTU End-Semester paper gives an *internal choice* between two full 14-mark questions. Both must be attempted in many institutions' internal tests.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a) Discuss in detail the various challenges in 3D map representation for autonomous robots, with reference to memory, sensor noise, and real-time constraints.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**1. Memory Scaling with Resolution (2 marks)**
- For a 3D voxel grid, memory scales as $M \propto r^{-3}$.
- A warehouse of $50 \text{ m} \times 30 \text{ m} \times 6 \text{ m}$ at $r = 0.05$ m needs:
$$
N = 1000 \times 600 \times 120 = 72\,000\,000 \text{ voxels} \approx 72 \text{ MB (binary)}
$$
- Solution: **Voxel hashing (Octomap)** achieves 10× compression by allocating only *observed* voxels.

**2. Sensor Noise & Calibration Drift (2 marks)**
- LIDAR range noise $\sigma_r \approx 0.02$ m introduces voxel-flipping artefacts.
- IMU bias drift $\sim 1^\circ/\text{hr}$ corrupts elevation estimates.
- Solution: **Probabilistic log-odds update** (Elfes 1989) and **online IMU-LIDAR extrinsic recalibration**.

**3. Real-Time Constraints (2 marks)**
- A 64-beam LIDAR at 10 Hz produces $\sim 1.3 \times 10^6$ points/s.
- Per-frame ICP on $10^6$ points = $\sim 200$ ms on embedded CPUs.
- Solution: **VOXEL down-sampling + NDT (Normal Distributions Transform)** for sub-50 ms updates.

**4. Conclusion (1 mark)**
- 3D mapping is fundamentally limited by the **memory-resolution-latency trilemma**. Current research uses **hybrid hierarchical maps** (local 3D + global topological).

---

**(b) Explain the SLAM problem. Discuss any three current challenges in SLAM-based map building.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**1. SLAM Definition (1.5 marks)**
**Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** is the problem of concurrently estimating a robot's pose $x_t$ and a map $\mathcal{M}$ from a stream of sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and controls $u_{1:t}$:
$$
P(x_t, \mathcal{M} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$
It is a *chicken-and-egg* problem: a good map needs accurate pose, and accurate pose needs a good map.

**2. Three Current SLAM Challenges (5.5 marks, ~1.8 each)**

**(i) Loop Closure at Scale**
- Detecting *“have I been here before?”* in 10 km trajectories requires **place-recognition CNNs** (NetVLAD, SuperPoint).
- False positives corrupt the global graph; current solutions use **geometric verification + temporal consistency**.

**(ii) Long-Term / Lifelong SLAM**
- After **months**, the world changes. *Dynamic SLAM* (Dynablox, Removert) filters out moving points using free-space carving.
- Open problem: balancing **forgetting old observations** vs **preserving landmarks**.

**(iii) Multi-Robot SLAM**
- Merging $k$ partial maps requires **inter-robot loop closure** and **distributed pose-graph optimization** (e.g., DOOR-SLAM, CGSM).
- Inter-robot initialisation (relative pose) is unsolved in GPS-denied environments.

**3. Conclusion (1 mark)**
SLAM has matured from *offline, static, single-robot* to *online, dynamic, multi-robot*. Remaining challenges are **semantic, lifelong, and collaborative**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often write *"SLAM is when the robot makes a map"* — this loses the **1.5 mark** for the joint-probability definition. Always write $P(x_t, \mathcal{M} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ or a verbal equivalent.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest]`

**(a) Describe the challenges in lifelong and semantic mapping. How do neural implicit representations (NeRF, SDF) attempt to solve them, and what are their limitations?** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**1. Lifelong Mapping Challenge (2 marks)**
A lifelong map $\mathcal{M}_t$ must be updated continuously for $t \to \infty$ **without unbounded memory growth** and **without catastrophic forgetting**.
- Information-theoretic bound: a map cannot store more than $H(\mathcal{M}) \le \log_2 \lvert \mathcal{M} \rvert$ bits.
- Standard grids hit this limit in **~hours**.

**2. Semantic Mapping Challenge (2 marks)**
Attaching *meaning* (kitchen, doorway, emergency exit) to geometry requires:
- Object detection CNNs (YOLO, Mask R-CNN)
- Panoptic segmentation
- 3D box fitting & tracking
The **open-set problem** — encountering objects never seen in training — is unsolved.

**3. Neural Implicit Solutions (2 marks)**
- **NeRF (Neural Radiance Fields)** encodes a scene as MLP weights; query is $O(1)$.
- **SDF (Signed Distance Function)** networks (DeepSDF, iMAP) provide continuous, differentiable geometry.
- Memory is **constant** regardless of scene size — solves the memory bottleneck.

**4. Limitations (1 mark)**
- **Slow training** (hours per scene).
- **No online update** — adding a new chair requires retraining the entire MLP.
- **Catastrophic forgetting** when scenes are learned sequentially.
- This is the *active frontier* of 2024 robotics research.

---

**(b) Explain the issues in multi-robot map fusion with a suitable block diagram. List any two algorithms used for distributed SLAM.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**1. The Fusion Problem Statement (1.5 marks)**
Given $k$ robots, each with local map $\mathcal{M}_i$ and pose graph $G_i$, produce a **globally consistent** map $\mathcal{M}_{\text{global}}$:
$$
\mathcal{M}_{\text{global}} = \mathop{\arg\min}_{\mathcal{M}} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lVert \mathcal{M} - T_i(\mathcal{M}_i) \rVert^2
$$
where $T_i$ are the unknown rigid transformations (relative poses).

**2. Three Core Issues (3.5 marks, ~1.2 each)**

**(i) Unknown Inter-Robot Transform** $T_{ij}$
- Without shared reference, robots cannot align maps.
- Solutions: encounter-based rendezvous, UWB ranging, or visual place recognition with RANSAC.

**(ii) Communication Bandwidth**
- Exchanging full point clouds is infeasible over WiFi at $10$ Hz.
- Solutions: **map compression** (octomap, point-cloud descriptors like Scan-Context).

**(iii) Conflict Resolution**
- Two robots may disagree on whether a cell is free or occupied.
- Solution: Bayesian averaging with weights $\propto 1/\sigma_i^2$.

**3. Block Diagram (1.5 marks)**
Draw the flow: *Robot 1/2/K → Local Pose Graph → Inter-Robot Loop Closure → Distributed Optimizer → Conflict Resolver → Global Map.* (Use Diagram 4 from Section 4 as reference.)

**4. Two Distributed SLAM Algorithms (0.5 mark)**

> **Algorithms to mention (any two):**
> 1. **DOOR-SLAM** — Distributed Online Outlier-Robust SLAM.
> 2. **CGSM** — Compact & Global SLAM for Multi-Robot (MRS).
> 3. **Multi-Agent SLAM Frontend** (Riazuelo et al., 2023).
> 4. **Kimera-Multi** — MIT 2021 distributed semantic mesh SLAM.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** In multi-robot questions, students forget the **unknown transform** $T_{ij}$. Always state explicitly that *“robots have no common global reference frame”* before writing any fusion equation. This is worth **1.5 marks** on its own.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before the Exam**

- ✅ **Map representation** = formal spatial encoding → four components: **G, S, U, T**.
- ✅ **Two big families:** **Metric** (grids, point clouds) and **Topological** (graphs).
- ✅ **Hybrid maps** combine both and are the current research standard.
- ✅ **Occupancy grid memory formula:** $M = N_x N_y N_z \cdot B$ bytes.
- ✅ **Log-odds update:** $l_t = l_{t-1} + l_{\text{sensor}} - l_0$ (Moravec & Elfes, 1989).
- ✅ **Pose drift** grows as $\sigma_{\text{drift}} = \sigma_{\Delta} \sqrt{d/\delta}$ — justifies **loop closure**.
- ✅ **Six current challenges to remember by heart:** Scale, Dynamics, Resolution–Real-time, Sensor Heterogeneity, Semantic Open-Set, Multi-Robot Fusion.
- ✅ **3D grid scaling:** $M \propto 1/r^3$ — halving resolution multiplies memory by **8×**.
- ✅ **NeRF / SDF** = constant memory, but **cannot update online** — frontier 2024 research.
- ✅ **Lifelong SLAM** = the problem of updating for $t \to \infty$ without catastrophic forgetting.
- ✅ **Multi-robot fusion** = requires solving **unknown inter-robot transform $T_{ij}$** first.
- ✅ **Bayesian cell update:** $P(m \mid z) = \dfrac{P(z \mid m) P(m)}{P(z)}$.
- ✅ **Probabilistic formula to recover occupancy** from log-odds:
  $$P_t(x) = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{\,l_t(x)}}$$
- ✅ **Common 14-mark question format:** *(a) 7-mark theory + (b) 7-mark with diagram/algorithm.*
- ✅ **Always draw a block diagram** in Part B — KTU awards **1.5 marks** purely for a *labelled* diagram.
- ✅ **Famous algorithms to quote:** Octomap, Cartographer, ORB-SLAM3, Kimera, NeRF, DOOR-SLAM.
- ✅ **Standard units used in answers:** metres (m), bytes (B/MB), probability (0–1), time (s).

> **End of Notes — Module 2, Topic: Current Challenges in Map Representation.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probabilistic Map-Based Localization - Markov Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Probabilistic Map-Based Localization - Markov Localization # Probabilistic Map-Based Localization: Markov Localization ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Markov Localization** is a recursive Bayesian filtering algorithm that estimates a robot's pose (position and orientation) within a known environment map by maintaining a probability density function (belief) over all possible robot locations. The technique is rooted in the **Markov Assumption**, which states that the robot's current state $x_t$ is conditionally independent of all earlier states given its immediately preceding state $x_{t-1}$. Mathematically, the posterior belief of the robot's pose is expressed as: $$Bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$$ where $x_t$ is the pose at time $t$, $z_{1:t}$ represents the sequence of sensor measurements, $u_{1:t}$ represents the sequence of control actions, and $m$ is the known environment map. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Blindfolded Guest in a Familiar House"** > > Imagine you are blindfolded and placed somewhere inside your own house (the map is known to you). You cannot see, but you can: > 1. **Feel the floor beneath your feet** (odometry/control $u_t$ — you know approximately how many steps you took and in which direction) > 2. **Touch nearby walls or furniture with a stick** (sensor measurement $z_t$ — you sense something solid in front of you) > > At every step, you update two mental "guesses": > - **Prediction**: "Based on my last known position and how far I think I walked, I am *probably* somewhere in this region." > - **Correction**: "But the wall I just touched tells me I must be in the hallway, not the bedroom!" > > Markov Localization does exactly this — it continuously fuses **noisy motion estimates** with **noisy sensor readings** to converge on the most likely robot pose. The standard precision benchmark for indoor localization systems is **±0.05 m** position accuracy with **±1°** orientation. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### The Two Fundamental Steps of Markov Localization The algorithm operates as a **two-stage recursive loop**: #### Stage A — Prediction (Motion Update) The robot predicts its new belief based on the executed control command $u_t$, **before** incorporating the new sensor reading. $$\overline{Bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot Bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ - $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ is the **motion model** (or state transition probability). - $\overline{Bel}(x_t)$ is the *predicted* belief (prior, before sensor update). #### Stage B — Correction (Measurement Update) The robot fuses the latest sensor measurement $z_t$ using Bayes' Theorem, producing the **posterior belief**. $$Bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \overline{Bel}(x_t)$$ - $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ is the **measurement model** (likelihood of observing $z_t$ at pose $x_t$ given map $m$). - $\eta$ is the **normalizer**, ensuring probabilities sum to 1: $$\eta = \frac{1}{\int p(z_t \mid x_t', m) \cdot \overline{Bel}(x_t') \, dx_t'}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The Markov Assumption ensures that only the **immediately previous state** $x_{t-1}$ is required to predict $x_t$. All earlier history is implicitly summarized in $Bel(x_{t-1})$. This is what makes the filter *recursive* and computationally tractable. ### Variants of Markov Localization | **Variant** | **Belief Representation** | **Computational Cost** | **Resolution** | **Best Suited For** | |-------------|---------------------------|------------------------|----------------|---------------------| | **Grid-Based Markov Localization** | Discrete grid cells, each holding a probability | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot G)$ where $G$ = grid cells | Bounded by grid resolution (e.g., **10 cm**) | Small-to-medium indoor maps | | **Topological Markov Localization** | Discrete set of distinctive places/nodes | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot P)$ where $P$ = places | Place-level granularity | Hallway, corridor environments | | **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** | Set of weighted particles | $\mathcal{O}(M)$ where $M$ = particle count | Continuous (approximate) | Large-scale, multi-modal environments | | **Kalman Filter Localization (EKF)** | Single Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$ | $\mathcal{O}(d^2)$ | Continuous, unimodal only | Unimodal, well-known starting pose | ### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Symbol / Expression** | **Meaning** | **Formula / Law** | **Domain / Unit** | |-------------------------|-------------|-------------------|-------------------| | $Bel(x_t)$ | Posterior belief at time $t$ | $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$ | Probability $\in [0, 1]$ | | $\overline{Bel}(x_t)$ | Predicted belief (motion update) | $\int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) Bel(x_{t-1}) dx_{t-1}$ | Probability density | | $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Motion/transition model | Odometry-based Gaussian | Probability density | | $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ | Sensor measurement model | Beam/landmark likelihood | Probability density | | $\eta$ | Bayesian normalizer | $1 / \sum_{x_t} p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \overline{Bel}(x_t)$ | Normalization constant | | Markov Assumption | State memoryless property | $p(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Logical axiom | | Total Probability Theorem | Belief integration over states | $\int p(A \mid B) p(B) dB$ | Probability law | | Bayes' Theorem | Posterior update | $p(A \mid B) = \eta \, p(B \mid A) p(A)$ | Probability law | | Initial belief (uniform) | Global localization start | $Bel(x_0) = 1 / \vert X \vert$ | Uniform distribution | | Initial belief (delta) | Position tracking start | $Bel(x_0) = \delta(x_0 - x_{init})$ | Dirac delta function | ### Engineering Real-World Utility Markov Localization is the **foundational workhorse** of: - **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs)** in Amazon, FedEx, and DHL warehouses — they maintain **sub-meter pose accuracy** while navigating. - **Autonomous Vehicle Lane-Keeping** subsystems using pre-built HD maps. - **Hospital Service Robots** delivering medication, requiring **±0.1 m** pose accuracy to align with elevator doors. - **Vacuum Cleaning Robots** (e.g., iRobot Roomba) — they use simplified topological Markov variants. - **Mars Rovers (NASA)** — MER and Perseverance rovers employ particle-filter-based variants of Markov Localization for terrain-relative navigation. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Derivation I: From Bayes' Rule to the Markov Localization Recurrence **Goal:** Show that the recursive localization update emerges naturally from Bayes' rule plus the Markov and static-world assumptions. **Step 1 — Start with the desired posterior via Bayes' Theorem:** $$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Markov Assumption to the measurement likelihood:** The current measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current pose $x_t$ and the static map $m$. Hence: $$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$ **Step 3 — Expand the prior $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)$ via Total Probability Theorem (integrating over $x_{t-1}$):** $$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \cdot p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \, dx_{t-1}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the Markov Assumption to the transition term:** Since $x_t$ depends only on $x_{t-1}$ and the most recent control $u_t$: $$p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$ **Step 5 — Substitute and identify the recursive structure:** The term $p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)$ reduces (under the static world assumption, $u_t$ gives no information about $x_{t-1}$) to: $$p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}, m) = Bel(x_{t-1})$$ Therefore, combining all steps: $$Bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot Bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ This is the **canonical Markov Localization recursion**. $\blacksquare$ --- ### Derivation II: Numerical Worked Example — Grid-Based Markov Localization **Setup (toy 1-D corridor of 5 cells):** | Cell index $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | |----------------|---|---|---|---|---| | Landmark? | ✗ | Wall (sensor detects) | ✗ | ✗ | Door (sensor detects) | **Initial belief — Global Localization (uniform):** $$Bel_0(i) = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20 \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$$ **Step 1 — Control $u_1$ = "Move right 1 cell" (with 0.8 probability of success, 0.1 of overshoot/undershoot):** The motion model (3-cell convolution kernel): $$p(x_t = j \mid x_{t-1} = i, u_1) = \begin{cases} 0.1 & j = i-1 \\ 0.8 & j = i \\ 0.1 & j = i+1 \end{cases}$$ Compute the predicted belief $\overline{Bel}_1$ for each cell: $$\overline{Bel}_1(j) = \sum_{i} p(j \mid i, u_1) \cdot Bel_0(i)$$ **Step 2 — Measurement $z_1$ = "Wall detected ahead":** Sensor model — probability of detecting a wall when at cell $i$: $$p(z_1 = \text{wall} \mid i) = \begin{cases} 0.90 & i = 2 \\ 0.05 & i \in \{1, 3, 4, 5\} \end{cases}$$ **Step 3 — Compute unnormalized posterior:** $$Bel_1^{\text{unnorm}}(i) = p(z_1 = \text{wall} \mid i) \cdot \overline{Bel}_1(i)$$ | $i$ | $\overline{Bel}_1(i)$ | $p(z_1 \mid i)$ | $Bel_1^{\text{unnorm}}(i)$ | $Bel_1(i)$ (normalized) | |-----|----------------------|-----------------|---------------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | $0.10 \cdot 0.20 + 0.80 \cdot 0.20 = 0.18$ | 0.05 | $0.0090$ | $0.0108$ | | 2 | $0.10 \cdot 0.20 + 0.80 \cdot 0.20 + 0.10 \cdot 0.20 = 0.20$ | 0.90 | $0.1800$ | $0.2167$ | | 3 | $0.10 \cdot 0.20 + 0.80 \cdot 0.20 + 0.10 \cdot 0.20 = 0.20$ | 0.05 | $0.0100$ | $0.0120$ | | 4 | $0.10 \cdot 0.20 + 0.80 \cdot 0.20 = 0.18$ | 0.05 | $0.0090$ | $0.0108$ | | 5 | $0.10 \cdot 0.20 = 0.02$ | 0.05 | $0.0010$ | $0.0012$ | | **Sum** | — | — | **0.2090** | **1.0000** | **Step 4 — Compute normalizer $\eta$:** $$\eta = \frac{1}{0.2090} \approx 4.7847$$ **Step 5 — Final normalized belief:** $$Bel_1(2) = 0.90 \times 0.20 \times 4.7847 \approx 0.8612 \text{ (peak shifts decisively to cell 2)}$$ After just **one** motion + one measurement, the algorithm has successfully localized to a single cell with **>86%** confidence — demonstrating the **exponential convergence** of Markov Localization. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1: Markov Localization Algorithmic Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Start: Time t] --> initB[Initialize Bel x0
Uniform: Global Localisation
Delta: Position Tracking] initB --> ctrlC[Receive Control Input ut
Odometry IMU Command] ctrlC --> predD[Prediction Step
Motion Model p xt xt-1 ut
Convolve Bel x-1 with kernel] predD --> obsE[Acquire Sensor Reading zt
Lidar Camera Sonar] obsE --> corrF[Correction Step
Bayes Update p zt xt m
Multiply with predicted belief] corrF --> normG[Normalisation
eta = 1 sum all cells] normG --> poseH[Extract Pose Estimate
argmax Bel xt or mean] poseH --> loopI{More Time Steps?} loopI -- Yes --> ctrlC loopI -- No --> stopJ[Output Final Pose Belief] style startA fill:#FFE4B5 style predD fill:#B0E0E6 style corrF fill:#98FB98 style normG fill:#FFB6C1 style poseH fill:#DDA0DD ``` ### Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Markov Localization System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SENSING[Sensor and Odometry Inputs] sensorA[Wheel Odometry ut] sensorB[Range Finder zt] sensorC[IMU Data] end subgraph MODELS[Probabilistic Models] modelA[Motion Model
p xt xt-1 ut] modelB[Measurement Model
p zt xt m] modelC[Known Map m] end subgraph FILTER[Markov Localisation Filter] filtA[Predicted Belief
xbar t] filtB[Posterior Belief
Bel t] filtC[Normaliser eta] end subgraph OUTPUT[Pose Estimation Output] outA[argmax Bel xt] outB[Covariance Sigma t] outC[Multi Modal Distribution] end sensorA --> modelA sensorB --> modelB modelC --> modelB modelA --> filtA sensorA --> filtA filtA --> filtB sensorB --> filtB filtB --> filtC filtC --> filtB filtB --> outA filtB --> outB filtB --> outC style SENSING fill:#FFF8DC style MODELS fill:#E0FFFF style FILTER fill:#FFE4E1 style OUTPUT fill:#E6E6FA ``` ### Diagram 3: Belief Evolution Over Time (Conceptual Heatmap) ```mermaid flowchart TD stateA[t=0 Uniform Belief
All cells = 0.20
Multi-modal diffuse] -->|First motion + measurement| stateB[t=1 Peak at landmark
One cell approx 0.86
Rapid convergence] stateB -->|Continued updates| stateC[t=k Converged
Single sharp peak
Approximately delta function] stateA -->|Kidnapped robot| stateD[Belief re-injected uniform
Global relocalisation triggered] stateD --> stateB style stateA fill:#FFE4B5 style stateB fill:#98FB98 style stateC fill:#87CEEB style stateD fill:#FFB6C1 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q1. State the Markov Assumption in the context of robot localization. Why is it computationally important?** **Model Answer:** The **Markov Assumption** states that the robot's current state $x_t$ is conditionally independent of all earlier states and measurements, given only the immediately preceding state $x_{t-1}$ and the most recent control $u_t$. Formally: $$p(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$ **Computational importance:** 1. It eliminates the need to store the **entire history** of states, controls, and measurements. 2. It reduces the belief update from an integral over an exponentially growing history to a tractable recursion: $Bel(x_t) = f(Bel(x_{t-1}), u_t, z_t)$. 3. It makes real-time implementation feasible on embedded processors with limited memory. 4. It enables the use of efficient data structures such as grids, particles, or Gaussians. **(3 marks)** --- > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Q2. Differentiate between Global Localization and Position Tracking problems. How does Markov Localization address both?** **Model Answer:** | **Aspect** | **Global Localization** | **Position Tracking** | |------------|------------------------|----------------------| | Initial belief | Uniform over the entire state space | Sharp peak near the initial pose (often Dirac delta) | | Belief shape | Initially **multi-modal** | Initially **unimodal** | | Difficulty | Harder — robot has no initial pose hint | Easier — robot's pose is approximately known | | Typical application | Robot starting up in an unknown part of a known map | Continuous pose update during navigation | | Computational load | Requires denser sampling to resolve multiple hypotheses | Converges quickly with sparse updates | **How Markov Localization addresses both:** The recursive Bayes filter naturally handles both scenarios. For global localization, the algorithm is initialized with a **uniform belief** $Bel(x_0) = 1 / \vert X \vert$; for position tracking, it is initialized with a **concentrated** belief. The same two-step predict-then-correct loop works in both cases, with multi-modal distributions handled via grids or particle clouds. **(3 marks)** --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive the recursive update equation for Markov Localization starting from Bayes' rule. Clearly state all assumptions used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 1-D mobile robot operates in a corridor divided into 6 cells. The initial belief is uniform. The robot executes a "move right" command with motion model probabilities $p(j \mid i, u) = 0.1$ for $j = i-1$, $0.8$ for $j = i$, and $0.1$ for $j = i+1$. After the move, a sensor detects a landmark only at cell 4 with $p(z \mid x=4) = 0.85$ and $p(z \mid x \neq 4) = 0.03$. Compute the posterior belief over all 6 cells. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q1(a): Starting from Bayes' rule: $$Bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)$$ **Assumption 1 (Static World):** The map $m$ is time-invariant. **Assumption 2 (Markov on measurements):** $p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$. **Assumption 3 (Total Probability):** $$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \cdot p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \, dx_{t-1}$$ **Assumption 4 (Markov on state):** $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$. **Assumption 5 (Static world on prior):** $p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = Bel(x_{t-1})$. Substituting and combining: $$\boxed{Bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot Bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}$$ This is the two-step Markov localization recursion. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q1(b): **Step 1 — Initial belief (uniform over 6 cells):** $$Bel_0(i) = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667 \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$$ **Step 2 — Predicted belief after motion (convolution with motion kernel):** For each target cell $j$, $\overline{Bel}_1(j) = \sum_i p(j \mid i, u) Bel_0(i)$. | $j$ | Computation | $\overline{Bel}_1(j)$ | |-----|-------------|----------------------| | 1 | $0.8 \times 0.1667$ | $0.1333$ | | 2 | $(0.1 + 0.8) \times 0.1667$ | $0.1500$ | | 3 | $(0.1 + 0.8 + 0.1) \times 0.1667$ | $0.1667$ | | 4 | $(0.1 + 0.8 + 0.1) \times 0.1667$ | $0.1667$ | | 5 | $(0.1 + 0.8 + 0.1) \times 0.1667$ | $0.1667$ | | 6 | $(0.1 + 0.8) \times 0.1667$ | $0.1500$ | **Step 3 — Multiply with measurement model $p(z \mid j)$:** | $j$ | $\overline{Bel}_1(j)$ | $p(z \mid j)$ | Unnormalized | |-----|----------------------|---------------|--------------| | 1 | 0.1333 | 0.03 | 0.0040 | | 2 | 0.1500 | 0.03 | 0.0045 | | 3 | 0.1667 | 0.03 | 0.0050 | | 4 | 0.1667 | 0.85 | 0.1417 | | 5 | 0.1667 | 0.03 | 0.0050 | | 6 | 0.1500 | 0.03 | 0.0045 | | **Sum** | — | — | **0.1647** | **Step 4 — Normalizer:** $$\eta = \frac{1}{0.1647} \approx 6.0728$$ **Step 5 — Posterior belief:** | $j$ | $Bel_1(j)$ (normalized) | |-----|--------------------------| | 1 | $0.0040 \times 6.0728 \approx 0.0243$ | | 2 | $0.0045 \times 6.0728 \approx 0.0273$ | | 3 | $0.0050 \times 6.0728 \approx 0.0304$ | | 4 | $0.1417 \times 6.0728 \approx 0.8605$ | | 5 | $0.0050 \times 6.0728 \approx 0.0304$ | | 6 | $0.0045 \times 6.0728 \approx 0.0273$ | The robot is correctly localized at cell 4 with **86%** confidence. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **(a)** Explain the difference between Grid-Based and Monte Carlo Localization. Discuss their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A robot uses a particle filter (MCL variant) with $M = 100$ particles. After resampling, the effective particle count $M_{\text{eff}}$ drops to 22. Compute $M_{\text{eff}}$ using the standard formula and explain the implications. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q2(a): | **Criterion** | **Grid-Based Markov Localization** | **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** | |---------------|-----------------------------------|------------------------------------| | **Belief representation** | Discrete probability grid, one value per cell | Set of weighted samples (particles) in continuous pose space | | **Resolution** | Fixed by grid cell size (e.g., **10 cm**, **5°**) | Adaptive; higher density where belief is concentrated | | **Computational cost** | $\mathcal{O}(G)$ per update, where $G$ is number of cells | $\mathcal{O}(M)$ per update, where $M$ is particle count | | **Memory** | High (one float per cell) | Moderate (one pose + weight per particle) | | **Multi-modal handling** | Naturally supported — multiple peaks can coexist | Supported, but particle depletion can collapse modes | | **Continuous pose** | Approximated by discrete cells | Native continuous support | | **Kidnapping recovery** | Excellent — easy to re-inject uniform belief | Poor without additional mechanisms (e.g., random particle injection) | | **Best for** | Small/medium maps, low-dimensional pose | Large-scale, high-dimensional maps | | **Key limitation** | Curse of dimensionality in 3-D pose spaces | Particle degeneracy, requires $M \geq 500$–$5000$ for reliability | Grid-based methods are **deterministic** and **dense** but suffer from the curse of dimensionality. MCL is **sampling-based**, scales better to higher dimensions, but requires careful resampling to avoid particle impoverishment. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q2(b): The **effective sample size** is defined as: $$M_{\text{eff}} = \frac{M}{1 + \text{Var}(w_t^{*(i)})} \approx \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} (w_t^{(i)})^2}$$ where $w_t^{(i)}$ are the **normalized** particle weights after the measurement update. **Step 1 — Given:** - Total particles: $M = 100$ - Reported effective sample size: $M_{\text{eff}} = 22$ **Step 2 — Compute the implied weight variance:** $$22 \approx \frac{1}{\sum_i (w_t^{(i)})^2} \implies \sum_i (w_t^{(i)})^2 \approx 0.0455$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation:** - Since $M_{\text{eff}} / M = 22 / 100 = 0.22$, only about **22%** of the particles effectively contribute to the posterior. - This is below the commonly used resampling threshold of $M_{\text{eff}} / M = 0.5$ (**50% rule**). - **Implication:** Resampling should be triggered. Many particles have negligible weights and are wasting computational effort. - After resampling, low-weight particles are replaced by copies of high-weight particles, increasing diversity at the cost of sample independence. **Step 4 — Recommended countermeasure:** Inject **5–10%** random particles to recover from potential particle depletion, especially in **kidnapped robot** scenarios. **(7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Forgetting the normalizer $\eta$** in Bayes updates costs full marks for the correction step. Always show the computation of $\eta$ explicitly. > 2. **Confusing $\overline{Bel}(x_t)$ (predicted) with $Bel(x_t)$ (posterior)** is a classic error. State which one is being computed at each step. > 3. **In numerical problems, students often forget to clip boundary cells** at the edges of the grid (cells 1 and 6 in the 6-cell example). Boundary handling is part of the marks. > 4. **Mixing up motion model and measurement model notation** ($p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ vs. $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$) leads to a cascade of wrong steps — examiners deduct heavily for this. > 5. **Skipping the Markov Assumption statement** before deriving the recursion loses the conceptual foundation marks. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Markov Localization** is a recursive Bayes filter that estimates the robot's pose by fusing motion and sensor data on a known map. - The **Markov Assumption** is the cornerstone: current state depends only on the previous state, not on the full history. - The **two-step recursion** is: **Predict** (motion update via $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$), then **Correct** (measurement update via $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$). - The **normalizer** $\eta$ is mandatory — it keeps the belief a valid probability distribution. - **Initialization matters:** uniform for *global localization* (no pose hint), delta/Dirac for *position tracking* (known initial pose). - **Variants** trade off resolution, cost, and dimensionality: **Grid-Based**, **Topological**, **MCL (Particle Filter)**, and **Kalman Filter** localization. - **Convergence** is exponential in the number of distinguishable landmarks observed. - **Kidnapped robot problem** requires either re-injection of uniform belief (grid) or random particle injection (MCL). - **Real-world benchmarks** to remember: indoor pose accuracy of **±0.05 m**, orientation of **±1°**, sampling rates of **10–40 Hz** for typical AMRs. - The algorithm is a **continuous belief filter** in theory but is **discretized** in practice (grids or particles) for computational feasibility.
