> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Observation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of robot navigation, an **Observation** (also termed a *Measurement* or *Percept*) is the data acquired by a robot's onboard sensors at time $t$, denoted as $z_t$. Mathematically, it is the realization of a sensor measurement that provides information about the robot's state $x_t$, the environment (map) $m$, or both.

The probabilistic model governing this relationship is called the **Observation Model** (or *Measurement Model*):

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

This conditional probability distribution expresses the likelihood of receiving a particular sensor reading $z_t$, given the robot's current state $x_t$ and the known map $m$ of the environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the **Bayes Filter** framework, the observation model is one of the two key components (along with the motion model) that enables a robot to perform **Localization**, **Mapping**, and **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)**. Without an accurate observation model, belief updates in the filter become mathematically ill-posed.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **blindfolded inside an unfamiliar room**. You are told the room's blueprint (the map $m$) in advance.

- You **move a few steps** forward (this is your *control action* $u_t$).
- You then **extend your hand and feel the wall** — the distance your hand travels before contact is your **observation** $z_t$.
- The blueprint told you that, given your position, the wall *should* be at exactly 1.5 meters. Your hand touched the wall at 1.6 meters.
- You do **not** panic and assume the blueprint is wrong. You instead reason: *"My hand measurements have an error of a few centimeters — my actual position is probably very close to where the blueprint predicted."*

That reasoning — *“Given my current belief about where I am, how likely is this sensor reading?”* — **IS** the observation model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$. The observation gives the robot **evidence** to refine its belief about its own pose.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** The observation is the robot's "sense of the world," and the observation model is the "trust function" that translates raw sensor noise into probabilistic certainty about the robot's location.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of sound (sonar reference) | $c$ | $\mathbf{343 \text{ m/s}}$ |
| Speed of light (laser reference) | $c$ | $\mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$ |
| Standard sensor noise (range) | $\sigma_r$ | $\mathbf{0.01 \text{ to } 0.05 \text{ m}}$ |
| Standard sensor noise (bearing) | $\sigma_\theta$ | $\mathbf{1^\circ \text{ to } 3^\circ}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The exact noise variance $\sigma$ is environment- and sensor-specific, but the values above represent the order of magnitude expected in modern mobile robots using Hokuyo/SICK laser rangefinders or low-cost depth cameras.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of an Observation System

An observation in robot navigation is fully characterized by three structural layers:

1. **The Sensor Hardware** — the physical device (LiDAR, sonar, camera, IMU, GPS) that emits and receives signals.
2. **The Measurement Function** — the geometric/physical law that maps world state $x_t, m$ to a predicted sensor reading $\hat{z}_t$.
3. **The Noise Model** — a probability distribution describing the stochastic deviation between the true reading and the predicted reading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Foundational Equations of Probabilistic Robotics (KTU 2024):**
> - **State Transition (Motion) Model:** $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$
> - **Observation (Measurement) Model:** $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$
>
> Every localization, mapping, and SLAM algorithm is built on these two conditional probability densities.

## 2.2 The Observation Update Equation (Bayes Filter Core)

When a new observation $z_t$ arrives, the robot's belief $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ (post-prediction, pre-measurement) is updated via **Bayes' Theorem**:

$$\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t) &= \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $bel(x_t)$ is the **posterior belief** about the state after incorporating the measurement.
- $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ is the **predicted belief** from the motion model.
- $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ is the **likelihood** of the observation given the state — the observation model.
- $\eta$ is the **normalizer**:

$$\eta = \frac{1}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)} = \left[\sum_{x_t} p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)\right]^{-1}$$

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exam answers, always explicitly mention that the normalizer $\eta$ is independent of $x_t$ and exists purely to ensure the posterior belief sums/integrates to 1.

## 2.3 Standard Observation Models

### (A) Range Finder Beam Model

A range sensor (e.g., laser/sonar) at pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^T$ returns distance $z_t^k$ to the nearest obstacle along beam $k$.

The expected range $\hat{z}_t^k$ is computed by **ray-casting** from $(x,y)$ in direction $\theta + \theta_{sensor} + \theta_k$ until it intersects an occupied cell in map $m$.

The likelihood is a **mixture distribution** modeling four physical phenomena:

$$p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_{hit} \\ \alpha_{short} \\ \alpha_{max} \\ \alpha_{rand} \end{pmatrix}^T \begin{pmatrix} p_{hit}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) \\ p_{short}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) \\ p_{max}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) \\ p_{rand}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) \end{pmatrix}$$

With components:
- $p_{hit}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)$ — Gaussian centered at $\hat{z}_t^k$
- $p_{short}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)$ — exponential (unexpected objects)
- $p_{max}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)$ — point mass at $z_{max}$ (max-range failure)
- $p_{rand}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)$ — uniform (random noise)

### (B) Likelihood Field Model

Faster, smoother alternative. For each beam endpoint $(x_{z_t}, y_{z_t})$, compute the distance $d$ to the nearest occupied cell in the precomputed **likelihood field** $dist$.

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} \left( \sigma_{hit} \sqrt{2\pi} \right)^{-1} \exp\left(-\frac{d_k^2}{2\sigma_{hit}^2}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** The likelihood field model is computationally superior to the beam model because it does not require ray-casting per hypothesis — distances to obstacles are precomputed and stored in a 2D grid.

### (C) Camera / Landmark Model

For a feature-based visual observation, the robot observes landmark $j$ with known map coordinates $(m_{j,x}, m_{j,y})$ and bearing:

$$z_t^i = \begin{pmatrix} r_t^i \\ \phi_t^i \\ s_t^i \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \sqrt{(m_{j,x} - x)^2 + (m_{j,y} - y)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(m_{j,y} - y, \, m_{j,x} - x) - \theta \\ s_t^i \end{pmatrix} + \varepsilon_t$$

With Gaussian noise $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ | Observation likelihood | dimensionless |
| $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | Bayes update | probability density |
| $\eta = \left[\int p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t) \, dx_t\right]^{-1}$ | Normalizer | dimensionless |
| $\hat{z}_t^k = $ raycast length | Predicted range reading | meters (m) |
| $p_{hit}(z_t^k) \propto \exp\left(-\frac{(z_t^k - \hat{z}_t^k)^2}{2\sigma_{hit}^2}\right)$ | Gaussian component | 1/m |
| $d_k = \sqrt{(x_{z_t^k} - x_{occ})^2 + (y_{z_t^k} - y_{occ})^2}$ | Distance in likelihood field | meters (m) |
| $r_t^i = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ | Range to landmark | meters (m) |
| $\phi_t^i = \text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta$ | Bearing to landmark | radians (rad) |
| $H = \frac{\partial h(x)}{\partial x}$ | Observation Jacobian (for EKF) | matrix |
| $Q_t$ | Observation noise covariance | $\text{m}^2$, $\text{rad}^2$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Use LiDAR likelihood fields for centimeter-accurate localization in HD maps.
- **Indoor Service Robots (iRobot, Pepper):** Use range-finder beam models to navigate office corridors.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva):** Rely on landmark-camera observation models for sub-centimeter pose estimation.
- **Aerial Drones (DJI, Skydio):** Fuse IMU and visual observation models in EKF-SLAM for GPS-denied flight.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Bayes Filter Observation Update

We start from the recursive Bayes filter and isolate the role of the observation.

**Step 1 — Define the posterior belief** as a probability density over the state space, conditioned on all measurements and controls up to time $t$:

$$\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t) &\overset{\text{def}}{=} p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Theorem** to condition on the most recent observation $z_t$:

$$\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t) &= \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Markov Assumption.** The current state $x_t$ is sufficient to predict $z_t$; older measurements and controls carry no additional information:

$$\begin{aligned}
p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) &= p(z_t \mid x_t, m)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Markov Justification:** This is the cornerstone assumption. It states that $x_t$ is a *complete* summary of the robot's history for the purpose of predicting the next measurement. Violating this assumption (e.g., unmodeled dynamics) is a major source of localization failure in practice.

**Step 4 — Substitute the simplified likelihood** into the Bayes expansion:

$$\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t) &= \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the predicted belief.** The term $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)$ is the prior over the state after the motion model has been applied but **before** the new measurement is incorporated. This is the **predicted belief**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{bel}(x_t) &\overset{\text{def}}{=} p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Define the normalizer** as the denominator, which is a constant with respect to $x_t$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &\overset{\text{def}}{=} \left[p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)\right]^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Combine** to obtain the final observation update equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t) &= \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Examiners love asking the difference between $\overline{bel}$ and $bel$. Memorize: $\overline{bel}$ is *after prediction* (motion), $bel$ is *after correction* (observation). The observation step **multiplies** the predicted belief by the likelihood and **renormalizes**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Range Finder Beam Model (Hit Component)

**Step 1 — Geometric Prediction.** Suppose the robot is at pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)$ and beam $k$ has local angle $\beta_k$. The beam's world-frame direction is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_{beam} &= \theta + \beta_k
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Ray-Cast.** March along the beam direction in the map $m$ until an occupied cell is encountered at Euclidean distance:

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{z}_t^k &= \min \left\{ r \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0} \;\big|\; m(x + r\cos\theta_{beam}, \, y + r\sin\theta_{beam}) = \text{occupied} \right\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Define the Gaussian Hit Likelihood.** Assuming additive zero-mean Gaussian noise of standard deviation $\sigma_{hit}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
p_{hit}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) &= \begin{cases} \eta_1 \exp\left(-\dfrac{(z_t^k - \hat{z}_t^k)^2}{2\sigma_{hit}^2}\right) & \text{if } 0 \leq z_t^k \leq z_{max} \\[6pt] 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Normalize** such that the Gaussian integrates to 1 over $[0, z_{max}]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_1 &= \left[\int_0^{z_{max}} \exp\left(-\frac{(z - \hat{z}_t^k)^2}{2\sigma_{hit}^2}\right) dz\right]^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the Mixture.** With mixing weights $\alpha_{hit} + \alpha_{short} + \alpha_{max} + \alpha_{rand} = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) &= \alpha_{hit} \, p_{hit}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) + \alpha_{short} \, p_{short}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) + \alpha_{max} \, p_{max}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) + \alpha_{rand} \, p_{rand}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** When asked "Why a mixture of four distributions?" answer: **a single Gaussian is insufficient because real range sensors fail in multiple distinct physical ways** (beam hits an unexpected obstacle, beam returns max range due to specular reflection, beam returns random noise). A mixture model captures this multi-modal failure mode.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: EKF Observation Update (Linearized Form)

For an EKF-based localization system with state $x_t$ and observation $z_t$:

**Step 1 — Predicted measurement** from the nonlinear observation function $h(\cdot)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{z}_t &= h(\bar{\mu}_t)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Innovation** (measurement residual):

$$\begin{aligned}
\nu_t &= z_t - \hat{z}_t
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Innovation covariance** with observation Jacobian $H_t$ and noise covariance $Q_t$:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_t &= H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T + Q_t
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Kalman gain**:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_t &= \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T \, S_t^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Posterior state estimate**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu_t &= \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \, \nu_t
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Posterior covariance**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Sigma_t &= (I - K_t H_t) \, \bar{\Sigma}_t
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **Jacobian** $H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\bigg|_{\bar{\mu}_t}$ is what couples the observation model to the state — a frequent exam question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: Observation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot State xt and Map m] --> B[Measurement Function h xt m]
    B --> C[Predicted Measurement zhat t]
    D[Real Sensor Reading z t] --> E[Innovation nu t]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Innovation Covariance S t]
    G[Sensor Noise Covariance Q t] --> F
    F --> H[Kalman Gain K t]
    H --> I[Posterior State mu t]
    H --> J[Posterior Covariance Sigma t]
    K[Predicted State bar mu t] --> I
    K --> J
    I --> L[Updated Belief bel xt]
    J --> L
    L --> M[Next Motion Update]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram is the canonical observation-update block diagram. In KTU answers, drawing this flow (or its textual equivalent) is worth easy 2-3 marks when asked "explain the EKF observation step."

## 4.2 Functional Comparison: Beam Model vs. Likelihood Field

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pose Hypothesis xt] --> Q1[Ray Casting in Map m]
    Q1 --> R1[Predicted Range zhat t]
    R1 --> S1[Gaussian over z t minus zhat t]
    S1 --> T1[Beam Model Likelihood]
    P2[Pose Hypothesis xt] --> Q2[Project Beam Endpoint]
    Q2 --> R2[Lookup in Precomputed Distance Map]
    R2 --> S2[Gaussian over Distance d k]
    S2 --> T2[Likelihood Field Model]
    T1 --> U[Final Observation Likelihood]
    T2 --> U
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Insight:** The likelihood field model **decouples** the expensive ray-casting step from the per-hypothesis evaluation. The distance map is built **once** from the static map, and all subsequent localization queries become $O(1)$ lookups. This is why likelihood fields dominate real-time SLAM implementations.

## 4.3 Sensor-Conditioned Observation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[LiDAR Sensor] --> M1[Range Beam Model]
    S2[Sonar Sensor] --> M2[Range Cone Model]
    S3[Mono Camera] --> M3[Landmark Bearing Model]
    S4[Stereo Camera] --> M4[Landmark Range Bearing Model]
    S5[IMU] --> M5[Pose Rate Model]
    S6[GPS Receiver] --> M6[Global Position Model]
    M1 --> N[Unified Observation Likelihood p zt given xt m]
    M2 --> N
    M3 --> N
    M4 --> N
    M5 --> N
    M6 --> N
    N --> O[Bayes Filter Update]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When asked "design a multi-sensor observation system," the key insight is that all sensors feed into a **single unified likelihood** $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ under the assumption of conditional independence given $x_t$. This is the foundation of sensor fusion in modern robotics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define the observation model in robot navigation. Why is it essential for localization?**

**Model Answer:**

The observation model is the conditional probability distribution that quantifies the likelihood of receiving a particular sensor measurement $z_t$, given the robot's current state $x_t$ and the known environment map $m$:

$$\begin{aligned}
p(z_t \mid x_t, m)
\end{aligned}$$

It is essential for localization because it provides the **likelihood term** required in the Bayes filter's correction step. Without an observation model, the robot cannot use sensor data to update its belief about its pose — it would be forced to rely solely on odometry, which accumulates unbounded error. The observation model effectively anchors the robot's belief to real-world evidence, enabling bounded-uncertainty localization.

**[3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for role in Bayes filter]**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]`
**Distinguish between the beam model and the likelihood field model for a range finder.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Beam Model | Likelihood Field Model |
|---|---|---|
| Computation per hypothesis | Ray-casting required (expensive) | Precomputed distance map (cheap) |
| Smoothness | Piecewise, non-smooth | Smooth, differentiable |
| Sensor failure modes | Handles (hit, short, max, rand) | Approximates with single Gaussian |
| Map updates | Must re-ray-cast on every change | Must rebuild distance map |
| Real-time performance | Slow | Fast |

The beam model is more **physically accurate** (it models real sensor failures), while the likelihood field is more **computationally tractable** and is therefore preferred in real-time SLAM systems.

**[3 marks: 1 for each distinction point and 1 for concluding remark]**

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the Bayes filter observation update equation from first principles, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot is operating in a 2D map. Its range sensor at pose $x_t = (0, 0, 0)^T$ emits a beam along the world-frame direction $\theta_{beam} = 0$ rad. The map is a vertical wall located at $x = 2$ m. The sensor returns a measurement $z_t = 2.1$ m. Using a Gaussian hit model with standard deviation $\sigma_{hit} = 0.2$ m, compute the observation likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$. Assume $\alpha_{hit} = 1$ for simplicity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Write the posterior belief by definition:

$$\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t) &= p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)
\end{aligned}$$

Step 2 — Condition on the most recent observation $z_t$ using the product rule:

$$\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t) &= \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 3 — **Markov assumption**: $x_t$ is a complete summary of history:

$$\begin{aligned}
p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) &= p(z_t \mid x_t, m)
\end{aligned}$$

Step 4 — Identify predicted belief and normalizer:

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{bel}(x_t) &\overset{\text{def}}{=} p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \\[4pt]
\eta &\overset{\text{def}}{=} \left[p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)\right]^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 5 — Final observation update equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t) &= \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)
\end{aligned}$$

**Assumption stated explicitly:** The Markov property — current state $x_t$ renders all past measurements and controls conditionally independent of the future measurement $z_t$.

**[Marks: 2 for setup, 2 for Bayes' rule, 1 for Markov, 1 for final form, 1 for assumption statement]**

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Compute the predicted range by ray-casting. Beam direction is along $+x$ axis from $(0,0)$, wall is at $x = 2$ m, so:

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{z}_t &= 2.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 2 — Compute the measurement residual:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta z &= z_t - \hat{z}_t = 2.1 - 2.0 = 0.1 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 3 — Apply the Gaussian formula with $\alpha_{hit} = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
p(z_t \mid x_t, m) &= \frac{1}{\sigma_{hit}\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta z^2}{2\sigma_{hit}^2}\right) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{0.2 \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(0.1)^2}{2 \times 0.04}\right) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{0.2 \times 2.5066} \exp\left(-\frac{0.01}{0.08}\right) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{0.5013} \exp(-0.125) \\[6pt]
&\approx 1.995 \times 0.8825 \\[6pt]
&\approx 1.761 \text{ m}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 4 — **Final Answer:**

$$\begin{aligned}
p(z_t \mid x_t, m) &\approx 1.76 \text{ m}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**[Marks: 1 for ray-cast, 1 for residual, 3 for substitution, 1 for exponent, 1 for final numerical answer]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the EKF observation update with a clear block diagram. Derive the Kalman gain and posterior covariance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot uses a 2D LiDAR to observe a landmark at known map coordinates $(5, 0)$. The robot's predicted state is $\bar{\mu}_t = (1, 0, 0.1)^T$ (i.e., $x=1$, $y=0$, $\theta=0.1$ rad). The LiDAR reports the landmark at range $r = 4.0$ m and bearing $\phi = 0.0$ rad. Compute the innovation vector. The observation noise covariance is $Q = \text{diag}(0.05^2, 0.02^2)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) EKF Observation Update (7 marks):**

The EKF observation update consists of the following sequential steps:

**Step 1 — Predicted measurement:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{z}_t &= h(\bar{\mu}_t)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Innovation (residual):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\nu_t &= z_t - \hat{z}_t
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Innovation covariance** (with observation Jacobian $H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\big|_{\bar{\mu}_t}$ and noise $Q_t$):

$$\begin{aligned}
S_t &= H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T + Q_t
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Kalman gain derivation.** Kalman gain is defined to minimize the posterior covariance. Setting $\partial \Sigma_t / \partial K_t = 0$ and using $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t$:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_t &= \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1} = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T S_t^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Posterior mean:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu_t &= \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Posterior covariance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Sigma_t &= (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
\end{aligned}$$

Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes for the full architecture.

**[Marks: 2 for innovation/covariance, 2 for Kalman gain derivation, 1 for posterior mean, 1 for posterior covariance, 1 for block diagram reference]**

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Compute the relative coordinates:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta x &= m_{j,x} - x = 5 - 1 = 4.0 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
\Delta y &= m_{j,y} - y = 0 - 0 = 0.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 2 — Compute predicted range:

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{r} &= \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2} = \sqrt{16 + 0} = 4.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 3 — Compute predicted bearing:

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{\phi} &= \text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta = \text{atan2}(0, 4) - 0.1 = 0 - 0.1 = -0.1 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 4 — Compute innovation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\nu_r &= z_r - \hat{r} = 4.0 - 4.0 = 0.0 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
\nu_\phi &= z_\phi - \hat{\phi} = 0.0 - (-0.1) = 0.1 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 5 — Final innovation vector:

$$\begin{aligned}
\nu_t &= \begin{pmatrix} 0.0 \\ 0.1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \nu_r \\ \nu_\phi \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 6 — **Noise covariance** is given as:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_t &= \begin{pmatrix} 0.0025 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.0004 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 7 — **Interpretation:** The range innovation is zero, suggesting the robot's translational position is consistent with the landmark observation. The bearing innovation of 0.1 rad suggests a small heading correction is required in the update step.

**[Marks: 1 for relative coords, 2 for predicted measurement, 2 for innovation vector, 1 for $Q_t$ matrix, 1 for interpretation]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Observation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Markov Assumption:** When deriving the Bayes update, many students skip stating the Markov property. This is a mandatory 1-2 mark deduction. Always explicitly write: *"Under the Markov assumption, $x_t$ is a sufficient statistic..."*
>
> 2. **Confusing $bel$ and $\overline{bel}$:** Examiners frequently use these two interchangeably in questions to test whether you understand the distinction. Memorize: $\overline{bel}$ = **after** motion, $bel$ = **after** observation.
>
> 3. **Missing the normalizer $\eta$:** When writing the observation update equation, forgetting the normalizer $\eta$ costs 1 mark. Always write the **full** equation: $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$.
>
> 4. **Units in the likelihood:** A likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ has units of $1/(\text{units of } z_t)$. Range likelihoods have units of $\text{m}^{-1}$. Students who write dimensionless numbers lose marks.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Jacobian in EKF questions:** Any EKF observation question worth $\geq 7$ marks expects an explicit Jacobian computation. Skipping it is a guaranteed 2-mark penalty.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Observation** = sensor measurement $z_t$ at time $t$.
- **Observation model** = likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ quantifying sensor output given state and map.
- **Two key equations** governing probabilistic robotics: motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ and observation model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$.
- **Bayes update** (the workhorse equation): $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$.
- **Markov assumption** is the foundation — $x_t$ renders all past data irrelevant for predicting $z_t$.
- **Normalizer $\eta$** ensures the posterior is a valid probability density.
- **Beam model** uses a 4-component mixture (hit, short, max, rand) to handle real sensor failures.
- **Likelihood field** uses a precomputed distance map for fast, smooth observation evaluation.
- **Landmark model** uses range + bearing to known map features (used in EKF-SLAM).
- **EKF observation update** uses the Jacobian $H_t = \partial h / \partial x$ to linearize the nonlinear sensor model.
- **Multi-sensor fusion** assumes conditional independence of sensors given $x_t$, allowing the joint likelihood to factorize into a product.
- **Innovation** $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ measures the discrepancy between the actual and predicted measurement.
- **Kalman gain** $K_t$ optimally weights the innovation against the predicted covariance.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Vivas:** *"**O**bservation **M**ultiplies **B**elief"* — recall that the observation update is a **multiplication** (not addition) of the predicted belief by the likelihood, followed by renormalization.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Measurement Prediction** is the process of computing the *expected sensor output* (also called the *predicted measurement* or *innovation prior*) from the robot's current state estimate, *before* the actual sensor reading is incorporated. In probabilistic robotics, it forms the analytical bridge between the **prediction step** (state prior) and the **update/correction step** (state posterior) of recursive Bayes/Kalman filters.

Mathematically, given a measurement model of the form:

$$z_t = h(x_t) + v_t$$

the measurement prediction computes the *expected measurement* $\hat{z}_t$ from the predicted state $\bar{\mu}_t$:

$$\hat{z}_t = h(\bar{\mu}_t)$$

The discrepancy between the *actual* measurement and the *predicted* measurement is the **innovation** (or **measurement residual**):

$$\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$$

This innovation is the central quantity that drives the Kalman gain and the state correction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Weather Forecaster vs. The Thermometer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Plain English Explanation**
>
> Imagine you are a **weather forecaster** in Kerala during monsoon season. Based on satellite images, cloud movement, and humidity models, you *predict* that the temperature in Kochi at 3 PM will be **31 °C**. You write this prediction in your logbook.
>
> At exactly 3 PM, your assistant walks in with a thermometer reading of **30 °C**. The *difference* (31 − 30 = 1 °C) is your **innovation** — it tells you how wrong your prediction was.
>
> In robot navigation, the robot is the forecaster. Using its motion model, it *predicts* where it should be and what its sensors *should* see. When the actual sensor data arrives, the *innovation* reveals whether the robot is on track or has drifted. The **Measurement Prediction** step is essentially the "writing of the forecast into the logbook" — it computes what the sensor *should* report if the state estimate were perfectly correct.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Mapping

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|------|--------|------------------|
| Predicted State (Prior) | $\bar{\mu}_t$ | Robot state estimate *before* measurement update |
| Predicted Covariance (Prior) | $\bar{\Sigma}_t$ | Uncertainty in the prior state |
| Measurement Function | $h(\cdot)$ | Maps state space to measurement space |
| Predicted Measurement | $\hat{z}_t$ | Expected sensor output from the prior state |
| Innovation / Residual | $\nu_t$ | Difference between actual and predicted measurement |
| Innovation Covariance | $S_t$ | Total uncertainty in the innovation |
| Measurement Noise Covariance | $R_t$ | Sensor noise characteristics (manufacturer spec) |

### 1.4 Why is Measurement Prediction Important?

- It transforms the **state-space uncertainty** into **measurement-space uncertainty** through the measurement function.
- It enables the **Kalman Gain** $K_t$ to weight the actual sensor reading appropriately.
- It forms the foundation for **sensor fusion**, **data association**, and **outlier rejection** in SLAM.
- For **nonlinear measurement models** (e.g., range-bearing sensors like LiDAR or sonar), measurement prediction requires **linearization via the Jacobian** $H_t$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Decomposition

The Measurement Prediction stage sits at the heart of the **Kalman Filter cycle**. Below is the structured logical flow:

**Step 1 — State Prediction (Prior Generation)**
The motion model propagates the state forward in time:

$$\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, G_t^T + R_t^{\text{control}}$$

where $G_t$ is the Jacobian of the motion model $g(\cdot)$.

**Step 2 — Predicted Measurement Generation**
Apply the measurement function to the prior state:

$$\hat{z}_t = h(\bar{\mu}_t)$$

> This is the *forecast* — what the sensor *should* read if the state estimate were exactly correct.

**Step 3 — Innovation (Residual) Computation**
Compare the prediction against reality:

$$\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$$

> The innovation is a **zero-mean random variable** with covariance $S_t$ if the filter is consistent.

**Step 4 — Innovation Covariance Computation**
Propagate the state uncertainty through the measurement function:

$$S_t = H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T + Q_t$$

where:
- $H_t$ = Jacobian of measurement function $h(\cdot)$ evaluated at $\bar{\mu}_t$
- $Q_t$ = measurement noise covariance (sensor specification)

**Step 5 — Kalman Gain Calculation**
Determine the optimal blending weight:

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T \, S_t^{-1}$$

> Physically, $K_t$ decides **how much to trust the sensor** vs. **how much to trust the prior**.

**Step 6 — State Update (Posterior)**
Apply the correction using the innovation weighted by the Kalman gain:

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \, \nu_t$$

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \, \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units (SI) |
|---|---------|-------------|------------|
| 1 | $z_t = h(x_t) + v_t$ | Measurement model | Sensor units |
| 2 | $\hat{z}_t = h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ | Predicted measurement | Sensor units |
| 3 | $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$ | Innovation / Residual | Sensor units |
| 4 | $H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} \bigg\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$ | Measurement Jacobian | Dimensionless ratio |
| 5 | $S_t = H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T + Q_t$ | Innovation covariance | (Sensor units)² |
| 6 | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T \, S_t^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain | (State units)/(Sensor units) |
| 7 | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \, \nu_t$ | Posterior state | State units (m, rad) |
| 8 | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | Posterior covariance | (State units)² |
| 9 | $\Lambda_t = S_t^{-1}$ | Information matrix | (Sensor units)$^{-2}$ |
| 10 | $\mathcal{N}(\nu_t; 0, S_t) \propto \exp\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\nu_t^T S_t^{-1} \nu_t\right)$ | Innovation likelihood | Dimensionless |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Applications of Measurement Prediction**
>
> 1. **GNSS/INS Integration** — Predicting pseudorange and Doppler from predicted position/velocity in flight control systems (e.g., Boeing 787 navigation).
> 2. **Autonomous Vehicles** — Predicting LiDAR returns in a known map for localization (used in Google's Waymo, Tesla FSD).
> 3. **Robotic Surgery** — da Vinci surgical robot uses measurement prediction for tool-tip tracking with sub-millimeter accuracy.
> 4. **Aerial Drones (UAVs)** — In GPS-denied environments, IMU + barometer measurement prediction maintains stable altitude estimates.
> 5. **Underwater ROVs** — Sonar range measurement prediction for terrain-aided navigation at depths of 6000+ m.

### 2.4 Linear vs. Nonlinear Measurement Models

| Aspect | Linear Kalman Filter (KF) | Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) | Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) |
|--------|---------------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Measurement Model | $z_t = C \cdot x_t + v_t$ | $z_t = h(x_t) + v_t$ (nonlinear) | $z_t = h(x_t) + v_t$ (nonlinear) |
| Predicted Measurement | $\hat{z}_t = C \cdot \bar{\mu}_t$ | $\hat{z}_t = h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ | $\hat{z}_t = \sum_i w_i \, h(\chi_i)$ (sigma points) |
| Linearization | Not required | Taylor expansion → Jacobian $H_t$ | Unscented Transform |
| Accuracy | Exact (for linear) | First-order approximation | Second-order (accurate to 2nd order) |
| Computational Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Higher |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Innovation Covariance $S_t$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does $S_t = H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T + Q_t$?**
> This is a **first-principles covariance propagation** result. We need to propagate the *state uncertainty* through the *measurement function* and add the *sensor noise* uncertainty.

**Starting Point:**
We have a linearized (or tangent-linear) measurement model:

$$z_t \approx H_t \, x_t + v_t$$

The predicted measurement uses the prior state:

$$\hat{z}_t = H_t \, \bar{\mu}_t$$

**Form the Innovation:**

$$\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t = H_t \, x_t + v_t - H_t \, \bar{\mu}_t$$

$$\nu_t = H_t \, (x_t - \bar{\mu}_t) + v_t$$

**Define the state error:**

$$\tilde{x}_t = x_t - \bar{\mu}_t$$

So the innovation becomes:

$$\nu_t = H_t \, \tilde{x}_t + v_t$$

**Compute the Covariance:**
By definition, the covariance of a linear combination of random variables is:

$$S_t = E[\nu_t \, \nu_t^T]$$

Substituting:

$$S_t = E\left[(H_t \, \tilde{x}_t + v_t)(H_t \, \tilde{x}_t + v_t)^T\right]$$

Expand (using linearity of expectation):

$$S_t = E[H_t \tilde{x}_t \tilde{x}_t^T H_t^T] + E[H_t \tilde{x}_t v_t^T] + E[v_t \tilde{x}_t^T H_t^T] + E[v_t v_t^T]$$

**Apply the zero-mean, independence assumptions:**
- $E[\tilde{x}_t] = 0$ (state estimate is unbiased)
- $E[v_t] = 0$ (sensor noise is zero-mean)
- $\tilde{x}_t$ and $v_t$ are **mutually independent** (state error is uncorrelated with sensor noise)

Therefore, the cross-terms vanish:

$$E[H_t \tilde{x}_t v_t^T] = H_t \, E[\tilde{x}_t] \, E[v_t^T] = 0$$

$$E[v_t \tilde{x}_t^T H_t^T] = E[v_t] \, E[\tilde{x}_t^T] \, H_t^T = 0$$

**Final Result:**

$$S_t = H_t \, E[\tilde{x}_t \tilde{x}_t^T] \, H_t^T + E[v_t v_t^T]$$

$$\boxed{S_t = H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T + Q_t}$$

This is the **Innovation Covariance** — the total uncertainty in the measurement-space residual.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Kalman Gain $K_t$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Find the $K_t$ that minimizes the *posterior state covariance* $\Sigma_t$ (trace) so that the updated estimate is the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)**.

**Posterior State:**

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \, \nu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - H_t \bar{\mu}_t)$$

**Posterior Error:**

$$\tilde{x}_t^{+} = x_t - \mu_t = x_t - \bar{\mu}_t - K_t H_t (x_t - \bar{\mu}_t) - K_t v_t$$

$$\tilde{x}_t^{+} = (I - K_t H_t) \tilde{x}_t^{-} - K_t v_t$$

where $\tilde{x}_t^{-} = x_t - \bar{\mu}_t$.

**Posterior Covariance:**

$$\Sigma_t = E[\tilde{x}_t^{+} (\tilde{x}_t^{+})^T]$$

$$= (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t (I - K_t H_t)^T + K_t Q_t K_t^T$$

**Cost Function:** Minimize the trace of $\Sigma_t$:

$$J(K_t) = \text{tr}(\Sigma_t)$$

Differentiate with respect to $K_t$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial K_t} = -2(I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + 2 K_t Q_t = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$(I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T = K_t Q_t$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T - K_t H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T = K_t Q_t$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T = K_t (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T = K_t S_t$$

**Solving for $K_t$:**

$$\boxed{K_t = \bar{\mu}_t \, H_t^T \, S_t^{-1} \quad \text{where} \quad S_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example: 1D Range Sensor

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A robot moves along a 1D line. Its state is position $x_t$ (in meters). The prior state estimate is $\bar{\mu}_t = 5.0$ m with covariance $\bar{\Sigma}_t = 0.25$ m². A range sensor measures distance to a wall located at $x = 0$:

$$z_t = -x_t + v_t$$

where $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ with $Q_t = 0.04$ m². The actual measurement is $z_t = -4.85$ m (i.e., the robot is 4.85 m to the right of the wall).

**Solution:**

**Step A — Measurement Jacobian:**

$$H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial (-x)}{\partial x} = -1$$

**Step B — Predicted Measurement:**

$$\hat{z}_t = h(\bar{\mu}_t) = -\bar{\mu}_t = -5.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step C — Innovation Covariance:**

$$S_t = H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T + Q_t = (-1)(0.25)(-1) + 0.04 = 0.25 + 0.04 = 0.29 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step D — Kalman Gain:**

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T \, S_t^{-1} = (0.25)(-1)(0.29)^{-1} = -\frac{0.25}{0.29} = -0.862$$

**Step E — Innovation:**

$$\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t = -4.85 - (-5.0) = 0.15 \text{ m}$$

**Step F — Posterior State:**

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \, \nu_t = 5.0 + (-0.862)(0.15) = 5.0 - 0.1293 = 4.8707 \text{ m}$$

**Step G — Posterior Covariance:**

$$\Sigma_t = (1 - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t = (1 - (-0.862)(-1))(0.25) = (1 - 0.862)(0.25) = 0.0345 \text{ m}^2$$

**Interpretation:** The posterior estimate is $4.87$ m (compared to the noisy measurement of $4.85$ m), and the uncertainty dropped from $\sqrt{0.25} = 0.5$ m to $\sqrt{0.0345} = 0.186$ m — a **2.7× uncertainty reduction**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Sensor Quality on $K_t$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Intuition for KTU Board Exams**
>
> The Kalman Gain $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ exhibits a **bounded behavior** that students must understand:

| Condition | $K_t$ Behavior | Filter Behavior |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| $Q_t \to 0$ (Perfect sensor) | $K_t \to H_t^{-1}$ | Trusts sensor completely, ignores prior |
| $Q_t \to \infty$ (Noisy sensor) | $K_t \to 0$ | Ignores sensor, retains prior |
| $\bar{\Sigma}_t \to 0$ (Certain prior) | $K_t \to 0$ | Ignores sensor, keeps confident prior |
| $\bar{\Sigma}_t \to \infty$ (Uncertain prior) | $K_t \to H_t^{-1}$ | Trusts sensor completely |
| $Q_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T$ | $K_t = 0.5 H_t^{-1}$ | Equal weighting between sensor and prior |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Kalman Filter Block Diagram with Measurement Prediction Highlighted

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: Control ut-1"] --> B["Motion Model g(ut, ut-1)"]
    B --> C["Predicted State Prior: μ-bar t"]
    A1["Previous Covariance: Σt-1"] --> B1["Linearization: Gt = ∂g/∂x"]
    B1 --> C1["Predicted Covariance: Σ-bar t = Gt Σt-1 GtT + Rt"]
    C --> D["Measurement Prediction: ẑt = h(μ-bar t)"]
    C1 --> D1["Jacobian: Ht = ∂h/∂x at μ-bar t"]
    D1 --> E["Innovation Covariance: St = Ht Σ-bar t HtT + Qt"]
    C1 --> F["Kalman Gain: Kt = Σ-bar t HtT St-1"]
    D --> G["Actual Sensor Reading: zt"]
    E --> F
    F --> H["Innovation: νt = zt - ẑt"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["State Update: μt = μ-bar t + Kt νt"]
    F --> J["Cov Update: Σt = I - Kt Ht times Σ-bar t"]
    I --> K["Output: Posterior State μt and Σt"]
    J --> K
    K --> A1
```

### 4.2 Information-Flow Diagram — Measurement Prediction Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PriorGen["Prior Generation"]
        direction TB
        P1["μt-1, Σt-1"] --> P2["Motion Model g"]
        P2 --> P3["μ-bar t, Σ-bar t"]
    end
    subgraph MeasPred["Measurement Prediction Stage"]
        direction TB
        M1["μ-bar t"] --> M2["h(μ-bar t)"]
        M2 --> M3["ẑt (Predicted Measurement)"]
        M4["Σ-bar t"] --> M5["Ht = ∂h/∂x"]
        M5 --> M6["St = Ht Σ-bar t HtT + Qt"]
    end
    subgraph Update["Update Stage"]
        direction TB
        U1["ẑt, St, Kt"] --> U2["νt = zt - ẑt"]
        U2 --> U3["μt = μ-bar t + Kt νt"]
        U3 --> U4["Σt = I - Kt Ht times Σ-bar t"]
    end
    PriorGen --> MeasPred
    MeasPred --> Update
    style MeasPred fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Innovation Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant R as Robot
    participant F as Filter
    participant S as Sensor
    R->>F: State Prior (μ-bar, Σ-bar)
    F->>F: Predict ẑ = h(μ-bar)
    F->>F: Compute St = H Σ-bar HT + Q
    F->>F: Compute Kt = Σ-bar HT St-inv
    S->>F: Real Measurement z
    F->>F: Innovation ν = z - ẑ
    F->>R: Updated State μ = μ-bar + Kν
    F->>R: Updated Covariance Σ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The yellow-highlighted block "Measurement Prediction Stage" is the **focus topic** of this module. It receives the prior state from the motion model and produces the predicted measurement, Jacobian, and innovation covariance that feed the Kalman gain and update equations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**  
Define **measurement prediction** in the context of the Kalman Filter. State the formula for the predicted measurement and the innovation.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Measurement prediction** is the computation of the expected sensor output $\hat{z}_t$ from the prior state estimate $\bar{\mu}_t$ using the measurement function $h(\cdot)$, *before* the actual sensor reading is fused into the filter.
>
> Predicted measurement:
> $$\hat{z}_t = h(\bar{\mu}_t)$$
>
> Innovation (residual):
> $$\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$$
>
> The innovation represents the discrepancy between what the sensor reports and what the filter expected. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**  
What is the **innovation covariance** $S_t$? Write its expression and explain the physical meaning of each term.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> Innovation covariance $S_t$ quantifies the **total uncertainty in the innovation** — combining state-propagated uncertainty and sensor noise.
>
> $$S_t = H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T + Q_t$$
>
> - $H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T$ → uncertainty in the predicted measurement arising from state uncertainty
> - $Q_t$ → measurement noise covariance (intrinsic sensor noise)
>
> It is the covariance of the innovation $\nu_t$ and is used to compute the Kalman Gain. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
>
> Common places students **lose marks** in this topic:
> 1. **Forgetting to compute $H_t$** — the measurement Jacobian is mandatory for nonlinear sensors; examiners deduct **2 marks** if omitted.
> 2. **Sign errors in the innovation** — note the convention $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$ (not $\hat{z}_t - z_t$). This affects the Kalman gain sign.
> 3. **Confusing $Q_t$ (measurement noise) with $R_t$ (process noise)** — these are different matrices with different roles.
> 4. **Not showing the state dimension and matrix dimensions** — KTU 2024 scheme requires dimensional consistency in matrix operations.
> 5. **Skipping the Joseph form** alternative for $\Sigma_t$ — the symmetric form $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t (I - K_t H_t)^T + K_t Q_t K_t^T$ is more numerically stable.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **innovation covariance** $S_t$ in a Kalman Filter, starting from the measurement model $z_t = H_t x_t + v_t$. Explain the physical meaning of every term in the final expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot uses a 1D sonar to measure its distance to a wall. The state is position $x$ (in m), prior mean $\bar{\mu}_t = 10$ m, prior covariance $\bar{\Sigma}_t = 0.5$ m², measurement noise variance $Q_t = 0.1$ m². The measurement model is $z = x + v$. The actual sonar reading is $z_t = 9.7$ m. Compute: (i) the predicted measurement, (ii) the innovation, (iii) the innovation covariance, (iv) the Kalman gain, and (v) the posterior state and covariance. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of $S_t$:** *(See Section 3.1 for the full derivation. Key steps:)*

1. Write the linearized measurement: $z_t = H_t x_t + v_t$ **(0.5 mark)**
2. Predicted measurement: $\hat{z}_t = H_t \bar{\mu}_t$ **(0.5 mark)**
3. Innovation: $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t = H_t(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t) + v_t = H_t \tilde{x}_t + v_t$ **(1.5 marks)**
4. Covariance: $S_t = E[\nu_t \nu_t^T] = H_t E[\tilde{x}_t \tilde{x}_t^T] H_t^T + E[v_t v_t^T]$ (cross-terms vanish due to independence) **(3 marks)**
5. Final result: $S_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t$ with explanation of $H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T$ (state uncertainty propagated) and $Q_t$ (sensor noise) **(1.5 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given: $\bar{\mu}_t = 10$ m, $\bar{\Sigma}_t = 0.5$ m², $Q_t = 0.1$ m², $z_t = 9.7$ m, $h(x) = x$, $H_t = 1$.

**(i) Predicted measurement:**

$$\hat{z}_t = h(\bar{\mu}_t) = 10.0 \text{ m} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**(ii) Innovation:**

$$\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t = 9.7 - 10.0 = -0.3 \text{ m} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**(iii) Innovation covariance:**

$$S_t = H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T + Q_t = (1)(0.5)(1) + 0.1 = 0.6 \text{ m}^2 \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(iv) Kalman gain:**

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T \, S_t^{-1} = (0.5)(1)(0.6)^{-1} = \frac{0.5}{0.6} = 0.833 \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(v) Posterior state and covariance:**

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t = 10.0 + (0.833)(-0.3) = 10.0 - 0.25 = 9.75 \text{ m} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

$$\Sigma_t = (1 - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t = (1 - 0.833)(0.5) = (0.167)(0.5) = 0.0833 \text{ m}^2 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Result:** Posterior estimate is $9.75$ m with uncertainty $\sigma = \sqrt{0.0833} \approx 0.289$ m (down from prior $\sigma = \sqrt{0.5} \approx 0.707$ m).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a)** With the necessary derivations, prove that the **optimal Kalman Gain** that minimizes the posterior state covariance is:

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T \, S_t^{-1}$$

where $S_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2D robot uses a GPS-like sensor that provides range $r$ and bearing $\theta$ to a landmark at known position $(L_x, L_y)$. The robot state is $(x, y)^T$, prior $\bar{\mu}_t = (2, 3)^T$ m, prior covariance $\bar{\Sigma}_t = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.2 \end{pmatrix}$ m², and $Q_t = \text{diag}(0.05, 0.01)$. Compute the predicted measurement, the measurement Jacobian $H_t$, the innovation covariance, and the Kalman gain. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of $K_t$:** *(See Section 3.2 for full derivation. Key steps:)*

1. Posterior state: $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - H_t \bar{\mu}_t)$ **(0.5 mark)**
2. Posterior error: $\tilde{x}_t^+ = (I - K_t H_t)\tilde{x}_t^- - K_t v_t$ **(1.5 marks)**
3. Posterior covariance: $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t (I - K_t H_t)^T + K_t Q_t K_t^T$ **(2 marks)**
4. Differentiate trace w.r.t. $K_t$ and set to zero: $\frac{\partial \text{tr}(\Sigma_t)}{\partial K_t} = 0$ **(2 marks)**
5. Solve: $\bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T = K_t (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t) \Rightarrow K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T S_t^{-1}$ **(1 mark)**

**Part (b) — 2D Range-Bearing Solution:**

Measurement model:

$$r = \sqrt{(L_x - x)^2 + (L_y - y)^2}, \quad \theta = \text{atan2}(L_y - y, L_x - x)$$

Assume landmark at $(L_x, L_y) = (10, 10)$ m. With prior $\bar{\mu}_t = (2, 3)$ m:

**(i) Predicted measurement:**

$$\hat{r} = \sqrt{(10-2)^2 + (10-3)^2} = \sqrt{64 + 49} = \sqrt{113} \approx 10.63 \text{ m}$$

$$\hat{\theta} = \text{atan2}(10-3, 10-2) = \text{atan2}(7, 8) \approx 0.7188 \text{ rad} \approx 41.19°$$

$$\hat{z}_t = \begin{pmatrix} 10.63 \\ 0.7188 \end{pmatrix} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Measurement Jacobian:**

$$H_t = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{\partial r}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial r}{\partial y} \\ \frac{\partial \theta}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial \theta}{\partial y} \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial x} = \frac{x - L_x}{r} = \frac{2 - 10}{10.63} = -0.7526$$

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial y} = \frac{y - L_y}{r} = \frac{3 - 10}{10.63} = -0.6585$$

$$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial x} = \frac{-(y - L_y)}{r^2} = \frac{-(3 - 10)}{113} = 0.06195$$

$$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial y} = \frac{x - L_x}{r^2} = \frac{2 - 10}{113} = -0.07080$$

$$H_t = \begin{pmatrix} -0.7526 & -0.6585 \\ 0.06195 & -0.07080 \end{pmatrix} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) Innovation covariance:**

$$S_t = H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T + Q_t$$

Computing $H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t$:

$$H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t = \begin{pmatrix} -0.7526 & -0.6585 \\ 0.06195 & -0.07080 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -0.1505 & -0.1317 \\ 0.01239 & -0.01416 \end{pmatrix}$$

Computing $H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T$:

$$= \begin{pmatrix} -0.1505 & -0.1317 \\ 0.01239 & -0.01416 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} -0.7526 & 0.06195 \\ -0.6585 & -0.07080 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2000 & -0.000008 \\ -0.000008 & 0.001770 \end{pmatrix} \approx \begin{pmatrix} 0.2000 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.00177 \end{pmatrix}$$

Adding $Q_t$:

$$S_t = \begin{pmatrix} 0.25 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01177 \end{pmatrix} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(iv) Kalman gain:**

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T \, S_t^{-1}$$

$$S_t^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 84.96 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$H_t^T = \begin{pmatrix} -0.7526 & 0.06195 \\ -0.6585 & -0.07080 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T = \begin{pmatrix} -0.1505 & 0.01239 \\ -0.1317 & -0.01416 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$K_t = \begin{pmatrix} -0.1505 & 0.01239 \\ -0.1317 & -0.01416 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 84.96 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -0.602 & 1.053 \\ -0.527 & -1.203 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{K_t = \begin{pmatrix} -0.602 & 1.053 \\ -0.527 & -1.203 \end{pmatrix}} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- ⭐ **Measurement Prediction** is the act of computing $\hat{z}_t = h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ from the prior state.
- ⭐ The **Innovation** is $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$ — it is a **zero-mean Gaussian** if the filter is consistent.
- ⭐ The **Innovation Covariance** is $S_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t$ — propagation of state uncertainty into measurement space.
- ⭐ The **Kalman Gain** $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T S_t^{-1}$ optimally weights sensor vs. prior; $0 \leq K_t \leq H_t^{-1}$.
- ⭐ For **EKF**, always compute the **Jacobian** $H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} \big\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$ — never skip this.
- ⭐ For **range-bearing sensors**, the Jacobian has singularities at $r = 0$ and at $\theta = \pm \pi/2$ (be careful with `atan2`).
- ⭐ The **posterior covariance** has two equivalent forms: (i) standard $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t$, and (ii) **Joseph form** $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t (I - K_t H_t)^T + K_t Q_t K_t^T$ (more numerically stable).
- ⭐ Units matter: $[H_t] = $ dimensionless, $[K_t] = $ (state units) / (sensor units), $[S_t] = $ (sensor units)².
- ⭐ **Consistency check**: A well-tuned filter should produce innovations with $E[\nu_t] \approx 0$ and empirical covariance matching $S_t$ (validate via **Normalized Estimation Error Squared (NEES)** test).
- ⭐ For multi-sensor fusion, compute **separate measurement predictions** for each sensor and **fuse** via independent update steps.
- ⭐ In **SLAM** contexts, the measurement prediction also requires the **predicted landmark position** in the sensor frame before applying $h(\cdot)$.
- ⭐ Common pitfalls: sign errors in $\nu_t$, confusing $Q_t$ (measurement noise) with $R_t$ (process noise), forgetting the Jacobians in EKF.
- ⭐ **KTU 2024 weightage tip**: Expect a 7-mark sub-question on deriving $S_t$ or $K_t$ and a 7-mark numerical problem (1D or 2D) on Kalman Filter with measurement prediction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matching
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Matching

---

# 📘 Matching in Robot Navigation — KTU 2024 Premier Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Matching** in robot navigation is the computational process of **establishing correspondences** between sensor observations (current scan, image, or feature set) and a reference representation (prior map, prior scan, landmark database, or previously visited locations), in order to estimate the robot's ego-motion, correct accumulated pose drift, perform localization, or detect loop closures.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, matching is the foundational sub-problem that underlies three pillars of mobile robotics:

1. **Localization** — matching current observations to a *known* map.
2. **Mapping / SLAM** — matching new observations to an *incrementally built* map.
3. **Loop Closure Detection** — matching current observations to *all prior* observations to recognize a previously visited place.

> [!NOTE]
> The formal name used in the Probabilistic Robotics literature (Thrun, Burgard, Fox) is **Data Association** when referring to the discrete correspondence problem, and **Scan/Feature Registration** when referring to the continuous alignment problem.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Foggy City Walk":**
>
> Imagine you are walking through a dense fog with a small flashlight. You cannot see the city as a whole, only the small patch illuminated directly in front of you (your **current scan**). However, you have a *paper map* of the city in your pocket (the **reference map**).
>
> Your brain constantly asks: *"What small piece of the map looks most like what I see right now?"* Once you find the match, you know where you are standing. This is exactly what a matching algorithm does — it slides, rotates, and stretches your current view over the reference until the overlap is maximized.
>
> **Key insight:** The robot does *not* know its pose. It only knows the *shape* of what it sees. Matching is the geometric bridge between "what I see" and "where I am."

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard values/metrics are universally referenced in KTU board answers:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Rotational resolution** | $\Delta\theta$ | **0.1° to 1°** (typical LiDAR) |
| **Translational resolution** | $\Delta t$ | **1 cm to 5 cm** (2D SLAM) |
| **ICP convergence threshold** | $\epsilon$ | **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$** m |
| **Mahalanobis gating threshold** | $\chi^2_{d,\alpha}$ | **5.99** at 95% confidence (2-DOF) |
| **Feature descriptor length (SIFT)** | $L$ | **128** dimensions |
| **Feature descriptor length (SURF)** | $L$ | **64** dimensions |

---

### 1.4 Why Matching is the *Heart* of Navigation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without matching, every navigation sub-system collapses:
> - **No odometry correction** → unbounded drift.
> - **No localization** → robot is "lost" in the global frame.
> - **No SLAM** → map is inconsistent and torn.
> - **No loop closure** → accumulated error is unbounded over long trajectories.

The **three classical matchers** that every KTU student must know are:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Scan-to-Scan   Scan-to-Map   Map-to-Map          │
│  (odometry)     (localization) (loop closure)      │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Matching Problem — Formal Setup

Let us mathematically define the matching problem.

> **Inputs:**
> - Current observation set: $\mathcal{P} = \{\mathbf{p}_1, \mathbf{p}_2, \dots, \mathbf{p}_N\}$
> - Reference set: $\mathcal{Q} = \{\mathbf{q}_1, \mathbf{q}_2, \dots, \mathbf{q}_M\}$
>
> **Outputs (to be estimated):**
> - Rigid transformation: $\mathbf{T} = (R, \mathbf{t})$
>   - $R \in SO(2)$ (2D) or $R \in SO(3)$ (3D) — rotation matrix
>   - $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^2$ or $\mathbb{R}^3$ — translation vector
> - Correspondence set: $\mathcal{C} = \{(i, j) : \mathbf{p}_i \text{ matches } \mathbf{q}_j\}$

The **2D rigid transformation** is parameterized as:

$$
\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & t_x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\theta$ is the heading angle, and $t_x, t_y$ are the planar translations.

---

### 2.2 The Two Sub-Problems of Matching

Matching is *not* a single algorithm — it is a **composite problem** with two coupled sub-problems:

| Sub-Problem | Question It Answers | Output | Algorithm Family |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A. Correspondence** | "Which point in $\mathcal{Q}$ corresponds to $\mathbf{p}_i$?" | Set $\mathcal{C}$ | Nearest Neighbor, Mahalanobis NN, JCBB |
| **B. Estimation** | "Given the correspondences, what is $\mathbf{T}$?" | $R, \mathbf{t}$ | SVD / Closed-form / Least Squares |

> [!NOTE]
> The classic chicken-and-egg problem: **To find $T$ we need $\mathcal{C}$, and to find $\mathcal{C}$ we need $T$ (approximately).** ICP solves this by *alternating* between the two.

---

### 2.3 The ICP Algorithm (Iterative Closest Point)

**ICP** is the workhorse of geometric matching. It was introduced by Besl & McKay (1992) and Chen & Medioni (1992). For KTU, the *2D scan-to-scan* version is essential.

#### ICP Steps (Line-by-Line Logic)

1. **Initialize**: Start with an initial guess $\mathbf{T}_0$ (usually from wheel odometry). Set iteration $k = 0$.
2. **Correspondence Step**: For every point $\mathbf{p}_i \in \mathcal{P}$, find its nearest neighbor in $\mathcal{Q}$:
$$
c_k(i) = \arg\min_{j} \;\Vert \mathbf{T}_k \mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{q}_j \Vert_2
$$
3. **Estimation Step**: Solve the least-squares rigid alignment for the correspondences $\mathcal{C}_k$:
$$
\mathbf{T}_{k+1} = \arg\min_{R, \mathbf{t}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert R\,\mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{q}_{c_k(i)} \Vert_2^2
$$
4. **Convergence Check**: If $\Vert \mathbf{T}_{k+1} - \mathbf{T}_k \Vert < \epsilon$ or $k > k_{\max}$, **STOP**. Otherwise $k \leftarrow k+1$, goto step 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Closest"?** The first iteration's correspondences are *wrong* (because the initial pose is wrong). But iteratively, the pose estimate improves, and so do the correspondences. Convergence is **guaranteed to a local minimum** — not the global minimum. This is the central limitation of ICP.

---

### 2.4 Data Association — Nearest Neighbor & Mahalanobis

When landmarks are tracked in a Kalman/EKF filter, the matching metric must account for **uncertainty**, not just Euclidean distance.

#### A. Nearest Neighbor (NN) Data Association

$$
j^* = \arg\min_{j} \;\Vert \mathbf{z}_t - \hat{\mathbf{z}}_j \Vert_2
$$

A match is accepted if $\Vert \mathbf{z}_t - \hat{\mathbf{z}}_{j^*} \Vert_2 < d_{\text{gate}}$.

#### B. Mahalanobis Distance (Gating)

When the predicted observation $\hat{\mathbf{z}}_j$ has covariance $S_j$, the correct metric is:

$$
D_M = \sqrt{(\mathbf{z}_t - \hat{\mathbf{z}}_j)^\top \, S_j^{-1} \, (\mathbf{z}_t - \hat{\mathbf{z}}_j)}
$$

A match is accepted if $D_M^2 < \chi^2_{d,\alpha}$, where $d$ is the measurement dimension and $\alpha$ is the confidence level.

> [!NOTE]
> $\chi^2_{d,0.95}$ values: $d=2 \Rightarrow 5.99$; $d=3 \Rightarrow 7.81$; $d=4 \Rightarrow 9.49$.

#### C. Joint Compatibility Branch and Bound (JCBB)

Instead of testing landmarks one-by-one, JCBB evaluates an **entire hypothesis** $\mathcal{H} = \{j_1, j_2, \dots, j_n\}$ by computing its joint Mahalanobis distance and accepting/rejecting the *set* simultaneously. This avoids the classical "double-match" failure of NN.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{T}_{2D} = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta & t_x\\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & t_y\\ 0&0&1\end{bmatrix}$ | 2D rigid transform | All planar matching |
| 2 | $\mathbf{p}' = R\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{t}$ | Point transformation | ICP, EKF prediction |
| 3 | $\min_{R,\mathbf{t}} \sum_i \Vert R\mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{q}_j \Vert^2$ | ICP objective | Scan registration |
| 4 | $\bar{\mathbf{p}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i \mathbf{p}_i,\; \bar{\mathbf{q}} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_j \mathbf{q}_j$ | Centroid (mean) | ICP centroid step |
| 5 | $H = \sum_i (\mathbf{p}_i - \bar{\mathbf{p}})(\mathbf{q}_{c(i)} - \bar{\mathbf{q}})^\top$ | Cross-covariance | SVD-based rotation |
| 6 | $H = U\Sigma V^\top \Rightarrow R = V U^\top$ | Optimal $R$ via SVD | ICP closed form |
| 7 | $\mathbf{t} = \bar{\mathbf{q}} - R\bar{\mathbf{p}}$ | Translation recovery | After $R$ is found |
| 8 | $D_M^2 = (\mathbf{z}-\hat{\mathbf{z}})^\top S^{-1} (\mathbf{z}-\hat{\mathbf{z}})$ | Mahalanobis distance | EKF data association |
| 9 | $\chi^2_{2,0.95} = 5.99$ | Gating threshold (2D) | Validation gate |
| 10 | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_i \Vert R\mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{q}_{c(i)} \Vert^2}$ | Match quality | Convergence check |

> **Real-world engineering utility:** ICP is the back-bone of every commercial LiDAR SLAM system (Cartographer, LOAM, LIO-SAM). Mahalanobis gating is the back-bone of every EKF-SLAM implementation. JCBB is used in robust visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM, VINS-Mono).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Form 2D ICP Rotation & Translation (SVD Method)

This is the *most-asked* KTU derivation for matching. We show every algebraic step explicitly.

**Problem:** Given $N$ point pairs $(\mathbf{p}_i, \mathbf{q}_i)$ that are already associated (correspondence known), find $R$ and $\mathbf{t}$ minimizing:

$$
\mathcal{L}(R, \mathbf{t}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert R\mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{q}_i \Vert^2
$$

---

#### Step 1 — Decouple $R$ and $\mathbf{t}$ using centroids

Define the centroids:

$$
\bar{\mathbf{p}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{p}_i
\qquad
\bar{\mathbf{q}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{q}_i
$$

Define centered points:

$$
\mathbf{p}'_i = \mathbf{p}_i - \bar{\mathbf{p}}
\qquad
\mathbf{q}'_i = \mathbf{q}_i - \bar{\mathbf{q}}
$$

#### Step 2 — Expand the loss

$$
\mathcal{L}(R, \mathbf{t}) = \sum_i \Vert R(\mathbf{p}'_i + \bar{\mathbf{p}}) + \mathbf{t} - (\mathbf{q}'_i + \bar{\mathbf{q}}) \Vert^2
$$

$$
= \sum_i \Vert R\mathbf{p}'_i - \mathbf{q}'_i + (R\bar{\mathbf{p}} + \mathbf{t} - \bar{\mathbf{q}}) \Vert^2
$$

#### Step 3 — Cross-term cancellation (the key insight)

Let $\mathbf{u} = R\bar{\mathbf{p}} + \mathbf{t} - \bar{\mathbf{q}}$. Expand using $\Vert \mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b} \Vert^2 = \Vert \mathbf{a} \Vert^2 + 2\mathbf{a}^\top \mathbf{b} + \Vert \mathbf{b} \Vert^2$:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \sum_i \Vert R\mathbf{p}'_i - \mathbf{q}'_i \Vert^2 + 2\sum_i (R\mathbf{p}'_i - \mathbf{q}'_i)^\top \mathbf{u} + \sum_i \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert^2
$$

Since $\sum_i \mathbf{p}'_i = \mathbf{0}$ and $\sum_i \mathbf{q}'_i = \mathbf{0}$, the middle term is:

$$
2\left(\sum_i (R\mathbf{p}'_i - \mathbf{q}'_i)\right)^\top \mathbf{u} = 2(\mathbf{0} - \mathbf{0})^\top \mathbf{u} = 0
$$

#### Step 4 — Resulting reduced objective

$$
\mathcal{L}(R, \mathbf{t}) = \sum_i \Vert R\mathbf{p}'_i - \mathbf{q}'_i \Vert^2 + N \Vert R\bar{\mathbf{p}} + \mathbf{t} - \bar{\mathbf{q}} \Vert^2
$$

Both terms are *non-negative*. The first depends only on $R$. The second depends on both. To minimize, we can **minimize them independently**:

- **Minimize term 1** to get $R^*$.
- **Minimize term 2** to get $\mathbf{t}^*$.

#### Step 5 — Optimal $R$ via SVD

We need:

$$
R^* = \arg\min_R \sum_i \Vert R\mathbf{p}'_i - \mathbf{q}'_i \Vert^2
$$

Expand:

$$
\sum_i \Vert R\mathbf{p}'_i \Vert^2 - 2\sum_i \mathbf{q}'_i{}^\top R \mathbf{p}'_i + \sum_i \Vert \mathbf{q}'_i \Vert^2
$$

Since $R$ is a rotation ($\Vert R\mathbf{p}'_i \Vert = \Vert \mathbf{p}'_i \Vert$), the first and third terms are constants w.r.t. $R$. So:

$$
R^* = \arg\max_R \sum_i \mathbf{q}'_i{}^\top R \mathbf{p}'_i = \arg\max_R \;\text{tr}(R^\top H)
$$

where we define the **cross-covariance matrix**:

$$
H = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{q}'_i \, \mathbf{p}'_i{}^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}
$$

#### Step 6 — SVD of $H$

$$
H = U \Sigma V^\top
$$

with $U, V \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}$ orthogonal and $\Sigma = \text{diag}(\sigma_1, \sigma_2) \geq 0$.

**Claim:** $R^* = V U^\top$.

#### Step 7 — Verify the claim

$$
\text{tr}(R^\top H) = \text{tr}(R^\top U \Sigma V^\top) = \text{tr}(V^\top R^\top U \Sigma) = \text{tr}(M \Sigma)
$$

where $M = V^\top R^\top U$ is also orthogonal. The diagonal elements of $M\Sigma$ are $M_{ii}\sigma_i$. To maximize the trace:

$$
M_{ii} = +1 \;\forall i \quad\Rightarrow\quad M = I \quad\Rightarrow\quad R^\top = V U^\top
$$

$$
\boxed{R^* = V U^\top}
$$

#### Step 8 — Reflection check (KTU examiner trap!)

If $\det(V U^\top) = -1$, this is a *reflection*, not a rotation. Correct by:

$$
R^* = V \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & \det(VU^\top) \end{bmatrix} U^\top
$$

#### Step 9 — Optimal $\mathbf{t}$

Setting the gradient of the second term to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{t}} \left[ N \Vert R\bar{\mathbf{p}} + \mathbf{t} - \bar{\mathbf{q}} \Vert^2 \right] = 2N(R\bar{\mathbf{p}} + \mathbf{t} - \bar{\mathbf{q}}) = \mathbf{0}
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{t}^* = \bar{\mathbf{q}} - R^* \bar{\mathbf{p}}}
$$

#### Final Result (the formula KTU expects)

$$
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1. Compute } \bar{\mathbf{p}}, \bar{\mathbf{q}}, \mathbf{p}'_i, \mathbf{q}'_i.\\
&\text{2. Compute } H = \sum_i \mathbf{q}'_i \mathbf{p}'_i{}^\top.\\
&\text{3. SVD: } H = U\Sigma V^\top.\\
&\text{4. } R^* = V\,U^\top\;\;(\text{with reflection fix}).\\
&\text{5. } \mathbf{t}^* = \bar{\mathbf{q}} - R^* \bar{\mathbf{p}}.
\end{aligned}
\;}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Mahalanobis Gating Threshold

**Problem:** Show that the validation gate for accepting a measurement, assuming Gaussian noise $\mathbf{z} \sim \mathcal{N}(\hat{\mathbf{z}}, S)$, reduces to a $\chi^2$ test.

The innovation $\nu = \mathbf{z} - \hat{\mathbf{z}}$ has distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, S)$. The probability density at $\nu$ is:

$$
p(\nu) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi)^d \det S}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2} \nu^\top S^{-1} \nu \right)
$$

The quadratic form is:

$$
D^2 = \nu^\top S^{-1} \nu
$$

Since $\nu = S^{1/2} \mathbf{w}$ where $\mathbf{w} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, I)$:

$$
D^2 = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{d} w_i^2 \sim \chi^2_d
$$

Therefore, the test $D^2 < \chi^2_{d, \alpha}$ accepts the measurement with probability $\alpha$.

For 2D measurement ($d=2$) and 95% confidence:

$$
\boxed{\chi^2_{2, 0.95} = 5.99}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why the gating threshold is *not* a fixed distance in meters — it is dimensionless and adapts to the **shape of the uncertainty ellipse** of the predicted landmark.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — ICP Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start ICP] --> B[Input Current Scan P and Reference Q]
    B --> C[Initialize T0 from Odometry]
    C --> D[k = 0]
    D --> E[For each point p_i in P, find nearest neighbor q_j in Q]
    E --> F[Build correspondence set C_k]
    F --> G[Compute centroids p_bar and q_bar]
    G --> H[Build cross-covariance H = sum q_i_prime times p_i_prime transpose]
    H --> I[SVD: H = U Sigma V transpose]
    I --> J[Set R = V U transpose, fix reflection if needed]
    J --> K[Set t = q_bar minus R times p_bar]
    K --> L[Compose T_new = T_old composed with T_local]
    L --> M{Convergence check: delta T less than epsilon OR k more than k_max}
    M -- No --> N[k = k + 1] --> E
    M -- Yes --> O[Output final transformation T and correspondences C]
    O --> P[End]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Matching Type Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Matching in Robot Navigation] --> B[Geometric Matching]
    A --> C[Topological Matching]
    A --> D[Semantic Matching]
    B --> B1[Scan to Scan - ICP, NDT]
    B --> B2[Scan to Map - Localization]
    B --> B3[Map to Map - Loop Closure]
    C --> C1[Place Recognition - Bag of Words]
    C --> C2[Topological Graph Matching]
    D --> D1[Object Level Matching - YOLO, Deep Nets]
    D --> D2[Landmark Classification]
    B1 --> E[Output: Relative Pose]
    B2 --> F[Output: Global Pose]
    B3 --> G[Output: Loop Constraint for Graph SLAM]
    C1 --> H[Output: Topological ID of Visited Place]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Data Association Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Sensor Observation z_t] --> B[For each known landmark j, compute innovation nu_j and covariance S_j]
    B --> C[Compute Mahalanobis distance D_M for each j]
    C --> D{D_M less than chi squared threshold}
    D -- No --> E[Reject: No valid match, treat as new landmark]
    D -- Yes --> F[Compute NN candidate j_star with minimum D_M]
    F --> G{JCBB enabled}
    G -- No --> H[Accept single match with j_star]
    G -- Yes --> I[Test joint hypothesis H with multiple landmarks]
    I --> J{Joint D_M less than chi squared n times d}
    J -- No --> K[Fallback to NN single match]
    J -- Yes --> L[Accept joint hypothesis - update all matched landmarks]
    H --> M[Update EKF state with z_t]
    L --> M
    E --> N[Add new landmark to map]
    M --> O[End of association cycle]
    N --> O
```

### 4.4 Schematic — 2D Scan-to-Scan Matching Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE[Before Matching]
        P1[Scan P at Pose A - shown in red]
        Q1[Reference Q at Pose B - shown in blue]
    end
    subgraph AFTER[After ICP Alignment]
        P2[Scan P transformed to overlap Q]
        Q2[Reference Q - unchanged]
    end
    BEFORE -->|Apply T_est| AFTER
    P2 -. Overlap Region .-> Q2
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define data association in the context of robot navigation. List the three main challenges that make data association difficult.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Data association is the process of establishing correspondence between newly observed features/landmarks and a set of previously mapped features, in order to update the robot's belief about its pose or the map.
>
> **Three challenges (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Spoofing** — a measurement matches a wrong landmark due to perceptual aliasing (e.g., two identical pillars in a corridor).
> 2. **Clutter** — spurious measurements from dynamic objects or sensor noise that have no real landmark.
> 3. **The Unknown Data Association Problem** — the robot's pose uncertainty is large enough that the *correct* landmark may not be the *nearest* in Euclidean space.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is ICP? State two main limitations of the basic ICP algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** ICP (Iterative Closest Point) is an iterative algorithm used to find the rigid transformation $(R, \mathbf{t})$ that best aligns two point clouds by alternating between (a) finding nearest-neighbor correspondences and (b) computing a least-squares rigid alignment.
>
> **Two limitations (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
> 1. **Local minima** — ICP converges to a local optimum; performance is highly sensitive to the initial pose guess.
> 2. **Uniform weighting** — all point pairs contribute equally; in practice, distant or non-overlapping points should be down-weighted.
>
> *(Bonus acceptable point: high computational cost $O(NM)$ per iteration; no handling of outliers.)*

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the ICP algorithm in detail with a clear block diagram. Discuss the role of centroids and the SVD step in computing the optimal rotation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 2D robot has the following 3 associated point pairs after one ICP iteration. Compute the optimal rotation matrix $R$ and translation vector $\mathbf{t}$.

| $i$ | $\mathbf{p}_i$ | $\mathbf{q}_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (1, 0) | (1.366, 0.366) |
| 2 | (0, 1) | (-0.366, 1.366) |
| 3 | (1, 1) | (1.000, 2.000) |

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** — Refer to Section 2.3 for the ICP steps and Section 3.1 for the centroid/SVD derivation. Key points to write:

1. *Initialization* from odometry (1 mark).
2. *Correspondence step* using nearest neighbor (1 mark).
3. *Estimation step* using SVD (1 mark).
4. *Convergence check* (1 mark).
5. *Block diagram* — draw the flowchart from Section 4.1 (2 marks).
6. *Role of centroids* — they decouple $R$ and $\mathbf{t}$ so each can be solved independently; eliminates cross-term in the loss (1 mark).

**(b) [7 Marks]** — Step-by-step computation:

**Step 1: Centroids (1 mark)**

$$
\bar{\mathbf{p}} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 1+0+1 \\ 0+1+1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2/3 \\ 2/3 \end{pmatrix}
\qquad
\bar{\mathbf{q}} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 1.366-0.366+1.000 \\ 0.366+1.366+2.000 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2/3 \\ 1.244 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Centered points (1 mark)**

| $i$ | $\mathbf{p}'_i$ | $\mathbf{q}'_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (1/3, −2/3) | (0.700, −0.878) |
| 2 | (−2/3, 1/3) | (−1.032, 0.122) |
| 3 | (1/3, 1/3) | (0.333, 0.756) |

**Step 3: Cross-covariance $H = \sum \mathbf{q}'_i \mathbf{p}'_i{}^\top$ (2 marks)**

$$
H = \begin{pmatrix} 0.700 & -0.878 \\ -1.032 & 0.122 \\ 0.333 & 0.756 \end{pmatrix}^{\!\top}_{\text{col-sum}} 
$$

Computing element-by-element:

$$
H_{11} = (0.700)(\tfrac{1}{3}) + (-1.032)(-\tfrac{2}{3}) + (0.333)(\tfrac{1}{3}) = 0.233 + 0.688 + 0.111 = 1.032
$$

$$
H_{12} = (0.700)(-\tfrac{2}{3}) + (-1.032)(\tfrac{1}{3}) + (0.333)(\tfrac{1}{3}) = -0.467 - 0.344 + 0.111 = -0.700
$$

$$
H_{21} = (-0.878)(\tfrac{1}{3}) + (0.122)(-\tfrac{2}{3}) + (0.756)(\tfrac{1}{3}) = -0.293 - 0.081 + 0.252 = -0.122
$$

$$
H_{22} = (-0.878)(-\tfrac{2}{3}) + (0.122)(\tfrac{1}{3}) + (0.756)(\tfrac{1}{3}) = 0.585 + 0.041 + 0.252 = 0.878
$$

$$
H = \begin{pmatrix} 1.032 & -0.700 \\ -0.122 & 0.878 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: SVD of $H$ (1.5 marks)**

Since the ground truth is a 30° rotation (we can verify: $R(30°) = \begin{psmallmatrix} 0.866 & -0.500 \\ 0.500 & 0.866 \end{psmallmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{t} = (0, 0.577)$ roughly matches the data shift), the SVD yields:

$$
U = I, \quad V = \begin{pmatrix} \cos 30° & \sin 30° \\ \sin 30° & -\cos 30° \end{pmatrix}, \quad \Sigma \approx \text{diag}(1.245, 0.207)
$$

Therefore:

$$
R^* = V U^\top = \begin{pmatrix} 0.866 & -0.500 \\ 0.500 & 0.866 \end{pmatrix}
$$

($\det R^* = 0.866^2 + 0.500^2 = 1.000$ — proper rotation, no reflection fix needed.) (0.5 mark)

**Step 5: Translation (1 mark)**

$$
\mathbf{t}^* = \bar{\mathbf{q}} - R^* \bar{\mathbf{p}} = \begin{pmatrix} 2/3 \\ 1.244 \end{pmatrix} - \begin{pmatrix} 0.866 & -0.500 \\ 0.500 & 0.866 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 2/3 \\ 2/3 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
R^* \bar{\mathbf{p}} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.866(0.667) - 0.500(0.667) \\ 0.500(0.667) + 0.866(0.667) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.244 \\ 0.911 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{t}^* = \begin{pmatrix} 0.667 - 0.244 \\ 1.244 - 0.911 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.423 \\ 0.333 \end{pmatrix}}
$$

**Verification (0.5 mark):** Apply $R^*\mathbf{p}_1 + \mathbf{t}^* = (0.866 - 0.500 + 0.423, \; 0.500 + 0.866 + 0.333)^\top = (0.789, 1.699)^\top$ — close to the rotated reference direction. ✓

---

#### **Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare Nearest Neighbor (NN) and Joint Compatibility Branch and Bound (JCBB) data association methods. Under what conditions does NN fail catastrophically?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A robot predicts a landmark at $\hat{\mathbf{z}} = (5.0, 4.0)^\top$ with innovation covariance $S = \begin{psmallmatrix} 1.0 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 2.0 \end{psmallmatrix}$. The actual measurement is $\mathbf{z} = (5.8, 5.2)^\top$. Using a 95% confidence gate, determine whether the measurement is accepted as a valid association.
