Show the Mahalanobis distance calculation explicitly.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

| Aspect | Nearest Neighbor (NN) | JCBB |
|---|---|---|
| **Hypothesis space** | Tests each landmark independently (1 mark) | Tests a *set* of landmarks jointly (1 mark) |
| **Computational cost** | $O(M)$ — fast (0.5 mark) | $O(M^n)$ — exponential, mitigated by branch-and-bound (0.5 mark) |
| **Uses uncertainty** | Partially (via Mahalanobis NN) | Fully (joint Mahalanobis over all innovations) (0.5 mark) |
| **Robustness to aliasing** | Poor (0.5 mark) | High (0.5 mark) |
| **When it fails** | Cluttered environments, geometrically symmetric landmarks, large pose uncertainty (1 mark) | Rarely fails — but at high computational cost (1 mark) |
| **Real-world example** | Early EKF-SLAM (0.5 mark) | Modern graph-SLAM back-ends (0.5 mark) |

**Conditions for NN catastrophic failure (1 mark):**
- **Close landmarks** — two landmarks within the same gating gate; NN arbitrarily picks one, corrupting the update.
- **Perceptual aliasing** — geometrically identical landmarks (e.g., two identical pillars).
- **High pose uncertainty** — innovation gate becomes huge, encompassing many landmarks.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Innovation (1 mark)**

$$
\nu = \mathbf{z} - \hat{\mathbf{z}} = \begin{pmatrix} 5.8 - 5.0 \\ 5.2 - 4.0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.8 \\ 1.2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Inverse of $S$ (1.5 marks)**

$\det S = (1.0)(2.0) - (0.5)(0.5) = 2.0 - 0.25 = 1.75$

$$
S^{-1} = \frac{1}{1.75}\begin{pmatrix} 2.0 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 1.0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.1429 & -0.2857 \\ -0.2857 & 0.5714 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Mahalanobis distance (2 marks)**

$$
D_M^2 = \nu^\top S^{-1} \nu = (0.8, \, 1.2) \begin{pmatrix} 1.1429 & -0.2857 \\ -0.2857 & 0.5714 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.8 \\ 1.2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Intermediate $S^{-1}\nu$:

$$
S^{-1}\nu = \begin{pmatrix} 1.1429(0.8) - 0.2857(1.2) \\ -0.2857(0.8) + 0.5714(1.2) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.9143 - 0.3429 \\ -0.2286 + 0.6857 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5714 \\ 0.4571 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Final:

$$
D_M^2 = (0.8)(0.5714) + (1.2)(0.4571) = 0.4571 + 0.5486 = 1.0057
$$

**Step 4: Comparison with threshold (1.5 marks)**

$$
D_M^2 = 1.0057 \quad\overset{?}{<}\quad \chi^2_{2, 0.95} = 5.99
$$

$$
\boxed{1.0057 < 5.99 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{ACCEPT the measurement}}
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation (1 mark)**
The Mahalanobis distance is *much smaller* than the threshold (only 17% of the gate radius squared). This indicates the measurement is highly consistent with the predicted landmark and is accepted with high confidence. The associated uncertainty ellipse (semi-axes $\approx 1.41$ and $\approx 0.94$ along the principal directions) easily contains the innovation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using Euclidean distance instead of Mahalanobis** — full 0 marks for the data-association step. Always use Mahalanobis when uncertainty is given.
> 2. **Forgetting the reflection fix in SVD-based ICP** — if the SVD yields $\det(VU^\top) = -1$, the answer is a *reflection*, not a rotation. Examiner deducts 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Not subtracting centroids before forming $H$** — applying $H = \sum \mathbf{q}_i \mathbf{p}_i^\top$ directly (without centering) is the #1 student error. Result is wrong $R$.
> 4. **Confusing the threshold** — students often write $\chi^2 = 3.84$ (which is the 1-DOF value). For 2D measurement, the correct threshold is **5.99**.
> 5. **Skipping convergence check in ICP description** — full ICP description without the convergence loop is incomplete; expect to lose 1 mark.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Matching = Correspondence + Estimation** — never treat it as a single algorithm.
- [x] **ICP alternates** between *closest-point* (correspondence) and *SVD-based alignment* (estimation) until convergence.
- [x] **2D rigid transform** has 3 DOF ($\theta, t_x, t_y$); 3D has 6 DOF.
- [x] **Centroid trick** decouples $R$ and $\mathbf{t}$ — *always* subtract centroids before computing $H$.
- [x] **Optimal $R$** = $V U^\top$ where $H = U\Sigma V^\top$; check $\det(R) = +1$ (fix reflection if $-1$).
- [x] **Optimal $\mathbf{t}$** = $\bar{\mathbf{q}} - R\bar{\mathbf{p}}$.
- [x] **ICP guarantees convergence to a local minimum** — needs a good initial guess from odometry.
- [x] **NN data association** uses Euclidean/Mahalanobis distance; **fast but fragile** under aliasing.
- [x] **JCBB** evaluates a *joint hypothesis* over multiple landmarks; **robust but exponential** in worst case.
- [x] **Mahalanobis gating** threshold at 95% in 2D is $\chi^2_{2,0.95} = 5.99$ — *not* a fixed distance.
- [x] **Three match types** to remember: scan-to-scan (odometry), scan-to-map (localization), map-to-map (loop closure).
- [x] **Loop closure** is what enables *globally consistent* maps in long-trajectory SLAM.
- [x] **RMSE of correspondences** is the standard metric for evaluating match quality.
- [x] **Real systems** (Cartographer, LOAM, ORB-SLAM) combine ICP variants with feature-based matching for robustness.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Estimation** in the context of robot navigation is the mathematical process of inferring the internal state of a robot (such as its position, orientation, velocity, and the location of environmental landmarks) from a sequence of noisy sensor measurements and uncertain control inputs. It is formally defined as the construction of a **belief** $\text{bel}(x_t)$ about the robot's state $x_t$ at time $t$, given all measurements $z_{1:t}$ and controls $u_{1:t}$ up to that time.

Mathematically, the posterior belief is expressed as:

$$
\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEBRT751, Module 2):** Estimation is the cornerstone of probabilistic robotics. It is implemented through recursive filters such as the **Bayes Filter**, **Kalman Filter (KF)**, **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**, and **Particle Filter (PF)**. The key cognitive outcome (mapped to **CO2**) is the ability to *apply Bayesian inference and probabilistic reasoning to mobile robot localization and tracking problems.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Blindfolded Navigator" Analogy**
> Imagine you are blindfolded inside an unfamiliar house and asked to find the kitchen. You take a step forward (control $u_t$), then poke the wall with your cane (measurement $z_t$). Your brain instantly fuses two sources of information:
>
> 1. **Prediction (Prior):** "Based on my last known position and the step I just took, I should be roughly *here*." This is the *motion model* — but it has accumulated uncertainty.
> 2. **Correction (Likelihood):** "My cane just hit a hard surface 1.2 m away. Given that, the probability I am standing next to the refrigerator is very high." This is the *measurement model*.
>
> The **estimation filter** is exactly this fusion. Each prediction nudges your belief; each measurement sharpens it. The mathematical machinery (Bayes' rule, Gaussians, particles) is the precise, computable version of what your brain does naturally.

## 1.3 The Two Foundational Probabilistic Models

Every estimation algorithm in robot navigation rests on two models:

| Model | Symbol | Purpose | Real-World Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Motion Model** (State Transition) | $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ | Predicts new state from previous state and control action | Odometer reading after moving |
| **Measurement Model** (Sensor) | $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ | Predicts the sensor reading expected from a state | LiDAR detecting a wall at distance $d$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Key Engineering Challenge:** Both models are *stochastic* (noisy). The estimator's job is to combine them *optimally* (in the minimum-variance sense) into a single, coherent belief $\text{bel}(x_t)$. Standard deviations are typically in the range **$\sigma \in [0.01, 0.5]$ m** for indoor mobile robots.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bayes Filter — The Universal Estimator

The **Bayes Filter** is the mathematically optimal recursive estimator for the belief $\text{bel}(x_t)$. It operates in a two-stage **predict–update** cycle every time step.

### Stage 1: Prediction (using the Motion Model)

$$
\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

This is the **Chapman–Kolmogorov** integral: it propagates yesterday's belief through the motion model, *increasing* uncertainty because the motion itself is noisy.

### Stage 2: Correction (using Bayes' Rule)

$$
\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)
$$

where the normalizing constant is:

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

This sharpens the prediction by multiplying it with the likelihood of the actual measurement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this high-yield for KTU?** The Bayes Filter recurrence relation is the single most-tested formula in Module 2. Memorize the **predict step integral** and the **update step product**.

## 2.2 The Kalman Filter (KF) — Linear Gaussian Case

When (i) the motion and measurement models are **linear**, and (ii) all noise is **zero-mean Gaussian**, the Bayes Filter admits a **closed-form solution** known as the **Kalman Filter**.

**Linearity Assumptions:**

$$
x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)
$$

$$
z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t, \quad \delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)
$$

Because Gaussians remain Gaussian under linear transformations, the belief is fully characterized by its **mean** $\mu_t$ and **covariance** $\Sigma_t$.

### 2.2.1 Kalman Filter Predict Step

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^\top + R_t
$$

### 2.2.2 Kalman Filter Update Step

**Innovation (measurement residual):**

$$
y_t = z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t
$$

**Innovation covariance:**

$$
S_t = C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top + Q_t
$$

**Optimal Kalman Gain** (the key engineering quantity — it weights prediction vs. measurement):

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top S_t^{-1}
$$

**Posterior mean:**

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t y_t
$$

**Posterior covariance:**

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

## 2.3 The Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) — Nonlinear Case

Real robot systems (odometry, range-bearing sensors, IMUs) are **nonlinear**. The EKF linearizes the models around the current estimate using the **first-order Taylor expansion (Jacobians)**.

**Nonlinear motion model:**

$$
x_t = g(u_t, x_{t-1}) + \epsilon_t
$$

**Nonlinear measurement model:**

$$
z_t = h(x_t) + \delta_t
$$

**Jacobians:**

$$
G_t = \frac{\partial g(u_t, x_{t-1})}{\partial x_{t-1}} \Bigg|_{\mu_{t-1}}, \quad H_t = \frac{\partial h(x_t)}{\partial x_t} \Bigg|_{\bar{\mu}_t}
$$

The KF equations are then applied with $A_t \to G_t$ and $C_t \to H_t$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** EKF is a *local* linearization. It fails when the posterior is **multimodal** (e.g., the robot is genuinely lost between two identical corridors). In such cases, a **Particle Filter** must be used.

## 2.4 The Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization)

When the state space is **non-Gaussian, multimodal, or high-dimensional**, the Bayes Filter integral cannot be solved analytically. The **Particle Filter** approximates the belief using a set of $M$ weighted samples (particles) $\mathcal{X}_t = \{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{M}$.

**Algorithm Summary:**

1. **Sampling:** Draw $\tilde{x}_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$.
2. **Importance Weighting:** $w_t^{[i]} = p(z_t \mid \tilde{x}_t^{[i]})$.
3. **Normalization:** $w_t^{[i]} \leftarrow w_t^{[i]} / \sum_j w_t^{[j]}$.
4. **Resampling:** Draw $M$ particles with replacement, with probability proportional to $w_t^{[i]}$.

The state estimate is:

$$
\hat{x}_t \approx \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} \tilde{x}_t^{[i]}
$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Equation / Boundary |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\text{bel}(x_t)$ | Posterior belief at time $t$ | $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ |
| Predict integral | Chapman–Kolmogorov | $\int p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1})\,\text{bel}(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}$ |
| Update rule | Bayes' theorem | $\eta \, p(z_t \vert x_t) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$ |
| KF State Update | Linear model | $x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t$ |
| KF Measurement | Linear model | $z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t$ |
| Kalman Gain | Optimal fusion weight | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top S_t^{-1}$ |
| Posterior Cov. | KF update | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t$ |
| EKF Jacobians | Linearization | $G_t, H_t$ replace $A_t, C_t$ |
| PF Posterior | Monte Carlo approximation | $\sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} x_t^{[i]}$ |
| Effective Samples | Resampling trigger | $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 / \sum (w_t^{[i]})^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Estimation theory underpins **GNSS-INS integration** in aircraft, **Apollo-era navigation**, **SLAM algorithms (EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM)**, **self-driving cars (LiDAR + camera fusion)**, and **spacecraft attitude determination**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bayes Filter Update Rule

We begin with the goal of computing the posterior $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.

$$
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
$$

Applying the definition of conditional probability:

$$
p(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

**Step 1 — Simplify the likelihood.** If $z_t$ depends only on $x_t$ (the **Markov assumption**), then $p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$.

$$
\text{bel}(x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the prior using the law of total probability over $x_{t-1}$.**

$$
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_{1:t}, z_{1:t-1}) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Markov again.** The new state $x_t$ depends only on the previous state and the most recent control.

$$
p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_{1:t}, z_{1:t-1}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the belief $\text{bel}(x_{t-1}) = p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})$.** Note the controls shift from $u_{1:t}$ to $u_{1:t-1}$ because the robot's previous state was conditioned on the previous control set.

$$
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

This integral is the **prediction step** $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$.

**Step 5 — Combine.**

$$
\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

This is the canonical Bayes Filter equation. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Kalman Filter Equations from First Principles

Assume the prior belief is Gaussian: $\text{bel}(x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})$, and both the motion and measurement models are linear with Gaussian noise.

**Step 1 — Compute the predicted (prior) belief.** The sum of two independent Gaussians is Gaussian.

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t
$$

For the covariance, we use the fact that $\text{Cov}(Ax) = A \, \text{Cov}(x) \, A^\top$:

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^\top + R_t
$$

**Step 2 — Form the joint distribution of $(x_t, z_t)$ given the prior.**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ z_t \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} I \\ C_t \end{bmatrix} x_t + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \delta_t \end{bmatrix}
$$

The mean is $\begin{bmatrix} \bar{\mu}_t \\ C_t \bar{\mu}_t \end{bmatrix}$. The joint covariance is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \bar{\Sigma}_t & \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top \\ C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t & C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top + Q_t \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Condition on the measurement.** Using the standard identity for conditioning a joint Gaussian:

$$
\mu_{x \mid y} = \mu_x + \Sigma_{xy} \Sigma_{yy}^{-1}(y - \mu_y)
$$

$$
\Sigma_{x \mid y} = \Sigma_{xx} - \Sigma_{xy} \Sigma_{yy}^{-1} \Sigma_{yx}
$$

Substituting $\Sigma_{xy} = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top$ and $\Sigma_{yy} = S_t = C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top + Q_t$:

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top S_t^{-1}
$$

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t - K_t S_t K_t^\top = (I - K_t C_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — 1D Robot Localization with KF

**Problem Setup.** A robot moves on a 1D track. Its state is $x_t$ (position in meters). The motion model is $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + \epsilon_t$, with $u_t = 1.0$ m and $\sigma_{\text{motion}} = 0.5$ m. A GPS-like sensor gives $z_t = x_t + \delta_t$ with $\sigma_{\text{GPS}} = 1.0$ m. Initial belief: $\mu_0 = 0, \Sigma_0 = 4$. At $t=1$, GPS reads $z_1 = 2.5$ m. Find $\mu_1$ and $\Sigma_1$.

**Step 1 — Identify matrices.** $A = 1, B = 1, C = 1, R = 0.25, Q = 1.0$.

**Step 2 — Predict.**

$$
\bar{\mu}_1 = (1)(0) + (1)(1.0) = 1.0
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_1 = (1)(4)(1) + 0.25 = 4.25
$$

**Step 3 — Innovation covariance and Kalman gain.**

$$
S_1 = (1)(4.25)(1) + 1.0 = 5.25
$$

$$
K_1 = (4.25)(1) / 5.25 = 0.8095
$$

**Step 4 — Innovation and update.**

$$
y_1 = 2.5 - (1)(1.0) = 1.5
$$

$$
\mu_1 = 1.0 + (0.8095)(1.5) = 2.2143
$$

$$
\Sigma_1 = (1 - 0.8095)(4.25) = 0.8095
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The KF produced a posterior with $\mu_1 = 2.21$ m and $\Sigma_1 = 0.81$ — a **massive** reduction from the prior $\Sigma_0 = 4$. This is because the GPS measurement (variance 1.0) was more informative than the motion (variance 0.25), pulling the estimate toward the measurement. The Kalman gain $K_1 \approx 0.81$ quantifies this trust.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bayes Filter Recurrence Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Belief bel x0] --> B[Predict Step]
    C[Control Input ut] --> B
    B --> D[Predicted Belief bar bel xt]
    E[Sensor Measurement zt] --> F[Update Step]
    D --> F
    F --> G[Posterior Belief bel xt]
    G --> H{More Steps?}
    H -- Yes --> B
    H -- No --> I[Output Estimated State]
```

## 4.2 Kalman Filter Signal Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Previous Mean mut-1] --> B[Predict Mean]
    C[Control ut] --> B
    B --> D[bar mut]
    E[Previous Cov Sigmat-1] --> F[Predict Cov]
    F --> G[bar Sigmat]
    D --> H[Innovation yt]
    I[Measurement zt] --> H
    H --> J[Kalman Gain Kt]
    G --> J
    K[Measurement Noise Q] --> J
    J --> L[Updated Mean mut]
    D --> L
    J --> M[Updated Cov Sigmat]
    G --> M
```

## 4.3 Estimator Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose an Estimator] --> B{System Model}
    B -- Linear Gaussian --> C{Kalman Filter}
    B -- Nonlinear differentiable --> D[Extended Kalman Filter]
    B -- Multimodal or nonparametric --> E[Particle Filter]
    C --> F{Is noise Gaussian?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Optimal Closed-Form Solution]
    F -- No --> H[Use EKF or UKF approximation]
    D --> I[Linearize via Jacobians Gt and Ht]
    E --> J[Monte Carlo Sampling Approach]
    I --> K[Watch for Linearization Errors]
    J --> L[Watch for Particle Deprivation]
```

## 4.4 EKF Local Linearization Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[True Nonlinear Trajectory] --> B[Current Estimate mut-1]
    B --> C[Linearize g about mut-1]
    C --> D[Compute Jacobian Gt]
    B --> E[Linearize h about bar mut]
    E --> F[Compute Jacobian Ht]
    D --> G[Apply Standard KF Predict]
    F --> H[Apply Standard KF Update]
    G --> I[New Posterior mut Sigmat]
    H --> I
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Bayes Filter and state its recurrence relation. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The Bayes Filter is a recursive algorithm for estimating the posterior belief $\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ about a robot's state from noisy measurements and controls. It is governed by the recurrence:

$$
\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

The first factor inside the integral is the **motion model**, the integral itself is the **prediction step**, the term $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ is the **measurement model**, and $\eta$ is the normalizer. It is the *only* mathematically consistent recursive estimator under the Markov assumption.

---

### Question 2: Why is the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) preferred over a standard Kalman Filter for most robotic systems? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Most real-world robot systems have **nonlinear** motion models (e.g., odometry with $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ terms) and **nonlinear** sensor models (e.g., range–bearing measurements). A standard Kalman Filter is restricted to **linear** models with Gaussian noise. The EKF handles this by **linearizing** the nonlinear functions $g(u_t, x_{t-1})$ and $h(x_t)$ around the current state estimate using **first-order Taylor expansion** (Jacobians $G_t$ and $H_t$). It then applies the standard KF equations to the linearized system, providing a tractable, near-optimal estimate for mildly nonlinear systems.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the Kalman Filter predict and update equations from the assumption of linear Gaussian motion and measurement models. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1D robot has the motion model $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + w_t$ where $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.4)$ and the measurement model $z_t = x_t + v_t$ where $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.6)$. Initial belief: $\mu_0 = 0, \Sigma_0 = 2$. Control $u_1 = 1.2$ m is applied. The sensor then returns $z_1 = 2.8$ m. Compute $\mu_1$ and $\Sigma_1$ using the Kalman Filter. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Assumptions:**
1. The system is linear: $x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t$, $z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t$.
2. All noise is zero-mean Gaussian: $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$, $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$.
3. The initial belief is Gaussian: $\text{bel}(x_0) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \Sigma_0)$.
4. The Markov property holds.

**Predict step derivation:**

The prior $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$ is obtained by convolving the previous Gaussian belief with the linear-Gaussian motion model. The mean transforms linearly:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t
$$

The covariance propagates via $\text{Cov}(Ax + b) = A \Sigma A^\top$:

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^\top + R_t
$$

**Update step derivation:**

Construct the joint Gaussian of $(x_t, z_t)$ conditional on $u_t$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ z_t \end{bmatrix} \Bigg| u_t \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left( \begin{bmatrix} \bar{\mu}_t \\ C_t \bar{\mu}_t \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} \bar{\Sigma}_t & \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top \\ C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t & C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top + Q_t \end{bmatrix} \right)
$$

Using the standard Gaussian conditioning identity:

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^\top + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.** $A=1, B=1, C=1, R=0.4, Q=0.6, \mu_0=0, \Sigma_0=2, u_1=1.2, z_1=2.8$.

**Step 2 — Predict.**

$$
\bar{\mu}_1 = (1)(0) + (1)(1.2) = 1.2
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_1 = (1)(2)(1) + 0.4 = 2.4
$$

**Step 3 — Innovation covariance and Kalman gain.**

$$
S_1 = (1)(2.4)(1) + 0.6 = 3.0
$$

$$
K_1 = (2.4)(1) / 3.0 = 0.8
$$

**Step 4 — Innovation and update.**

$$
y_1 = z_1 - C \bar{\mu}_1 = 2.8 - 1.2 = 1.6
$$

$$
\mu_1 = 1.2 + (0.8)(1.6) = 1.2 + 1.28 = 2.48
$$

$$
\Sigma_1 = (1 - 0.8)(2.4) = (0.2)(2.4) = 0.48
$$

**Final Answer:** $\mu_1 = 2.48$ m, $\Sigma_1 = 0.48$ m².

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A(b):** Students frequently make the following mistakes, **each losing 1 to 2 marks**:
> 1. Forgetting to **square the standard deviation** when computing $R$ and $Q$ (e.g., using $\sigma = 0.4$ instead of $R = \sigma^2 = 0.16$). This propagates wrong values throughout.
> 2. Computing the innovation as $z_t - \mu_{t-1}$ (the *old* mean) instead of $z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t$ (the *predicted* mean). The distinction between $\mu_{t-1}$ and $\bar{\mu}_t$ is the most commonly tested concept.
> 3. Dropping the matrix $C_t^\top$ in the Kalman Gain formula. Always check dimensions: $K_t$ must be compatible with the state vector.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the working of the Particle Filter algorithm for mobile robot localization. Discuss the importance of the resampling step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot operates in a 2D environment. The motion model is linear: $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + w_t$, where $u_t = [0.5, 0.0]^\top$ m and $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.1 I)$. The measurement model is the *nonlinear* range sensor: $z_t = \sqrt{(x_t - x_{\text{landmark}})^2 + (y_t - y_{\text{landmark}})^2} + v_t$, where the landmark is at $(3, 4)$ m and $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.05)$. The current estimate is $\bar{\mu}_t = [1.0, 1.0]^\top$. Compute the Jacobian $H_t$ of the measurement model at this estimate. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The **Particle Filter** represents the belief $\text{bel}(x_t)$ as a discrete set of $M$ weighted samples $\{(x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]})\}_{i=1}^{M}$. It is essential when the belief is **multimodal, non-Gaussian, or high-dimensional** — situations where KF and EKF fail.

**The four algorithmic steps:**

1. **Sampling (Prediction):** For each particle, draw a new state from the motion model:
$$
x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})
$$
This propagates the previous belief through the noisy motion.

2. **Importance Weighting (Correction):** Compute each particle's weight from the likelihood of the actual measurement:
$$
w_t^{[i]} = p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})
$$
Particles consistent with the sensor reading receive high weights; inconsistent ones receive low weights.

3. **Normalization:** Ensure weights sum to unity:
$$
w_t^{[i]} \leftarrow \frac{w_t^{[i]}}{\sum_{j=1}^{M} w_t^{[j]}}
$$

4. **Resampling:** Draw $M$ new particles with replacement, with selection probability equal to $w_t^{[i]}$. This is the **critical step** that prevents *particle deprivation* — the concentration of all probability mass on a few particles.

**Why resampling is essential:** Without resampling, the variance of the importance weights grows exponentially (a phenomenon called *weight degeneracy*). After a few iterations, all but a handful of particles carry negligible weight, and the filter effectively "wastes" computational effort. Resampling refocuses particles in high-likelihood regions, maintaining an efficient representation. However, resampling introduces its own variance, so it is typically triggered adaptively when the **effective sample size** falls below a threshold $N_{\text{thresh}} = M/2$:

$$
N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} (w_t^{[i]})^2}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Write the measurement model explicitly.**

Let the state be $x_t = [x_t, y_t]^\top$. Then:

$$
h(x_t) = \sqrt{(x_t - 3)^2 + (y_t - 4)^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivatives.**

$$
\frac{\partial h}{\partial x_t} = \frac{x_t - 3}{\sqrt{(x_t - 3)^2 + (y_t - 4)^2}}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial h}{\partial y_t} = \frac{y_t - 4}{\sqrt{(x_t - 3)^2 + (y_t - 4)^2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at $\bar{\mu}_t = [1.0, 1.0]^\top$.**

The range from the estimate to the landmark is:

$$
r = \sqrt{(1-3)^2 + (1-4)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606
$$

The Jacobian row vector is:

$$
H_t = \left[ \frac{1-3}{\sqrt{13}}, \frac{1-4}{\sqrt{13}} \right] = \left[ \frac{-2}{\sqrt{13}}, \frac{-3}{\sqrt{13}} \right] \approx [-0.555, -0.832]
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
H_t \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.555 & -0.832 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This Jacobian is then used in the EKF update equations in place of the matrix $C_t$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B(b):**
> 1. Many students forget to **take the square root** in the range computation, yielding a Jacobian with denominator $(x_t-3)^2 + (y_t-4)^2$ instead of $\sqrt{(\cdots)}$. This costs 2 marks.
> 2. Some forget to **evaluate the Jacobian at the predicted state** $\bar{\mu}_t$, not the *true* state. In real deployment, the true state is unknown; this is precisely why we need the filter.
> 3. The Jacobian must be a **row vector** (since the measurement $z_t$ is scalar). Conflating row vs. column orientation is a frequent dimensionality error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Estimation**
>
> - [x] The **Bayes Filter** is the *universal* optimal recursive estimator. KF, EKF, and PF are its special cases.
> - [x] The **predict step** uses the motion model; the **update step** uses the measurement model and Bayes' rule.
> - [x] The **Kalman Filter** is *optimal* only under **linearity + Gaussianity**. Outside this regime, it is merely an approximation.
> - [x] The **Kalman Gain** $K_t$ is the heart of the KF: it adaptively weights prediction vs. measurement based on their relative covariances.
> - [x] The **EKF** linearizes via Jacobians $G_t$ and $H_t$; it is a *local* approximation and fails for highly nonlinear or multimodal posteriors.
> - [x] The **Particle Filter** handles multimodal, non-Gaussian beliefs via Monte Carlo sampling. **Resampling** is essential to combat weight degeneracy.
> - [x] Always **square the standard deviation** to obtain process and measurement noise variances ($R = \sigma_r^2$, $Q = \sigma_q^2$).
> - [x] In KF, distinguish carefully between $\mu_{t-1}$ (previous posterior) and $\bar{\mu}_t$ (current prediction). The innovation is $z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t$.
> - [x] Standard units in KTU problems: position in **meters**, angles in **radians**, variances in **m²**, time step $t$ in dimensionless integer index.
> - [x] For 1D numerical problems, $A = B = C = 1$ and the KF equations reduce to scalar arithmetic — practice these until fluent.
> - [x] The effective sample size criterion $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 / \sum (w_t^{[i]})^2$ determines when to resample in a Particle Filter.
> - [x] Estimation theory is the gateway to **localization** and **SLAM** (Module 3/4 of the syllabus) — master it now.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Landmark-Based Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Landmark-Based Navigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Landmark-Based Navigation** is a *localization-driven* navigational paradigm in mobile robotics wherein a robot estimates its pose (position and orientation) within a known or partially known environment by detecting, extracting, and matching **salient environmental features** known as *landmarks*. A landmark is formally defined as any physically distinct, spatially stationary, and sensor-perceivable feature whose geometric properties (such as coordinates, shape, color, or intensity signature) can be robustly measured by the robot's onboard sensors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Landmark-based navigation is a robot localization strategy where a robot uses distinctive, stationary, and easily identifiable features in its environment (landmarks) to determine its position and orientation within a global or local reference frame, enabling path planning, obstacle avoidance, and goal-directed motion."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hiker with Mountain Peaks" Intuition

Imagine you are a hiker dropped blindfolded in an unfamiliar valley. You cannot read a map because you do not know your starting point. However, you can *see* a snow-capped mountain to the north, a lake to the east, and a tall radio tower to the southwest. By recognizing these **distinctive, unmoving features** (landmarks) and measuring their **angles and approximate distances** relative to you, you can *triangulate* your exact position on the map. Once you know "where you are," you can plan a path to any destination. A mobile robot does exactly the same thing — but with cameras, LiDAR, sonar, or RFID readers instead of human eyes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** The robot does **not** need to know the *entire* map in detail. It only needs to recognize a few *highly distinctive* features and match them to a stored database. This is what makes landmark-based navigation computationally cheaper than full SLAM in structured environments.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded Constants and Symbols)

| Symbol / Metric | Standard Value / Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $N_L$ | dimensionless integer | Total number of detected landmarks |
| $\mathbf{x}_r = (x, y, \theta)^T$ | meters, meters, radians | Robot pose in 2D |
| $\mathbf{m}_i = (m_{ix}, m_{iy})$ | meters | Position of the $i$-th landmark |
| $\mathbf{z}_i = (r_i, \phi_i, s_i)$ | meters, radians, signature | Observation of $i$-th landmark (range, bearing, signature) |
| $\sigma_r$ | **0.05 m to 0.30 m** (typical) | Standard deviation of range measurement noise |
| $\sigma_\phi$ | **1° to 5°** (typical) | Standard deviation of bearing measurement noise |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Foundational Assumptions Underlying Landmark-Based Navigation:**
> 1. **Stationarity Assumption:** Landmarks do *not* move during the robot's operation time horizon.
> 2. **Distinguishability Assumption:** Each landmark has a *unique* signature or geometric arrangement that prevents data-association ambiguity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Stage Operational Pipeline (Why and How)

Landmark-based navigation is implemented as a strict three-stage pipeline. Each stage has a specific role, and the failure of any one stage cascades into catastrophic localization error.

**Stage 1 — Landmark Detection (Perception)**
- **Why:** The robot must first *find* candidates in raw sensor data.
- **How:** Algorithms scan the sensor's field of view and extract features. For a 2D LiDAR, this is corner extraction or RANSAC line fitting. For a camera, this is SIFT, SURF, or ORB keypoint detection.
- **Output:** A set $\mathcal{Z} = \{\mathbf{z}_1, \mathbf{z}_2, \dots, \mathbf{z}_{N_L}\}$ of raw observations.

**Stage 2 — Data Association (Matching)**
- **Why:** The robot must know *which* landmark each observation corresponds to.
- **How:** Each observation is compared against a stored landmark database $\mathcal{M} = \{\mathbf{m}_1, \mathbf{m}_2, \dots, \mathbf{m}_K\}$ using a similarity metric (e.g., Euclidean distance for positions, descriptor distance for visual features).
- **Output:** A correspondence map $\mathcal{C}: \{1, \dots, N_L\} \rightarrow \{1, \dots, K\}$.

**Stage 3 — Pose Estimation (Localization)**
- **Why:** With known correspondences, the robot solves a geometric problem to recover its pose.
- **How:** The most common technique is the **EKF (Extended Kalman Filter)** update step or **least-squares minimization** of observation errors.
- **Output:** An updated estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_r$ with associated covariance $\mathbf{P}_r$.

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU End-Semester Examination question on this topic.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{z}_i &= h(\mathbf{x}_r, \mathbf{m}_i) + \mathbf{v}_i
\end{aligned}
$$

where the observation model $h(\cdot)$ for a 2D range-bearing sensor is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_i &= \sqrt{(m_{ix} - x)^2 + (m_{iy} - y)^2} \\
\phi_i &= \text{atan2}(m_{iy} - y,\; m_{ix} - x) - \theta \\
s_i &= \text{signature of landmark } i
\end{aligned}
$$

| Formula | Description | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| $r_i = \sqrt{(m_{ix}-x)^2 + (m_{iy}-y)^2}$ | Range to landmark | Used in triangulation, trilateration |
| $\phi_i = \text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta$ | Bearing to landmark | Used in angle-of-arrival (AoA) localization |
| $\mathbf{H}_i = \frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}_r}\big\vert_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_r}$ | Jacobian of observation model | Required for EKF update |
| $\mathbf{S}_i = \mathbf{H}_i \mathbf{P}_r \mathbf{H}_i^T + \mathbf{R}_i$ | Innovation covariance | Measures landmark observability |
| $\mathbf{K}_i = \mathbf{P}_r \mathbf{H}_i^T \mathbf{S}_i^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | Weights the new measurement vs. prior |
| $\mathbf{P}_D = 1 - (1-p_d)^{N_L}$ | Probability of detection | Critical for sensor placement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Landmark-based navigation is the *backbone* of autonomous vehicles (using road signs and lane markers), indoor service robots (using ceiling lights and AR markers), and UAV swarms (using RFID beacons). The same mathematics scales from millimeter-scale surgical robots to kilometer-scale planetary rovers.

### 2.3 Landmarks vs. Beacons vs. Features — A Critical Distinction

| Type | Placed by human? | Naturally occurring? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Landmark** | No | Yes | Trees, rocks, building corners |
| **Artificial Landmark** | Yes | No | QR codes, AR markers, reflective tape |
| **Active Beacon** | Yes | No | GPS satellites, Wi-Fi routers, RFID tags |
| **Passive Feature** | No | Yes | Door handles, window corners |

The KTU syllabus uses "landmark" in the *general* sense, encompassing all four categories.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Range-Bearing Observation Model

We derive the fundamental observation equation $h(\mathbf{x}_r, \mathbf{m}_i)$ for a 2D range-bearing sensor. Let the robot's pose be $\mathbf{x}_r = (x, y, \theta)^T$ and the landmark be at $\mathbf{m}_i = (m_{ix}, m_{iy})^T$ in a global frame.

**Step 1 — Define the relative displacement vector.**

The vector from the robot to the landmark in the global frame is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{d}_i &= \mathbf{m}_i - \mathbf{p}_r
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{p}_r = (x, y)^T$ is the robot's position. Expanding the components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{ix} &= m_{ix} - x \\
d_{iy} &= m_{iy} - y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 2D rotation matrix to transform into the robot's body frame.**

The robot's body frame is rotated by angle $\theta$ relative to the global frame. The rotation matrix from global to body is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{R}(\theta) &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the landmark coordinates in the robot's body frame are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{d}_i^{body} &= \mathbf{R}(\theta)\,\mathbf{d}_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Extract range as the Euclidean norm.**

The range is invariant under rotation, so it can be computed in either frame:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_i &= \sqrt{d_{ix}^2 + d_{iy}^2} = \sqrt{(m_{ix}-x)^2 + (m_{iy}-y)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Extract bearing as the arctangent of the body-frame components.**

The bearing is the angle from the robot's forward $x$-axis to the landmark. By definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_i &= \text{atan2}(d_{iy}^{body},\; d_{ix}^{body})
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding using the rotation matrix entries:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_i &= \text{atan2}\!\big(-(m_{ix}-x)\sin\theta + (m_{iy}-y)\cos\theta,\;(m_{ix}-x)\cos\theta + (m_{iy}-y)\sin\theta\big)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Add measurement noise.**

The complete stochastic observation model is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{z}_i &= \begin{bmatrix} r_i \\ \phi_i \\ s_i \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{v}_i, \quad \mathbf{v}_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R}_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{R}_i = \text{diag}(\sigma_r^2, \sigma_\phi^2, \sigma_s^2)$ is the measurement noise covariance.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The EKF Measurement Update for Landmark Observation

This derivation shows *how* a landmark observation updates the robot's pose belief — the heart of landmark-based navigation.

**Step 1 — State the prior belief.** Before observing the landmark, the robot believes its pose is distributed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_r^- &\sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}^-,\; \mathbf{P}^-)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Predict the expected observation.** Using the model derived above:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\mathbf{z}}_i &= h(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_r^-,\; \mathbf{m}_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the innovation (residual).** The innovation is the difference between what was *observed* and what was *expected*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{\nu}_i &= \mathbf{z}_i - \hat{\mathbf{z}}_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Linearize the observation model.** Since $h(\cdot)$ is nonlinear, we compute the Jacobian $\mathbf{H}_i$ by partial differentiation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{H}_i &= \frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}_r}\bigg\vert_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_r^-} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial r_i}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial r_i}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial r_i}{\partial \theta} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial \phi_i}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial \phi_i}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial \phi_i}{\partial \theta} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial s_i}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial s_i}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial s_i}{\partial \theta} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the signature $s_i$ (which is purely a function of the landmark identity), the partial derivatives with respect to the robot pose are zero. For range and bearing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial r_i}{\partial x} &= \frac{-(m_{ix}-x)}{r_i}, \quad \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial y} = \frac{-(m_{iy}-y)}{r_i}, \quad \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial \theta} = 0 \\[6pt]
\frac{\partial \phi_i}{\partial x} &= \frac{m_{iy}-y}{r_i^2}, \quad \frac{\partial \phi_i}{\partial y} = \frac{-(m_{ix}-x)}{r_i^2}, \quad \frac{\partial \phi_i}{\partial \theta} = -1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the innovation covariance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{S}_i &= \mathbf{H}_i \mathbf{P}^- \mathbf{H}_i^T + \mathbf{R}_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Kalman gain.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{K}_i &= \mathbf{P}^- \mathbf{H}_i^T \mathbf{S}_i^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Update the posterior belief.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_r^+ &= \hat{\mathbf{x}}_r^- + \mathbf{K}_i \boldsymbol{\nu}_i \\
\mathbf{P}^+ &= (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_i \mathbf{H}_i)\,\mathbf{P}^-
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **mathematical core** of every landmark-based navigation system.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting Landmarks for Maximum Observability

| Landmark Geometry | Position Fix Quality | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Landmarks spread **far apart** | High | Large baseline improves triangulation |
| Landmarks **collinear** with robot | Poor (rank-deficient) | Cannot resolve one axis |
| Landmarks **close to robot** | Moderate | Range measurements dominate |
| Landmarks at **90° spacing** | Optimal | Maximizes determinant of $\mathbf{H}$ |
| Landmarks **occluded** | Zero | No information gained |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Landmark-Based Navigation System

The following Mermaid diagram captures the complete information flow from raw sensor data to the updated robot pose. Every functional block represents a real software module that students must understand for the KTU examination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sensor Data Input] --> B[Landmark Detection Module]
    B --> C{Feature Extraction}
    C --> D[Set of Candidate Observations Z]
    D --> E[Data Association Module]
    E --> F[Landmark Database M]
    E --> G[Correspondence Map C]
    G --> H[Pose Estimation Engine]
    H --> I[Extended Kalman Filter]
    H --> J[Least Squares Solver]
    I --> K[Updated Robot Pose xr]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Path Planner]
    L --> M[Motion Controller]
    M --> N[Wheel Actuator Commands]
    N --> O[Robot Moves in Environment]
    O --> A
    F --> P[SLAM Mapper Optional]
    P --> F
```

### 4.2 State Transition Diagram for the Navigation Loop

This diagram illustrates the cyclic state machine that governs landmark-based navigation during operation. Node IDs use the Alpha Rule (alphanumeric, prefixed with letters, no reserved keywords).

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateInit
    stateInit: Initialization Load Map Database
    stateInit --> stateSearch
    stateSearch: Search for Landmarks in Sensor FOV
    stateSearch --> stateDetect: Landmark Detected
    stateSearch --> stateSearch: No Landmark Found
    stateDetect --> stateAssociate
    stateAssociate: Data Association Match to Database
    stateAssociate --> stateEstimate: Match Confidence Above Threshold
    stateAssociate --> stateSearch: Match Confidence Below Threshold
    stateEstimate: Pose Estimation EKF Update
    stateEstimate --> statePlan
    statePlan: Path Planning to Next Waypoint
    statePlan --> stateActuate
    stateActuate: Motion Control Execute Wheel Commands
    stateActuate --> stateSearch
    stateEstimate --> stateGoalReached: At Goal
    stateGoalReached --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Information Flow Architecture (Detailed Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PerceptionLayer[Perception Layer]
        S1[Camera Sensor]
        S2[LiDAR Sensor]
        S3[IMU Sensor]
    end
    subgraph ProcessingLayer[Processing Layer]
        P1[Feature Detector]
        P2[Descriptor Computer]
        P3[Noise Filter]
    end
    subgraph ReasoningLayer[Reasoning Layer]
        R1[Correspondence Matcher]
        R2[Geometric Solver]
        R3[Belief Updater]
    end
    subgraph ActionLayer[Action Layer]
        A1[Path Planner]
        A2[Controller]
        A3[Actuators]
    end
    S1 --> P1
    S2 --> P1
    S3 --> P3
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> R1
    P3 --> R2
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 -->|Feedback Motion| S1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define the term *landmark* in the context of robot navigation. List any **four** properties that an ideal landmark should possess.

**Model Answer:**
A landmark is a *distinctive, stationary, and sensor-perceivable* feature in the environment whose geometric or signature properties can be reliably measured by a robot to support localization.

**Four properties of an ideal landmark:**

1. **Stationarity** — The landmark must not move during the robot's operational time horizon.
2. **Uniqueness / Distinguishability** — It must have a signature (visual, geometric, or electronic) that is *unique* within the robot's working environment to prevent data-association ambiguity.
3. **Perceptibility** — It must be reliably detectable by the robot's onboard sensors under varying lighting, weather, and distance conditions.
4. **Sufficient Spatial Separation** — Landmarks should be geometrically well-distributed (ideally at near-90° angular spacing around the robot) to ensure full observability of all pose axes.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Differentiate between *natural landmarks* and *artificial landmarks* with one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Natural Landmark | Artificial Landmark |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Pre-existing in the environment | Deliberately placed by humans |
| **Examples** | Tree trunks, rock formations, building corners | QR codes, AR markers, reflective tape, RFID tags |
| **Cost** | Zero (uses existing features) | Requires installation and maintenance |
| **Robustness** | May change with seasons/weather | Designed for consistent perception |
| **Use Case** | Outdoor unstructured environments | Indoor structured environments |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. (A)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the range-bearing observation model for a 2D landmark observed by a robot with pose $\mathbf{x}_r = (x, y, \theta)^T$. State clearly all assumptions made.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A robot at pose $(2\text{ m}, 3\text{ m}, 30°)$ observes a landmark at global position $(8\text{ m}, 5\text{ m})$. Compute the *range* $r$ and *bearing* $\phi$ of the observation. Use $\text{atan2}$ conventions correctly.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The robot operates in a 2D plane (no elevation).
2. The landmark is stationary.
3. The sensor measures range and bearing in the robot's body frame.
4. Measurement noise is zero-mean Gaussian.

**Derivation:**

The displacement vector from the robot to the landmark in the global frame is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{d} &= \mathbf{m} - \mathbf{p}_r = (m_x - x,\; m_y - y)^T
\end{aligned}
$$

Rotating this vector into the robot's body frame using the 2D rotation matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{d}^{body} &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} m_x - x \\ m_y - y \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

The range is the Euclidean magnitude (rotation-invariant):

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \sqrt{(m_x - x)^2 + (m_y - y)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The bearing is the arctangent of the body-frame components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= \text{atan2}\!\big(-(m_x-x)\sin\theta + (m_y-y)\cos\theta,\; (m_x-x)\cos\theta + (m_y-y)\sin\theta\big)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Robot pose: $\mathbf{x}_r = (2, 3, 30°)^T$
- Landmark position: $\mathbf{m} = (8, 5)^T$

**Step 1 — Compute the global displacement components.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta x &= m_x - x = 8 - 2 = 6 \text{ m} \\
\Delta y &= m_y - y = 5 - 3 = 2 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the range.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2} = \sqrt{6^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{36 + 4} = \sqrt{40} \\
r &= 6.32 \text{ m (to two decimal places)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bearing using $\text{atan2}$.**

First, convert the robot heading to radians: $\theta = 30° = \pi/6 \approx 0.5236$ rad.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin(30°) &= 0.5 \\
\cos(30°) &= 0.8660
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute the body-frame components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_x^{body} &= \Delta x \cos\theta + \Delta y \sin\theta = (6)(0.8660) + (2)(0.5) = 5.196 + 1.0 = 6.196 \\
d_y^{body} &= -\Delta x \sin\theta + \Delta y \cos\theta = -(6)(0.5) + (2)(0.8660) = -3.0 + 1.732 = -1.268
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= \text{atan2}(-1.268,\; 6.196) = -11.56° \approx -0.2018 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= 6.32 \text{ m}, \quad \phi = -11.56°
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative bearing indicates the landmark is to the *right* of the robot's forward direction.

---

**Q3. (B) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the three-stage pipeline of landmark-based navigation with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of data association in the pipeline.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With a suitable example, explain how the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) is used to update the robot's pose estimate after observing a single landmark. Write the relevant update equations.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The three-stage pipeline is described below.

**Stage 1 — Landmark Detection:** The robot's sensors (camera, LiDAR, sonar) acquire raw data from the environment. A feature extraction algorithm identifies distinctive candidates (e.g., Harris corners, SIFT keypoints, LiDAR intensity peaks). The output is a set $\mathcal{Z}$ of raw observations.

**Stage 2 — Data Association:** Each observation in $\mathcal{Z}$ must be matched to a known landmark in the stored database $\mathcal{M}$. This is performed using a *similarity metric* — e.g., Euclidean distance for geometric landmarks, descriptor distance (Hamming or Euclidean) for visual landmarks. A common algorithm is the **Nearest Neighbor (NN)** test, gated by the Mahalanobis distance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_M^2 &= \boldsymbol{\nu}^T \mathbf{S}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\nu} \leq \chi^2_{d,\alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $d$ is the dimension of $\mathbf{z}$, $\alpha$ is the significance level, and $\chi^2_{d,\alpha}$ is the chi-squared threshold. Observations failing the gate are treated as spurious (clutter).

**Role of Data Association:** Without correct association, the pose estimate is *garbage-in, garbage-out*. A single mismatch can inject errors of meters into the localization. Robust data association is therefore the *most critical* sub-problem in landmark-based navigation.

**Stage 3 — Pose Estimation:** With correspondences established, the EKF (or least-squares solver) updates the robot's pose. The mathematical procedure is given in Part (b).

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Example:** A wheeled robot with prior pose belief $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_r^- = (1.5, 2.0, 0.0)^T$ and covariance $\mathbf{P}^- = \text{diag}(0.25, 0.25, 0.04)$ observes a known landmark at $\mathbf{m} = (5.0, 3.0)^T$ with measurement $\mathbf{z} = (3.6, 45°)^T$ and noise covariance $\mathbf{R} = \text{diag}(0.04, 0.01)$.

**Step 1 — Predict the expected observation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{r} &= \sqrt{(5-1.5)^2 + (3-2)^2} = \sqrt{12.25 + 1.0} = \sqrt{13.25} = 3.640 \\
\hat{\phi} &= \text{atan2}(3-2,\; 5-1.5) - 0 = \text{atan2}(1.0,\; 3.5) = 15.95°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the innovation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nu_r &= 3.6 - 3.640 = -0.040 \text{ m} \\
\nu_\phi &= 45° - 15.95° = 29.05° = 0.5071 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Jacobian $\mathbf{H}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{H} &= \begin{bmatrix} -(5-1.5)/3.640 & -(3-2)/3.640 & 0 \\ (3-2)/13.25 & -(5-1.5)/13.25 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \\
\mathbf{H} &= \begin{bmatrix} -0.9615 & -0.2747 & 0 \\ 0.0755 & -0.2642 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Innovation covariance and Kalman gain.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{S} &= \mathbf{H}\mathbf{P}^-\mathbf{H}^T + \mathbf{R} \\
\mathbf{K} &= \mathbf{P}^-\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{S}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Update the pose.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_r^+ &= \hat{\mathbf{x}}_r^- + \mathbf{K}\boldsymbol{\nu}
\end{aligned}
$$

This single-step update reduces the robot's pose uncertainty, demonstrating the power of landmark observations for navigation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (2–3 marks lost):** Forgetting that $\phi$ is measured *relative to the robot's body frame*, not the global frame. The rotation by $-\theta$ in the bearing equation is the most common mistake.
> 2. **Radian/Degree Mixing (1–2 marks lost):** $\text{atan2}$ in most programming libraries uses radians. Students who compute in degrees and pass to $\text{atan2}$ in radians will get the *wrong answer* despite correct logic. Always convert.
> 3. **Jacobian Sign Errors (3–4 marks lost):** The partial derivatives of the range and bearing functions have *specific sign patterns* that students often invert. Memorize the Jacobian structure, do not re-derive it under exam pressure.
> 4. **Skipping Assumptions (1 mark lost):** Always state the 2D plane assumption, stationary landmark assumption, and Gaussian noise assumption before any derivation. Examiners explicitly reward this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Landmark-based navigation uses *distinctive, stationary environmental features* to localize a robot.
- **Three Stages:** Detection → Data Association → Pose Estimation.
- **Observation Model:** $\mathbf{z}_i = h(\mathbf{x}_r, \mathbf{m}_i) + \mathbf{v}_i$ with range $r_i = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$ and bearing $\phi_i = \text{atan2}(\cdot) - \theta$.
- **Core Algorithm:** Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) measurement update is the standard solution technique.
- **Jacobian:** The observation Jacobian $\mathbf{H}_i$ is required for EKF linearization; memorize its structure.
- **Landmark Quality:** Well-separated, non-collinear, stationary, unique, perceivable — these five properties make a *good* landmark.
- **Data Association is Critical:** A single wrong match ruins the entire pose estimate. Always use gated nearest-neighbor or JCBB (Joint Compatibility Branch and Bound).
- **Real-World Examples:** GPS satellites (beacons), AR markers (artificial), road signs (natural), ceiling lights in warehouses (passive features).
- **Common Mistake:** Confusing *landmarks* (in the environment) with *beacons* (actively transmitting signals). The mathematics overlaps, but the engineering implementation differs.
- **Examination Mantra:** *"State assumptions → Derive model → Linearize via Jacobian → Apply EKF update → Compute new pose."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion in Potential Field and Navigation Function - Problem Statement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Motion in Potential Field and Navigation Function - Problem Statement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Motion in a Potential Field** is a reactive robot navigation paradigm in which the free workspace $\mathcal{W} \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ is endowed with a scalar artificial potential function $U(\mathbf{q}) : \mathcal{W}_{\text{free}} \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ such that the robot is treated as a point mass sliding along the negated gradient $-\nabla U(\mathbf{q})$. The robot's instantaneous velocity is governed by:

$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = -\nabla U(\mathbf{q})$$

A **Navigation Function** is a special class of potential function formally defined by Koditschek and Rimon (1990). A map $\varphi : \mathcal{W}_{\text{free}} \to [0,1]$ is a navigation function if it is:

1. **Smooth** ($C^2$ at least) on $\mathcal{W}_{\text{free}}$
2. **Polar** with respect to the goal $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$
3. **Admissible** (uniformly maximal on the workspace boundary $\partial \mathcal{W}$)
4. Has a **unique critical point** in each connected component of $\mathcal{W}_{\text{free}}$, which is the goal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Recall-Grade):** "A Navigation Function is a real-valued map whose negated gradient vector field has no local minima except the goal configuration, guaranteeing almost-global asymptotic convergence from any admissible start."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ball on a Trampoline"

Imagine stretching a **trampoline** (the workspace) horizontally and placing a heavy **bowling ball** at the goal location — this creates a smooth **valley** (attractive potential). Now raise several **tent poles** from below the trampoline at every obstacle location — these create **bumps/hills** (repulsive potentials). If you release a **marble** (the robot) anywhere on the trampoline, it rolls downhill, naturally avoiding the bumps and settling in the deepest valley (the goal).

| Real World Object | Potential Field Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Bowling ball at goal | Attractive well |
| Tent poles at obstacles | Repulsive hills |
| Marble (robot) | Point undergoing $-\nabla U$ motion |
| Trampoline surface | Free workspace manifold |

> [!NOTE]
> The critical pitfall in this analogy: real trampolines have many small dips — analogous to **local minima**. A *true* Navigation Function guarantees the marble never gets trapped in a false valley, which is precisely the **Problem Statement** we must solve.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Influence distance** $\rho_0$ — the range at which obstacles begin to repel (typical: **1–3 m** for indoor robots)
- **Attractive gain** $\zeta$ — strength of pull toward goal
- **Repulsive gain** $\eta$ — strength of push from obstacles
- **Workspace dimension** $n \in \{2, 3\}$ for mobile manipulators
- **Distance metric** $\rho(\mathbf{q}) = \Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}} \Vert$ in **meters (m)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Problem Statement

The **Problem Statement** for motion in a potential field is formally posed as the tuple $(\mathcal{W}, \mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}, \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}, \mathcal{O}, U)$. The KTU 2024 scheme requires the student to articulate the following five pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Workspace Declaration:**
$$\mathcal{W} = \mathcal{W}_{\text{free}} \cup \mathcal{O}, \quad \mathcal{W}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{W} \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{O}_i$$

where $\mathcal{O}_i$ are closed, bounded, connected obstacle sets.

**Pillar 2 — Configuration Space (C-space) Embedding:**
The robot is reduced to a point $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ via the standard configuration-space inflation of obstacles.

**Pillar 3 — Artificial Potential Decomposition:**
$$U(\mathbf{q}) = U_{\text{att}}(\mathbf{q}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} U_{\text{rep}}^{(i)}(\mathbf{q})$$

**Pillar 4 — Gradient-Based Dynamical Law:**
The robot evolves according to the first-order dynamical system $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = -\nabla U(\mathbf{q})$, yielding the **steepest descent trajectory** to the goal.

**Pillar 5 — Convergence Guarantee (Navigation Function Criterion):**
We require the potential $U$ (or $\varphi$) to be a **Navigation Function** so that every non-goal trajectory asymptotically converges to $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$ without getting trapped in spurious local minima.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Pillar

- **Why decompose $U$ into attractive + repulsive?** Because it allows independent tuning of goal-seeking ($\zeta$) and obstacle-avoidance ($\eta$) behavior, and the gradients are independently computable.
- **Why $-\nabla U$?** A physical particle sliding down a potential landscape experiences force $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U$; for an overdamped system (mobile robot with friction-dominated dynamics), velocity aligns with force.
- **Why Navigation Function?** Classical artificial potentials (Khatib 1986) suffer from **local minima traps** (e.g., equidistant from two walls and the goal). A Navigation Function eliminates this by construction.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Domain / Validity | Units |
|---|----------|------------------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Attractive Potential (Quadratic)** | $U_{\text{att}}(\mathbf{q}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\zeta \Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} \Vert^{2}$ | All $\mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{W}_{\text{free}}$ | dimensionless (or J) |
| 2 | **Attractive Potential (Conic)** | $U_{\text{att}}(\mathbf{q}) = \zeta \Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} \Vert$ | $\Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} \Vert \leq d^{\ast}$ | dimensionless (or J) |
| 3 | **Attractive Force** | $\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} = -\zeta (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}})$ | All $\mathbf{q}$ | N |
| 4 | **Repulsive Potential (Khatib)** | $U_{\text{rep}}^{(i)}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta \left(\tfrac{1}{\rho_i} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}, & \rho_i \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho_i > \rho_0 \end{cases}$ | $\rho_i = \Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs},i} \Vert$ | dimensionless (or J) |
| 5 | **Repulsive Force (Khatib)** | $\mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}}^{(i)} = \begin{cases} \eta \left(\tfrac{1}{\rho_i} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \tfrac{1}{\rho_i^{2}} \, \mathbf{n}_{i}, & \rho_i \le \rho_0 \\ \mathbf{0}, & \rho_i > \rho_0 \end{cases}$ | where $\mathbf{n}_{i} = \tfrac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs},i}}{\Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs},i} \Vert}$ | N |
| 6 | **Navigation Function (Koditschek–Rimon)** | $\varphi(\mathbf{q}) = \dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} \Vert^{2}}{\left(\Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} \Vert^{2k} + \beta(\mathbf{q})\right)^{1/k}}$ | $\beta(\mathbf{q}) = \prod_{i=0}^{m} \beta_i(\mathbf{q})$, $\beta_0 = \mathcal{W}$ boundary | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Sphere World Obstacle Function** | $\beta_i(\mathbf{q}) = \Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{i} \Vert^{2} - r_{i}^{2}$ | $\beta_i > 0$ inside $\mathcal{W}_{\text{free}}$ | $\text{m}^{2}$ |
| 8 | **Workspace Boundary Function** | $\beta_0(\mathbf{q}) = R^{2} - \Vert \mathbf{q} \Vert^{2}$ | $\beta_0 > 0$ inside sphere of radius $R$ | $\text{m}^{2}$ |
| 9 | **Total Robot Velocity** | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = -\nabla U(\mathbf{q}) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} - \sum_{i} \nabla U_{\text{rep}}^{(i)}$ | Overdamped limit | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 10 | **Lyapunov Convergence Criterion** | $\dot{\varphi} = \nabla\varphi^{\top} \dot{\mathbf{q}} = -\Vert \nabla\varphi \Vert^{2} \le 0$ | Always $\le 0$; $=0$ only at $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Hint (KTU Valuation):** Always write $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ for the Euclidean norm — never use the bare pipe `\|` inside a markdown table, as it breaks the KTU 2024 PDF renderer. Use `\Vert` for in-text norms and `\vert x \vert` for the absolute value of a scalar.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva robots):** Use artificial potential fields as a fallback layer when A* fails in dynamic environments.
- **Autonomous Vehicle Lane-Keeping:** Road boundaries act as repulsive "walls" and the lane centerline as an attractive well.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Anatomical no-go zones are encoded as repulsive potentials in the surgeon's haptic feedback loop.
- **Drone Swarms:** Each drone behaves as a moving repulsive obstacle for its neighbors, enabling reactive collision avoidance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Attractive Force from a Quadratic Potential

**Step 1.** Write the candidate attractive potential in the standard Khatib form:
$$U_{\text{att}}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{2}\,\zeta \, \rho_{\text{goal}}^{2}(\mathbf{q})$$
where $\rho_{\text{goal}}(\mathbf{q}) = \Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} \Vert$ is the Euclidean distance from the robot to the goal.

**Step 2.** Recognize that $U_{\text{att}}$ is a composition of two functions: a quadratic outer $f(u) = \tfrac{1}{2}\zeta u^2$ and the inner Euclidean norm $u = \rho_{\text{goal}}(\mathbf{q})$.

**Step 3.** Apply the chain rule for gradients. For any $U(\mathbf{q}) = f(\Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} \Vert)$:
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} U = f'(\rho_{\text{goal}}) \cdot \nabla_{\mathbf{q}} \rho_{\text{goal}}(\mathbf{q})$$

**Step 4.** Differentiate the outer function: $f'(u) = \zeta u = \zeta \, \rho_{\text{goal}}$.

**Step 5.** Differentiate the inner function. The gradient of a Euclidean norm is the unit vector toward the target:
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} \Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} \Vert = \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}}{\Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} \Vert}$$

**Step 6.** Substitute Steps 4 and 5 into the chain rule result:
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} U_{\text{att}} = \zeta \, \rho_{\text{goal}} \cdot \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}}{\rho_{\text{goal}}} = \zeta \left( \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} \right)$$

**Step 7.** The attractive force is the negative gradient (physical convention: force opposes potential rise):
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} = -\zeta \left( \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} \right)$$

> **Interpretation:** The attractive force is a linear "spring" pulling the robot toward the goal, with stiffness $\zeta$. In 2D, if $\mathbf{q} = (x, y)^{\top}$ and $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} = (x_g, y_g)^{\top}$, then:
> $$\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} = \begin{pmatrix} -\zeta (x - x_g) \\ -\zeta (y - y_g) \end{pmatrix}$$

### 3.2 Derivation II — Repulsive Force from Khatib's Potential

**Step 1.** Define the distance to the $i$-th obstacle: $\rho_i(\mathbf{q}) = \Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs},i} \Vert$.

**Step 2.** Write the FIESTA (Force Field Including an Intelligent System of Tunable Avoidance) form of the repulsive potential (valid only inside the influence zone $\rho_i \le \rho_0$):
$$U_{\text{rep}}^{(i)}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{2}\,\eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho_i} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 3.** Define the auxiliary scalar for compactness:
$$g_i(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{\rho_i(\mathbf{q})} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}$$
so that $U_{\text{rep}}^{(i)} = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta g_i^{2}$.

**Step 4.** Apply the chain rule:
$$\nabla U_{\text{rep}}^{(i)} = \eta \, g_i \cdot \nabla g_i(\mathbf{q})$$

**Step 5.** Compute $\nabla g_i$. Since $1/\rho_0$ is a constant, only $1/\rho_i$ contributes:
$$\nabla \left( \frac{1}{\rho_i} \right) = -\frac{1}{\rho_i^{2}} \cdot \nabla \rho_i = -\frac{1}{\rho_i^{2}} \cdot \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs},i}}{\rho_i} = -\frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs},i}}{\rho_i^{3}}$$

**Step 6.** Therefore:
$$\nabla g_i = -\frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs},i}}{\rho_i^{3}}$$

**Step 7.** Substitute back into Step 4:
$$\nabla U_{\text{rep}}^{(i)} = \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho_i} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \left( -\frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs},i}}{\rho_i^{3}} \right)$$

**Step 8.** The repulsive force (with sign flip) is:
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}}^{(i)} = -\nabla U_{\text{rep}}^{(i)} = \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho_i} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho_i^{2}} \cdot \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs},i}}{\rho_i} = \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho_i} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{\mathbf{n}_i}{\rho_i^{2}}$$

where $\mathbf{n}_i = (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs},i}) / \rho_i$ is the outward unit normal pointing from the obstacle toward the robot.

> **Interpretation:** The repulsive force is **zero at the boundary** $\rho_i = \rho_0$, **grows monotonically** as the robot approaches the obstacle, and **diverges to infinity** as $\rho_i \to 0$ (collision). It acts along the line connecting the robot to the obstacle center.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Koditschek–Rimon Navigation Function

**Step 1.** Define the goal-distance function in the workspace:
$$\gamma(\mathbf{q}) = \Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} \Vert^{2}$$

**Step 2.** Define the obstacle-distance product (one factor per obstacle, plus the workspace boundary):
$$\beta(\mathbf{q}) = \prod_{i=0}^{m} \beta_i(\mathbf{q}), \quad \beta_i(\mathbf{q}) = \Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_i \Vert^{2} - r_i^{2}$$

Here, $i=0$ corresponds to the workspace boundary (a fictitious spherical wall of radius $R$).

**Step 3.** Assemble the navigation function candidate:
$$\varphi_k(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{\gamma(\mathbf{q})}{\left( \gamma^{k}(\mathbf{q}) + \beta(\mathbf{q}) \right)^{1/k}}$$
where $k$ is a tuning exponent (typically $k \ge 1$ for spherical worlds).

**Step 4.** Verify smoothness. Both $\gamma$ and $\beta$ are $C^{\infty}$ on $\mathcal{W}_{\text{free}}$ (since $\beta_i > 0$ strictly inside the free space). The denominator is bounded below by $\beta(\mathbf{q})^{1/k} > 0$, ensuring no division by zero.

**Step 5.** Verify admissibility on the boundary. As $\mathbf{q} \to \partial \mathcal{W}$, at least one $\beta_i \to 0$, so $\beta \to 0$, so $\varphi_k \to \gamma / \gamma = 1$ (assuming $\gamma$ remains finite, which it does for bounded workspaces). Hence $\varphi_k = 1$ on $\partial \mathcal{W}$.

**Step 6.** Verify the unique critical point. Compute the gradient using the quotient rule:
$$\nabla \varphi_k = \frac{\nabla \gamma \cdot (\gamma^{k} + \beta)^{1/k} - \gamma \cdot \frac{1}{k}(\gamma^{k} + \beta)^{1/k - 1} \cdot (k \gamma^{k-1} \nabla \gamma + \nabla \beta)}{(\gamma^{k} + \beta)^{2/k}}$$

**Step 7.** Setting $\nabla \varphi_k = 0$ and clearing the positive denominator:
$$\nabla \gamma \cdot (\gamma^{k} + \beta)^{1/k} = \gamma \cdot (\gamma^{k} + \beta)^{1/k - 1} \cdot (\gamma^{k-1} \nabla \gamma + \tfrac{1}{k}\nabla \beta)$$

**Step 8.** After cancellation (Koditschek–Rimon Theorem, 1990, Theorem 4.1), the critical point equation reduces to:
$$\nabla \beta = 0 \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{q} = \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$$

This shows that the **only interior critical point** of $\varphi_k$ is the goal $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$, provided the obstacles are spherical and the tuning parameter $k$ is large enough.

### 3.4 Worked Example — 2D Workspace with Single Obstacle

> **Given:** $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} = (5, 5)^{\top}$, $\mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}} = (3, 3)^{\top}$ with $r = 0.5$, $\rho_0 = 1.5$, $\zeta = 1$, $\eta = 2$. Robot at $\mathbf{q} = (2, 2)^{\top}$.

**Step A — Compute $\rho_{\text{goal}}$:**
$$\rho_{\text{goal}} = \sqrt{(2-5)^{2} + (2-5)^{2}} = \sqrt{9 + 9} = \sqrt{18} = 3\sqrt{2} \approx 4.243 \text{ m}$$

**Step B — Compute $\rho_{\text{obs}}$:**
$$\rho_{\text{obs}} = \sqrt{(2-3)^{2} + (2-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{1 + 1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \text{ m}$$

**Step C — Compute Attractive Force:**
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} = -\zeta (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}) = -1 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Step D — Compute Repulsive Force** (note: $\rho_{\text{obs}} = 1.414 < \rho_0 = 1.5$, so the obstacle is within the influence zone):
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}} = \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho_{\text{obs}}} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{\mathbf{n}}{\rho_{\text{obs}}^{2}}$$

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{\text{obs}}} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} = \frac{1}{1.414} - \frac{1}{1.5} \approx 0.7071 - 0.6667 = 0.0404$$

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}}}{\rho_{\text{obs}}} = \frac{1}{1.414} \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -0.7071 \\ -0.7071 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\rho_{\text{obs}}^{2} = 2$$

$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}} = 2 \cdot 0.0404 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} -0.7071 \\ -0.7071 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -0.0286 \\ -0.0286 \end{pmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Step E — Total Force:**
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{total}} = \mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} + \mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 - 0.0286 \\ 3 - 0.0286 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2.9714 \\ 2.9714 \end{pmatrix} \text{ N}$$

> **Interpretation:** The robot is repelled slightly away from the obstacle (diagonally toward the lower-left from the obstacle's perspective), but the dominant attractive force still pulls it toward the goal at $(5,5)$. The path will curve gently around the obstacle.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Motion-in-Potential-Field

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RobotStartPose q_start] --> B[ConfigurationSpace Inflation]
    B --> C[Identify Obstacles and Goal]
    C --> D[Compute Distance rho to Goal]
    C --> E[Compute Distance rho_i to each Obstacle]
    D --> F[Evaluate Attractive Potential U_att]
    E --> G[Evaluate Repulsive Potential U_rep per Obstacle]
    F --> H[Sum U_total equals U_att plus U_rep]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute Gradient nabla U_total]
    I --> J[Apply Robot Dynamics dq dt equals minus nabla U]
    J --> K{Is q equal to q_goal within tolerance}
    K -- No --> L[Update q equals q plus dq dt]
    L --> D
    K -- Yes --> M[Stop: Goal Reached]
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
    style M fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style K fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Navigation Function Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workspace W] --> B[Decompose into Spheres]
    B --> C[Define gamma equals norm q minus q_goal squared]
    B --> D[Define beta equals product of beta_i over all obstacles]
    C --> E[Build Navigation Function phi_k]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Verify phi_k equals 1 on boundary]
    E --> G[Compute gradient of phi_k]
    G --> H{Critical point check}
    H --> I[Unique minimum at q_goal]
    H --> J[No local minima elsewhere]
    I --> K[Guaranteed Global Convergence]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Robot follows minus gradient of phi_k]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style K fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style L fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Problem Statement Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Problem Statement] --> P1[Given Workspace W subset R^n]
    P --> P2[Given Start q_start and Goal q_goal]
    P --> P3[Given Obstacles O_i for i equals 1 to m]
    P --> P4[Define Potential U equals U_att plus sum U_rep]
    P1 --> Q[Find collision free path tau t]
    P2 --> Q
    P3 --> Q
    P4 --> Q
    Q --> R1[tau 0 equals q_start]
    Q --> R2[tau T equals q_goal]
    Q --> R3[tau t in W_free for all t]
    Q --> R4[tau t follows dq dt equals minus nabla U]
    style P fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style Q fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Topography of $U(\mathbf{q})$

| Zone | $U$ value | Force direction | Robot behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deep interior (near goal) | Low | Small attractive, no repulsion | Cruises to goal |
| Mid-zone | Moderate | Dominant attractive | Direct motion |
| Influence boundary ($\rho_i = \rho_0$) | Smooth | Repulsive just activates | Slight deflection |
| Near obstacle | High | Large repulsive | Sharp turn |
| On boundary $\partial \mathcal{W}$ | Maximum (=1 for NF) | Tangent to boundary | Slides along wall |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State and explain the problem statement for robot motion planning using the artificial potential field method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The problem statement for motion in an artificial potential field is: *Given a robot with configuration $\mathbf{q}$ in a free workspace $\mathcal{W}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{W} \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{O}_i$, a start configuration $\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}$ and a goal configuration $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$, find a continuous collision-free path $\tau : [0, T] \to \mathcal{W}_{\text{free}}$ such that $\tau(0) = \mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}$, $\tau(T) = \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$, and the robot's velocity follows the gradient descent law $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = -\nabla U(\mathbf{q})$, where $U(\mathbf{q}) = U_{\text{att}}(\mathbf{q}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} U_{\text{rep}}^{(i)}(\mathbf{q})$.* The challenge is to design $U$ as a **Navigation Function** so that no spurious local minima exist. **(3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for math formulation, 1 for navigation function mention)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between an artificial potential field and a Navigation Function. Why is a Navigation Function preferred in critical applications?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **Artificial Potential Field** (Khatib, 1986) is a general scalar function $U(\mathbf{q}) = U_{\text{att}} + U_{\text{rep}}$ that may possess local minima away from the goal, causing the robot to get stuck.
