# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the free configuration space of a robot. How is it related to the configuration space obstacle?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The free configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is the set of all configurations of a robot that do not result in collision with any obstacle in the workspace or with itself. Mathematically, if $\mathcal{C}$ denotes the complete configuration space and $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ the set of colliding configurations, then $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. Equivalently, $\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ with $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cap \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \emptyset$. The free configuration space is therefore the safe region of the configuration manifold on which motion planners operate.

**Marking Scheme (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) Correct definition of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.
- (1 mark) Expression $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$.
- (1 mark) Statement of disjointness and physical interpretation (collision-free set).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**What is the Minkowski sum operation? Why is it used in the construction of C-space obstacles?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Minkowski sum of two sets $\mathcal{A}$ and $\mathcal{B}$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is defined as $\mathcal{A} \oplus \mathcal{B} = \{\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b} \mid \mathbf{a} \in \mathcal{A},\, \mathbf{b} \in \mathcal{B}\}$. In the context of configuration space construction, for a translating planar robot, the C-space obstacle is computed as $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{W}_{\text{obs}} \oplus (-\mathcal{B})$, where $-\mathcal{B}$ is the robot body reflected through its reference point. This operation effectively "grows" each workspace obstacle by the shape of the robot, so that the robot can subsequently be treated as a point while planning.

**Marking Scheme (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) Formal definition of Minkowski sum.
- (1 mark) Application $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{W}_{\text{obs}} \oplus (-\mathcal{B})$ for translating robots.
- (1 mark) Explanation of "growing" the obstacle to reduce the robot to a point.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — Choice 1

**(a)** Define the configuration space and free configuration space of a rigid body robot. Derive the expression for the configuration space obstacle for a translating polygonal robot using the Minkowski sum, and explain each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-D square obstacle has vertices at $(2, 2)$, $(5, 2)$, $(5, 5)$, $(2, 5)$. The robot is a square of side $1.0$ centred at its reference point. Compute the configuration space obstacle $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ for the translating case and determine the dimension of the free configuration space. State the connectivity condition for the existence of a collision-free path. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The *configuration space* $\mathcal{C}$ of a robot is the set of all possible configurations (poses) the robot can attain. Each configuration $\mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{C}$ completely specifies the position and orientation of every point on the robot. The *free configuration space* is the subset $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \subseteq \mathcal{C}$ of configurations that cause no collision with any workspace obstacle and no self-collision:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}
$$

For a **translating polygonal robot** $\mathcal{B}$ in 2-D, a configuration is $\mathbf{q} = (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$. The forward kinematic map places the robot at $R(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{q} + \mathcal{B}$. Collision occurs iff $(\mathbf{q} + \mathcal{B}) \cap \mathcal{W}_{\text{obs}} \neq \emptyset$. Equivalently, $\exists\, \mathbf{b} \in \mathcal{B}$ with $\mathbf{q} + \mathbf{b} \in \mathcal{W}_{\text{obs}}$, i.e., $\mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{W}_{\text{obs}} - \mathbf{b}$. Taking the union over all $\mathbf{b} \in \mathcal{B}$:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \bigcup_{\mathbf{b} \in \mathcal{B}} \left( \mathcal{W}_{\text{obs}} - \mathbf{b} \right) = \mathcal{W}_{\text{obs}} \oplus (-\mathcal{B})
$$

The term $\mathcal{W}_{\text{obs}}$ is the workspace obstacle set, $-\mathcal{B}$ is the robot body reflected through its reference point, and $\oplus$ is the Minkowski sum. The free configuration space is its complement in $\mathcal{W}$. **(7 marks: definition 2 m, derivation 3 m, explanation 2 m.)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

The obstacle $\mathcal{W}_{\text{obs}}$ is a $3 \times 3$ square (vertices $(2,2), (5,2), (5,5), (2,5)$); half-side is $b = 1.5$, centroid at $(3.5, 3.5)$. The robot $\mathcal{B}$ is a square of side $1.0$ centred at its reference; half-side $a = 0.5$. The reflected robot $-\mathcal{B} = \mathcal{B}$ (symmetric). The Minkowski sum of two axis-aligned squares of half-sides $a$ and $b$ is an axis-aligned square of half-side $a + b$:

$$
a + b = 0.5 + 1.5 = 2.0
$$

Therefore, centred at $(3.5, 3.5)$:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = [1.5,\, 5.5] \times [1.5,\, 5.5]
$$

**Dimension of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$:** Since the robot is purely translating (no rotation), DOF = 2, and $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$. So $\dim \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = 2$.

**Connectivity condition:** A collision-free path from $\mathbf{q}_s$ to $\mathbf{q}_g$ exists if and only if both configurations lie in the same connected component of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. **(7 marks: half-side computation 2 m, Minkowski sum 2 m, dimension 1 m, connectivity 2 m.)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — Choice 2

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the difference between a workspace and a configuration space for a rigid body robot. Discuss why a free configuration space is required in robot motion planning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A free-flying rectangular robot of dimensions $2 \, \text{m} \times 1 \, \text{m}$ operates in a 2-D workspace containing a single circular obstacle of radius $1 \, \text{m}$ centred at the origin. Compute the dimension of the free configuration space. If the robot is constrained to translate only (no rotation), determine the C-space obstacle in the $(x, y)$-plane using the Minkowski sum. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The *workspace* $\mathcal{W} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ (or $\mathbb{R}^3$) is the physical environment in which the robot operates. The *configuration space* $\mathcal{C}$ is an abstract $n$-dimensional manifold (where $n$ is the DOF) in which each point $\mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{C}$ represents one complete pose of the robot. For a planar rigid body, $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times \mathbb{S}^1$, three-dimensional: $\mathcal{C} = SE(3) = \mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$.

**Sketch (described):** A 2-D workspace shows the rectangular robot, the obstacle, and a desired goal location. The C-space is shown as a 3-D "tower" with axes $(x, y, \theta)$. At each $\theta$-slice, a 2-D $(x, y)$ slice of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}(\theta)$ is depicted, and the union over $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ forms the 3-D C-obstacle.

The free configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is required because:

1. **Search space abstraction** — A point in $\mathcal{C}$ encodes the entire pose; the planner can ignore the robot's shape and treat it as a particle.
2. **Standard graph-search** — Classical algorithms (A*, Dijkstra, RRT) operate on a single configuration at a time, feasible only in C-space.
3. **Theoretical foundation** — Path existence reduces to connectivity of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, a well-defined topological question.

**(7 marks: definitions 2 m, sketch description 2 m, three reasons for $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ 3 m.)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Dimension:** A free-flying 2-D rigid body has 3 DOF: $(x, y, \theta)$. The free configuration space is a 3-D manifold $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \subset \mathbb{R}^2 \times \mathbb{S}^1$. So $\dim \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = 3$.

**Translation-only case (no rotation):** The robot is reduced to a rectangle of half-dimensions $(1, 0.5)$ centred at its reference. The reflected robot is the same rectangle. The workspace obstacle is a disc of radius $R = 1$ centred at $(0, 0)$. The Minkowski sum of a disc of radius $R$ and a rectangle of half-widths $(a, b) = (1, 0.5)$ is the disc **grown by an $L_\infty$-ball of size $(a, b)$** — geometrically, a "rounded square" — also called the **Minkowski sum of a disc and a rectangle**. The boundary is composed of:

- Two horizontal line segments of length $2b$ at vertical distance $R + a$ from the centre (top and bottom),
- Two vertical line segments of length $2a$ at horizontal distance $R + b$ from the centre (left and right),
- Four quarter-circle arcs of radius $R$ at the corners.

The simplified *bounding disc* approximation gives an upper bound:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} \subset \text{disc}\bigl(\text{origin},\, R + \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}\bigr) = \text{disc}\bigl(\text{origin},\, 1 + \sqrt{1 + 0.25}\bigr) \approx \text{disc}\bigl(\text{origin},\, 2.118\bigr)
$$

The exact $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ is the *rounded rectangle* with $R + a = 2$ vertical extent and $R + b = 1.5$ horizontal extent, plus four arcs of radius $1$.

**(7 marks: DOF 2 m, Minkowski sum 3 m, exact shape 2 m.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $\mathcal{C}$ with $\mathcal{W}$:** Students frequently draw a 2-D picture and label it "C-space" — but a 2-D picture is only valid for a translating robot. For a 2-D rigid body, $\mathcal{C}$ is 3-D. Loss: up to **3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in the Minkowski sum:** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{W}_{\text{obs}} \oplus (-\mathcal{B})$ is **not** the same as $\mathcal{W}_{\text{obs}} \oplus \mathcal{B}$. The reflection is non-trivial for non-symmetric robots. Loss: up to **2 marks**.
> 3. **Not stating the disjointness of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ and $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$:** Examiners specifically test the partition property $\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ with disjoint sets. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Skipping the connectivity statement:** A path-planning question that omits "both endpoints must lie in the same connected component of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$" loses **2 marks** in Part (b).
> 5. **Writing DOF without the Grübler formula or explicit count:** Even if the answer is correct, showing the calculation method earns full credit. Loss: **1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is the collision-free subset** of the configuration space: $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$.
- [ ] **$\mathcal{C}$ decomposes** into a disjoint union: $\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$.
- [ ] **Forward kinematic map** $\mathbf{fk}(\mathbf{q})$ places the robot at $\mathbf{q}$; the C-obstacle is the *preimage* of $\mathcal{W}_{\text{obs}}$ under this map.
- [ ] **Minkowski sum** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{W}_{\text{obs}} \oplus (-\mathcal{B})$ is the canonical construction for translating planar robots.
- [ ] **Dimension of $\mathcal{C}$** equals the robot's **DOF** (Grübler–Kutzbach criterion).
- [ ] **Translating point robot:** $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$, $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ (open subset).
- [ ] **Translating + rotating planar rigid body:** $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times \mathbb{S}^1$ (cylindrical topology).
- [ ] **$n$-revolute-joint arm:** $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{T}^n$ ($n$-torus).
- [ ] **Path existence** requires both $\mathbf{q}_s$ and $\mathbf{q}_g$ in the **same connected component** of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.
- [ ] **The Minkowski sum of two convex polygons** of $m$ and $n$ vertices has $m + n$ vertices and $O(m + n)$ complexity.
- [ ] **The free configuration space is open** in $\mathcal{C}$ (a consequence of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ being closed).
- [ ] **Planners (PRM, RRT, A\*) operate in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$** and the resulting path is lifted back to $\mathcal{W}$ via $\mathbf{fk}^{-1}$.
- [ ] **The "narrow passage problem"** arises when connected components of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ are linked only by thin bottlenecks.
- [ ] **For a free-flying 2-D rigid body**, $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ is the *swept volume* over $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ of 2-D slices $\mathcal{W}_{\text{obs}} \oplus (-R_\theta \mathcal{B})$.
- [ ] **Workspace free space $\mathcal{W}_{\text{free}}$** and $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ are *homeomorphic* for translating rigid robots (both are 2-D open sets), but *not* for rotating or articulated robots.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gradient Descent Method of Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gradient Descent Method of Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Gradient Descent Method** is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to find the local minimum of a differentiable multivariable function. In the context of KTU's PEBRT751 - Robot Navigation syllabus (2024 Scheme), it is the foundational mathematical workhorse used to minimize cost functions representing robot path length, control energy, localization error, or sensor residuals.

Formally, given an objective function $f(\mathbf{x}): \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$ that is differentiable at point $\mathbf{x}$, the gradient descent update rule is:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_{k} - \eta \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k})$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x}_{k}$ is the current position (or parameter vector) at iteration $k$
- $\eta$ (eta) is the **learning rate** (a small positive scalar, typically $0 < \eta \leq 1$)
- $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k})$ is the **gradient vector** of the cost function evaluated at $\mathbf{x}_{k}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Gradient Descent is grouped under *Optimization Methods for Path Planning*. It directly supports Course Outcome **CO3**: "Apply optimization techniques to solve robot navigation and trajectory planning problems."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine a blindfolded hiker standing on a steep mountain ridge who wants to reach the lowest valley (sea level). The hiker cannot see the terrain, but can feel the slope of the ground beneath their feet. At every step:

1. They feel the **direction of steepest descent** (the negative gradient).
2. They take a small step in that direction.
3. They repeat until the ground feels flat (gradient $\approx 0$).

**Robot Navigation Mapping:**
- The hiker = the algorithm (or robot controller)
- The mountain = the cost/error landscape
- The valley = the optimal path / minimum error configuration
- The step size = learning rate $\eta$

If $\eta$ is too large, the hiker might overshoot the valley and tumble up the other side. If $\eta$ is too small, the descent takes forever (or stalls on a plateau). If the landscape has multiple valleys, the hiker may get trapped in a local minimum rather than reaching the global minimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Callout:** Gradient descent iteratively updates parameters in the direction opposite to the gradient of the cost function, since the negative gradient points toward the steepest local decrease of the function.

### 1.3 Role in Robot Navigation

In a navigation stack, gradient descent is deployed for:
- **Path smoothing and length minimization** (post-processing A* or RRT paths).
- **SLAM pose graph optimization** (minimizing re-projection error).
- **Trajectory optimization** (minimizing snap/jerk integrals for smooth motion).
- **Inverse kinematics** (solving joint angles to reach a target end-effector pose).
- **Neural-network-based perception** (training obstacle classifiers used in autonomous navigation).
- **Localization** (e.g., scan-matching between LiDAR sweeps using gradient-based registration like ICP variants).

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Learning rate $\eta$ | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{0}$ (dimensionless) | Step-size scalar |
| Convergence tolerance $\epsilon$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Stopping threshold on $\vert \nabla f \vert$ |
| Iteration cap $k_{\max}$ | $100$ to $10000$ | Computational safety bound |
| Gradient $\nabla f$ | Function-dependent (e.g., N/rad) | Sensitivity of cost w.r.t. parameters |
| Cost $f(\mathbf{x})$ | Energy, error, or distance in SI units | Objective being minimized |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Logic (Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown)

The gradient descent method follows a strict iterative procedure:

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
Pick an initial guess $\mathbf{x}_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$. Choose a learning rate $\eta > 0$ and a small convergence tolerance $\epsilon > 0$. Set iteration counter $k = 0$.

**Step 2 — Gradient Evaluation:**
Compute the gradient vector $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) = \left[ \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_2}, \ldots, \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \right]^{\mathsf{T}}$ at the current point.

**Step 3 — Direction Check:**
The direction of steepest descent is $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$. The negative sign ensures movement toward decreasing function values.

**Step 4 — Parameter Update:**
Apply the update rule:
$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_{k} - \eta \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k})$$

**Step 5 — Convergence Test:**
If $\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) \Vert \leq \epsilon$ (gradient norm is approximately zero, indicating a stationary point) **OR** if the iteration count exceeds $k_{\max}$, terminate. Otherwise, increment $k$ and return to Step 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work?** By the first-order Taylor expansion, $f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k) = f(\mathbf{x}_k) - \eta \Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert^{2}$. Since $\eta > 0$ and the squared norm is non-negative, $f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) \leq f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ — the cost is **monotonically non-increasing** at each step.

### 2.2 Variants of Gradient Descent (High-Yield for KTU)

| Variant | Update Rule | Robot-Nav Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Batch GD** | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \eta \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ using all samples | Offline trajectory optimization |
| **Stochastic GD (SGD)** | Uses one sample at a time to estimate gradient | Online learning of perception models |
| **Mini-Batch GD** | Uses a subset of $m$ samples per step | Training CNNs for obstacle detection |
| **Momentum GD** | Adds $\beta (\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_{k-1})$ term | Accelerates convergence on long paths |
| **Gradient Descent with Line Search** | Adaptive $\eta$ via Armijo/Wolfe rules | Safe step sizing in unknown terrain cost maps |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Condition | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_{k} - \eta \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k})$ | Standard gradient descent update |
| 2 | $\nabla f = \left[\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \ldots, \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}\right]^{\mathsf{T}}$ | Gradient vector definition |
| 3 | $\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) \Vert \leq \epsilon$ | Convergence (stationary point) condition |
| 4 | $\vert f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) - f(\mathbf{x}_{k}) \vert \leq \delta$ | Alternative cost-change stopping rule |
| 5 | $f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) - \eta \Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert^{2}$ | Linear descent guarantee (Taylor) |
| 6 | $\eta \in (0, 2 / L)$ | Stable step-size range, $L$ = Lipschitz constant of $\nabla f$ |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{\left( \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}^{*} \right)^{\mathsf{T}} \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)}{\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert^{2}}$ | Exact line-search optimal learning rate (quadratic case) |
| 8 | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_{k} - \eta \mathbf{H}^{-1} \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k})$ | Newton's method (mentioned for contrast — uses Hessian $\mathbf{H}$) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Gradient descent trains the perception neural networks that detect lanes, pedestrians, and traffic signs.
- **Robotic Manipulators (KUKA, ABB):** Inverse kinematics solvers minimize end-effector pose error using gradient descent over joint angles.
- **Drone Swarms:** Trajectory optimization for collision-free flight uses gradient descent to minimize energy and path length.
- **SLAM (ORB-SLAM, Cartographer):** Bundle adjustment minimizes re-projection error with Gauss-Newton, a second-order cousin of gradient descent.
- **Industrial Robotic Welding:** Path optimization smooths jagged point sequences to minimize arc travel time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Gradient descent only guarantees convergence to a **local minimum**. For convex cost surfaces (e.g., quadratic SLAM residuals), the local minimum is the global minimum. For non-convex landscapes (deep neural networks, multi-modal path costs), multiple restarts or stochastic perturbations are required.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the Update Rule Points Downhill

**Statement to prove:** Moving in the direction $-\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ produces the largest local decrease of $f$ among all unit directions.

**Step 1 — Directional Derivative Definition:**
For a unit direction vector $\mathbf{u}$ (with $\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert = 1$), the directional derivative of $f$ at $\mathbf{x}_k$ in direction $\mathbf{u}$ is defined as:

$$D_{\mathbf{u}} f(\mathbf{x}_k) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(\mathbf{x}_k + h\mathbf{u}) - f(\mathbf{x}_k)}{h}$$

**Step 2 — Apply First-Order Taylor Expansion:**
For a small step $h$ in direction $\mathbf{u}$:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + h\mathbf{u}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) + h \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{u}$$

Subtracting $f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ and dividing by $h$:

$$D_{\mathbf{u}} f(\mathbf{x}_k) = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{u}$$

**Step 3 — Maximize the Descent:**
We want the *most negative* directional derivative. Using the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality:

$$D_{\mathbf{u}} f(\mathbf{x}_k) = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{u} \geq -\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert = -\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert$$

The minimum value $-\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert$ is achieved when $\mathbf{u} = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) / \Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert$.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
Hence, the direction of steepest descent is exactly $-\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$. Taking a step of size $\eta$ along this direction yields the update rule $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \eta \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$, as stated. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem:** A mobile robot's cost function in a 2D map is modeled as $f(x, y) = (x - 3)^{2} + (y - 4)^{2}$. Starting from the initial guess $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^{\mathsf{T}}$ with a learning rate $\eta = 0.1$, perform three iterations of gradient descent.

**Step 1 — Compute the Gradient of $f$:**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2(x - 3), \qquad \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 2(y - 4)$$

Hence, the gradient vector is:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} 2(x - 3) \\ 2(y - 4) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate Gradient at $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^{\mathsf{T}}$:**

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0) = \begin{bmatrix} 2(0 - 3) \\ 2(0 - 4) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -6 \\ -8 \end{bmatrix}$$

Note: For minimization, the algorithm moves **opposite** to this gradient, so the robot will move in the positive $(x, y)$ direction — toward the optimum.

**Step 3 — Iteration 1 Update:**

$$\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_0 - \eta \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} - 0.1 \begin{bmatrix} -6 \\ -8 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Gradient at $\mathbf{x}_1 = (0.6, 0.8)^{\mathsf{T}}$:**

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_1) = \begin{bmatrix} 2(0.6 - 3) \\ 2(0.8 - 4) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2(-2.4) \\ 2(-3.2) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -4.8 \\ -6.4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Iteration 2 Update:**

$$\mathbf{x}_2 = \mathbf{x}_1 - 0.1 \begin{bmatrix} -4.8 \\ -6.4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.8 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.48 \\ 0.64 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.08 \\ 1.44 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate Gradient at $\mathbf{x}_2 = (1.08, 1.44)^{\mathsf{T}}$:**

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_2) = \begin{bmatrix} 2(1.08 - 3) \\ 2(1.44 - 4) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3.84 \\ -5.12 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Iteration 3 Update:**

$$\mathbf{x}_3 = \mathbf{x}_2 - 0.1 \begin{bmatrix} -3.84 \\ -5.12 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.08 \\ 1.44 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.384 \\ 0.512 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.464 \\ 1.952 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 8 — Convergence Verification:**
The true optimum is at $(3, 4)$. After 3 iterations, the robot is at $(1.464, 1.952)$, with a remaining error of $\sqrt{(1.536)^{2} + (2.048)^{2}} \approx 2.56$. The gradient norm is shrinking monotonically ($\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_3) \Vert = \sqrt{3.072^{2} + 4.096^{2}} \approx 5.12$), confirming convergence.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix (Iteration-by-Iteration Summary)

| Iteration $k$ | $\mathbf{x}_k = (x_k, y_k)$ | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Cost $f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | $\Vert \nabla f \Vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $(0.000, \, 0.000)$ | $(-6.000, -8.000)$ | $25.000$ | $10.000$ |
| 1 | $(0.600, \, 0.800)$ | $(-4.800, -6.400)$ | $14.440$ | $8.000$ |
| 2 | $(1.080, \, 1.440)$ | $(-3.840, -5.120)$ | $8.342$ | $6.400$ |
| 3 | $(1.464, \, 1.952)$ | $(-3.072, -4.096)$ | $4.819$ | $5.120$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $\infty$ | $(3.000, \, 4.000)$ | $(0.000, \, 0.000)$ | $0.000$ | $0.000$ |

The cost decays **geometrically** with ratio $\approx 0.8$ per iteration — this is the hallmark of gradient descent on a quadratic function.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Gradient Descent in a Robot Navigation Loop)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the gradient descent optimizer sits inside a typical mobile-robot navigation pipeline. It maps the data flow between sensors, the cost-function builder, the optimization core, and the motion controller.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Robot Sensors] --> A2[State Estimator]
    A2 --> A3[Cost Function Builder]
    A3 --> A4[Initial Guess x0]
    A4 --> B1[Gradient Descent Core]
    B1 --> B2[Compute Gradient nabla f]
    B2 --> B3{Convergence Test}
    B3 -- Not Converged --> B4[Update Step x = x - eta times nabla f]
    B4 --> B1
    B3 -- Converged --> C1[Optimal Trajectory Output]
    C1 --> C2[Motion Controller]
    C2 --> C3[Robot Actuators]
    C3 --> D1[Robot Motion in Environment]
    D1 --> A1
    style A1 fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1
    style B1 fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309
    style C1 fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D
    style D1 fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D
```

### 4.2 Detailed Iterative Loop Diagram (Gradient Descent Internal Steps)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Initialize x0 eta epsilon] --> S2[Set k = 0]
    S2 --> S3[Evaluate nabla f at xk]
    S3 --> S4[Check Norm less than epsilon]
    S4 -- No --> S5[Update xk1 = xk - eta nabla f]
    S5 --> S6[Increment k to k+1]
    S6 --> S3
    S4 -- Yes --> S7[Output x star as Optimum]
    style S1 fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8
    style S5 fill:#FED7AA,stroke:#C2410C
    style S7 fill:#BBF7D0,stroke:#166534
```

### 4.3 Cost-Landscape Visualization (Schematic Descent Trajectory)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Start x0 on Slope] --> P2[Step 1 Move Downhill] --> P3[Step 2 Continue Descent] --> P4[Step 3 Near Valley] --> P5[Step 4 Converged at Minimum]
    style P1 fill:#FCA5A5,stroke:#7F1D1D
    style P5 fill:#86EFAC,stroke:#14532D
```

### 4.4 Interpretation Notes for the Diagrams

- **Block A (Sensing & State):** Raw sensor data (LiDAR, IMU, GPS, encoders) flows into the state estimator (e.g., EKF) to obtain an initial guess $\mathbf{x}_0$.
- **Block B (Optimization Core):** This is the iterative gradient descent module. The loop between `B1 → B2 → B3 → B4` represents one iteration.
- **Block C (Control & Actuation):** Once the optimal trajectory is found, the motion controller generates wheel/motor commands.
- **Block D (Closed-Loop Feedback):** The robot's actual motion in the environment updates the sensors, completing the perception-action cycle.
- **Convergence Test (`B3`):** A decision node that checks $\Vert \nabla f \Vert \leq \epsilon$ or $k > k_{\max}$. If either condition triggers, the algorithm exits with the current best estimate.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define the gradient descent optimization method. Write its update rule and state the role of the learning rate.**

**Model Answer:**
Gradient descent is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to find the minimum of a differentiable function. The update rule is:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_{k} - \eta \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k})$$

The **learning rate** $\eta$ is a positive scalar that controls the step size. If $\eta$ is too small, convergence is slow; if too large, the algorithm may overshoot the minimum and diverge. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2. State the convergence criteria commonly used to terminate a gradient descent algorithm.**

**Model Answer:**
Two common convergence criteria are used:

1. **Gradient-norm criterion:** $\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) \Vert \leq \epsilon$, where $\epsilon$ is a small tolerance (e.g., $10^{-6}$).
2. **Cost-change criterion:** $\vert f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) - f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \leq \delta$, where $\delta$ is a small threshold.

Additionally, a maximum iteration limit $k_{\max}$ is enforced to guarantee termination. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]**

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the gradient descent update rule starting from the first-order Taylor expansion of the objective function. Explain why the negative-gradient direction is chosen. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the cost function $f(x, y) = x^{2} + 4y^{2} - 4x - 8y$. Starting from $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^{\mathsf{T}}$ with learning rate $\eta = 0.1$, perform **three iterations** of gradient descent and tabulate the values of $\mathbf{x}_k$, $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$, and $f(\mathbf{x}_k)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**
Let $\mathbf{u}$ be a unit direction vector ($\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert = 1$). The first-order Taylor expansion around $\mathbf{x}_k$ gives:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + h\mathbf{u}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) + h \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{u}$$

The change in cost is:

$$\Delta f \approx h \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{u}$$

To minimize $\Delta f$ (i.e., make it as negative as possible), by the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, the maximum decrease occurs when $\mathbf{u}$ is anti-parallel to $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$, i.e., $\mathbf{u} = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) / \Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert$. Taking a step of size $\eta = h$ along this direction gives:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \eta \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

The negative-gradient direction is chosen because it is the *unique* unit direction that produces the maximum local decrease in $f$. **(7 marks — full marks for derivation + justification)**

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**
First, compute the gradient:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2x - 4, \qquad \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 8y - 8$$

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} 2x - 4 \\ 8y - 8 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Iteration 0 → 1:**
$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0) = \nabla f(0, 0) = (-4, -8)$. Cost $f(0, 0) = 0$.

$$\mathbf{x}_1 = (0, 0) - 0.1 (-4, -8) = (0.4, 0.8)$$

$$f(\mathbf{x}_1) = (0.4)^2 + 4(0.8)^2 - 4(0.4) - 8(0.8) = 0.16 + 2.56 - 1.6 - 6.4 = -5.28$$

**Iteration 1 → 2:**
$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_1) = \nabla f(0.4, 0.8) = (2(0.4) - 4, 8(0.8) - 8) = (-3.2, -1.6)$.

$$\mathbf{x}_2 = (0.4, 0.8) - 0.1(-3.2, -1.6) = (0.72, 0.96)$$

$$f(\mathbf{x}_2) = (0.72)^2 + 4(0.96)^2 - 4(0.72) - 8(0.96)$$
$$= 0.5184 + 3.6864 - 2.88 - 7.68 = -6.3552$$

**Iteration 2 → 3:**
$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_2) = (2(0.72) - 4, 8(0.96) - 8) = (-2.56, -0.32)$.

$$\mathbf{x}_3 = (0.72, 0.96) - 0.1(-2.56, -0.32) = (0.976, 0.992)$$

$$f(\mathbf{x}_3) = (0.976)^2 + 4(0.992)^2 - 4(0.976) - 8(0.992)$$
$$= 0.9526 + 3.9363 - 3.904 - 7.936 = -6.9511$$

**Tabulated Result:**

| $k$ | $\mathbf{x}_k = (x_k, y_k)$ | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | $f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $(0.000, \, 0.000)$ | $(-4.000, -8.000)$ | $0.000$ |
| 1 | $(0.400, \, 0.800)$ | $(-3.200, -1.600)$ | $-5.280$ |
| 2 | $(0.720, \, 0.960)$ | $(-2.560, -0.320)$ | $-6.355$ |
| 3 | $(0.976, \, 0.992)$ | $(-2.048, -0.064)$ | $-6.951$ |

True optimum: $\nabla f = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2, y = 1$, with $f(2, 1) = 4 + 4 - 8 - 8 = -8$. The iterates are converging toward $(2, 1)$. **(7 marks — full marks for table + verification)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** What are the limitations of the standard gradient descent method when applied to non-convex cost functions? Suggest **two remedies** and justify each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot's localization cost is $f(\theta) = \theta^{2} - 6\theta + 13$, where $\theta$ is the heading-angle estimate in radians. Using gradient descent with $\eta = 0.2$ and initial guess $\theta_0 = 0$, find the value of $\theta$ after **four iterations**. Comment on the convergence behavior. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**
**Limitations:**
1. **Local minima trapping:** On non-convex landscapes, gradient descent converges to the nearest local minimum, which may not be the global minimum.
2. **Sensitivity to learning rate:** A constant $\eta$ may cause divergence (if too large) or stall (if too small) on ill-conditioned cost surfaces.
3. **Slow convergence on plateaus and ravines:** The gradient becomes very small in flat regions, halting progress; in narrow ravines, the algorithm oscillates across the walls.
4. **No momentum:** Pure gradient descent ignores curvature information, leading to zig-zag paths in anisotropic cost functions.

**Remedies:**
1. **Multiple random restarts:** Run gradient descent from many initial points $\mathbf{x}_0^{(i)}$ and pick the lowest-cost result — increases the chance of finding the global basin.
2. **Adaptive learning rates (e.g., Adam, RMSProp):** Scale $\eta$ per parameter based on running gradient statistics, accelerating convergence on ravines and stabilizing plateaus.
3. **Momentum methods:** Add a fraction $\beta$ of the previous update: $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \eta \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \beta (\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_{k-1})$. This damps oscillations and accelerates through flat regions.
4. **Second-order methods (Newton's method):** Use the Hessian inverse $\mathbf{H}^{-1}$ to rescale the gradient by local curvature, providing optimal step directions on quadratic-like surfaces. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**
Gradient: $f'(\theta) = 2\theta - 6$. Update rule: $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k - \eta f'(\theta_k) = \theta_k - 0.2(2\theta_k - 6) = \theta_k - 0.4\theta_k + 1.2 = 0.6\theta_k + 1.2$.

**Iteration 0 → 1:** $\theta_1 = 0.6(0) + 1.2 = 1.200$
**Iteration 1 → 2:** $\theta_2 = 0.6(1.2) + 1.2 = 0.72 + 1.2 = 1.920$
**Iteration 2 → 3:** $\theta_3 = 0.6(1.92) + 1.2 = 1.152 + 1.2 = 2.352$
**Iteration 3 → 4:** $\theta_4 = 0.6(2.352) + 1.2 = 1.4112 + 1.2 = 2.6112$

**Tabulated:**

| $k$ | $\theta_k$ | $f'(\theta_k) = 2\theta_k - 6$ | $f(\theta_k)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $0.000$ | $-6.000$ | $13.000$ |
| 1 | $1.200$ | $-3.600$ | $7.240$ |
| 2 | $1.920$ | $-2.160$ | $5.286$ |
| 3 | $2.352$ | $-1.296$ | $4.874$ |
| 4 | $2.611$ | $-0.778$ | $4.831$ |

**Convergence behavior:** True optimum is at $\theta^{*} = 3$ (where $f'(\theta) = 0 \Rightarrow \theta = 3$, and $f(3) = 9 - 18 + 13 = 4$). The iterates approach $3$ with a contraction factor of $0.6$ per step. The cost $f(\theta_k)$ is decreasing monotonically from $13$ toward $4$, confirming stable convergence. Since the cost is a single-variable quadratic, gradient descent is guaranteed to find the global optimum for any $0 < \eta < 1 / L = 0.5$ — wait, here $L = 2$, so the stable range is $0 < \eta < 1$, and $\eta = 0.2$ lies safely within it. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Update Rule:** Writing $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \eta \nabla f$ instead of $-$ (this climbs the gradient, not descends — full 7 marks lost on Part B derivations).
> 2. **Forgetting to Compute the Gradient Symbolically First:** Jumping straight to numerical evaluation without showing $\partial f / \partial x$ and $\partial f / \partial y$ explicitly loses 2–3 marks in the "setup" portion.
> 3. **Mis-stating Convergence:** Writing "algorithm converges to the minimum" without the qualifier "local" or "global" — for non-convex problems this is technically incorrect and loses marks.
> 4. **Skipping Iteration Tables:** Examiners award marks for systematic tabulation. A messy "calculation trail" without a table typically loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **No Units in Real-World Context:** If the cost has units (e.g., meters, joules), the gradient must be quoted with consistent units — students often omit this.
> 6. **Forgetting the Stopping Criterion:** In derivations, students often write "repeat until converged" without specifying the actual mathematical stopping rule.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Core equation:** $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_{k} - \eta \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k})$
- **Negative gradient = steepest local descent** (Cauchy-Schwarz proves uniqueness).
- **Learning rate bounds:** For a smooth cost with $L$-Lipschitz gradient, stability requires $0 < \eta < 2/L$.
- **Convex → global minimum; non-convex → local minimum only.**
- **Termination:** $\Vert \nabla f \Vert \leq \epsilon$ **OR** $k \geq k_{\max}$ **OR** $\vert \Delta f \vert \leq \delta$.
- **First-order method** — uses only $\nabla f$, ignores curvature.
- **Cost is monotonically non-increasing** at every iteration (guaranteed for proper $\eta$).
- **Newton's method** upgrades to second-order: $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \eta \mathbf{H}^{-1} \nabla f$ (Hessian $\mathbf{H}$).
- **Momentum variants** add $\beta (\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_{k-1})$ for faster convergence in ravines.
- **SGD** approximates the gradient with one sample — useful for online learning in robots.
- **Robot nav applications:** trajectory smoothing, SLAM bundle adjustment, IK solving, perception-network training.
- **Quadratic case:** error decays geometrically with rate $(1 - \eta \mu)$, where $\mu$ is the smallest eigenvalue of the Hessian.
- **Always tabulate iterations** in KTU exam answers — examiners reward systematic working.
- **Mention units and physical meaning** when the cost function has real-world engineering context (energy, distance, time).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gradient Descent Without and With Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gradient Descent Without and With Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gradient Descent** is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to find the **local minimum** of a differentiable scalar-valued cost function $f(\mathbf{x}): \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, by repeatedly moving in the direction of **steepest descent**, which is the **negative of the gradient** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$ of the function at the current point.

In the context of **Robot Navigation (PEBRT751, Module 3)**, gradient descent is treated as the mathematical engine behind:
- **Trajectory optimization** (minimizing path length, energy, time)
- **Potential field methods** (steering a robot toward a goal while avoiding obstacles)
- **Localization refinement** (minimizing the residual between predicted and observed sensor measurements)

### Two Canonical Variants

| Variant | Mathematical Domain | Governing Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **Unconstrained Gradient Descent** | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (free variable) | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ |
| **Constrained Gradient Descent** | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ (within feasible region) | $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$, $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ |

The **standard learning rate** symbol used throughout KTU board papers is **$\alpha_k$** (step size at iteration $k$), and the canonical **convergence tolerance** is **$\epsilon = 10^{-6}$** (board-exam default).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blind Hiker on a Foggy Mountain"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a hiker trapped on a fog-covered mountain at night. They cannot see the bottom of the valley, but they can *feel* the slope of the ground beneath their feet. Their strategy is to **take a small step in the direction where the ground falls steepest**, then re-evaluate the slope, and repeat until the ground feels flat.

This analogy maps perfectly onto the algorithm:
- The **mountain's height** at any point = the cost $f(\mathbf{x})$
- The **slope of the ground** = the gradient $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$
- The **step length** = the learning rate $\alpha$
- The **valley floor** = the local minimum $\mathbf{x}^*$

If the hiker is forbidden from stepping on **certain rocks (obstacles)** or must remain within a **park boundary (constraints)**, the analogy extends naturally to **constrained gradient descent** — the hiker must occasionally step *sideways* or be *redirected* to stay within the legal region.

---

## 1.3 Why Gradient Descent Matters in Robot Navigation

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEBRT751 — Robot Navigation**, gradient-based optimization is positioned as the **mathematical backbone of reactive navigation**. The key engineering applications are:

1. **Artificial Potential Field (APF) Navigation** — The robot is modeled as a charged particle moving through a potential field $U(\mathbf{q}) = U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) + U_{rep}(\mathbf{q})$, where the control input (velocity) is computed as the **negative gradient** of this potential.
2. **Trajectory Optimization** — Path smoothness and control effort are minimized as a quadratic cost in joint space.
3. **SLAM Back-End Optimization** — Pose graphs are optimized by minimizing the sum of squared reprojection or odometry errors.
4. **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** — Future control sequences are obtained by solving a constrained quadratic program (QP) at every time step using projected gradient methods.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Whenever a KTU question says "explain how a robot reaches a goal while avoiding obstacles," the **expected answer almost always starts with the gradient of an artificial potential field**, followed by a discussion of local minima and constraint handling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Unconstrained Gradient Descent — Operational Logic

The unconstrained variant operates under a single governing principle:

> **The fastest way to decrease a function locally is to move opposite to its gradient.**

The algorithm proceeds through the following **structured logical steps**:

1. **Initialization** — Choose a starting point $\mathbf{x}_0 \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (in robot navigation, this is the robot's initial pose $\mathbf{q}_0$) and a step size $\alpha > 0$ (possibly varying per iteration as $\alpha_k$).
2. **Gradient Evaluation** — Compute the gradient vector $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ at the current iterate.
3. **Descent Step** — Update the iterate:
   $$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$
4. **Convergence Test** — Halt when $\vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \vert_2 \le \epsilon$ or when $\vert \vert \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k \vert \vert_2 \le \epsilon$.
5. **Otherwise** — Return to Step 2 with $k \leftarrow k+1$.

### Why Does It Converge?

Consider the first-order Taylor expansion of $f$ about $\mathbf{x}_k$:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T (\mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k)$$

Substituting the update rule $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k = -\alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) - \alpha_k \vert \vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \vert_2^2$$

Since $\alpha_k > 0$ and $\vert \vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \vert_2^2 \ge 0$, the function value **monotonically decreases** at every iteration — this is the **descent property**.

### 2.1.1 Choice of Step Size (The "$\alpha_k$ Problem")

The KTU board tests three families:

| Step-Size Strategy | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed (Constant) $\alpha$** | $\alpha_k = \alpha$ | Simple implementation; requires manual tuning |
| **Exact Line Search** | $\alpha_k = \arg\min_{\alpha > 0} f(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k))$ | Theoretically optimal; expensive to compute |
| **Backtracking (Armijo)** | Shrink $\alpha$ until $f(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)) \le f(\mathbf{x}_k) - c \alpha \vert \vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \vert^2$ | Default in robot navigation codes |

### 2.1.2 Convergence Rate (KTU Favourite Sub-Question)

- **Convex + Lipschitz gradient** $\rightarrow$ $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$ rate (sub-linear)
- **Strongly convex + Lipschitz gradient** $\rightarrow$ **linear (geometric)** rate with contraction factor $\left( \frac{L - \mu}{L + \mu} \right)^2$, where $L$ = Lipschitz constant of $\nabla f$, $\mu$ = strong-convexity modulus.
- **Local (near minimum)** $\rightarrow$ **quadratic** rate (Newton-like behaviour, but not via pure gradient descent).

---

## 2.2 Constrained Gradient Descent — The Three Standard Frameworks

When the robot's state $\mathbf{x}$ is restricted to a feasible set $\mathcal{C} = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n : g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0,\ h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0\}$, the plain update $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ can **violate constraints**. Three KTU-approved remedies exist:

### 2.2.1 Projected Gradient Descent (PGD)

After the standard unconstrained step, **project** the result back onto the feasible set:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \Pi_{\mathcal{C}} \left( \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \right)$$

where $\Pi_{\mathcal{C}}(\mathbf{y}) = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{C}} \vert \vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \vert \vert_2$ is the Euclidean projector.

> [!NOTE]
> For **box constraints** $\mathcal{C} = \{\mathbf{x} : \ell_i \le x_i \le u_i\}$, the projector is a **simple elementwise clip**: $\Pi_{\mathcal{C}}(\mathbf{y})_i = \max(\ell_i, \min(u_i, y_i))$. This is the form most commonly tested in KTU papers.

### 2.2.2 Lagrangian / KKT Method

Reformulate the constrained problem as an unconstrained saddle-point search using **Lagrange multipliers** $\lambda_i, \nu_j$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\nu}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i} \lambda_i g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j} \nu_j h_j(\mathbf{x})$$

The KKT first-order conditions for optimality are:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_i \lambda_i \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_j \nu_j \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}$$

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \le 0,\quad \lambda_i \ge 0,\quad \lambda_i g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$$

$$h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$$

These are the **Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions** and are the most-tested 14-mark topic in the entire KTU 2024 scheme for this module.

### 2.2.3 Penalty / Barrier Methods

**Penalty method** (exterior) — add a penalty for constraint violation:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x}} \Phi(\mathbf{x}, \rho) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{\rho}{2} \sum_i \max(0, g_i(\mathbf{x}))^2 + \frac{\rho}{2} \sum_j h_j(\mathbf{x})^2$$

**Logarithmic barrier method** (interior) — for inequality constraints only:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x}} \Phi(\mathbf{x}, \mu) = f(\mathbf{x}) - \frac{1}{\mu} \sum_i \log(-g_i(\mathbf{x}))$$

The parameter $\rho \to \infty$ (penalty) or $\mu \to 0$ (barrier) drives the iterates toward the constrained optimum.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Day Quick Reference)

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Symbol Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Unconstrained update | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | $\alpha_k$ = step size, $k$ = iteration |
| 2 | Descent direction | $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Always the negative gradient |
| 3 | Convergence tolerance | $\vert \vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \vert_2 \le \epsilon$ | KTU default $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$ |
| 4 | Armijo backtracking | $f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k) \le f(\mathbf{x}_k) + c \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d}_k$ | $c \in (0, 1)$, typically $c = 10^{-4}$ |
| 5 | Projected gradient update | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \Pi_{\mathcal{C}}(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k))$ | $\Pi_{\mathcal{C}}$ = Euclidean projector |
| 6 | Lagrangian function | $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\nu}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_i \lambda_i g_i + \sum_j \nu_j h_j$ | $\lambda_i \ge 0$ for inequalities |
| 7 | KKT stationarity | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_i \lambda_i^* \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_j \nu_j^* \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ | Necessary under constraint qualification |
| 8 | Complementary slackness | $\lambda_i^* g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | Inactive if $\lambda_i^* = 0$ |
| 9 | Penalty method objective | $\Phi(\mathbf{x}, \rho) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{\rho}{2} \sum_i \max(0, g_i)^2 + \frac{\rho}{2} \sum_j h_j^2$ | $\rho \to \infty$ as $k \to \infty$ |
| 10 | Logarithmic barrier | $\Phi(\mathbf{x}, \mu) = f(\mathbf{x}) - \frac{1}{\mu} \sum_i \log(-g_i(\mathbf{x}))$ | Requires strict feasibility $g_i < 0$ |
| 11 | APF attractive potential | $U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \vert \vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \vert \vert^2$ | Gradient drives robot toward goal |
| 12 | APF repulsive potential | $U_{rep}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2$ for $\rho \le \rho_0$ | $\rho_0$ = influence radius |
| 13 | Robot control input | $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{q}) = -\nabla U(\mathbf{q})$ | Force = negative potential gradient |
| 14 | Convergence rate (convex) | $f(\mathbf{x}_k) - f(\mathbf{x}^*) \le \frac{\vert \vert \mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{x}^* \vert \vert^2}{2 \alpha k}$ | Sub-linear $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$ |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Mobile Robot Navigation** | APF-based local planners (e.g., used in ROS Navigation stack) |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | MPC trajectory tracking with obstacle constraints |
| **Aerial Robotics (UAVs)** | Trajectory optimization in cluttered environments |
| **Industrial Manipulators** | Joint-space redundancy resolution under torque limits |
| **SLAM Back-End** | Bundle adjustment & pose graph optimization |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | Policy gradient methods in actor-critic algorithms |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When a question asks "Why use gradient descent in robot navigation?" your answer should mention **real-time computability** (no matrix inversion needed for pure gradient descent), **local nature** (works in unknown dynamic environments), and **smoothness guarantees** (the robot's trajectory inherits the smoothness of the potential field).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Unconstrained Gradient Descent from the Taylor Series

We want to prove that $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ is a **descent direction**.

**Step 1 — First-Order Taylor Expansion of $f$ about $\mathbf{x}_k$**

For a small displacement $\Delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k$, the first-order Taylor series gives:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \Delta \mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \Delta \mathbf{x} + \mathcal{O}(\vert \vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \vert \vert^2)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Candidate Update $\Delta \mathbf{x} = \alpha \mathbf{d}$**

Choosing a direction $\mathbf{d}$ and step size $\alpha$:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}) = f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d} + \mathcal{O}(\alpha^2 \vert \vert \mathbf{d} \vert \vert^2)$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Direction that Minimizes the Linear Term**

To make $f$ decrease, we need $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d} < 0$. By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d} \ge -\vert \vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \vert \cdot \vert \vert \mathbf{d} \vert \vert$$

The **most negative** value of $\nabla f^T \mathbf{d}$ is $-\vert \vert \nabla f \vert \vert \cdot \vert \vert \mathbf{d} \vert \vert$, achieved when $\mathbf{d}$ points exactly **opposite** to the gradient:

$$\mathbf{d}^* = -\frac{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)}{\vert \vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \vert}$$

**Step 4 — Construct the Update Rule**

Substituting $\mathbf{d}^*$ with step size $\alpha$:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

**Step 5 — Verify the Descent Property**

The change in function value is:

$$\Delta f = f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) - f(\mathbf{x}_k) = -\alpha \vert \vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \vert^2 + \mathcal{O}(\alpha^2)$$

For sufficiently small $\alpha$, the leading term dominates:

$$\Delta f \approx -\alpha \vert \vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \vert^2 < 0 \quad \text{whenever } \nabla f \neq \mathbf{0}$$

This proves the **monotone descent property** of the unconstrained algorithm. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Constrained Optimality via the Lagrangian (KKT Conditions)

We aim to derive the KKT first-order necessary conditions for:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x}} f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0,\ i = 1, \dots, m, \quad h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0,\ j = 1, \dots, p$$

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian**

Augment the cost with weighted constraints:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\nu}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \nu_j h_j(\mathbf{x})$$

where $\boldsymbol{\lambda} \in \mathbb{R}^m_{\ge 0}$ and $\boldsymbol{\nu} \in \mathbb{R}^p$ are the **Lagrange multipliers**.

**Step 2 — Set the Gradient of the Lagrangian to Zero (Stationarity)**

At the optimum $\mathbf{x}^*$:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i^* \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \nu_j^* \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 3 — Enforce Primal Feasibility**

The candidate solution must satisfy the original constraints:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \le 0 \quad \forall i, \qquad h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0 \quad \forall j$$

**Step 4 — Enforce Dual Feasibility**

Multipliers for inequality constraints must be non-negative (this is what turns the inequality into a valid penalty against violation):

$$\lambda_i^* \ge 0 \quad \forall i$$

**Step 5 — Complementary Slackness**

For each inequality constraint, either the constraint is **active** (binding) or the multiplier is **zero**:

$$\lambda_i^* g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0 \quad \forall i$$

These four conditions together form the **Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions**, which are necessary for $\mathbf{x}^*$ to be a constrained local minimum, provided a **constraint qualification** (e.g., LICQ — Linear Independence Constraint Qualification) holds at $\mathbf{x}^*$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Projected Gradient Descent Update

We want to derive the projected gradient update from first principles.

**Step 1 — State the Constrained Problem**

$$\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{C}} f(\mathbf{x})$$

**Step 2 — Linearize $f$ at $\mathbf{x}_k$**

The local linear model is:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_k)$$

**Step 3 — Minimize the Linear Model over the Feasible Set**

A natural surrogate step is the **proximal-gradient sub-problem**:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{C}} \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_k) + \frac{1}{2 \alpha} \vert \vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_k \vert \vert^2$$

The $\frac{1}{2\alpha}\vert \vert \cdot \vert \vert^2$ term is a **proximal regularizer** that penalizes large steps and keeps the surrogate well-posed.

**Step 4 — Complete the Square**

Drop the constants with respect to $\mathbf{x}$:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{C}} \frac{1}{2 \alpha} \left[ \vert \vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_k \vert \vert^2 + 2 \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_k) \right]$$

$$= \min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{C}} \frac{1}{2 \alpha} \vert \vert \mathbf{x} - (\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)) \vert \vert^2 - \frac{\alpha}{2} \vert \vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \vert^2$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the Projection**

The minimizer of $\frac{1}{2\alpha}\vert \vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \vert \vert^2$ over $\mathcal{C}$ is exactly the Euclidean projector $\Pi_{\mathcal{C}}(\mathbf{y})$ with $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \Pi_{\mathcal{C}} \left( \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \right) = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{C}} \vert \vert \mathbf{x} - \left( \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \right) \vert \vert^2 \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — Constrained Quadratic in Robot Path Planning

**Problem:** A 2-DOF planar robot arm must reach the goal pose $\mathbf{q}_{goal} = (2, 2)^T$ while avoiding an obstacle at $\mathbf{q}_{obs} = (1, 1)^T$ with a safety radius of $0.5$. Minimize:

$$f(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{2} \vert \vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \vert \vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad g(\mathbf{q}) = 0.25 - \vert \vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs} \vert \vert^2 \le 0$$

**Step 1 — Compute $\nabla f$**

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} = \begin{pmatrix} q_1 - 2 \\ q_2 - 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\nabla g$**

$$g(\mathbf{q}) = 0.25 - (q_1 - 1)^2 - (q_2 - 1)^2, \qquad \nabla g(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{pmatrix} -2(q_1 - 1) \\ -2(q_2 - 1) \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KKT at a Hypothetical Active-Constraint Point**

At the optimal feasible point, the constraint is active: $\vert \vert \mathbf{q}^* - \mathbf{q}_{obs} \vert \vert^2 = 0.25$. KKT stationarity gives:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{q}^*) + \lambda^* \nabla g(\mathbf{q}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$

$$\begin{pmatrix} q_1^* - 2 \\ q_2^* - 2 \end{pmatrix} + \lambda^* \begin{pmatrix} -2(q_1^* - 1) \\ -2(q_2^* - 1) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the System**

This yields two scalar equations, e.g.:

$$q_1^* - 2 - 2\lambda^*(q_1^* - 1) = 0 \implies (1 - 2\lambda^*) q_1^* = 2 - 2\lambda^* \implies q_1^* = \frac{2 - 2\lambda^*}{1 - 2\lambda^*}$$

A similar expression holds for $q_2^*$. Together with the active constraint $\vert \vert \mathbf{q}^* - \mathbf{q}_{obs} \vert \vert^2 = 0.25$, this forms a system solvable for $(\mathbf{q}^*, \lambda^*)$. The standard KTU board solution then verifies $\lambda^* \ge 0$ to confirm feasibility.

**Step 5 — Iterate Using Projected Gradient (Numerical)**

Starting from $\mathbf{q}_0 = (0, 0)^T$ with $\alpha = 0.1$:

$$\mathbf{q}_1 = \mathbf{q}_0 - 0.1 \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.2 \end{pmatrix}$$

Check constraint: $\vert \vert (0.2, 0.2) - (1, 1) \vert \vert^2 = 1.28 > 0.25$ → feasible, continue.

The full numerical trajectory eventually settles at the constrained optimum on the boundary of the safety circle. This example directly illustrates how **the robot is "pushed" along the constraint surface** to the goal, mimicking obstacle avoidance.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Gradient Descent in a Robot Navigation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Robot Pose x0] --> B[Sensor Module: Obstacle and Goal Data]
    B --> C[Cost Function Builder: f of x = f_att plus f_rep]
    C --> D[Gradient Computer: nabla f of x_k]
    D --> E{Constraint Active?}
    E -- No --> F[Unconstrained Update: x_new = x_k minus alpha nabla f]
    E -- Yes --> G[Lagrangian Formulation with Lambda and Nu]
    G --> H[KKT Solver: Stationarity plus Feasibility plus Slackness]
    H --> I[Constrained Update: x_new = Pi_C of x_k minus alpha nabla f]
    F --> J[Convergence Test: norm of nabla f less than epsilon]
    I --> J
    J -- Not Converged --> K[k = k plus 1]
    K --> C
    J -- Converged --> L[Final Optimal Pose x_star]
    L --> M[Actuator Command: u = minus nabla U of q]
    M --> N[Robot Motion in Workspace]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above maps the **complete information flow** from sensor acquisition through cost computation, gradient evaluation, constraint handling, and finally to actuator command generation. Every node in this architecture corresponds to a **module** that a student can be asked to explain in a 14-mark KTU question.

---

## 4.2 Detailed Unconstrained Gradient Descent Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Initialize k = 0, x0, alpha, epsilon] --> A[Compute gradient g_k = nabla f of x_k]
    A --> B{norm of g_k less than epsilon?}
    B -- Yes --> Z[Output: x_star = x_k, STOP]
    B -- No --> C[Compute search direction d_k = minus g_k]
    C --> D[Choose step size alpha_k via backtracking Armijo]
    D --> E[Update x_{k+1} = x_k plus alpha_k d_k]
    E --> F[Increment k = k plus 1]
    F --> A
```

---

## 4.3 Constrained Gradient Descent — KKT Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Problem: min f of x subject to g_i less than 0, h_j equals 0] --> Q[LICQ Holds at x star?]
    Q -- Yes --> R[Write Lagrangian L of x, lambda, nu]
    R --> T[Stationarity: nabla_x L = 0]
    T --> U[Primal Feasibility: g_i less than 0, h_j equals 0]
    U --> V[Dual Feasibility: lambda_i greater than 0]
    V --> W[Complementary Slackness: lambda_i g_i = 0]
    W --> X[Solution x star, lambda star, nu star]
    Q -- No --> Y[Constraint Qualification Fails, KKT not applicable]
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison Schematic: Unconstrained vs Constrained Update Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UNCON[Unconstrained Case]
        U1[Robot at x_k] -- gradient descent step --> U2[Robot at x_{k+1}]
        U2 -. free to move anywhere in R^n .-> U3[Feasible Region: Entire R^n]
    end
    subgraph CON[Constrained Case]
        C1[Robot at x_k] -- raw gradient step --> C2[Tentative point y]
        C2 -- projection Pi_C --> C3[Projected point x_{k+1} in C]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Interpretation:** In the unconstrained case, the robot moves freely in $\mathbb{R}^n$ following the negative gradient. In the constrained case, the robot first **takes a tentative step** along the negative gradient, and if it lands outside the feasible set $\mathcal{C}$, it is **"snapped" back** to the closest feasible point via the Euclidean projector $\Pi_{\mathcal{C}}$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the gradient descent algorithm. Explain the role of the learning rate $\alpha$ in the convergence of the algorithm.**

**Model Answer:**

> Gradient descent is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm that minimizes a differentiable function $f(\mathbf{x})$ by updating the variable in the direction opposite to the gradient. The update rule is:
>
> $$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$
>
> The learning rate $\alpha_k$ controls the **step size** taken in the descent direction at iteration $k$. If $\alpha_k$ is **too small**, convergence is slow and may stall before reaching the minimum. If $\alpha_k$ is **too large**, the algorithm may **overshoot** the minimum, oscillate, or even diverge. The optimal $\alpha_k$ is often determined by **line search** (exact or backtracking Armijo) to guarantee sufficient decrease. For convex $L$-smooth functions, a constant step size $\alpha \in (0, 1/L]$ guarantees convergence at rate $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the KKT complementary slackness condition? Why is it important in constrained optimization for robot navigation?**

**Model Answer:**

> The complementary slackness condition states that for each inequality constraint $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ with optimal Lagrange multiplier $\lambda_i^*$:
>
> $$\lambda_i^* g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$$
>
> This means **at most one of the two can be non-zero** at the optimum. If the constraint is **inactive** (strictly satisfied, $g_i < 0$), then $\lambda_i^* = 0$ — the constraint does not influence the optimum. If the constraint is **active** (binding, $g_i = 0$), then $\lambda_i^* \ge 0$ and the multiplier reflects the "force" exerted by the constraint.
>
> In **robot navigation**, this corresponds to the situation where the robot's optimal path is either **not touching an obstacle** (constraint inactive) or **grazing the obstacle boundary** (constraint active). The corresponding multiplier $\lambda_i^*$ is the **contact force** the obstacle exerts on the robot's trajectory — directly analogous to a normal force in mechanics.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module 3 Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the unconstrained gradient descent update rule from the first-order Taylor expansion of the cost function. Show explicitly why the negative gradient is the direction of **steepest local descent**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following robot trajectory optimization problem:

$$\min_{\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^2} f(\mathbf{q}) = (q_1 - 3)^2 + (q_2 - 4)^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad q_1 + q_2 \le 5$$

Using the **Lagrangian method**, write the KKT conditions and determine the **optimal solution** $\mathbf{q}^* = (q_1^*, q_2^*)^T$ and the multiplier $\lambda^*$.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Taylor Expansion:** [1 Mark]

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}) = f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d} + \mathcal{O}(\alpha^2 \vert \vert \mathbf{d} \vert \vert^2)$$

**Step 2 — Condition for Descent:** [1 Mark]

We require $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d} < 0$. By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d} \ge -\vert \vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \vert \cdot \vert \vert \mathbf{d} \vert \vert$$

**Step 3 — Maximizing the Decrease:** [1 Mark]

The most negative value of $\nabla f^T \mathbf{d}$ is $-\vert \vert \nabla f \vert \vert \cdot \vert \vert \mathbf{d} \vert \vert$, achieved when:

$$\mathbf{d}^* = -\frac{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)}{\vert \vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \vert}$$

**Step 4 — Update Rule:** [1 Mark]

With step size $\alpha$:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

**Step 5 — Descent Verification:** [1 Mark]

$$\Delta f = f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) - f(\mathbf{x}_k) \approx -\alpha \vert \vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \vert^2 < 0$$

**Step 6 — Geometric Interpretation:** [1 Mark]

The negative gradient $-\nabla f$ is **perpendicular to the level curve** $f(\mathbf{x}) = c$ at $\mathbf{x}_k$ and points toward the **lowest-value level set** reachable by an infinitesimal step.

**Step 7 — Convergence Note:** [1 Mark]

For $L$-smooth and $\mu$-strongly convex $f$, gradient descent with constant step size $\alpha = 1/L$ achieves a linear convergence rate:

$$\vert \vert \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}^* \vert \vert \le \left( 1 - \frac{\mu}{L} \right) \vert \vert \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}^* \vert \vert$$

---

#### Part (b) — KKT Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Formulate the Lagrangian** [1 Mark]

With $g(\mathbf{q}) = q_1 + q_2 - 5 \le 0$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{q}, \lambda) = (q_1 - 3)^2 + (q_2 - 4)^2 + \lambda (q_1 + q_2 - 5)$$

**Step 2 — Stationarity** [2 Marks]

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_1} = 2(q_1 - 3) + \lambda = 0 \implies q_1 = 3 - \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_2} = 2(q_2 - 4) + \lambda = 0 \implies q_2 = 4 - \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Check if the Unconstrained Minimum is Feasible** [1 Mark]

Setting $\lambda = 0$: $q_1 = 3, q_2 = 4$. Check: $q_1 + q_2 = 7 > 5$ → **infeasible**, so the constraint is active.

**Step 4 — Active-Constraint Assumption** [1 Mark]

With $g(\mathbf{q}^*) = 0$:

$$q_1^* + q_2^* = 5 \implies \left(3 - \frac{\lambda}{2}\right) + \left(4 - \frac{\lambda}{2}\right) = 5 \implies 7 - \lambda = 5 \implies \lambda^* = 2$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Optimal Primal Variables** [1 Mark]

$$q_1^* = 3 - 1 = 2, \qquad q_2^* = 4 - 1 = 3$$

**Step 6 — Verify KKT Conditions** [1 Mark]

- Primal feasibility: $q_1^* + q_2^* = 5 \le 5$ ✓
- Dual feasibility: $\lambda^* = 2 \ge 0$ ✓
- Complementary slackness: $\lambda^* g(\mathbf{q}^*) = 2 \cdot 0 = 0$ ✓
- Stationarity: verified above ✓

**Final Answer:** $\mathbf{q}^* = (2, 3)^T$ and $\lambda^* = 2$, with optimal cost $f(\mathbf{q}^*) = 1 + 1 = 2$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Projected Gradient Descent (PGD)** method for solving constrained optimization problems. Derive the update rule from the surrogate minimization of a linearized cost function with a proximal regularizer. Show clearly how the Euclidean projector $\Pi_{\mathcal{C}}$ appears in the final formula.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A mobile robot must navigate from $\mathbf{q}_0 = (0, 0)^T$ to $\mathbf{q}_{goal} = (5, 5)^T$ while avoiding a circular obstacle centred at $\mathbf{q}_{obs} = (2.5, 2.5)^T$ with safety radius $r = 1$. Formulate the cost function and constraints, and apply **two iterations of unconstrained gradient descent** starting from $\mathbf{q}_0$ using a fixed step size $\alpha = 0.1$. Comment on whether the resulting trajectory violates the safety constraint and what modification would be needed.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Projected Gradient Descent Derivation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Problem Statement** [1 Mark]

$$\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{C}} f(\mathbf{x}), \quad \mathcal{C} = \{\mathbf{x} : g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0,\ h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0\}$$

**Step 2 — Surrogate Model** [1 Mark]

Replace $f$ by its first-order Taylor expansion and add a proximal regularizer:

$$\phi(\mathbf{x}; \mathbf{x}_k) = f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_k) + \frac{1}{2 \alpha} \vert \vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_k \vert \vert^2$$

**Step 3 — Constrained Surrogate Minimization** [1 Mark]

The PGD update is obtained by minimizing $\phi$ over $\mathcal{C}$:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{C}} \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_k) + \frac{1}{2 \alpha} \vert \vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_k \vert \vert^2$$

**Step 4 — Complete the Square** [1 Mark]

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{C}} \frac{1}{2 \alpha} \vert \vert \mathbf{x} - \left( \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \right) \vert \vert^2$$

**Step 5 — Identification with the Projector** [1 Mark]

This is precisely the Euclidean projection:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \Pi_{\mathcal{C}} \left( \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \right)$$

**Step 6 — Special Case: Box Constraints** [1 Mark]

For $\mathcal{C} = \{\mathbf{x} : \ell_i \le x_i \le u_i\}$:

$$\Pi_{\mathcal{C}}(\mathbf{y})_i = \max(\ell_i, \min(u_i, y_i))$$

**Step 7 — Convergence Statement** [1 Mark]

For convex $L$-smooth $f$ and a non-empty closed convex $\mathcal{C}$, PGD with $\alpha = 1/L$ satisfies $f(\mathbf{x}_k) - f(\mathbf{x}^*) \le \frac{\vert \vert \mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{x}^* \vert \vert^2}{2 \alpha k}$.

---

#### Part (b) — Robot Navigation with Two Iterations [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Cost Function** [1 Mark]

$$f(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{2} \vert \vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \vert \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2} \left[ (q_1 - 5)^2 + (q_2 - 5)^2 \right]$$

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{pmatrix} q_1 - 5 \\ q_2 - 5 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Constraint** [1 Mark]

$$g(\mathbf{q}) = 1 - \vert \vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{obs} \vert \vert^2 \le 0 \iff \vert \vert \mathbf{q} - (2.5, 2.5)^T \vert \vert \le 1$$

**Step 3 — Iteration 0 → 1** [1 Mark]

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_0) = \begin{pmatrix} -5 \\ -5 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{q}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} - 0.1 \begin{pmatrix} -5 \\ -5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

Check constraint: $\vert \vert (0.5, 0.5) - (2.5, 2.5) \vert \vert = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83 > 1$ → **feasible**.

**Step 4 — Iteration 1 → 2** [1 Mark]

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{q}_1) = \begin{pmatrix} -4.5 \\ -4.5 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{q}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix} - 0.1 \begin{pmatrix} -4.5 \\ -4.5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.95 \\ 0.95 \end{pmatrix}$$

Check constraint: $\vert \vert (0.95, 0.95) - (2.5, 2.5) \vert \vert = \sqrt{2 \cdot 1.55^2} \approx 2.19 > 1$ → **feasible** for now.

**Step 5 — Continued Iterates Will Eventually Violate the Constraint** [1 Mark]

As $k$ increases, $\mathbf{q}_k$ approaches $\mathbf{q}_{goal} = (5, 5)^T$ and will eventually cross the safety circle around $(2.5, 2.5)$. At that point, **unconstrained gradient descent will produce infeasible iterates**.

**Step 6 — Modification: Switch to Projected Gradient Descent** [1 Mark]

Replace the unconstrained update with:

$$\mathbf{q}_{k+1} = \Pi_{\mathcal{C}} \left( \mathbf{q}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{q}_k) \right)$$

For the circular constraint, the projection involves scaling the vector $(\mathbf{q}_k - \mathbf{q}_{obs})$ to have norm exactly $r$ when the unconstrained step would exceed the safety boundary. This keeps the robot on or outside the obstacle surface.

**Step 7 — Engineering Insight** [1 Mark]

This simple example demonstrates the fundamental reason **constrained optimization is essential in robot navigation**: pure gradient descent ignores obstacles, but the cost landscape is *deformed* near obstacles via **artificial potential fields** so that the gradient itself pushes the robot around them. The PGD framework generalizes this to **arbitrary** geometric and dynamic constraints.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Descent Direction** (–1 mark): Students frequently write $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \nabla f$ — this is **gradient ascent**, not descent. The minus sign is mandatory.
> 2. **Forgetting Complementary Slackness** (–2 marks): When asked for KKT, students often list only stationarity and feasibility. The complementary slackness condition $\lambda_i^* g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ is a **separate mandatory condition** worth full credit.
> 3. **Active vs Inactive Constraint Confusion** (–1 to –2 marks): Before solving KKT, you **must check** whether the unconstrained minimum is feasible. If yes, $\lambda^* = 0$ and the constraint is irrelevant. If no, the constraint is active and you must solve with $g_i(\mathbf{q}^*) = 0$.
> 4. **Projector vs Gradient Mix-up** (–1 mark): The projector $\Pi_{\mathcal{C}}$ acts on a **point** (the tentative iterate), not on a **vector** (the gradient). Do not write $\Pi_{\mathcal{C}}(\nabla f)$.
> 5. **Step Size Justification Missing** (–1 mark): When proposing a fixed $\alpha$, briefly mention that it must satisfy $0 < \alpha \le 1/L$ where $L$ is the Lipschitz constant of $\nabla f$ for guaranteed descent.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Pre-Exam 5-Minute Revision — Pin This Section**

- [x] **Unconstrained Update Rule** — $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$
- [x] **Descent Property** — $\Delta f \approx -\alpha \vert \vert \nabla f \vert \vert^2 < 0$ for small $\alpha$
- [x] **Steepest Descent Direction** — $\mathbf{d}^* = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) / \vert \vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \vert$ (normalized)
- [x] **Convergence Test** — $\vert \vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \vert_2 \le \epsilon$ (KTU default $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$)
- [x] **Step Size Rule** — $0 < \alpha \le 1/L$ for $L$-smooth $f$; or use Armijo backtracking
- [x] **Projected Gradient Update** — $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \Pi_{\mathcal{C}}(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k))$
- [x] **Box Constraint Projector** — elementwise $\max(\ell, \min(u, y))$
- [x] **Lagrangian Form** — $\mathcal{L} = f + \sum \lambda_i g_i + \sum \nu_j h_j$, with $\lambda_i \ge 0$
- [x] **Four KKT Conditions** — Stationarity, Primal Feasibility, Dual Feasibility, Complementary Slackness
- [x] **Complementary Slackness Formula** — $\lambda_i^* g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$
- [x] **Penalty Method Objective** — $f + \frac{\rho}{2}\left(\sum \max(0, g_i)^2 + \sum h_j^2\right)$, $\rho \to \infty$
- [x] **Logarithmic Barrier** — $f - \frac{1}{\mu}\sum \log(-g_i)$, $\mu \to 0$
- [x] **APF Attractive Potential** — $U_{att} = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \vert \vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \vert \vert^2$
- [x] **APF Repulsive Potential** — $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep}\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2$ for $\rho \le \rho_0$
- [x] **Control Input from Potential** — $\mathbf{u} = -\nabla U_{total} = -\nabla U_{att} - \nabla U_{rep}$
- [x] **Convergence Rate (Convex)** — $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$ for plain GD; linear for strongly convex
- [x] **LICQ** — Linear Independence Constraint Qualification is the standard assumption for KKT applicability
- [x] **Geometric Meaning of Negative Gradient** — Perpendicular to level set, points toward steepest local decrease
- [x] **KTU Default Tolerance** — $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$ unless otherwise stated
- [x] **Engineering Application Triad** — APF Navigation + Trajectory Optimization + SLAM Back-End

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Note:** Whenever a Module 3 question asks you to "optimize" or "minimize" something in a robot navigation context, **default to gradient descent** as your first-choice algorithm unless the problem explicitly demands a second-order method (Newton, Gauss-Newton) or a global optimizer (RRT*, A*, genetic algorithm).
