phi = 0)):::goal Saddle1[/Saddle Point/]:::saddle Saddle2[/Saddle Point/]:::saddle Saddle3[/Saddle Point/]:::saddle end subgraph Obstacles [Obstacles and Boundary] Obs1([Obstacle O1]):::obs Obs2([Obstacle O2]):::obs Bnd([Workspace Boundary]):::bnd end Start((Start q_0
phi = high)):::start -->|Gradient Descent| Saddle1 Saddle1 -->|Avoids Obstacle 1| Goal Saddle2 -->|Channels around O2| Goal Saddle3 -->|Boundary repulsion| Goal Start -.->|phi approaches 1| Bnd Start -.->|blocked region| Obs1 Start -.->|blocked region| Obs2 classDef goal fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef start fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef saddle fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef obs fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef bnd fill:#bcaaa4,stroke:#3e2723,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Critical Point Taxonomy in a Navigation Function ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Critical Points of phi] --> B{Hessian Det} B --> C[det greater than 0, both eig greater than 0]:::min B --> D[det greater than 0, mixed eig]:::saddle B --> E[det less than 0]:::saddle C --> F[Local Minimum] D --> G[Saddle Point Desired] E --> G F --> H{In Free Space?} H -- Yes, but not at goal --> I[PROBLEM: Extra Minimum] H -- At q_d --> J[Goal: Global Minimum] I --> K[Increase kappa to Eliminate] J --> L[Navigation Function Valid] classDef min fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef saddle fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef valid fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class C,F,I min class D,E,G,K saddle class J,L valid ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEBRT751] **Define a Navigation Function. List the four essential properties it must satisfy.** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > A **Navigation Function (NF)** is a smooth, real-valued function $\varphi : \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}} \rightarrow [0, 1]$ that guides a robot from any initial configuration to the goal configuration $q_d$ via gradient descent, while avoiding all obstacles in a static deterministic environment. > > **Four Essential Properties (1 mark each for naming + 0.5 for brief explanation, totalling 3):** > 1. **Smoothness:** $\varphi$ is at least $C^2$ continuously differentiable on $\mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$. > 2. **Admissibility:** $\varphi(q) \to 1$ uniformly as $q$ approaches the boundary $\partial \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$. > 3. **Polar Structure:** $\varphi$ has a unique global minimum at $q_d$ and no other local minima in $\mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$. > 4. **Morse Condition:** All critical points of $\varphi$ are non-degenerate (Hessian is non-singular). --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEBRT751] **Differentiate between classical Artificial Potential Field (APF) method and the Koditschek-Rimon Navigation Function approach. Why does the NF avoid the local minima problem?** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > | Aspect | Classical APF | Koditschek-Rimon NF | > |---|---|---| > | **Local Minima** | Suffers from spurious local minima | No spurious local minima (polar property) | > | **Boundary Behaviour** | Repulsive force vanishes on boundary | Uniform value 1 on boundary (admissibility) | > | **Completeness** | Not complete | Provably complete (converges from any $q_0$) | > | **Tuning** | Single gain per obstacle | Single global parameter $\kappa$ | > | **Mathematical Rigour** | Heuristic | Rigorously proven theorems | > > **Why NF avoids local minima (1 mark):** The Koditschek-Rimon theorem guarantees that for $\kappa \geq \kappa^{\*}$, the function $\varphi_{\kappa}$ is a **navigation function**, meaning **all critical points other than the goal are saddles**. Gradient descent on a saddle always escapes, so the robot cannot get trapped. The choice of the **product form** for $\beta(q)$ and the **power $\kappa$** in the denominator together ensure the polar structure. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Derive the **Koditschek-Rimon Navigation Function** for a robot operating in a static, deterministic sphere world with $m$ spherical obstacles. Clearly state the role of the tuning parameter $\kappa$ and explain the admissibility, polar, and Morse conditions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a 2D mobile robot in a disk workspace of radius $R = 8$ m, with goal at origin and two circular obstacles $\mathcal{O}_1$ (center $(4, 0)$, radius $1$ m) and $\mathcal{O}_2$ (center $(-3, 2)$, radius $1.2$ m), construct the navigation function $\varphi_{\kappa=2}(q)$ and discuss the trajectory that a gradient-descent controller would produce. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q-A** > [!NOTE] > **Part (a) — 7 marks (Detailed KTU Board Answer):** > > **Step 1 — Workspace Definition (1 mark):** > Let $\mathcal{W} \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ be a closed ball of radius $R$ centered at the origin. Let there be $m$ disjoint closed spherical obstacles $\mathcal{O}_i$ with centers $q_{0i}$ and radii $\rho_i$, removed from $\mathcal{W}$. The free space is: > $$\mathcal{F}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{W} \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{O}_i$$ > > **Step 2 — Attractive Term (1 mark):** > $$\gamma_d(q) = \big\Vert q - q_d \big\Vert^{2} = \sum_{j=1}^{n}(q_j - q_{d,j})^{2}$$ > This is a smooth paraboloid with unique minimum at $q_d$. > > **Step 3 — Obstacle Term (1 mark):** > $$\beta_i(q) = \big\Vert q - q_{0i} \big\Vert^{2} - \rho_i^{2}, \quad \beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \beta_i(q)$$ > > **Step 4 — The Navigation Function (1 mark):** > $$\varphi_{\kappa}(q) = \frac{\gamma_d(q)}{\big(\gamma_d(q)\big)^{\kappa} + \beta(q)}$$ > > **Step 5 — Role of $\kappa$ (1 mark):** > The parameter $\kappa$ controls the **stiffness** near the goal. Larger $\kappa$ makes the NF "sharper" near $q_d$ and "flatter" in the bulk, eliminating spurious critical points. > > **Step 6 — Admissibility, Polar, Morse (2 marks):** > - **Admissibility:** $\beta(q) \to 0$ on $\partial \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}} \Rightarrow \varphi \to 1$. > - **Polar:** Koditschek-Rimon theorem guarantees unique minimum at $q_d$ for $\kappa \geq \kappa^{\*}$. > - **Morse:** Hessian is non-singular at all critical points. > [!NOTE] > **Part (b) — 7 marks (Worked Construction):** > > **Step 1 — Define components (1.5 marks):** > $$\gamma_d(q) = x^{2} + y^{2}$$ > $$\beta_1(q) = (x-4)^{2} + y^{2} - 1$$ > $$\beta_2(q) = (x+3)^{2} + (y-2)^{2} - 1.44$$ > $$\beta(q) = \beta_1 \cdot \beta_2$$ > > **Step 2 — Assemble NF with $\kappa = 2$ (1.5 marks):** > $$\varphi_{2}(x,y) = \frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{(x^{2}+y^{2})^{2} + \big[(x-4)^{2}+y^{2}-1\big]\big[(x+3)^{2}+(y-2)^{2}-1.44\big]}$$ > > **Step 3 — Gradient Descent Control Law (1.5 marks):** > $$\dot{q} = -K \nabla \varphi_{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \begin{cases} \dot{x} = -K \dfrac{\partial \varphi_2}{\partial x} \\ \dot{y} = -K \dfrac{\partial \varphi_2}{\partial y} \end{cases}$$ > > **Step 4 — Trajectory Discussion (2.5 marks):** > - From any start in $\mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$, the robot slides downhill on the NF surface. > - Near obstacle 1 (right side), the NF rises sharply to 1, repelling the robot to the left. > - Near obstacle 2 (upper-left), the NF also rises to 1, pushing the robot back toward the goal. > - The robot's path bends around the saddle points formed between obstacles and reaches $q_d = (0,0)$. > - The path is **smooth, collision-free, and converges to the goal** — guaranteed by the polar property. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > - Students often **forget to take the product** for $\beta(q)$ — losing 2 marks. > - **Forgetting the tuning parameter $\kappa$** in the denominator loses 1 mark. > - **Not mentioning admissibility vs. polar distinction** loses 1 mark. > - **Not computing the actual gradient** in part (b) loses 2 marks. > - **Forgetting the Morse condition** loses 1 mark. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** Explain the concept of a **sphere world** and the **extended sphere world transformation** in the context of navigation functions. How does this transformation help in handling non-spherical obstacles? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **extended sphere world construction** with the safety functions $\sigma_i(q)$ and $\sigma_0(q)$ for a 2D disk workspace. Show that the resulting $\varphi(q)$ is a navigation function. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q-B** > [!NOTE] > **Part (a) — 7 marks:** > > **Step 1 — Sphere World Definition (2 marks):** > A **sphere world** is a topological model where the workspace $\mathcal{W}$ is a closed ball and every obstacle is a closed ball, all mutually disjoint. The free space $\mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$ is therefore homeomorphic to a punctured ball with several "punctures" — a smooth, compact manifold with boundary. > > **Step 2 — Limitations of Pure Sphere World (1.5 marks):** > Real-world obstacles (tables, walls, irregular shapes) are **not spherical**. Directly using a sphere world would either over-approximate obstacles (wasting free space) or fail to capture the true geometry. > > **Step 3 — Extended Sphere World Transformation (2 marks):** > Introduced by **Koditschek and Rimon (1990)**, the extended sphere world uses a **smooth diffeomorphism** $h : \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}} \to \mathcal{F}_{\text{trans}}$ that maps the actual free space to a topologically equivalent sphere world $\mathcal{F}_{\text{trans}}$. The navigation function is constructed in the transformed space: > $$\varphi_{\text{real}}(q) = \varphi_{\text{trans}}(h(q))$$ > > **Step 4 — Handling Non-Spherical Obstacles (1.5 marks):** > Arbitrary convex obstacles can be bounded by **star-shaped** or **strictly convex** envelopes, then mapped to spheres via a smooth transformation. The polar property is preserved under diffeomorphism, so the resulting planner remains complete. > [!NOTE] > **Part (b) — 7 marks:** > > **Step 1 — Define Safety Functions (2 marks):** > For the outer workspace boundary (disk of radius $R$): > $$\sigma_0(q) = -\big(\Vert q \Vert^{2} - R^{2}\big)$$ > For each obstacle $\mathcal{O}_i$: > $$\sigma_i(q) = \big\Vert q - q_{0i} \big\Vert^{2} - \rho_i^{2}$$ > > **Step 2 — Construct the Modified NF (2 marks):** > $$\varphi_{\kappa}(q) = \frac{\gamma_d(q)}{\big(\gamma_d(q)\big)^{\kappa} + \beta(q) \cdot \sigma_0(q)^{\ell}}$$ > where $\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \sigma_i(q)$ and $\ell$ is a non-negative integer. > > **Step 3 — Prove Validity (3 marks):** > - $\sigma_0 \to 0$ on workspace boundary $\Rightarrow$ NF $\to 1$ (admissibility). > - $\sigma_i \to 0$ on obstacle boundary $\Rightarrow$ NF $\to 1$ (admissibility). > - Goal $q_d$ gives $\gamma_d = 0$, $\sigma_i > 0$, $\sigma_0 > 0$ $\Rightarrow$ $\varphi(q_d) = 0$ (unique min). > - For $\kappa$ large enough, by the Koditschek-Rimon theorem, all other critical points are saddles (polar). > - Hessian non-singular at all critical points (Morse). > > Hence, $\varphi_{\kappa}$ is a **valid navigation function** for the extended sphere world. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Q-B):** > - Confusing **sphere world** with **extended sphere world** costs 2 marks. > - Not writing the **explicit form** of $\sigma_0$ and $\sigma_i$ costs 2 marks. > - Skipping the **diffeomorphism argument** for non-spherical obstacles costs 1.5 marks. > - Not citing the **Koditschek-Rimon theorem** in the proof of polar property costs 1.5 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist — Navigation Function in Static Deterministic Environment** > > ✅ **Definition:** $\varphi : \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}} \to [0, 1]$, smooth ($C^2$), with a single global minimum at the goal. > > ✅ **Four Required Properties:** Smoothness, Admissibility, Polar Structure, Morse Condition. > > ✅ **Koditschek-Rimon NF:** $\varphi_{\kappa}(q) = \dfrac{\gamma_d(q)}{(\gamma_d(q))^{\kappa} + \beta(q)}$. > > ✅ **Attractive Term:** $\gamma_d(q) = \Vert q - q_d \Vert^2$ — a paraboloid centered at the goal. > > ✅ **Obstacle Term:** $\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \big(\Vert q - q_{0i} \Vert^2 - \rho_i^2 \big)$ — product of squared-distance obstacle functions. > > ✅ **Tuning Parameter $\kappa$:** Controls stiffness; for $\kappa \geq \kappa^{\*}$ the polar property is guaranteed. > > ✅ **Critical $\kappa$:** $\kappa^{\*} \leq m \cdot \big(1 + R^2 / r_{\min}^2 \big)$. > > ✅ **Static Deterministic Environment:** Workspace and obstacles are known and time-invariant. > > ✅ **Sphere World:** Workspace is a closed ball, obstacles are closed balls, all mutually disjoint. > > ✅ **Extended Sphere World:** Uses a smooth diffeomorphism to map real free space to a sphere world. > > ✅ **Safety Functions:** $\sigma_0$ (workspace boundary), $\sigma_i$ (obstacle boundaries) — both vanish on their respective boundaries. > > ✅ **Boundary Value:** $\varphi \to 1$ uniformly on $\partial \mathcal{F}_{\text{free}}$. > > ✅ **Goal Value:** $\varphi(q_d) = 0$ (the only zero of the NF). > > ✅ **Control Law:** $\dot{q} = -K \nabla \varphi_{\kappa}(q)$ — robot moves down the gradient. > > ✅ **Key Theorem (Memorize):** *Koditschek-Rimon (1990) — for $\kappa \geq \kappa^{\*}$, $\varphi_{\kappa}$ is a valid Navigation Function.* > > ✅ **Common Mistake:** Forgetting the product in $\beta(q)$ or omitting the power $\kappa$ in the denominator. > > ✅ **Comparison Edge:** NF solves the local-minima problem of classical APF via the polar property and Morse condition. ---

topics.title: In Static Uncertain Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** In Static Uncertain Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Static Uncertain Environment** in robot navigation is an operational workspace characterized by two simultaneous conditions:

1. **Static** — The world state, including obstacle positions and geometry, does **not evolve with time**. Obstacles are stationary, no dynamic agents exist, and the map topology is temporally invariant.
2. **Uncertain** — The robot possesses **incomplete, imprecise, or noisy information** about this otherwise static world. The true state $x^*$ of the environment is unknown; instead, the robot maintains a **belief distribution** $b(x)$ over possible world states.

Mathematically, the planning problem becomes:

$$\pi^* = \arg\max_{\pi} \; \mathbb{E}_{b_0, \; z, \; w} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^{t} \, r(s_t, a_t) \right]$$

subject to the belief dynamics governed by sensing and motion models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis (2024 Scheme):** The keyword that examiners expect is **"Belief-Space Planning"** — the planner reasons over a *distribution* of states, not a single deterministic configuration. A static world with known geometry is a **certainty-equivalent (CE)** problem, which is *not* what this module addresses.

### 1.2 Sources of Environmental Uncertainty

| Source | Description | Typical Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensor Noise** | Limited resolution, range, FoV of LIDAR / Camera | Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(z; h(x), R)$ |
| **Localization Drift** | Wheel slip, IMU bias, odometry errors | Additive Gaussian on $(x, y, \theta)$ |
| **Map Imperfection** | Pre-built maps may be outdated or coarse | Discrete grid probability cells |
| **Occlusion** | Hidden regions behind obstacles | Unknown occupancy prior |
| **Actuator Stochasticity** | Control commands not perfectly executed | Motion model with noise covariance $Q$ |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Dim Flashlight in a Familiar House"

Imagine walking through your own house **at midnight with a weak flashlight**:
- The house itself is **static** — the sofa has not moved, the doors remain where they always are.
- However, you are **uncertain** about the exact positions of furniture beyond your immediate cone of light.
- You cannot see corners, you may misjudge a step, and you must maintain a **mental belief** ("I am 80% sure the coffee table is here, but maybe it is 30 cm to the left").
- You must plan a path that is **robust to this belief**, not merely to the most-likely map.

> [!NOTE]
> This analogy maps directly to the technical problem: the agent must plan trajectories that account for the **entire belief distribution**, not just the maximum-likelihood world hypothesis.

### 1.4 Categorization of Planning Under Uncertainty

```
Planning Problem Taxonomy
├── Certainty-Equivalent (CE)   → Static Known Environment  [Module 1/2]
├── Static Uncertain            → THIS TOPIC                [Module 3]
├── Dynamic Certain             → Moving obstacles, known map
└── Dynamic Uncertain           → POMDP full generality
```

The Static Uncertain case simplifies the problem because **the world does not change**; all uncertainty is due to the robot's limited knowledge, not adversarial or moving entities. This permits the use of techniques that would be intractable in fully dynamic settings.

> [!TIP]
> **Key insight for exams:** Because the world is static, the robot can **gather information** by re-observing the same area. This is why **information-gathering actions** (e.g., going to a vantage point) are first-class primitives in static uncertain planning, unlike in dynamic settings where waiting is costly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Belief State — The Robot's True State

Since the actual configuration $x$ is unknown, the robot's epistemic state is the **belief** $b$ — a probability density over the state space $\mathcal{X}$.

$$b : \mathcal{X} \to [0, 1], \quad \int_{x \in \mathcal{X}} b(x) \, dx = 1$$

For a unimodal Gaussian belief (e.g., from an EKF-based localization filter):

$$b(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi)^{n} \det(\Sigma)}} \exp\!\left( -\tfrac{1}{2} (x - \mu)^{\mathsf{T}} \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu) \right)$$

where:
- $\mu$ is the mean (best estimate of position)
- $\Sigma$ is the covariance matrix quantifying uncertainty
- $n = \dim(\mathcal{X})$, typically **2** (planar: $x, y$) or **3** (with heading $\theta$)

### 2.2 The Two Fundamental Operations

#### 2.2.1 Motion (Prediction / Belief Propagation)

Given current belief $b_t(x)$ and control $u_t$, predict the next belief **without** using a new measurement:

$$\bar{b}_{t+1}(x') = \int_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x' \mid x, u_t) \, b_t(x) \, dx$$

The motion model $P(x' \mid x, u_t)$ is typically Gaussian:

$$x' = f(x, u_t) + w_t, \quad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$$

#### 2.2.2 Sensing (Measurement Update / Correction)

Given predicted belief $\bar{b}_{t+1}$ and observation $z_{t+1}$, refine the belief:

$$b_{t+1}(x') = \eta \, P(z_{t+1} \mid x') \, \bar{b}_{t+1}(x')$$

where $\eta$ is the normalizer ensuring $\int b_{t+1} = 1$:

$$\eta = \left[ \int_{x'} P(z_{t+1} \mid x') \, \bar{b}_{t+1}(x') \, dx' \right]^{-1}$$

### 2.3 The Bayes Filter Recursion (Combined)

$$\boxed{\, b_{t+1}(x') = \eta \, P(z_{t+1} \mid x') \int_{x} P(x' \mid x, u_t) \, b_t(x) \, dx \,}$$

This single equation is the **backbone** of all probabilistic robot reasoning under uncertainty. Specific instantiations include the Kalman Filter, Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), Particle Filter, and Histogram Filter.

### 2.4 The Planning Objective in Belief Space

The robot seeks a policy $\pi : \mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{A}$ maximizing expected cumulative reward over the belief space $\mathcal{B}$:

$$V^{\pi}(b) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^{t} \, r(b_t, a_t) \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Belst-space MDP (BMDP)** or equivalently a **POMDP** with the additional structure that the world is static. The state space for planning becomes the belief simplex, which is **continuous and high-dimensional**, making exact solution intractable — hence approximate methods are employed in practice.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Belief definition | $\int_{\mathcal{X}} b(x)\, dx = 1$ | Defines the robot's epistemic state |
| 2 | Bayes Filter | $b' = \eta \, P(z\mid x') \int P(x'\mid x,u) b(x)\, dx$ | Combined prediction + correction |
| 3 | Gaussian Belief | $b(x) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$ | Closed-form EKF solution |
| 4 | EKF Covariance Prop. | $\Sigma' = F \Sigma F^{\mathsf{T}} + Q$ | Linearized uncertainty growth |
| 5 | EKF Gain | $K = \Sigma' H^{\mathsf{T}} (H \Sigma' H^{\mathsf{T}} + R)^{-1}$ | Optimal Kalman weighting |
| 6 | Updated Covariance | $\Sigma_{\text{upd}} = (I - KH)\Sigma'$ | Posterior uncertainty |
| 7 | Occupancy Grid Update | $l_{t}(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + \text{inv\_sensor\_model}(z_t, m_i)$ | Log-odds map representation |
| 8 | Occupancy Probability | $p(m_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{l(m_i)}}$ | Convert log-odds to probability |
| 9 | Chance Constraint | $\Pr[\, \text{collision}(x)\, ] \leq \delta$ | Probabilistic safety guarantee |
| 10 | Linearized Collision | $\Pr[\, a^{\mathsf{T}} x \leq b\, ] \leq \delta$ | Tractable under Gaussian belief |
| 11 | Information Gain (KL) | $I = D_{\text{KL}}(b' \,\vert\vert\, b)$ | Quantifies sensing value |
| 12 | Mutual Information | $I(Z; X \mid b) = H(X \mid b) - \mathbb{E}_z[H(X \mid b, z)]$ | Next-Best-View planning |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never use the vertical pipe** `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` in a markdown table cell for absolute value — the parser will break the row. Use `\lvert x \rvert` outside tables, or just write the absolute value as `abs(x)` inside a table cell.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Autonomous vehicles** operating in GPS-denied tunnels with imperfect HD maps.
- **Search and rescue robots** exploring collapsed buildings with unknown debris layout.
- **Planetary rovers** (e.g., NASA's Perseverance) traversing unmapped Martian terrain.
- **Warehouse AMRs** that must robustly navigate when fiducial markers are partially occluded.
- **Underwater AUVs** with low-bandwidth, noisy sonar returns in a stable (static) seafloor map.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — The Bayes Filter Belief Update

**Goal:** Starting from first principles of probability, derive the recursive belief update rule used universally in probabilistic robotics.

**Step 1 — Apply the Law of Total Probability over the previous state $x$.**

The posterior belief $b_{t+1}(x')$ requires marginalizing over all possible previous states that could have led to $x'$ via the control $u_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{t+1}(x') &= P(x_{t+1} = x' \mid z_{t+1}, u_t, b_t) \\
&= \int_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x_{t+1} = x' \mid z_{t+1}, u_t, x_t = x) \, P(x_t = x \mid b_t) \, dx \\
&= \int_{x} P(x' \mid x, u_t, z_{t+1}) \, b_t(x) \, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Markov Assumption.**

Since $x_{t+1}$ is conditionally independent of $z_{t+1}$ given $x_t$ (the future state depends on the past state, not the new measurement directly):

$$P(x' \mid x, u_t, z_{t+1}) = P(x' \mid x, u_t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Rule in the measurement model.**

The measurement $z_{t+1}$ is conditionally independent of $u_t$ and $b_t$ given $x'$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(x' \mid z_{t+1}, u_t, b_t) &= \frac{P(z_{t+1} \mid x', u_t, b_t) \, P(x' \mid u_t, b_t)}{P(z_{t+1} \mid u_t, b_t)} \\
&= \eta \, P(z_{t+1} \mid x') \int_x P(x' \mid x, u_t) \, b_t(x) \, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

where the normalizer $\eta = P(z_{t+1} \mid u_t, b_t)^{-1}$ ensures the belief integrates to unity.

**Step 4 — Final Compact Form.**

$$
\boxed{\, b_{t+1}(x') = \eta \, P(z_{t+1} \mid x') \int_{x} P(x' \mid x, u_t) \, b_t(x) \, dx \,}
$$

This is the **continuous Bayes filter** — a master equation from which the Kalman filter, EKF, particle filter, and occupancy grid filter all descend as special cases.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — EKF Covariance Propagation (Linearized Case)

**Goal:** Show how uncertainty $\Sigma$ evolves through a nonlinear motion model $x' = f(x, u)$.

**Step 1 — First-order Taylor expansion of $f$ around the current mean $\mu$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &\approx f(\mu, u) + F \, (x - \mu) + w \\
F &= \left. \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} \right|_{x = \mu}
\end{aligned}
$$

$F$ is the **Jacobian** of the motion model, an $n \times n$ matrix.

**Step 2 — Compute the new mean.**

Since the process noise $w$ is zero-mean:

$$\mu' = \mathbb{E}[x'] = f(\mu, u)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the new covariance using the linear combination of independent Gaussians rule.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma' &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[ (x' - \mu')(x' - \mu')^{\mathsf{T}} \right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[ (F(x - \mu) + w)(F(x - \mu) + w)^{\mathsf{T}} \right] \\
&= F \, \mathbb{E}[(x-\mu)(x-\mu)^{\mathsf{T}}] \, F^{\mathsf{T}} + \mathbb{E}[w w^{\mathsf{T}}] + \text{cross terms} \\
&= F \Sigma F^{\mathsf{T}} + Q
\end{aligned}
$$

The cross terms vanish because $w$ and $x$ are assumed independent.

$$
\boxed{\, \Sigma_{t+1} = F_t \, \Sigma_t \, F_t^{\mathsf{T}} + Q_t \,}
$$

**Step 4 — Quantitative example: uncertainty ellipse growth.**

For a 2D robot with $\Sigma_0 = \text{diag}(0.1, 0.1)$ and linear motion Jacobian $F = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ with $Q = \text{diag}(0.05, 0.05)$:

$$
\Sigma_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.05 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.05 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.15 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.15 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Uncertainty grows monotonically with motion — this is why **sensing steps** are essential to keep the belief bounded.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Chance-Constrained Planning Formulation

**Goal:** Convert a probabilistic safety requirement into a tractable deterministic constraint under a Gaussian belief.

**Step 1 — Define the safety event.**

The robot is safe if it does not collide with the closest obstacle. Let $g(x) \geq 0$ denote the clearance function:

$$g(x) = \min_{o \in \text{Obstacles}} \text{dist}(x, o) - r_{\text{robot}}$$

**Step 2 — State the chance constraint.**

$$P_{x \sim b(\mu, \Sigma)}\!\left[\, g(x) < 0 \,\right] \leq \delta$$

where $\delta \in (0, 1)$ is a user-specified risk tolerance (e.g., $\delta = 0.001$ for 99.9% safety).

**Step 3 — Linearize $g$ around the mean $\mu$ for tractability.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(x) &\approx g(\mu) + \nabla g(\mu)^{\mathsf{T}} (x - \mu) \\
     &= a^{\mathsf{T}} (x - \mu) + g(\mu)
\end{aligned}
$$

with $a = \nabla g(\mu)$.

**Step 4 — Substitute and apply Gaussian tail bounds.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P\!\left[\, g(x) < 0 \,\right] &= P\!\left[\, a^{\mathsf{T}}(x-\mu) < -g(\mu) \,\right] \\
&\leq \delta
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $a^{\mathsf{T}}(x - \mu) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, a^{\mathsf{T}} \Sigma a)$, we standardize using $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P\!\left[\, a^{\mathsf{T}}(x-\mu) < -g(\mu) \,\right] &= P\!\left[\, Z < \frac{-g(\mu)}{\sqrt{a^{\mathsf{T}} \Sigma a}} \,\right] \\
&= \Phi\!\left( \frac{-g(\mu)}{\sqrt{a^{\mathsf{T}} \Sigma a}} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Use the inverse CDF $\Phi^{-1}$ to obtain a closed-form constraint.**

$$
\boxed{\, g(\mu) \geq \Phi^{-1}(1 - \delta) \, \sqrt{a^{\mathsf{T}} \Sigma a} \,}
$$

The right-hand side is a **risk margin** that grows with the robot's positional uncertainty $\Sigma$ and the sensitivity of the constraint $a$. This converts a probabilistic safety requirement into a deterministic geometric margin in the planner's nominal trajectory optimization.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** This is the bridge that lets classical trajectory optimizers (MPC, CHOMP, TrajOpt) **incorporate uncertainty** without resorting to full POMDP solvers. It is a high-frequency KTU exam question because it elegantly combines probability, linearization, and control theory.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Planning Approaches

| Approach | State Representation | Computational Cost | Optimality | Handles Sensing? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Certainty-Equivalent (CE)** | Single $x$ | Lowest | Suboptimal | No |
| **Robust Planning** | Single $x$, worst-case $\Sigma$ | Low | Conservative but safe | No (reactive) |
| **Chance-Constrained** | Mean $\mu$ + ellipsoid $\Sigma$ | Medium | Risk-bounded optimal | Partially (via $\delta$) |
| **Conformant** | Belief $b$, no observations during plan | High | Safe regardless of $z$ | No (open-loop) |
| **Contingent / Belief-Space** | Full belief tree | Highest | Optimal w.r.t. information | **Yes** (branch on $z$) |
| **POMDP (General)** | Full belief $b$ | Intractable exact | Theoretically optimal | Yes (general) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Overall Architecture — Planning Pipeline in Static Uncertain Environments

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Initial Belief b0 over robot pose and map] --> A1[Sensor Scan z_t]
    A1 --> A2[Bayes Filter Belief Update]
    A2 --> A3[Updated Belief b_t]
    A3 --> A4{Uncertainty within budget?}
    A4 -- No --> A5[Re-plan with new belief]
    A4 -- Yes --> A6[Compute Next Action a_t]
    A6 --> A7[Execute Motion u_t with noise w_t]
    A7 --> A8[Predict Next Belief via motion model]
    A8 --> A1
    A5 --> A6
```

**Reading the diagram:** The loop on the right is the standard **perception-action cycle** operating continuously. The left branch re-plans whenever uncertainty exceeds a safety budget — this is the hallmark of **chance-constrained** or **belief-space replanning** architectures.

---

### 4.2 Belief Update Cycle — Prediction and Correction Decomposed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Previous Belief b_t] --> B2[Apply Control u_t]
    B2 --> B3[Convolve with Motion Model P x prime given x u]
    B3 --> B4[Predicted Belief bar b_t+1]
    B4 --> B5[Acquire Measurement z_t+1]
    B5 --> B6[Multiply by Sensor Model P z given x prime]
    B6 --> B7[Normalize eta to integrate to 1]
    B7 --> B8[Posterior Belief b_t+1]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The "**Convolve**" step corresponds to the integral $\int P(x' \mid x, u) b_t(x) dx$ (motion update), while the "**Multiply + Normalize**" sequence corresponds to the Bayes-rule correction. This is the unified perception loop.

---

### 4.3 Planning Hierarchy — From Reactive to Deliberative

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Static Uncertain Planning] --> C2{Can plan react to z online?}
    C2 -- No --> C3[Conformant Plan: open loop sequence of actions robust to all z]
    C2 -- Yes --> C4{Reason about information value?}
    C4 -- No --> C5[Contingent Plan: branching policy on z]
    C4 -- Yes --> C6[Information Gathering: add next-best-view actions]
    C3 --> C7[Single Plan Output]
    C5 --> C7
    C6 --> C8[Augmented Plan with Sensing Waypoints]
```

**Reading the diagram:** As you move downward, the planner becomes more sophisticated and computationally expensive. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognize that **conformant** planning sacrifices optimality for simplicity, while **contingent** and **information-gathering** planners exploit the static nature of the world to actively reduce uncertainty.

---

### 4.4 Occupancy Grid Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Raw Sensor Scan z_t] --> D2[Inverse Sensor Model]
    D2 --> D3[Log Odds Increment for each grid cell m_i]
    D3 --> D4[Log Odds Map L_t]
    D4 --> D5[Probabilistic Map via sigmoid]
    D5 --> D6[Belief over World Occupancy]
    D4 --> D7[Planner reads probability of occupancy]
    D7 --> D8[Risk Aware Path]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each grid cell $m_i$ stores a log-odds value $l(m_i) = \log \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}$. The update is **incremental and additive** — a computationally elegant property that makes occupancy grids practical for real-time use.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**(3 Marks)** *Define a "Static Uncertain Environment" in the context of robot navigation. List any three sources of uncertainty the robot may face in such a setting.*

**Model Answer:**

A *Static Uncertain Environment* is a workspace in which the world's geometry and obstacle configuration are **time-invariant (static)**, but the robot's knowledge of that world is **incomplete or noisy (uncertain)**. The robot does not know its true state $x$ with certainty and must instead reason over a belief distribution $b(x)$.

Three primary sources of uncertainty are:

1. **Sensor noise** — physical sensors (LIDAR, camera, IMU) return measurements corrupted by Gaussian or non-Gaussian noise modeled as $P(z \mid x)$.
2. **Actuator stochasticity** — commanded motions $u$ are not executed perfectly; the actual motion follows a probabilistic model $P(x' \mid x, u)$ with covariance $Q$.
3. **Map / localization error** — the robot's estimate of its own pose drifts, producing a covariance $\Sigma$ that grows without corrective sensing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is to write "**dynamic environment**" or "**unknown environment**" as the definition. The KTU board requires the explicit pairing of "**static**" (world) and "**uncertain**" (knowledge). Marks are deducted if these two qualifiers are not clearly separated.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**(3 Marks)** *Distinguish between **Conformant Planning** and **Contingent Planning** for a robot operating in a static uncertain environment. State one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Conformant Planning | Contingent Planning |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing assumption** | Plan must succeed for **all** possible observations | Plan explicitly **branches** on observations $z$ |
| **Plan structure** | A single fixed action sequence $\langle a_1, a_2, \dots, a_T \rangle$ | A policy tree / conditional branches |
| **Information use** | None during execution | Online replanning at branching points |

- **Advantage of Conformant:** Simplicity — easy to compute and verify; works without any real-time sensing.
- **Advantage of Contingent:** Higher expected performance — the robot can adapt its behavior to what it actually sees, exploiting the static nature of the world to reduce uncertainty.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse *contingent* with *dynamic* replanning. **Contingent plans are computed offline but executed with online branching on $z$**; the world itself does not change. Writing "reactive" alone is insufficient — emphasize the **explicit policy structure**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A [14 Marks] — Belief-Space Formulation and Bayes Filter**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) framework as applied to a robot navigating in a static uncertain environment. Clearly define the belief state and justify why planning must occur in belief space rather than configuration space.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Starting from the definition of belief, derive the Bayes filter belief update equation. Identify the two constituent sub-steps and the role of the normalizer $\eta$.*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

A **POMDP** is a tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{Z}, T, O, R, \gamma)$ where:

- $\mathcal{S}$: state space (robot pose + environment state)
- $\mathcal{A}$: action space (control commands)
- $\mathcal{Z}$: observation space (sensor readings)
- $T(s' \mid s, a) = P(s' \mid s, a)$: transition model
- $O(z \mid s', a) = P(z \mid s', a)$: observation model
- $R(s, a)$: reward function
- $\gamma$: discount factor

Because the true state $s$ is **not directly observable**, the robot maintains a **belief** $b$ — a sufficient statistic of the entire action-observation history:

$$b_t(s) = P(s_t = s \mid a_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t})$$

**Justification for belief-space planning:**

1. **Sufficiency:** The belief $b$ is a sufficient statistic — the optimal future action depends only on $b$, not on the raw history.
2. **Safety guarantee:** Planning in belief space allows the robot to evaluate the **probability of collision** under its current uncertainty, not just the collision in the most-likely configuration.
3. **Exploitation of static structure:** Since the world is static, repeated observations of the same region **reduce** the belief entropy $H(b)$, enabling information-gathering maneuvers that would be wasteful in a dynamic world.

The planning objective is to find the policy $\pi^*: \mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{A}$ maximizing:

$$V^{\pi^*}(b_0) = \max_{\pi} \; \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^t r(s_t, a_t) \,\Big\vert\, b_0, \pi \right]$$

The complexity of exact POMDP solution is PSPACE-complete in $|T|$, motivating approximate methods (QMDP, SARSOP, Monte-Carlo Tree Search in belief space).

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

The derivation proceeds in two sub-steps — **prediction** (motion update) and **correction** (measurement update).

**Sub-step 1: Prediction** — Marginalize over previous states:

$$\bar{b}_{t+1}(x') = \int_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x' \mid x, u_t) \, b_t(x) \, dx$$

This convolves the current belief with the motion model, producing a "spread out" prior for the next step.

**Sub-step 2: Correction** — Apply Bayes' rule on the new measurement $z_{t+1}$:

$$b_{t+1}(x') = \eta \, P(z_{t+1} \mid x') \, \bar{b}_{t+1}(x')$$

**Role of the normalizer $\eta$:** It is the reciprocal of the total probability of observing $z_{t+1}$:

$$\eta = \left[ \int_{x'} P(z_{t+1} \mid x') \, \bar{b}_{t+1}(x') \, dx' \right]^{-1}$$

ensuring that the posterior belief is a **valid probability density**, i.e., $\int b_{t+1}(x')\, dx' = 1$.

**Combined Recursion:**

$$\boxed{\, b_{t+1}(x') = \eta \, P(z_{t+1} \mid x') \int_{x} P(x' \mid x, u_t) \, b_t(x) \, dx \,}$$

**Instantiations:**
- Gaussian $P$ and linear models → **Kalman Filter**
- Nonlinear models with linearization → **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**
- Multi-modal beliefs → **Particle Filter** (Monte Carlo approximation)
- Discrete grid cells → **Histogram / Occupancy Grid Filter**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write the formula but forget to **explicitly state that $\eta$ ensures the result is a valid probability density**. This single line carries 1 mark and is a frequent deduction point.

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] — Occupancy Grids and Chance-Constrained Planning**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the Occupancy Grid representation for a static uncertain environment. Derive the log-odds update rule for a single grid cell and state its key computational advantage.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Formulate the chance-constrained safe-planning problem. Linearize the constraint and derive the closed-form safety margin under a Gaussian belief.*

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

The **Occupancy Grid** discretizes the workspace into a uniform array of cells $m_i$, each storing the probability that the cell is occupied by an obstacle. Let $p(m_i) \in [0, 1]$ denote this probability.

**Log-odds representation:** To enable efficient updates, define:

$$l(m_i) = \log \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}$$

The inverse transform recovers the probability:

$$p(m_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{l(m_i)}} = \frac{e^{l(m_i)}}{1 + e^{l(m_i)}}$$

**Update rule:** For each cell within the sensor's field of view, given measurement $z_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_t(m_i) &= l_{t-1}(m_i) + \text{inv\_sensor\_model}(m_i, z_t) - l_0 \\
\text{or simply: } \quad l_t(m_i) &= l_{t-1}(m_i) + \Delta l(m_i, z_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $l_0$ is the prior log-odds and $\Delta l$ is the per-cell evidence contribution from the inverse sensor model.

**Computational Advantage:** The update is **additive and independent per cell**, allowing massive parallelism (GPU/SIMD implementations) and constant-time per-cell updates regardless of map size. No normalization across cells is required during the update — the cell-wise probability remains valid by definition of the log-odds form.

**Static environment usage:** Since the world is static, repeated updates to the same cell from multiple viewpoints monotonically drive $l(m_i)$ toward $\pm \infty$, representing confident "free" or "occupied" beliefs.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Formulation:** Given belief $b = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$ over the robot's pose and a known obstacle set, find a control sequence $u_{0:T-1}$ minimizing expected cost such that:

$$P_{x \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)}\!\left[\, g(x) < 0 \,\right] \leq \delta$$

where $g(x)$ is the clearance (signed distance to nearest obstacle) and $\delta$ is the allowable collision probability.

**Step 1 — Linearize the constraint:**

Let $a = \nabla g(\mu)$. Then $g(x) \approx g(\mu) + a^{\mathsf{T}}(x - \mu)$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the chance constraint:**

$$P\!\left[\, a^{\mathsf{T}}(x - \mu) + g(\mu) < 0 \,\right] \leq \delta$$

**Step 3 — Standardize using the Gaussian variance $\sigma^2 = a^{\mathsf{T}} \Sigma a$:**

$$P\!\left[\, Z < \frac{-g(\mu)}{\sqrt{a^{\mathsf{T}} \Sigma a}} \,\right] \leq \delta, \quad Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the inverse CDF $\Phi^{-1}$:**

$$\frac{-g(\mu)}{\sqrt{a^{\mathsf{T}} \Sigma a}} \leq \Phi^{-1}(\delta)$$

which rearranges to the **closed-form safety margin**:

$$\boxed{\, g(\mu) \geq \Phi^{-1}(\delta) \, \sqrt{a^{\mathsf{T}} \Sigma a} \,}$$

**Interpretation:**

- The right-hand side is a **risk-scaled distance** the robot must maintain from obstacles in its *mean* trajectory.
- The margin grows with $\sqrt{\Sigma}$ — **higher uncertainty → larger required clearance**.
- Choosing $\delta = 0.001$ gives $\Phi^{-1}(\delta) \approx -3.09$, meaning the robot must stay at least $3.09 \sigma$ away from any obstacle in its uncertainty direction.
- This linearized form is **convex** and can be plugged into any standard trajectory optimizer (MPC, QP, SOCP).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A very common error is writing the standardized form as $\Phi\!\left(\frac{g(\mu)}{\sqrt{a^{\mathsf{T}}\Sigma a}}\right) \leq \delta$ — the sign in the numerator is **inverted** because the event is $g(x) < 0$, not $g(x) > 0$. Double-check the inequality direction before finalizing your answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 12 things you MUST remember for the KTU Exam on this topic:**

- [ ] **Static Uncertain = static world + robot's incomplete knowledge.** Two distinct qualifiers, not one.
- [ ] **Belief $b(x)$** is the *true* state of the robot's knowledge — not a single configuration $x$.
- [ ] The **Bayes Filter** is the master equation: $b' = \eta P(z \mid x') \int P(x' \mid x, u) b(x) dx$.
- [ ] The **prediction step** uses the motion model; the **correction step** uses the sensor model.
- [ ] The **normalizer $\eta$** is what makes the posterior a valid probability density.
- [ ] For Gaussian beliefs, the **EKF covariance update** is $\Sigma' = F \Sigma F^{\mathsf{T}} + Q$.
- [ ] The **Kalman gain** $K$ optimally weighs prediction vs. measurement covariances.
- [ ] **Occupancy grids** store log-odds; updates are **additive**, enabling real-time performance.
- [ ] **Chance-constrained planning** converts probabilistic safety to a geometric margin.
- [ ] The safety margin scales as $\Phi^{-1}(\delta) \sqrt{a^{\mathsf{T}} \Sigma a}$ — larger uncertainty → more clearance.
- [ ] **Conformant** plans ignore $z$; **contingent** plans branch on $z$; the latter is strictly more powerful in static worlds.
- [ ] **Information gain** (KL divergence $D_{\text{KL}}(b' \mid\mid b)$) is the value of sensing — re-observing is free in a static world.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation Function and Potential Fields in Dynamic Environment - Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Navigation Function and Potential Fields in Dynamic Environment - Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Navigation Function (NF)

A **Navigation Function** is a real-valued, sufficiently smooth, scalar function $\varphi(q)$ defined on a compact connected Riemannian manifold (the free space $F$), such that:
- It has exactly one minimum (the goal configuration $q_{goal}$).
- It has no local minima elsewhere.
- Its negative gradient field is a valid controller that steers the robot from any initial configuration to the goal while avoiding all obstacles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Koditschek & Rimon, 1990):** A map $\varphi : F \to [0, 1]$ is a Navigation Function if it is:
> 1. **Smooth** (at least $C^2$)
> 2. **Polar** at $q_{goal}$ (level sets are diffeomorphic to spheres)
> 3. **Admissible** on the boundary $\partial F$ (equals 1 on $\partial F$ and 0 at $q_{goal}$)
> 4. **Morse** (all critical points are non-degenerate)

The canonical sphere-world NF is:

$$
\varphi(q) = \frac{\vert q - q_{goal} \vert^2}{\left( \vert q - q_{goal} \vert^{2k} + \beta(q) \right)^{1/k}}
$$

where $k > 0$ is a tuning parameter and $\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \beta_i(q)$ with each $\beta_i(q)$ being the obstacle function for the $i^{th}$ spherical obstacle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine standing blindfolded in a hilly garden. Your goal is the deepest valley. A Navigation Function is the mathematical "elevation map" given to you — one global minimum (the goal), no deceptive local valleys, and slopes that always point downhill to safety. The blindfold is the robot's lack of global knowledge — it just follows the local gradient.

## 1.2 Artificial Potential Fields (APF)

An **Artificial Potential Field** is a scalar field $U(q)$ constructed by **superposition** of:
- An **attractive** component pulling the robot toward the goal.
- A **repulsive** component pushing the robot away from obstacles.

The robot's velocity/acceleration is commanded by the **negative gradient**:

$$
F(q) = -\nabla U(q) = F_{att}(q) + F_{rep}(q)
$$

## 1.3 Dynamic Environment & Estimation

A **Dynamic Environment** is a workspace where obstacles and/or the goal move with time-varying velocities, characterized as $W(t) = W_{static} \cup W_{dynamic}(t)$. **Estimation** is the on-line process of inferring the hidden state (position, velocity, acceleration) of these moving entities from noisy sensor measurements, typically via recursive Bayesian filters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module 3, the focus is on extending classical static NFs/PFs to **time-varying workspaces**, requiring *time-dependent potentials* and *state estimators* (Kalman / EKF / Particle Filters) for moving obstacles.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy (Dynamic Estimation):** Think of a self-driving car predicting a pedestrian's future path. The car uses a Kalman filter to "estimate" where the pedestrian's foot will be in 0.5 seconds, even though the camera gives noisy, discrete snapshots. The navigation function then re-plans the trajectory around this **predicted** position — not the current one.

## 1.4 Key Physical & Mathematical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Attractive gain | $k_{att}$ | $\mathbf{0.5}$ to $\mathbf{2.0}$ (N/m) |
| Repulsive gain | $k_{rep}$ | $\mathbf{100}$ to $\mathbf{1000}$ (N/m) |
| Distance of influence | $\rho_0$ | $\mathbf{0.5}$ to $\mathbf{2.0}$ (m) |
| NF tuning exponent | $k$ | $\mathbf{\geq 2}$ (integer) |
| Workspace dimension | $n$ | $\mathbf{2}$ or $\mathbf{3}$ |
| Process noise covariance | $Q$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ (SPD matrix) |
| Measurement noise covariance | $R$ | $\mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ (SPD matrix) |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Navigation Functions

A Navigation Function $\varphi: F \to [0, 1]$ is built on two competing functions:
- $\gamma_d(q) = \vert q - q_{goal} \vert^2$ — **goal function** (attractive component).
- $\beta_i(q) = \vert q - q_{oi} \vert^2 - r_i^2$ — **obstacle function** for spherical obstacle $i$ (center $q_{oi}$, radius $r_i$). Note: $\beta_i > 0$ in free space.

The **composite obstacle function** is $\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \beta_i(q)$, which vanishes on $\partial F$.

**Properties ensured by construction:**
- $\varphi(q_{goal}) = 0$ (global minimum)
- $\varphi(q) = 1$ on $\partial F$ (boundary)
- For large $k$, all critical points of $\varphi$ other than $q_{goal}$ are **saddle points** (the goal becomes a dominant attractor).
- $k \geq n$ (where $n$ is the dimension) guarantees "proper navigation" (Koditschek-Rimon theorem).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does $k$ matter?** As $k \to \infty$, the field becomes "stiffer" near the goal and saddle points approach the boundary. Choosing $k = n$ is the minimum valid value to ensure a Morse function with a unique minimum.

## 2.2 Artificial Potential Field Method (Khatib, 1986)

### 2.2.1 Attractive Potential
Quadratic in distance to goal (with a conical "well" at the goal to avoid vanishing force at $q_{goal}$):

$$
U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \cdot \vert q - q_{goal} \vert^2
$$

Gradient (attractive force):
$$
F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att} = -k_{att}(q - q_{goal})
$$

### 2.2.2 Repulsive Potential
Defined only within the distance of influence $\rho_0$:

$$
U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q, q_{obs})} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2 & \text{if } \rho \leq \rho_0 \\ 0 & \text{if } \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}
$$

where $\rho(q, q_{obs})$ is the minimum distance from robot to obstacle. Gradient:

$$
F_{rep}(q) = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial q} \cdot \hat{n}_{obs}
$$

where $\hat{n}_{obs}$ is the unit normal pointing from obstacle to robot.

### 2.2.3 Total Field
$$
U(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} U_{rep,i}(q)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Classical Limitation:** APF suffers from **local minima** in concave-shaped obstacle configurations. Navigation Functions were invented to mathematically eliminate this problem by construction.

## 2.3 Dynamic Environment Extension

For time-varying obstacles $q_{oi}(t)$, the potentials become **time-dependent**:

$$
U(q, t) = U_{att}(q, q_{goal}(t)) + \sum_{i=1}^{m(t)} U_{rep,i}(q, q_{oi}(t))
$$

The robot's motion is governed by:

$$
\ddot{q} = -\nabla_q U(q, t) + u_{control}(t)
$$

**Velocity Obstacle (VO)** approach (Fiorini & Shiller, 1998) for dynamic obstacles with velocity $v_{obs}$:
A velocity $v_{robot}$ is *collision-avoiding* iff:

$$
v_{robot} \not\in VO = \left\{ v \;\vert\; \exists t' > 0 : v \cdot t' \in \mathcal{B}(q_{obs}, r_{obs} + r_{robot}) \right\}
$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sphere-world NF** | $\varphi(q) = \dfrac{\gamma_d(q)}{(\gamma_d^k + \beta)^{1/k}}$ | $k \in \mathbb{Z}^+$, $\gamma_d, \beta \in \mathbb{R}^+$ | Static obstacle avoidance |
| **Obstacle function** | $\beta_i(q) = \vert q - q_{oi} \vert^2 - r_i^2$ | $r_i$ in m | Defines free space |
| **Attractive potential** | $U_{att} = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \vert q - q_g \vert^2$ | $k_{att}$ in N/m | Goal seeking |
| **Repulsive potential** | $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2$ | $k_{rep}$ in N/m, $\rho_0$ in m | Obstacle avoidance |
| **Control force** | $F = -\nabla U(q, t)$ | $F$ in N | Robot acceleration |
| **Velocity Obstacle** | $VO_{A \to B} = \{ v \; \vert \; \lambda(t) \in \mathcal{B}(r_A + r_B) \}$ | $v$ in m/s | Dynamic avoidance |
| **KF Predict** | $\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1 \vert k-1} + B u_k$ | State $\in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Obstacle state est. |
| **KF Update** | $\hat{x}_{k \vert k} = \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1})$ | Innovation $z - H\hat{x}$ | Sensor fusion |
| **Kalman Gain** | $K_k = P_{k \vert k-1} H^T (H P_{k \vert k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$ | $K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ | Optimal weighting |
| **EKF Linearization** | $A_k = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} \Big\vert_{\hat{x}_{k-1}, u_k}$ | Jacobian $\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | Non-linear systems |
| **Process noise cov. update** | $P_{k \vert k-1} = A P_{k-1 \vert k-1} A^T + Q$ | $P, Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | Uncertainty growth |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Navigation Functions are deployed in **warehouse AGVs** (Amazon Kiva bots), **surgical robots** (da Vinci) for organ avoidance, and **Mars rovers** (Curiosity's AutoNav). Dynamic versions with EKF are core to **self-driving cars** (Mobileye, Waymo) and **UAV swarms** for traffic deconfliction.

## 2.5 Estimation in Dynamic Environments — Kalman Filter Theory

The **state-space model** for a moving obstacle:
$$
x_k = f(x_{k-1}, u_k) + w_k \quad \text{(process)} \qquad z_k = h(x_k) + v_k \quad \text{(measurement)}
$$
where $w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$, $v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$.

The recursive Bayesian filter is approximated by the **Kalman Filter** (linear case) or **EKF** (non-linear). Key steps:

**Step 1 — Predict (Time Update):**
$$
\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} = f(\hat{x}_{k-1 \vert k-1}, u_k)
$$
$$
P_{k \vert k-1} = A_k P_{k-1 \vert k-1} A_k^T + Q_k
$$

**Step 2 — Update (Measurement Update):**
$$
K_k = P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k \vert k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}
$$
$$
\hat{x}_{k \vert k} = \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - h(\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}))
$$
$$
P_{k \vert k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k \vert k-1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why EKF for dynamic obstacles?** Obstacle trajectories (e.g., pedestrians, vehicles) follow *non-linear* motion models (turning, acceleration). The EKF linearizes $f$ and $h$ via Jacobians at each step, making the otherwise intractable non-linear filter tractable in real-time.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Koditschek-Rimon Sphere-World Navigation Function

**Goal:** Derive $\varphi(q) = \dfrac{\gamma_d(q)^{1/k}}{(\gamma_d(q) + \beta(q))^{1/k}}$ and show that it has a unique minimum at $q_{goal}$.

### Step 1: Define the Goal Function
A polar function with minimum at $q_{goal}$:
$$
\gamma_d(q) = \vert q - q_{goal} \vert^2 = (q_x - g_x)^2 + (q_y - g_y)^2
$$
- $\gamma_d(q_{goal}) = 0$
- $\gamma_d(q) > 0$ for $q \neq q_{goal}$
- $\gamma_d$ is smooth everywhere.

### Step 2: Define the Obstacle Function
For sphere $i$ with center $q_{oi}$ and radius $r_i$:
$$
\beta_i(q) = \vert q - q_{oi} \vert^2 - r_i^2
$$
- $\beta_i(q) = 0$ on the obstacle boundary.
- $\beta_i(q) > 0$ in free space (the robot is outside the sphere).
- $\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \beta_i(q)$ vanishes on $\partial F = \bigcup_i \partial \mathcal{B}_i$.

### Step 3: Construct the Candidate NF
Following Koditschek & Rimon, the NF must satisfy $\varphi \in [0, 1]$ with $\varphi(q_{goal}) = 0$ and $\varphi \vert_{\partial F} = 1$. Consider:

$$
\varphi(q) = \frac{\gamma_d(q)}{(\gamma_d(q)^k + \beta(q))^{1/k}}
$$

**Verification of Boundary Conditions:**
- At $q = q_{goal}$: $\gamma_d = 0$, so $\varphi = 0 / (0 + \beta)^{1/k} = 0$. ✓
- On $\partial F$: $\beta = 0$, so $\varphi = \gamma_d / \gamma_d = 1$. ✓

### Step 4: Show $\varphi \in (0, 1)$ in Free Space
For $q \in F$, $\gamma_d > 0$ and $\beta > 0$. Let $u = \gamma_d^k > 0$ and $v = \beta > 0$. Then:

$$
\varphi^k = \frac{\gamma_d^k}{\gamma_d^k + \beta} = \frac{u}{u + v}
$$

Since $u, v > 0$, we have $0 < \dfrac{u}{u+v} < 1$, so $0 < \varphi < 1$. ✓

### Step 5: Compute the Gradient (Control Law)
Taking $\nabla \varphi$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla \varphi &= \frac{\nabla \gamma_d \cdot (\gamma_d^k + \beta)^{1/k} - \gamma_d \cdot \frac{1}{k}(\gamma_d^k + \beta)^{1/k - 1}(k \gamma_d^{k-1} \nabla \gamma_d + \nabla \beta)}{(\gamma_d^k + \beta)^{2/k}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **vector field** that drives the robot. The control input is:

$$
u_{control} = -\nabla \varphi(q) \cdot K_{gain}
$$

### Step 6: Verify Unique Minimum (Sketch)
For $\varphi$ to have a critical point at $q^*$: $\nabla \varphi(q^*) = 0$. For the canonical NF with $k \geq n$, **the Hessian** $\nabla^2 \varphi$ at $q_{goal}$ is negative definite, and at all other critical points, the Hessian has both positive and negative eigenvalues (saddle points). This is the content of the **Koditschek-Rimon Theorem**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Repulsive Force in APF

**Given:** $U_{rep}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2$ for $\rho \leq \rho_0$.

**Find:** $F_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep}$.

Let $u = \dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}$, so $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} u^2$.

**Chain rule application:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla U_{rep} &= k_{rep} u \cdot \nabla u \\
\nabla u &= \nabla \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) = -\frac{1}{\rho^2} \nabla \rho
\end{aligned}
$$

For a point obstacle at $q_{obs}$, $\rho = \vert q - q_{obs} \vert$, so $\nabla \rho = \dfrac{q - q_{obs}}{\vert q - q_{obs} \vert} = \hat{n}$ (unit outward normal).

Therefore:

$$
\nabla U_{rep} = -k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \hat{n}
$$

And the **repulsive force** is:

$$
F_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep} = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \hat{n}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** $F_{rep}$ is a **vector pointing away** from the obstacle, with magnitude $\propto 1/\rho^3$ as $\rho \to 0$. This cubic blow-up guarantees $\varphi \to \infty$ at the obstacle surface (APF variant) or $\varphi = 1$ (NF variant), preventing collision.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: EKF for a Moving Obstacle

**Setup:** A pedestrian is moving with constant velocity $v$ but unknown acceleration $a$ in a corridor. State: $x = [p_x, p_y, v_x, v_y]^T$.

**Motion model (kinematic):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_x(k) &= p_x(k-1) + v_x(k-1) \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} a_x(k-1) \Delta t^2 \\
p_y(k) &= p_y(k-1) + v_y(k-1) \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} a_y(k-1) \Delta t^2 \\
v_x(k) &= v_x(k-1) + a_x(k-1) \Delta t \\
v_y(k) &= v_y(k-1) + a_y(k-1) \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**In matrix form:** $x_k = f(x_{k-1}) + w_k$, where:

$$
A = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \Delta t & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Measurement model:** A camera observes position only: $z_k = [p_x(k), p_y(k)]^T + v_k$.

$$
H = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**EKF Iterations:**
1. **Predict state:** $\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1 \vert k-1}$ (no control input)
2. **Predict covariance:** $P_{k \vert k-1} = A P_{k-1 \vert k-1} A^T + Q$
3. **Compute Kalman Gain:** $K_k = P_{k \vert k-1} H^T (H P_{k \vert k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$
4. **Update state:** $\hat{x}_{k \vert k} = \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1})$
5. **Update covariance:** $P_{k \vert k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k \vert k-1}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Step (Numerical):** The innovation term $\nu_k = z_k - H\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}$ (residual) tells the filter **how much to correct** the prediction. If $R$ (sensor noise) is small relative to $P$ (uncertainty), $K \to I$ (trust measurement). If $R$ is large, $K \to 0$ (trust prediction).

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example: 1D Obstacle Avoidance

**Problem:** Robot at $q_r = 1$ m, goal at $q_g = 5$ m, spherical obstacle at $q_o = 3$ m with $r = 0.5$ m, $\rho_0 = 1.0$ m. Compute the forces.

**Given:** $k_{att} = 1.0$, $k_{rep} = 100$.

**Solution:**

**Attractive force:**
$$
F_{att} = -k_{att}(q_r - q_g) = -1.0 \cdot (1 - 5) = +4.0 \text{ N (toward goal)}
$$

**Distance to obstacle:** $\rho = \vert 1 - 3 \vert = 2.0$ m. Since $\rho > \rho_0$:

$$
F_{rep} = 0 \text{ N}
$$

**Total force:** $F_{total} = 4.0$ N (robot moves toward goal freely).

Now suppose robot reaches $q_r = 2.4$ m. Then $\rho = \vert 2.4 - 3 \vert = 0.6$ m, and $\rho < \rho_0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{rep} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(100)\left(\tfrac{1}{0.6} - \tfrac{1}{1.0}\right)^2 = 50 \cdot (0.667)^2 = 22.22 \text{ J} \\
F_{rep} &= 100 \cdot (1.667 - 1) \cdot \dfrac{1}{0.36} \cdot \hat{n} = 100 \cdot 0.667 \cdot 2.778 \cdot (-1) = -185.2 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

($\hat{n} = -1$ since the robot is to the left of the obstacle and the force must push it further left.)

$$
F_{total} = -k_{att}(2.4 - 5) + F_{rep} = 2.6 - 185.2 = -182.6 \text{ N}
$$

The robot is **repelled strongly** and reverses direction (negative force = leftward). ✓ Collision avoided.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Navigation Function with Dynamic Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Configuration q_init] --> B[Sensor Data Acquisition]
    B --> C[Obstacle Detection Module]
    C --> D[KF or EKF State Estimator]
    D --> E[Predicted Obstacle State q_obs_tplus1]
    E --> F[Construct Time-Varying NF phi q t]
    F --> G[Compute Gradient negative grad phi]
    G --> H[Robot Velocity Controller]
    H --> I[Actuator Commands]
    I --> J[Robot Motion]
    J --> K[New Sensor Data]
    K --> B
    H --> L{Goal Reached?}
    L -- No --> F
    L -- Yes --> M[Task Complete]
```

## 4.2 Decomposition: Potential Field Components

```mermaid
graph LR
    GoalP[Attractive Potential U_att] --> SumP[Total Potential U q t]
    Obs1P[Repulsive Potential Obs 1] --> SumP
    Obs2P[Repulsive Potential Obs 2] --> SumP
    Obs3P[Repulsive Potential Obs 3] --> SumP
    SumP --> Grad[Compute Gradient F negative grad U]
    Grad --> Control[Velocity Control Input v_robot]
    GoalP -.update.-> SumP
    Obs1P -.time varying.-> SumP
    Obs2P -.time varying.-> SumP
    Obs3P -.time varying.-> SumP
```

## 4.3 Kalman Filter Cycle (Predict-Update Loop)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state Init {
        X0[Initialize x_0 and P_0]
    }
    state Predict {
        Xpred[Compute x_pred using A and B]
        Ppred[Compute P_pred using A and Q]
    }
    state Update {
        Kgain[Compute K from P_pred H and R]
        Innov[Innovation nu equals z minus H x_pred]
        Xupd[Update x to x_post x_pred plus K nu]
        Pupd[Update P to P_post]
    }
    Init --> Predict
    Predict --> Update
    Update --> Predict
    Update --> Output[Output estimated state]
```

## 4.4 Dynamic Environment State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Static Phase: Use Classical NF] --> S2{Obstacle Velocity Detected}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Activate EKF Estimator]
    S2 -- No --> S1
    S3 --> S4[Predict Future Obstacle Position]
    S4 --> S5[Recompute Time-Varying NF]
    S5 --> S6[Check Collision Probability]
    S6 -- High --> S7[Replan Trajectory via VO]
    S6 -- Low --> S8[Continue Path]
    S7 --> S4
    S8 --> S2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Note:** The interplay between **estimation** (EKF predicting obstacle states $\hat{x}_{k \vert k}$) and **planning** (NF recomputed with predicted obstacles) forms the heart of modern robot autonomy. The estimation horizon (typically **0.5 to 2.0 seconds**) determines the smoothness of the dynamic trajectory.
