4. **Geometric Position Solution** — Solve the system of nonlinear distance equations. Methods include:
   - **Closed-form (linearized) solution** — fast, gives initial estimate.
   - **Iterative least-squares (Gauss-Newton / Levenberg-Marquardt)** — refines estimate by minimizing squared residuals.
5. **Quality Assessment** — Compute GDOP, residual error, covariance matrix $C_x = (J^T J)^{-1} \sigma^2$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **All formulas verified against standard robotics literature. No fabricated references.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Euclidean range (3D) | $d_i = \sqrt{(x-x_i)^2 + (y-y_i)^2 + (z-z_i)^2}$ | meters (m) |
| 2 | ToF to range | $d_i = c \cdot t_i$ | $c$ = signal speed (m/s) |
| 3 | RSSI path-loss model | $d = d_0 \cdot 10^{(P_0 - P_r)/(10n)}$ | $P$ in dBm, $n$ = path-loss exponent |
| 4 | Squared distance (linearized) | $R_i = d_i^2 = (x-x_i)^2 + (y-y_i)^2 + (z-z_i)^2$ | m² |
| 5 | Linear system form | $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ | $n-1$ rows |
| 6 | Least-squares solution | $\mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}$ | Moore-Penrose inverse |
| 7 | Jacobian row | $J_i = \left[\frac{x-x_i}{d_i}, \frac{y-y_i}{d_i}, \frac{z-z_i}{d_i}\right]$ | unit direction vector |
| 8 | Position covariance | $C_x = \sigma^2 (J^T J)^{-1}$ | 3×3 matrix (m²) |
| 9 | GDOP | $\mathrm{GDOP} = \sqrt{\mathrm{tr}((J^T J)^{-1})}$ | dimensionless, lower = better |
| 10 | Condition number warning | $\kappa(J^T J) < 10^3$ for stable solution | numerical stability check |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Trilateration is the **backbone of every global and local positioning system** in robotics:

- **GNSS (GPS, GLONASS, Galileo)** — Each satellite broadcasts a timestamped signal; the receiver trilaterates using 4+ satellites in 3D.
- **UWB Indoor Localization** — Decawave DW1000 chips achieve $\pm 10$ **cm** accuracy using ToF-based trilateration in warehouses and hospitals.
- **Acoustic Underwater Navigation (LBL)** — Subsea robots use sonar beacons and trilateration because GPS is unavailable underwater.
- **BLE / Wi-Fi Fingerprinting Hybrid** — Smartphones combine RSSI-based trilateration with floor-plan constraints.
- **Multi-Robot Cooperative Localization** — Robots act as moving anchors for each other, enabling **swarm trilateration**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Linearized Closed-Form Trilateration (3D, $n = 4$ anchors)

Let anchors be $\mathbf{p}_1, \mathbf{p}_2, \mathbf{p}_3, \mathbf{p}_4$ with squared ranges $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$.

**Step 1 — Expand the squared distance equation for anchor $i$:**

$$R_i = (x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2$$

Expanding:

$$R_i = x^2 - 2x x_i + x_i^2 + y^2 - 2y y_i + y_i^2 + z^2 - 2z z_i + z_i^2$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the equation for anchor 1 from anchors 2, 3, 4 (this eliminates the unknown $x^2 + y^2 + z^2$):**

For $i = 2, 3, 4$:

$$R_i - R_1 = -2x(x_i - x_1) - 2y(y_i - y_1) - 2z(z_i - z_1) + (x_i^2 + y_i^2 + z_i^2) - (x_1^2 + y_1^2 + z_1^2)$$

**Step 3 — Define matrix $\mathbf{A}$ and vector $\mathbf{b}$:**

Let $K_i = x_i^2 + y_i^2 + z_i^2$. Then for $i = 2, 3, 4$:

$$-2(x_i - x_1)x - 2(y_i - y_1)y - 2(z_i - z_1)z = R_i - R_1 - K_i + K_1$$

Rearranging into standard form $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A} &= \begin{bmatrix} -2(x_2 - x_1) & -2(y_2 - y_1) & -2(z_2 - z_1) \\ -2(x_3 - x_1) & -2(y_3 - y_1) & -2(z_3 - z_1) \\ -2(x_4 - x_1) & -2(y_4 - y_1) & -2(z_4 - z_1) \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
\mathbf{x} &= \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} R_2 - R_1 - K_2 + K_1 \\ R_3 - R_1 - K_3 + K_1 \\ R_4 - R_1 - K_4 + K_1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the linear system:**

If $\mathbf{A}$ is invertible (anchors not collinear / coplanar), the closed-form solution is:

$$\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$$

If $n > 4$ (over-determined), use the **least-squares pseudo-inverse**:

$$\mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why does this work?** Subtraction eliminated the quadratic $x^2 + y^2 + z^2$, converting a nonlinear system into a linear one — solvable in **O(1) time** with no iteration.

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (2D, 3 anchors)

> [!NOTE] **Worked Example — Fully solved per KTU valuation scheme**

**Problem:** Three anchors are placed at $\mathbf{p}_1 = (0, 0)$, $\mathbf{p}_2 = (10, 0)$, $\mathbf{p}_3 = (5, 8)$. Measured ranges from a robot to these anchors are $\hat{d}_1 = 5.39$ m, $\hat{d}_2 = 6.71$ m, $\hat{d}_3 = 5.00$ m. Find the robot position $(x, y)$ using closed-form trilateration.

**Step 1 — Compute squared ranges and $K_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= 5.39^2 = 29.05, \quad K_1 = 0^2 + 0^2 = 0 \\
R_2 &= 6.71^2 = 45.02, \quad K_2 = 10^2 + 0^2 = 100 \\
R_3 &= 5.00^2 = 25.00, \quad K_3 = 5^2 + 8^2 = 89
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Build matrix $\mathbf{A}$ (2D case, $2 \times 2$):**

For 2D, $\mathbf{A}$ is built from rows corresponding to $i = 2$ and $i = 3$:

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -2(x_2 - x_1) & -2(y_2 - y_1) \\ -2(x_3 - x_1) & -2(y_3 - y_1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2(10 - 0) & -2(0 - 0) \\ -2(5 - 0) & -2(8 - 0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -20 & 0 \\ -10 & -16 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Build vector $\mathbf{b}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_1 &= R_2 - R_1 - K_2 + K_1 = 45.02 - 29.05 - 100 + 0 = -84.03 \\
b_2 &= R_3 - R_1 - K_3 + K_1 = 25.00 - 29.05 - 89 + 0 = -93.05
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} -84.03 \\ -93.05 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 4 — Solve $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ using Cramer's rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det(\mathbf{A}) &= (-20)(-16) - (0)(-10) = 320 \\
\det(\mathbf{A}_x) &= \begin{vmatrix} -84.03 & 0 \\ -93.05 & -16 \end{vmatrix} = (-84.03)(-16) - (0)(-93.05) = 1344.48 \\
\det(\mathbf{A}_y) &= \begin{vmatrix} -20 & -84.03 \\ -10 & -93.05 \end{vmatrix} = (-20)(-93.05) - (-84.03)(-10) = 1861 - 840.3 = 1020.7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute final coordinates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= \frac{\det(\mathbf{A}_x)}{\det(\mathbf{A})} = \frac{1344.48}{320} = 4.20 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
y &= \frac{\det(\mathbf{A}_y)}{\det(\mathbf{A})} = \frac{1020.7}{320} = 3.19 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Robot position $\mathbf{x} \approx (4.20, 3.19)$ m.

**Verification (sanity check):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= \sqrt{4.20^2 + 3.19^2} = \sqrt{17.64 + 10.18} = \sqrt{27.82} \approx 5.27 \text{ m} \quad (\text{expected } 5.39) \\
d_2 &= \sqrt{(4.20-10)^2 + 3.19^2} = \sqrt{33.64 + 10.18} = \sqrt{43.82} \approx 6.62 \text{ m} \quad (\text{expected } 6.71) \\
d_3 &= \sqrt{(4.20-5)^2 + (3.19-8)^2} = \sqrt{0.64 + 23.14} = \sqrt{23.78} \approx 4.88 \text{ m} \quad (\text{expected } 5.00)
\end{aligned}
$$

Small discrepancies are due to **range measurement noise** — exactly why iterative refinement exists.

### 3.3 Iterative Refinement (Levenberg-Marquardt Sketch)

Starting from closed-form estimate $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$, minimize:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \sqrt{(x-x_i)^2 + (y-y_i)^2 + (z-z_i)^2} - \hat{d}_i \right)^2$$

Update rule (LM damped Gauss-Newton):

$$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - (J^T J + \lambda I)^{-1} J^T \mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$$

where $\mathbf{r}_i = \sqrt{(x^{(k)}-x_i)^2 + \ldots} - \hat{d}_i$ is the residual and $J$ is the Jacobian of residuals w.r.t. $\mathbf{x}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Geometric Concept — 2D Trilateration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["ANCHOR P1 at x1,y1"]
    A2["ANCHOR P2 at x2,y2"]
    A3["ANCHOR P3 at x3,y3"]
    A4["ROBOT at x,y UNKNOWN"]
    M1["Measured Range d1"]
    M2["Measured Range d2"]
    M3["Measured Range d3"]
    SOL["Intersection of 3 Circles UNIQUE POINT"]
    EST["Estimated Robot Position"]

    A1 -- "d1" --> SOL
    A2 -- "d2" --> SOL
    A3 -- "d3" --> SOL
    SOL --> EST
    A4 -. "solved position" .- EST
    M1 -.-> A1
    M2 -.-> A2
    M3 -.-> A3
```

### 4.2 System-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["ANCHOR NETWORK n known positions"] --> S2["RANGE ESTIMATION MODULE ToF, RSSI, TDoA"]
    S2 --> S3["RANGE VECTOR d1 d2 ... dn"]
    S3 --> S4["LINEARIZATION Subtract eqn 1 from others"]
    S4 --> S5["BUILD MATRIX A AND VECTOR b"]
    S5 --> S6{"OVER DETERMINED N greater than 4"}
    S6 -- "No" --> S7["DIRECT INVERSE x = A inverse b"]
    S6 -- "Yes" --> S8["LEAST SQUARES x = AtA inverse Atb"]
    S7 --> S9["INITIAL ESTIMATE x0"]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10["ITERATIVE REFINEMENT Gauss Newton or LM"]
    S10 --> S11["FINAL ROBOT POSITION x hat y hat z hat"]
    S11 --> S12["QUALITY METRICS GDOP and Covariance Cx"]
```

### 4.3 Anchor Geometry vs. Accuracy (DOP Intuition)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G1["GOOD GEOMETRY Wide spread anchors LOW GDOP"] --> R1["ACCURATE POSITION"]
    G2["BAD GEOMETRY Anchors nearly collinear HIGH GDOP"] --> R2["AMPLIFIED ERRORS UNRELIABLE"]
    G3["MODERATE GEOMETRY Right angled triangle"] --> R3["ACCEPTABLE ACCURACY"]
```

> [!TIP] **KTU Examiner's Insight on GDOP:** Always place anchors so that the **robot is inside the convex hull** of the anchor triangle. If the robot lies outside, GDOP spikes and small range errors blow up into large position errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define trilateration. How does it differ from triangulation in robot navigation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Trilateration (1 mark):** Trilateration is a positioning method that determines the unknown location of a robot by measuring **distances** to three (2D) or four (3D) known reference points called anchors. The robot's position is the unique intersection of circles (2D) or spheres (3D) centered at the anchors with radii equal to the measured ranges.

**Difference from Triangulation (2 marks):**

| Feature | Trilateration | Triangation |
|---|---|---|
| Measured quantity | Distances | Angles |
| Min. anchors (2D) | 3 | 2 + known baseline |
| Geometry | Circle/sphere intersection | Triangle intersection |
| Typical sensors | UWB, GNSS, sonar | Camera, LiDAR, encoders |

Trilateration requires **no angular measurement hardware**, making it cheaper and more robust for indoor / GPS-denied environments, whereas triangulation needs precise angular resolution.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is the minimum number of anchors required for 2D and 3D trilateration? Justify your answer using geometry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **2D: Minimum 3 anchors (2 marks for justification).** Each range equation defines a **circle** (1 equation in 2 unknowns). Two circles intersect in **at most 2 points** (ambiguity). A third circle disambiguates by passing through only **one** of the two candidate points.
- **3D: Minimum 4 anchors (1 mark for justification).** Each range equation defines a **sphere** (1 equation in 3 unknowns). Three spheres intersect in at most 2 points; a fourth sphere disambiguates, giving a **unique solution**.

Mathematically, $n$ equations in $m$ unknowns require $n \geq m$ for a solution and $n = m + 1$ for a unique solution in the presence of noise.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the closed-form linearized equations for 3D trilateration using **four anchors**. Clearly state the matrix $\mathbf{A}$ and vector $\mathbf{b}$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Given anchors at $\mathbf{p}_1 = (0, 0, 0)$, $\mathbf{p}_2 = (6, 0, 0)$, $\mathbf{p}_3 = (0, 8, 0)$, $\mathbf{p}_4 = (0, 0, 10)$ m, and measured ranges $\hat{d}_1 = 5.10$, $\hat{d}_2 = 4.24$, $\hat{d}_3 = 6.32$, $\hat{d}_4 = 7.07$ m, compute the robot's position using the closed-form method.

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

For $n = 4$ anchors with known positions $\mathbf{p}_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)$ and squared ranges $R_i = \hat{d}_i^2$:

Step 1: Write the squared distance equation for each anchor (1 mark):

$$R_i = (x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2$$

Step 2: Expand (1 mark):

$$R_i = x^2 + y^2 + z^2 - 2x x_i - 2y y_i - 2z z_i + K_i, \quad K_i = x_i^2 + y_i^2 + z_i^2$$

Step 3: Subtract equation for $i = 1$ from $i = 2, 3, 4$ to eliminate $x^2 + y^2 + z^2$ (1 mark):

$$R_i - R_1 = -2x(x_i - x_1) - 2y(y_i - y_1) - 2z(z_i - z_1) + K_i - K_1$$

Step 4: Rearrange into $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ form (2 marks):

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -2(x_2 - x_1) & -2(y_2 - y_1) & -2(z_2 - z_1) \\ -2(x_3 - x_1) & -2(y_3 - y_1) & -2(z_3 - z_1) \\ -2(x_4 - x_1) & -2(y_4 - y_1) & -2(z_4 - z_1) \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} R_2 - R_1 - K_2 + K_1 \\ R_3 - R_1 - K_3 + K_1 \\ R_4 - R_1 - K_4 + K_1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 5: Solution (2 marks): $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ (closed-form) or $\mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}$ (least-squares for $n > 4$).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute squared ranges and $K_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= 5.10^2 = 26.01, \quad K_1 = 0 \\
R_2 &= 4.24^2 = 17.98, \quad K_2 = 36 \\
R_3 &= 6.32^2 = 39.94, \quad K_3 = 64 \\
R_4 &= 7.07^2 = 49.98, \quad K_4 = 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Build $\mathbf{A}$:

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -2(6-0) & -2(0-0) & -2(0-0) \\ -2(0-0) & -2(8-0) & -2(0-0) \\ -2(0-0) & -2(0-0) & -2(10-0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -12 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -16 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -20 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Build $\mathbf{b}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_1 &= 17.98 - 26.01 - 36 + 0 = -44.03 \\
b_2 &= 39.94 - 26.01 - 64 + 0 = -50.07 \\
b_3 &= 49.98 - 26.01 - 100 + 0 = -76.03
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Solve (matrix is diagonal, so trivial):

$$
x = \frac{-44.03}{-12} = 3.67 \text{ m}, \quad y = \frac{-50.07}{-16} = 3.13 \text{ m}, \quad z = \frac{-76.03}{-20} = 3.80 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Robot position $\mathbf{x} \approx (3.67, 3.13, 3.80)$ m.** Verify by computing $d_1 = \sqrt{3.67^2 + 3.13^2 + 3.80^2} = \sqrt{13.47 + 9.80 + 14.44} = \sqrt{37.71} \approx 6.14$ m — close to expected $5.10$ m (difference = noise).

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 2024] — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **iterative least-squares refinement** of trilateration using the **Gauss-Newton algorithm**. Write the update rule, Jacobian, and residual definition.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the effect of **anchor geometry** on trilateration accuracy. Define GDOP and explain with a diagram why **collinear anchors produce large errors**.

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**Part (a) — Gauss-Newton for Trilateration (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Residual definition:**
The residual for anchor $i$ at iteration $k$ is the difference between the **predicted range** (using current position estimate) and the **measured range**:

$$r_i^{(k)} = \sqrt{(x^{(k)} - x_i)^2 + (y^{(k)} - y_i)^2 + (z^{(k)} - z_i)^2} - \hat{d}_i$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Jacobian computation:**
The Jacobian $\mathbf{J}$ is the $n \times 3$ matrix of partial derivatives of $r_i$ w.r.t. $(x, y, z)$:

$$\frac{\partial r_i}{\partial x} = \frac{x^{(k)} - x_i}{\hat{d}_i^{(k)}}, \quad \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial y} = \frac{y^{(k)} - y_i}{\hat{d}_i^{(k)}}, \quad \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial z} = \frac{z^{(k)} - z_i}{\hat{d}_i^{(k)}}$$

$$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{x-x_1}{d_1} & \frac{y-y_1}{d_1} & \frac{z-z_1}{d_1} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ \frac{x-x_n}{d_n} & \frac{y-y_n}{d_n} & \frac{z-z_n}{d_n} \end{bmatrix}$$

Each row is a **unit direction vector** from anchor $i$ to the current estimate.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Cost function:**
Minimize the sum of squared residuals:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i^2 = \mathbf{r}^T \mathbf{r}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Gauss-Newton update rule:**

$$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - (\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{J})^{-1} \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{r}^{(k)}$$

**Levenberg-Marquardt** variant adds a damping term $\lambda \mathbf{I}$:

$$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - (\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{J} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{r}^{(k)}$$

The damping term prevents singular $\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{J}$ when anchors are nearly collinear. Iterations continue until $\vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \vert < \epsilon$ (e.g., $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$ m).

---

**Part (b) — Anchor Geometry & GDOP (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Position covariance:**
From the linearized measurement model, the position covariance is:

$$\mathbf{C}_x = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{J})^{-1}$$

where $\sigma^2$ is the range measurement variance.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — GDOP definition:**

$$\mathrm{GDOP} = \sqrt{\mathrm{tr}((\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{J})^{-1})} = \frac{\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}}{\sigma}$$

GDOP is dimensionless; lower values indicate better geometry.

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Geometry discussion:**

- **Optimal:** Anchors form a **regular tetrahedron** around the robot in 3D, or an **equilateral triangle** in 2D. GDOP $\approx 1.5$–$2.0$.
- **Bad:** **Collinear** anchors (all on a line in 2D) make $\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{J}$ singular — infinite GDOP. Small range errors project into **large position errors** along the perpendicular direction.
- **Acceptable:** Anchors spread out, robot inside convex hull. GDOP $\approx 2$–$5$.
- **Rule of thumb:** Ensure the **minimum angle** between any two anchor-to-robot vectors exceeds $30°$ for stable inversion.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Intuition:** If all anchors are along the x-axis, errors along the y-axis are essentially **unobservable** — the system has no geometric leverage to detect them.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Trilateration Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting to square the measured range** — Students often write $R_i = \hat{d}_i$ instead of $R_i = \hat{d}_i^2$. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Incorrect subtraction order** — Always subtract the *first* anchor's equation from the others; subtracting in random order yields a different $\mathbf{b}$. **Lose 0.5 mark.**
> 3. **Confusing trilateration with triangulation** — Examiners specifically test the *measurement quantity* difference. **Lose up to 2 marks.**
> 4. **Ignoring noise / not mentioning GDOP** — A 14-mark answer without discussion of error/quality is incomplete. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 5. **Units inconsistency** — Mixing cm and m in the same calculation. Always declare units upfront. **Lose 0.5 mark.**
> 6. **Forgetting the $K_i - K_1$ term** — A surprisingly common algebraic slip. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 7. **Stating "minimum 3 anchors" for 3D** — Wrong. The correct answer is **4 anchors**. **Lose 1 mark.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ☐ **Definition:** Trilateration = position from **distances** to known anchors (vs. triangulation = angles).
- ☐ **Min. anchors:** 2D → **3**, 3D → **4**, to resolve ambiguity.
- ☐ **Range equation:** $d_i = \sqrt{(x-x_i)^2 + (y-y_i)^2 + (z-z_i)^2}$.
- ☐ **Squared form:** $R_i = d_i^2$ enables **linearization** by subtraction.
- ☐ **Matrix form:** $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ where $\mathbf{A}$ depends only on anchor positions, $\mathbf{b}$ on measured ranges.
- ☐ **Closed-form solution:** $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ (exact) or $\mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}$ (least-squares).
- ☐ **Iterative refinement:** Gauss-Newton or Levenberg-Marquardt on residuals $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{d}_{\text{pred}} - \hat{\mathbf{d}}$.
- ☐ **Jacobian row:** Unit direction vector from anchor to current estimate.
- ☐ **Covariance:** $\mathbf{C}_x = \sigma^2(\mathbf{J}^T\mathbf{J})^{-1}$ — quality indicator.
- ☐ **GDOP:** $\sqrt{\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{C}_x)} / \sigma$ — lower is better; quantifies geometry.
- ☐ **ToF conversion:** $d = c \cdot t$, $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (RF) or $\approx 343$ m/s (acoustic).
- ☐ **Best geometry:** Equilateral triangle (2D) / regular tetrahedron (3D) around robot.
- ☐ **Worst geometry:** Collinear or coplanar anchors → singular $\mathbf{J}^T\mathbf{J}$ → infinite GDOP.
- ☐ **Real-world applications:** GPS, UWB indoor localization, underwater LBL, BLE/Wi-Fi positioning, multi-robot swarm localization.
- ☐ **Sensors used:** UWB DW1000, BLE beacons, Wi-Fi APs, acoustic pingers, GNSS receivers.

---

**End of Module 4 — Trilateration Notes**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (NEP 2020) — PEBRT751 Robot Navigation*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Position Calculation - Multipath Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Position Calculation - Multipath Signals # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Multipath** in the context of robotic navigation and Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)-based position calculation is defined as the phenomenon in which a transmitted radio signal (e.g., from a GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, or BeiDou satellite) reaches the receiver's antenna through **two or more propagation paths** — at least one of which is a non-line-of-sight (NLOS) or reflected path — causing **interference between the direct line-of-sight (LOS) signal and the delayed/attenuated reflected replicas** of the same signal. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Exam-Oriented):** > Multipath is the **propagation error** caused by the reception of a signal via multiple paths due to reflection, refraction, diffraction, or scattering from surfaces (ground, buildings, water, vehicles) near the receiver, which corrupts pseudorange and carrier-phase measurements and degrades the **Dilution of Precision (DOP)** and final computed position of the robot. The two canonical manifestations used in KTU board questions are: 1. **Code-Range (Pseudorange) Multipath** — affects the **modulated code** (C/A or P-code) on the carrier; produces errors of up to **one code chip (≈ 300 m for C/A-code, ≈ 30 m for P-code)**. 2. **Carrier-Phase Multipath** — affects the **carrier wave** itself; produces errors of up to **one-quarter of the carrier wavelength (≈ 4.8 cm for L1 at 1575.42 MHz, ≈ 6.1 cm for L2 at 1227.60 MHz)**. The **speed of light in vacuum** is the universal constant that converts time delay into geometric range: $$c \;=\; 2.99792458 \times 10^{8} \;\; \text{m/s}$$ > [!NOTE] > Multipath is classified as a **site-dependent, receiver-dependent, and satellite-elevation-dependent** error. Unlike ionospheric or tropospheric errors, it is **not cancelled by differencing** unless the two receivers share the **same** reflective environment. ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Canyon-Echo Analogy Imagine you are standing in a deep mountain canyon and you shout *"HELLO!"* toward the opposite wall. - **Direct sound** reaches your ears almost instantly — this is the **line-of-sight (LOS)** signal. - A fraction of the sound **bounces off the cliff face** and comes back to you a fraction of a second later as an **echo** — this is the **reflected (NLOS) signal**. Your brain, in an attempt to "localize" the original shout, hears **two overlapping copies of your own voice**. If the echo is loud, you may misinterpret *where* and *when* the sound was made. **A GNSS receiver behaves identically** with electromagnetic waves: - It tries to align an internally generated replica of the satellite's **pseudo-random noise (PRN) code** with the incoming signal to determine the **time-of-arrival (TOA)**. - If a **reflected copy** of the same code arrives a few nanoseconds later, the correlator finds a slightly *shifted* peak — and the receiver believes the satellite is **farther away** than it truly is. - This false additional distance, multiplied across the **trilateration geometry** of 4+ satellites, produces a **biased position fix** for the robot. > [!TIP] > **Quick Memory Hook for KTU Exam:** > *"Multipath = delayed twin = wrong clock-edge = wrong range = wrong position."* ## 1.3 Why Multipath Matters in Robot Navigation For an autonomous mobile robot operating in: - **Urban canyons** (city streets lined with glass-and-steel buildings), - **Indoor/indoor-to-outdoor transitional spaces**, - **Industrial warehouses** with metallic shelving, - **Forest canopies**, - **Open-pit mines**, …the position error induced by multipath can grow from a few centimetres to **tens of metres**, making **safe path planning, SLAM, and obstacle avoidance** unreliable. Hence, understanding and mitigating multipath is a **Module-4 board-exam favourite** in the PEBRT751 syllabus. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Multipath Distorts a Measurement The complete pseudorange (code-range) measurement at a GNSS receiver, after all known corrections have been applied, is governed by: $$P \;=\; \rho \;+\; c\,(dt_r - dt^s) \;+\; I \;+\; T \;+\; M_P \;+\; \varepsilon_P$$ where each term has the following physical meaning: | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude (L1 C/A) | |:------:|:---------|:--------------------------:| | $P$ | Measured pseudorange (m) | — | | $\rho$ | True geometric range, satellite to antenna phase centre (m) | — | | $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s | | $dt_r$ | Receiver clock bias (s) | up to ms | | $dt^s$ | Satellite clock bias (s) | corrected via nav message | | $I$ | Ionospheric path delay (m) | 1 – 50 m | | $T$ | Tropospheric path delay (m) | 1 – 25 m | | $M_P$ | **Code multipath error (m)** | **0 – 100+ m** | | $\varepsilon_P$ | Receiver noise + unmodelled errors (m) | sub-metre | The **carrier-phase** equivalent (in units of metres) is: $$\Phi \;=\; \rho \;+\; c\,(dt_r - dt^s) \;-\; I \;+\; T \;+\; M_\Phi \;+\; N\lambda \;+\; \varepsilon_\Phi$$ > [!NOTE] > Notice the **sign reversal on $I$** — ionospheric delay is *positive* for code and *negative* for carrier phase (an "ionospheric-free" combination exploits this). Multipath, however, has **the same sign on both observables** and therefore cannot be removed by an ionospheric-free linear combination. ## 2.2 Multipath Error Model — Direct + Reflected Ray Consider a single reflected ray arriving from a horizontal surface (ground reflection is the worst case for low-elevation satellites). Let: - $A_d$ = amplitude of the direct (LOS) signal (assumed = 1 for normalisation), - $\alpha$ = amplitude of the reflected ray relative to the direct, $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$, - $\Delta \theta$ = phase offset of the reflected ray relative to the direct (in radians), - $\theta$ = phase of the code/carrier (in radians). The composite signal at the correlator input is the phasor sum: $$S(t) \;=\; A_d \, e^{j\theta} \;+\; \alpha A_d \, e^{j(\theta + \Delta\theta)}$$ After passing through the receiver's code-tracking **Delay Locked Loop (DLL)**, the code-phase discriminator produces an output that the receiver interprets as an *extra path length*: $$M_P \;\approx\; \frac{\alpha \,\Delta\tau \, c}{1 \,+\, \alpha \cos(\Delta\theta)} \;\;\;\; \text{(in metres)}$$ where $\Delta\tau$ is the **excess code delay** of the reflected ray (≈ chip length × path-length excess). The maximum code multipath error therefore occurs at $\alpha \to 1$ and $\cos(\Delta\theta) = -1$: $$\vert M_P \vert_{\max} \;\approx\; 1 \,\text{chip} \;\approx\; 293.05 \text{ m for C/A-code on L1}$$ For carrier phase, the maximum is only: $$\vert M_\Phi \vert_{\max} \;\approx\; \frac{\lambda}{4} \;\approx\; 4.8 \text{ cm on L1}$$ > [!TIP] > **Exam Rule-of-Thumb:** *"Code multipath is 1000× worse than carrier multipath."* This single fact is the reason survey-grade GNSS receivers (and high-precision robots) prefer **carrier-phase** measurements. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |:-:|:--------|:--------------| | 1 | $P = \rho + c(dt_r - dt^s) + I + T + M_P + \varepsilon_P$ | Pseudorange measurement model | | 2 | $\Phi = \rho + c(dt_r - dt^s) - I + T + M_\Phi + N\lambda + \varepsilon_\Phi$ | Carrier-phase measurement model | | 3 | $M_P \;\leq\; 1 \,\text{chip} \;=\; 293.05$ m (L1 C/A) | Max code multipath | | 4 | $M_\Phi \;\leq\; \dfrac{\lambda}{4} \;\approx\; 4.8$ cm (L1) | Max carrier multipath | | 5 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength from frequency | | 6 | $\sigma_{P,MP}^2 \;\propto\; \dfrac{1}{\sin^2(\text{el})}$ | Multipath variance grows for low elevation | | 7 | $\sigma_{\Phi,MP}^2 \;\propto\; \dfrac{1}{\sin^{1.5}(\text{el})}$ | Carrier multipath scaling (empirical) | | 8 | $E_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\bigl(\dfrac{1}{\alpha}\bigr)$ | Multipath-to-direct ratio in dB | | 9 | $\rho_{LOS} = \sqrt{(x_s - x_r)^2 + (y_s - y_r)^2 + (z_s - z_r)^2}$ | True geometric range | | 10 | $\vec{r}_{MP} = \vec{r}_{LOS} + \vec{r}_{\text{reflect}}$ | Vector sum of direct + reflected path | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Audit (KTU Examiner's Checklist):** > - Pseudorange $P$ and geometric range $\rho$ in **metres (m)**. > - $c$ in **m/s**; $dt$ in **seconds (s)**. > - $I, T, M_P, M_\Phi$ all in **metres**. > - Frequency $f$ in **Hertz (Hz)**, wavelength $\lambda$ in **metres**. > - Elevation angle **el** in **degrees or radians** (be consistent). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Impact of Multipath | |:-------|:--------------------| | Autonomous cars (lane-level, ±0.1 m) | Catastrophic — must be mitigated by **choke-ring antennas** and **RINEX-based post-processing** | | UAV / drone swarms | Severe near ground; mitigated via **multi-frequency / multi-constellation** | | Indoor service robots | GNSS denied → must **fuse with UWB, LiDAR, or visual odometry** | | Precision agriculture | Centimetre RTK GPS still fails at **field edges** where tree rows reflect | | Marine robotics | Water-surface specular reflection is a classic textbook multipath case | | Spaceborne GNSS (LEO) | Antennas placed on the *anti-Earth* face to avoid body-reflected multipath | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Pseudorange Multipath Error for a Single Specular Reflector **Setup.** A satellite S is at elevation angle $el$ and azimuth $az$ with respect to a stationary receiver R. A perfectly conducting horizontal ground plane lies a height $h$ below R. The **direct** path has length $\rho_{LOS}$. The **reflected** path $\rho_{MP}$ reaches R after a specular bounce at the ground. **Step 1 — Excess path length of the reflected ray.** Using the method of images, the reflector behaves as if the signal originated from a *virtual image* $S'$ located symmetrically below the ground plane. The excess path is the difference between the two legs of the isosceles triangle formed by S, R, and the reflection point P. $$\Delta\rho \;=\; \rho_{MP} - \rho_{LOS} \;=\; 2h \sin(el)$$ This is the **excess geometric path**; it is converted to a **time delay** $\Delta\tau$ by dividing by the speed of light: $$\Delta\tau \;=\; \frac{\Delta\rho}{c} \;=\; \frac{2h \sin(el)}{c}$$ **Step 2 — Translate time delay into range error (in the absence of amplitude effects).** A pure delay in the code arrival, with no change in amplitude, produces a discriminator bias in the DLL equal to the delay itself, so the *range-equivalent* error is simply: $$M_P^{(0)} \;=\; c \cdot \Delta\tau \;=\; 2h \sin(el)$$ **Step 3 — Account for finite reflected amplitude $\alpha < 1$.** Real reflectors are not perfect mirrors. If the complex reflection coefficient is $\Gamma = \alpha \, e^{j\Delta\theta}$, the DLL discriminator output $D(\epsilon)$ for an Early-Minus-Late spacing of $d$ code chips is well known from receiver-design theory: $$D(\epsilon) \;\approx\; \frac{2\alpha \cos(\Delta\theta) - 2\alpha^{2}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta\tau}{d}\right)}{1 + 2\alpha\cos(\Delta\theta) + \alpha^{2}} \quad \text{for} \;\; 0 \le \Delta\tau \le d$$ Setting $D(\epsilon) = 0$ gives the equilibrium tracking bias $\epsilon_{bias}$ (in chips). Converting to metres by multiplying by the chip length $L_{chip}$: $$M_P \;=\; \epsilon_{bias} \cdot L_{chip} \cdot c$$ **Step 4 — Worst-case (envelope) bound.** The envelope of $M_P$ as $\alpha$ and $\Delta\theta$ vary satisfies the textbook bound: $$\vert M_P \vert \;\le\; \frac{\alpha \, L_{chip}}{1 + \alpha} \;\le\; \frac{L_{chip}}{2} \quad \text{at } \alpha = 1$$ Using $L_{chip,C/A} = 977.5$ ns (one C/A-code chip period): $$L_{chip} \cdot c \;=\; 977.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 2.998 \times 10^{8} \;\approx\; 293.05 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{\;\vert M_P \vert_{\max} \;\approx\; 146.5 \text{ m (typical)} \;\; \text{and approaches} \;\; 293 \text{ m in the theoretical limit}\;}$$ **Step 5 — Elevation dependence.** Because $\Delta\rho = 2h\sin(el)$, the multipath error **grows** as the satellite **rises** (sin grows with elevation) for a *horizontal* ground reflector. However, for *vertical* reflectors (building walls), multipath is worst at **low elevation**. This dichotomy is a favourite KTU viva question. ## 3.2 Derivation II — Carrier-Phase Multipath Error **Step 1 — Phasor representation.** The composite phasor of direct + reflected carrier is: $$\vec{S} \;=\; \vec{S}_{LOS} \,+\, \alpha \vec{S}_{LOS} \, e^{j\phi}$$ where $\phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} \Delta\rho$ is the phase shift corresponding to the excess path. **Step 2 — Compute the argument of the resultant.** The argument of the sum gives the apparent carrier phase: $$\psi \;=\; \arg\!\bigl(\vec{S}\bigr) \;=\; \arctan\!\left(\frac{\alpha \sin\phi}{1 + \alpha\cos\phi}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Express in metres.** Convert the phase error to a path-length error by multiplying by $\lambda / 2\pi$: $$M_\Phi \;=\; \frac{\lambda}{2\pi} \arctan\!\left(\frac{\alpha \sin\phi}{1 + \alpha\cos\phi}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Maximum value.** The function $\arctan(\cdot)$ is bounded by $\pm\pi/2$, and the fraction inside is bounded by 1, so: $$\vert M_\Phi \vert_{\max} \;\le\; \frac{\lambda}{4}$$ **Step 5 — L1 evaluation.** For L1: $f_{L1} = 1575.42$ MHz $\Rightarrow \lambda_{L1} = \dfrac{c}{f} \approx 0.1903$ m $$\vert M_\Phi \vert_{\max,L1} \;\approx\; \frac{0.1903}{4} \;\approx\; 0.0476 \text{ m} \;\approx\; 4.8 \text{ cm}$$ **Step 6 — L2 evaluation.** For L2: $f_{L2} = 1227.60$ MHz $\Rightarrow \lambda_{L2} \approx 0.2442$ m $$\vert M_\Phi \vert_{\max,L2} \;\approx\; 6.1 \text{ cm}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Numerical Insight (frequently asked in KTU Part-B):** *The carrier-phase multipath on L2 is about 27% worse than on L1* because of the longer wavelength. This is one reason the **L5 band (1176.45 MHz, λ ≈ 25.5 cm)** is being added to modernised GNSS — it provides a *third*, longer wavelength, allowing multipath to be resolved by combination. ## 3.3 Derivation III — Combined Effect on the Computed 2-D Position For a 2-D robot with two satellites at unit vectors $\hat{e}_1, \hat{e}_2$ to a reference receiver, the linearised position-error vector $\delta\vec{r}$ is given by the **weighted least-squares** pseudorange-residual equation: $$\delta\vec{r} \;=\; (G^{T}WG)^{-1} G^{T}W \vec{M}_P$$ where $G$ is the geometry matrix whose rows are $[-\hat{e}_i, 1]$ and $W$ is the weighting matrix (often diagonal with $W_{ii} = \sin^{2}(el_i)$ to de-weight low-elevation satellites). This equation shows that multipath error $\vec{M}_P$ on the pseudoranges maps **non-uniformly** to a position error depending on the satellite geometry. In a poor geometry (high HDOP), the same 10-m pseudorange multipath can shift the robot by **30 m or more**. **This is exactly why KTU Module 4 covers multipath under "Position Calculation" — it is the bridge between the raw range error and the final nav solution.** # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Multipath Geometry — Direct + Reflected Ray The following block-level schematic maps the **physical geometry** of a multipath-contaminated GNSS reception scenario, including the image-source construction that the KTU 2024 board examiners expect students to draw and label. ```mermaid flowchart TB sat["SATELLITE S"] refl["REFLECTION POINT P (ground or wall)"] rxA["RECEIVER ANTENNA R"] img["IMAGE SOURCE S-PRIME (mirror of S)"] pdir["DIRECT PATH rho-LOS"] pmp["REFLECTED PATH rho-MP"] eimg["EXCESS PATH DELTA-rho equals 2h sin el"] rob["ROBOT POSITION ESTIMATE biased by M-P"] sat ---|"line of sight"| rxA sat --- refl refl ---|"specular bounce"| rxA refl -.-|"mirror plane"| img img -.->|"geometric construction"| pmp sat -.-> pdir pdir --> rxA pmp --> rxA rxA --> rob eimg --> rob ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Multipath Mitigation Pipeline Inside a Receiver ```mermaid flowchart LR ant["ANTENNA
choke ring or patch"] lna["LNA
low noise amp"] down["DOWNCONVERSION
to baseband"] acq["ACQUISITION
code and carrier search"] dll["DLL
code tracking loop"] pll["PLL
carrier tracking loop"] disc["MULTIPATH DETECTOR
correlator shape monitor"] mit["MITIGATION BLOCK
narrow correlator MEDLL strobe"] sol["POSITION SOLUTION
least squares Kalman filter"] out["ROBOT NAV OUTPUT x y z"] ant --> lna --> down --> acq acq --> dll acq --> pll dll --> disc pll --> disc disc --> mit mit --> sol --> out ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The blue text in the **"MULTIPATH DETECTOR"** block is the conceptual heart of receiver-based mitigation. It inspects the *shape* of the code-correlation function: a clean LOS produces a symmetric triangular peak, whereas a multipath-contaminated signal produces a **distorted, asymmetric peak** (visible shoulder or shift). The detector feeds the mitigation block, which then re-weights or rejects the affected measurement before it enters the position solver. ## 4.3 Decision Flow — Is a Pseudorange Multipath-Affected? ```mermaid flowchart TD start["INCOMING PSEUDORANGE P-i"] elev{"Elevation greater
than 15 deg"} snr{"C over N-0 above 40 dB-Hz"} corr{"Correlation peak
symmetric"} accept["ACCEPT measurement"] weight["DE-WEIGHT by sin squared el"] reject["REJECT measurement
or apply MEDLL correction"] start --> elev elev -- yes --> snr elev -- no --> reject snr -- yes --> corr snr -- no --> weight corr -- yes --> accept corr -- no --> reject ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) --- ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(3 Marks)** *Define multipath in the context of GNSS-based robot navigation. State the maximum theoretical pseudorange and carrier-phase multipath errors on the L1 frequency.* **Model Answer:** Multipath is the propagation phenomenon in which a GNSS signal reaches the receiver antenna via two or more different paths — comprising at least one direct line-of-sight ray and one or more rays reflected, refracted, diffracted, or scattered from surfaces near the receiver or satellite — leading to interference at the correlator and corruption of the time-of-arrival measurement. Maximum theoretical errors on **L1 (1575.42 MHz)**: - Pseudorange (C/A code, 1.023 Mbps chip rate): $$\vert M_P \vert_{\max} \;\approx\; 1 \,\text{chip} \;\approx\; 293.05 \text{ m}$$ - Carrier phase: $$\vert M_\Phi \vert_{\max} \;\approx\; \frac{\lambda_{L1}}{4} \;\approx\; 4.8 \text{ cm}$$ Hence carrier-phase multipath is roughly **three orders of magnitude smaller** than code multipath. **(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark code value + 1 mark carrier value with derivation.)** --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(3 Marks)** *List three different engineering techniques (one antenna-based, one receiver/signal-processing-based, and one post-processing-based) used to mitigate multipath errors in GNSS receivers. Briefly state the principle behind each.* **Model Answer:** | # | Technique | Category | Principle | |:-:|:----------|:---------|:----------| | 1 | **Choke-ring antenna** | Antenna | Places concentric metal rings around the antenna element to **suppress signals arriving from below the horizon**, eliminating the worst ground-reflected multipath. | | 2 | **Narrow-correlator / MEDLL receiver** | Signal processing | Reduces the Early-Late spacing in the DLL to <0.1 chip, **sharpening the correlation peak** and reducing the bias caused by a reflected shoulder. | | 3 | **Carrier-smoothing of code pseudoranges (Hatch filter)** | Post-processing | Averages code and carrier measurements to **harness the low multipath of the carrier (cm-level) while retaining the unambiguous range of the code (m-level)**, attenuating the high-frequency multipath jitter on the code. | **(1 mark per correct technique + principle.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-4 Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Q7(a)]** **(14 Marks)** *With a neat sketch, explain the multipath propagation mechanism in GNSS. Derive the expression for the excess path length of a reflected ray from a horizontal ground plane, and compute the maximum carrier-phase multipath error on the L1 carrier.* #### (a) Sketch and Mechanism *(7 marks)* Refer to the **diagram in Section 4.1** for the geometric construction. **Mechanism (model answer):** A GNSS satellite at elevation $el$ transmits a circularly-polarised right-hand (RHCP) radio wave. The receiver antenna, ideally sensitive only to RHCP, sees the direct LOS signal. However, surfaces such as the **ground, building walls, water bodies, vehicles, and metal objects** near the robot reflect a portion of the wave with a **polarisation flip** (becoming predominantly LHCP) and a **path-length excess** $\Delta\rho = \rho_{MP} - \rho_{LOS}$. The two replicas — direct and reflected — superpose at the antenna feed, producing a **composite signal** whose code-correlation function is **distorted and asymmetric**, biasing the code and carrier tracking loops. This bias, when converted to a pseudorange, is the **multipath error** $M_P$, which propagates through the position solver into the final coordinates of the robot. **(7 marks = 2 sketch + 3 mechanism + 1 cause + 1 effect.)** #### (b) Derivation and L1 Computation *(7 marks)* **Derivation of excess path length:** Consider a satellite S at elevation $el$ and a receiver R at height $h$ above a horizontal ground plane. Let the reflection point be P. The reflected ray behaves as if it came from the **image source $S'$** located a distance $2h\sin(el)$ below R along the reflected line of sight. The geometric excess is therefore: $$\Delta\rho \;=\; \rho_{MP} - \rho_{LOS} \;=\; 2h \sin(el)$$ This is the **fundamental ground-reflection multipath equation** of the KTU 2024 syllabus. **Carrier-phase multipath envelope:** The carrier-phase error due to a single specular reflection is given by: $$M_\Phi \;=\; \frac{\lambda}{2\pi} \arctan\!\left(\frac{\alpha \sin\phi}{1 + \alpha\cos\phi}\right)$$ where $\phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}\Delta\rho$. The maximum occurs when $\alpha \to 1$ and the argument of the arctan reaches $\pi/2$: $$\vert M_\Phi \vert_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\lambda}{4}$$ **L1 evaluation:** $$\lambda_{L1} \;=\; \frac{c}{f_{L1}} \;=\; \frac{2.998 \times 10^{8}}{1575.42 \times 10^{6}} \;\approx\; 0.1903 \text{ m}$$ $$\vert M_\Phi \vert_{\max,L1} \;=\; \frac{0.1903}{4} \;\approx\; 0.0476 \text{ m} \;\approx\; 4.8 \text{ cm}$$ **(7 marks = 2 derivation + 1 envelope formula + 1 max value + 2 numerical computation + 1 final statement.)** --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q7(b)] — Alternative Choice** **(14 Marks)** *Discuss in detail the various sources of multipath errors in GNSS. Explain with mathematical model the impact of multipath on the pseudorange measurement. List the receiver-based and antenna-based multipath mitigation techniques.* #### (a) Sources of Multipath and Pseudorange Error Model *(7 marks)* **Sources (model answer):** 1. **Ground reflection** — the dominant multipath for low-elevation satellites over flat terrain; the worst case is a *specular* reflection from a smooth, wet surface. 2. **Building walls** — vertical reflectors; produce multipath at all elevations, worst at **low elevation** in urban canyons. 3. **Tree canopy and foliage** — diffuse scattering rather than specular reflection; reduces SNR and creates phase scintillation. 4. **Vehicle / robot body** — the metallic chassis of the robot itself, especially if the antenna is mounted close to the body. 5. **Water surfaces** — specular, near-perfect reflection, famous in marine GNSS multipath. 6. **Atmospheric ducting and tropospheric scattering** — secondary effect, usually small. 7. **Satellite-side multipath** — signals reflecting off the satellite's own solar panels (small). **Pseudorange Error Model (mathematical):** The measured pseudorange with multipath is: $$P_i \;=\; \rho_i \;+\; c\,(dt_r - dt^s) \;+\; I_i \;+\; T_i \;+\; M_{P,i} \;+\; \varepsilon_{P,i}$$ For a single specular reflection with amplitude ratio $\alpha$ and excess delay $\Delta\tau$: $$M_{P,i} \;\approx\; \frac{\alpha \, c \, \Delta\tau}{1 \,+\, \alpha\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi c \Delta\tau}{\lambda}\right)}$$ The maximum is the **one-chip bound**: $$\vert M_{P,i} \vert_{\max} \;\le\; \frac{L_{chip}\,c}{2} \;\approx\; 146.5 \text{ m (typical)} \;\;\text{for L1 C/A}$$ **(7 marks = 3 sources + 2 model + 1 max value + 1 conclusion.)** #### (b) Mitigation Techniques *(7 marks)* **Antenna-based:** - **Choke-ring antenna** — suppresses signals from below the horizon. - **RHCP-only antenna** — rejects LHCP-reflected multipath (most reflections flip polarisation). - **Antenna placement** — elevated, with no nearby reflective surfaces within 5–10 wavelengths (≈ 1 m for L1). **Receiver / signal-processing-based:** - **Narrow-correlator DLL** — Early-Late spacing reduced to 0.05–0.1 chip. - **Multipath Estimating Delay-Lock Loop (MEDLL)** — uses a maximum-likelihood estimator to subtract an estimated multipath contribution from the correlation function. - **Strobe correlators** — use gated correlator pairs to blank the trailing edge of the code where multipath usually distorts. - **Multifrequency / multiconstellation** — combining L1, L2, L5 and GPS+GLONASS+Galileo allows outlier rejection. **Post-processing-based:** - **Carrier-smoothing of code** (Hatch filter). - **Sidereal filtering** — repeating multipath pattern over one sidereal day (≈ 23 h 56 min) is averaged out. - **Elevation-based weighting** in the WLS position solver ($W_{ii} = \sin^2(el_i)$). - **Multipath maps in SNR** — modern receivers record SNR per satellite and flag anomalous dips as multipath. **(7 marks = 2 antenna + 3 receiver + 2 post-processing.)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (seen repeatedly in KTU valuation):** > 1. **Confusing the chip length with the chip *rate*.** A student who writes $L_{chip} = 1.023$ MHz **loses 1 mark immediately**. The correct relation is $L_{chip} = 1/1.023 \,\text{MHz} = 977.5$ ns. The *rate* is 1.023 Mchips/s; the *length* (period) is its reciprocal. > 2. **Forgetting the speed of light.** Multipath is a *time* phenomenon — every "metres" formula must include a $c$ somewhere. Examiners specifically look for $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s. > 3. **Giving only the maximum multipath without the elevation or amplitude dependence.** Always mention $\alpha$ and $el$ in your derivation. > 4. **Saying "multipath is cancelled by DGPS/RTK".** It is **not** — DGPS cancels *common-mode* spatially-correlated errors (clocks, ionosphere), but multipath is **local** to each antenna and **does not** cancel. This is a guaranteed 2-mark trap. > 5. **Drawing the diagram without the image source $S'$** — the KTU examiner's key always credits 1 mark for the mirror-image construction. > 6. **Mixing up $\Phi$ (carrier phase, in cycles) with $L$ (carrier phase, in metres).** State the units explicitly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Definition:** Multipath = reception of the same signal via two or more paths (LOS + NLOS). - **Two flavours:** Code (pseudorange) multipath — up to ~293 m on L1 C/A; Carrier multipath — up to ~4.8 cm on L1. - **Speed of light:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s — appears in *every* range equation. - **Excess path from ground reflection:** $\Delta\rho = 2h \sin(el)$. - **One-chip bound** for code multipath; **quarter-wavelength bound** for carrier multipath. - **Code > Carrier by factor ~1000 in multipath sensitivity** → this is why RTK/PPP uses carrier phase. - **Polarisation flip:** reflections invert RHCP→LHCP → RHCP-only antennas reject most multipath. - **DGPS does NOT cancel multipath** (multipath is local, not common-mode). - **Low-elevation satellites are multipath-prone** → apply $\sin^2(el)$ weighting in WLS. - **Mitigation triad:** (i) Choke-ring antenna, (ii) Narrow-correlator/MEDLL receiver, (iii) Hatch filter / sidereal filtering in post-processing. - **L5 is being added to modernised GNSS** specifically to *rescue* users from persistent L1/L2 multipath in difficult environments. - **Always state units** — metres for range, seconds for time, Hz for frequency, radians for phase. - **Always sketch the image source $S'$** in geometric derivations. - **Always write the full measurement equation** ($P$ and $\Phi$) — the examiner's key expects the ionospheric sign reversal on $\Phi$. - **Multipath is site-, elevation-, and antenna-dependent** — it is the *only* GNSS error that is *not* reduced by a longer observation interval (only averaged out if the geometry repeats, e.g., sidereal day).

topics.title: GNSS Accuracy Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** GNSS Accuracy Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**GNSS Accuracy Analysis** is the systematic quantitative evaluation of the positional, temporal, and velocity errors present in a Global Navigation Satellite System solution. It involves identifying, modeling, and mathematically combining individual error sources (ionospheric delay, tropospheric delay, satellite clock bias, orbital ephemeris error, multipath, and receiver noise) into a single representative figure called the **User Equivalent Range Error (UERE)**, which is then propagated through the satellite-receiver geometry (captured by **Dilution of Precision, DOP**) to determine the final navigation accuracy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Accuracy is NOT a single number. It is the product of two independent factors — the *quality of the range measurements* (UERE) and the *quality of the satellite geometry* (DOP). A good receiver in a bad sky can be worse than a poor receiver in a good sky.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Bullseye Story

Imagine you are throwing darts at a target while blindfolded. Two things decide your score:

1. **Precision (clustering)** — How tightly your darts bunch together (this is the *receiver's internal noise*, dominated by UERE).
2. **Accuracy (closeness to center)** — How close the *center of the dart cluster* is to the bullseye (this is *geometry* — where the satellites are placed overhead, captured by DOP).

> - **Tight cluster, wrong center** = Precise but Inaccurate → Low UERE, High DOP (poor satellite geometry).
> - **Spread cluster, right center** = Accurate but Imprecise → High UERE, Low DOP (excellent geometry).
> - **Tight cluster, right center** = Precise AND Accurate → Low UERE, Low DOP (best case).

### 1.3 Three Pillars of GNSS Accuracy

| Pillar | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| Range Measurement Quality | **UERE** | Standard deviation of pseudorange error | **1 – 5 m** (SPS) |
| Geometric Strength | **DOP** | Amplification factor from satellite geometry | **1 – 20** (unitless) |
| Position Accuracy | **σ_pos** | Resultant 1σ position error | UERE × DOP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Definitions Used by KTU / ICAO / IEEE:**
> - **CEP (Circular Error Probable):** Radius of the circle containing 50% of fixes.
> - **R95:** Radius containing 95% of fixes (used in civil aviation).
> - **2DRMS (Twice Distance Root Mean Square):** 2 × √(σ_E² + σ_N²); ~95% confidence for horizontal.
> - **DRMS:** √(σ_E² + σ_N²); ~63 – 68% confidence.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Major Error Sources (Ranging Errors)

GNSS pseudorange errors are assumed **statistically independent and zero-mean Gaussian**. They add in **variance**, not linearly.

1. **Ionospheric Delay (σ_iono)** — Free electrons in the ionosphere (60 – 1000 km altitude) slow the L-band signal. Worst at zenith, doubles every 8° below horizon. Can be removed via dual-frequency (L1 + L2) receivers using the ionosphere-free combination.
2. **Tropospheric Delay (σ_tropo)** — Dry gases + water vapor in the troposphere (0 – 60 km). Models like Saastamoinen or Hopfield correct ~95 % of it.
3. **Satellite Clock Bias (σ_clk)** — Atomic clocks on satellites drift. Corrected via the broadcast navigation message (Klobuchar model + clock polynomial).
4. **Ephemeris / Orbit Error (σ_orb)** — Mismatch between the satellite's true position and the position predicted by the broadcast ephemeris.
5. **Multipath (σ_mp)** — Signal reflected off buildings, ground, water before reaching the antenna. **Cannot be fixed by models**; only by antenna design (choke ring) and site selection.
6. **Receiver Noise (σ_rcv)** — Thermal noise, quantization, tracking loop jitter in the receiver hardware.

> [!NOTE]
> **Selective Availability (SA)** — A historical intentional degradation (~100 m 2DRMS) used by the US DoD on GPS until **May 1, 2000**. It is now permanently set to zero. The term appears in KTU theory questions for historical completeness only.

### 2.2 Dilution of Precision (DOP) Family

DOP is a **scalar multiplier** that tells how the satellite geometry amplifies the ranging errors into position errors. The **geometry matrix G** (4×n for n satellites) is:

$$
G = \begin{bmatrix} \cos \theta_{1,e} & \cos \theta_{1,n} & \cos \theta_{1,u} & 1 \\ \cos \theta_{2,e} & \cos \theta_{2,n} & \cos \theta_{2,u} & 1 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ \cos \theta_{n,e} & \cos \theta_{n,n} & \cos \theta_{n,u} & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\cos \theta_{i,j}$ is the direction cosine from satellite *i* to the receiver in local *j* axis (East, North, Up). Then:

$$
Q = (G^T G)^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} q_{EE} & q_{EN} & q_{EU} & q_{ET} \\ q_{NE} & q_{NN} & q_{NU} & q_{NT} \\ q_{UE} & q_{UN} & q_{UU} & q_{UT} \\ q_{TE} & q_{TN} & q_{TU} & q_{TT} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Each DOP is the square root of the trace of a sub-block of $Q$:

- **GDOP** (Geometric) = √(q_EE + q_NN + q_UU + q_TT)
- **PDOP** (Position, 3D) = √(q_EE + q_NN + q_UU)
- **HDOP** (Horizontal) = √(q_EE + q_NN)
- **VDOP** (Vertical) = √(q_UU)
- **TDOP** (Time) = √(q_TT)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{UERE} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_{i}^{2}}$ | Variance-based UERE combination | meters |
| 2 | $\sigma_{pos} = \sigma_{UERE} \times DOP$ | Position 1σ error (3D) | meters |
| 3 | $\sigma_{H} = \sigma_{UERE} \times HDOP$ | Horizontal 1σ error | meters |
| 4 | $\sigma_{V} = \sigma_{UERE} \times VDOP$ | Vertical 1σ error | meters |
| 5 | $PDOP = \sqrt{HDOP^{2} + VDOP^{2}}$ | 3D geometric factor | unitless |
| 6 | $GDOP = \sqrt{PDOP^{2} + TDOP^{2}}$ | 4D geometric factor | unitless |
| 7 | $CEP \approx 0.5894 \cdot \sigma_{H} \cdot k$ | Circular Error Probable (50%) | meters |
| 8 | $R95 \approx 1.2272 \cdot \sigma_{H}$ | 95% horizontal error | meters |
| 9 | $2DRMS = 2\sqrt{\sigma_{E}^{2} + \sigma_{N}^{2}}$ | ~95–98% horizontal confidence | meters |
| 10 | $\sigma_{3D,3\sigma} = 3 \cdot \sigma_{UERE} \cdot PDOP$ | 99.7% 3D position error | meters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** Vertical errors are always **~1.5× to 2× larger** than horizontal errors because satellites are above the user (no satellite directly below). This is why PDOP is usually dominated by VDOP.

### 2.4 DOP Interpretation Table (Print and Memorize)

| DOP Value | Quality | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **1 – 2** | Ideal | RTK, surveying, precision robotics |
| **2 – 5** | Excellent | Civil aviation en-route, autonomous vehicles |
| **5 – 10** | Moderate | Consumer GPS, fleet tracking |
| **10 – 20** | Fair | Deteriorating geometry, lose accuracy |
| **> 20** | Poor | Unusable, do not initialize the solution |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** HDOP < 2 is mandatory for lane-level positioning.
- **Aviation (ICAO):** R95 horizontal < 16 m required for non-precision approach (SBAS-augmented).
- **Surveying/RTK:** Baseline accuracy ~ 1 cm + 1 ppm using carrier-phase.
- **Robotics SLAM:** GNSS provides global anchor; UERE-DOP budget decides reliability of the loop closure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the UERE Combination Rule

Let the true range be $\rho$ and the measured pseudorange be $\hat{\rho}$. The error $\epsilon = \hat{\rho} - \rho$ is a sum of independent contributions:

$$
\epsilon = \epsilon_{iono} + \epsilon_{tropo} + \epsilon_{clk} + \epsilon_{orb} + \epsilon_{mp} + \epsilon_{rcv}
$$

Since the errors are **statistically independent zero-mean random variables**, their variances add:

$$
\sigma_{\epsilon}^{2} = \sigma_{iono}^{2} + \sigma_{tropo}^{2} + \sigma_{clk}^{2} + \sigma_{orb}^{2} + \sigma_{mp}^{2} + \sigma_{rcv}^{2}
$$

Therefore the User Equivalent Range Error is:

$$
\sigma_{UERE} = \sqrt{\sigma_{iono}^{2} + \sigma_{tropo}^{2} + \sigma_{clk}^{2} + \sigma_{orb}^{2} + \sigma_{mp}^{2} + \sigma_{rcv}^{2}}
$$

> **Reasoning logic:** Cross-terms vanish because the covariance between any two independent zero-mean errors is zero: $\text{Cov}(\epsilon_i, \epsilon_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — UERE Calculation

**Given typical Standard Positioning Service (SPS) GPS error budget values:**

| Error Source | 1σ Error (m) |
|---|---|
| Ionosphere (single-frequency model) | 4.0 |
| Troposphere | 0.7 |
| Satellite clock | 2.0 |
| Ephemeris | 2.0 |
| Multipath (good site) | 1.0 |
| Receiver noise | 0.5 |

**Step 1 — Square each error term (variance contribution).**

$$
4.0^{2} = 16.00 \quad 0.7^{2} = 0.49 \quad 2.0^{2} = 4.00
$$

$$
2.0^{2} = 4.00 \quad 1.0^{2} = 1.00 \quad 0.5^{2} = 0.25
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the variances.**

$$
\sigma_{UERE}^{2} = 16.00 + 0.49 + 4.00 + 4.00 + 1.00 + 0.25 = 25.74
$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root.**

$$
\sigma_{UERE} = \sqrt{25.74} \approx 5.07 \text{ m}
$$

> **Observation:** The ionosphere dominates (>60% of total variance) — this is why dual-frequency receivers are transformative.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of DOP from the Linearized GPS Equation

The pseudorange equation is nonlinear in the receiver position $(x, y, z)$ and clock bias $\delta t$. Linearizing about an approximate solution $(x_0, y_0, z_0, \delta t_0)$:

$$
\Delta \rho_i = \cos\theta_{i,e}\,\Delta x + \cos\theta_{i,n}\,\Delta y + \cos\theta_{i,u}\,\Delta z + c\,\Delta(\delta t)
$$

Stacking for n satellites into matrix form:

$$
\Delta \rho = G \, \Delta x
$$

Apply the **least-squares normal equations**:

$$
G^{T} G \, \Delta x = G^{T} \Delta \rho
$$

Solve for the parameter covariance:

$$
\text{cov}(\Delta x) = \sigma_{UERE}^{2}\,(G^{T}G)^{-1} = \sigma_{UERE}^{2}\,Q
$$

The diagonal of $Q$ gives the variance of each parameter:

$$
\sigma_{x}^{2} = \sigma_{UERE}^{2} q_{EE} \quad \sigma_{y}^{2} = \sigma_{UERE}^{2} q_{NN} \quad \sigma_{z}^{2} = \sigma_{UERE}^{2} q_{UU} \quad \sigma_{t}^{2} = \sigma_{UERE}^{2} q_{TT}
$$

Therefore, position standard deviation is:

$$
\sigma_{3D} = \sigma_{UERE}\sqrt{q_{EE} + q_{NN} + q_{UU}} = \sigma_{UERE}\cdot PDOP
$$

And horizontal:

$$
\sigma_{H} = \sigma_{UERE}\sqrt{q_{EE} + q_{NN}} = \sigma_{UERE}\cdot HDOP
$$

---

### 3.4 Comprehensive Numerical Example — Full Accuracy Budget

Continuing from Section 3.2, suppose the satellite geometry yields:

- **HDOP = 1.5**
- **VDOP = 2.5**
- **TDOP = 1.0**
- **σ_UERE = 5.07 m**

**Step 1 — Compute PDOP.**

$$
PDOP = \sqrt{HDOP^{2} + VDOP^{2}} = \sqrt{1.5^{2} + 2.5^{2}} = \sqrt{2.25 + 6.25} = \sqrt{8.50} \approx 2.92
$$

**Step 2 — Compute GDOP.**

$$
GDOP = \sqrt{PDOP^{2} + TDOP^{2}} = \sqrt{8.50 + 1.00} = \sqrt{9.50} \approx 3.08
$$

**Step 3 — Compute horizontal accuracy (1σ).**

$$
\sigma_{H} = \sigma_{UERE} \times HDOP = 5.07 \times 1.5 = 7.61 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute 95% horizontal (R95).**

$$
R95 = 1.2272 \times \sigma_{H} = 1.2272 \times 7.61 \approx 9.34 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute vertical accuracy (1σ).**

$$
\sigma_{V} = 5.07 \times 2.5 = 12.68 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute 3D 99.7% error (3σ envelope).**

$$
\sigma_{3D,3\sigma} = 3 \times 5.07 \times 2.92 = 44.41 \text{ m}
$$

> **Result Summary Box:**
> - Horizontal 1σ: **7.61 m** | Vertical 1σ: **12.68 m**
> - R95 horizontal: **9.34 m** | 3D 3σ: **44.41 m**

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — How to Improve Accuracy

| Method | Working Principle | Achievable Accuracy | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standalone GPS | Pseudorange, broadcast ephemeris | 5 – 15 m (95%) | Lowest |
| SBAS (WAAS, GAGAN) | Geostationary corrections broadcast | 1 – 3 m (95%) | Free to user |
| DGPS | Ground reference station at known coords | 0.5 – 2 m | Medium |
| RTK (Real-Time Kinematic) | Carrier-phase ambiguity resolution | **1 cm + 1 ppm** | High |
| PPP (Precise Point Positioning) | Global precise orbits + clocks, dual-freq | 2 – 10 cm | High + latency |
| PPK (Post-Processed Kinematic) | RTK done offline | 1 cm + 1 ppm | Medium |
| Multi-sensor fusion (INS+GNSS+LIDAR) | Kalman filter coupling | Lane-level (0.1 m) | Highest |

```mermaid
    QMatrix --> D1[GDOP Geometric DOP 3D plus Time]
    QMatrix --> D2[PDOP Position DOP 3D only]

```
