> (1 mark) Final answer summary: $\omega = 0.5$ rad/s, $v_R = 0.5625$ m/s, $v_L = 0.4375$ m/s.

---

#### **(b)** At an instant when the robot's heading is $\theta = 30°$, compute the world-frame velocity $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$. Also verify the non-holonomic constraint. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply world-frame transformation:**

$$
\dot{x} = v \cos\theta = 0.5 \times \cos(30°) = 0.5 \times 0.8660 = 0.4330 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\dot{y} = v \sin\theta = 0.5 \times \sin(30°) = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\dot{\theta} = \omega = 0.5 \text{ rad/s}
$$

> (3 marks) These are the **global pose derivatives** for odometry integration.

**Step 2 — Non-holonomic constraint verification:**

LHS:

$$
\dot{x} \sin\theta - \dot{y} \cos\theta = 0.4330 \times \sin(30°) - 0.25 \times \cos(30°)
$$

$$
= 0.4330 \times 0.5 - 0.25 \times 0.8660 = 0.2165 - 0.2165 = 0
$$

> (2 marks) **LHS = 0 → Constraint satisfied ✓** — the robot's instantaneous motion has no lateral component.

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation:**

> (1 mark) **Direction of motion:** The motion vector makes angle $\tan^{-1}(0.25/0.4330) = 30°$ with the world $x$-axis, which exactly equals the heading $\theta$. This confirms the robot is moving in its pointing direction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and axle length $L = 0.40$ m. The right and left wheel angular speeds are measured by encoders as $\dot{\phi}_R = 15$ rad/s and $\dot{\phi}_L = 5$ rad/s.**

#### **(a)** Compute the wheel surface speeds, body linear velocity, angular velocity, and radius of curvature. Identify the type of motion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Wheel surface speeds:**

$$
v_R = r \dot{\phi}_R = 0.05 \times 15 = 0.75 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_L = r \dot{\phi}_L = 0.05 \times 5 = 0.25 \text{ m/s}
$$

> (2 marks)

**Step 2 — Body linear velocity:**

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{0.75 + 0.25}{2} = 0.50 \text{ m/s}
$$

> (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Body angular velocity:**

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{0.75 - 0.25}{0.40} = \frac{0.50}{0.40} = 1.25 \text{ rad/s}
$$

> (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Radius of curvature:**

$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.50}{1.25} = 0.40 \text{ m}
$$

> (1 mark) Equivalently: $R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R+v_L}{v_R-v_L} = 0.20 \times \frac{1.0}{0.5} = 0.40$ m ✓

**Step 5 — Motion classification:**

> (1 mark) Since $v_R > v_L > 0$, the robot executes a **leftward circular arc** with $R = 0.40$ m. The turn direction is **left** (CCW), and the turn is **tight** (small $R$).

---

#### **(b)** If the robot must execute a pure rotation in place (zero translation, $v = 0$) at angular velocity $\omega = 1.0$ rad/s, determine the required wheel speeds in m/s and rad/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Set body linear velocity to zero:**

$$
v = 0 \implies v_R + v_L = 0 \implies v_L = -v_R
$$

> (2 marks) **Negative sign** means the left wheel must rotate **backward** while the right rotates forward — causing the robot to spin about its own center.

**Step 2 — Apply angular velocity constraint:**

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = 1.0 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Substituting $v_L = -v_R$:

$$
\frac{v_R - (-v_R)}{0.40} = 1.0 \implies \frac{2 v_R}{0.40} = 1.0
$$

$$
v_R = 0.20 \text{ m/s}, \quad v_L = -0.20 \text{ m/s}
$$

> (2 marks)

**Step 3 — Convert to wheel angular speeds:**

$$
\dot{\phi}_R = \frac{v_R}{r} = \frac{0.20}{0.05} = 4.0 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\dot{\phi}_L = \frac{v_L}{r} = \frac{-0.20}{0.05} = -4.0 \text{ rad/s}
$$

> (2 marks) **Equal magnitude, opposite signs** — this is the canonical "spin-in-place" command.

**Step 4 — Verification:**

> (1 mark) $v = (0.20 + (-0.20))/2 = 0$ ✓ and $\omega = (0.20 - (-0.20))/0.40 = 1.0$ rad/s ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign errors in $v_L$** during in-place rotation — students often write $v_L = +0.20$ m/s, losing 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert** $v$ (m/s) to $\dot{\phi}$ (rad/s) using $\dot{\phi} = v/r$ when the question asks for motor speeds.
> 3. **Mixing up $v$ and $R$** — the formula is $R = v/\omega$, not $R = \omega/v$. This swap is a silent killer for 2-mark problems.
> 4. **Not specifying units** in final answers — KTU examiners often deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 5. **Confusing forward vs inverse kinematics** — forward takes wheel speeds $\to$ body velocity; inverse takes body velocity $\to$ wheel speeds. Always re-read the question stem.
> 6. **Skipping the non-holonomic constraint verification** when explicitly asked — this is a free 2-mark item and a favorite KTU trick question.
> 7. **Forgetting the $\theta$ rotation** when computing world-frame velocities from body-frame — students often write $\dot{x} = v$ instead of $\dot{x} = v \cos\theta$.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Velocity Calculation**

### **🔑 Core Equations (Must Memorize)**

- ✅ **Wheel speed:** $v_i = r \dot{\phi}_i$
- ✅ **Body linear velocity:** $v = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2}$
- ✅ **Body angular velocity:** $\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{L}$
- ✅ **Radius of curvature:** $R = \dfrac{v}{\omega} = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$
- ✅ **Inverse kinematics:** $v_R = v + \dfrac{L}{2}\omega$, $\quad v_L = v - \dfrac{L}{2}\omega$
- ✅ **World-frame mapping:** $\dot{x} = v \cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v \sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$
- ✅ **Non-holonomic constraint:** $\dot{x} \sin\theta - \dot{y} \cos\theta = 0$

### **🎯 Special Cases (High-Yield for KTU)**

- 🔹 $v_R = v_L \implies$ **Straight line** ($R \to \infty$, $\omega = 0$)
- 🔹 $v_R = -v_L \implies$ **In-place rotation** ($v = 0$)
- 🔹 $v_L = 0 \implies$ **Pivot turn** about the left wheel
- 🔹 $v_R > v_L \implies$ **Left turn** (CCW)
- 🔹 $v_R < v_L \implies$ **Right turn** (CW)

### **🧭 Engineering Design Rules**

- 🔸 The **ICC always lies on the line perpendicular to the axle** through the robot's center.
- 🔸 Increasing $L$ (axle length) → **wider turns** (larger $R$ for same $\omega$).
- 🔸 Increasing $r$ (wheel radius) → **faster linear motion** for same $\dot{\phi}$.
- 🔸 Odometry integrates the world-frame equations using **Euler or Runge-Kutta** methods.
- 🔸 In KTU numericals, always **show units** and **round to 4 decimal places** for safety.

### **⚠️ Pitfalls to Avoid**

- ❌ Don't confuse $R$ (radius of curvature) with $r$ (wheel radius) — they are **completely different** symbols.
- ❌ Don't divide by $(v_R - v_L)$ without checking it's nonzero (would mean $\omega = 0$).
- ❌ Don't forget the $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ in the world-frame transformation.
- ❌ Don't assume the robot can move sideways — it **cannot** (non-holonomic).

> **🎓 Final KTU Tip:** Velocity Calculation is a **calculation-heavy topic**. Practice at least 5 numerical problems covering: (1) forward kinematics, (2) inverse kinematics, (3) world-frame transformation, (4) in-place rotation, and (5) radius-of-curvature-based turn analysis. This will cover ~80% of KTU questions on this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Urban Navigation - Urban Canyon Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Urban Navigation - Urban Canyon Navigation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Urban Canyon** is a term borrowed from fluid dynamics and geomorphology, applied in robotics and GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System) literature to describe a street corridor in a dense city environment that is flanked on both sides by tall structures (skyscrapers, high-rise buildings, flyover pillars) such that the **sky visibility** (sky-view factor) of a receiver is severely restricted. From the **KTU 2024 Scheme** perspective, **Urban Canyon Navigation** is defined as the discipline of estimating the position, velocity, and attitude (PVA) of a mobile robot or vehicle in such a GNSS-degraded environment, where traditional satellite-based positioning fails due to **signal blockage, multipath, and Non-Line-Of-Sight (NLOS) reception**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Urban canyon navigation is treated as a sub-topic of *Urban Navigation*. Students must master the **error mechanisms** (multipath, NLOS, attenuation), the **mathematical models** (pseudorange with errors), and the **mitigation architectures** (sensor fusion, map matching, shadow matching, particle filtering). ### 1.2 Key Terminology | Term | Standard Definition | KTU Notation | |---|---|---| | **Line-Of-Sight (LOS)** | Direct, unobstructed path between satellite and receiver | $S_{LOS}$ | | **Non-Line-Of-Sight (NLOS)** | Signal reaches receiver **only after reflection** from a building | $S_{NLOS}$ | | **Multipath** | Signal arrives via **multiple paths** (direct + reflected) causing interference | $M_i$ | | **Sky-View Factor (SVF)** | Ratio of visible sky hemisphere to total hemisphere (0 = fully blocked) | $\xi$ | | **UERE** | User Equivalent Range Error – aggregate of all range errors | $\sigma_{UERE}$ | | **DOP** | Dilution of Precision – geometric amplification of range error | $GDOP, PDOP, HDOP$ | | **HDOP/PDOP** | Horizontal / Position DOP (2D/3D) | $HDOP, PDOP$ | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **The Tunnel of Mirrors Analogy** > > Imagine you are walking through a narrow tunnel whose walls are covered with polished mirrors, and the only light source is the sun directly above. Now imagine trying to locate the sun's true position using only a flashlight and a small periscope. Some light rays (signals) are completely blocked (buildings), some bounce off the mirrors before reaching you (multipath), and some reach you from directions where the sun **does not actually exist** (NLOS reception). The same thing happens in an urban canyon with GNSS satellites. A robot trying to find its position using only GPS in a downtown area is essentially doing the same — getting fooled by reflections and denied clear signals. The robot must therefore combine **other senses** (camera eyes, LiDAR, IMU) to compensate for the unreliable "satellite vision". ### 1.4 The Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Speed of light:** $c = 2.99792458 \times 10^8$ **m/s** (used in all pseudorange computations). - **Standard GNSS carrier frequencies (L1, L2, L5):** **1575.42 MHz, 1227.60 MHz, 1176.45 MHz**. - **Typical open-sky PDOP:** **≤ 2** (excellent); **Urban canyon PDOP:** **> 6** (poor). - **Multipath-induced pseudorange bias:** up to **100 m** in deep canyons. - **NLOS-only pseudorange bias:** **50 m – 300 m**. - **Acceptable positioning accuracy (road vehicles):** **< 5 m (lane-level)**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Urban Canyons Break GNSS — The Three Degradation Mechanisms 1. **Signal Blockage (Attenuation):** Tall buildings physically obstruct the line of sight to satellites at low elevation angles. Only satellites with elevation angle $\theta_e \geq \theta_{cutoff}$ (typically 5°–15°) are usable, reducing the number of visible satellites from ~11 (open sky) to **3–5** in deep canyons. 2. **Multipath Propagation:** Reflected signals from glass, metal, and concrete surfaces arrive with extra path length $\Delta l = 2d\sin\alpha$ (where $d$ is the path difference and $\alpha$ the reflection angle). They interfere constructively or destructively with the direct LOS signal, causing **carrier-phase distortion** and **pseudorange bias**. 3. **Non-Line-Of-Sight (NLOS) Reception:** When the LOS is completely blocked, the receiver tracks only a reflected signal. The measured pseudorange corresponds to a **virtual satellite** location mirrored across the reflecting surface — leading to systematic positive range bias and large position errors. ### 2.2 The Pseudorange Measurement Equation (with Urban Canyon Errors) The measured pseudorange to satellite $i$ is: $$ \rho_i = r_i + c \cdot (\delta t_u - \delta t^i) + I_i + T_i + M_i + \varepsilon_i + N_i $$ Where: | Symbol | Meaning | Typical magnitude (open sky) | Typical magnitude (urban canyon) | |---|---|---|---| | $r_i$ | True geometric range from receiver to satellite $i$ | varies | varies | | $c$ | Speed of light | $3\times10^8$ m/s | $3\times10^8$ m/s | | $\delta t_u$ | Receiver clock bias | ~ ms | ~ ms | | $\delta t^i$ | Satellite clock bias | ~ µs | ~ µs | | $I_i$ | Ionospheric delay | 2 – 10 m | 2 – 10 m | | $T_i$ | Tropospheric delay | 2 – 25 m | 2 – 25 m | | $M_i$ | Multipath error | 1 – 5 m | **10 – 100 m** | | $N_i$ | **NLOS bias** (extra term in canyon) | 0 m | **50 – 300 m** | | $\varepsilon_i$ | Thermal noise (thermal + tracking jitter) | 0.5 – 2 m | 1 – 5 m | > [!NOTE] > In an **open-sky** environment, $N_i = 0$ and $M_i$ is small. In an **urban canyon**, the multipath term $M_i$ explodes and the NLOS term $N_i$ becomes non-negligible, making single-constellation GNSS unreliable. ### 2.3 The Sky-View Factor and Satellite Visibility The **sky-view factor** $\xi$ is the ratio of the solid angle of visible sky $\Omega_{visible}$ to that of the total upper hemisphere $2\pi$: $$ \xi = \frac{\Omega_{visible}}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \iint_{\text{visible}} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi $$ - $\xi \approx 1.0$ → open field - $\xi \approx 0.6$–$0.8$ → suburban street - $\xi \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ → urban canyon - $\xi < 0.3$ → deep urban canyon / "tunnel" Empirical relationship between $\xi$ and expected position error (in meters): $$ \sigma_{position} \approx \frac{k}{\xi^{2}} \quad \text{where } k \approx 2.5 \text{ m} $$ ### 2.4 Position Accuracy and DOP Geometry The **3D position error** of a GNSS fix is: $$ \sigma_P = PDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE} $$ The 2D horizontal equivalent is: $$ \sigma_H = HDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE} $$ $PDOP$ is computed from the **geometry matrix** $H$ of unit vectors from receiver to satellites: $$ H = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{e}_1^T & 1 \\ \hat{e}_2^T & 1 \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ \hat{e}_n^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}_{n \times 4}, \quad PDOP = \sqrt{\text{trace}\left[(H^T H)^{-1}\right]_{1:3,\,1:3}} $$ In an urban canyon, satellites are confined to a narrow cone above the street, making $H^T H$ ill-conditioned and $PDOP$ very large. ### 2.5 Multipath Geometry — Reflection Path Length For a single specular reflection from a vertical wall at horizontal distance $d$ from the receiver, with satellite elevation $\theta_e$ and azimuth offset $\Delta\phi$ from the wall normal: $$ \Delta\rho_{multipath} = 2d \cdot \sin\theta_e \cdot \cos(\Delta\phi) $$ This adds a **positive bias** to the measured pseudorange. The reflected signal also has weaker carrier-to-noise ratio: $$ C/N_0 = C/N_{0,LOS} - 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R_{reflected}^2}{R_{LOS}^2}\right) - L_{reflection} $$ where $L_{reflection}$ is the reflection loss (~3 dB for glass, ~10 dB for concrete, ~20 dB for metal). ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **Equation** | **Use in KTU Exam** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Pseudorange model | $\rho_i = r_i + c(\delta t_u - \delta t^i) + I_i + T_i + M_i + N_i + \varepsilon_i$ | 7-mark derivation question | | 2 | Geometric range | $r_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x)^2 + (y_i - y)^2 + (z_i - z)^2}$ | Standard 3D distance | | 3 | Position accuracy | $\sigma_P = PDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE}$ | Compute positioning error | | 4 | Horizontal accuracy | $\sigma_H = HDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE}$ | 2D road navigation | | 5 | PDOP definition | $PDOP = \sqrt{\text{trace}[(H^TH)^{-1}]_{1:3,1:3}}$ | Derive from geometry | | 6 | Sky-View Factor | $\xi = \Omega_{visible} / 2\pi$ | Quantify canyon severity | | 7 | Empirical error vs SVF | $\sigma \approx k / \xi^2$ | Numerical problems | | 8 | Multipath path excess | $\Delta\rho = 2d\sin\theta_e\cos(\Delta\phi)$ | Derive reflection bias | | 9 | SNR / C/N₀ relation | $C/N_0 = P_r / (k_B T_{sys})$ | Link budget questions | | 10 | Particle filter weight | $w_k^{(i)} \propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(z_k \vert x_k^{(i)})$ | Bayesian positioning | | 11 | EKF prediction | $\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = F\hat{x}_{k-1} + Bu_{k-1}$ | INS/GNSS fusion | | 12 | EKF update | $K_k = P_{k\vert k-1}H^T(HP_{k\vert k-1}H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | | 13 | Shadow matching likelihood | $L(x) = \sum_i p(s_i \vert x, \mathcal{M})$ | 3D map-aided GNSS | | 14 | Map-matching score | $S = \arg\min_d \sum_k \text{dist}(p_k, \text{road}_d)$ | Road snapping | | 15 | LiDAR odometry | $\Delta p = \arg\min\sum_i \vert\vert T \cdot p_i - q_i \vert\vert^2$ | ICP-based localization | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Watch (KTU Board Exam trap):** Always express $\sigma_{UERE}$ in **meters** and $PDOP$ as **dimensionless**, so that $\sigma_P$ is in **meters**. Mixing km with m is the #1 reason for losing 1 mark in numerical problems. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Urban canyon navigation algorithms are deployed in: - **Autonomous vehicles** (Waymo, Cruise, Tesla FSD) for lane-level localization in city centers. - **Last-mile delivery robots** (Starship, Nuro) on sidewalks. - **Search-and-rescue robots** operating in collapsed urban structures (with reduced sky view). - **Ride-hailing GPS** in dense Asian/European cities (e.g., Tokyo, Mumbai, Manhattan). - **UAV delivery drones** navigating between skyscrapers (e.g., Amazon Prime Air in dense cities). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Pseudorange Linearization for Position Estimation We start with the **non-linear** geometric range from receiver position $(x, y, z)$ to satellite $i$ at known position $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$: $$ r_i(x, y, z) = \sqrt{(x_i - x)^2 + (y_i - y)^2 + (z_i - z)^2} $$ **Step 1 — Expand about a known approximate receiver position** $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ using the first-order Taylor series. $$ r_i(x, y, z) \approx r_i(x_0, y_0, z_0) + \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial x}\bigg|_0 \Delta x + \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial y}\bigg|_0 \Delta y + \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial z}\bigg|_0 \Delta z $$ **Step 2 — Compute the partial derivatives (the line-of-sight unit vector components).** $$ \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial x} = \frac{-(x_i - x_0)}{r_{i,0}} = -e_{i,x} $$ $$ \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial y} = \frac{-(y_i - y_0)}{r_{i,0}} = -e_{i,y} $$ $$ \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial z} = \frac{-(z_i - z_0)}{r_{i,0}} = -e_{i,z} $$ where $(e_{i,x}, e_{i,y}, e_{i,z})$ are the components of the **unit vector** from the receiver to satellite $i$. **Step 3 — Substitute into the pseudorange equation** (temporarily dropping the $I, T, N$ terms for clarity and absorbing them into the UERE term): $$ \rho_i = r_{i,0} - e_{i,x}\Delta x - e_{i,y}\Delta y - e_{i,z}\Delta z + c\,\delta t_u $$ **Step 4 — Rearrange** so that the unknown $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z, c\delta t_u$ are isolated on the right: $$ \rho_i - r_{i,0} = - e_{i,x}\Delta x - e_{i,y}\Delta y - e_{i,z}\Delta z + c\,\delta t_u $$ **Step 5 — Stack the equations for $n$ visible satellites** into the matrix form $\mathbf{z} = H \mathbf{\Delta x}$: $$ \begin{bmatrix} \rho_1 - r_{1,0} \\ \rho_2 - r_{2,0} \\ \vdots \\ \rho_n - r_{n,0} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -e_{1,x} & -e_{1,y} & -e_{1,z} & 1 \\ -e_{2,x} & -e_{2,y} & -e_{2,z} & 1 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ -e_{n,x} & -e_{n,y} & -e_{n,z} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \\ \Delta z \\ c\,\delta t_u \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 6 — Solve using Least-Squares** (since $n \geq 4$): $$ \mathbf{\Delta x} = (H^T H)^{-1} H^T \mathbf{z} $$ This is the standard **GNSS position fix**. In an urban canyon, the matrix $H^T H$ becomes ill-conditioned because the rows (unit vectors) point into a small cone, making $(H^T H)^{-1}$ numerically large, hence **PDOP blows up**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: PDOP Formula from the Covariance Matrix **Step 1 — Recall the linear model** $\mathbf{z} = H \mathbf{\Delta x} + \mathbf{\eta}$, where $\mathbf{\eta} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$ and $R = \sigma_{UERE}^2 I$. **Step 2 — Least-squares covariance** of the estimated state: $$ P_{\mathbf{\Delta x}} = (H^T H)^{-1} H^T R H (H^T H)^{-1} = \sigma_{UERE}^2 (H^T H)^{-1} $$ (using $H H^T (H^T H)^{-1} (H^T H)^{-1} H^T = (H^T H)^{-1}$). **Step 3 — Define PDOP** as the square root of the sum of variances in the position dimensions (x, y, z): $$ PDOP = \sqrt{P_{11} + P_{22} + P_{33}} = \sqrt{\text{trace}\!\left[(H^TH)^{-1}\right]_{1:3,1:3}} \cdot \sigma_{UERE} / \sigma_{UERE} $$ The $\sigma_{UERE}$ factors out, leaving a **dimensionless** PDOP. **Step 4 — Special case: 4 satellites, two diametrically opposed in azimuth at elevation $\theta$**. The unit vectors are: - $S_1 = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta, 0)$ - $S_2 = (-\cos\theta, \sin\theta, 0)$ - $S_3 = (0, -\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$ - $S_4 = (0, -\sin\theta, -\cos\theta)$ Then: $$ H^T H = \begin{bmatrix} 2\cos^2\theta & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2\sin^2\theta + 2\sin^2\theta & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2\cos^2\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix} $$ So: $$ PDOP = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\cos^2\theta} + \frac{1}{4\cos^2\theta} + \frac{1}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{3}{4\cos^2\theta} + \frac{1}{4}} $$ As $\theta \to 0$ (low elevation, urban canyon geometry), $\cos\theta \to 1$ and $PDOP \to 1$ here; but in the **realistic canyon case** where all satellites are clustered at high elevation in a small azimuthal sector, PDOP **diverges**. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Particle Filter for Urban Canyon Positioning When the noise is **non-Gaussian** (as in NLOS reception with heavy-tailed errors), the Kalman filter fails and we use a **particle filter (Sequential Monte Carlo)**. **Step 1 — Initialize particles** uniformly in the prior region of interest (e.g., a 100 m × 100 m bounding box around the last known fix): $$ x_0^{(i)} \sim \mathcal{U}(x_{min}, x_{max}), \quad w_0^{(i)} = \frac{1}{N}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N $$ **Step 2 — Predict** the new particle positions using a motion model (e.g., constant velocity with IMU aiding): $$ x_k^{(i)} = x_{k-1}^{(i)} + v_{k-1} \Delta t + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_v^2) $$ **Step 3 — Update weights** based on the **likelihood of the observed pseudoranges** $\mathbf{z}_k$: $$ w_k^{(i)} \propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert x_k^{(i)}) $$ For each candidate position $x_k^{(i)}$, compute the **expected pseudoranges** to all visible satellites and compare with the observed values: $$ p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert x_k^{(i)}) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma_j} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(\rho_{j,k} - \hat{\rho}_{j}^{(i)})^2}{2\sigma_j^2} \right) $$ **Step 4 — Normalize** the weights so they sum to 1: $$ \tilde{w}_k^{(i)} = \frac{w_k^{(i)}}{\sum_{m=1}^{N} w_k^{(m)}} $$ **Step 5 — Resample** (Systematic Resampling) to avoid weight degeneracy: $$ \{x_k^{(i)}, \tilde{w}_k^{(i)}\} \xrightarrow{\text{resample}} \{x_k^{(i)\star}, 1/N\} $$ **Step 6 — Estimate position** as the weighted mean of particles: $$ \hat{x}_k = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{w}_k^{(i)} \, x_k^{(i)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The particle filter is **inherently multi-modal**, which makes it ideal for urban canyon scenarios where the receiver may have several plausible positions due to symmetric building geometry — Kalman filters, being uni-modal, would lock onto a single wrong estimate. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Mitigation Techniques | **Technique** | **Sensor Used** | **Working Principle** | **Strengths** | **Weaknesses** | **KTU Weightage** | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **GNSS only** | GNSS receiver | Trilateration from satellites | Simple, low cost | Fails in deep canyon | Low (the baseline) | | **INS / Dead Reckoning** | IMU (gyro + accel) | Integrate acceleration | Works indoors & canyons | Drift over time | Medium | | **Loosely-coupled INS/GNSS** | IMU + GNSS | EKF fusion | Smooth, fills GNSS gaps | Assumes Gaussian errors | High | | **Tightly-coupled INS/GNSS** | IMU + GNSS raw | Uses raw pseudoranges | Robust with <4 sats | More complex | High | | **Map Matching** | GNSS + road map | Snap to nearest road | Eliminates cross-road errors | Requires HD map | Medium | | **Shadow Matching** | GNSS + 3D city model | Use signal blockage info | Reduces NLOS bias | Needs 3D model | High | | **Ray Tracing** | 3D city model + GNSS | Predict which sats are NLOS | Theoretically optimal | Computationally heavy | High | | **LiDAR SLAM** | 3D LiDAR | Scan-match to local map | cm-level accuracy | Drift, costly | Medium | | **Visual SLAM** | Camera | Feature tracking | Cheap, rich data | Lighting dependent | Medium | | **Particle Filter (3D-map-aided)** | GNSS + 3D building model | Bayesian weight update | Handles multipath well | Slow, complex | High | | **UWB / 5G positioning** | UWB anchors / 5G towers | Time-of-arrival trilateration | Robust in canyon | Needs infrastructure | Low-Medium | | **Carrier-phase RTK** | Dual-freq GNSS | cm-level relative positioning | Excellent open-sky | Multipath ruins fix | Medium | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Urban Canyon — GNSS Signal Propagation (Mermaid Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD satA[SATELLITE A
Elevation 80 deg] satB[SATELLITE B
Elevation 45 deg] satC[SATELLITE C
Elevation 15 deg BLOCKED] satD[SATELLITE D
Elevation 25 deg NLOS] bldL[TALL BUILDING
LEFT WALL] bldR[TALL BUILDING
RIGHT WALL] ground[GROUND ROAD] recv[GNSS RECEIVER
ON VEHICLE] reflA[SPECULAR REFLECTOR
GLASS FACADE] proc[POSITION ENGINE
BANK OF FILTERS] resOut[POSITION FIX
ERROR 30 m] satA -->|DIRECT LOS| recv satB -->|DIRECT LOS| recv satC -->|BLOCKED| bldL satD -->|REFLECTED NLOS| reflA reflA -->|BIASED PATH| recv recv --> proc proc --> resOut bldL --- ground bldR --- ground recv --- ground ``` **Interpretation of the diagram:** - **Satellite A** is nearly overhead → strong LOS, healthy pseudorange. - **Satellite B** is at mid-elevation, visible down the street → usable. - **Satellite C** is at low elevation → physically blocked by left building (no signal). - **Satellite D** is geometrically visible but its LOS is blocked; only a **reflected path** off the glass facade of the right building reaches the receiver — this is **NLOS reception**, the most damaging error source. --- ### 4.2 Multi-Sensor Fusion Architecture for Urban Canyon ```mermaid flowchart LR gnss[GNSS RECEIVER
RAW PSEUDORANGE] imu[IMU
ACCEL GYRO] lidar[3D LIDAR] cam[STEREO CAMERA] wheel[WHEEL ODOMETRY] uwb[UWB BEACONS] mapdb[3D CITY MAP DB] pred[PREDICTION MODULE
EKF TIME UPDATE] upd[UPDATE MODULE
EKF MEASUREMENT UPDATE] meas[MEASUREMENT MODEL
LIKELIHOOD FUNCTION] state[STATE VECTOR
x y z vx vy vz roll pitch yaw] out[FUSED POSE
RATE 50 Hz] gnss --> meas lidar --> meas cam --> meas wheel --> pred uwb --> meas imu --> pred mapdb --> meas pred --> state meas --> upd upd --> state state --> out ``` **Interpretation of the architecture:** - The **IMU + wheel odometry** drive the high-rate (≥ 50 Hz) **prediction** step of the EKF, keeping the state vector alive during GNSS blackouts. - The **GNSS, LiDAR, camera, and UWB** provide low-rate (1–10 Hz) **measurement updates** that correct the IMU drift. - The **3D city map** is consulted during the **measurement model** stage to predict which satellites should be **blocked** (and thus their measurements are rejected) and which should be **strong** (and trusted) — this is the heart of **map-aided GNSS**. --- ### 4.3 Shadow Matching — Conceptual Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD start[RECEIVED SNR PER SATELLITE] sky[SKY-PLOT OF SATELLITES] bldg[3D BUILDING MODEL] predP[COMPUTE PREDICTED
BLOCKAGE AT EACH
CANDIDATE POSITION] cmp[COMPARE PREDICTED VS
OBSERVED BLOCKAGE] grid[SCAN GRID OF CANDIDATE
POSITIONS] out[SHADOW MATCHING
LIKELIHOOD MAP] fuse[FUSE WITH PSEUDORANGE
TO GET FINAL FIX] start --> cmp sky --> cmp bldg --> predP predP --> cmp cmp --> grid grid --> out out --> fuse ``` **Interpretation:** Shadow matching uses **only the blockage pattern** (which satellites are seen vs blocked) to constrain the position. Even if the pseudoranges are corrupted by NLOS, the **pattern of visible satellites** uniquely identifies the canyon segment the receiver is in. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** **Q1. Define "urban canyon" in the context of GNSS-based robot navigation. List any two of its characteristic effects on satellite signals.** `[3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** An *urban canyon* is a city street corridor flanked by tall buildings that restrict the sky-view of a GNSS receiver, typically reducing the sky-view factor below 0.5. **Two characteristic effects:** 1. **Signal blockage** of low-elevation satellites due to line-of-sight obstruction by buildings, reducing the number of visible satellites to 3–5. 2. **Multipath and Non-Line-Of-Sight (NLOS) reception**, where signals reach the receiver only after one or more reflections, introducing large positive pseudorange biases (50–300 m) and degrading positioning accuracy. --- **Q2. What is the significance of the sky-view factor (SVF) in urban canyon navigation? How does it affect the position error?** `[3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** The **sky-view factor (SVF)** $\xi$ is the ratio of the visible solid angle of sky to the upper hemisphere ($2\pi$). It is a geometric measure of canyon severity. **Significance:** It directly correlates with the number of usable satellites and the magnitude of multipath/NLOS errors. An empirical relation is $\sigma_{position} \approx k / \xi^2$ with $k \approx 2.5$ m. As $\xi$ decreases, the position error grows **quadratically**, so $\xi = 0.3$ yields $\sigma \approx 28$ m, while $\xi = 0.9$ yields $\sigma \approx 3$ m. Thus SVF is a single scalar that quantifies how badly the canyon will degrade GNSS. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, ESE Model, Module 4]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q(a). Derive the linearized pseudorange observation model for GNSS position estimation. Clearly show how the design matrix $H$ is constructed and how the PDOP is obtained from it.** `[7 Marks]` **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Non-linear pseudorange model:** For satellite $i$, the measured pseudorange is: $$ \rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x)^2 + (y_i - y)^2 + (z_i - z)^2} + c\,\delta t_u + \varepsilon_i $$ where $(x, y, z)$ is the unknown receiver position, $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ is the known satellite position, $c$ is the speed of light, $\delta t_u$ is the receiver clock bias, and $\varepsilon_i$ is the measurement noise. **Step 2 — Linearization via first-order Taylor expansion** about an approximate position $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$: $$ r_i \approx r_{i,0} + \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial x}\bigg|_0 \Delta x + \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial y}\bigg|_0 \Delta y + \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial z}\bigg|_0 \Delta z $$ **Step 3 — Partial derivatives:** $$ \frac{\partial r_i}{\partial x} = -\frac{(x_i - x_0)}{r_{i,0}} = -e_{i,x} $$ Similarly for $y$ and $z$. The terms $(e_{i,x}, e_{i,y}, e_{i,z})$ are the components of the unit vector from receiver to satellite. **Step 4 — Substituting back:** $$ \rho_i - r_{i,0} = -e_{i,x}\Delta x - e_{i,y}\Delta y - e_{i,z}\Delta z + c\,\delta t_u $$ **Step 5 — Stacking $n$ satellite equations** into matrix form $\mathbf{z} = H \mathbf{\Delta x}$: $$ \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \rho_1 - r_{1,0} \\ \rho_2 - r_{2,0} \\ \vdots \\ \rho_n - r_{n,0} \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{z}} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} -e_{1,x} & -e_{1,y} & -e_{1,z} & 1 \\ -e_{2,x} & -e_{2,y} & -e_{2,z} & 1 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ -e_{n,x} & -e_{n,y} & -e_{n,z} & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{H} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \\ \Delta z \\ c\,\delta t_u \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 6 — Least-squares solution:** $$ \mathbf{\Delta x} = (H^T H)^{-1} H^T \mathbf{z} $$ **Step 7 — PDOP derivation.** The covariance of $\mathbf{\Delta x}$ is: $$ P_{\mathbf{\Delta x}} = \sigma_{UERE}^2 (H^T H)^{-1} $$ Define $Q = (H^T H)^{-1}$. Then: $$ PDOP = \sqrt{Q_{11} + Q_{22} + Q_{33}} $$ This is dimensionless and represents the **geometric dilution** of the position fix. **In an urban canyon**, all unit vectors are clustered in a small solid angle, so $H^T H$ becomes near-singular, $Q$ has large entries, and $PDOP$ becomes very large (often $> 6$), directly inflating $\sigma_P = PDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE}$. --- **Q(b). With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a tightly-coupled INS/GNSS system for urban canyon navigation. How does it improve robustness compared to a loosely-coupled system?** `[7 Marks]` **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Tightly-coupled INS/GNSS architecture:** ``` ┌──────────────┐ ┌─────────────────────┐ │ IMU │───▶│ INS Mechanization │──▶ x̂⁻ (predicted state) │ (acc, gyro) │ │ (strapdown integ.) │ └──────────────┘ └────────┬─────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌───────────────┐ │ EKF Predict │ │ P⁻ = FPFᵀ+Q │ └───────┬───────┘ │ (predicted pseudoranges computed using │ candidate position from INS) ▼ ┌──────────────┐ ┌─────────────────────┐ │ GNSS Raw │───▶│ Innovation (z) = │ │ Pseudorange │ │ ρ_meas − ρ_pred │ └──────────────┘ └────────┬─────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌───────────────┐ │ EKF Update │ │ K = P⁻Hᵀ(...) │ │ x̂ = x̂⁻ + Kz │ └───────┬───────┘ │ ▼ ┌───────────────┐ │ x̂ (final) │ │ position, │ │ velocity, │ │ attitude │ └───────────────┘ ``` **Working principle (Step 1):** The INS mechanization integrates IMU outputs to produce a **predicted** pose, velocity, and the **predicted pseudoranges** to all visible satellites (using the known satellite ephemeris). The actual raw GNSS pseudoranges are then subtracted to form an **innovation** vector $z$. **Step 2 — EKF Update:** The Kalman gain $K$ blends the innovation into the INS state using the covariance $P^-$ and measurement noise $R$: $$ K = P^- H^T (H P^- H^T + R)^{-1}, \quad \hat{x} = \hat{x}^- + K z $$ **Step 3 — Why tightly-coupled is better in urban canyons:** | Aspect | Loosely-coupled | Tightly-coupled | |---|---|---| | Input to fusion | Computed (x,y,z) from GNSS | Raw pseudoranges | | Needs ≥ 4 satellites? | **Yes** (to compute fix) | **No** (works with 1–3 sats) | | Behavior in canyon | Whole GNSS solution dropped if < 4 sats | Uses each pseudorange individually | | NLOS handling | Position jumps are fed in → contaminates EKF | Innovations are weighted; outliers can be rejected | | Robustness | Moderate | **High** | **Step 4 — Additional robustness techniques used in tandem:** - **Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (RAIM):** detects and excludes faulty pseudoranges. - **Innovation gating:** rejects innovations exceeding $3\sigma$ (NLOS). - **3D map aiding:** uses a building model to predict which satellites should be blocked. This architecture is the **industry standard** for autonomous vehicles operating in dense cities. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q(a). Explain the phenomenon of multipath and NLOS reception in urban canyons. Derive the expression for the multipath-induced pseudorange bias due to a single specular reflection from a vertical wall.** `[7 Marks]` **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define multipath and NLOS:** - **Multipath:** the GNSS signal reaches the receiver via **two or more paths** — a direct LOS path and one (or more) reflected path(s) from a building, ground, or vehicle surface. - **NLOS reception:** the direct LOS is completely **blocked** and only a reflected signal reaches the receiver; the measured range corresponds to a **virtual satellite** mirrored across the reflecting surface. **Step 2 — Geometry of single specular reflection.** Consider a vertical wall at horizontal distance $d$ from the receiver. Let the satellite have elevation $\theta_e$ and azimuth offset $\Delta\phi$ from the wall normal. The geometric path difference between the reflected path and the (blocked) direct path is: $$ \Delta\rho = 2 d \sin\theta_e \cos(\Delta\phi) $$ **Derivation of the path difference:** The **direct** geometric range from the receiver to the satellite is $r_{LOS}$. The **reflected** path goes from the receiver to a point $P$ on the wall (height of the receiver, since wall is vertical and reflection is specular) and then to the satellite. The path length is: $$ r_{ref} = \sqrt{d^2 + h_P^2} + \sqrt{d'^2 + h_P^2} $$ where $d'$ is the horizontal distance from $P$ to the **mirror image** of the satellite. Using the **law of reflection** (angle of incidence = angle of reflection) and trigonometric identities, the minimum path length occurs when $P$ is on the line connecting the receiver to the satellite's mirror image, and the resulting excess path length is $\Delta\rho = 2 d \sin\theta_e \cos(\Delta\phi)$. **Step 3 — Pseudorange bias:** The receiver measures the **reflected** path length (since the direct LOS is weak/blocked), so the pseudorange is biased by: $$ \delta\rho_{multipath} = 2 d \sin\theta_e \cos(\Delta\phi) $$ For typical values $d = 10$ m, $\theta_e = 30°$, $\Delta\phi = 0°$: $$ \delta\rho_{multipath} = 2 \times 10 \times 0.5 \times 1 = 10 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4 — Carrier-phase effect:** Because the reflected signal has a different phase from the direct signal, the **composite** signal at the receiver shows a phase distortion: $$ \phi_{composite} = \arctan\!\left( \frac{\alpha \sin(\Delta\phi)}{1 + \alpha \cos(\Delta\phi)} \right) $$ where $\alpha$ is the amplitude ratio of reflected to direct signal (typically $0.1$–$0.5$ for diffuse surfaces, up to $0.9$ for glass/metal). **Step 5 — Real-world impact:** A multipath bias of 10 m at the range level translates (after a poor PDOP of, say, 5) to a horizontal position error of up to **50 m**, which is unacceptable for lane-level navigation. Mitigation requires either a 3D city model (to detect and reject reflected signals) or carrier-phase-based techniques. --- **Q(b). Describe the 3D-map-aided shadow matching technique for urban canyon positioning. How does it differ from map matching and ray tracing?** `[7 Marks]` **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — What is shadow matching?** Shadow matching is a **GNSS-only** positioning technique that uses a **3D building model** of the urban environment to predict which satellites should be **visible** (LOS) and which should be **blocked** (shadow) at each candidate receiver position. The position is the one whose predicted blockage pattern **best matches** the observed pattern. **Step 2 — Algorithmic procedure:** **Step 2a — Discretize the search region** (e.g., a 200 m × 200 m grid around the last known fix) into cells of size 5 m × 5 m. **Step 2b — For each candidate cell $x_j$:** - Place a hypothetical receiver at the cell center. - For each satellite $i$ in the almanac, ray-trace from the receiver to the satellite. - If the ray intersects any building face in the 3D model, mark satellite $i$ as **blocked** at $x_j$; otherwise mark it as **visible**. **Step 2c — For each cell, compute a likelihood score:** $$ L(x_j) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log p(s_i \vert x_j, \mathcal{M}) $$ where $s_i$ is the observed signal state (visible/blocked), $\mathcal{M}$ is the 3D map, and $p$ is the likelihood function (often a binary indicator). **Step 2d — Output the position** as the cell with the highest $L$ (or fuse $L$ with pseudorange likelihood in a particle filter). **Step 3 — Comparison with related techniques:** | Technique | Uses | Strength | Weakness | |---|---|---|---| | **Map Matching** | Road network (2D) | Simple, eliminates cross-road errors | Only works on roads; cannot handle off-road | | **Shadow Matching** | 3D building model | Sub-lane accuracy; uses blockage info | Needs accurate 3D model; binary signal state | | **Ray Tracing** | 3D building model | Predicts pseudorange bias (not just blockage) | Computationally heavy (full EM simulation) | | **Particle Filter (3D-map-aided)** | 3D model + pseudoranges | Combines blockage and range info | Slower than pure shadow matching | **Step 4 — Integration with GNSS pseudoranges:** In practice, shadow matching is **fused** with pseudorange positioning in a particle filter framework. Each particle carries a weight that is the product of (a) its shadow-matching likelihood and (b) its pseudrange likelihood. This hybrid approach achieves **sub-5 m accuracy** in deep urban canyons where pure GNSS gives 30–50 m errors. **Step 5 — Limitations:** - Requires an accurate, up-to-date 3D city model. - Only useful in environments with **significant buildings** (otherwise the blockage pattern is uninformative). - Computational cost scales with grid resolution and number of satellites. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Urban Canyon Questions** > > 1. **Confusing multipath with NLOS:** Multipath has BOTH direct and reflected signals; NLOS has ONLY a reflected signal. Marks are lost when students claim "multipath adds noise" without clarifying that NLOS adds a **systematic positive bias** of 50–300 m. > 2. **Forgetting the receiver clock bias** $c\delta t_u$ in the pseudorange equation — this is a 4th unknown requiring a 4th satellite. In an urban canyon with only 3 satellites, the system is **under-determined** and a clock-aiding (e.g., from a stable oscillator) is needed. > 3. **Mixing up HDOP and PDOP:** HDOP is 2D (horizontal only); PDOP is 3D. Numerical answers must match the requested quantity. > 4. **Stating "more satellites = better"** without explaining the **geometric** aspect. The KTU examiner wants you to say "satellites must be well-distributed in azimuth and elevation" — not just numerous. > 5. **Ignoring units in numerical problems:** $\sigma_{UERE}$ is in meters, PDOP is dimensionless, $\sigma_P$ is in meters. A common error is reporting $\sigma_P = 5$ when the calculation gives 5 km because units were mixed. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Urban canyon** = street flanked by tall buildings, sky-view factor $\xi < 0.5$. - [ ] **Three GNSS degradation mechanisms:** Blockage, Multipath, NLOS. - [ ] **Pseudorange equation:** $\rho_i = r_i + c(\delta t_u - \delta t^i) + I_i + T_i + M_i + N_i + \varepsilon_i$ — remember all seven terms. - [ ] **Sky-View Factor:** $\xi = \Omega_{visible} / 2\pi$. Empirical position error: $\sigma \approx k / \xi^2$ with $k \approx 2.5$ m. - [ ] **PDOP formula:** $PDOP = \sqrt{\text{trace}[(H^T H)^{-1}]_{1:3,1:3}}$ — dimensionless, **not** in meters. - [ ] **Position accuracy:** $\sigma_P = PDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE}$ — multiply, don't add. - [ ] **Multipath bias:** $\Delta\rho = 2d\sin\theta_e\cos(\Delta\phi)$ — single-bounce specular reflection. - [ ] **NLOS bias:** positive, 50–300 m, undetectable by RAIM alone. - [ ] **Loosely-coupled INS/GNSS** needs ≥ 4 sats; **Tightly-coupled** works with 1–3 sats — a KTU-favourite comparison. - [ ] **Particle filter weight update:** $w_k^{(i)} \propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert x_k^{(i)})$ — handles non-Gaussian NLOS errors. - [ ] **Shadow matching** uses blockage pattern; **Map matching** uses road network; **Ray tracing** predicts full pseudorange bias. - [ ] **Multi-sensor fusion = the answer** in urban canyons: IMU + GNSS + LiDAR + camera + 3D map. - [ ] **Key real-world constant:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (use $2.998 \times 10^8$ for precision). - [ ] **Numbers to remember:** Open-sky PDOP ≤ 2; canyon PDOP > 6; canyon accuracy without aiding 30–100 m; with fusion < 5 m. - [ ] **Terminology traps:** Multipath ≠ NLOS; HDOP ≠ PDOP; SVF $\in [0, 1]$; DOP is dimensionless.

topics.title: Map Matching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Map Matching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Map Matching** is the computational process of aligning an observed sequence of geographic positions (typically derived from noisy Global Positioning System / GNSS, Inertial Measurement Unit / IMU, or odometry sensors) to a corresponding digital road network, trajectory, or geometric map. The objective is to infer the *true* path or location of a moving agent (robot, vehicle, pedestrian) on a known map by reconciling the discrepancies between raw sensor readings and the underlying spatial structure of the environment.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme taxonomy of Robot Navigation, map matching is classified as a **localization enhancement technique** that bridges dead-reckoning/sensor-based localization with map-based localization, thereby minimizing the lateral positioning error along road centerlines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Map matching is treated as a *probabilistic / geometric fusion problem* — students must be familiar with both classical geometric approaches (point-to-curve projection) and modern probabilistic approaches (Hidden Markov Models, Kalman Filters, Particle Filters) as applied to GPS-to-road alignment.

## 1.2 Intuition & Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are walking through a dense city with your smartphone's GPS showing a blue dot jumping around the buildings. The blue dot occasionally appears *inside* a building or on a non-existent road, even though you are physically walking on a clear pavement. Now, if you overlay a printed street map on your screen and "snap" the blue dot to the nearest pavement at every instant, the trajectory suddenly becomes smooth, logical, and accurate. That snapping process — the act of *matching raw GPS points to a logical map* — is exactly **Map Matching**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy:** Think of map matching as a student (the GPS reading) trying to find the correct bus route on a city map. The student can see the bus stops (map features) and the route (road network), but their sense of direction is slightly off (sensor noise). The map matcher acts like a teacher who constantly guides the student back onto the correct bus route by comparing where the student *thinks* they are versus where the map *says* they should be.

## 1.3 Why Map Matching is Needed

| Source of Error | Impact | How Map Matching Helps |
|-----------------|--------|-------------------------|
| GPS multipath in urban canyons | Position drift up to **50 m** | Snaps to nearest road centerline |
| IMU drift over time | Cumulative trajectory curvature | Re-anchors to map geometry |
| Low satellite visibility | Outliers / jumps | Filters out impossible positions |
| Odometry wheel slip | Position skew during turns | Uses map topology as correction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metric:** A good map matcher reduces lateral (off-road) error from typical GPS noise of **10–15 m RMS** down to a road-snapped accuracy of **2–5 m**, satisfying the **3 m positional accuracy** benchmark required by most intelligent transportation systems.

## 1.4 Inputs and Outputs of a Map Matcher

- **Inputs:**
  - Sequence of raw position fixes: $Z = \{z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_T\}$
  - Digital road network / map: $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ are nodes (junctions) and $E$ are edges (road segments)
  - Sensor characteristics (noise covariance, sampling rate)

- **Outputs:**
  - Matched sequence: $S^* = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_T\}$ where each $s_t \in E$ (a road segment or map element)
  - Updated estimated pose aligned to the map geometry

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Map Matching Algorithms

Map matching algorithms are broadly classified into four generations based on their sophistication:

1. **Geometric (Geometrical) Map Matchers** — Use only the shape/geometry of the GPS track and road network.
2. **Topological Map Matchers** — Add connectivity and turn-restriction information from the road graph.
3. **Probabilistic (Advanced) Map Matchers** — Use formal statistical inference (HMM, Kalman, Particle Filters).
4. **Sensor-Fusion Map Matchers** — Integrate multiple sensors (GPS + IMU + odometry + LiDAR + camera).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** The Module 4 syllabus explicitly emphasizes **Geometric, Topological, and Probabilistic (Kalman / HMM)** approaches. Sensor-fusion variants using LiDAR/camera are typically covered in advanced electives, not the core paper.

## 2.2 Geometric Map Matching — Operational Logic

Geometric map matching operates purely on the Euclidean geometry of points and curves. The three canonical operations are:

### (a) Point-to-Point Matching
- Each GPS point is matched to the *closest node* (junction) on the map.
- **Disadvantage:** Ignores road connectivity — a point equidistant from two parallel roads cannot be disambiguated.

### (b) Point-to-Curve Matching
- Each GPS point is matched to the *closest point on any road curve* (polyline).
- **Standard technique:** Perpendicular projection of the GPS point onto the infinite line extending each road segment.
- This is the most widely used geometric method (employed by simple in-vehicle navigation systems).

### (c) Curve-to-Curve Matching
- The entire GPS trajectory is treated as a curve and compared against road curves.
- **Typical metric:** **Fréchet distance** — intuitively the "leash length" required for a person walking along one curve while another walks along a second curve, both moving forward, to remain connected.
- **Disadvantage:** Computationally expensive, sensitive to trajectory length and outliers.

## 2.3 Topological Map Matching — Operational Logic

Topological map matching extends geometric matching by incorporating the **graph structure** of the road network:

1. Identify candidate road segments within a search radius $R$ (typically **50–100 m**) of each GPS point.
2. Form a candidate set $C_t = \{c_{t,1}, c_{t,2}, \ldots\}$ for each timestamp $t$.
3. Apply **connectivity analysis**: a transition from candidate $c_{t,i}$ to $c_{t+1,j}$ is *valid* only if there exists a path in the road graph between them that does not violate turn restrictions.
4. Score each transition based on (i) perpendicular distance, (ii) heading difference, and (iii) topological reachability.
5. Select the highest-scoring connected path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Topology Matters:** Two parallel roads may be geometrically close, but a vehicle on a highway cannot suddenly appear on a service lane. Topological constraints prune such impossible transitions.

## 2.4 Probabilistic Map Matching

Probabilistic methods model the problem as a statistical inference task, providing mathematically rigorous confidence estimates.

### 2.4.1 Kalman Filter (KF) Based Map Matching
- **State vector:** Vehicle position $(x, y)$ and velocity $(v_x, v_y)$ — or, in 1D form, the position *along* a road.
- **Prediction step:** Standard KF prediction using motion model.
- **Measurement update:** Two measurements combined —
  - GPS reading $z_t$ (noisy)
  - Map constraint: projection of the predicted position onto the nearest road segment (treated as a hard/soft measurement)
- The KF optimally fuses these with the **Kalman gain** $K_t$, yielding a corrected, map-anchored estimate.

### 2.4.2 Hidden Markov Model (HMM) Based Map Matching
This is the **gold standard** in modern map matching literature.

- **Hidden states:** Road segments $s_t \in E$ (we cannot directly observe which segment the vehicle is on).
- **Observations:** GPS points $z_t$.
- **Emission probability** $P(z_t \mid s_t)$: Typically modeled as a **Gaussian** based on the perpendicular distance from $z_t$ to segment $s_t$.

$$P(z_t \mid s_t) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{d(z_t, s_t)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

- **Transition probability** $P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t)$: Based on the difference between the great-circle (or road-network) distance from $s_t$ to $s_{t+1}$ and the GPS displacement $\vert z_{t+1} - z_t \vert$.

- **Decoding:** The **Viterbi algorithm** finds the globally optimal sequence of road segments.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Typical Values / Units | Used In |
|---|------------------|------------|------------------------|---------|
| 1 | Euclidean distance (point-to-point) | $d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ | metres (m) | Geometric MM |
| 2 | Point-to-line perpendicular distance | $d = \frac{\vert (x_2 - x_1)(y_1 - y_0) - (x_1 - x_0)(y_2 - y_1) \vert}{\sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}}$ | m | Point-to-curve MM |
| 3 | Point-to-segment distance (projection parameter $t$) | $t = \frac{(P - A) \cdot (B - A)}{\Vert B - A \Vert^2}$; $d = \Vert P - (A + t(B-A)) \Vert$ if $0 \le t \le 1$, else endpoint distance | m | Point-to-curve MM |
| 4 | Emission probability (HMM, Gaussian) | $P(z_t \mid s_t) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{d^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | dimensionless | Probabilistic MM |
| 5 | HMM transition log-probability | $\log P \propto -\frac{\vert \Vert g_{t \to t+1} \Vert - \Vert z_{t+1} - z_t \Vert \vert}{\beta}$ | dimensionless | HMM MM |
| 6 | Fréchet distance (curve-to-curve) | $F(\gamma, \sigma) = \max_t \min_s d(\gamma(t), \sigma(s))$ | m | Curve-to-curve MM |
| 7 | Kalman state prediction | $\hat{x}_{t \mid t-1} = A \hat{x}_{t-1 \mid t-1} + B u_t$ | m, m/s | KF MM |
| 8 | Kalman measurement update | $\hat{x}_{t \mid t} = \hat{x}_{t \mid t-1} + K_t (z_t - H \hat{x}_{t \mid t-1})$ | m | KF MM |
| 9 | Kalman gain | $K_t = P_{t \mid t-1} H^T (H P_{t \mid t-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$ | dimensionless | KF MM |
| 10 | GPS noise standard deviation (typical) | $\sigma_{GPS}$ | **5–15 m** | All MM |
| 11 | Map-matching search radius $R$ | — | **50–100 m** | Geometric / Topo |
| 12 | Heading difference penalty | $\Delta\theta = \vert \theta_{GPS} - \theta_{road} \vert$ | radians / degrees | Topological MM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on notation:** $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ denotes the Euclidean / L2 norm. $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value of a scalar. $\gamma, \sigma$ are the two trajectories being compared in the Fréchet formulation. The notation $\vert x \vert$ in formulas has been replaced by $\vert x \vert$ (with `\vert` in LaTeX) only where it appears outside table cells; inside table cells, the absolute value is denoted by $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ or by explicit phrasing to comply with the no-pipe-in-tables rule.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Companies like Waymo, Tesla, and Mobileye use HMM-based and particle-filter-based map matching as a pre-processing layer before path planning.
- **Fleet Management & Logistics:** Real-time tracking of delivery vehicles on road networks for ETA prediction.
- **Robotic Vacuum Cleaners / AGVs:** Map matching inside a known building map to refine wheel-odometry estimates.
- **Smartphone Navigation Apps (Google Maps, Waze):** Use lightweight topological + geometric map matching to display the user's location on the correct road even in tunnels or near skyscrapers.
- **Public Transport Analytics:** Matching bus GPS traces to bus-route polylines for schedule adherence analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Point-to-Line Perpendicular Distance

**Given:** A point $P = (x_0, y_0)$ and a line passing through two points $A = (x_1, y_1)$ and $B = (x_2, y_2)$.

**Goal:** Compute the shortest (perpendicular) distance from $P$ to the *infinite line* through $A$ and $B$.

**Step 1 — Vector representation of the line.**

The direction vector of the line $AB$ is:

$$
\vec{d} = B - A = (x_2 - x_1,\ y_2 - y_1)
$$

**Step 2 — Vector from A to P.**

$$
\vec{AP} = P - A = (x_0 - x_1,\ y_0 - y_1)
$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude of the cross product (2D).**

In 2D, the area of the parallelogram formed by $\vec{AP}$ and $\vec{d}$ equals the magnitude of the cross product:

$$
\vert \vec{AP} \times \vec{d} \vert = \vert (x_0 - x_1)(y_2 - y_1) - (y_0 - y_1)(x_2 - x_1) \vert
$$

This scalar represents the *area* of the parallelogram.

**Step 4 — Convert area to perpendicular distance.**

The perpendicular distance is the area divided by the base length $\Vert \vec{d} \Vert$:

$$
d = \frac{\vert \vec{AP} \times \vec{d} \vert}{\Vert \vec{d} \Vert}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the components.**

$$
d = \frac{\vert (x_0 - x_1)(y_2 - y_1) - (y_0 - y_1)(x_2 - x_1) \vert}{\sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This formula gives the shortest distance from the GPS point to the *infinite* line. If the perpendicular foot lies *outside* the actual road segment $AB$, the segment endpoints must be considered — leading to the **point-to-segment** formulation below.

## 3.2 Derivation: Point-to-Segment Distance (with Projection Parameter)

This is the more practical formula used in real map matchers.

**Step 1 — Project P onto the line AB.**

Define the projection parameter:

$$
t = \frac{(P - A) \cdot (B - A)}{\Vert B - A \Vert^2}
$$

where $(P - A) \cdot (B - A) = (x_0 - x_1)(x_2 - x_1) + (y_0 - y_1)(y_2 - y_1)$ is the dot product.

**Step 2 — Geometric meaning of t.**

- If $t \in [0, 1]$: the perpendicular foot $F = A + t(B - A)$ lies *on* the segment $AB$.
- If $t < 0$: foot lies "behind" A — the closest point on the segment is A.
- If $t > 1$: foot lies "beyond" B — the closest point on the segment is B.

**Step 3 — Piecewise distance formula.**

$$
d(P, \overline{AB}) = 
\begin{cases}
\Vert P - (A + t(B - A)) \Vert, & 0 \le t \le 1 \\[4pt]
\min(\Vert P - A \Vert,\ \Vert P - B \Vert), & t < 0 \text{ or } t > 1
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example.**

Let $A = (0, 0)$, $B = (10, 0)$, $P = (5, 3)$.

Compute the projection parameter:

$$
t = \frac{(5)(10) + (3)(0)}{10^2 + 0^2} = \frac{50}{100} = 0.5
$$

Since $0 \le 0.5 \le 1$, the foot is on the segment. The foot is:

$$
F = A + 0.5(B - A) = (0, 0) + 0.5(10, 0) = (5, 0)
$$

The distance is:

$$
d = \Vert (5, 3) - (5, 0) \Vert = \sqrt{0^2 + 3^2} = 3\ \text{metres}
$$

This matches the expected perpendicular distance from $(5, 3)$ to the x-axis.

## 3.3 Derivation: HMM Emission Probability

**Step 1 — Gaussian noise assumption.**

GPS errors along the lateral (perpendicular-to-road) axis are typically modeled as zero-mean Gaussian with variance $\sigma^2$.

**Step 2 — Probability density of observing point $z_t$ given that the vehicle is on segment $s_t$.**

By definition of a Gaussian density evaluated at distance $d$:

$$
P(z_t \mid s_t) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{d(z_t, s_t)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to log-probability for numerical stability.**

For long GPS sequences, raw probabilities underflow. Take the natural log:

$$
\log P(z_t \mid s_t) = -\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi) - \log \sigma - \frac{d(z_t, s_t)^2}{2\sigma^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Drop the constant terms (since Viterbi only compares relative scores).**

The score used in the Viterbi algorithm becomes:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{emission}(z_t, s_t) = -\frac{d(z_t, s_t)^2}{2\sigma^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Implication:** A road segment lying 20 m away from a GPS point with $\sigma = 10$ m contributes a log-score of $-2.0$, while a segment 5 m away contributes $-0.125$ — the closer segment is overwhelmingly preferred.

## 3.4 Derivation: HMM Transition Probability

**Step 1 — GPS displacement vs. road displacement.**

Let $g_t$ = great-circle (or euclidean) distance from GPS point $z_t$ to $z_{t+1}$. Let $r_t(s_t, s_{t+1})$ = shortest path distance along the road network from segment $s_t$ to segment $s_{t+1}$.

**Step 2 — Exponential decay of the difference.**

The transition probability is often modeled as:

$$
P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t) = \frac{1}{\beta} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert g_t - r_t \vert}{\beta}\right)
$$

where $\beta$ is a scaling factor (typically **empirically tuned**, often $\beta \approx 50$ m).

**Step 3 — Log-form for Viterbi.**

$$
\log P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t) = -\log \beta - \frac{\vert g_t - r_t \vert}{\beta}
$$

**Step 4 — Viterbi recursion (final form).**

The optimal path score up to time $t$ ending at segment $s_t$ is:

$$
V_t(s_t) = \mathcal{L}_{emission}(z_t, s_t) + \max_{s_{t-1}} \Big[ V_{t-1}(s_{t-1}) + \log P(s_t \mid s_{t-1}) \Big]
$$

The argmax at each step is stored for **backtracking**, which yields the globally optimal matched sequence.

## 3.5 Summary: Analytical Decision Matrix

| Algorithm | Inputs Used | Computational Cost | Accuracy | Best Use Case |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------|----------|---------------|
| Point-to-Curve (Geometric) | GPS points only | **Lowest** ($O(NM)$) | Low | Rural areas, low GPS noise |
| Topological | GPS + road graph | Moderate ($O(NM^2)$) | Medium | Urban, sparse GPS |
| Kalman Filter | GPS + IMU + map | Low (linear) | Medium-High | Real-time, single-road scenarios |
| HMM / Viterbi | GPS + road graph | High ($O(T \cdot K^2)$) | **Highest** | Offline / batch post-processing |
| Particle Filter | GPS + IMU + map | Very high | Highest (real-time) | Autonomous driving |

> **Legend:** $N$ = number of GPS points, $M$ = average candidate segments per point, $T$ = sequence length, $K$ = candidate set size at each timestep.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Map Matching System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw GPS Sensor Stream] --> B[Pre-processing Module]
    A2[IMU and Odometry Stream] --> B
    A3[Digital Road Network Map] --> C[Candidate Search Module]
    B --> C
    C --> D{Algorithm Selector}
    D --> E[Geometric Matcher]
    D --> F[Topological Matcher]
    D --> G[Probabilistic HMM Matcher]
    D --> H[Kalman Filter Matcher]
    E --> I[Score Aggregation Block]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Viterbi Decoding Engine]
    J --> K[Optimal Matched Sequence]
    K --> L[Map-Anchored Pose Output]
    K --> M[Application Layer Navigation and Tracking]
```

## 4.2 State Machine: Viterbi Decoding for HMM Map Matching

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialize
    Initialize --> CandidateGen: Generate candidate segments within radius R
    CandidateGen --> EmissionScore: Compute Gaussian emission probabilities
    EmissionScore --> TransitionScore: Compute exponential transition probabilities
    TransitionScore --> ViterbiRecurse: Update V_t score for each candidate
    ViterbiRecurse --> CheckConvergence: All timesteps processed
    CheckConvergence --> Backtrack: Trace argmax pointers in reverse
    CheckConvergence --> ViterbiRecurse: Continue to next timestep
    Backtrack --> OutputSequence: Emit optimal road segment sequence
    OutputSequence --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Point-to-Segment Distance Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Point P equals x0 y0] --> B[Compute Vector AP]
    C[Input Segment Endpoints A and B] --> D[Compute Direction Vector d]
    B --> E[Compute Dot Product AP dot d]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compute Projection Parameter t]
    F --> G{Is t in 0 to 1}
    G -->|Yes| H[Foot F equals A plus t times d]
    G -->|No| I[Foot equals nearest endpoint A or B]
    H --> J[Compute Distance equals norm of P minus F]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Output Perpendicular Distance d in metres]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow: Complete Map Matching Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Acquire GPS Trace Z] --> S2[Step 2: Load Digital Map G]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Pre-process and Filter Outliers]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: For each GPS point generate candidate road segments]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute emission probabilities]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Compute transition probabilities]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Run Viterbi algorithm to find optimal path]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Backtrack to retrieve matched road sequence]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Output map-snapped trajectory]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Apply to navigation or analytics]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Map Matching
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

> **Model Answer:**
> Map matching is the computational process of aligning a sequence of observed geographic positions (typically obtained from GPS, IMU, or odometry sensors) with a corresponding digital road network or map, in order to determine the most probable true path or location of a moving agent. It corrects sensor noise and outliers by exploiting the geometric and topological constraints of the map.

*(3 marks — definition, inputs/outputs, purpose)*

### Question 2: What is the difference between Geometric and Topological Map Matching?
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

> **Model Answer:**
> *Geometric map matching* uses only the spatial geometry (distances and angles) between GPS points and map features, with no regard to road connectivity. *Topological map matching* extends this by incorporating the connectivity of the road network graph — including turn restrictions, one-way rules, and adjacency — to prune geometrically plausible but topologically impossible candidate paths. Topological matching is more accurate in complex urban areas.

*(3 marks — 1.5 marks each for the two approaches)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the four main generations / categories of map matching algorithms with examples. Discuss the relative strengths and limitations of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a GPS point $P = (12, 5)$ and a road segment with endpoints $A = (0, 0)$ and $B = (20, 0)$, compute the perpendicular distance from the GPS point to the road segment using the point-to-segment projection formula. Show all steps. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

The four categories are:

1. **Geometric (Point-to-Curve / Curve-to-Curve):**
   - Operates purely on Euclidean geometry.
   - Example: Snapping each GPS point to the nearest point on the nearest road polyline.
   - **Strength:** Simple, fast, low computational cost.
   - **Limitation:** Ignores connectivity; fails on parallel roads.

2. **Topological:**
   - Adds graph-connectivity constraints to geometric matching.
   - Example: Candidate pruning using road graph reachability and turn restrictions.
   - **Strength:** Disambiguates parallel roads and impossible transitions.
   - **Limitation:** Requires a well-structured digital map; still uses heuristics.

3. **Probabilistic (Kalman / HMM / Particle Filter):**
   - Models uncertainty formally using probability theory.
   - Example: HMM-based map matching using Viterbi decoding (Newson & Krumm, 2009).
   - **Strength:** Mathematically rigorous, globally optimal, robust to noise.
   - **Limitation:** Computationally expensive, requires parameter tuning.

4. **Sensor-Fusion:**
   - Integrates multiple sensors (LiDAR, camera, radar) with map matching.
   - Example: Lane-level localization in autonomous vehicles using LiDAR + HD map.
   - **Strength:** Highest accuracy (sub-meter).
   - **Limitation:** Expensive hardware; complex software stack.

*(7 marks: ~1.5 marks per category + 1 mark for comparative summary)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $P = (12, 5)$, $A = (0, 0)$, $B = (20, 0)$.

**Step 1 — Compute direction vector $\vec{d}$:**

$$
\vec{d} = B - A = (20 - 0,\ 0 - 0) = (20, 0)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute vector from A to P:**

$$
\vec{AP} = P - A = (12 - 0,\ 5 - 0) = (12, 5)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute dot product for projection parameter $t$:**

$$
t = \frac{\vec{AP} \cdot \vec{d}}{\Vert \vec{d} \Vert^2} = \frac{(12)(20) + (5)(0)}{20^2 + 0^2} = \frac{240}{400} = 0.6
$$

**Step 4 — Check if $t$ lies in $[0, 1]$:**

Since $0 \le 0.6 \le 1$, the perpendicular foot lies on the segment. ✓

**Step 5 — Compute the foot $F$:**

$$
F = A + t \cdot \vec{d} = (0, 0) + 0.6 \cdot (20, 0) = (12, 0)
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the distance:**

$$
d = \Vert P - F \Vert = \sqrt{(12 - 12)^2 + (5 - 0)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 25} = 5\ \text{metres}
$$

**Final Answer:** The perpendicular distance from the GPS point $P = (12, 5)$ to the road segment $AB$ is **5 metres**.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per step + 1 mark for the final answer with units)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the functional architecture of a typical map matching system. List the major modules and explain the role of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the Hidden Markov Model (HMM) based emission probability for map matching. Why is the natural log of this probability used in practice? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

A typical map matching system consists of the following sequential modules (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the block diagram):

1. **Sensor Input Module:** Receives raw position fixes from GPS, IMU, and odometry sensors.
2. **Pre-processing Module:** Filters outliers, smoothes noise, resamples to uniform time intervals.
3. **Candidate Generation Module:** For each GPS point, identifies the set of road segments lying within a search radius $R$ (typically 50–100 m).
4. **Algorithm Selection Module:** Chooses between geometric, topological, or probabilistic matchers based on environment and accuracy requirements.
5. **Score Aggregation Module:** Computes emission and transition scores.
6. **Decoding Engine (Viterbi):** Finds the globally optimal path through the candidate lattice.
7. **Output Module:** Emits the matched road sequence and the map-anchored pose for downstream applications.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per module + 1 mark for neat block diagram)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — State the Gaussian noise model:**

GPS measurement error in the lateral direction is assumed to follow a zero-mean Gaussian distribution with standard deviation $\sigma$.

**Step 2 — Define the emission probability:**

The probability of observing GPS point $z_t$ given that the true road segment is $s_t$ is:

$$
P(z_t \mid s_t) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{d(z_t, s_t)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

where $d(z_t, s_t)$ is the perpendicular distance from the GPS point to the segment.

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm:**

$$
\log P(z_t \mid s_t) = -\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi) - \log \sigma - \frac{d(z_t, s_t)^2}{2\sigma^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Justification for using log-probability:**

- **Numerical stability:** Raw probabilities for long sequences become vanishingly small (underflow). The log-form keeps values in a manageable numerical range.
- **Computational efficiency:** Log converts multiplications into additions, which the Viterbi max-plus operation handles efficiently.
- **Constant terms can be dropped:** Only the relative score matters for argmax selection; hence the score is reduced to $-d^2 / (2\sigma^2)$.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for stating the model, 2 marks for the derivation, 3 marks for the log justification)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Map Matching Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to clamp the projection parameter $t$:** Students often compute $t$ but fail to check if $0 \le t \le 1$. If $t$ is outside this range, the formula must switch to endpoint distance — not perpendicular distance. **Penalty: up to 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing point-to-LINE and point-to-SEGMENT:** The infinite-line formula and the segment formula yield different answers when the foot lies outside the segment. **Penalty: up to 1.5 marks.**
> 3. **Omitting units:** Always state the final distance in **metres (m)**. **Penalty: 0.5 marks.**
> 4. **Skipping the Viterbi backtracking step:** The Viterbi algorithm requires storing the argmax at each step. Students who only compute forward scores without backtracking cannot recover the optimal path. **Penalty: up to 2 marks in 14-mark questions.**
> 5. **Using the wrong noise model:** GPS errors are NOT uniform — they are *approximately Gaussian in the lateral direction* but may be biased in tunnels/urban canyons. Mentioning this nuance earns bonus credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Map matching** aligns noisy sensor trajectories to a digital map to recover the *true* path of a moving agent.
- The **four categories** are: Geometric, Topological, Probabilistic (KF/HMM/PF), and Sensor-Fusion. **HMM is the gold standard** for offline accuracy.
- The **point-to-segment distance** uses the projection parameter $t$:

$$
t = \frac{(P - A) \cdot (B - A)}{\Vert B - A \Vert^2}
$$

If $t \in [0, 1]$: use perpendicular distance; otherwise: use the nearer endpoint.

- The **HMM emission probability** is Gaussian in the perpendicular distance:

$$
P(z_t \mid s_t) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{d^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

- The **HMM transition probability** is exponential in the difference between GPS displacement and road-network displacement:

$$
P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert g_t - r_t \vert}{\beta}\right)
$$

- The **Viterbi algorithm** finds the globally optimal sequence via forward recursion + backtracking.
- The **Kalman filter** performs map matching by treating the projection onto the road as a measurement update.
- The **Fréchet distance** is the standard curve-to-curve similarity metric, intuitively the "leash length" between two curves.
- **Typical accuracy:** Map matching reduces lateral error from **10–15 m GPS RMS** to **2–5 m road-snapped** accuracy.
- **Search radius** $R$ is typically **50–100 m** for candidate generation.
- **Use log-probabilities** in HMM to avoid numerical underflow and to convert multiplications to additions.
- **Topological constraints** (turn restrictions, one-way rules) are critical for disambiguating parallel roads.
- **Key applications:** Autonomous driving, fleet tracking, smartphone navigation, AGV localization, public transport analytics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dead Reckoning - Inertial Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dead Reckoning - Inertial Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dead Reckoning (DR)** is a self-contained, non-referenced method of robot localization in which the current pose (position $\mathbf{p}(t)$ and orientation $\boldsymbol{\theta}(t)$) of a mobile platform is estimated by integrating (numerically accumulating) the outputs of onboard **Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs)** relative to a known initial state.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the pose vector at discrete time-step $k$ is propagated as:

$$\mathbf{x}_k = f(\mathbf{x}_{k-1}, \mathbf{u}_k) + \mathbf{w}_k$$

where $\mathbf{u}_k$ is the inertial sensor measurement (acceleration + angular velocity), $f(\cdot)$ is the nonlinear motion model, and $\mathbf{w}_k$ is the **process noise** vector drawn from a zero-mean Gaussian distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inertial Sensors** are electromechanical transducers that measure kinematic quantities (linear acceleration $\mathbf{a}$ and angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}$) referenced entirely to an internal inertial frame, **without relying on external infrastructure** (no GPS, no landmarks, no beacons). The two primary classes are **Accelerometers** and **Gyroscopes**, often co-packaged into an **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are **blindfolded inside a dark, unfamiliar room** and asked to walk from one corner to the opposite corner and stop. You cannot see the walls. What can you do?

- You feel the **pull of the floor** (gravity) on your feet.
- You sense your **body's tilt** (whether you are leaning forward or upright).
- You count your **steps** and estimate **how long** each step was.
- You estimate your **turning angle** when you pivot.

This is *exactly* what a robotic system does with inertial sensors:
- The **accelerometer** measures the reaction force on a tiny proof mass (analogous to your inner ear's vestibular system sensing gravity and motion).
- The **gyroscope** measures how fast the sensor body is rotating (analogous to your sense of turning while spinning).
- The **microprocessor** double-integrates acceleration to obtain position and integrates angular velocity to obtain heading.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a blindfolded walker gradually drifts off course because each step has a tiny unknown length error, an inertial navigation system accumulates **drift** over time. This is the central engineering challenge of inertial dead reckoning.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.80665 m/s²** |
| Earth rotation rate | $\Omega_E$ | **7.2921 × 10⁻⁵ rad/s** |
| Standard IMU update rate | $f_s$ | **100 Hz – 1 kHz** |
| Tactical-grade gyro bias | $b_g$ | **1 – 10 deg/hr** |
| MEMS-grade gyro bias | $b_g$ | **10 – 1000 deg/hr** |
| Navigation-grade gyro bias | $b_g$ | **< 0.01 deg/hr** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Dead Reckoning Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic

The inertial dead reckoning process is fundamentally a **two-stage integration cascade**:

### Stage 1 — Gyroscope Channel (Orientation Estimation)
The gyroscope outputs the angular velocity vector expressed in the **body frame** $\{B\}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\omega}_{B}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} \omega_x \\ \omega_y \\ \omega_z \end{bmatrix} \quad [\text{rad/s}]$$

1. **Subtract the bias** $b_g$ to remove the deterministic offset:
   $$\boldsymbol{\omega}_{corrected}(t) = \boldsymbol{\omega}_B(t) - \mathbf{b}_g$$
2. **Integrate angular velocity** to obtain the change in orientation. For a ZYX Euler angle parameterization $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ corresponding to roll, pitch, yaw:
   $$\boldsymbol{\theta}(t) = \boldsymbol{\theta}(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} \boldsymbol{\omega}_{corrected}(\tau) \, d\tau$$
3. **Convert** the Euler angles (or a quaternion $\mathbf{q}$) into a rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}_B^W$ that maps body-frame vectors into the world frame.

### Stage 2 — Accelerometer Channel (Position Estimation)
The accelerometer measures **specific force** $\mathbf{f}_{B}$ (acceleration minus gravity, in $\text{m/s}^2$):

$$\mathbf{f}_B(t) = \mathbf{a}_B(t) - \mathbf{R}_W^B \mathbf{g}^W$$

where $\mathbf{g}^W = [0, 0, -g]^T$ is the gravity vector in the world frame.

1. **Subtract bias** $b_a$ from raw accelerometer output.
2. **Rotate** the specific force into the world frame using the rotation matrix from Stage 1:
   $$\mathbf{a}^W(t) = \mathbf{R}_B^W \, \mathbf{f}_{B,corrected}(t) + \mathbf{g}^W$$
3. **Integrate once** to get linear velocity:
   $$\mathbf{v}(t) = \mathbf{v}(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} \mathbf{a}^W(\tau) \, d\tau$$
4. **Integrate again** to get position:
   $$\mathbf{p}(t) = \mathbf{p}(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} \mathbf{v}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two integrations?** The accelerometer outputs acceleration (the *second derivative* of position). Each integration step multiplies a small measurement error by time, so errors grow as $\mathcal{O}(t^2)$ for velocity and $\mathcal{O}(t^3)$ for position. This is the **fundamental drift problem** of pure inertial navigation.

---

## 2.2 Sensor Error Model (KTU High-Yield)

A realistic low-cost MEMS inertial sensor output is modeled as:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{u}}(t) = \mathbf{u}_{true}(t) + \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{S} \, \mathbf{u}_{true}(t) + \mathbf{N}(t) + \mathbf{c}(t)$$

where:
- $\mathbf{b}$ = **bias** (deterministic turn-on offset + random walk)
- $\mathbf{S}$ = **scale-factor and cross-coupling matrix**
- $\mathbf{N}(t)$ = **white measurement noise**
- $\mathbf{c}(t)$ = **temperature-dependent compensation residual**

The bias itself evolves as a random walk:
