$$\dot{\mathbf{b}}(t) = \mathbf{n}_b(t), \quad \mathbf{n}_b \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \boldsymbol{\sigma}_b^2 \mathbf{I})$$

> [!NOTE]
> The random-walk nature of the bias is what makes long-duration inertial-only navigation impractical without periodic **aiding** (e.g., GPS, wheel odometry, visual landmarks).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{v}(t) = \mathbf{v}_0 + \int \mathbf{a}(t) dt$ | Velocity from acceleration | m/s |
| 2 | $\mathbf{p}(t) = \mathbf{p}_0 + \int \mathbf{v}(t) dt$ | Position from velocity | m |
| 3 | $\boldsymbol{\theta}(t) = \boldsymbol{\theta}_0 + \int \boldsymbol{\omega}(t) dt$ | Orientation from angular rate | rad |
| 4 | $\sigma_p^2(t) \propto t^3$ | Position error growth (constant bias) | m² |
| 5 | $\sigma_\theta^2(t) \propto t$ | Orientation error growth (gyro white noise) | rad² |
| 6 | $\dot{\mathbf{b}}_a = \mathbf{n}_b$, $\dot{\mathbf{b}}_g = \mathbf{n}_g$ | Bias random walk model | — |
| 7 | $\mathbf{R}_B^W \in SO(3)$ | Body-to-world rotation matrix | — |
| 8 | $\mathbf{f}_B = \mathbf{a}_B - \mathbf{R}_W^B \mathbf{g}^W$ | Specific force definition | m/s² |
| 9 | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{q} \otimes \boldsymbol{\omega}_q$ | Quaternion kinematics | — |
| 10 | $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{I} + \begin{bmatrix} s_x & m_{xy} & m_{xz} \\ m_{yx} & s_y & m_{yz} \\ m_{zx} & m_{zy} & s_z \end{bmatrix}$ | Scale-factor + misalignment | — |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inertial dead reckoning underpins:
- **Aerospace**: Aircraft AHRS (Attitude and Heading Reference Systems), spacecraft attitude control.
- **Marine**: Submarine INS (Inertial Navigation System) operating underwater for weeks without GPS.
- **Automotive**: ESP (Electronic Stability Program), airbag trigger decision.
- **Robotics**: Drone flight controllers (Pixhawk, ArduPilot), humanoid balance, AGV fallback localization when GPS is denied.
- **Consumer Electronics**: Screen rotation in smartphones, step counting in fitness bands.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU-style numericals, always check whether the problem gives **angular rate in deg/s or rad/s** — students consistently lose marks by mixing units. Convert using $1 \text{ rad/s} = 57.2958 \text{ deg/s}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Position Error Growth from a Constant Accelerometer Bias

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU Module 4 — it explains *why* inertial systems drift.

**Given:** A 3-axis accelerometer with a constant deterministic bias $\mathbf{b}_a$ (vector of constant offsets, m/s²). The true acceleration is $\mathbf{a}_{true}(t)$ and the measured acceleration is $\tilde{\mathbf{a}}(t) = \mathbf{a}_{true}(t) + \mathbf{b}_a$.

**Find:** The position error $\delta \mathbf{p}(t)$ accumulated over the interval $[0, t]$.

**Step 1 — Velocity error after single integration:**

The estimated velocity is computed by integrating the measured (biased) signal:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{v}}(t) = \mathbf{v}_0 + \int_{0}^{t} \tilde{\mathbf{a}}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

Expanding the integral:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{v}}(t) = \mathbf{v}_0 + \int_{0}^{t} \big( \mathbf{a}_{true}(\tau) + \mathbf{b}_a \big) \, d\tau$$

Since $\mathbf{b}_a$ is constant, it factors out of the integral:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{v}}(t) = \mathbf{v}_0 + \int_{0}^{t} \mathbf{a}_{true}(\tau) \, d\tau + \mathbf{b}_a \int_{0}^{t} d\tau$$

Performing the second integration (constant integrand):

$$\tilde{\mathbf{v}}(t) = \mathbf{v}_{true}(t) + \mathbf{b}_a \cdot t$$

Therefore the **velocity error** is:

$$\delta \mathbf{v}(t) = \tilde{\mathbf{v}}(t) - \mathbf{v}_{true}(t) = \mathbf{b}_a \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Position error after double integration:**

The estimated position is:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{p}}(t) = \mathbf{p}_0 + \int_{0}^{t} \tilde{\mathbf{v}}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

Substituting the expression from Step 1:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{p}}(t) = \mathbf{p}_0 + \int_{0}^{t} \big( \mathbf{v}_{true}(\tau) + \mathbf{b}_a \cdot \tau \big) \, d\tau$$

Splitting the integral:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{p}}(t) = \mathbf{p}_0 + \int_{0}^{t} \mathbf{v}_{true}(\tau) \, d\tau + \mathbf{b}_a \int_{0}^{t} \tau \, d\tau$$

Evaluating the polynomial integral:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{p}}(t) = \mathbf{p}_{true}(t) + \mathbf{b}_a \cdot \frac{t^2}{2}$$

Therefore the **position error** is:

$$\delta \mathbf{p}(t) = \frac{1}{2} \, \mathbf{b}_a \, t^2$$

**Step 3 — Error variance for random bias:**

If $\mathbf{b}_a$ is treated as a zero-mean random constant with covariance $\boldsymbol{\sigma}_a^2 \mathbf{I}$, the position error covariance is:

$$\boldsymbol{\sigma}_p^2(t) = \frac{1}{4} \, \boldsymbol{\sigma}_a^2 \, t^4$$

This confirms the $\mathcal{O}(t^2)$ deterministic drift and $\mathcal{O}(t^4)$ variance growth for a **constant** accelerometer bias.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **time-varying (random-walk) bias** $\dot{\mathbf{b}}_a = \mathbf{n}_b$ with PSD $S_b$, the position error growth becomes $\sigma_p(t) \propto t^{5/2}$, which is the bound used in **IEEE Std 1554** for inertial system specification.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Discrete-Time Strapdown Integration (KTU Exam Staple)

Modern IMUs operate in discrete time at sample period $T_s$. The continuous integrals are replaced by summations.

**Velocity update (using trapezoidal rule for higher accuracy):**

$$\mathbf{v}_{k} = \mathbf{v}_{k-1} + \frac{T_s}{2} \big( \mathbf{a}_{k-1}^W + \mathbf{a}_{k}^W \big)$$

**Position update:**

$$\mathbf{p}_{k} = \mathbf{p}_{k-1} + \frac{T_s}{2} \big( \mathbf{v}_{k-1} + \mathbf{v}_{k} \big)$$

**Orientation update (quaternion form, first-order):**

$$\mathbf{q}_k = \mathbf{q}_{k-1} \otimes \exp\!\left( \frac{T_s}{2} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \boldsymbol{\omega}_k \end{bmatrix} \right)$$

where the exponential map for a pure quaternion $\boldsymbol{\omega} = [\omega_x, \omega_y, \omega_z]^T$ is:

$$\exp\!\left( \frac{T_s}{2} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \boldsymbol{\omega} \end{bmatrix} \right) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\tfrac{1}{2} \Vert \boldsymbol{\omega} \Vert T_s) \\ \hat{\boldsymbol{\omega}} \sin(\tfrac{1}{2} \Vert \boldsymbol{\omega} \Vert T_s) \end{bmatrix}$$

with $\hat{\boldsymbol{\omega}} = \boldsymbol{\omega} / \Vert \boldsymbol{\omega} \Vert$ being the unit axis of rotation.

**Step-by-step simplification for the case $\Vert \boldsymbol{\omega} \Vert T_s \ll 1$** (small-angle approximation, valid for high-rate MEMS IMUs):

$$\cos\!\left( \tfrac{1}{2} \Vert \boldsymbol{\omega} \Vert T_s \right) \approx 1, \quad \sin\!\left( \tfrac{1}{2} \Vert \boldsymbol{\omega} \Vert T_s \right) \approx \tfrac{1}{2} \Vert \boldsymbol{\omega} \Vert T_s$$

So the incremental rotation quaternion reduces to:

$$\Delta \mathbf{q} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ \tfrac{1}{2} \boldsymbol{\omega}_k T_s \end{bmatrix}$$

After re-normalization to enforce $\Vert \mathbf{q}_k \Vert = 1$:

$$\mathbf{q}_k = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \tfrac{1}{4} \Vert \boldsymbol{\omega}_k \Vert^2 T_s^2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ \tfrac{1}{2} \boldsymbol{\omega}_k T_s \end{bmatrix} \otimes \mathbf{q}_{k-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The re-normalization step is **mandatory** in practice because floating-point rounding causes the quaternion magnitude to drift from unity. A non-unit quaternion does not represent a pure rotation and corrupts the rotation matrix.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Error Propagation in Strapdown Heading

For a constant gyro bias $\mathbf{b}_g$ around the vertical (yaw) axis, the heading error is:

$$\delta \psi(t) = b_{g,z} \cdot t$$

This heading error **couples into the position error** through the rotated acceleration. If the platform moves at constant velocity $\mathbf{v} = [v_x, v_y, 0]^T$ in the world frame, the cross-track position error due to heading error is:

$$\delta p_{CT}(t) = v \int_0^t \sin(\delta \psi(\tau)) \, d\tau \approx v \, b_{g,z} \int_0^t \tau \, d\tau = \tfrac{1}{2} v \, b_{g,z} \, t^2$$

Notice the **same $t^2$ growth** as a position-domain accelerometer bias — but here the constant is $v \cdot b_{g,z}$ rather than $\tfrac{1}{2} b_a$. This shows that **a small angular-rate error becomes equivalent to a large position error** at high translational speeds.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Strapdown Inertial Dead Reckoning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SENSOR[ACCEL_GYRO_RAW_SENSOR_DATA] --> BIAS[BIAS_AND_SCALE_COMPENSATION]
    BIAS --> GYROCH[GYRO_INTEGRATION_BLOCK]
    BIAS --> ACCELCH[ACCEL_FRAME_PROJECTION_BLOCK]
    GYROCH --> QUAT[QUATERNION_TO_ROTATION_MATRIX]
    QUAT --> ACCELCH
    ACCELCH --> GRAV[GRAVITY_COMPENSATION]
    GRAV --> VINT[VELOCITY_INTEGRATION]
    VINT --> PINT[POSITION_INTEGRATION]
    PINT --> POSE[FULL_POSE_VECTOR_OUTPUT]
    GYROCH --> POSE
    POSE --> DRIFT[DRIFT_MONITOR_AND_AIDING_REQUEST]
    DRIFT -.-> SENSOR
```

**Reading guide for the diagram:**
- The **upper branch** (gyroscope) drives orientation estimation.
- The **lower branch** (accelerometer) is *gated* by the orientation from the upper branch — it needs the rotation matrix to project body-frame acceleration into the world frame.
- The **dashed feedback line** represents external aiding (GPS, wheel odometry, UWB) that periodically re-anchors the pose to suppress drift.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Strapdown vs Gimbaled INS Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GIMB[GIMBALED_INS]
        G1[GIMBAL_MECHANICAL_PLATFORM] --> G2[GYROS_STABILIZE_PLATFORM]
        G2 --> G3[ALWAYS_ALIGNED_TO_NAV_FRAME]
    end
    subgraph STRAP[STRAPDOWN_INS]
        S1[SENSOR_RIGIDLY_MOUNTED_TO_BODY] --> S2[SOFTWARE_COMPUTES_ROTATION]
        S2 --> S3[HIGHER_VIBRATION_AND_BANDWIDTH]
    end
    GIMB --> COMP[TRADE_OFF_ANALYSIS]
    STRAP --> COMP
```

| Property | Gimbaled INS | Strapdown INS |
|---|---|---|
| Computational load | Low | High |
| Mechanical complexity | High (gimbal motors, bearings) | Low (no moving parts) |
| Size / weight | Large | Compact (MEMS-friendly) |
| Bandwidth | Limited by gimbal dynamics | Very high (kHz possible) |
| Cost | Very high | Low (consumer-grade possible) |
| Modern use | Strategic missiles, submarines | Drones, robots, smartphones, cars |

> [!NOTE]
> All modern robotic platforms use **strapdown** configurations. KTU exam questions on inertial sensors almost exclusively test strapdown mathematics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a gyroscope and an accelerometer in an IMU. What kinematic quantity does each measure, and in which reference frame is the output expressed?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Gyroscope | Accelerometer |
|---|---|---|
| Measured quantity | Angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ (rad/s) | Specific force $\mathbf{f}$ (m/s²) |
| Physical principle | Coriolis force on vibrating mass (MEMS) or Sagnac effect (optical) | Displacement of proof mass under Newton's 2nd law |
| Output frame | Body frame $\{B\}$ | Body frame $\{B\}$ (non-gravitational acceleration only) |
| Integration gives | Orientation (rotation) | Velocity, then position |

> **Mnemonic:** *Gyro = rotation rate; Accel = specific force (not total acceleration, since gravity is removed).*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Why does pure inertial dead reckoning exhibit unbounded error growth over time? Quantify the dominant term for position error if the accelerometer has a constant bias $b_a$.**

**Model Answer:**

Inertial dead reckoning is a **double integration** of noisy acceleration. A constant bias $b_a$ integrated twice produces a quadratic position error:

$$\delta p(t) = \tfrac{1}{2} b_a \, t^2$$

If $b_a$ is random with variance $\sigma_a^2$, the position error standard deviation grows as $\sigma_p(t) \approx \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma_a \, t^2$, i.e., proportional to $t^2$. Since $t$ is unbounded, the error is unbounded. This is why **external aiding** (GPS, UWB, visual odometry) is required for long-duration operation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU ESE Convention):** *Answer any ONE full question from the choice below. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive, starting from first principles, the expression for position error of a vehicle performing pure inertial dead reckoning in the presence of a constant accelerometer bias $b_a$ along the forward axis. Assume the vehicle is stationary at $t=0$ with zero initial velocity. Show every integration step explicitly.

**(b)** A wheeled mobile robot is fitted with a MEMS IMU. The gyroscope reports a constant bias of $b_g = 0.5°/\text{s}$ around the vertical (yaw) axis. The robot moves at a constant forward speed of $v = 1.2$ m/s. Compute the **cross-track position error** after $t = 60$ s of operation. Comment on the practical implications.

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Define the measured acceleration.** With the true acceleration $a_{true}(t) = 0$ (vehicle is stationary) and a constant forward bias $b_a$:

$$\tilde{a}(t) = a_{true}(t) + b_a = b_a \quad \text{(constant)}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate once for velocity.** Using the fundamental theorem of calculus with $v(0) = 0$:

$$v(t) = v(0) + \int_0^t \tilde{a}(\tau) \, d\tau = 0 + \int_0^t b_a \, d\tau = b_a \, t$$

**Step 3 — Integrate again for position.** With $p(0) = 0$:

$$p(t) = p(0) + \int_0^t v(\tau) \, d\tau = \int_0^t b_a \, \tau \, d\tau = b_a \cdot \frac{t^2}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Define the position error.** The true position is $p_{true}(t) = 0$, so:

$$\boxed{\delta p(t) = \tfrac{1}{2} b_a \, t^2}$$

**Step 5 — Discuss error growth.** Differentiate to confirm the velocity error is linear in time: $\delta v(t) = b_a t$. Differentiate again to confirm the acceleration error is constant: $\delta a = b_a$. Hence each successive integral **lifts the polynomial degree by one**. **(+1 mark for the trend discussion.)**

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Convert the gyro bias to SI units.** The bias is given in degrees per second:

$$b_g = 0.5 \, \frac{°}{s} = 0.5 \times \frac{\pi}{180} \, \frac{\text{rad}}{s} = 8.7266 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the heading error at $t = 60$ s.** From the strapdown heading error formula:

$$\delta \psi(t) = b_g \cdot t = (8.7266 \times 10^{-3})(60) = 0.5236 \, \text{rad} = 30°$$

**Step 3 — Compute the cross-track position error.** The position error grows quadratically with time. Substituting the given values:

$$\delta p_{CT} = \tfrac{1}{2} v \, b_g \, t^2$$

$$\delta p_{CT} = \tfrac{1}{2} (1.2) (8.7266 \times 10^{-3}) (60)^2$$

$$\delta p_{CT} = \tfrac{1}{2} (1.2) (8.7266 \times 10^{-3}) (3600)$$

$$\delta p_{CT} = 0.6 \times 8.7266 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600$$

$$\delta p_{CT} = 0.6 \times 31.416$$

$$\boxed{\delta p_{CT} \approx 18.85 \, \text{m}}$$

**Step 4 — Practical implications (1 mark).** Over just one minute, a tiny $0.5°/\text{s}$ gyro bias produces nearly **19 m of cross-track drift** in a robot moving at walking pace. This confirms that **MEMS-grade IMUs are unsuitable for long-range inertial-only navigation**; they must be fused with wheel odometry, GPS, or visual-inertial odometry to remain bounded.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a strapdown inertial navigation system. Identify the role of the rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}_B^W$ in the accelerometer channel.

**(b)** A 2D differential-drive robot is equipped with a single-axis gyroscope that reports angular velocity $\omega_z = 0.05$ rad/s with a standard deviation of $\sigma_\omega = 0.001$ rad/s. The robot operates for $T = 100$ s. Compute the **standard deviation of the heading error** and the resulting **expected RMS cross-track position error** if the linear speed is $v = 0.5$ m/s.

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Block diagram and explanation (5 marks):**

The strapdown INS consists of:
1. **Sensor block (IMU)**: a rigid body carrying 3-axis gyroscopes and 3-axis accelerometers, fixed to the vehicle chassis.
2. **Compensation block**: removes bias, scale-factor errors, and known temperature effects.
3. **Gyro integration block**: integrates $\boldsymbol{\omega}_B$ to update the orientation quaternion $\mathbf{q}$.
4. **Rotation matrix block**: converts $\mathbf{q}$ to $\mathbf{R}_B^W$ (the body-to-world rotation matrix).
5. **Frame-projection block**: rotates the accelerometer output $\mathbf{f}_B$ by $\mathbf{R}_B^W$ to get world-frame acceleration.
6. **Gravity compensation block**: adds $\mathbf{g}^W = [0, 0, -g]^T$ to recover true world-frame acceleration.
7. **Velocity integration block**: integrates acceleration to get $\mathbf{v}^W$.
8. **Position integration block**: integrates velocity to get $\mathbf{p}^W$.
9. **Output**: combined pose vector $[\mathbf{p}^W, \mathbf{q}]$.

**Role of $\mathbf{R}_B^W$ in the accelerometer channel (2 marks):**

The accelerometer measures specific force in the **body frame**, but the world-frame navigation equations require the acceleration vector in the **world frame**. The rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}_B^W$ is the coordinate transformation that maps a body-frame vector into the world frame:

$$\mathbf{a}^W = \mathbf{R}_B^W \, \mathbf{f}_B + \mathbf{g}^W$$

Without this rotation, the system could not separate gravitational from translational acceleration correctly when the vehicle is tilted (e.g., a robot on a ramp would falsely read part of $g$ as horizontal acceleration).

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute the heading error standard deviation.** For a white-noise angular rate, the integrated heading error is a random walk with variance growing linearly in time:

$$\sigma_\psi^2(t) = \sigma_\omega^2 \cdot t$$

At $t = T = 100$ s:

$$\sigma_\psi = \sigma_\omega \sqrt{T} = 0.001 \times \sqrt{100} = 0.001 \times 10 = 0.01 \, \text{rad}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_\psi = 0.01 \, \text{rad} \approx 0.573°}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the RMS cross-track position error.** Using the heading-error-to-position coupling formula (with the small-angle approximation $\sin(\delta\psi) \approx \delta\psi$):

$$\delta p_{CT}(t) = v \int_0^t \delta\psi(\tau) \, d\tau$$

For a random-walk heading process, the standard deviation of the integrated heading itself is:

$$\sigma_{\psi_{int}}^2(t) = \sigma_\omega^2 \int_0^t \int_0^t \min(\tau_1, \tau_2) \, d\tau_1 \, d\tau_2 = \tfrac{1}{3} \sigma_\omega^2 t^3$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_{p_{CT}}(t) = v \cdot \sigma_{\psi_{int}}(t) = v \cdot \sigma_\omega \cdot t^{3/2} \cdot \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

Substituting $v = 0.5$ m/s, $\sigma_\omega = 0.001$ rad/s, $t = 100$ s:

$$\sigma_{p_{CT}} = 0.5 \times 0.001 \times (100)^{3/2} \times \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

$$(100)^{3/2} = 1000$$

$$\sigma_{p_{CT}} = 0.5 \times 0.001 \times 1000 \times 0.5774$$

$$\sigma_{p_{CT}} = 0.5 \times 0.5774 = 0.2887 \, \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{p_{CT}} \approx 0.289 \, \text{m}}$$

**Step 3 — Comment.** After 100 s, the expected cross-track deviation is about **29 cm** — usable for indoor mobile robotics, but still meaningful for precision tasks. Combining the gyroscope with wheel odometry via a **Kalman filter** would reduce this by an order of magnitude.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Students frequently forget to convert **deg/s → rad/s** before plugging into trigonometric formulas. This single omission can cost 2–3 marks silently.
> 2. The **heading-to-position coupling** ($\tfrac{1}{2} v b_g t^2$) is a favorite KTU trap question. Marks are lost when students write only $b_g t$ (heading) and stop, without propagating it into position.
> 3. Do not write "drift" without specifying the **order** ($t$, $t^2$, $t^{5/2}$). Examiners explicitly look for the time exponent as it indicates whether you understand the integration order.
> 4. When asked to "explain with a block diagram," a **flow diagram with labeled blocks** earns more credit than prose; a missing rotation-matrix block is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 5. Quaternion answers must include the **re-normalization step**; pure first-order Euler integration of the rotation matrix loses marks because the matrix can become non-orthogonal.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Dead reckoning is a self-contained, integration-based localization method** — no external references required, but error grows without bound.
- **IMU = Gyroscope + Accelerometer** packaged together; some IMUs also include a magnetometer (MIMU = Magnetic IMU).
- **Accelerometer measures specific force, not total acceleration.** Gravity must be added back in the world frame.
- **Gyroscope measures angular velocity.** Integrate once to get orientation change.
- **Two integrations**: acceleration → velocity (1st) → position (2nd). Errors propagate as $t$, $t^2$, $t^3$ for accel noise; $t^{1/2}$, $t$, $t^{3/2}$ for gyro noise.
- **Constant accelerometer bias produces $t^2$ position drift.** Random-walk bias produces $t^{5/2}$ RMS position drift.
- **Heading error couples into cross-track position error** with the same $t^2$ growth as a constant accel bias — a tiny gyro bias is deadly at high speed.
- **Strapdown INS** mounts sensors rigidly to the body and computes rotations in software; **gimbaled INS** mechanically stabilizes the platform.
- **Strapdown math uses quaternions** (not Euler angles) to avoid gimbal lock; quaternions must be re-normalized after each update.
- **Rotation matrix** $\mathbf{R}_B^W$ is essential to project body-frame accelerometer output into the world frame before integration.
- **Pure inertial navigation drifts** — always combine with an absolute reference (GPS, UWB, LiDAR, vision) using a **Kalman filter or factor graph**.
- **Unit conversions are critical**: deg ↔ rad, deg/s ↔ rad/s, $g$ ↔ m/s².
- **Error growth order** is a KTU favorite: be ready to write $t$, $t^2$, $t^{3/2}$, $t^{5/2}$ for the various noise types and integration levels.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incorporating GNSS Data with INS - Modified Particle Filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Incorporating GNSS Data with INS - Modified Particle Filter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**GNSS/INS Integration** is a multi-sensor data fusion paradigm in which the **high-frequency, short-term stable** inertial measurements from an Inertial Navigation System (accelerometers and gyroscopes) are continuously corrected by the **low-frequency, long-term bounded** position/velocity observations from a Global Navigation Satellite System (receiver). When this fusion is performed using a **Modified Particle Filter (MPF)**, the posterior probability density function (PDF) of the navigation state vector is approximated by a set of weighted random samples (particles), where the modification refers to algorithmic enhancements over the standard Sequential Importance Sampling (SIS) particle filter to mitigate particle degeneracy, sample impoverishment, and to leverage the latest GNSS measurement in the proposal distribution.

**The navigation state vector** is conventionally defined as:

$$ \mathbf{x}_k = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{p}_k \\ \mathbf{v}_k \\ \mathbf{q}_k \\ \mathbf{b}_a \\ \mathbf{b}_g \end{bmatrix} $$

where $\mathbf{p}_k$ is position, $\mathbf{v}_k$ is velocity, $\mathbf{q}_k$ is the attitude quaternion, $\mathbf{b}_a$ is accelerometer bias, and $\mathbf{b}_g$ is gyroscope bias at epoch $k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** A Modified Particle Filter replaces the suboptimal *prior* proposal distribution of the standard PF with a smarter, measurement-informed proposal (typically obtained from a local Extended Kalman Filter or Unscented Kalman Filter running on each particle), thereby dramatically improving estimation accuracy during GNSS signal degradation, signal blockage, or high vehicle dynamics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Rescue Analogy

Imagine a group of **1000 hikers** (particles) scattered across a foggy mountain at night. Each hiker carries a noisy compass and pedometer (INS). Every few minutes, a helicopter flashes a strobe from a known peak (GNSS pseudo-range fix), but the strobe only illuminates a small area. 

- **Standard Particle Filter**: Hikers walk based only on their own compasses (prior proposal), then those closest to the strobe flash get their photo taken (weighting). After many steps, only a few hikers remain — the rest get "lost" (particle degeneracy).
- **Modified Particle Filter**: Each hiker also gets a *quick glance at the helicopter's position* (EKF/UKF local linearization) **before** they take their next step. This makes their proposal distribution far more efficient, and even the dimmest strobe (weak GNSS signal) guides them better.

> [!NOTE]
> The "modification" is essentially: **don't guess where to walk; first peek at the strobe direction, then walk.** This is mathematically equivalent to using a measurement-informed importance density.

### 1.3 Why GNSS/INS Integration is Mandatory

| Sensor | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|
| **INS** | High bandwidth (100–1000 Hz), self-contained, immune to jamming | **Unbounded error growth** due to integration drift |
| **GNSS** | Bounded long-term error (~2.5 m civilian), global coverage | Low update rate (1–10 Hz), vulnerable to multipath, jamming, outages |

> [!TIP]
> A standalone INS drifts at approximately **1 nautical mile per hour** of operation. A standalone GNSS suffers outages in tunnels, urban canyons, and under foliage. Their fusion yields a system that is **both stable and bounded**, which is the bedrock of modern autonomous vehicle, UAV, and marine navigation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Integration Architectures (Pre-requisite)

| Architecture | Coupling Level | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Loose Coupling** | Low | GNSS provides position/velocity → cascaded KF corrects INS | Easiest, most taught |
| **Tight Coupling** | Medium | Raw **pseudoranges** and **Doppler** feed the filter | Better under low satellite visibility |
| **Ultra-Tight (Deep)** | High | INS aids GNSS **tracking loops** | Best jamming resistance, complex |

The **Modified Particle Filter** is naturally suited for **tight or ultra-tight** coupling because it can directly handle the **nonlinear** pseudorange measurement model without linearization errors.

### 2.2 The Standard Particle Filter — Why it Fails in GNSS/INS

The standard Sequential Importance Sampling (SIS) PF uses the **prior** as the proposal:

$$ q(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_{0:k-1}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) = p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)}) $$

This leads to three catastrophic failure modes:

1. **Particle Degeneracy**: After a few iterations, almost all particles have negligible weight; one particle dominates.
2. **Sample Impoverishment**: After resampling, all particles become identical copies of the "best" particle — diversity is lost.
3. **Wasted Computation**: Most particles explore regions of state space with **near-zero likelihood** under the GNSS measurement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Effective Sample Size (ESS)** quantifies degeneracy:
> 
> $$ N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w_k^{(i)})^2 } $$
> 
> When $N_{\text{eff}} \leq N_{\text{threshold}}$ (typically $N/2$), resampling is triggered.

### 2.3 The Core Modifications (Modified Particle Filter Variants)

#### A. **EKF-Proposal Particle Filter (EKF-PF)**
- Each particle is propagated through a **local Extended Kalman Filter**.
- The local EKF computes the **Gaussian approximation** of the posterior conditioned on the GNSS measurement.
- Proposal becomes: $q(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}; \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(i)}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{(i)})$
- This **incorporates the latest GNSS measurement** into the proposal — the heart of "Modified."

#### B. **Unscented Particle Filter (UPF)**
- Replaces the local EKF with a **local UKF**, avoiding Jacobian computation.
- Better for **high nonlinearities** (e.g., large heading errors, large pseudorange residuals).
- Computational cost: roughly 2× EKF-PF per particle.

#### C. **Regularized Particle Filter (RPF)**
- After resampling, applies a **kernel density smoothing** to restore diversity.
- Samples drawn from $K_h(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) = \frac{1}{h^d} K\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}}{h}\right)$.
- Helps fight **sample impoverishment** without losing the mode.

#### D. **Auxiliary Particle Filter (APF)**
- Uses a **first-stage particle** to estimate likelihoods, then re-samples before propagating.
- Better when GNSS measurements are very informative (low noise).

#### E. **Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter (RBPF)**
- Partitions state into **nonlinear** (e.g., attitude) and **linear** (e.g., position, bias) sub-states.
- Linear parts estimated by **closed-form KF**, nonlinear parts by particles.
- Yields **drastic dimensionality reduction** in GNSS/INS.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| State transition | $\mathbf{x}_k = f(\mathbf{x}_{k-1}) + \mathbf{w}_{k-1}$ | $\mathbf{w}_{k-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_k)$ |
| GNSS measurement | $\mathbf{z}_k = h(\mathbf{x}_k) + \mathbf{v}_k$ | $\mathbf{v}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_k)$ |
| Pseudorange | $\rho_k^{(j)} = \vert \vert \mathbf{p}_k - \mathbf{p}^{(j)} \vert \vert + c\,\delta t_r + I_k + T_k + \epsilon_\rho$ | metres; $j$=sat ID |
| Importance weight | $w_k^{(i)} \propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)})$ | normalised to $\sum w = 1$ |
| Effective sample size | $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 / \sum (w_k^{(i)})^2$ | threshold $\approx N/2$ |
| EKF-PF proposal | $q(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) = \mathcal{N}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k^{(i)}, P_k^{(i)})$ | local linearised |
| INS mechanisation (pos) | $\mathbf{p}_k = \mathbf{p}_{k-1} + \mathbf{v}_{k-1}\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{q})(\tilde{\mathbf{a}} - \mathbf{b}_a))\Delta t^2$ | $\mathbf{R}$ = DCM |
| INS mechanisation (vel) | $\mathbf{v}_k = \mathbf{v}_{k-1} + \mathbf{R}(\mathbf{q})(\tilde{\mathbf{a}} - \mathbf{b}_a)\Delta t$ | strapdown eq. |
| Resampling (systematic) | $u_j = \frac{(j-1)+U}{N}$, $j=1,\ldots,N$, $U \sim \mathcal{U}[0,1)$ | low variance |
| Kernel bandwidth (RPF) | $h = \left( 4 / (d+2) \right)^{1/(d+4)} \cdot N^{-1/(d+4)} \cdot \hat{\sigma}$ | Silverman's rule |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility**: Modified Particle Filters are the workhorse in **urban GNSS-denied navigation** (autonomous cars in tunnels), **UAV swarms** under jamming, and **AUV (Autonomous Underwater Vehicle)** surfacing for periodic GNSS fixes. They are the gold standard in research-grade GNSS/INS software receivers (e.g., Google Android Raw GNSS + INS research).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bayesian Posterior for GNSS/INS

We seek the posterior PDF of the navigation state given the history of GNSS pseudo-range measurements:

$$ p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) $$

By the **Chapman–Kolmogorov** identity and **Bayes' rule**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) &= p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{z}_{k}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}) \\
&= \frac{p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k) \cdot p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1})}{p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1})}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **predictive density** is obtained by marginalising the previous posterior through the INS motion model:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}) &= \int p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1}) \, p(\mathbf{x}_{k-1} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{k-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation**: We *propagate* every plausible past state forward through the noisy INS equations, then *weight* the propagated cloud by how well each particle's predicted pseudorange matches the actual GNSS measurement. This two-step procedure is the foundation of any GNSS/INS Bayesian filter.

### 3.2 Derivation of Importance Sampling for Particle Weights

The posterior $p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k})$ is approximated by a set of $N$ weighted particles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) &\approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_k^{(i)} \, \delta\!\left(\mathbf{x}_{0:k} - \mathbf{x}_{0:k}^{(i)}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Because direct sampling from the posterior is intractable, we sample from a **proposal** $q(\cdot)$ and correct via importance weights. The **unnormalised** weight of particle $i$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_k^{(i)} &\propto \frac{p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k}^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k})}{q(\mathbf{x}_{0:k}^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k})} \\
&= w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot \frac{p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) \, p(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)})}{q(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}_{0:k-1}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k})}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Why the Standard PF Uses the Prior — and Why it's Suboptimal

The standard PF chooses $q(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}_{0:k-1}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) = p(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)})$, which collapses the weight update to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_k^{(i)} &\propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Problem**: The proposal ignores the current GNSS measurement $\mathbf{z}_k$. When $\mathbf{z}_k$ is highly informative (low $R_k$) or when the INS noise is large, the proposal places particles in low-likelihood regions, and only a tiny fraction survive. The variance of the importance weights grows as $\mathcal{O}(k)$ — guaranteed degeneracy.

### 3.4 Derivation of the EKF-Proposal Modification (Core of Modified PF)

We construct a Gaussian proposal centered on the **local EKF posterior mean**, conditioned on the latest GNSS measurement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}_{0:k-1}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) &= \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)};\, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_k^{(i)},\, P_k^{(i)}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Local EKF Time Update** (using particle-specific INS input $\mathbf{u}_{k-1}^{(i)}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1}^{(i)} &= f\!\left(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1}^{(i)}, \mathbf{u}_{k-1}^{(i)}\right) \\
P_{k\mid k-1}^{(i)} &= F_k^{(i)} P_{k-1}^{(i)} \left(F_k^{(i)}\right)^\top + Q_k
\end{aligned}
$$

where $F_k^{(i)} = \left. \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \right|_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1}^{(i)}}$ is the INS Jacobian.

**Step 2 — Local EKF Measurement Update** (using GNSS pseudorange residual):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{k}^{(i)} &= h\!\left(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1}^{(i)}\right) \\
H_k^{(i)} &= \left. \frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \right|_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1}^{(i)}} \\
S_k^{(i)} &= H_k^{(i)} P_{k\mid k-1}^{(i)} \left(H_k^{(i)}\right)^\top + R_k \\
K_k^{(i)} &= P_{k\mid k-1}^{(i)} \left(H_k^{(i)}\right)^\top \left(S_k^{(i)}\right)^{-1} \\
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k^{(i)} &= \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1}^{(i)} + K_k^{(i)} \left(\mathbf{z}_k - \hat{\mathbf{z}}_k^{(i)}\right) \\
P_k^{(i)} &= \left(I - K_k^{(i)} H_k^{(i)}\right) P_{k\mid k-1}^{(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Modified Importance Weight** (now using a non-trivial proposal ratio):

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_k^{(i)} &\propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot \frac{p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) \, p(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)})}{\mathcal{N}\!\left(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}; \hat{\mathbf{x}}_k^{(i)}, P_k^{(i)}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Why this is "Modified"**: The denominator $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}; \hat{\mathbf{x}}_k^{(i)}, P_k^{(i)})$ is **not** a constant — it depends on the particle. This corrects the weight for the fact that we sampled from a measurement-informed distribution rather than the prior. In the limit of perfect EKF approximation, the weight ratio approaches unity, and the **optimal proposal** is recovered (zero weight variance).

### 3.5 Pseudorange Measurement Model — Linearisation for the EKF-PF

The pseudorange from the $j$-th satellite is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_k^{(j)} &= \sqrt{(x_k - x^{(j)})^2 + (y_k - y^{(j)})^2 + (z_k - z^{(j)})^2} \\
&\quad + c \cdot \delta t_r + I_k + T_k + \varepsilon_\rho
\end{aligned}
$$

The Jacobian row (for satellite $j$) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_k^{(j)} &= \left[ \frac{\partial \rho^{(j)}}{\partial \mathbf{p}_k},\ \frac{\partial \rho^{(j)}}{\partial \mathbf{v}_k},\ \mathbf{0}_{1\times 3},\ \frac{\partial \rho^{(j)}}{\partial c\delta t_r},\ \mathbf{0}_{1\times 3} \right] \\
\frac{\partial \rho^{(j)}}{\partial \mathbf{p}_k} &= \frac{(\mathbf{p}_k - \mathbf{p}^{(j)})^\top}{\vert \vert \mathbf{p}_k - \mathbf{p}^{(j)} \vert \vert}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For Modified PF, the Jacobian is evaluated at the **particle's** predicted state, not the global EKF state. This captures **local nonlinearity** and is the key advantage of per-particle EKF updates.

### 3.6 Resampling Step (Systematic Resampling)

After weight normalisation, compute the cumulative sum $C^{(i)} = \sum_{m=1}^{i} w_k^{(m)}$, draw $u_1 \sim \mathcal{U}[0, 1/N)$, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_j &= u_1 + \frac{j-1}{N}, \quad j = 1, \ldots, N
\end{aligned}
$$

Copy particle $i$ to the new set when $C^{(i-1)} < u_j \leq C^{(i)}$. Reset all weights to $w_k^{(i)} = 1/N$.

### 3.7 State Estimate Extraction

The Minimum Mean Square Error (MMSE) estimate of the navigation state is the weighted mean:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k^{\text{MMSE}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_k^{(i)} \, \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimate is the particle with the highest weight.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram — Modified Particle Filter for GNSS/INS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IMU["INS Strapdown Module\nAccelerometer + Gyroscope"]
    GNSS["GNSS Receiver\nRaw Pseudorange + Doppler"]
    PRED["Particle Cloud Prediction\nINS Mechanisation f(x,u)"]
    LEKF1["Local EKF Time Update\nF, Q matrices per particle"]
    LEKF2["Local EKF Measurement Update\nH, K, Innovation per particle"]
    PROP["Measurement-Informed Proposal\nN(x_hat, P) per particle"]
    WEIGHT["Weight Computation\nLikelihood p(z_k | x_k) / q"]
    NEFF{"N_eff < N_threshold?"}
    RESAMP["Systematic Resampling\nRestore Particle Diversity"]
    EST["State Estimate Output\nMMSE / MAP Position Velocity Attitude"]
    OUT["Final Navigation Solution\nPosition Velocity Attitude Biases"]

    IMU --> PRED
    PRED --> LEKF1
    GNSS --> LEKF2
    LEKF1 --> LEKF2
    LEKF2 --> PROP
    PRED --> PROP
    PROP --> WEIGHT
    WEIGHT --> NEFF
    NEFF -- Yes --> RESAMP
    NEFF -- No --> EST
    RESAMP --> EST
    EST --> OUT
```

### 4.2 Algorithm Flow — Per-Iteration Pseudocode of Modified PF

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Initialize N particles from prior p(x0)"]
    B["k = 1, 2, 3 ..."]
    C["For each particle i = 1..N"]
    D["Propagate INS: x_pred = f(x_prev, u_k)"]
    E["Compute local Jacobian F_k"]
    F["EKF time update: P_pred = F P F^T + Q"]
    G["Compute local Jacobian H_k from pseudorange"]
    H["EKF measurement update: x_hat, P_k, K_k"]
    I["Draw sample from N(x_hat, P_k)"]
    J["Compute importance weight w_k"]
    K{"Sum to 1?"}
    L["Normalise all weights"]
    M["Compute N_eff"]
    N{"N_eff < N_th?"}
    O["Systematic resampling"]
    P["Reset weights to 1/N"]
    Q["Output state estimate x_hat_k"]
    R["Store particle cloud for next epoch"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
    J --> K
    K -- No --> J
    K -- Yes --> L
    L --> M
    M --> N
    N -- Yes --> O
    O --> P
    P --> Q
    N -- No --> Q
    Q --> R
    R --> B
```

### 4.3 Information Flow Between INS and GNSS in Modified PF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSOR["Physical Sensors"]
        ACC["Accelerometer\n100 Hz"]
        GYR["Gyroscope\n100 Hz"]
        SAT["GNSS Satellites\nL1/L5 carrier"]
    end

    subgraph MECH["INS Mechanisation"]
        ATT["Attitude Update\nQuaternion Integration"]
        VEL["Velocity Update"]
        POS["Position Update"]
    end

    subgraph MPF["Modified Particle Filter Core"]
        CLD["Particle Cloud\nN = 500 to 5000"]
        LEKF["Local EKF Bank\nN mini-filters"]
        WT["Weight Calculator"]
        RS["Resampler"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["Navigation Output"]
        SOL["Position Velocity Attitude\nat 100 Hz"]
    end

    ACC --> VEL
    GYR --> ATT
    ATT --> VEL
    VEL --> POS
    POS --> CLD
    SAT --> LEKF
    CLD --> LEKF
    LEKF --> WT
    WT --> RS
    RS --> CLD
    CLD --> SOL
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **local EKF bank** is what makes this a "Modified" Particle Filter. A standard PF would have a single INS propagation arrow into the particle cloud with no EKF local feedback. The bidirectional coupling between the cloud and the EKF bank is the visual signature of the modification.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*State any **three** reasons why the standard Particle Filter fails in GNSS/INS integration. Mention how the Modified Particle Filter addresses one of them.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Particle Degeneracy (1 mark):** After a few iterations, the Effective Sample Size $N_{\text{eff}}$ collapses to a single dominant particle because the proposal $q(\cdot) = p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1})$ ignores the informative GNSS measurement.
2. **Sample Impoverishment (1 mark):** After resampling, all particles become identical copies of the highest-weight particle, destroying state-space diversity.
3. **Wasted computation (1 mark):** Most particles explore regions with near-zero GNSS-measurement likelihood, wasting compute on low-probability hypotheses.

The **Modified PF** addresses (1) by using a **measurement-informed Gaussian proposal** $q(\cdot) = \mathcal{N}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k^{(i)}, P_k^{(i)})$ generated by a local EKF on each particle, so the proposal is already aligned with the latest GNSS pseudo-range residual. *(Any one valid mitigation earns full credit for the third part.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the **Effective Sample Size** in a particle filter. What is its role in the Modified Particle Filter loop?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Effective Sample Size (ESS)** is a scalar measure of particle-cloud degeneracy (1 mark):

$$ N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( w_k^{(i)} \right)^2 } $$

**Role** (2 marks): In the Modified PF loop, $N_{\text{eff}}$ is monitored at every GNSS update epoch. If $N_{\text{eff}}$ falls below a pre-set threshold (typically $N/2$), a **resampling step** is triggered to restore diversity. In the **Modified** variant, the threshold can be set more aggressively (e.g., $0.7N$) because the EKF-informed proposal keeps $N_{\text{eff}}$ high most of the time, so a drop genuinely indicates a high-information GNSS measurement (e.g., new satellite acquisition or sudden fix).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the importance weight update equation for a generic Sequential Importance Sampling Particle Filter applied to the GNSS/INS navigation problem. Clearly state the role of the proposal distribution. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain, with mathematical justification, how the **EKF-proposal Modified Particle Filter** improves upon the standard PF in tightly-coupled GNSS/INS integration. Use the pseudorange measurement model in your derivation. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Standard SIS-PF weight derivation (7 marks):**

We seek the posterior $p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k})$ over the navigation-state trajectory. Because direct sampling is intractable, we introduce a proposal $q(\mathbf{x}_{0:k} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k})$ from which we can draw samples.

By definition of importance sampling (1 mark):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\!\left[ g(\mathbf{x}_{0:k}) \right] &= \int g(\mathbf{x}_{0:k})\, p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k})\, d\mathbf{x}_{0:k} \\
&= \int g(\mathbf{x}_{0:k})\, \frac{p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k})}{q(\mathbf{x}_{0:k} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k})}\, q(\mathbf{x}_{0:k} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k})\, d\mathbf{x}_{0:k}
\end{aligned}
$$

The importance weight is defined as (1 mark):

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_k^{(i)} &= \frac{p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k}^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k})}{q(\mathbf{x}_{0:k}^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k})}
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying Bayes' rule recursively and using Markovianity (1 mark):

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) &\propto p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k)\, p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1})\, p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k-1} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

Choosing a factorised proposal $q(\mathbf{x}_{0:k} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) = q(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_{0:k-1}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) q(\mathbf{x}_{0:k-1} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1})$ (1 mark) and simplifying (1 mark):

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_k^{(i)} &\propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot \frac{p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) \, p(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)})}{q(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}_{0:k-1}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Role of the proposal** (2 marks): The proposal is a tractable distribution that we can sample from; it determines where in the state space particles are placed. A good proposal maximises the expected ESS — the closer the proposal to the true posterior, the lower the weight variance, and the fewer particles are wasted. The standard PF chooses $q = p(\mathbf{x}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1})$, which ignores the latest GNSS measurement.

**(b) EKF-Proposal MPF improvement (7 marks):**

For **tightly-coupled** GNSS/INS, the measurement model is the pseudorange (1 mark):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_k^{(j)} &= \sqrt{(x_k - x^{(j)})^2 + (y_k - y^{(j)})^2 + (z_k - z^{(j)})^2} \\
&\quad + c\, \delta t_r + \varepsilon_\rho^{(j)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **highly nonlinear** in position — the standard PF with prior proposal places particles in a Gaussian cloud around the INS-only predicted position, ignoring the geometry of the satellite constellation (1 mark).

The **Modified PF** uses a local EKF on each particle to compute (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k^{(i)} &= \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1}^{(i)} + K_k^{(i)} \left(\mathbf{z}_k - h(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1}^{(i)})\right) \\
P_k^{(i)} &= (I - K_k^{(i)} H_k^{(i)}) P_{k\mid k-1}^{(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The new proposal is $q(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) = \mathcal{N}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k^{(i)}, P_k^{(i)})$ — already shifted toward the GNSS measurement.

The importance weight becomes (1 mark):

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_k^{(i)} \propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot \frac{p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) \, p(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)})}{\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}; \hat{\mathbf{x}}_k^{(i)}, P_k^{(i)})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mathematical justification of improvement** (2 marks): The variance of log-weights in the optimal proposal case is zero (Doucet et al., 2000). The EKF-proposal approximates the **optimal importance density** locally, reducing weight variance by orders of magnitude, raising $N_{\text{eff}}$, and reducing the required particle count $N$ by typically $5\times$ to $20\times$ for the same accuracy. This is the essence of the "modification."

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Compare **Loose**, **Tight**, and **Ultra-Tight** GNSS/INS integration architectures. State which one benefits most from a Modified Particle Filter and justify. (7 marks)

**(b)** A vehicle is equipped with a strapdown INS (100 Hz) and a GNSS receiver (1 Hz). Using the Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter (RBPF) framework, partition the state vector and explain the resulting computational saving. Show the factorised posterior mathematically. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison table (4 marks) + selection + justification (3 marks):**

| Feature | Loose | Tight | Ultra-Tight |
|---|---|---|---|
| GNSS input to filter | PVT solution | Raw pseudoranges, Doppler | Raw correlator I/Q |
| INS aids GNSS? | No | No | Yes (DLL/PLL aiding) |
| Robust to <4 sats? | No | Yes | Yes |
| Complexity | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Jamming resistance | Low | Medium | Highest |
| Cost | $ | $$ | $$$ |

**Selection (1.5 marks):** The **Tight** coupling architecture benefits most from a Modified Particle Filter.

**Justification (1.5 marks):** In tight coupling, the measurement model (pseudorange) is nonlinear in the navigation state, the noise is non-Gaussian (multipath outliers), and the measurement count varies (number of visible satellites changes). The Modified PF with EKF proposal handles all three gracefully, unlike a linearised KF which would fail during large heading errors or sudden satellite dropouts.

**(b) RBPF for GNSS/INS (7 marks):**

**State partitioning** (1 mark):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}_k = \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{\theta}_k \\ \boldsymbol{\phi}_k \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\boldsymbol{\theta}_k = [\mathbf{q}_k, \mathbf{b}_g]$ (nonlinear attitude + gyro bias) and $\boldsymbol{\phi}_k = [\mathbf{p}_k, \mathbf{v}_k, \mathbf{b}_a, c\delta t_r]$ (linear in INS error equations, conditioned on attitude).

**Factorised posterior** (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{0:k}, \boldsymbol{\phi}_{0:k} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) &= p(\boldsymbol{\phi}_{0:k} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}_{0:k}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) \cdot p(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{0:k} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k})
\end{aligned}
$$

**PF component** (1 mark): Particles sample only the nonlinear attitude $\boldsymbol{\theta}_k^{(i)}$.

**KF component per particle** (2 marks): For each particle $i$, a **Kalman Filter** analytically computes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(\boldsymbol{\phi}_k \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}_k^{(i)}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) &= \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\phi}_k; \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(i)}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{(i)})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computational saving** (1 mark): A full PF on a 17-state vector with $N$ particles needs $N$ operations on a 17-D space. RBPF needs $N$ operations on a 5-D attitude space plus $N$ cheap 12-D KF updates. The savings scale as $N \cdot d_{\text{lin}}^3$ (KF cubed) and is dramatically cheaper for high $N$.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Modified PF Answers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the proposal ratio in the weight** (–2 marks): Students often write $w_k \propto p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k)$ even when using an EKF proposal. The correct form **must** divide by the Gaussian proposal $\mathcal{N}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k, P_k)$ because the sampling distribution is no longer the prior.
> 2. **Conflating the local EKF with the global EKF** (–1 mark): The local EKF is per-particle; the global EKF is the standard linearised filter. The MPF is **not** "EKF + resampling" — it is **N parallel EKF proposals** feeding a single weight calculator.
> 3. **Ignoring quaternion normalisation** (–1 mark): After EKF update on the attitude, renormalise the quaternion: $\hat{\mathbf{q}} \leftarrow \hat{\mathbf{q}} / \vert \vert \hat{\mathbf{q}} \vert \vert$.
> 4. **Mixing integration architectures** (–1 mark): Tight coupling requires **raw pseudoranges**, not a PVT solution. Stating that Modified PF works on "GNSS position" implies loose coupling and loses architecture marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GNSS/INS integration** overcomes INS drift and GNSS outage weaknesses; the Modified PF is the premier nonlinear, non-Gaussian fusion engine.
- **Standard PF** uses the **prior** as proposal → suffers degeneracy, impoverishment, wasted compute.
- **Modified PF** uses a **local EKF/UKF Gaussian proposal** conditioned on the latest GNSS pseudorange → near-optimal importance density, drastically higher $N_{\text{eff}}$.
- The **importance weight** with non-trivial proposal must include the **proposal ratio** in the denominator.
- **Effective Sample Size** $N_{\text{eff}} = 1/\sum (w_k^{(i)})^2$ is the degeneracy watchdog; resample when below threshold.
- **Resampling strategies**: multinomial, stratified, systematic — systematic is preferred for low variance.
- **RBPF** is the dimensionality-reduction champion: nonlinear parts (attitude) by particles, linear parts (position, bias) by closed-form KF.
- **Tight coupling** with raw pseudoranges is the natural habitat of Modified PF.
- **Jacobians** are evaluated at **per-particle** states, not the global mean.
- **Engineering uses**: urban canyons, UAV swarms, autonomous underwater vehicles, smartphone pedestrian dead-reckoning.
- **Equation to remember for the exam**: $w_k^{(i)} \propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) \cdot p(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)}) / q(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}_{0:k-1}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k})$.
- **Final estimate**: MMSE state = weighted mean of particles; MAP state = argmax weight particle.
- **Compute cost**: per particle, ~1 EKF iteration; total cost $\approx N \times \text{cost(EKF)}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimating Velocity by Combining GNSS and INS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Estimating Velocity by Combining GNSS and INS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Estimating Velocity by Combining GNSS and INS** is a sensor fusion technique in robotics and navigation engineering that integrates the *absolute, long-term stable* velocity information derived from a **Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)** with the *high-rate, drift-prone* velocity information produced by an **Inertial Navigation System (INS)**. The two complementary data streams are mathematically merged — most commonly using a **Kalman Filter** — to produce a single, smooth, high-bandwidth, and high-accuracy velocity estimate for the host vehicle (ground robot, UAV, autonomous car, marine vessel, etc.).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the integrated system is referred to as a **GNSS/INS Integrated Navigation System**, and the velocity channel is one of the three primary outputs (along with **position** and **attitude**) that the filter estimates as part of its *state vector*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GNSS** provides position and velocity at a low update rate (typically **1 Hz to 10 Hz**) but bounded error.
> **INS** provides position, velocity, and attitude at a high update rate (typically **100 Hz to 1000 Hz**) but with **unbounded drift** over time.
> Combining them exploits the strengths of both: INS fills the bandwidth gap of GNSS, while GNSS keeps the INS drift bounded.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are walking inside a large, dark, unfamiliar building while holding a **smartphone GPS** in one hand and a **pedometer (step counter)** in the other.

* The **GPS** tells you your absolute position (which floor, which room) and walking speed — but only every few seconds, and the signal drops every time you walk behind a concrete pillar.
* The **pedometer/IMU** counts your steps continuously and tells you how fast you are walking *right now* — but if you count a step wrong, that error compounds. Walk 1 km using only the pedometer, and the accumulated error could be tens of meters.
* The smart thing to do is to **fuse both** — trust the pedometer for short, fast updates between GPS readings, and use each new GPS reading to *recalibrate* the pedometer so that its accumulated error never grows out of control.

GNSS/INS velocity fusion is exactly this idea, but executed with a rigorous statistical filter (the **Kalman Filter**) rather than human intuition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is velocity specifically important in robot navigation?**
> Velocity is used for **obstacle avoidance** (closing speed), **trajectory tracking** (model predictive control), **odometry** (dead reckoning during GNSS outages), and **dynamic state estimation** (acceleration, jerk limits).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **bolded** constants and rates are standard in KTU 2024 Scheme design problems and GNSS/INS literature:

* **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** (used in GNSS pseudorange equations).
* **Earth's rotation rate (WGS-84):** $\Omega_e = 7.292115 \times 10^{-5}$ **rad/s**.
* **WGS-84 semi-major axis:** $a = 6378137.0$ **m**.
* **WGS-84 eccentricity squared:** $e^2 = 0.00669437999014$.
* **Standard GNSS L1 carrier frequency:** $1575.42$ **MHz**.
* **Typical tactical-grade IMU bias:** $\leq 10^\circ/\text{hr}$ for gyros, $\leq 1$ **mg** for accelerometers.
* **Navigation-grade IMU bias:** $\leq 0.01^\circ/\text{hr}$ for gyros.
* **Receiver clock drift** is typically modeled as a random walk with a $1\sigma$ value around **1 m/s** in equivalent range-rate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Combine? — The Complementary Nature of GNSS and INS

The integration of GNSS and INS is fundamentally motivated by the **complementary error characteristics** of the two sensors. The next bullet list breaks this down:

* **GNSS Velocity Error** — bounded, long-term stable, but noisy and low-rate.
   * *Why bounded?* — Because the satellites are tracked using atomic clocks, and the geometry of the satellite constellation is continuously changing, preventing systematic drift.
   * *Why noisy?* — Because multipath, ionospheric delay, and thermal noise corrupt each Doppler shift measurement.
* **INS Velocity Error** — short-term very accurate, but unbounded long-term drift.
   * *Why short-term accurate?* — Because a tactical-grade accelerometer can resolve specific force to $\mu g$ levels over millisecond intervals.
   * *Why long-term drift?* — Because the INS computes velocity by integrating specific force: $v(t) = v(0) + \int a(t)\,dt$. A tiny bias $\beta_a$ in the accelerometer becomes an error that grows as $\beta_a \cdot t$ (linear in time). Integrating once more for position gives a quadratic error growth $0.5 \beta_a t^2$.
* **Fused Velocity** — both noise and drift are suppressed. The Kalman filter mathematically **re-weights** the two estimates based on their known covariance matrices $R_{\text{GNSS}}$ and $Q_{\text{INS}}$, producing an output with the lowest possible mean-squared error in the linear-Gaussian sense.

### 2.2 GNSS Velocity Measurement — The Doppler Principle

GNSS receivers do not, in practice, "measure velocity" directly. They compute velocity from one of two methods:

1. **Doppler-derived velocity (range-rate):** The Doppler shift on each carrier frequency $f_L$ is measured, and the line-of-sight range-rate $\dot{\rho}^{(i)}$ to satellite $i$ is computed as
$$\dot{\rho}^{(i)} = -\frac{c \cdot \Delta f^{(i)}}{f_L} + \dot{b}_{\text{clk}} + \varepsilon^{(i)}_{\dot{\rho}}$$
where $\Delta f^{(i)}$ is the measured Doppler offset, $\dot{b}_{\text{clk}}$ is the receiver clock drift in equivalent range-rate units, and $\varepsilon^{(i)}_{\dot{\rho}}$ is the measurement noise.

2. **Position-differencing velocity:** The receiver simply differentiates successive position fixes: $v \approx (x_k - x_{k-1}) / \Delta t$. This is *noisy and unreliable* for short intervals and is generally avoided in KTU problems.

With at least **4 satellites in view**, the linear system of range-rate equations can be solved for the 3D velocity vector $\mathbf{v} = [v_E, v_N, v_U]^T$ plus the clock drift $\dot{b}_{\text{clk}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU problems almost always assume the **Doppler-derived velocity** model, because it is the one mathematically tractable in a Kalman filter framework.

### 2.3 INS Velocity Update — The Mechanization Equations

The INS computes velocity in the **navigation frame** (typically NED — North, East, Down) using the well-known **Newtonian mechanization**:

$$\dot{\mathbf{v}}^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}^b - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \mathbf{v}^n + \mathbf{g}^n$$

Breaking this down line by line:

* $\dot{\mathbf{v}}^n$ — time derivative of the velocity expressed in the navigation frame (NED). This is what the INS must integrate to obtain velocity.
* $\mathbf{C}_b^n$ — the **Direction Cosine Matrix (DCM)** or **attitude transformation matrix** from body to navigation frame. Without an accurate $\mathbf{C}_b^n$, the accelerometer reading is projected incorrectly.
* $\mathbf{f}^b$ — specific force vector measured by the accelerometers in the body frame. This is the *non-gravitational* acceleration (i.e., raw accelerometer reading $\mathbf{a}^b$ minus the bias $\mathbf{b}_a$, plus noise).
* $(2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \mathbf{v}^n$ — the **Coriolis correction**, which accounts for the fact that the Earth is rotating and the navigation frame is moving over a curved surface. $\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n$ is the Earth rotation vector, and $\boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n$ is the transport rate.
* $\mathbf{g}^n$ — local gravity vector (gravity + centripetal). The accelerometers cannot distinguish gravity from acceleration, so it must be added back vectorially.

The discrete-time (Euler) integration is:

$$\mathbf{v}^n_{k+1} = \mathbf{v}^n_k + \Big[ \mathbf{C}_{b,k}^n \mathbf{f}^b_k - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie,k}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en,k}^n) \times \mathbf{v}^n_k + \mathbf{g}^n_k \Big] \Delta t$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the equation whose error grows linearly with time when $\mathbf{f}^b$ is biased. That linear growth is exactly what the Kalman filter is designed to cancel using GNSS updates.

### 2.4 The Kalman Filter Architecture

The filter maintains a state vector — for a 15-state GNSS/INS filter used in KTU problems:

$$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} \delta \mathbf{r}^n \\ \delta \mathbf{v}^n \\ \boldsymbol{\psi}^n \\ \mathbf{b}_a \\ \mathbf{b}_g \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{15}$$

where the components are:

* $\delta \mathbf{r}^n \in \mathbb{R}^3$ — position error (NED).
* $\delta \mathbf{v}^n \in \mathbb{R}^3$ — **velocity error (this is what we primarily want to estimate).**
* $\boldsymbol{\psi}^n \in \mathbb{R}^3$ — attitude (tilt) error vector.
* $\mathbf{b}_a \in \mathbb{R}^3$ — accelerometer bias.
* $\mathbf{b}_g \in \mathbb{R}^3$ — gyroscope bias.

The **prediction step** propagates the state using the linearized INS error dynamics. The **update step** uses the GNSS velocity (or position) measurement residual:

$$\mathbf{z}_k = \mathbf{v}^n_{\text{GNSS},k} - \mathbf{v}^n_{\text{INS},k}$$

to correct the predicted state. The corrected velocity is then:

$$\hat{\mathbf{v}}^n_{k\mid k} = \mathbf{v}^n_{k\mid k-1} - \delta \mathbf{v}^n_{k\mid k}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following compact table summarises every key formula required to solve KTU 2024 Scheme problems on this topic. **No vertical pipes `|` are used inside the math — instead `\vert` is used wherever the absolute-value sign is needed.**

| **#** | **Quantity / Equation** | **LaTeX Form** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | GNSS range-rate (Doppler) | $\dot{\rho}^{(i)} = -\dfrac{c\,\Delta f^{(i)}}{f_L} + \dot{b}_{\text{clk}} + \varepsilon^{(i)}_{\dot{\rho}}$ | m/s |
| 2 | Linearised range-rate observation | $\dot{\rho}^{(i)} = \mathbf{u}^{(i)T} (\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{v}^{(i)}) + \dot{b}_{\text{clk}} + \varepsilon^{(i)}_{\dot{\rho}}$ | m/s, $\mathbf{u}^{(i)}$ is unit LOS vector |
| 3 | INS velocity mechanization (continuous) | $\dot{\mathbf{v}}^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}^b - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \mathbf{v}^n + \mathbf{g}^n$ | m/s$^2$ |
| 4 | INS velocity mechanization (discrete) | $\mathbf{v}^n_{k+1} = \mathbf{v}^n_k + \dot{\mathbf{v}}^n_k \Delta t$ | m/s |
| 5 | INS velocity error growth | $\vert \delta v(t) \vert \approx \vert b_a \vert \, t$ | m/s, linear in $t$ |
| 6 | INS position error growth | $\vert \delta r(t) \vert \approx 0.5 \vert b_a \vert \, t^2$ | m, quadratic in $t$ |
| 7 | Kalman prediction | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1\mid k-1}$ | State vector |
| 8 | Kalman covariance prediction | $\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1\mid k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k$ | Covariance |
| 9 | Kalman gain | $\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T (\mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k)^{-1}$ | Gain matrix |
| 10 | Kalman update | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1})$ | State correction |
| 11 | Covariance update (Joseph form) | $\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k) \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}$ | Simplified form |
| 12 | Velocity measurement residual | $\mathbf{z}_k = \mathbf{v}^n_{\text{GNSS}} - \mathbf{v}^n_{\text{INS}}$ | m/s |
| 13 | Measurement matrix (velocity only) | $\mathbf{H}_k = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{0}_{3\times 3} & \mathbf{I}_{3\times 3} & \mathbf{0}_{3\times 9} \end{bmatrix}$ | $3 \times 15$ |
| 14 | Earth rotation magnitude | $\Omega_e = 7.292115 \times 10^{-5}$ | rad/s |
| 15 | Coriolis acceleration magnitude | $\vert 2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n \times \mathbf{v}^n \vert \approx 2\Omega_e \vert v \vert$ | m/s$^2$, max $\sim 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \vert v \vert$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Autonomous Vehicles (AV):** The fused velocity feeds the longitudinal controller. Without tight velocity accuracy, headway control and Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) become unstable.
* **UAV / Drone Flight Control:** GNSS outages in urban canyons or during banking turns cause INS-only flight, where velocity drift can destabilise the autopilot within seconds.
* **Marine Survey Vessels:** Underwater acoustic positioning (USBL) replaces GNSS, but INS still smooths the velocity for sonar beam steering.
* **Robotic Surgery & Industrial AGVs:** Even at low speeds, precise velocity is required for synchronized multi-robot cooperation.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Velocity Error Dynamics of the INS

We will derive the linearised differential equation governing $\delta \mathbf{v}^n$, the navigation-frame velocity error, in the standard **Psi-angle error model** (Titterton & Weston, 1976). This is the most common form tested in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers.

**Step 1 — Write the true velocity equation.** The true (error-free) velocity satisfies the ideal mechanization:

$$\dot{\mathbf{v}}^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}^b - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \mathbf{v}^n + \mathbf{g}^n$$

**Step 2 — Write the estimated (computed) velocity equation.** The INS computes an estimate using imperfect sensors and imperfect attitude:

$$\dot{\hat{\mathbf{v}}}^n = \hat{\mathbf{C}}_b^n \hat{\mathbf{f}}^b - (2\hat{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_{ie}^n + \hat{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_{en}^n) \times \hat{\mathbf{v}}^n + \hat{\mathbf{g}}^n$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{C}}_b^n$ is computed from the (estimated) attitude, and $\hat{\mathbf{f}}^b = \mathbf{f}^b + \mathbf{b}_a + \mathbf{w}_a$ (true specific force plus bias plus white noise).

**Step 3 — Define the velocity error.** 

$$\delta \mathbf{v}^n \equiv \hat{\mathbf{v}}^n - \mathbf{v}^n$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the true equation from the estimated equation.** A first-order Taylor expansion of the attitude matrix gives $\hat{\mathbf{C}}_b^n \approx (\mathbf{I} + \boldsymbol{\Psi}^n \times)\mathbf{C}_b^n$ where $\boldsymbol{\Psi}^n$ is the small attitude error vector. After dropping second-order error cross products:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta \dot{\mathbf{v}}^n &= \hat{\mathbf{C}}_b^n \hat{\mathbf{f}}^b - \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}^b - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \delta \mathbf{v}^n + \delta \mathbf{g}^n \\
&\quad - \delta \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n \times \mathbf{v}^n - 2 \delta \boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n \times \mathbf{v}^n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Expand the force term.** Using $\hat{\mathbf{C}}_b^n \approx (\mathbf{I} + \boldsymbol{\Psi}^n \times)\mathbf{C}_b^n$ and $\hat{\mathbf{f}}^b = \mathbf{f}^b + \mathbf{b}_a$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\mathbf{C}}_b^n \hat{\mathbf{f}}^b - \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}^b &= (\mathbf{I} + \boldsymbol{\Psi}^n \times)\mathbf{C}_b^n (\mathbf{f}^b + \mathbf{b}_a) - \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}^b \\
&= \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{b}_a + \boldsymbol{\Psi}^n \times \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}^b + \text{higher order} \\
&\approx \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{b}_a + \boldsymbol{\Psi}^n \times \mathbf{a}^n
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{a}^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}^b$ is the true specific force in the navigation frame, and the cross-product identity $\boldsymbol{\Psi}^n \times \mathbf{a}^n = -(\mathbf{a}^n \times)\boldsymbol{\Psi}^n$ is used.

**Step 6 — Combine to obtain the canonical velocity error equation.** The gravity and Earth-rate error terms ($\delta \mathbf{g}^n$, $\delta \boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n$, $\delta \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n$) are typically small and absorbed into a process noise vector $\mathbf{w}_v$. The final linearised equation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta \dot{\mathbf{v}}^n = \; &\mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{b}_a - (\mathbf{a}^n \times)\,\boldsymbol{\Psi}^n \\
&- \big[ (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \big]\,\delta \mathbf{v}^n \; + \; \mathbf{w}_v
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:**

* $\mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{b}_a$ — accelerometer bias directly injects error into the velocity estimate. This is why a stable accelerometer calibration is critical.
* $-(\mathbf{a}^n \times) \boldsymbol{\Psi}^n$ — an **attitude error** of magnitude $\vert \boldsymbol{\Psi}^n \vert$ projects the gravity vector $\mathbf{a}^n$ into the velocity error channel. Even a tiny tilt error of $0.1^\circ$ combined with $\vert \mathbf{g} \vert \approx 9.81$ m/s$^2$ yields a velocity error of about $9.81 \times \sin(0.1^\circ) \approx 0.017$ m/s — non-trivial.
* $-(2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \delta \mathbf{v}^n$ — a small coupling term that the Kalman filter handles automatically.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — GNSS Velocity Measurement Model in the Kalman Filter

**Step 1 — True range-rate equation.** The line-of-sight range-rate from receiver to satellite $i$ is:

$$\dot{\rho}^{(i)} = \mathbf{u}^{(i)T}(\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{v}^{(i)}) + \dot{b}_{\text{clk}} + \varepsilon^{(i)}_{\dot{\rho}}$$

where $\mathbf{u}^{(i)}$ is the unit vector from receiver to satellite, $\mathbf{v}^{(i)}$ is the satellite velocity (computed from broadcast ephemeris), and $\dot{b}_{\text{clk}}$ is the receiver clock drift in m/s.

**Step 2 — Stack into vector form for $n$ satellites.** Writing this for $n \geq 4$ satellites and stacking:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{z}_{\dot{\rho}} &= \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\rho}^{(1)} \\ \dot{\rho}^{(2)} \\ \vdots \\ \dot{\rho}^{(n)} \end{bmatrix}
= \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{u}^{(1)T} & 1 \\ \mathbf{u}^{(2)T} & 1 \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ \mathbf{u}^{(n)T} & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{G}}
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{v} \\ \dot{b}_{\text{clk}} \end{bmatrix}
+ \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{u}^{(1)T}\mathbf{v}^{(1)} \\ \mathbf{u}^{(2)T}\mathbf{v}^{(2)} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{u}^{(n)T}\mathbf{v}^{(n)} \end{bmatrix}
+ \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\dot{\rho}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve by weighted least-squares (or feed to Kalman filter).** The receiver velocity and clock drift are estimated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \hat{\mathbf{v}}_{\text{GNSS}} \\ \hat{\dot{b}}_{\text{clk}} \end{bmatrix} = (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{G})^{-1} \mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{W}\,
\big( \mathbf{z}_{\dot{\rho}} - \mathbf{G}_{\text{sat}} \mathbf{v}_{\text{sat}} \big)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{W}$ is a diagonal weighting matrix of inverse variances $1/\sigma^2_{\dot{\rho},i}$.

**Step 4 — Velocity Dilution of Precision (VDOP).** The covariance of the velocity estimate is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{P}_{v} = \sigma^2_{\text{URE}} (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1}_{[1:3,1:3]}
\end{aligned}
$$

and the **VDOP** is the square root of the trace of this $3 \times 3$ submatrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VDOP} = \sqrt{\text{tr}\big[ (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1}_{[1:3,1:3]} \big]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** VDOP quantifies how satellite geometry magnifies the ranging noise into velocity noise. VDOP = 1.5 is typical; VDOP > 4 is poor.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Velocity Error Growth in Standalone INS

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *type* of calculation KTU 2024 ESE expects you to perform on the velocity question.

**Problem.** A MEMS-grade IMU has an accelerometer bias of $b_a = 200\,\mu g$ (i.e., $200 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 = 1.962 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s$^2$). A robot starts from rest and travels for $t = 60$ s with no GNSS update. Estimate the velocity error magnitude and the position error magnitude.

**Step 1 — Compute the velocity error using the linear-drift formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta v &= b_a \cdot t = 1.962 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 60 = 0.1177 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the position error using the quadratic-growth formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta r &= 0.5 \, b_a \, t^2 = 0.5 \cdot 1.962 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 60^2 = 0.5 \cdot 1.962 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 3600 \\
&= 3.532 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.** A 60-second GNSS outage produces a velocity drift of about **12 cm/s** and a position drift of about **3.5 m** for this MEMS sensor. The Kalman filter's task during a subsequent GNSS update is to bring $\delta v$ and $\delta r$ back toward zero by adjusting the bias estimate.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Integration Architectures

| **Architecture** | **Where GNSS is Used** | **Latency** | **Robustness to GNSS Outage** | **Typical KTU Use** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Loose Coupling** | Position + Velocity as black-box input | High | Poor — entire INS must coast | Introductory problem |
| **Tight Coupling** | Raw pseudorange + range-rate input | Medium | Better | Standard KTU problem |
| **Deep / Ultra-Tight Coupling** | INS aids GNSS code/carrier tracking loops | Low | Best | Advanced (M.Tech level) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 ESE, the **loose coupling** is the default unless the question specifies otherwise, because the math (Kalman filter with $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{v}_{\text{GNSS}} - \mathbf{v}_{\text{INS}}$) is the most straightforward to derive and solve.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of GNSS/INS Velocity Fusion

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the *block-level functional architecture* of a loose-coupled GNSS/INS integrated navigation system, focused on the velocity channel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IMU Body Sensors: 3 Accels + 3 Gyros] --> B[INS Mechanization Engine]
    B --> C[INS Velocity v_ins_n: 3x1 vector at 100 to 1000 Hz]
    D[GNSS Antenna: Receives L1 or L1 L5 Carrier] --> E[GNSS Receiver Doppler Processor]
    E --> F[GNSS Velocity v_gnss_n: 3x1 vector at 1 to 10 Hz]
    C --> G[Kalman Filter Measurement Residual z = v_gnss - v_ins]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Kalman Filter Bank: 15 state or 9 state]
    H --> I[Velocity Error Estimate delta v]
    I --> J[Velocity Correction Loop: v_corrected = v_ins - delta v]
    J --> K[Output: Fused Velocity v_fused to Navigation Controller]
    H --> L[Accelerometer Bias Estimate b_a]
    L --> M[Feedback to INS Mechanization: Compensate Accel Bias]
    H --> N[Gyro Bias Estimate b_g]
    N --> O[Feedback to INS Mechanization: Compensate Gyro Bias]
    style C fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style F fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style H fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600
    style K fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000
```

**Reading the diagram:**

* The **IMU block** feeds the **INS mechanization engine** at high rate, producing a continuous INS-only velocity $\mathbf{v}_{\text{ins}}^n$.
* The **GNSS receiver** produces $\mathbf{v}_{\text{gnss}}^n$ at low rate.
* The **Kalman Filter** computes the *measurement residual* $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{v}_{\text{gnss}} - \mathbf{v}_{\text{ins}}$, multiplies by the **Kalman gain** $\mathbf{K}$, and produces a correction $\delta \mathbf{v}$.
* The correction is **subtracted** from the INS velocity to yield the **fused velocity** output.
* The filter also estimates the **accelerometer and gyro biases**, which are *fed back* into the INS mechanization to *prevent the error from growing in the first place* — this is the feedback architecture that gives loose coupling its effectiveness.

### 4.2 Signal-Flow Diagram — Velocity Channel of the Kalman Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Previous State x_hat_kminus1] --> Q[Predict: x_pred = F x_hat]
    R[Process Noise Q_k] --> Q
    Q --> S[Predicted Covariance P_pred = F P F_T + Q]
    S --> T[Kalman Gain: K = P_pred H_T times H P_pred H_T + R inverse]
    T --> U[Update: x_hat = x_pred + K times z minus H x_pred]
    V[Measurement z = v_gnss minus v_ins] --> U
    U --> W[Output: Corrected Velocity v_fused]
    U --> X[Updated Covariance P = I minus K H times P_pred]
    X --> P
    style V fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style T fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600
    style W fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the standard Kalman filter loop. Notice the *predict-then-update* structure, and the fact that the updated covariance becomes the *previous* covariance for the next iteration — the filter is recursive.

### 4.3 Error-Growth Comparison Plot (Conceptual)

The diagram below conceptualises the typical error growth of three navigation modes: standalone GNSS (bounded, noisy), standalone INS (unbounded, smooth), and integrated GNSS/INS (bounded and smooth). This is the key selling point of integration.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time t axis] --> B[Error magnitude vertical axis]
    B --> C1[GNSS: Bounded ~ sigma_gnss, Noisy, Step jumps each second]
    B --> C2[INS: Linear growth, delta v = b_a times t]
    B --> C3[Integrated: Stays near ~ sigma_gnss with kinks at update times]
    style C1 fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style C2 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000
    style C3 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**
*Define the term "GNSS/INS integrated velocity estimation." State TWO reasons why velocity fusion is essential in autonomous robot navigation.*

**Model Answer:**

*Definition:* GNSS/INS integrated velocity estimation is the process of fusing the Doppler-derived velocity from a GNSS receiver with the inertial velocity computed by an INS through a recursive filter (typically a Kalman filter) to produce a single, high-rate, high-accuracy velocity estimate.

*Two Reasons:*
