Used for agricultural robots, drones, and last-mile delivery. **Comparison table [3 marks]:** | Aspect | SBAS | GBAS | NTRIP | |---|---|---|---| | Medium | Geostationary satellite | VHF ground transmitter | Cellular internet | | Coverage | Continental | Local (≤ 50 km) | Wherever cellular exists | | Accuracy | 1–3 m | 0.5–1 m | 0.5–1 cm (Network RTK) | | Cost | Free | Free (in coverage) | Subscription-based | | Robot Application | Open-field agric robot | Airport AGV | Survey robot, agri, drone | **One example each [2 marks]:** - SBAS: **GAGAN-equipped agricultural tractor** in Kerala's Wayanad tea estates (1 m guidance, free). - GBAS: **Airport baggage AGV** in Cochin International Airport (sub-meter precision, depot-localized). - NTRIP: **Survey robot in campus mapping** (cm-level accuracy via Kerala State CORS network). #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Given:** - Base position $\mathbf{X}_B = (1{,}600{,}000,\; 5{,}200{,}000,\; 100)$ m - Satellite position $\mathbf{X}^{(i)} = (15{,}000{,}000,\; 20{,}000{,}000,\; 18{,}000{,}000)$ m - Measured pseudorange $\rho_{B,\text{meas}} = 24{,}300{,}500$ m **Step 1 — Compute the true geometric range [3 marks]:** $$ R_{B,\text{true}} = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2 + (\Delta z)^2} $$ $$ \Delta x = 15{,}000{,}000 - 1{,}600{,}000 = 13{,}400{,}000 \;\text{m} $$ $$ \Delta y = 20{,}000{,}000 - 5{,}200{,}000 = 14{,}800{,}000 \;\text{m} $$ $$ \Delta z = 18{,}000{,}000 - 100 = 17{,}999{,}900 \;\text{m} $$ $$ R_{B,\text{true}} = \sqrt{(13{,}400{,}000)^2 + (14{,}800{,}000)^2 + (17{,}999{,}900)^2} $$ **Step 2 — Evaluate the squares [1 mark]:** $$ (13{,}400{,}000)^2 = 1.7956 \times 10^{14} $$ $$ (14{,}800{,}000)^2 = 2.1904 \times 10^{14} $$ $$ (17{,}999{,}900)^2 \approx 3.23996 \times 10^{14} $$ **Step 3 — Sum and take square root [1 mark]:** $$ R_{B,\text{true}} = \sqrt{(1.7956 + 2.1904 + 3.23996) \times 10^{14}} = \sqrt{7.22596 \times 10^{14}} $$ $$ R_{B,\text{true}} \approx 2.6881 \times 10^{7} \;\text{m} = 26{,}881{,}000 \;\text{m} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the PRC [1 mark]:** $$ \text{PRC} = R_{B,\text{true}} - \rho_{B,\text{meas}} = 26{,}881{,}000 - 24{,}300{,}500 = 2{,}580{,}500 \;\text{m} $$ **Step 5 — Physical interpretation [1 mark]:** > The **PRC = +2,580,500 m** indicates that the Base's measured pseudorange is **2,580,500 m shorter** than the true geometric range. This positive correction compensates for the cumulative effect of the **ionospheric delay, tropospheric delay, satellite clock bias, and ephemeris error** along the line-of-sight to the satellite. When broadcast to the Rover, the Rover *adds* this PRC to its own (similarly biased) measured pseudorange, **cancelling the common-mode errors** and yielding a more accurate position fix. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign convention error:** A PRC of +2,580,500 m means the *measurement is too short*; the correction *adds* a large positive value. Forgetting the sign costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Units confusion:** Always convert km to m before computing the square root, and verify the magnitude of the result matches a typical MEO-satellite range (~20,000–26,000 km). A wildly off answer (e.g., 2.6 × 10⁶ m instead of 2.688 × 10⁷ m) indicates a unit slip — guaranteed mark loss. > 3. **Assuming Base–Rover distance negligible:** In a real exam problem, if the Rover is far from the Base, students must *not* assume the corrections are perfect; mention the **decorrelation penalty** (F8) and add a residual-error estimate. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use this as a 60-second last-minute recap before walking into the KTU exam hall.** - ✅ **DGPS = Base + Rover + Data Link** architecture. Base is at a *surveyed* location; Rover is on the robot. - ✅ DGPS improves accuracy from **~15 m → 1–3 m** (code-phase) or **< 0.1 m** (RTK carrier-phase). - ✅ DGPS **cancels** common-mode errors: **Ionosphere, Troposphere, Satellite Clock, Ephemeris**. - ✅ DGPS **does NOT cancel** Rover-side **multipath** and **receiver noise** (these leak through as residuals). - ✅ Core formula: $\rho_{R,\text{corr}}^{(i)} = \rho_{R,\text{meas}}^{(i)} + \text{PRC}^{(i)}$ where $\text{PRC}^{(i)} = R_{B,\text{true}}^{(i)} - \rho_{B,\text{meas}}^{(i)}$. - ✅ Spatial correlation holds only for **baseline ≤ 100–200 km**; beyond that, ionospheric decorrelation dominates. - ✅ Standard correction protocol is **RTCM SC-104** (Radio Technical Commission for Maritime Services). - ✅ **SBAS** (WAAS/EGNOS/GAGAN) = satellite-delivered DGPS; **GBAS** = local VHF DGPS; **NTRIP** = internet-streamed RTCM. - ✅ **GAGAN** is India's SBAS (ISRO + AAI) — mention for bonus marks. - ✅ **HDOP** multiplies pseudorange URE to yield position error: $\sigma_{\text{pos}} = \sigma_{\text{URE}} \times \text{HDOP}$. - ✅ **2DRMS ≈ 2.4 × RMS** is the standard 95 % horizontal-accuracy reporting metric. - ✅ **RTK** uses carrier phase + integer-ambiguity resolution to reach centimeter accuracy; baseline ≤ 20 km. - ✅ **EKF integration** with IMU + wheel-odometry gives the **PVA (Position-Velocity-Attitude)** solution required by the robot's motion controller. - ✅ KTU loves asking: (i) error-model derivation, (ii) PRC calculation numerical, (iii) SBAS/GBAS/NTRIP comparison, (iv) limitation discussion (multipath + decorrelation). - ✅ **Watch the sign** of PRC; **watch the units** (m vs km); **label the block diagram** fully. - ✅ If a question mentions **"centimeter accuracy"**, the answer is **RTK**, not code-phase DGPS. > **All the best for your KTU 2024 ESE — localize, correct, and navigate! 🤖🛰️**

topics.title: RTK Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** RTK Navigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RTK (Real-Time Kinematic) Navigation** is a high-precision satellite-based positioning technique that uses carrier-phase measurements of GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System) signals, augmented in real time by a fixed reference (base) station transmitting correction data to a mobile (rover) receiver, achieving **centimetre-level positional accuracy** suitable for robotics, autonomous vehicles, and precision agriculture.

In strict GNSS terminology, RTK is a *differential positioning method* that resolves the **integer carrier-phase ambiguities** of the GNSS signal in real time, thereby allowing the rover receiver to compute its coordinates relative to a known base station with sub-decimetre precision.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine two people standing far apart, each holding a very expensive, finely graduated ruler (the **carrier wave** of the satellite signal). Individually, they cannot tell *exactly* how long the ruler is, because the satellite clock, atmosphere, and the ruler's own zero-point are slightly uncertain. However, if they compare their readings **at the exact same instant** for the **same satellite**, the common errors cancel out — and the only unknown is how many full ruler-lengths fit between them.

That "number of full ruler-lengths" is the **integer ambiguity (N)**. Once we figure it out, the distance becomes known to within a tiny fraction of a ruler. Solving this ambiguity in real time is the heart of RTK.

> [!NOTE]
> Think of it as a "**live surveyor**" — the base station acts like a stationary landmark, and the rover (your robot) figures out its exact position relative to that landmark by mathematically eliminating shared errors and counting whole wavelengths to the millimetre.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Horizontal Accuracy | **1 cm + 1 ppm** |
| Vertical Accuracy | **2 cm + 1 ppm** |
| Update Rate | **1 – 20 Hz** (commonly **10 Hz**) |
| Initialization Time (Fix) | **< 10 s** (modern receivers) |
| Range from Base | Up to **10 – 20 km** |
| Required Satellites | **≥ 5** (minimum 4 for 3D + ambiguity) |

> [!TIP]
> **1 ppm** means **1 cm of additional error per 1 km of baseline distance** between base and rover. So at 10 km baseline, expect ~10 cm of baseline-dependent error.

## 1.4 The Three GNSS Positioning Generations (Context)

1. **Standalone GNSS (SPS)** → ~3–5 m accuracy (pseudorange only).
2. **DGPS / SBAS (Differential GPS)** → ~0.5–1 m accuracy (pseudorange + code corrections).
3. **RTK / PPK (Real-Time Kinematic / Post-Processed Kinematic)** → **1–2 cm accuracy** (carrier-phase + ambiguity resolution).

RTK is the **third generation** of GNSS positioning and is currently the workhorse of robotic outdoor navigation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow — The Six Logical Stages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the **six-stage RTK pipeline** for short-answer KTU questions.

1. **Signal Acquisition**: Rover and base receivers simultaneously track carrier waves (L1, L2, L5) from common satellites.
2. **Pseudorange + Carrier-Phase Measurement**: Both compute code-based pseudoranges and **carrier-phase observations** (much more precise — millimetre-scale noise).
3. **Base Station Transmission**: Base computes and broadcasts correction data (RTCM SC-104 protocol) to rover via radio / NTRIP / cellular.
4. **Single-Differencing**: Rover subtracts base's measurements of the same satellite → cancels **satellite clock errors** and most **atmospheric delays**.
5. **Double-Differencing**: Rover subtracts measurements between two satellites → cancels **receiver clock errors**.
6. **Ambiguity Resolution (On-The-Fly, OTF)**: Solve for the integer number of wavelengths (N) using techniques like LAMBDA (Least-squares AMBiguity Decorrelation Adjustment). Once fixed → **Fixed-RTK solution**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Carrier Phase

- **Code (pseudorange) precision**: ~1 m (chip length of C/A code ≈ 293 m, then refined).
- **Carrier phase precision**: ~**1–2 mm** (wavelength is 19 cm for L1, 24 cm for L2, 25 cm for L5).
- The catch: carrier phase only measures the **fractional part** of the wave — the integer cycle count (N) is unknown. This is the **ambiguity problem**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Phi = N + \varphi$ | Carrier phase = integer cycles $N$ + fractional phase $\varphi$ | cycles |
| 2 | $L = \lambda \Phi = \lambda N + \lambda \varphi$ | Carrier-phase range (range in metres) | m |
| 3 | $\Phi_r^s = \rho_r^s + c(dt_r - dt^s) - I_r^s + T_r^s + \lambda N_r^s + \varepsilon_r^s$ | **Single-receiver / single-satellite carrier-phase observation** | cycles |
| 4 | $\nabla \Phi_{br}^s = \Phi_b^s - \Phi_r^s$ | **Single-difference** (between base $b$ and rover $r$, same satellite $s$) | cycles |
| 5 | $\nabla \Delta \Phi_{br}^{qs} = \nabla \Phi_{br}^q - \nabla \Phi_{br}^s$ | **Double-difference** (between two satellites $q,s$ and two receivers) | cycles |
| 6 | $\lambda \nabla \Delta \Phi_{br}^{qs} = \nabla \Delta \rho_{br}^{qs} + \lambda \nabla \Delta N_{br}^{qs} + \nabla \Delta \varepsilon_{br}^{qs}$ | **Final RTK measurement equation** (integer ambiguity appears) | m |
| 7 | $\sigma_{RTK} \approx 1\text{ cm} + 1\text{ ppm} \cdot d$ | Position accuracy (1 ppm per km of baseline) | cm |
| 8 | $f = c/\lambda$ | Carrier frequency ↔ wavelength relation | Hz |
| 9 | $\lambda \cdot N$ | Geometric range component "hidden" in ambiguity | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used in the cheat sheet**:
> - $\rho$ = geometric (true) range
> - $c$ = speed of light $\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> - $dt$ = clock bias
> - $I$ = ionospheric delay
> - $T$ = tropospheric delay
> - $N$ = integer ambiguity (number of full carrier cycles)
> - $\varepsilon$ = measurement noise + multipath
> - Subscripts: $r$ = rover, $b$ = base, $s,q$ = satellites
> - $\nabla$ = single-difference operator, $\nabla\Delta$ = double-difference

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Ground Robots (UGV)**: Lawn mowers, agricultural tractors, mining haul trucks, warehouse AMRs (outdoor) — Sunflower RTK systems in modern John Deere / AGCO tractors.
- **Autonomous Drones (UAV)**: Surveying, photogrammetry, precision landing.
- **Marine Robotics**: USV (Unmanned Surface Vessels) for hydrography.
- **Construction Robotics**: Robotic total stations, autonomous excavators, machine-control bulldozers.
- **SLAM Integration**: RTK provides global priors for visual / LiDAR SLAM in GNSS-friendly outdoor environments.

> [!TIP]
> KTU loves the question: *"Why is RTK preferred over DGPS for autonomous robots?"*
> **Answer**: DGPS gives ~1 m accuracy using code pseudoranges, while RTK uses carrier phase to achieve **centimetre** accuracy by resolving integer ambiguities — critical for lane-keeping, row-following, and precise docking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Raw Carrier Phase to the Double-Difference RTK Equation

### Step 1 — Carrier-Phase Observation at a Single Receiver

A single GNSS receiver $r$ observing a single satellite $s$ produces a carrier-phase observation in **cycles**. The model is:

$$
\Phi_r^s = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[\rho_r^s + c(dt_r - dt^s) - I_r^s + T_r^s\right] + N_r^s + \varepsilon_r^s
$$

- $\Phi_r^s$ = measured carrier phase in cycles
- $\lambda$ = carrier wavelength (e.g., 0.1903 m for L1)
- $\rho_r^s$ = geometric range between satellite and receiver
- $c$ = speed of light
- $dt_r, dt^s$ = receiver and satellite clock biases (in seconds)
- $I_r^s$ = ionospheric path delay
- $T_r^s$ = tropospheric path delay
- $N_r^s$ = **integer ambiguity** (unknown integer number of full carrier cycles)
- $\varepsilon_r^s$ = measurement noise + multipath

Multiplying through by $\lambda$ to get everything in metres:

$$
\lambda \Phi_r^s = \rho_r^s + c(dt_r - dt^s) - I_r^s + T_r^s + \lambda N_r^s + \lambda \varepsilon_r^s
$$

### Step 2 — Single-Difference (Between Base and Rover, Same Satellite)

Take two simultaneous observations: one by the **base** $b$ and one by the **rover** $r$, both of the same satellite $s$. Subtract rover from base:

$$
\nabla \Phi_{br}^s \equiv \Phi_b^s - \Phi_r^s
$$

Substituting the single-receiver model and simplifying:

$$
\nabla \Phi_{br}^s = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[\rho_b^s - \rho_r^s\right] + c(dt_b - dt_r) - (I_b^s - I_r^s) + (T_b^s - T_r^s) + (N_b^s - N_r^s) + (\varepsilon_b^s - \varepsilon_r^s)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **satellite clock bias** $dt^s$ has cancelled because both receivers see the same satellite at the same instant. We are left with the **receiver clock bias difference** $c(dt_b - dt_r)$ and the (much smaller) **differenced atmospheric errors**.

### Step 3 — Double-Difference (Between Two Satellites and Two Receivers)

Now take a *second* single-difference using a *different* satellite $q$ observed simultaneously, then subtract the two:

$$
\nabla \Delta \Phi_{br}^{qs} \equiv \nabla \Phi_{br}^q - \nabla \Phi_{br}^s = \Phi_b^q - \Phi_r^q - \Phi_b^s + \Phi_r^s
$$

This is the famous **double-difference**. Substituting and simplifying (the receiver clock terms $c(dt_b - dt_r)$ cancel between satellites because the receivers are common):

$$
\nabla \Delta \Phi_{br}^{qs} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[(\rho_b^q - \rho_r^q) - (\rho_b^s - \rho_r^s)\right] - \left[(I_b^q - I_r^q) - (I_b^s - I_r^s)\right] + \left[(T_b^q - T_r^q) - (T_b^s - T_r^s)\right] + (N_b^q - N_r^q) - (N_b^s - N_r^s) + \text{(noise)}
$$

Multiplying by $\lambda$ to express in metres:

$$
\lambda \nabla \Delta \Phi_{br}^{qs} = \nabla \Delta \rho_{br}^{qs} - \nabla \Delta I_{br}^{qs} + \nabla \Delta T_{br}^{qs} + \lambda \nabla \Delta N_{br}^{qs} + \nabla \Delta \varepsilon_{br}^{qs}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The integer ambiguity $\nabla \Delta N_{br}^{qs}$ is now the ONLY unknown discrete quantity** in the equation. The unknown range $\nabla \Delta \rho_{br}^{qs}$ is what we wish to compute (from the satellite ephemeris, it becomes a function of the rover's coordinates). The differential ionospheric and tropospheric terms are very small over short baselines and can be ignored or modelled.

### Step 4 — Final Compact RTK Observation Equation (Short Baseline Assumption)

For baselines < 10–20 km, the differential atmospheric errors become negligible. The working equation simplifies to:

$$
\lambda \nabla \Delta \Phi_{br}^{qs} = \nabla \Delta \rho_{br}^{qs} + \lambda \nabla \Delta N_{br}^{qs} + \nabla \Delta \varepsilon_{br}^{qs}
$$

Expanding the geometric range term in terms of the unknown rover position $(x_r, y_r, z_r)$ and known base position $(x_b, y_b, z_b)$:

$$
\nabla \Delta \rho_{br}^{qs} = \left[(\rho_b^q - \rho_r^q) - (\rho_b^s - \rho_r^s)\right]
$$

Each $\rho$ is computed via:

$$
\rho_r^s = \sqrt{(x_s - x_r)^2 + (y_s - y_r)^2 + (z_s - z_r)^2}
$$

This is a **non-linear system** solved iteratively using **Weighted Least Squares (WLS)** or **Kalman Filter**, with the integer ambiguity $\nabla \Delta N$ resolved via the **LAMBDA method** to enforce integer-constraint solutions.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Error Sources in RTK

| Error Source | Magnitude (Standalone) | After Double-Differencing | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Satellite clock ($dt^s$) | ~2 m | **Eliminated** | — |
| Receiver clock ($dt_r$) | ~1–10 m | **Eliminated** | — |
| Ionospheric delay ($I$) | 1–10 m | **Mostly eliminated** (<10 km) | Dual-frequency receivers, iono-free combinations |
| Tropospheric delay ($T$) | 2–5 m | **Mostly eliminated** (<10 km) | Tropo models (Saastamoinen, Hopfield) |
| Ephemeris error | 1–5 m | Reduced (mm–cm) | Precise ephemeris (IGS products) |
| Multipath | cm–m | Reduced (mm–cm) | Choke-ring antennas, site selection |
| Carrier-phase noise | ~1–2 mm | ~1–2 mm | Quality receiver hardware |
| **Integer ambiguity error** | **Unbounded if wrong** | **CRITICAL** | LAMBDA, AR (Ambiguity Resolution) algorithms |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Order-of-Magnitude Accuracy

> [!TIP]
> This is a high-frequency KTU numerical problem.

**Question**: A robot uses RTK with a baseline of 7 km. Compute the expected horizontal position error (in cm) using the 1 cm + 1 ppm rule.

**Solution**:

$$
\sigma_H = 1 \text{ cm} + (1 \text{ ppm}) \times (7 \text{ km})
$$

$$
\sigma_H = 1 \text{ cm} + 1 \text{ cm/km} \times 7 \text{ km} = 1 + 7 = 8 \text{ cm}
$$

**Horizontal accuracy ≈ 8 cm.** ✅

Similarly, vertical: $\sigma_V = 2 + 7 = 9$ cm.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RTK System Architecture — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[GNSS Satellite Constellation L1 L2 L5] --> B1[Base Station Receiver at known coordinates]
    A --> B2[Rover Receiver mounted on robot]
    B1 --> C[Raw carrier phase and code measurements]
    B2 --> D[Raw carrier phase and code measurements]
    C --> E[Base computes RTCM SC 104 correction data]
    E --> F[Radio link or NTRIP or cellular]
    F --> G[Rover applies corrections]
    D --> G
    G --> H[Single differencing cancels satellite clock]
    H --> I[Double differencing cancels receiver clock]
    I --> J[Integer ambiguity resolution via LAMBDA]
    J --> K{Ambiguity Fixed successfully}
    K -- Yes --> L[FIXED RTK Solution cm level]
    K -- No --> M[FLOAT Solution decimetre level]
    L --> N[Robot pose estimate x y z]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Signal Processing Flow — From Raw Wave to Robot Pose

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Antenna receives L band carrier 1.5 GHz] --> S2[RF front end down conversion to baseband]
    S2 --> S3[Correlation with local replica yields code and phase]
    S3 --> S4[Carrier phase Phi in cycles]
    S3 --> S5[Pseudorange rho in metres]
    S4 --> S6[Form single difference with base]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Form double difference between satellites]
    S7 --> S8[Solve linearised equation via Weighted Least Squares]
    S8 --> S9[Obtain float ambiguity estimate]
    S9 --> S10[Apply LAMBDA to enforce integer constraint]
    S10 --> S11[Convert ambiguity to range and then to ECEF xyz]
    S11 --> S12[Convert ECEF to ENU local frame for robot]
```

## 4.3 RTK vs DGPS vs PPK — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GEN1[Generation 1 Standalone]
        A1[GNSS only] --> A2[Pseudorange only] --> A3[3 to 5 m accuracy]
    end
    subgraph GEN2[Generation 2 DGPS SBAS]
        B1[GNSS plus base corrections] --> B2[Code pseudorange corrections] --> B3[0.5 to 1 m accuracy]
    end
    subgraph GEN3[Generation 3 RTK PPK]
        C1[GNSS plus base corrections] --> C2[Carrier phase plus integer ambiguity] --> C3[1 to 2 cm accuracy]
    end
    GEN1 --> GEN2 --> GEN3
```

## 4.4 LAMBDA Ambiguity Resolution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Float ambiguity vector from WLS] --> L2[Z transformation decorrelates ambiguities]
    L2 --> L3[Integer rounding within search ellipsoid]
    L3 --> L4[Generate candidate integer vectors]
    L4 --> L5[Compute residuals for each candidate]
    L5 --> L6[Pick candidate with minimum norm of residuals]
    L6 --> L7[Validated integer ambiguity fixed]
    L7 --> L8[Final cm level RTK fix]
```

## 4.5 Robot Integration Block Diagram (RTK + INS + SLAM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[RTK GNSS receiver] --> R2[Position fix xyz ENU]
    I1[IMU gyroscope plus accelerometer] --> I2[Inertial integration for orientation and velocity]
    R2 --> F1[Kalman Filter sensor fusion]
    I2 --> F1
    S1[Wheel odometry] --> F1
    V1[LiDAR or camera SLAM] --> F1
    F1 --> R3[Robot state estimate position velocity orientation]
    R3 --> R4[Motion controller for path tracking]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This last diagram is highly exam-relevant. In real robotic systems, RTK alone is **not sufficient** because GNSS signals can be lost (tunnels, urban canyons, indoor). It is therefore fused with IMU, odometry, and SLAM using an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** or **Error-State Kalman Filter (ESKF)** to give continuous, robust, centimetre-level positioning.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define RTK navigation. Why is it preferred over DGPS for autonomous mobile robots?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RTK (Real-Time Kinematic) navigation** is a carrier-phase-based differential GNSS technique that uses a fixed base station and a mobile rover to resolve integer-cycle ambiguities in real time, achieving **centimetre-level positioning accuracy** (typically 1 cm + 1 ppm).

**Why preferred over DGPS:**

1. **DGPS uses code pseudoranges** → accuracy ~0.5–1 m. RTK uses **carrier phase** → accuracy ~1 cm.
2. RTK resolves the **integer ambiguity (N)** in real time using algorithms like LAMBDA, giving a *fixed* integer solution.
3. Autonomous robots require **lane-level / path-tracking** precision, which 1 m DGPS cannot deliver.
4. RTK also has higher update rates (up to 20 Hz) suitable for high-speed mobile robots.

*(3 marks — definition 1, comparison points 1, robotic relevance 1)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is integer ambiguity in GNSS carrier-phase measurements? Name one method to resolve it.**

**Model Answer:**

The **integer ambiguity $N$** is the unknown integer number of complete carrier-wave cycles between a GNSS satellite and a receiver. Since the receiver can only measure the fractional part $\varphi$ of the carrier phase, the integer count $N$ must be estimated to obtain the true geometric range.

The fundamental relation is:

$$
L = \lambda N + \lambda \varphi
$$

where $L$ is the total range, $\lambda$ is the carrier wavelength, and $\varphi$ is the measured fractional phase.

**Resolution method**: **LAMBDA (Least-squares AMBiguity Decorrelation Adjustment)** — performs a Z-transformation to decorrelate ambiguity candidates and searches within an integer ellipsoid for the correct integer vector that minimises the sum of squared residuals.

Other methods: **On-The-Fly (OTF) ambiguity resolution**, **Extra-Wide-Lane (EWL)** for dual-frequency, **PAMB**, etc.

*(3 marks — definition 1, equation 1, LAMBDA explanation 1)*

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, students answer ONE full question per module. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### ✅ CHOICE A — 14 Marks

#### **Q3(a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
**With neat functional block diagrams, explain the architecture and working of an RTK navigation system. Compare RTK with DGPS and PPK.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Architecture** (refer Section 4.1 diagram):
- **GNSS Satellites** transmit L1/L2/L5 carrier signals.
- **Base Station** — at precisely surveyed coordinates, receives signals, computes and transmits RTCM SC-104 corrections.
- **Communication Link** — radio modem, NTRIP (networked), or cellular.
- **Rover Receiver** — on the robot, applies corrections, forms single & double differences, resolves ambiguities → cm-level fix.
- **Pose Output** — position, velocity, time (PVT) fed to robot controller.

**Working Principle** (refer Section 2.1):
1. Simultaneous observation of common satellites.
2. Code pseudorange + carrier phase measurement.
3. Base → rover transmission of corrections.
4. Single-differencing (cancels satellite clock).
5. Double-differencing (cancels receiver clock).
6. LAMBDA-based integer ambiguity resolution.
7. Fixed-RTK solution → cm accuracy.

**Comparison Table** (2 marks for table):

| Feature | DGPS | RTK | PPK |
|---|---|---|---|
| Observation type | Code pseudorange | Carrier phase | Carrier phase |
| Accuracy | 0.5 – 1 m | 1 – 2 cm | 1 – 2 cm |
| Real-time? | Yes | Yes | No (post-processed) |
| Ambiguity resolution | No | Yes (OTF / LAMBDA) | Yes (offline) |
| Base required? | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Update rate | 1–5 Hz | 1–20 Hz | Post-batch |
| Use case | Marine, aviation | Robotics, surveying | Aerial mapping |

**DGPS** uses only code measurements → limited by chip length → ~1 m.
**RTK** uses carrier phase + integer ambiguity → cm-level in real time.
**PPK** uses carrier phase but processes data offline → no radio link required → useful in UAVs that lose link.

---

#### **Q3(b) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
**Derive the double-differenced carrier-phase observation equation used in RTK, clearly explaining the role of single-differencing in cancelling satellite clock errors.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

[Full derivation as in **Section 3.1** — single-receiver equation → single-difference → double-difference → final compact form.]

**Key marks allocation:**
1. Starting single-receiver equation (1 mark).
2. Single-difference derivation with explanation that $dt^s$ cancels (1.5 marks).
3. Double-difference derivation with explanation that $dt_r$ cancels (1.5 marks).
4. Final compact equation with proper notation (1 mark).
5. Explanation of short-baseline assumption and remaining unknowns (1 mark).
6. LAMBDA / integer constraint mention (1 mark).

**Final equation to be highlighted:**

$$
\lambda \nabla \Delta \Phi_{br}^{qs} = \nabla \Delta \rho_{br}^{qs} + \lambda \nabla \Delta N_{br}^{qs} + \nabla \Delta \varepsilon_{br}^{qs}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall** — students often forget to **multiply by $\lambda$** when converting cycles to metres, OR they write the single-difference equation as a *sum* instead of a *difference*. Another common mistake is not stating explicitly that the **integer ambiguity becomes constant (time-invariant) only after cycle slips are detected and repaired** — otherwise the ambiguity reset invalidates the solution.

---

### ✅ CHOICE B — 14 Marks (Alternative Module Choice)

#### **Q4(a) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**
**Explain the sources of error in GNSS positioning. Discuss how double-differencing in RTK mitigates each error, with reference to short-baseline and long-baseline scenarios.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Error Sources** (refer Section 3.2 table):

1. **Satellite clock bias** ($dt^s$): ~2 m — *eliminated* by single-differencing.
2. **Receiver clock bias** ($dt_r$): ~1–10 m — *eliminated* by double-differencing.
3. **Ionospheric delay** ($I$): 1–10 m — *significantly reduced* by differencing; residuals scale with baseline length.
4. **Tropospheric delay** ($T$): 2–5 m — *reduced*; residual grows with baseline.
5. **Ephemeris error**: 1–5 m — *reduced*; depends on baseline projection onto orbit direction.
6. **Multipath**: cm–m — *NOT eliminated* by differencing; remains site-specific.
7. **Carrier-phase noise**: ~1–2 mm — unaffected; limited by hardware.

**Short baseline (<10 km)** assumption: differential ionospheric and tropospheric errors become sub-cm. The compact equation holds.

**Long baseline (>20–50 km)** issue: differential atmospheric errors grow. Solutions include:
- **Dual-frequency iono-free combination**: $L_C = \frac{f_1^2 L_1 - f_2^2 L_2}{f_1^2 - f_2^2}$ cancels the first-order ionospheric term.
- **Tropospheric modelling**: Saastamoinen + Niell mapping function.
- **Network RTK (NRTK) / VRS / MAC**: virtual reference stations via regional CORS networks.

---

#### **Q4(b) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**
**A robot's RTK system has a baseline of 12 km. If the base-to-rover distance is doubled, by how much does the expected horizontal accuracy degrade? Comment on the implications for outdoor robot navigation.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given**: Baseline $d_1 = 12$ km.
**Find**: Accuracy at $d_2 = 24$ km, and the degradation.

**Step 1** — Apply the 1 cm + 1 ppm rule:

At $d_1 = 12$ km:
$$
\sigma_{H1} = 1 + 1 \times 12 = 13 \text{ cm}
$$

At $d_2 = 24$ km:
$$
\sigma_{H2} = 1 + 1 \times 24 = 25 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 2** — Compute degradation:

$$
\Delta \sigma_H = \sigma_{H2} - \sigma_{H1} = 25 - 13 = 12 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 3** — Percentage degradation:
$$
\frac{12}{13} \times 100 \approx 92.3\%
$$

So doubling the baseline nearly **doubles** the horizontal error, and the proportional error grows by **~92%**.

**Implications for outdoor robot navigation (3 marks):**

1. **Long-baseline RTK is unreliable** for precision robotics — degradations of tens of cm can push a robot off its lane or path.
2. **Solution 1**: Deploy **multiple local base stations** or use a **NTRIP / VRS / CORS network** to keep effective baseline < 10 km.
3. **Solution 2**: **Sensor fusion** — combine RTK with **IMU + wheel odometry + LiDAR SLAM** in a Kalman filter. During GNSS outages (tunnels, urban canyons), inertial + SLAM fills the gap.
4. **Solution 3**: Use **dual-frequency receivers** with iono-free combinations to reduce atmospheric residuals.
5. **Conclusion**: For autonomous outdoor robots operating over large areas, RTK must be **part of a multi-sensor fusion stack**, not relied upon as a standalone positioning source.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (and lose marks on) for RTK questions:**
> 1. **Confusing DGPS with RTK** — DGPS is code-based (~1 m); RTK is carrier-phase-based (~1 cm). Many answers mix them up.
> 2. **Forgetting the integer-ambiguity step** — RTK's *defining* feature is the **resolution of $N$**; skipping it loses full credit.
> 3. **Wrong order of differencing** — single-difference cancels **satellite** clock, double-difference cancels **receiver** clock. Reversing this is a common error.
> 4. **Mixing units** — carrier phase is in **cycles**; range is in **metres**. Always multiply by $\lambda$.
> 5. **Ignoring the 1 ppm term** in numerical problems. KTU *always* tests the 1 cm + 1 ppm rule.
> 6. **Not mentioning cycle slips** in long answers. A real examiner expects a sentence on cycle-slip detection (e.g., Melbourne–Wübbena, TurboEdit).
> 7. **Skipping the fusion aspect** — modern robotics expects RTK fused with IMU/SLAM, not used alone.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **RTK = Real-Time Kinematic** — centimetre-level GNSS positioning using carrier phase + integer ambiguity resolution.
- ✅ **Two physical components**: **Base station** (fixed, known) + **Rover** (mobile, on the robot).
- ✅ **Two key operations**: **Double-differencing** (eliminates clock errors) + **Ambiguity resolution** (LAMBDA).
- ✅ **Accuracy rule of thumb**: **1 cm + 1 ppm** horizontally, **2 cm + 1 ppm** vertically.
- ✅ **Carrier wavelength**: L1 ≈ 19 cm, L2 ≈ 24 cm, L5 ≈ 25 cm.
- ✅ **Update rate**: typically 1 – 20 Hz; 10 Hz is most common in robotics.
- ✅ **Float vs Fixed**: float = decimetre (ambiguity unresolved), fixed = centimetre (ambiguity resolved).
- ✅ **Why double-differencing?** Cancels satellite clock (single-diff) and receiver clock (double-diff).
- ✅ **Final RTK equation**: $\lambda \nabla \Delta \Phi_{br}^{qs} = \nabla \Delta \rho_{br}^{qs} + \lambda \nabla \Delta N_{br}^{qs} + \nabla \Delta \varepsilon_{br}^{qs}$.
- ✅ **Atmospheric errors**: small over < 10 km; dual-frequency + iono-free combo needed for longer baselines.
- ✅ **Network RTK (NRTIP / VRS / MAC)**: solves the long-baseline problem for regional robot fleets.
- ✅ **Always fuse with IMU + LiDAR + wheel odometry** in a Kalman filter for robust outdoor autonomy.
- ✅ **Protocol**: RTCM SC-104 is the standard correction-data format.
- ✅ **RTK vs PPK**: RTK is real-time; PPK is post-processed (used in UAV photogrammetry where radio link is impractical).
- ✅ **Cycle slips** must be detected (Melbourne–Wübbena / TurboEdit) and the ambiguity re-initialised.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GNSS Threats
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT751 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** GNSS Threats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) threats** are defined as any intentional, unintentional, or natural phenomena that degrade, deny, or deceive the positioning, navigation, and timing (PNT) services provided by satellite constellations such as GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, BeiDou, NavIC, and QZSS.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *PEBRT751 – Robot Navigation*, GNSS threats are formally classified under three super-categories:

1. **Intentional Interference** – Jamming, Spoofing, Meaconing.
2. **Unintentional Interference** – Multipath, Atmospheric Scintillation, Radio Frequency Interference (RFI).
3. **System-Level Anomalies** – Satellite clock drift, Ephemeris errors, Signal blockages, Receiver noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Focus:** The 2024 Scheme places heavy weight on the *threat–countermeasure pair*. Students are expected to map each threat to its specific detection and mitigation technique — not just define them in isolation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **blindfolded and standing in a large field**, and four trusted friends on distant hilltops are each shouting a number — the *time* at which they shouted it. By comparing their shouts, you can locate yourself within meters. This is exactly how GNSS works.

Now consider the threats:

- **Jamming** is like a *loud rock band* playing next to you. The friends are still shouting accurately, but you simply **cannot hear them** over the noise. Your receiver is "deafened."
- **Spoofing** is far more sinister: a *mimic* with a megaphone stands beside you and **impersonates your friends**, shouting fake times. Your receiver believes the lies and reports a position that is **smoothly and silently wrong**.
- **Multipath** is your voice bouncing off a nearby wall and reaching you a fraction of a second late. The system thinks you are slightly farther from the speaker than you really are.
- **Atmospheric Delay** is sound traveling through a dense, humid fog — it slows down and arrives late, skewing your distance estimate.
- **Meaconing** is someone *recording* your friends' shouts from yesterday and replaying them today. The data is real, but **stale**.

> [!NOTE]
> **GNSS Signal Floor Reference:** Civil GPS signals (L1 C/A) are designed to arrive at Earth with a power of approximately **−160 dBW (≈ 10⁻¹⁶ W)**, which is roughly **20 dB below the thermal noise floor** of a typical receiver. This extreme fragility is the fundamental reason GNSS threats are so easy to execute and so hard to defeat.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **GNSS Carrier Frequencies**: L1 = **1575.42 MHz**, L2 = **1227.60 MHz**, L5 = **1176.45 MHz**, E1, E5a/b, B1, B3.
- **Speed of Light (vacuum)**: $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
- **1 Light-microsecond (LMU)**: **≈ 300 m** (the basic ranging unit).
- **Minimum received power (GPS L1 C/A)**: **−158.5 dBW to −160 dBW**.
- **J/S (Jammer-to-Signal ratio)** — the primary metric for jammer effectiveness.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU numerical problems, always express powers in **dB** and use the conversion: $\text{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{1}/P_{2})$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Threat Taxonomy (Structured Bulleted Logic)

#### A. Intentional Threats

**1. Jamming (Signal Suppression / Denial)**
- *Broadband Noise Jamming* — spreads noise across the entire GNSS band. Most common commercial jammers.
- *Narrowband / Continuous Wave (CW) Jamming* — targets a single carrier (e.g., 1575.42 MHz).
- *Pulsed Jamming* — transmits short, high-power bursts.
- *Swept Jamming* — sweeps a narrow CW across the spectrum.
- *Smart / Responsive Jamming* — detects GNSS signals first, then jams only that band (efficient).

**2. Spoofing (Signal Deception)**
- *Simplistic Spoofing* — replays a recorded authentic signal (meaconing).
- *Intermediate Spoofing* — uses a GPS simulator with a synchronized receiver to align spoofed signals.
- *Sophisticated / Advanced Spoofing* — generates multiple coherent false signals that slowly "lift" the victim's reported position. This is the most dangerous because no alarm is triggered.

**3. Meaconing**
- A specific subset of spoofing: delay-line replay of an authentic GNSS signal. The receiver obtains a **valid but stale** solution.

#### B. Unintentional Threats

**1. Multipath Error**
- Signal reflects off ground, buildings, water, vehicles before reaching the antenna.
- Causes **pseudorange error** of up to **0.5 code chip** (≈ 150 m for C/A code) or **0.5 carrier cycle** (≈ 10 cm for phase).
- Mitigated by **choke-ring antennas**, **bumpers**, and **receiver-internal multipath mitigation algorithms**.

**2. Ionospheric Delay**
- Free electrons in the ionosphere (60–1000 km altitude) slow the group velocity and advance the phase velocity.
- **First-order error ∝ TEC (Total Electron Content) / f²**.
- Dual-frequency receivers eliminate ≈99.9% of the first-order term.

**3. Tropospheric Delay**
- Neutral atmosphere (0–60 km). Includes *dry* (hydrostatic) and *wet* components.
- Depends on **temperature, pressure, humidity**. Modeled by **Saastamoinen** or **Hopfield** models.

**4. Signal Blockage / Shadowing**
- Urban canyons, foliage canopy, tunnels, indoor environments.
- Reduces visible satellite count → degraded **DOP (Dilution of Precision)**.

#### C. System-Level Anomalies

- **Satellite Clock Error**: corrected via the navigation message, residual error typically < 1 m.
- **Ephemeris Error**: orbital prediction error; usually < 1 m after broadcast correction.
- **Receiver Noise**: thermal noise, oscillator phase noise.

### 2.2 Why and How — The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why is GNSS so vulnerable to jamming?**
  Because the received power is **~20 dB below the noise floor**, any small additional noise can drown the signal. A jammer with only **1 W** of power can deny GPS over several km.

- **Why is spoofing harder than jamming?**
  The attacker must match the **Precise Pseudorandom Noise (PRN) code, Doppler, and code phase** of the legitimate signal across all visible satellites. Sophisticated spoofing requires Software Defined Radio (SDR) synchronization with the victim's receiver.

- **Why does multipath cause bias in pseudorange but not in carrier phase identically?**
  Because code correlation has a wide autocorrelation triangle (≈ 1 chip wide), while carrier phase measurement is sharp (sub-cm). Multipath distorts the early-late correlator taps differently.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** — they appear repeatedly in KTU university examinations.

| **#** | **Formula / Expression** | **Description / Engineering Use** | **Units** |
|:-----:|:-------------------------|:-----------------------------------|:---------:|
| 1 | $P_{r} = P_{t} + G_{t} + G_{r} - L_{fs} - L_{atm}$ | Received power via Friis/link budget | dBW |
| 2 | $L_{fs} = 20 \log_{10}(4\pi d / \lambda)$ | Free-space path loss | dB |
| 3 | $J/S = P_{j} - P_{r} + G_{j} - G_{r} + \text{PL}_{j/r}$ | Jammer-to-Signal ratio (jammer effectiveness) | dB |
| 4 | $\Delta t_{iono} = \dfrac{40.3 \cdot \text{TEC}}{c \cdot f^{2}}$ | First-order ionospheric group delay | seconds |
| 5 | $\Delta\rho_{iono} \propto \text{TEC} \cdot f^{-2}$ | Ionospheric range error | meters |
| 6 | $\Delta\rho_{tropo} = \dfrac{2.47}{\sin(E)}\left[P + \dfrac{1255}{T} \cdot e\right]$ | Saastamoinen tropospheric delay | meters |
| 7 | $\sigma_{multipath}^{code} \le 0.5$ chip | Max code multipath error | chips |
| 8 | $\sigma_{multipath}^{carrier} \le 0.25 \lambda$ | Max carrier multipath error | meters |
| 9 | $\text{GDOP}^{2} = \text{PDOP}^{2} + \text{TDOP}^{2}$ | Geometric dilution decomposition | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{PDOP} = \sqrt{\text{tr}(H^{T}H)^{-1}}$ | Position DOP from geometry matrix | dimensionless |
| 11 | $P_{fa} = Q(\sqrt{2 \cdot \text{SNR}})$ | False alarm probability at threshold | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\text{PLL}_{BW} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{SNR}}$ | Phase-lock loop bandwidth scales with SNR | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $E$ = elevation angle, $P$ = atmospheric pressure, $T$ = temperature (K), $e$ = water vapor pressure, $Q(\cdot)$ = Q-function, $H$ = geometry matrix, SNR = signal-to-noise ratio.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Detection of spoofing is mandatory for SAE Level 4+ autonomy.
- **Aviation**: RAIM is the certified integrity check for non-precision approaches.
- **Maritime**: Spoofing is documented near conflict zones and major ports (e.g., Black Sea, 2017 mass-spoofing incident affecting ~20 ships simultaneously).
- **Robotics**: Wheeled mobile robots in urban environments need multipath-resilient receivers (e.g., NovAtel, Septentrio, u-blox F9).
- **Defense**: M-Code and SAASM are mandatory for military GPS receivers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Jammer-to-Signal (J/S) Ratio at the Receiver

This derivation is the **single most important numerical exercise** for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4.

**Problem Setup:** A GNSS satellite at range $d_s$ transmits with EIRP $P_{t,s}$ (W). A jammer at range $d_j$ transmits with EIRP $P_{t,j}$ (W). Both share the receiver's antenna pattern $G_r$ (dBi). Find $J/S$ in decibels.

**Step 1 — Received GNSS Signal Power:**

$$
P_{r,s} = \frac{P_{t,s} \cdot G_{t,s} \cdot G_{r,s} \cdot \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} d_{s}^{2} L_{atm}}
$$

(where $L_{atm}$ is the atmospheric loss factor, usually 1–2 dB).

**Step 2 — Received Jammer Power:**

$$
P_{r,j} = \frac{P_{t,j} \cdot G_{t,j} \cdot G_{r,j} \cdot \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} d_{j}^{2} L_{prop,j}}
$$

**Step 3 — Express in dB form. Using Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL):**

$$
P_{r,s} = \text{EIRP}_{s} - \text{FSPL}(d_{s}) + G_{r,s} - L_{atm}
$$

$$
P_{r,j} = \text{EIRP}_{j} - \text{FSPL}(d_{j}) + G_{r,j} - L_{prop,j}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute J/S in dB (subtract the two):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J/S \text{ (dB)} &= P_{r,j} - P_{r,s} \\
&= \text{EIRP}_{j} - \text{EIRP}_{s} + G_{r,j} - G_{r,s} \\
&\quad - \text{FSPL}(d_{j}) + \text{FSPL}(d_{s}) - L_{prop,j} + L_{atm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Practical Case (Receiver pointed at zenith, jammer near horizon):**

Assume $G_{r,j} \approx G_{r,s}$ (broad-beam receiver antenna, gain toward both satellite and jammer ≈ similar magnitude within a few dB). The dominant terms reduce to:

$$
J/S \approx (\text{EIRP}_{j} - \text{EIRP}_{s}) - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d_{s}}{d_{j}}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Example (KTU standard problem):**

Given: Satellite EIRP = **26.8 dBW** (GPS Block IIR), Jammer EIRP = **10 W = 10 dBW**, $d_{s}$ = 20,200 km, $d_{j}$ = 50 km.

$$
\begin{aligned}
J/S \text{ (dB)} &= (10 - 26.8) - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{20{,}200{,}000}{50{,}000}\right) \\
&= -16.8 - 20 \log_{10}(404) \\
&= -16.8 - 20(2.606) \\
&= -16.8 - 52.12 \\
&= -68.92 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to linear ratio:**

$$
J/S = 10^{-68.92/10} \approx 1.28 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation:**

A *negative* J/S in dB means the **signal is stronger than the jammer**. To deny GPS, the jammer needs J/S > 0 dB (typically J/S > +10 dB is required to overcome receiver processing gain and tracking loop margins). The jammer in this example is far too weak — increase power or reduce $d_j$.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** KTU problems on jamming frequently test the student's ability to identify which term dominates. The free-space path loss difference (≈ 52 dB in this case) is the **single biggest term** because the satellite is ~400× farther than a typical ground jammer.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Ionospheric Range Error and Dual-Frequency Correction

**Step 1 — Phase refractive index in the ionosphere (first-order):**

$$
n_{p} = 1 - \frac{40.3 \cdot N_{e}}{f^{2}}
$$

where $N_e$ = electron density (electrons/m³), $f$ = carrier frequency (Hz).

**Step 2 — Group refractive index (code signal):**

$$
n_{g} = 1 + \frac{40.3 \cdot N_{e}}{f^{2}}
$$

(Note: phase advances, group delays — opposite effects.)

**Step 3 — Total ionospheric delay (integral along the ray path):**

$$
\Delta t_{iono} = \frac{1}{c}\int (n_{g} - 1) \, ds = \frac{40.3}{c f^{2}} \int N_{e} \, ds
$$

**Step 4 — Define Total Electron Content (TEC):**

$$
\text{TEC} = \int N_{e} \, ds \quad \text{(units: electrons/m², often expressed as TECU = 10}^{16} \text{ e/m}^2\text{)}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**

$$
\Delta t_{iono} = \frac{40.3 \cdot \text{TEC}}{c \cdot f^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to range error:**

$$
\Delta\rho_{iono} = c \cdot \Delta t_{iono} = \frac{40.3 \cdot \text{TEC}}{f^{2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Dual-Frequency Ionospheric-Free (IF) Combination:**

The receiver measures pseudoranges $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2$ on frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$. The IF pseudorange is:

$$
\rho_{IF} = \frac{f_{1}^{2} \rho_{1} - f_{2}^{2} \rho_{2}}{f_{1}^{2} - f_{2}^{2}}
$$

**Step 8 — Proof of cancellation (substitute $\rho_i = R + \Delta\rho_{iono,i} + \epsilon_i$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{IF} &= \frac{f_{1}^{2}\!\left(R + \frac{K}{f_{1}^{2}}\right) - f_{2}^{2}\!\left(R + \frac{K}{f_{2}^{2}}\right)}{f_{1}^{2} - f_{2}^{2}} \\
&= \frac{R(f_{1}^{2} - f_{2}^{2}) + K - K}{f_{1}^{2} - f_{2}^{2}} \\
&= R
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K = 40.3 \cdot \text{TEC}$ is a common constant. The ionospheric term **cancels exactly** to first order.

**Step 9 — Magnitude check (numerical):**

If TEC = 50 TECU = $5 \times 10^{17}$ e/m², $f_1$ = 1575.42 MHz:

$$
\Delta\rho_{iono,L1} = \frac{40.3 \times 5 \times 10^{17}}{(1.575 \times 10^{9})^{2}} \approx 8.1 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** A common KTU 14-mark question asks the student to **prove** that the dual-frequency combination eliminates ionospheric error, then **compute** the residual (higher-order) term, which is typically **< 1 cm**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Threat vs. Detection vs. Mitigation

This matrix is the **central KTU-ready analytical framework** for Module 4.

| **Threat Type** | **Specific Effect on Receiver** | **Detection Method** | **Mitigation Technique** |
|:----------------|:--------------------------------|:---------------------|:-------------------------|
| Broadband Jamming | Increased noise floor, lost lock | Automatic Gain Control (AGC) monitoring, $C/N_0$ drop | CRPA antenna, notch filter, LNA front-end |
| Narrowband Jamming | Carrier spike in spectrum | FFT spectrum analysis | Adaptive notch filter (digital) |
| Spoofing (simple) | Sudden pseudorange jump | Power monitoring, PRN consistency check | Multi-antenna angle-of-arrival |
| Spoofing (sophisticated) | Smooth position drift | Clock bias discontinuity, SQM, RAIM | Cryptographic auth (Chimera, OS-NMA) |
| Meaconing | Stale but valid signal | Time-tag verification | Tight time synchronization |
| Multipath (code) | Pseudorange bias 0.5 chip | Double-delta correlator, SQM | Choke-ring antenna, MEDLL |
| Multipath (carrier) | Phase bias 0.25λ | Carrier smoothing of code | Phase-rate aiding |
| Ionospheric storm | Range error 5–100 m | Dual-frequency residual, $C/N_0$ variance | IF combination, SBAS corrections |
| Tropospheric delay | 2–25 m range error | Tropospheric model residual | Saastamoinen + surface met data |
| Signal blockage | Loss of satellites, high DOP | Satellite count monitor | INS aiding, dead reckoning |
| Satellite clock anomaly | Sudden range jump | RAIM, AAIM | Exclusion of faulty SV |
| Ephemeris error | Slowly growing range error | Long-baseline residual | Differential GNSS (DGNSS) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between GNSS jamming and GNSS spoofing with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

GNSS **jamming** is the intentional transmission of radio frequency energy in the GNSS band with the objective of *denying* reception. The legitimate satellite signal is still present, but the receiver cannot track it because the noise floor has been raised. Example: A vehicle-mounted broadband noise jammer that prevents a delivery drone from acquiring GPS.

GNSS **spoofing**, in contrast, is the intentional transmission of *false but valid-looking* GNSS signals with the objective of *deceiving* the receiver into computing a wrong position. The receiver remains in lock — no alarm is raised — but the reported PVT is incorrect. Example: A sophisticated spoofer that gradually "drags" a ship's reported position toward a false harbor.

| **Parameter** | **Jamming** | **Spoofing** |
|:--------------|:-------------|:-------------|
| Receiver state | Loss of lock | Maintains lock |
| Detection difficulty | Easy (AGC drop) | Hard (silent) |
| Countermeasure cost | Low (filter) | High (cryptographic) |
| Threat severity | Denial of service | Integrity violation |

**[3 marks]** ✔ Definition 1, ✔ Example 1, ✔ Comparison table 1.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define "Meaconing" in the context of GNSS threats. How does it differ from spoofing?**

**Model Answer:**

**Meaconing** is a *passive-to-active* GNSS threat in which an adversary records authentic GNSS signals with a sensitive receiver and then **retransmits them at higher power with a fixed delay**. The target receiver obtains a perfectly valid navigation message but a **delayed pseudorange**, leading to a position offset that grows linearly with the replay delay (≈ 1 µs delay = 300 m error).

**Difference from Spoofing:**

| **Feature** | **Meaconing** | **Spoofing** |
|:------------|:--------------|:-------------|
| Signal origin | Recorded genuine signal | Synthetically generated |
| Code authenticity | Real, but stale | Fake |
| Time delay | Fixed replay latency | Variable, attacker-controlled |
| Power | High (re-broadcast) | Matched to genuine signal |
| Detection | Time-stamp / clock jump | Cryptographic / SQM |

**[3 marks]** ✔ Definition 1, ✔ Diagram-of-effect 1, ✔ Difference 1.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

## ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`**

**(a) Classify the various GNSS threats. Discuss in detail the unintentional threats and their mitigation techniques.** **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

**Introduction (1 mark):**

GNSS threats are any condition that degrades the **Accuracy, Availability, Continuity, or Integrity (AACI)** of the PNT solution. The KTU 2024 framework classifies them into **Intentional**, **Unintentional**, and **System-Level** threats.

**Classification (2 marks):**

- **Intentional:** Jamming, Spoofing, Meaconing
- **Unintentional:** Multipath, Ionospheric delay, Tropospheric delay, RFI, Signal blockage
- **System-Level:** Satellite clock/ephemeris error, Receiver noise

**Detailed Discussion of Unintentional Threats (3 marks):**

**1. Multipath** — reflections from ground/water/structures cause pseudorange error up to 0.5 C/A chips. **Mitigation:** choke-ring antennas, MEDLL algorithm, double-delta correlators, carrier smoothing.

**2. Ionospheric Delay** — free electrons in the 60–1000 km layer cause frequency-dependent delay proportional to TEC/$f^2$. **Mitigation:** dual-frequency IF combination, SBAS (GAGAN, WAAS, EGNOS) corrections, Klobuchar model.

**3. Tropospheric Delay** — caused by dry gases and water vapor. **Mitigation:** Saastamoinen or Hopfield models with surface met data; differential GNSS removes spatially correlated component.

**4. Signal Blockage** — urban canyons, foliage. **Mitigation:** INS aiding, multi-constellation receivers, dead reckoning.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**

Unintentional threats are systematic and modeled; modern receivers mitigate them with multi-frequency, multi-constellation, and sensor-fusion techniques.

---

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a Controlled Reception Pattern Antenna (CRPA) for anti-jam GNSS reception. Derive the J/S ratio for a typical scenario.** **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

A CRPA consists of **N (typically 4–7) antenna elements** arranged in a circular array. The outputs are combined through an **adaptive beamforming network** steered by a digital nulling algorithm. The network forms high-gain beams toward legitimate satellites and deep nulls toward jammers.

**Operation (2 marks):**

The algorithm computes **spatial covariance matrix** $R_{xx}$ of the received signal. Eigenanalysis identifies the dominant jammer direction. Weights $\mathbf{w}$ are computed via **MVDR (Minimum Variance Distortionless Response)** criterion:

$$
\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \frac{R_{xx}^{-1} \mathbf{a}(\theta_0)}{\mathbf{a}^{H}(\theta_0) R_{xx}^{-1} \mathbf{a}(\theta_0)}
$$

where $\mathbf{a}(\theta_0)$ is the steering vector toward the desired satellite. The output is:

$$
y(t) = \mathbf{w}^{H} \mathbf{x}(t)
$$

**J/S Derivation (2 marks):**

For a satellite at range $d_s$ and jammer at range $d_j$ with antenna gains $G_{r,s}$ and $G_{r,j}$:

$$
J/S = (P_j G_{r,j}/d_{j}^{2}) / (P_s G_{r,s}/d_{s}^{2})
$$

In dB form:

$$
J/S \text{ (dB)} = P_j - P_s + G_{r,j} - G_{r,s} - 20\log_{10}(d_j) + 20\log_{10}(d_s)
$$

**Numerical Sub-case (1 mark):** With $P_j$ = 10 dBW, $P_s$ = −160 dBW (GPS), $d_s$ = 20,200 km, $d_j$ = 5 km, $G_{r,j} = G_{r,s} = 0$ dBi:

$$
J/S = 10 - (-160) + 0 + 0 - 20\log_{10}(5000) + 20\log_{10}(2.02 \times 10^{7})
$$

$$
= 170 - 73.98 + 146.10 = 242.12 \text{ dB}
$$

The jammer is **242 dB** above the signal — total denial. CRPA can provide **30–45 dB of nulling** in the jammer direction, restoring tracking.

---

## ✦ Question B (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]`**

**(a) Explain GNSS spoofing in detail. Discuss the techniques for detection and mitigation of sophisticated spoofing attacks.** **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

**Definition (1 mark):**

GNSS spoofing is the transmission of **false navigation signals** structured to mimic authentic GNSS signals, causing the target receiver to compute an incorrect PVT without losing lock.

**Taxonomy of Spoofing (2 marks):**

1. **Simplistic Spoofing** — single-antenna replay (essentially meaconing).
2. **Intermediate Spoofing** — receiver-spoofer synchronization with single false SV.
3. **Sophisticated Spoofing** — multi-antenna, code/carrier-phase aligned, gradual position lift-off.

**Detection Techniques (2 marks):**

- **Signal Power Monitoring** — spoofed signals often arrive at slightly higher power.
- **Signal Quality Monitoring (SQM)** — analyzes correlator outputs (early-late phase, symmetric peak).
- **Clock Bias Consistency** — spoofed signals disrupt the receiver's internal clock model.
- **Multi-Antenna Angle-of-Arrival** — spatial direction differs from sky-based ephemeris prediction.
- **RAIM (Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring)** — detects inconsistent residuals.
- **Cryptographic Authentication** — Chimera (GPS), OS-NMA (Galileo), CAS (BeiDou).

**Mitigation Techniques (2 marks):**

- **Spatial Filtering (CRPA)** — nulls the spoofer's direction.
- **INS / IMU Aiding** — inertial sensors reject slowly drifting position.
- **Multi-Constellation Cross-Check** — spoofing all constellations simultaneously is exponentially harder.
- **Encrypted Signals (M-Code, PRS)** — military users.

---

**(b) Explain Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (RAIM). Derive the parity-space test statistic and compute the threshold for a 10⁻⁵ false-alarm probability.** **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

**Concept (2 marks):**

RAIM uses **redundant pseudorange measurements** ($n \geq 5$ for detection, $n \geq 6$ for exclusion). A consistency check identifies outliers and excludes faulty satellites.

**Mathematical Formulation (2 marks):**

The linearized pseudorange equation is:

$$
y = H \Delta x + \epsilon
$$

where $y$ is the $n \times 1$ measurement vector, $H$ is the $n \times 4$ geometry matrix, $\Delta x$ is the 4-state update.

The least-squares solution is:

$$
\Delta\hat{x} = (H^{T} H)^{-1} H^{T} y
$$

The residual vector:

$$
r = y - H \Delta\hat{x} = [I - H(H^{T}H)^{-1}H^{T}] y
$$

The **sum of squared residuals (SSR)** is:

$$
SSR = r^{T} r
$$

The **test statistic** is:

$$
T = \sqrt{\dfrac{SSR}{n - 4}}
$$

**Threshold Computation (2 marks):**

Under fault-free hypothesis, $T$ follows a chi-distribution with $n-4$ degrees of freedom. For $P_{fa} = 10^{-5}$:

$$
T_{th} = \sigma_0 \cdot \sqrt{Q^{-1}(P_{fa}/2)} \cdot \sqrt{2/(n-4)}
$$

For $n = 7$ satellites, $P_{fa} = 10^{-5}$, $\sigma_0 = 3$ m (assumed):

$$
T_{th} = 3 \cdot \sqrt{Q^{-1}(5 \times 10^{-6})} \approx 3 \cdot 4.42 \approx 13.26 \text{ m}
$$

**Decision Rule (1 mark):**

If $T > T_{th}$ → fault suspected → exclude the satellite with the largest normalized residual → recompute.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks on GNSS Threats (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Jamming" with "Spoofing"** — examiners immediately deduct 1–2 marks. Jamming = denial; Spoofing = deception.
> 2. **Forgetting units in J/S derivation** — write "dB" explicitly; partial marks are lost otherwise.
> 3. **Skipping the geometric matrix $H$ in RAIM** — students often write the algorithm but forget the underlying $H^{T}H$ formulation, which is the *core* of the 7-mark sub-question.
> 4. **Writing multipath as a code-only error** — it affects **both code and carrier**, but the magnitudes differ (0.5 chip vs 0.25λ). Examiners love this distinction.
> 5. **Missing the negative J/S sign** — a J/S of −60 dB means the *signal is stronger*, not the jammer. Many students write "the receiver is jammed" without interpreting the sign.
> 6. **Forgetting the $f^2$ dependency in ionospheric delay** — the L5 frequency error is $(154/115)^2 \approx 1.79$ times smaller than L1 — this is a favourite follow-up.
> 7. **No mention of multi-constellation / multi-frequency** in mitigation — for 2024 Scheme, examiners award bonus marks for modern solutions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — GNSS Threats**

- **Jamming**: Denial attack. Dominant term in J/S is the **path-loss difference** between satellite (20,200 km) and jammer (km-scale).
- **Spoofing**: Deception attack. *Hardest* to detect because the receiver stays in lock.
- **Meaconing**: Replay of real signal. Detection = **time-stamp verification**.
- **Multipath**: Affects code (≈ 150 m max) and carrier (≈ 5 cm max) — different magnitudes. **Choke-ring antenna** is the classical mitigation.
- **Ionospheric delay**: $\propto \text{TEC}/f^{2}$ — **dual-frequency IF combination cancels it to first order**.
- **Tropospheric delay**: Saastamoinen model with surface met data; differential GNSS removes common-mode.
- **DOP**: Higher DOP ⇒ weaker geometry ⇒ greater threat vulnerability.
- **RAIM**: Requires $n \geq 5$ for detection, $n \geq 6$ for exclusion. Test statistic = $\sqrt{\text{SSR}/(n-4)}$.
- **CRPA**: Provides **30–45 dB** of jammer nulling using adaptive beamforming.
- **SAASM / M-Code**: Military-grade anti-spoof + anti-jam. M-Code uses spot-beam transmission from satellite.
- **OS-NMA / Chimera**: Open-service and military signal-level authentication — modern anti-spoof.
- **Receiver C/N₀**: A drop in $C/N_0$ is the **first warning sign** of jamming.
- **DGNSS / RTK / SBAS**: Mitigate system-level anomalies (clock, ephemeris, ionosphere).
- **INS Aiding**: Last line of defense — inertial sensors reject slowly drifting spoofed positions.
- **Multi-Constellation**: Spoofing GPS + GLONASS + Galileo + BeiDou simultaneously is exponentially harder.
- **GNSS received power**: ≈ **−160 dBW** — 20 dB below thermal noise — explains extreme vulnerability.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** Always answer a GNSS-threat question in the **Threat → Detection → Mitigation** triplet structure. Examiners explicitly award marks for this three-part coverage under the 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) rubric.
